title: Point estimators: bias, consistency, MSE, sufficiency
blurb: An estimator $\hat\theta_n=T(X_1,\ldots,X_n)$ is a measurable function of the sample; bias is $\mathbb{E}_\theta[\hat\theta_n]-\theta$, variance is $\mathrm{Var}_\theta(\hat\theta_n)$, and mean-square error decomposes as $\mathrm{MSE}=\mathrm{bias}^2+\mathrm{variance}$. Consistency means $\hat\theta_n\to\theta$ in probability; a sufficient statistic carries all sample information about $\theta$ via the Fisher–Neyman factorisation $f(x;\theta)=g(T(x);\theta)\,h(x)$.

title: Maximum likelihood and the score equation
blurb: The MLE maximises $\ell(\theta)=\sum_i\log f(X_i;\theta)$, equivalently solves the score equation $\partial_\theta\ell=0$. Under regularity, $\hat\theta_{\mathrm{MLE}}$ is consistent and asymptotically normal: $\sqrt n(\hat\theta_n-\theta)\xrightarrow{d}\mathcal{N}(0,\,1/I(\theta))$ where $I(\theta)=\mathbb{E}_\theta[(\partial_\theta\log f)^2]$ is Fisher information. MLE minimises empirical KL to the true law, so it is the statistical mirror of $D(p\,\|\,q)$.

title: Cramér–Rao bound, UMVU, Rao–Blackwell
blurb: Cramér–Rao: any unbiased $\hat\theta_n$ satisfies $\mathrm{Var}_\theta(\hat\theta_n)\ge 1/(nI(\theta))$ — Fisher information is the floor on estimator variance. An estimator hitting the floor is UMVU (uniformly minimum-variance unbiased). Rao–Blackwell: conditioning any unbiased estimator on a sufficient statistic strictly reduces variance, so UMVU lives among functions of complete sufficient statistics (Lehmann–Scheffé).

title: Hypothesis testing: Neyman–Pearson and likelihood ratios
blurb: A test of $H_0:\theta\in\Theta_0$ vs $H_1:\theta\in\Theta_1$ partitions the sample space into rejection / acceptance; size $\alpha=\sup_{\Theta_0}\mathbb{P}_\theta(\text{reject})$ is type-I error, power $1-\beta=\mathbb{P}_{\theta_1}(\text{reject})$. Neyman–Pearson lemma: for simple-vs-simple, the likelihood ratio test $\Lambda(x)=L(\theta_1;x)/L(\theta_0;x)\gtrless k$ is uniformly most powerful at every level. The $p$-value is the smallest $\alpha$ at which the observed data would have rejected.

title: Bayesian inference: posteriors, conjugate priors, credible intervals
blurb: Bayes' rule on parameters: $\pi(\theta\mid x)\propto\pi(\theta)\,L(\theta;x)$ — the posterior is prior times likelihood, normalised. Conjugate priors close the family under updating (Beta–Binomial, Gamma–Poisson, Normal–Normal). MAP $\hat\theta=\arg\max\pi(\theta\mid x)$ is the posterior mode; the posterior mean minimises squared-error loss. A $1-\alpha$ credible interval is any region of posterior mass $1-\alpha$ — typically the highest-posterior-density interval.

title: Asymptotics: delta method, Slutsky, Wilks
blurb: The delta method propagates CLT through smooth $g$: if $\sqrt n(\hat\theta_n-\theta)\xrightarrow{d}\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2)$ then $\sqrt n(g(\hat\theta_n)-g(\theta))\xrightarrow{d}\mathcal{N}(0,g'(\theta)^2\sigma^2)$. Slutsky lets you replace nuisance estimates by their probability limits inside a converging sequence. Wilks' theorem: under $H_0$, $-2\log\Lambda_n\xrightarrow{d}\chi^2_{r}$ where $r=\dim\Theta-\dim\Theta_0$ — the LRT calibrates itself asymptotically without simulation.

q: Let $X_1,\ldots,X_n$ be i.i.d. with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$. Which estimator of $\sigma^2$ is unbiased?
text: Let $X_1,\ldots,X_n$ be i.i.d. with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$. Which estimator of $\sigma^2$ is unbiased? The Bessel-corrected sample variance $S^2=\tfrac{1}{n-1}\sum(X_i-\bar X)^2$ has $\mathbb{E}[S^2]=\sigma^2$. The $1/n$ MLE version is biased downward by a factor $(n-1)/n$ because $\bar X$ already absorbed one degree of freedom.

q: Estimator $\hat\theta$ has bias $0.2$ and variance $0.09$ at the true parameter. What is its MSE?
text: Estimator $\hat\theta$ has bias $0.2$ and variance $0.09$ at the true parameter. What is its MSE? $\mathrm{MSE}=\mathrm{bias}^2+\mathrm{var}=0.04+0.09=0.13$. The bias-variance decomposition is the basic accounting identity for risk under squared loss.

q: By the Fisher–Neyman factorisation theorem, $T(X)$ is sufficient for $\theta$ iff the joint density factors as:
text: By the Fisher–Neyman factorisation theorem, $T(X)$ is sufficient for $\theta$ iff the joint density factors as: Fisher–Neyman: $T$ is sufficient iff $f(x;\theta)$ splits as a piece $g$ that depends on the data only through $T(x)$ times a $\theta$-free piece $h(x)$. Equivalently, the conditional law of $X$ given $T$ is free of $\theta$.

q: For i.i.d. $X_i\sim\mathrm{Exp}(\lambda)$ (density $\lambda e^{-\lambda x}$, $x>0$), compute the MLE of $\lambda$ when $n=5$ and $\sum X_i=10$.
text: For i.i.d. $X_i\sim\mathrm{Exp}(\lambda)$ (density $\lambda e^{-\lambda x}$, $x>0$), compute the MLE of $\lambda$ when $n=5$ and $\sum X_i=10$. $\ell(\lambda)=n\log\lambda-\lambda\sum X_i$; setting $\ell'=n/\lambda-\sum X_i=0$ gives $\hat\lambda=n/\sum X_i=5/10=0.5$. The MLE for an exponential rate is the reciprocal of the sample mean.

q: For $X_i\sim\mathcal{N}(\theta,1)$, the Fisher information per sample is $I(\theta)=$?
text: For $X_i\sim\mathcal{N}(\theta,1)$, the Fisher information per sample is $I(\theta)=$? $\log f=-\tfrac{1}{2}(x-\theta)^2+\text{const}$, so $\partial_\theta\log f=x-\theta$ and $I(\theta)=\mathbb{E}[(X-\theta)^2]=\sigma^2=1$. Asymptotic variance of $\hat\theta_{\mathrm{MLE}}=\bar X$ is $1/(nI)=1/n$, matching $\mathrm{Var}(\bar X)$ exactly.

q: MLE corresponds to minimising which divergence between the empirical distribution $\hat p_n$ and the model family $\{p_\theta\}$?
text: MLE corresponds to minimising which divergence between the empirical distribution $\hat p_n$ and the model family $\{p_\theta\}$? $D(\hat p_n\,\|\,p_\theta)=-\tfrac{1}{n}\sum\log p_\theta(X_i)+\text{const}(\hat p_n)$, so minimising KL in $\theta$ maximises $\sum\log p_\theta(X_i)$ — the log-likelihood. This is exactly why MLE inherits Sanov-style asymptotics.

q: For i.i.d. $X_i\sim\mathcal{N}(\mu,\sigma^2)$ with $\sigma^2$ known, the Cramér–Rao lower bound on $\mathrm{Var}(\hat\mu)$ for unbiased $\hat\mu$ from $n$ samples equals $\sigma^2/(cn)$. What is $c$?
text: For i.i.d. $X_i\sim\mathcal{N}(\mu,\sigma^2)$ with $\sigma^2$ known, the Cramér–Rao lower bound on $\mathrm{Var}(\hat\mu)$ for unbiased $\hat\mu$ from $n$ samples equals $\sigma^2/(cn)$. What is $c$? $I(\mu)=1/\sigma^2$ per observation, so the CRLB is $1/(nI)=\sigma^2/n$, i.e. $c=1$. The sample mean $\bar X$ achieves it — $\bar X$ is UMVU for $\mu$.

q: Rao–Blackwell says: given an unbiased estimator $W$ and a sufficient statistic $T$, the estimator $W^*=\mathbb{E}[W\mid T]$:
text: Rao–Blackwell says: given an unbiased estimator $W$ and a sufficient statistic $T$, the estimator $W^*=\mathbb{E}[W\mid T]$: By tower, $\mathbb{E}[W^*]=\mathbb{E}[W]=\theta$ (still unbiased). Conditional Jensen / law of total variance gives $\mathrm{Var}(W^*)\le\mathrm{Var}(W)$, with equality iff $W$ was already a function of $T$. Combined with completeness (Lehmann–Scheffé), this gives UMVU.

q: Which estimator achieves the Cramér–Rao bound?
text: Which estimator achieves the Cramér–Rao bound? An estimator hitting the CRLB is called efficient. Generically, only special unbiased estimators (e.g. $\bar X$ for Gaussian mean) hit it exactly; the MLE achieves it asymptotically — $\sqrt n(\hat\theta_{\mathrm{MLE}}-\theta)\to\mathcal{N}(0,1/I(\theta))$.

q: For testing $H_0:\theta=\theta_0$ vs $H_1:\theta=\theta_1$ (both simple), the Neyman–Pearson lemma says the most powerful level-$\alpha$ test rejects when:
text: For testing $H_0:\theta=\theta_0$ vs $H_1:\theta=\theta_1$ (both simple), the Neyman–Pearson lemma says the most powerful level-$\alpha$ test rejects when: Neyman–Pearson is constructive: fix $\alpha=\mathbb{P}_{\theta_0}(\Lambda>k)$, then rejection region $\{\Lambda>k\}$ is the most powerful at level $\alpha$ — every other size-$\alpha$ test has power $\le 1-\beta_{\text{NP}}$.

q: A z-test of $H_0:\mu=0$ vs $H_1:\mu>0$ (one-sided, known $\sigma=1$, $n=25$ samples) rejects at level $\alpha=0.05$ when $\bar X>1.645/\sqrt{25}$. What is the threshold on $\bar X$? (Round to 4 decimals.)
text: A z-test of $H_0:\mu=0$ vs $H_1:\mu>0$ (one-sided, known $\sigma=1$, $n=25$ samples) rejects at level $\alpha=0.05$ when $\bar X>1.645/\sqrt{25}$. What is the threshold on $\bar X$? (Round to 4 decimals.) Under $H_0$, $\bar X\sim\mathcal{N}(0,1/n)$. The $0.95$ quantile of $\mathcal{N}(0,1/n)$ is $z_{0.95}/\sqrt n=1.645/5=0.329$. Reject when $\bar X>0.329$.

q: The $p$-value of an observation $x_{\text{obs}}$ is best described as:
text: The $p$-value of an observation $x_{\text{obs}}$ is best described as: $p=\mathbb{P}_{H_0}(T(X)\ge T(x_{\text{obs}}))$ — a probability under the null, not a posterior on $H_0$. Confusing the two is the canonical frequentist–Bayesian fallacy.

q: Beta–Binomial conjugacy: with $\theta\sim\mathrm{Beta}(\alpha,\beta)$ prior and $X\sim\mathrm{Binom}(n,\theta)$ data, the posterior is:
text: Beta–Binomial conjugacy: with $\theta\sim\mathrm{Beta}(\alpha,\beta)$ prior and $X\sim\mathrm{Binom}(n,\theta)$ data, the posterior is: Posterior $\propto\theta^{\alpha-1}(1-\theta)^{\beta-1}\cdot\theta^x(1-\theta)^{n-x}=\theta^{\alpha+x-1}(1-\theta)^{\beta+n-x-1}$ — a $\mathrm{Beta}(\alpha+x,\beta+n-x)$. Conjugate priors are exactly the families closed under this update.

q: Flat prior $\theta\sim\mathrm{Beta}(1,1)$ on a coin's heads-probability; observe $7$ heads in $10$ flips. The posterior is $\mathrm{Beta}(8,4)$. Compute the posterior mean.
text: Flat prior $\theta\sim\mathrm{Beta}(1,1)$ on a coin's heads-probability; observe $7$ heads in $10$ flips. The posterior is $\mathrm{Beta}(8,4)$. Compute the posterior mean. $\mathbb{E}[\theta\mid x]=\alpha'/(\alpha'+\beta')=8/12=2/3\approx 0.667$. The MLE was $7/10=0.7$; the prior pulls the estimate slightly toward $1/2$.

q: MAP and posterior mean coincide when:
text: MAP and posterior mean coincide when: For symmetric unimodal posteriors, mode = mean. Asymmetric posteriors (e.g. $\mathrm{Beta}(2,5)$) have mode $\neq$ mean. MAP minimises a $0$–$1$ loss; posterior mean minimises squared-error loss.

q: Apply the delta method: if $\sqrt n(\hat\theta_n-\theta)\xrightarrow{d}\mathcal{N}(0,1)$ and $g(\theta)=\theta^2$ with $\theta=3$, then $\sqrt n(g(\hat\theta_n)-g(\theta))\xrightarrow{d}\mathcal{N}(0,v)$. Compute $v$.
text: Apply the delta method: if $\sqrt n(\hat\theta_n-\theta)\xrightarrow{d}\mathcal{N}(0,1)$ and $g(\theta)=\theta^2$ with $\theta=3$, then $\sqrt n(g(\hat\theta_n)-g(\theta))\xrightarrow{d}\mathcal{N}(0,v)$. Compute $v$. Delta method: $v=g'(\theta)^2\sigma^2=(2\theta)^2\cdot 1=4\theta^2=4\cdot 9=36$. The Jacobian of $g$ stretches the asymptotic variance by $g'(\theta)^2$.

q: Wilks' theorem says that under $H_0$, the LRT statistic $-2\log\Lambda_n$ converges in distribution to:
text: Wilks' theorem says that under $H_0$, the LRT statistic $-2\log\Lambda_n$ converges in distribution to: $-2\log\Lambda_n\xrightarrow{d}\chi^2_r$ with $r$ the difference in dimensions. This is what calibrates LRT-based confidence regions: $\{\theta:-2\log\Lambda(\theta)<\chi^2_{r,1-\alpha}\}$ has asymptotic coverage $1-\alpha$.

q: Slutsky's theorem says: if $X_n\xrightarrow{d}X$ and $Y_n\xrightarrow{p}c$ (constant), then:
text: Slutsky's theorem says: if $X_n\xrightarrow{d}X$ and $Y_n\xrightarrow{p}c$ (constant), then: Slutsky lets you replace a consistent nuisance estimate by its limit inside a converging-in-distribution sequence. Used constantly: $\sqrt n(\bar X-\mu)/S\xrightarrow{d}\mathcal{N}(0,1)$ because $S\xrightarrow{p}\sigma$.

title: Floating-point arithmetic
blurb: IEEE 754 represents reals as $(-1)^s \cdot m \cdot 2^e$ with finite mantissa: machine epsilon $\varepsilon_M\approx 2.22\times 10^{-16}$ in double precision bounds the relative roundoff $\mathrm{fl}(x)=x(1+\delta)$. Conditioning of a problem and numerical stability of an algorithm are two independent axes; a stable algorithm on an ill-conditioned problem still loses digits.

title: Rootfinding: bisection & Newton
blurb: Bisection halves an interval $[a,b]$ with $f(a)f(b)<0$, converging linearly with rate $1/2$. Newton's iteration $x_{n+1}=x_n-f(x_n)/f'(x_n)$ converges quadratically near a simple root: $|e_{n+1}|\le C|e_n|^2$. The order $p$ of convergence is the supremum with $|e_{n+1}|\le C|e_n|^p$; secant gives the golden ratio $p=(1+\sqrt5)/2$.

title: Quadrature: trapezoid, Simpson, Gauss
blurb: Composite trapezoid on $n$ panels has error $-\tfrac{(b-a)h^2}{12}f''(\xi)$ with $h=(b-a)/n$; Simpson's rule integrates cubics exactly with error $O(h^4)$; Gauss–Legendre with $n$ nodes integrates polynomials up to degree $2n-1$ exactly by choosing nodes as zeros of Legendre polynomials. The exponent of $h$ is the order of accuracy.

title: Linear systems: direct & iterative solvers
blurb: $Ax=b$ via LU $(O(n^3))$, Cholesky for SPD $(\tfrac12 n^3)$, or QR for least-squares; the condition number $\kappa(A)=\|A\|\,\|A^{-1}\|$ governs how perturbations $\delta b$ amplify into $\delta x$. For large sparse $A$, stationary iterations (Jacobi, Gauss–Seidel) converge when the spectral radius of the iteration matrix is $<1$, and Krylov methods (conjugate gradient on SPD systems) reach the solution in $O(\sqrt{\kappa})$ iterations.

title: Finite differences and CFL stability
blurb: Forward $(f(x+h)-f(x))/h$, backward, and central $(f(x+h)-f(x-h))/(2h)$ differences approximate $f'$ with truncation error $O(h)$, $O(h)$, $O(h^2)$. Discretizing $u_t=u_{xx}$ explicitly demands $\Delta t\le \tfrac{1}{2}(\Delta x)^2$; the wave equation explicit scheme demands the CFL condition $c\,\Delta t/\Delta x\le 1$. Cross those bounds and the scheme amplifies round-off geometrically.

title: Finite elements & Galerkin formulation
blurb:
On a mesh $\mathcal{T}_h$ with piecewise-linear hat basis $\{\varphi_i\}$, write $u_h=\sum c_i\varphi_i\in V_h\subset H^1_0(\Omega)$ and impose $\int
abla u_h\cdot
abla \varphi_j=\int f\varphi_j$ for every $j$. The stiffness matrix $K_{ij}=\int
abla\varphi_i\cdot
abla\varphi_j$ is sparse and SPD; Céa's lemma plus interpolation give the a priori estimate $\|u-u_h\|_{H^1}\le C h\|u\|_{H^2}$.

q: In IEEE 754 double precision, machine epsilon $\varepsilon_M$ is closest to which value?
text: In IEEE 754 double precision, machine epsilon $\varepsilon_M$ is closest to which value? Doubles use a 52-bit mantissa, so $\varepsilon_M = 2^{-52}\approx 2.22\times 10^{-16}$. Single precision (24-bit) gives $2^{-23}\approx 1.19\times 10^{-7}$; the other values are unrelated to the format.

q: A problem is ill-conditioned but the algorithm is backward-stable. What can you conclude about the computed result?
text: A problem is ill-conditioned but the algorithm is backward-stable. What can you conclude about the computed result? Backward stability means $\hat{y}=f(x+\delta x)$ with $\delta x$ small. The forward error is then bounded by $\kappa(\text{problem})\cdot \|\delta x\|$: a stable algorithm on an ill-conditioned problem still loses digits, and that's not the algorithm's fault.

q: If the relative roundoff per operation is $\varepsilon_M$ and you sum $n=10^6$ floats naively, the worst-case relative error is bounded by approximately $n\varepsilon_M$. With $\varepsilon_M=2^{-52}\approx 2.22\times 10^{-16}$, what is $n\varepsilon_M$?
text: If the relative roundoff per operation is $\varepsilon_M$ and you sum $n=10^6$ floats naively, the worst-case relative error is bounded by approximately $n\varepsilon_M$. With $\varepsilon_M=2^{-52}\approx 2.22\times 10^{-16}$, what is $n\varepsilon_M$? $10^6 \times 2.22\times 10^{-16} = 2.22\times 10^{-10}$. Kahan summation tightens this to $O(\varepsilon_M)$ regardless of $n$ by carrying a running compensation term.

q: Newton's method applied to $f(x)=x^2-2$ starting from $x_0=1$. What is $x_2$?
text: Newton's method applied to $f(x)=x^2-2$ starting from $x_0=1$. What is $x_2$? Newton: $x_{n+1}=x_n - (x_n^2-2)/(2x_n) = (x_n+2/x_n)/2$. From $x_0=1$: $x_1=(1+2)/2=1.5$, then $x_2=(1.5+4/3)/2=17/12\approx 1.41667$. Quadratic convergence — three iterations get you to machine precision.

q: Bisection on $[0,1]$ with tolerance $10^{-6}$ requires roughly how many iterations?
text: Bisection on $[0,1]$ with tolerance $10^{-6}$ requires roughly how many iterations? After $n$ steps the interval length is $2^{-n}$. We need $2^{-n} \log_2(10^6)\approx 19.93$, so $n=20$. Bisection's linear convergence with rate $1/2$ means each step buys exactly one bit.

q: What is the order of convergence of the secant method near a simple root?
text: What is the order of convergence of the secant method near a simple root? The secant error recursion $e_{n+1}\sim C e_n e_{n-1}$ leads to $p^2 = p+1$, whose positive root is the golden ratio $\phi=(1+\sqrt 5)/2\approx 1.618$. It's slower than Newton's $p=2$ but trades the derivative evaluation for cheaper iterations.

q: Composite trapezoid rule on $n$ panels has error scaling as $h^2$ where $h=(b-a)/n$. If doubling $n$ shrinks the error by what factor?
text: Composite trapezoid rule on $n$ panels has error scaling as $h^2$ where $h=(b-a)/n$. If doubling $n$ shrinks the error by what factor? Halving $h$ shrinks $h^2$ by $4$. So each doubling of $n$ buys two bits of accuracy. Simpson's $O(h^4)$ buys four bits per doubling.

q: Gauss–Legendre quadrature with $n$ nodes integrates polynomials of degree $\le ?$ exactly.
text: Gauss–Legendre quadrature with $n$ nodes integrates polynomials of degree $\le ?$ exactly. Choosing the $n$ nodes as zeros of the $n$-th Legendre polynomial buys an extra $n$ degrees of exactness over a generic $n$-point quadrature, giving exactness up to degree $2n-1$. This is optimal: no $n$-point rule integrates all polynomials of degree $2n$ exactly.

q: Simpson's rule is exact for polynomials of degree up to:
text: Simpson's rule is exact for polynomials of degree up to: By construction Simpson's rule integrates parabolas exactly, but a symmetry argument bumps the exactness to cubics: the cubic remainder is odd about the midpoint and integrates to zero. The error term is $-(b-a)h^4 f^{(4)}(\xi)/180$.

q: If $A$ is symmetric positive definite, which factorization is the natural choice and what is its leading cost?
text: If $A$ is symmetric positive definite, which factorization is the natural choice and what is its leading cost? Cholesky exploits SPD structure to halve LU's cost and avoid pivoting. It also fails fast: a non-positive pivot signals that $A$ is not SPD, useful as a numerical SPD test.

q: If $\kappa(A)=10^8$ and you solve $Ax=b$ in IEEE double precision ($\varepsilon_M\approx 10^{-16}$), the relative error in $x$ is bounded by roughly $\kappa(A)\varepsilon_M$. Enter this bound (a number).
text: If $\kappa(A)=10^8$ and you solve $Ax=b$ in IEEE double precision ($\varepsilon_M\approx 10^{-16}$), the relative error in $x$ is bounded by roughly $\kappa(A)\varepsilon_M$. Enter this bound (a number). $\kappa(A)\,\varepsilon_M = 10^8\cdot 10^{-16}=10^{-8}$. Rule of thumb: you lose $\log_{10}\kappa(A)$ digits of accuracy. Conjugate gradient sees a related but weaker dependence: convergence rate $\sim (\sqrt\kappa-1)/(\sqrt\kappa+1)$.

q: Conjugate gradient on an SPD system with condition number $\kappa$ converges in roughly how many iterations to a fixed tolerance?
text: Conjugate gradient on an SPD system with condition number $\kappa$ converges in roughly how many iterations to a fixed tolerance? The CG error decays as $((\sqrt\kappa-1)/(\sqrt\kappa+1))^k$, so reducing the error by a fixed factor needs $O(\sqrt\kappa)$ steps. This is exponentially better than Jacobi/Gauss–Seidel's $O(\kappa)$ — preconditioning is the standard way to shrink $\kappa$.

q: Which finite difference approximates $f'(x)$ with error $O(h^2)$ rather than $O(h)$?
text: Which finite difference approximates $f'(x)$ with error $O(h^2)$ rather than $O(h)$? Taylor expansion: $f(x\pm h)=f(x)\pm h f'(x) + \tfrac{h^2}{2}f''(x)\pm\tfrac{h^3}{6}f'''(x)+\cdots$. The central difference cancels the $f''$ term, leaving $f'(x)+\tfrac{h^2}{6}f'''(x)+\cdots$. The other one-sided differences keep the $f''(x)h/2$ leading error.

q: An explicit FD scheme for the heat equation $u_t=u_{xx}$ is stable when:
text: An explicit FD scheme for the heat equation $u_t=u_{xx}$ is stable when: Von Neumann analysis on $u^{n+1}_j=u^n_j + (\Delta t/\Delta x^2)(u^n_{j+1}-2u^n_j+u^n_{j-1})$ gives an amplification factor whose modulus stays $\le 1$ iff $\Delta t/\Delta x^2\le 1/2$. Cross that and tiny errors blow up exponentially. Implicit (Crank–Nicolson) schemes lift the restriction at the cost of a linear solve per step.

q: For the wave equation $u_{tt}=c^2 u_{xx}$ with $c=2$ and $\Delta x=0.1$, the CFL condition $c\Delta t/\Delta x\le 1$ caps $\Delta t$. What is the largest stable $\Delta t$?
text: For the wave equation $u_{tt}=c^2 u_{xx}$ with $c=2$ and $\Delta x=0.1$, the CFL condition $c\Delta t/\Delta x\le 1$ caps $\Delta t$. What is the largest stable $\Delta t$? $\Delta t\le \Delta x/c = 0.1/2 = 0.05$. The condition encodes that the numerical domain of dependence must contain the analytic one (the cone with slope $1/c$); otherwise information that should reach a node is missed by the stencil.

q: In the FEM Galerkin formulation for $-\Delta u=f$ on $\Omega$ with $u=0$ on $\partial\Omega$, the stiffness matrix entry $K_{ij}$ equals:
text:
In the FEM Galerkin formulation for $-\Delta u=f$ on $\Omega$ with $u=0$ on $\partial\Omega$, the stiffness matrix entry $K_{ij}$ equals: Multiply $-\Delta u=f$ by $\varphi_j$ and integrate by parts: $\int
abla u\cdot
abla\varphi_j = \int f\varphi_j$. Substituting $u=\sum c_i\varphi_i$ produces $K_{ij}=\int
abla\varphi_i\cdot
abla\varphi_j$ and the load vector $b_j=\int f\varphi_j$. The first option $\int\varphi_i\varphi_j$ is the mass matrix, used for time-dependent problems.

q: With piecewise-linear hat basis on a mesh of size $h$, Céa's lemma gives the a priori estimate $\|u-u_h\|_{H^1}\le ?$
text: With piecewise-linear hat basis on a mesh of size $h$, Céa's lemma gives the a priori estimate $\|u-u_h\|_{H^1}\le ?$ Céa's lemma + linear interpolation error in $H^1$ gives $\|u-u_h\|_{H^1}\le Ch\|u\|_{H^2}$. The $L^2$ estimate is one order better: $\|u-u_h\|_{L^2}\le Ch^2\|u\|_{H^2}$ via Aubin–Nitsche duality. Higher-order ($P^k$) elements scale as $h^k$ in $H^1$.

q: Which property of the FEM stiffness matrix $K$ for $-\Delta$ with Dirichlet BCs makes conjugate gradient an excellent solver?
text: Which property of the FEM stiffness matrix $K$ for $-\Delta$ with Dirichlet BCs makes conjugate gradient an excellent solver? Each $\varphi_i$ has compact support, so $K_{ij}\ne 0$ only when supports overlap — sparse. Symmetry is immediate from the symmetric bilinear form. Positivity: $v^\top K v=\int|\nabla(\sum v_i\varphi_i)|^2\ge 0$ with equality only at $v=0$ (Poincaré on $H^1_0$). SPD + sparse is exactly the regime where CG with multigrid preconditioning shines.

title: Primality testing
blurb: Fermat's little theorem, the Miller–Rabin probabilistic test, and the AKS deterministic polynomial-time algorithm. Composite witnesses turn $a^{n-1}\not\equiv 1\pmod n$ into a verifiable proof of compositeness; AKS removes randomness at the cost of a higher polynomial.

title: Integer factoring
blurb: Trial division, Pollard's $\rho$ method (Floyd cycle detection on $x \mapsto x^2+1 \bmod n$), Pollard's $p-1$ method, the quadratic sieve, and the general number-field sieve. Subexponential complexity $L_n[1/3,c]$ is the current frontier — no polynomial-time classical algorithm is known.

title: LLL & lattice reduction
blurb: The Lenstra–Lenstra–Lovász algorithm finds a basis whose first vector is at most $2^{(n-1)/2}$ times the shortest in polynomial time. Applications: integer-relation detection (PSLQ-style), Coppersmith's small-roots attack on RSA, knapsack cryptanalysis, and post-quantum lattice schemes.

title: Modular arithmetic algorithms
blurb: Fast modular exponentiation by repeated squaring ($O(\log n)$ multiplies), Tonelli–Shanks for square roots modulo $p$ when $\left(\tfrac{a}{p}\right)=1$, and the Chinese remainder theorem implemented as Garner's algorithm. The arithmetic primitives every higher-level routine assumes.

title: Schoof & point counting
blurb: Schoof's algorithm computes $\#E(\mathbb{F}_p) = p+1-t$ in time polynomial in $\log p$ by determining $t \bmod \ell$ for many small primes $\ell$ via the action of Frobenius on $\ell$-torsion, then reconstructing $t$ via CRT. Schoof–Elkies–Atkin (SEA) speeds this up using modular polynomials.

title: Class & unit group computation
blurb: Buchmann's subexponential algorithm (under GRH) computes the class number, regulator, and a system of fundamental units of a number field by relation-collection in a factor base of small prime ideals plus LLL reduction of the relation lattice. The algorithm's correctness assumes the generalized Riemann hypothesis for Hecke L-functions.

q: Why is Fermat's little theorem alone insufficient as a primality test?
text: Why is Fermat's little theorem alone insufficient as a primality test? Carmichael numbers are composite Fermat liars for every base coprime to $n$. Miller–Rabin strengthens the test by also checking square roots of $1$, ruling these out.

q: Run a single Miller–Rabin round on $n=15$ with base $a=2$. Write $n-1 = 14 = 2 \cdot 7$, so $d=7,\ s=1$. Compute $2^7 \bmod 15$.
text: Run a single Miller–Rabin round on $n=15$ with base $a=2$. Write $n-1 = 14 = 2 \cdot 7$, so $d=7,\ s=1$. Compute $2^7 \bmod 15$. $2^7 = 128 = 8\cdot 15 + 8$, so $2^7 \equiv 8 \pmod{15}$. Since $8 \not\equiv \pm 1$ and $s=1$ leaves no further squarings, $a=2$ is a Miller–Rabin witness — $15$ is composite.

q: What is the asymptotic running time of the AKS deterministic primality test in $\log n$?
text: What is the asymptotic running time of the AKS deterministic primality test in $\log n$? AKS (2002) was the first deterministic, unconditional, polynomial-time primality test. Tighter analyses bring the exponent down toward $6$; Miller–Rabin remains faster in practice but is probabilistic.

q: Pollard's $\rho$ method for factoring $n$ iterates $x \mapsto x^2 + c \bmod n$ and looks for a collision. Why does it work?
text: Pollard's $\rho$ method for factoring $n$ iterates $x \mapsto x^2 + c \bmod n$ and looks for a collision. Why does it work? The sequence reduced mod $p$ is shorter than mod $n$, so a cycle appears in $O(\sqrt p)$ steps. Floyd's tortoise–hare detector finds two iterates with $x_i \equiv x_j \pmod p$ but $x_i \not\equiv x_j \pmod n$, so $\gcd(x_i-x_j, n)$ is a nontrivial factor.

q: The general number-field sieve runs in time approximately:
text: The general number-field sieve runs in time approximately: GNFS achieves $L_n[1/3, c]$ with $c=(64/9)^{1/3}\approx 1.923$ for general integers. This is the current asymptotic record; no polynomial-time classical factoring algorithm is known.

q: Run Pollard's $\rho$ with $f(x)=x^2+1$, $x_0=2$ on $n=91$. After two steps the sequence is $x_1=5, x_2=26$. Compute $\gcd(x_2-x_1,\ 91)$.
text: Run Pollard's $\rho$ with $f(x)=x^2+1$, $x_0=2$ on $n=91$. After two steps the sequence is $x_1=5, x_2=26$. Compute $\gcd(x_2-x_1,\ 91)$. $x_2 - x_1 = 21$, and $\gcd(21, 91) = 7$. So $91 = 7 \cdot 13$.

q: What does LLL guarantee about its output basis $b_1, \dots, b_n$?
text: What does LLL guarantee about its output basis $b_1, \dots, b_n$? LLL trades exact shortness for polynomial-time computability: the first basis vector is within a factor $2^{(n-1)/2}$ of optimal — exponentially loose, but enough to break many cryptosystems and find integer relations.

q: Coppersmith's theorem uses LLL to find:
text: Coppersmith's theorem uses LLL to find: Coppersmith builds a lattice from shifted multiples of $f$ and applies LLL; a short vector encodes a polynomial with the same small roots over $\mathbb{Z}$, which are then findable. Used in stereotyped-message attacks on low-exponent RSA.

q: Which is NOT a standard application of LLL?
text: Which is NOT a standard application of LLL? LLL accelerates many sub-routines in factoring (Coppersmith) but no polynomial-time factoring algorithm is known — the other three are textbook applications.

q: Compute $7^{13} \bmod 11$ by repeated squaring. (Binary: $13 = 1101_2$.)
text: Compute $7^{13} \bmod 11$ by repeated squaring. (Binary: $13 = 1101_2$.) $7^2\equiv 5$, $7^4\equiv 25\equiv 3$, $7^8\equiv 9$. So $7^{13}=7^8\cdot 7^4\cdot 7^1 \equiv 9\cdot 3\cdot 7 = 189 \equiv 2\pmod{11}$.

q: Tonelli–Shanks computes $\sqrt{a} \bmod p$ when $\left(\tfrac{a}{p}\right)=1$. The simple case $p\equiv 3\pmod 4$ has the closed form:
text: Tonelli–Shanks computes $\sqrt{a} \bmod p$ when $\left(\tfrac{a}{p}\right)=1$. The simple case $p\equiv 3\pmod 4$ has the closed form: If $p\equiv 3\pmod 4$, set $r = a^{(p+1)/4}$. Then $r^2 = a^{(p+1)/2} = a\cdot a^{(p-1)/2} \equiv a$ since $a$ is a QR. The general algorithm handles $p\equiv 1\pmod 4$ with extra work.

q: Solve $x \equiv 2 \pmod 3,\ x \equiv 3 \pmod 5,\ x \equiv 2 \pmod 7$ via CRT. Give the smallest non-negative $x$.
text: Solve $x \equiv 2 \pmod 3,\ x \equiv 3 \pmod 5,\ x \equiv 2 \pmod 7$ via CRT. Give the smallest non-negative $x$. Modulus $3\cdot 5\cdot 7=105$. $x=23$ satisfies all three: $23=7\cdot 3+2$, $23=4\cdot 5+3$, $23=3\cdot 7+2$.

q: Schoof's algorithm computes $\#E(\mathbb{F}_p)=p+1-t$ by determining $t \bmod \ell$ for several small primes $\ell$. Why does this suffice?
text: Schoof's algorithm computes $\#E(\mathbb{F}_p)=p+1-t$ by determining $t \bmod \ell$ for several small primes $\ell$. Why does this suffice? Hasse bounds $t$ to a window of width $4\sqrt p$. Combining residues mod enough small $\ell$ via CRT pins $t$ down uniquely; the resulting algorithm is polynomial in $\log p$.

q: On what does Schoof compute the action of Frobenius $\phi_p:(x,y)\mapsto(x^p,y^p)$?
text: On what does Schoof compute the action of Frobenius $\phi_p:(x,y)\mapsto(x^p,y^p)$? Computing in $\mathbb{F}_p[x,y]/(\psi_\ell, E)$ where $\psi_\ell$ is the $\ell$-th division polynomial keeps everything finite-dimensional. Schoof then matches $\phi_p^2 - t\phi_p + p \equiv 0$ on $E[\ell]$ to extract $t \bmod \ell$.

q: The SEA improvement to Schoof exploits:
text: The SEA improvement to Schoof exploits: Schoof–Elkies–Atkin uses the modular polynomial $\Phi_\ell$ to detect when Frobenius has rational eigenvectors on $E[\ell]$ (Elkies primes), reducing the work from degree $\ell^2-1$ to roughly $\ell$.

q: Buchmann's algorithm computes the class group of a number field $K$ in subexponential time, but only conditionally. On what?
text: Buchmann's algorithm computes the class group of a number field $K$ in subexponential time, but only conditionally. On what? GRH is needed to bound the size of generators of the class group: under GRH, prime ideals up to $(\log |\Delta_K|)^{2}$ generate $\mathrm{Cl}(K)$, making the factor-base relation method provably correct in subexponential time.

q: Where does LLL enter Buchmann's algorithm?
text: Where does LLL enter Buchmann's algorithm? Each relation among factor-base prime ideals lives in $\mathbb{Z}^{|S|}$ together with a logarithm vector for units. LLL reduces this combined lattice; its Smith normal form yields the class group invariants and a basis of fundamental units (hence the regulator).

q: The class number of $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{-5})$ is:
text: The class number of $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{-5})$ is: The ring of integers $\mathbb{Z}[\sqrt{-5}]$ is the canonical example of a non-PID: $6 = 2\cdot 3 = (1+\sqrt{-5})(1-\sqrt{-5})$ are two essentially different factorizations. The class group is $\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}$, so $h=2$.

title: Functional derivative
blurb: $\delta J/\delta u$ measures the linear response of a functional to a perturbation; the Gateaux directional derivative $\lim_{\varepsilon\to 0}(J(u+\varepsilon h)-J(u))/\varepsilon$ upgrades to a Fréchet derivative when the linear part dominates uniformly in $h$.

title: Euler–Lagrange equations
blurb: Stationarity of $J(u)=\int L(x,u,u')\,dx$ against compactly-supported variations forces $\frac{d}{dx}\partial_{u'}L = \partial_u L$; the necessary condition every classical minimiser must satisfy.

title: Direct method (Tonelli)
blurb: Coercivity bounds a minimising sequence in a reflexive space; weak compactness extracts a subsequential limit; weak lower-semicontinuity ($J(u)\le\liminf J(u_k)$) certifies it as a minimiser. The blueprint behind every modern existence theorem in calculus of variations.

title: Mountain-pass theorem
blurb: Ambrosetti–Rabinowitz: if $J$ has a local min at the origin and dips below it at some far point, the saddle-height $c=\inf_\gamma\max_t J(\gamma(t))$ over connecting paths is a critical value — provided the Palais–Smale compactness condition holds.

title: Isoperimetric inequality
blurb: Among planar regions of fixed perimeter $L$, the disk uniquely maximises area: $4\pi A\le L^2$. The Euler–Lagrange analysis gives constant geodesic curvature, and a calibration argument certifies the disk as the global optimum — the soap-bubble principle.

title: Applications: geodesics, minimal surfaces, gauge theory
blurb: Geodesics minimise arc-length and satisfy $\nabla_{\dot\gamma}\dot\gamma=0$; Plateau's problem produces minimal surfaces with $H=0$; thin elastic plates minimise bending energy $\int|\Delta u|^2$; Yang–Mills connections are critical points of $\int|F_A|^2$. All of physics is a stationarity condition.

q: Let $J(u)=\tfrac12\int_0^1 (u'(x))^2\,dx$ on $H^1_0(0,1)$. The Gateaux derivative $\langle J'(u),h\rangle$ equals which expression?
text: Let $J(u)=\tfrac12\int_0^1 (u'(x))^2\,dx$ on $H^1_0(0,1)$. The Gateaux derivative $\langle J'(u),h\rangle$ equals which expression? Expand $J(u+\varepsilon h)=\tfrac12\int(u'+\varepsilon h')^2 = J(u)+\varepsilon\int u'h' + O(\varepsilon^2)$. The linear coefficient is the Gateaux derivative. After integrating by parts and using $h\in H^1_0$, this also equals $-\int u''h$, which is how the Euler-Lagrange equation $-u''=0$ pops out.

q: Which is the correct distinction between Gateaux and Fréchet derivatives?
text: Which is the correct distinction between Gateaux and Fréchet derivatives? Gateaux differentiability gives a directional derivative for each fixed $h$ but allows the remainder to depend non-uniformly on direction. Fréchet upgrades this: $J(u+h)=J(u)+\langle J'(u),h\rangle+o(\|h\|)$ uniformly in $h$, recovering classical differential calculus and chain rule.

q: For $J(u)=\int_0^1 u(x)^3\,dx$, compute $\langle J'(u),h\rangle / \int_0^1 h\,dx$ when $u\equiv 2$ and $h$ is any test function with $\int h = 1$.
text: For $J(u)=\int_0^1 u(x)^3\,dx$, compute $\langle J'(u),h\rangle / \int_0^1 h\,dx$ when $u\equiv 2$ and $h$ is any test function with $\int h = 1$. $\delta J/\delta u = 3u^2$, so at $u=2$ the functional derivative is the constant $12$. Hence $\langle J'(u),h\rangle = \int 12\,h = 12\int h$, and the ratio is $12$.

q: The Euler–Lagrange equation for the brachistochrone Lagrangian $L = \sqrt{1+(y')^2}/\sqrt{2gy}$ has which conserved quantity?
text: The Euler–Lagrange equation for the brachistochrone Lagrangian $L = \sqrt{1+(y')^2}/\sqrt{2gy}$ has which conserved quantity? Because $L$ does not depend on $x$ explicitly, the Beltrami identity $L - y'\partial_{y'}L = C$ (a first integral) holds. Solving it for the brachistochrone gives the cycloid $x = r(\theta-\sin\theta)$, $y=r(1-\cos\theta)$ — Bernoulli's celebrated 1697 answer.

q: For $J(u)=\int_a^b L(x,u,u')\,dx$ with $u(a),u(b)$ fixed, the Euler–Lagrange equation is
text: For $J(u)=\int_a^b L(x,u,u')\,dx$ with $u(a),u(b)$ fixed, the Euler–Lagrange equation is Setting $\frac{d}{d\varepsilon}J(u+\varepsilon h)|_{\varepsilon=0}=0$ for every $h$ vanishing at the endpoints, integrating $\partial_{u'}L\cdot h'$ by parts (boundary terms drop because $h(a)=h(b)=0$), and applying the fundamental lemma of the calculus of variations yields the Euler–Lagrange equation.

q: Minimise $J(u)=\int_0^1 ((u')^2 + u^2)\,dx$ with $u(0)=0,\,u(1)=1$. The Euler–Lagrange equation is $u''=u$. Compute $u(1/2)$ to 3 decimals.
text: Minimise $J(u)=\int_0^1 ((u')^2 + u^2)\,dx$ with $u(0)=0,\,u(1)=1$. The Euler–Lagrange equation is $u''=u$. Compute $u(1/2)$ to 3 decimals. $u''=u$ with $u(0)=0$, $u(1)=1$ has solution $u(x)=\sinh(x)/\sinh(1)$. Then $u(1/2)=\sinh(0.5)/\sinh(1) \approx 0.5211/1.1752 \approx 0.443$. Strictly less than the linear interpolant $0.5$ — the quadratic penalty $\int u^2$ pulls the curve down toward zero.

q: The direct method requires three ingredients on the energy $J\colon X\to\mathbb{R}$. Which is NOT one of them?
text: The direct method requires three ingredients on the energy $J\colon X\to\mathbb{R}$. Which is NOT one of them? Strict convexity gives uniqueness, but Tonelli's existence proof needs only coercivity (to bound a minimising sequence), reflexivity (Banach–Alaoglu gives a weakly convergent subsequence), and weak lower-semicontinuity (so the weak limit attains the infimum).

q: Why does coercivity fail for $J(u)=\int_0^1 (u'(x))^2\,dx$ on the full $H^1(0,1)$ without boundary conditions?
text: Why does coercivity fail for $J(u)=\int_0^1 (u'(x))^2\,dx$ on the full $H^1(0,1)$ without boundary conditions? Adding an arbitrary constant doesn't change $u'$, so $J$ is invariant under $u\mapsto u+c$. The norm $\|u\|_{H^1}^2 = \|u\|_{L^2}^2 + \|u'\|_{L^2}^2$ blows up while $J$ stays put — hence not coercive. Imposing $u(0)=0$ (the Poincaré inequality) restores coercivity.

q: Which functional FAILS to be weakly lower-semicontinuous on $H^1(0,1)$?
text: Which functional FAILS to be weakly lower-semicontinuous on $H^1(0,1)$? Convex continuous functionals on a reflexive space are automatically weakly lower-semicontinuous. The first two are convex; the fourth is convex (concave-up integrand in $u'$). The third is concave in $u'$, hence its negative is convex — so $-\int(u')^2$ is weakly upper-semicontinuous, the wrong direction.

q: The mountain-pass geometry requires that $J$ has a local min at $0$, AND
text: The mountain-pass geometry requires that $J$ has a local min at $0$, AND The picture: $0$ is a valley, $u_*$ is a far point at or below the valley floor. Any path from $0$ to $u_*$ must climb over a ridge; the lowest such ridge-height is the mountain-pass critical value. Without the second valley there's no mountain to cross.

q: The Palais–Smale compactness condition $(PS)$ says that every sequence with $J(u_k)$ bounded and $J'(u_k)\to 0$
text: The Palais–Smale compactness condition $(PS)$ says that every sequence with $J(u_k)$ bounded and $J'(u_k)\to 0$ (PS) demands strong convergence of a subsequence — this is what lets you pass from approximate critical points to genuine ones. On bounded domains with subcritical growth (Sobolev embedding compact) this is standard; at the critical Sobolev exponent, $(PS)$ can fail and concentration-compactness must replace it.

q: The mountain-pass theorem produces a critical point at the saddle-height. This critical point
text: The mountain-pass theorem produces a critical point at the saddle-height. This critical point By construction the critical value $c = \inf_\gamma\max_t J(\gamma(t))$ exceeds $J(0)$ but is bounded above on connecting paths — neither a global minimum nor a maximum. The Morse-theoretic interpretation: the topology of sublevel sets changes at $c$, forcing a critical point of index at least $1$.

q: The classical planar isoperimetric inequality reads
text: The classical planar isoperimetric inequality reads Among simple closed curves in the plane bounding area $A$ with perimeter $L$, $4\pi A \le L^2$ with equality iff the curve is a circle. Equivalently, fixing $L$ maximises $A$ at $L^2/(4\pi)$ — the disk — and fixing $A$ minimises $L$ at $2\sqrt{\pi A}$.

q: A region in the plane has perimeter $L=10$. What is the maximum possible area?
text: A region in the plane has perimeter $L=10$. What is the maximum possible area? By isoperimetry the disk maximises: $A_{\max} = L^2/(4\pi) = 100/(4\pi) \approx 7.958$. The maximiser is realised by the disk of circumference $10$, radius $r=10/(2\pi)\approx 1.59$.

q: Why is the Euler–Lagrange equation for the isoperimetric problem (maximise area at fixed perimeter) $\kappa = \text{const}$?
text: Why is the Euler–Lagrange equation for the isoperimetric problem (maximise area at fixed perimeter) $\kappa = \text{const}$? Constrained variation: at a critical point of $A$ subject to fixed $L$, $\delta A = \lambda\,\delta L$. The first variation of area is $\int h\,ds$ along normal perturbation $h$; the first variation of length is $\int \kappa\, h\,ds$. Equality for all $h$ forces $\kappa\equiv\lambda$ — constant curvature, hence a circle.

q: A geodesic on a Riemannian manifold satisfies $\nabla_{\dot\gamma}\dot\gamma=0$. This is the Euler–Lagrange equation for which functional?
text: A geodesic on a Riemannian manifold satisfies $\nabla_{\dot\gamma}\dot\gamma=0$. This is the Euler–Lagrange equation for which functional? Critical points of the energy functional (with parametrisation fixed) are exactly geodesics. The arc-length functional $L(\gamma)=\int|\dot\gamma|\,dt$ has the same minimisers but is reparametrisation-invariant — using $E$ instead pins the parametrisation to constant speed and avoids the degeneracy.

q: Plateau's problem asks for a surface of least area spanning a given closed curve in $\mathbb{R}^3$. The Euler–Lagrange equation is
text:
Plateau's problem asks for a surface of least area spanning a given closed curve in $\mathbb{R}^3$. The Euler–Lagrange equation is Stationarity of area against normal variations forces $H=0$ pointwise — the trace of the second fundamental form vanishes. Locally over a graph $z=f(x,y)$ this is $
abla\cdot(
abla f/\sqrt{1+|
abla f|^2})=0$, the minimal-surface equation. Soap films model this physically.

q: The Yang–Mills functional is $YM(A) = \int_M |F_A|^2\,d\mathrm{vol}$. Its critical points satisfy
text: The Yang–Mills functional is $YM(A) = \int_M |F_A|^2\,d\mathrm{vol}$. Its critical points satisfy Computing the first variation in the connection $A$ gives $\delta YM = 2\int\langle F_A, d_A(\delta A)\rangle = 2\int\langle d_A^* F_A,\delta A\rangle$ (Bianchi gives $d_A F_A = 0$ automatically). Stationarity is $d_A^* F_A = 0$. Self-dual instantons $F_A = *F_A$ are a privileged class of solutions — the bridge to Donaldson theory.

title: Banach contraction principle
blurb: A contraction $f$ with Lipschitz constant $L<1$ on a complete metric space has a unique fixed point, reached by Picard iteration $x_{n+1}=f(x_n)$ with geometric convergence.

title: Brouwer fixed-point theorem
blurb: Every continuous self-map of a closed ball $D^n\to D^n$ has a fixed point; the proof goes through degree theory or the no-retraction lemma.

title: Schauder fixed-point theorem
blurb: A continuous self-map of a non-empty convex compact subset of a Banach space — or a compact operator on a closed bounded convex set — has a fixed point. The infinite-dimensional companion to Brouwer.

title: Lefschetz fixed-point theorem
blurb: If the Lefschetz number $\Lambda(f)=\sum (-1)^k\,\tr(f_*\colon H_k\to H_k)$ is non-zero, then $f$ has a fixed point. Brouwer is the special case of a contractible space.

title: Kakutani fixed-point theorem
blurb: An upper-hemicontinuous set-valued map $\varphi$ from a non-empty compact convex set to its non-empty closed convex subsets has a fixed point $x\in\varphi(x)$. The engine behind Nash's existence proof.

title: Applications across mathematics
blurb: Picard–Lindelöf for ODEs, the implicit function theorem, Nash equilibrium existence, and Markov-chain stationary distributions all reduce to one of these four fixed-point theorems.

q: Which hypothesis is essential for the Banach contraction principle to guarantee a unique fixed point?
text: Which hypothesis is essential for the Banach contraction principle to guarantee a unique fixed point? Completeness is needed for the Cauchy iteration $x_{n+1}=f(x_n)$ to have a limit; the strict contraction $L<1$ forces uniqueness and geometric convergence at rate $L$.

q: Let $f(x)=\tfrac{1}{2}x+1$ on $\mathbb{R}$. What is the unique fixed point?
text: Let $f(x)=\tfrac{1}{2}x+1$ on $\mathbb{R}$. What is the unique fixed point? Solve $x=\tfrac{1}{2}x+1$, giving $x=2$. The Lipschitz constant is $1/2<1$, so Picard iteration from any starting point converges to $2$.

q: Define $f\colon\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$ by $f(x)=x+e^{-x}$. Why does Banach's theorem fail to apply, even though $|f'(x)|<1$ for all $x$?
text: Define $f\colon\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$ by $f(x)=x+e^{-x}$. Why does Banach's theorem fail to apply, even though $|f'(x)|<1$ for all $x$? Banach demands a uniform $L x$ everywhere, so $f$ has no fixed point.

q: Which of the following spaces does NOT satisfy the Brouwer fixed-point property?
text: Which of the following spaces does NOT satisfy the Brouwer fixed-point property? $S^1$ admits the antipodal map $x\mapsto -x$, which is continuous and fixed-point-free. Brouwer requires a contractible (or just convex compact) domain; circles are not contractible.

q: The standard proof of Brouwer reduces to which auxiliary statement?
text: The standard proof of Brouwer reduces to which auxiliary statement? If $f\colon D^n\to D^n$ had no fixed point, the ray from $f(x)$ through $x$ defines a retraction $D^n\to S^{n-1}$, contradicting the no-retraction lemma. The latter is proved by computing $H_{n-1}$ or the degree.

q: For the constant map $f\colon D^2\to D^2$, $f(x)=(0,0)$, how many fixed points does $f$ have?
text: For the constant map $f\colon D^2\to D^2$, $f(x)=(0,0)$, how many fixed points does $f$ have? $x=(0,0)$ is the unique fixed point. Brouwer guarantees at least one; this map shows the count can be exactly one (and the degree of $\mathrm{id}-f$ is $1$).

q: What is the role of compactness in Schauder's theorem that distinguishes it from Brouwer's?
text: What is the role of compactness in Schauder's theorem that distinguishes it from Brouwer's? In $\mathbb{R}^n$ closed-bounded equals compact, so Brouwer needs no extra hypothesis. In a Banach space the unit ball is non-compact (Riesz's lemma), so Schauder demands the convex set be compact, or that $T$ be a compact operator on a closed bounded convex set.

q: Schauder is typically applied to PDEs and integral equations because:
text: Schauder is typically applied to PDEs and integral equations because: Compact operators arise everywhere in analysis — Volterra, Fredholm, Green's functions. Arzelà–Ascoli guarantees the image of a bounded set is precompact, so $T$ maps a closed bounded convex set into a compact convex set.

q: Why is convexity of the domain essential in Schauder's theorem?
text: Why is convexity of the domain essential in Schauder's theorem? Schauder's proof uses finite $\varepsilon$-nets and Brouwer on their convex hulls. Without convexity the convex-hull retraction step fails, and indeed an annulus admits a fixed-point-free rotation.

q: For the identity map $\mathrm{id}\colon T^2\to T^2$ on the 2-torus, the homology Betti numbers are $b_0=1,\,b_1=2,\,b_2=1$. Compute the Lefschetz number $\Lambda(\mathrm{id})$.
text: For the identity map $\mathrm{id}\colon T^2\to T^2$ on the 2-torus, the homology Betti numbers are $b_0=1,\,b_1=2,\,b_2=1$. Compute the Lefschetz number $\Lambda(\mathrm{id})$. $\Lambda(\mathrm{id})=\sum(-1)^k b_k=1-2+1=0$, the Euler characteristic of the torus. Lefschetz gives no obstruction: indeed a non-trivial translation has no fixed points.

q: Which Brouwer-type theorem does Lefschetz recover when applied to a contractible space?
text: Which Brouwer-type theorem does Lefschetz recover when applied to a contractible space? On a contractible space all higher homology vanishes and $f_*\colon H_0\to H_0$ is the identity, so $\Lambda(f)=1$. Non-zero Lefschetz number forces a fixed point — exactly Brouwer.

q: What does $\Lambda(f)\ne 0$ tell you, and what does $\Lambda(f)=0$ tell you?
text: What does $\Lambda(f)\ne 0$ tell you, and what does $\Lambda(f)=0$ tell you? Lefschetz is one-directional: non-zero trace forces a fixed point. A zero Lefschetz number is silent — a torus rotation has $\Lambda=0$ and no fixed points; the identity also has $\Lambda=\chi$, often zero, yet fixes everything.

q: What does Kakutani's theorem add to Brouwer's?
text: What does Kakutani's theorem add to Brouwer's? Kakutani upgrades single-valued continuous maps to upper-hemicontinuous correspondences with non-empty compact convex images. The fixed-point condition becomes membership: $x\in\varphi(x)$.

q: Why is Kakutani — not Brouwer — the right tool for Nash equilibrium?
text: Why is Kakutani — not Brouwer — the right tool for Nash equilibrium? Each player's best-response set $B_i(\sigma_{-i})$ is a non-empty compact convex subset of their mixed-strategy simplex, but generally not a singleton. Kakutani applied to $\sigma\mapsto\prod_i B_i(\sigma_{-i})$ produces a Nash equilibrium.

q: Which property must $\varphi$ satisfy beyond non-empty closed convex values?
text: Which property must $\varphi$ satisfy beyond non-empty closed convex values? Upper hemicontinuity — equivalently, closed graph when the codomain is compact — is the analogue of continuity for set-valued maps and is exactly the regularity Kakutani requires.

q: Picard–Lindelöf existence and uniqueness for $\dot{x}=f(t,x)$ rests on which fixed-point theorem?
text: Picard–Lindelöf existence and uniqueness for $\dot{x}=f(t,x)$ rests on which fixed-point theorem? The integral operator $T(\varphi)(t)=x_0+\int_{t_0}^t f(s,\varphi(s))\,ds$ is a contraction on a small enough interval when $f$ is Lipschitz in $x$, giving the unique solution as its fixed point.

q: A finite-state Markov chain with stochastic matrix $P$ has a stationary distribution because:
text: A finite-state Markov chain with stochastic matrix $P$ has a stationary distribution because: Brouwer applied to the simplex $\Delta=\{\pi\ge 0:\sum\pi_i=1\}$ under $\pi\mapsto\pi P$ produces a stationary distribution — no irreducibility or symmetry needed.

q: The implicit function theorem can be derived from which fixed-point principle?
text: The implicit function theorem can be derived from which fixed-point principle? Linearizing $F(x,y)=0$ at the base point and applying Banach contraction to a Newton-iteration operator on a small ball yields the implicit function $y=g(x)$ with the desired smoothness.

title: Symplectic form
blurb: A symplectic form on $M^{2n}$ is a closed ($d\omega=0$) non-degenerate 2-form $\omega$. Non-degeneracy forces even dimension; the top power $\omega^n$ is a nowhere-vanishing volume form, so symplectic manifolds are canonically oriented. The pair $(M,\omega)$ is the geometric stage on which Hamiltonian mechanics lives.

title: Darboux's theorem
blurb: Around any point of $(M^{2n},\omega)$ there is a chart $(p_1,\dots,p_n,q_1,\dots,q_n)$ in which $\omega = \sum_i dp_i\wedge dq_i$. So symplectic manifolds have no local invariants — every Darboux chart looks like every other. All the geometry is global.

title: Hamiltonian vector fields and flows
blurb: A smooth $H\colon M\to\mathbb{R}$ defines its Hamiltonian vector field $X_H$ by $\iota_{X_H}\omega = dH$ (non-degeneracy makes this unique). The flow of $X_H$ preserves $\omega$ ($\mathcal{L}_{X_H}\omega = 0$) and conserves $H$ along trajectories — energy conservation is geometry, not a separate axiom.

title: Poisson bracket
blurb: The Poisson bracket $\{f,g\} = \omega(X_f,X_g)$ makes $C^\infty(M)$ into a Lie algebra, and the assignment $f\mapsto X_f$ is a Lie-algebra homomorphism into vector fields. Quantisation replaces $\{f,g\}$ by $[\hat A,\hat B]/(i\hbar)$ — the bracket is the classical shadow of the commutator.

title: Lagrangian submanifolds
blurb: A submanifold $L\subset M^{2n}$ is Lagrangian when $\dim L = n$ and $\omega|_L=0$. The graph of a closed 1-form on $N$ sits in $T^*N$ as a Lagrangian; canonical relations are Lagrangians in $(M_1\times M_2,\omega_1\ominus\omega_2)$, and generating functions are how symplectomorphisms package as Lagrangian graphs. "Everything is Lagrangian" (Weinstein).

title: Floer homology — a preview
blurb: Floer's idea: do Morse theory on the symplectic action functional defined on the free loop space (Hamiltonian Floer) or on a path space between two Lagrangians (Lagrangian Floer). Critical points are 1-periodic Hamiltonian orbits, or intersections of two Lagrangians; gradient-flow lines are pseudo-holomorphic cylinders/strips. The resulting $HF_*$ proved the Arnold conjecture.

q: A symplectic form $\omega$ on $M$ is a 2-form satisfying which two conditions?
text: A symplectic form $\omega$ on $M$ is a 2-form satisfying which two conditions? By definition $\omega$ is symplectic when $d\omega=0$ (closed) and $\omega_p$ is non-degenerate at every $p$ (the induced map $T_pM\to T_p^*M$, $v\mapsto \iota_v\omega$, is an isomorphism). Positive definiteness is the wrong condition — $\omega$ is alternating, not symmetric. $d\omega=0$ plus invertibility of $\omega_p\colon T_pM\to T_p^*M$.

q: Why must a symplectic manifold have even dimension?
text: Why must a symplectic manifold have even dimension? An antisymmetric $d\times d$ matrix $A$ satisfies $\det A = (-1)^d\det A$, so $\det A = 0$ when $d$ is odd. Non-degeneracy of $\omega_p$ (an antisymmetric bilinear form on $T_pM$) is impossible in odd dimension. Think about the determinant of an antisymmetric matrix in odd size.

q: On a symplectic $2n$-manifold, the top exterior power $\omega^n = \omega\wedge\cdots\wedge\omega$ is...
text: On a symplectic $2n$-manifold, the top exterior power $\omega^n = \omega\wedge\cdots\wedge\omega$ is... Non-degeneracy of $\omega$ at $p$ is equivalent to $\omega_p^n\ne 0\in\Lambda^{2n}T_p^*M$. So $\omega^n$ is a nowhere-vanishing top form — a volume form — and it gives $M$ a canonical orientation. (When $M$ is closed, $\omega$ cannot itself be exact: if $\omega=d\alpha$ then $\omega^n=d(\alpha\wedge\omega^{n-1})$ would integrate to $0$.) Non-degeneracy of $\omega$ is equivalent to $\omega^n\ne 0$ pointwise.

q: Darboux's theorem states that around every point of $(M^{2n},\omega)$ there is a chart in which $\omega$ equals which canonical form?
text: Darboux's theorem states that around every point of $(M^{2n},\omega)$ there is a chart in which $\omega$ equals which canonical form? Darboux: there exist local coordinates $(p_1,\dots,p_n,q_1,\dots,q_n)$ in which $\omega = \sum_{i=1}^n dp_i\wedge dq_i$. The form $\sum p_i\,dq_i$ is the Liouville/tautological 1-form whose exterior derivative is the canonical $\omega$ on $T^*N$. Pairs of coordinates wedged together.

q: What is the most striking pedagogical consequence of Darboux's theorem?
text: What is the most striking pedagogical consequence of Darboux's theorem? Darboux says there is no symplectic analogue of curvature: locally every $(M^{2n},\omega)$ looks like $(\mathbb{R}^{2n},\omega_0)$. All symplectic invariants are global. Dimension alone does not classify globally — Gromov capacities, symplectic homology, and Floer-theoretic invariants distinguish symplectic manifolds with the same dimension. Compare with the Riemannian case (curvature) or the Kähler case (complex structure).

q: Which of the following is NOT a corollary of Darboux's theorem?
text: Which of the following is NOT a corollary of Darboux's theorem? Darboux gives local equivalence only. Globally, classifying closed symplectic manifolds is a hard, open problem (think Kähler vs non-Kähler, Gromov non-squeezing, exotic symplectic structures on $\mathbb{R}^{2n}$). Tori are very far from being the only example. Darboux is local; classification of closed symplectic manifolds is wide open.

q: Given $H\colon M\to\mathbb{R}$, the Hamiltonian vector field $X_H$ is defined by which equation?
text: Given $H\colon M\to\mathbb{R}$, the Hamiltonian vector field $X_H$ is defined by which equation? The defining identity is $\iota_{X_H}\omega = dH$. Non-degeneracy of $\omega$ means the bundle map $TM\to T^*M$, $v\mapsto\iota_v\omega$, is an isomorphism, so $dH$ has a unique pre-image $X_H$. The gradient $\nabla H$ requires a metric, not a symplectic form. Use non-degeneracy of $\omega$ to invert the differential of $H$.

q: Why is the energy $H$ conserved along the flow of $X_H$?
text: Why is the energy $H$ conserved along the flow of $X_H$? $\dot H = X_H(H) = dH(X_H) = (\iota_{X_H}\omega)(X_H) = \omega(X_H,X_H) = 0$ since $\omega$ is alternating. Energy conservation is automatic — built into the geometry, not an extra hypothesis. Use the defining identity $\iota_{X_H}\omega = dH$ and that $\omega$ is alternating.

q: The flow of $X_H$ preserves the symplectic form $\omega$ because Cartan's magic formula gives $\mathcal{L}_{X_H}\omega = $...
text: The flow of $X_H$ preserves the symplectic form $\omega$ because Cartan's magic formula gives $\mathcal{L}_{X_H}\omega = $... Cartan's formula gives $\mathcal{L}_{X_H}\omega = d(\iota_{X_H}\omega) + \iota_{X_H}d\omega = d(dH) + \iota_{X_H}\cdot 0 = 0+0 = 0$. So the flow $\Phi_t^{X_H}$ is a symplectomorphism for every $t$ — Liouville's theorem in geometric form. Cartan: $\mathcal{L}_X = d\iota_X + \iota_X d$. Use $d\omega=0$.

q: On a symplectic manifold the Poisson bracket of $f,g\in C^\infty(M)$ is defined as...
text: On a symplectic manifold the Poisson bracket of $f,g\in C^\infty(M)$ is defined as... By definition $\{f,g\} = \omega(X_f,X_g) = X_f(g) = -X_g(f)$. The first equality is the definition; the others use $\iota_{X_f}\omega = df$. The bracket of ordinary functions is identically zero — they need to come from $\omega$ to interact. Plug the two Hamiltonian vector fields into $\omega$.

q: Which structural property does $(C^\infty(M),\{\cdot,\cdot\})$ NOT satisfy automatically?
text: Which structural property does $(C^\infty(M),\{\cdot,\cdot\})$ NOT satisfy automatically? Antisymmetry, Jacobi, and Leibniz hold (the first two make it a Lie algebra; Leibniz makes it a Poisson algebra). Commutativity fails — the whole point of the bracket is to be antisymmetric, the opposite of commutative. It's a Lie bracket, not a commutative product.

q: Quantisation replaces the Poisson bracket of classical observables with which quantum object?
text: Quantisation replaces the Poisson bracket of classical observables with which quantum object? The Dirac correspondence: $\{f,g\}\leadsto [\hat A,\hat B]/(i\hbar)$ where $\hat A,\hat B$ are the operators quantising $f,g$. To leading order in $\hbar$ the quantum commutator reproduces the classical bracket — that is what makes geometric quantisation a quantisation. Dirac's correspondence principle.

q: A submanifold $L$ of a symplectic $(M^{2n},\omega)$ is Lagrangian when which two conditions hold?
text: A submanifold $L$ of a symplectic $(M^{2n},\omega)$ is Lagrangian when which two conditions hold? A Lagrangian is a maximal isotropic submanifold: $\omega$ vanishes on $TL\times TL$ ("isotropic") and $\dim L = \frac12\dim M = n$ ("maximal"). Smaller-than-$n$ isotropics exist but are not Lagrangian. Half the dimension of $M$, and $\omega$ vanishes on tangent vectors of $L$.

q: On the cotangent bundle $T^*N$ with its canonical $\omega = -d\lambda$, the zero section $\{(x,0):x\in N\}$ is...
text: On the cotangent bundle $T^*N$ with its canonical $\omega = -d\lambda$, the zero section $\{(x,0):x\in N\}$ is... The zero section has dimension $\dim N = n = \frac12\dim T^*N$, and its tangent vectors all have momentum coordinate zero, so $\omega = \sum dp_i\wedge dq_i$ vanishes on them. More generally the graph of any closed 1-form $\alpha\in\Omega^1(N)$ is a Lagrangian in $T^*N$. Compute $\dim$ and check $\omega$ on tangent vectors of the zero section.

q: A canonical relation between $(M_1,\omega_1)$ and $(M_2,\omega_2)$ is a Lagrangian submanifold of which symplectic manifold?
text: A canonical relation between $(M_1,\omega_1)$ and $(M_2,\omega_2)$ is a Lagrangian submanifold of which symplectic manifold? The product $(M_1\times M_2,\omega_1\ominus\omega_2 := \pi_1^*\omega_1 - \pi_2^*\omega_2)$ is the right symplectic structure: the graph of a symplectomorphism $f\colon M_1\to M_2$ is then a Lagrangian. Canonical relations compose Lagrangians and turn $\mathbf{Symp}$ into a symplectic category. Graphs of symplectomorphisms are the prototype — try $\omega_1 - f^*\omega_2$.

q: Hamiltonian Floer homology applies Morse-theoretic ideas to which functional, on which space?
text: Hamiltonian Floer homology applies Morse-theoretic ideas to which functional, on which space? Floer studied $\mathcal{A}_H(\gamma) = \int_{D^2}\bar u^*\omega - \int_0^1 H(t,\gamma(t))\,dt$ on the free loop space $\mathcal{L}M$. Critical points of $\mathcal{A}_H$ are exactly the 1-periodic orbits of $X_{H_t}$, recovering Hamiltonian dynamics as a variational problem. Hamiltonian flow has periodic orbits; those should be the critical points.

q: In Lagrangian Floer homology of two Lagrangians $L_0,L_1\subset M$, the chain complex is generated by...
text: In Lagrangian Floer homology of two Lagrangians $L_0,L_1\subset M$, the chain complex is generated by... The action functional on paths $\gamma\colon [0,1]\to M$ with $\gamma(0)\in L_0,\gamma(1)\in L_1$ has critical points exactly at constant paths sitting at intersection points of $L_0$ and $L_1$. Generically the intersection is transverse and finite, giving a finite-rank chain complex. Where does the action functional on paths from $L_0$ to $L_1$ have its critical points?

q: What does Hamiltonian Floer homology of a closed symplectic manifold $M$ compute (Arnold conjecture)?
text: What does Hamiltonian Floer homology of a closed symplectic manifold $M$ compute (Arnold conjecture)? Floer (and later Fukaya–Ono, Liu–Tian) proved $HF_*(M;H) \cong H_*(M)$ as an invariant. Comparing chain ranks gives the Arnold lower bound: the number of 1-periodic orbits of any non-degenerate $H$ is at least $\sum_k b_k(M)$ — the Morse-inequality analogue, in infinite dimensions. The Arnold conjecture lower-bounds periodic orbit counts by Betti numbers.

title: Hopf algebras
blurb: A Hopf algebra packages multiplication $m$, unit $\eta$, comultiplication $\Delta$, counit $\varepsilon$, and antipode $S$ with compatibility axioms; the group ring $\mathbb{C}[G]$ with $\Delta(g)=g\otimes g$ and $S(g)=g^{-1}$ is the prototypical example, while $U(\mathfrak g)$ is the cocommutative Lie counterpart.

title: Drinfeld–Jimbo $U_q(\mathfrak{sl}_2)$
blurb: Generators $E,F,K^{\pm 1}$ with $KEK^{-1}=q^2 E$, $KFK^{-1}=q^{-2} F$, and $[E,F]=(K-K^{-1})/(q-q^{-1})$ define the quantized enveloping algebra; the parameter $q\to 1$ recovers $U(\mathfrak{sl}_2)$ as a deformation.

title: Universal $R$-matrix and Yang–Baxter
blurb: $U_q(\mathfrak{g})$ is quasitriangular: there is $R\in U_q(\mathfrak{g})\otimes U_q(\mathfrak{g})$ intertwining $\Delta$ with its opposite and satisfying the Yang–Baxter equation $R_{12}R_{13}R_{23}=R_{23}R_{13}R_{12}$ — the algebraic engine behind braid statistics.

title: Representations and crystal bases
blurb: For $q$ generic, finite-dimensional $U_q(\mathfrak g)$-modules of type 1 are parametrized by dominant weights exactly as in the classical case; Lusztig's canonical basis and Kashiwara's crystal basis specialize at $q=0$ to a coloured graph encoding tensor decompositions combinatorially.

title: Reshetikhin–Turaev knot invariants
blurb: Assigning the $R$-matrix to each crossing turns a labelled tangle diagram into a $U_q(\mathfrak{g})$-equivariant operator; the resulting trace is a polynomial knot invariant — Jones from $U_q(\mathfrak{sl}_2)$, HOMFLY from $U_q(\mathfrak{sl}_n)$, Kauffman from $U_q(\mathfrak{so}_n)$.

title: Affine, KZ, and categorification
blurb: Affine quantum groups $U_q(\widehat{\mathfrak g})$ govern integrable lattice models and the Knizhnik–Zamolodchikov equations whose monodromy is the $R$-matrix (Drinfeld–Kohno); quantum geometric Langlands and Khovanov–Lauda–Rouquier categorification lift these structures to derived and 2-categorical settings.

q: In the group Hopf algebra $\mathbb{C}[G]$, what is the comultiplication on a basis element $g\in G$?
text: In the group Hopf algebra $\mathbb{C}[G]$, what is the comultiplication on a basis element $g\in G$? Group elements are the grouplike elements: $\Delta(g)=g\otimes g$ and $\varepsilon(g)=1$. The primitive form $x\otimes 1+1\otimes x$ is what Lie algebra elements satisfy inside $U(\mathfrak g)$.

q: Which axiom forces the antipode $S$ to be unique once it exists?
text: Which axiom forces the antipode $S$ to be unique once it exists? $S$ is defined by $m\circ(S\otimes\mathrm{id})\circ\Delta = m\circ(\mathrm{id}\otimes S)\circ\Delta = \eta\circ\varepsilon$ — a convolution inverse, hence unique. $S$ is an antihomomorphism, and $S^2=\mathrm{id}$ only in the cocommutative case.

q: Why is $U(\mathfrak g)$ a Hopf algebra?
text: Why is $U(\mathfrak g)$ a Hopf algebra? The primitive coproduct on Lie generators extends by the universal property of $U(\mathfrak g)$. Cocommutativity reflects that $\mathfrak g$ has no braiding — quantization breaks exactly that symmetry.

q: In $U_q(\mathfrak{sl}_2)$, what does the relation $[E,F]=(K-K^{-1})/(q-q^{-1})$ become as $q\to 1$ with $K=q^H$?
text: In $U_q(\mathfrak{sl}_2)$, what does the relation $[E,F]=(K-K^{-1})/(q-q^{-1})$ become as $q\to 1$ with $K=q^H$? Expanding $K=q^H = 1 + (\log q) H + \cdots$ gives $(K-K^{-1})/(q-q^{-1}) \to H$ in the limit, recovering the classical $\mathfrak{sl}_2$ relation.

q: Which relation is the quantum analogue of $[H,E]=2E$?
text: Which relation is the quantum analogue of $[H,E]=2E$? Conjugation by the grouplike $K=q^H$ replaces the additive eigenvalue $2$ with the multiplicative $q^2$. Differentiating at $q=1$ recovers the classical bracket.

q: What is the comultiplication $\Delta(E)$ in $U_q(\mathfrak{sl}_2)$?
text: What is the comultiplication $\Delta(E)$ in $U_q(\mathfrak{sl}_2)$? The Drinfeld–Jimbo coproduct twists primitivity by the Cartan element: $\Delta(E)=E\otimes 1+K\otimes E$ and $\Delta(F)=F\otimes K^{-1}+1\otimes F$. This is what kills cocommutativity and produces a braiding.

q: The Yang–Baxter equation $R_{12}R_{13}R_{23}=R_{23}R_{13}R_{12}$ corresponds geometrically to which braid identity?
text: The Yang–Baxter equation $R_{12}R_{13}R_{23}=R_{23}R_{13}R_{12}$ corresponds geometrically to which braid identity? Reidemeister III / the third braid relation is exactly Yang–Baxter on three strands. The $R$-matrix gives a representation of the braid group $B_n$ on $V^{\otimes n}$.

q: What property of $U_q(\mathfrak g)$ does the universal $R$-matrix witness?
text: What property of $U_q(\mathfrak g)$ does the universal $R$-matrix witness? Drinfeld's definition: a quasitriangular Hopf algebra carries an invertible $R$ intertwining $\Delta$ and the flipped $\Delta^{op}$. Cocommutative means $\Delta=\Delta^{op}$, so $R=1\otimes 1$ and the braiding is trivial.

q: On a tensor product of two $U_q(\mathfrak g)$-modules, the operator $\tau\circ R$ (flip composed with $R$) is a/an…
text: On a tensor product of two $U_q(\mathfrak g)$-modules, the operator $\tau\circ R$ (flip composed with $R$) is a/an… $c_{V,W}=\tau\circ R_{V,W}$ is exactly the braiding making $\mathrm{Rep}(U_q(\mathfrak g))$ a braided monoidal category. Generic $q$ gives a non-symmetric braiding — the swap is no longer an involution.

q: For generic $q$, the irreducible type-1 finite-dimensional $U_q(\mathfrak{sl}_2)$-modules are parametrized by…
text: For generic $q$, the irreducible type-1 finite-dimensional $U_q(\mathfrak{sl}_2)$-modules are parametrized by… At generic $q$ the representation theory is a deformation of the classical one — same labels, same dimensions, $K$ acts on the highest-weight vector by $q^n$. Roots of unity bring genuinely new phenomena (small quantum groups, tilting modules).

q: What does Kashiwara's crystal basis recover when one specializes at $q=0$?
text: What does Kashiwara's crystal basis recover when one specializes at $q=0$? Crystals are coloured graphs where vertices are basis elements and edges record the $q\to 0$ shadow of $E_i,F_i$. The tensor product rule becomes a simple combinatorial recipe — Littelmann paths, Young tableaux, etc.

q: Lusztig's canonical basis is distinguished from an arbitrary highest-weight basis by which property?
text: Lusztig's canonical basis is distinguished from an arbitrary highest-weight basis by which property? Bar-invariance plus an integral lattice condition pin the canonical basis uniquely. The positivity of structure constants is a deep theorem (and the seed of categorification — these integers count dimensions of $\mathrm{Hom}$ spaces in a categorical lift).

q: Reshetikhin–Turaev assigns to a positive crossing of two strands labelled by $V$ which operator?
text: Reshetikhin–Turaev assigns to a positive crossing of two strands labelled by $V$ which operator? Each crossing becomes the braiding $c_{V,V}=\tau R$; the inverse crossing gets $c^{-1}$. Closing the braid and taking a quantum trace gives the knot invariant.

q: Which knot polynomial arises from $U_q(\mathfrak{sl}_2)$ acting on its 2-dimensional standard representation?
text: Which knot polynomial arises from $U_q(\mathfrak{sl}_2)$ acting on its 2-dimensional standard representation? Jones falls out of $U_q(\mathfrak{sl}_2)$ on $V_1$. HOMFLY comes from $U_q(\mathfrak{sl}_n)$ for variable $n$, and Kauffman / two-variable Kauffman from $U_q(\mathfrak{so}_n)$ or $U_q(\mathfrak{sp}_{2n})$.

q: Why is the assignment 'crossing $\mapsto R$' well-defined as a knot invariant?
text: Why is the assignment 'crossing $\mapsto R$' well-defined as a knot invariant? Yang–Baxter is exactly Reidemeister III. The Markov-style framing fix and the rigid duality of $U_q(\mathfrak g)$-mod handle Reidemeister II and (up to a framing factor) Reidemeister I.

q: The Drinfeld–Kohno theorem identifies the monodromy of the Knizhnik–Zamolodchikov equation with…
text: The Drinfeld–Kohno theorem identifies the monodromy of the Knizhnik–Zamolodchikov equation with… KZ is a flat connection on configuration space valued in $\mathfrak g\otimes\mathfrak g$; transporting around braids gives exactly the $U_q(\mathfrak g)$ braiding, with $q=e^{i\pi\hbar}$ for the deformation parameter $\hbar$.

q: Affine quantum groups $U_q(\widehat{\mathfrak g})$ govern which family of physical models?
text: Affine quantum groups $U_q(\widehat{\mathfrak g})$ govern which family of physical models? The $R$-matrix of $U_q(\widehat{\mathfrak{sl}_2})$ on a tensor power of the standard rep is the Boltzmann weight of the six-vertex model; commuting transfer matrices come from the universal $R$ in the second tensor slot.

q: What does Khovanov–Lauda–Rouquier (KLR) categorification replace?
text: What does Khovanov–Lauda–Rouquier (KLR) categorification replace? KLR (and Khovanov for $\mathfrak{sl}_2$) builds a graded 2-category whose Grothendieck group is $U_q(\mathfrak g)$; Khovanov homology categorifies the Jones polynomial, with Euler characteristic recovering it.

title: Quaternions $\mathbb{H}$
blurb: Hamilton's $\mathbb{H}=\mathbb{R}\langle 1,i,j,k\rangle$ is the 4-dimensional real algebra with $i^2=j^2=k^2=ijk=-1$. It is associative but non-commutative, with conjugate $\overline{q}=a-bi-cj-dk$, norm $|q|^2=q\overline{q}=a^2+b^2+c^2+d^2$, and inverse $q^{-1}=\overline{q}/|q|^2$ for $q\neq 0$ — making $\mathbb{H}\cong\mathbb{R}^4$ a (skew) division algebra.

title: Unit quaternions and $\mathrm{SU}(2)\to\mathrm{SO}(3)$
blurb: The unit quaternions $\{q:|q|=1\}\cong S^3$ form a Lie group $\mathrm{Sp}(1)\cong\mathrm{SU}(2)$. They act on the imaginary part $\mathrm{Im}\,\mathbb{H}\cong\mathbb{R}^3$ by conjugation $v\mapsto qvq^{-1}$, giving the double cover $\mathrm{SU}(2)\to\mathrm{SO}(3)$ (kernel $\{\pm 1\}$); at the Lie-algebra level $\mathrm{Im}\,\mathbb{H}\cong\mathfrak{so}(3)$.

title: The Cayley–Dickson tower
blurb: The Cayley–Dickson construction doubles a $*$-algebra $A$ to $A\oplus A$ with multiplication $(a,b)(c,d)=(ac-d^*b,\,da+bc^*)$ and conjugate $(a,b)^*=(a^*,-b)$. Iterating from $\mathbb{R}$ produces $\mathbb{R}\subset\mathbb{C}\subset\mathbb{H}\subset\mathbb{O}\subset\mathbb{S}$; each step doubles dimension and loses one structure (real $\to$ complex loses self-conjugation, $\mathbb{C}\to\mathbb{H}$ loses commutativity, $\mathbb{H}\to\mathbb{O}$ loses associativity, $\mathbb{O}\to\mathbb{S}$ loses the division-algebra property).

title: Octonions $\mathbb{O}$
blurb: $\mathbb{O}=\mathbb{R}^8$ with basis $\{1,e_1,\dots,e_7\}$ is the 8-dimensional real division algebra obtained by doubling $\mathbb{H}$. Multiplication is non-associative — $(e_1 e_2)e_4\neq e_1(e_2 e_4)$ in general — but every two-element subalgebra is associative; this is the $\textbf{alternative}$ law $x(xy)=(xx)y$ and $(yx)x=y(xx)$. Multiplication is encoded by the Fano plane: 7 oriented lines, each a copy of $\mathbb{H}$.

title: Frobenius's theorem
blurb: Frobenius (1877): the only finite-dimensional associative division algebras over $\mathbb{R}$ are $\mathbb{R}$, $\mathbb{C}$, and $\mathbb{H}$. Idea: pick $x\notin\mathbb{R}$; its minimal polynomial is irreducible of degree $\le 2$, so $\mathbb{R}[x]\cong\mathbb{C}$. The orthogonal complement of $\mathbb{R}$ inside the algebra carries a negative-definite form; choosing two anticommuting unit vectors $i,j$ forces $k=ij$ and the relations of $\mathbb{H}$.

title: Hurwitz's theorem
blurb: Hurwitz (1898): the only finite-dimensional real $\textbf{composition}$ algebras — unital algebras with a quadratic norm satisfying $|xy|=|x||y|$ — are $\mathbb{R}$, $\mathbb{C}$, $\mathbb{H}$, and $\mathbb{O}$, with dimensions $1,2,4,8$. The dimension restriction is the same that makes $\mathbb{R}^n$ admit a continuous bilinear product without zero divisors only for $n\in\{1,2,4,8\}$ (Bott–Milnor / Kervaire, via topology).

q: Using $i^2=j^2=k^2=ijk=-1$, what is $ji$?
text: Using $i^2=j^2=k^2=ijk=-1$, what is $ji$? From $ijk=-1$ we get $ij=-k^{-1}=k$ (since $k^{-1}=-k$). Multiplying $ij=k$ on the left by $j^{-1}=-j$ and on the right by $i^{-1}=-i$ gives $ji=-k$. The quaternions are non-commutative: $ij=-ji$. Multiply $ijk=-1$ on the right by $k^{-1}=-k$ to get $ij=k$, then use anticommutativity of $i,j$.

q: What is $|q|^2$ for $q=1+i+2j-2k$?
text: What is $|q|^2$ for $q=1+i+2j-2k$? $|q|^2=q\overline q=a^2+b^2+c^2+d^2=1+1+4+4=10$. Then $q^{-1}=\overline q/|q|^2=(1-i-2j+2k)/10$. Sum the squares of the four real components.

q: Which of the following best describes $\mathbb{H}$ as an algebra over $\mathbb{R}$?
text: Which of the following best describes $\mathbb{H}$ as an algebra over $\mathbb{R}$? $\mathbb{H}\cong\mathbb{R}^4$ is associative (matrix multiplication realises it), non-commutative ($ij=-ji$), and a division algebra (every non-zero $q$ has $q^{-1}=\overline q/|q|^2$). It is the unique such object beyond $\mathbb{R}$ and $\mathbb{C}$ (Frobenius). Count basis elements and check whether $i,j,k$ commute.

q: Which manifold is the group of unit quaternions diffeomorphic to?
text: Which manifold is the group of unit quaternions diffeomorphic to? Unit quaternions are $\{(a,b,c,d):a^2+b^2+c^2+d^2=1\}\subset\mathbb{R}^4$, which is exactly $S^3$. As a Lie group this is $\mathrm{Sp}(1)\cong\mathrm{SU}(2)$. Quotienting by $\{\pm 1\}$ gives $\mathrm{SO}(3)\cong\mathbb{RP}^3$. Solve $|q|^2=1$ in $\mathbb{R}^4$.

q: What is the kernel of the conjugation map $\mathrm{SU}(2)\to\mathrm{SO}(3),\ q\mapsto(v\mapsto qvq^{-1})$ acting on $\mathrm{Im}\,\mathbb{H}\cong\mathbb{R}^3$?
text: What is the kernel of the conjugation map $\mathrm{SU}(2)\to\mathrm{SO}(3),\ q\mapsto(v\mapsto qvq^{-1})$ acting on $\mathrm{Im}\,\mathbb{H}\cong\mathbb{R}^3$? $qvq^{-1}=v$ for every imaginary $v$ forces $q$ to commute with all of $\mathrm{Im}\,\mathbb{H}$, so $q\in\mathbb{R}\cap S^3=\{\pm 1\}$. Thus $\mathrm{SO}(3)\cong\mathrm{SU}(2)/\{\pm 1\}$ — the famous double cover. $q$ must commute with $i,j,k$; the centre of $\mathbb{H}$ is $\mathbb{R}$.

q: If $q=\cos(\theta/2)+\sin(\theta/2)\,\hat n$ with $\hat n\in\mathrm{Im}\,\mathbb{H}$ a unit imaginary, what does the map $v\mapsto qvq^{-1}$ do to $\mathbb{R}^3=\mathrm{Im}\,\mathbb{H}$?
text: If $q=\cos(\theta/2)+\sin(\theta/2)\,\hat n$ with $\hat n\in\mathrm{Im}\,\mathbb{H}$ a unit imaginary, what does the map $v\mapsto qvq^{-1}$ do to $\mathbb{R}^3=\mathrm{Im}\,\mathbb{H}$? Conjugation by a unit quaternion with half-angle $\theta/2$ rotates $\mathrm{Im}\,\mathbb{H}$ by the full angle $\theta$ around the axis $\hat n$. The half-angle is the source of the double cover: $q$ and $-q$ implement the same rotation. Why is the cover 2-to-1? The half-angle in $q$ doubles when you conjugate.

q: Going up the tower $\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{C}\to\mathbb{H}\to\mathbb{O}$, which property is lost at the step $\mathbb{C}\to\mathbb{H}$?
text: Going up the tower $\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{C}\to\mathbb{H}\to\mathbb{O}$, which property is lost at the step $\mathbb{C}\to\mathbb{H}$? At each doubling something is sacrificed. $\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{C}$ loses $x^*=x$. $\mathbb{C}\to\mathbb{H}$ loses commutativity ($ij\neq ji$). $\mathbb{H}\to\mathbb{O}$ loses associativity. $\mathbb{O}\to\mathbb{S}$ (sedenions) loses the no-zero-divisor property. Which of $i,j$ in $\mathbb{H}$ commute? They don't.

q: What is the real dimension of the algebra produced by 5 iterations of Cayley–Dickson starting at $\mathbb{R}$ (so $\mathbb{R},\mathbb{C},\mathbb{H},\mathbb{O},\mathbb{S},\dots$ — the 5th doubling)?
text: What is the real dimension of the algebra produced by 5 iterations of Cayley–Dickson starting at $\mathbb{R}$ (so $\mathbb{R},\mathbb{C},\mathbb{H},\mathbb{O},\mathbb{S},\dots$ — the 5th doubling)? Each step doubles dimension: $1\to 2\to 4\to 8\to 16\to 32$. After $\mathbb{O}$ comes the sedenions $\mathbb{S}$ (dim 16); the 5th doubling gives the 32-dimensional 'trigintaduonions'. From $\mathbb{S}$ on, zero divisors appear. $2^5$.

q: In Cayley–Dickson $(a,b)(c,d)=(ac-d^*b,da+bc^*)$ starting from $A=\mathbb{R}$ (so $a^*=a$), what algebra does the first doubling produce?
text: In Cayley–Dickson $(a,b)(c,d)=(ac-d^*b,da+bc^*)$ starting from $A=\mathbb{R}$ (so $a^*=a$), what algebra does the first doubling produce? Setting $i=(0,1)$, the formula gives $i^2=(0,1)(0,1)=(-1\cdot 1,\ 0)=-1$. The doubling of $\mathbb{R}$ is $\mathbb{C}$, with conjugation $(a,b)^*=(a,-b)$ recovering the usual complex conjugate. Compute $(0,1)(0,1)$.

q: Which identity holds in $\mathbb{O}$ but is strictly weaker than associativity?
text: Which identity holds in $\mathbb{O}$ but is strictly weaker than associativity? $\mathbb{O}$ is alternative: any subalgebra generated by two elements is associative, so $x(xy)=(xx)y$ and similar identities hold even though general triples can fail to associate. Artin's theorem: alternative $\Leftrightarrow$ every two-generated subalgebra is associative. Two-generated subalgebras are still associative.

q: What is $\dim_{\mathbb R}\mathbb{O}$?
text: What is $\dim_{\mathbb R}\mathbb{O}$? $\mathbb{O}$ is the Cayley–Dickson double of $\mathbb{H}$, so $\dim_{\mathbb R}\mathbb{O}=2\cdot 4=8$, with basis $\{1,e_1,\dots,e_7\}$. Double the dimension of $\mathbb{H}$.

q: How many copies of $\mathbb{H}$ sit inside $\mathbb{O}$ as 4-dimensional associative subalgebras containing $1$?
text: How many copies of $\mathbb{H}$ sit inside $\mathbb{O}$ as 4-dimensional associative subalgebras containing $1$? The 7 oriented lines of the Fano plane on $\{e_1,\dots,e_7\}$ each pick out a triple $\{e_a,e_b,e_c\}$ closed under multiplication, generating with $1$ a quaternion subalgebra $\mathbb{H}\subset\mathbb{O}$. So there are exactly 7 such copies of $\mathbb{H}$. Count the lines of the Fano plane.

q: What is the exact statement of Frobenius's theorem (1877)?
text: What is the exact statement of Frobenius's theorem (1877)?
