Frobenius classifies finite-dimensional $\textbf{associative}$ division algebras over $\mathbb{R}$: exactly $\mathbb{R},\mathbb{C},\mathbb{H}$. Dropping associativity admits $\mathbb{O}$ (Hurwitz, choice 3); finite extension fields of $\mathbb{R}$ are $\mathbb{R}$ or $\mathbb{C}$ (choice 4 is true but is the algebraic-closure fact, not Frobenius). Three algebras, all associative.

q: Why does Frobenius's argument force the dimension of an associative real division algebra to be $1$, $2$, or $4$?
text: Why does Frobenius's argument force the dimension of an associative real division algebra to be $1$, $2$, or $4$? Pick $x\notin\mathbb R$; its minimal polynomial is irreducible of degree $\le 2$, so $x^2\in\mathbb R$. The pure-imaginary part of the algebra is Euclidean. Associativity plus unit-norm anticommutativity force exactly $0$, $1$, or $3$ generators ($\mathbb R,\mathbb C,\mathbb H$); a fourth would violate $i(jk)=(ij)k$. After a third generator $i,j,k$, where could a fourth go without breaking associativity?

q: Which of these is $\textbf{not}$ a finite-dimensional associative real division algebra?
text: Which of these is $\textbf{not}$ a finite-dimensional associative real division algebra? $\mathbb{O}$ is a division algebra but is non-associative, so it is excluded by Frobenius (which assumes associativity). It does appear in Hurwitz's broader classification of composition algebras. Which one fails associativity?

q: Hurwitz's theorem (1898) classifies which class of real algebras?
text: Hurwitz's theorem (1898) classifies which class of real algebras? Hurwitz: the only finite-dimensional real composition algebras are $\mathbb{R},\mathbb{C},\mathbb{H},\mathbb{O}$. The $\textbf{norm-multiplicative}$ condition $|xy|=|x||y|$ is the key — it forces dimension $\in\{1,2,4,8\}$. What does $|xy|=|x||y|$ characterise?

q: How many distinct finite-dimensional real $\textbf{composition}$ algebras are there, up to isomorphism (Hurwitz)?
text: How many distinct finite-dimensional real $\textbf{composition}$ algebras are there, up to isomorphism (Hurwitz)? Exactly four: $\mathbb{R},\mathbb{C},\mathbb{H},\mathbb{O}$, of dimensions $1,2,4,8$. The dimension restriction matches the Bott–Milnor/Kervaire result that $\mathbb{R}^n$ admits a continuous bilinear product without zero divisors only for $n\in\{1,2,4,8\}$. Four dimensions: $1,2,4,8$.

q: The classical identity $(a^2+b^2+c^2+d^2)(e^2+f^2+g^2+h^2)=$ (sum of four squares) is an instance of which structure?
text: The classical identity $(a^2+b^2+c^2+d^2)(e^2+f^2+g^2+h^2)=$ (sum of four squares) is an instance of which structure? Euler's four-square identity is exactly $|q|^2|q'|^2=|qq'|^2$ for $q,q'\in\mathbb{H}$ — the multiplicativity of the quaternion norm. The eight-square identity (Degen) similarly comes from $\mathbb{O}$. There is no $16$-square identity, matching the failure of Cayley–Dickson at the sedenion step. Four squares = quaternion norm.

title: Presentations and the word problem
blurb: A presentation $G = \langle S \mid R \rangle$ realizes $G$ as the quotient of the free group $F(S)$ by the normal closure of the relators $R$. Finitely-generated means $|S| < \infty$, finitely-presented adds $|R| < \infty$. Tietze transformations adjust generators and relators without changing $G$, and the word problem — decide whether a word in $S^{\pm 1}$ equals the identity — is undecidable in general (Novikov–Boone) but linear-time for hyperbolic groups.

title: Cayley graphs and the word metric
blurb: Given $(G, S)$ with $S$ a symmetric generating set, the Cayley graph $\mathrm{Cay}(G, S)$ has vertex set $G$ and edges $\{(g, gs) : s \in S\}$. Left multiplication by $G$ acts by graph automorphisms — the graph is vertex-transitive — and the right action of $S$ moves along edges. The word metric $d_S(g, h) = $ length of the shortest word representing $g^{-1}h$ makes $G$ a metric space, well-defined up to bilipschitz when $S$ is changed.

title: Quasi-isometry and large-scale geometry
blurb: A map $f: X \to Y$ between metric spaces is a $(K, C)$-quasi-isometry when $\tfrac{1}{K}d_X(x,x') - C \le d_Y(f(x), f(x')) \le K\, d_X(x,x') + C$ and $f(X)$ is $C$-dense in $Y$. The Schwarz–Milnor lemma says any two finite generating sets of $G$ produce quasi-isometric Cayley graphs, so quasi-isometric invariants — growth, ends, hyperbolicity — are intrinsic to $G$.

title: Growth functions and Gromov's theorem
blurb: The growth function $\beta_S(n) = \#\{g \in G : d_S(e, g) \le n\}$ counts ball volumes in the Cayley graph. Up to quasi-isometry, $\beta$ falls into polynomial, intermediate (Grigorchuk), or exponential growth. Gromov's theorem (1981): a finitely-generated group has polynomial growth iff it is virtually nilpotent — a deep bridge between coarse geometry and group structure.

title: Gromov-hyperbolic groups
blurb: $G$ is $\delta$-hyperbolic when every geodesic triangle in $\mathrm{Cay}(G,S)$ is $\delta$-thin: each side lies in the $\delta$-neighborhood of the union of the other two. Equivalent to negative curvature in the large. Consequences: word and conjugacy problems solvable in linear time, finitely many conjugacy classes of torsion, a well-defined boundary $\partial G$ at infinity, and quasi-isometric rigidity in many cases.

title: Applications and frontiers
blurb: Decision problems (word/conjugacy/isomorphism), Mostow rigidity for lattices in rank-one Lie groups, CAT(0) cube complexes and Agol's resolution of the virtual Haken conjecture, mapping class groups of surfaces, and expanders built from Cayley graphs of $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{F}_p)$ — geometric group theory now sits at the crossroads of topology, combinatorics, and theoretical computer science.

q: What does the presentation $G = \langle S \mid R \rangle$ formally denote?
text: What does the presentation $G = \langle S \mid R \rangle$ formally denote? A presentation realizes $G$ as $F(S) / N$, where $N$ is the smallest normal subgroup of $F(S)$ containing $R$ — this guarantees every word in $R$ becomes trivial in $G$ along with all conjugates.

q: Which group is $\langle a, b \mid aba^{-1}b^{-1} \rangle$?
text: Which group is $\langle a, b \mid aba^{-1}b^{-1} \rangle$? The relator $aba^{-1}b^{-1} = e$ says $ab = ba$, so adding commutativity to the free group on $\{a,b\}$ yields $\mathbb{Z}^2$.

q: What is the word problem for a finitely-presented group?
text: What is the word problem for a finitely-presented group? Equivalently, decide whether a given word equals the identity. Novikov (1955) and Boone (1958) showed this is undecidable in general, but solvable for many large classes (hyperbolic, automatic, residually finite F.P.).

q: In $\mathrm{Cay}(G, S)$, what is the degree of every vertex when $S$ is symmetric (i.e. $s \in S \Rightarrow s^{-1} \in S$) and contains no involutions?
text: In $\mathrm{Cay}(G, S)$, what is the degree of every vertex when $S$ is symmetric (i.e. $s \in S \Rightarrow s^{-1} \in S$) and contains no involutions? Each $s \in S$ contributes one outgoing edge $g \to gs$ at every vertex. With $S$ symmetric and involution-free, edges come in $\{s, s^{-1}\}$ pairs, so each vertex has exactly $|S|$ neighbors.

q: Which action of $G$ on $\mathrm{Cay}(G, S)$ is by graph automorphisms?
text: Which action of $G$ on $\mathrm{Cay}(G, S)$ is by graph automorphisms? Left multiplication preserves edges: if $h \to hs$ is an edge, then $gh \to ghs = (gh)s$ is also an edge. Right multiplication moves along the edge labelled $s$, so it is not an automorphism (it permutes labels).

q: What does the word metric $d_S(g, h)$ measure?
text: What does the word metric $d_S(g, h)$ measure? $d_S(g, h)$ is the graph distance in $\mathrm{Cay}(G, S)$ — equivalently, the smallest $n$ such that $g^{-1}h = s_1 s_2 \cdots s_n$ with each $s_i \in S \cup S^{-1}$.

q: Which of the following is a quasi-isometry between $\mathbb{R}$ and $\mathbb{Z}$?
text: Which of the following is a quasi-isometry between $\mathbb{R}$ and $\mathbb{Z}$? $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$ is a $(1,1)$-quasi-isometry: $|\lfloor x \rfloor - \lfloor y \rfloor| \le |x-y| + 1$ and image is $1$-dense. Constants collapse distance; exponentials blow it up.

q: By the Schwarz–Milnor lemma, two finite generating sets $S, S'$ of the same group $G$ produce Cayley graphs that are:
text: By the Schwarz–Milnor lemma, two finite generating sets $S, S'$ of the same group $G$ produce Cayley graphs that are: Each generator of $S'$ has finite length in $S$, giving a bilipschitz comparison of word metrics — hence a quasi-isometry. The graphs are usually not isomorphic: $\mathbb{Z}$ with $S=\{1\}$ is a line, with $S=\{2,3\}$ it has different local structure.

q: Which property is NOT a quasi-isometry invariant of finitely-generated groups?
text: Which property is NOT a quasi-isometry invariant of finitely-generated groups? Element order is local arithmetic — invisible to large-scale geometry. Growth, ends, and hyperbolicity are all coarse invariants.

q: What is the growth type of $\mathbb{Z}^d$ with the standard generating set?
text: What is the growth type of $\mathbb{Z}^d$ with the standard generating set? The ball of radius $n$ in $\mathbb{Z}^d$ is a discrete diamond (the $\ell^1$-ball) of volume $\Theta(n^d)$ — polynomial growth of degree exactly $d$.

q: What is the growth type of the free group $F_2$?
text: What is the growth type of the free group $F_2$? The Cayley graph of $F_2 = \langle a, b \rangle$ is the $4$-regular tree, whose ball of radius $n$ has $1 + 4(3^n - 1)/2$ vertices — exponential growth with rate $3$.

q: What does Gromov's polynomial-growth theorem say?
text: What does Gromov's polynomial-growth theorem say? Gromov (1981): polynomial growth $\Leftrightarrow$ a finite-index nilpotent subgroup exists. The hard direction uses an asymptotic-cone limit argument and Montgomery–Zippin's solution to Hilbert's fifth problem.

q: A geodesic triangle in a $\delta$-hyperbolic space is $\delta$-thin in the sense that:
text: A geodesic triangle in a $\delta$-hyperbolic space is $\delta$-thin in the sense that: Thin-triangles capture negative curvature combinatorially: in $\mathbb{H}^2$ all triangles satisfy this with a uniform $\delta$, while in $\mathbb{R}^2$ no finite $\delta$ works.

q: Which of the following is hyperbolic?
text: Which of the following is hyperbolic? Trees are $0$-hyperbolic, and the Cayley graph of $F_n$ is a $2n$-regular tree. Abelian groups of rank $\ge 2$ contain flat planes and are not hyperbolic.

q: Why is the word problem solvable in linear time for a hyperbolic group?
text: Why is the word problem solvable in linear time for a hyperbolic group? Dehn's algorithm — repeatedly replacing more-than-half of a relator with the shorter complement — terminates linearly in word length on a hyperbolic group, decisively solving the word problem.

q: Mostow's rigidity theorem says that for closed hyperbolic manifolds of dimension $\ge 3$:
text: Mostow's rigidity theorem says that for closed hyperbolic manifolds of dimension $\ge 3$: A homotopy equivalence between two closed hyperbolic $n$-manifolds ($n \ge 3$) is homotopic to an isometry. So $\pi_1$ determines the metric — a striking failure of flexibility in higher rank.

q: Cayley graphs of which family give an explicit construction of expander graphs?
text: Cayley graphs of which family give an explicit construction of expander graphs? Margulis (1973) showed Cayley graphs of $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{F}_p)$ with a fixed generating set form an expander family — a key application of property (T) and a foundation of pseudorandom-graph constructions.

q: Which decision problem for finitely-presented groups is undecidable in full generality?
text: Which decision problem for finitely-presented groups is undecidable in full generality? Novikov–Boone (word), Miller (conjugacy), and Adian–Rabin (isomorphism) all proved undecidability for general f.p. groups. Each becomes tractable on restricted classes — hyperbolic groups, for instance, decide all three.

title: Multiresolution analysis
blurb: Mallat's MRA: a nested chain $\cdots\subset V_{-1}\subset V_0\subset V_1\subset\cdots$ of closed subspaces of $L^2(\mathbb{R})$ with $\bigcap V_j=\{0\}$, $\overline{\bigcup V_j}=L^2$, dilation symmetry $f\in V_j\iff f(2\cdot)\in V_{j+1}$, and a scaling function $\phi$ whose integer translates form an orthonormal basis of $V_0$. The orthogonal complements $W_j=V_{j+1}\ominus V_j$ are spanned by the wavelet $\psi$ at scale $j$.

title: The Haar wavelet
blurb: The simplest MRA: $\phi=\chi_{[0,1)}$ and $\psi=\chi_{[0,1/2)}-\chi_{[1/2,1)}$. Then $\{\psi_{j,k}(x)=2^{j/2}\psi(2^j x-k):j,k\in\mathbb{Z}\}$ is an orthonormal basis of $L^2(\mathbb{R})$. Each Haar coefficient is a difference of two block averages — wavelets see local *change* the way Fourier coefficients see global oscillation.

title: Daubechies wavelets
blurb: Compactly supported orthonormal wavelets with $N$ vanishing moments, $\int x^k\psi(x)\,dx=0$ for $k<N$. Daubechies constructed them by spectral-factoring the polynomial $P(y)=\sum_{k=0}^{N-1}\binom{N-1+k}{k}y^k$ to obtain a quadrature mirror filter $h_n$ — the smoother the wavelet, the longer its support ($\operatorname{supp}\psi=[0,2N-1]$).

title: Discrete wavelet transform
blurb: Mallat's pyramid algorithm computes the wavelet expansion of a length-$n$ signal in $O(n)$ time — strictly faster than the FFT's $O(n\log n)$. At each level the input is convolved with the QMF pair $(h,g)$ and downsampled by $2$; the low-pass branch recurses, the high-pass branch stores detail coefficients. Inverse: upsample, convolve with the dual filters, sum.

title: Time-frequency localisation
blurb: Each wavelet $\psi_{j,k}$ occupies a Heisenberg box of area $\Delta x\,\Delta\xi\gtrsim 1$ centred at $(2^{-j}k,\,2^j\xi_0)$: high-frequency atoms are short and many, low-frequency atoms are long and few. This dyadic tiling tracks transients sharply where windowed Fourier (Gabor) — using a fixed-width window for every frequency — cannot.

title: Applications
blurb: JPEG2000 replaces JPEG's blocked DCT with a Cohen–Daubechies–Feauveau biorthogonal DWT, giving smooth degradation instead of blocking artefacts. Donoho–Johnstone soft-thresholding $\hat c=\operatorname{sgn}(c)(|c|-\lambda)_+$ is asymptotically minimax for denoising. Vanishing moments make $\psi_{j,k}$ near-eigenvectors of Calderón–Zygmund operators, which compresses elliptic-PDE matrices to sparse form.

q: Which axiom is NOT part of Mallat's definition of an MRA on $L^2(\mathbb{R})$?
text: Which axiom is NOT part of Mallat's definition of an MRA on $L^2(\mathbb{R})$? An MRA explicitly violates finite-dimensionality: $V_0$ already has the integer translates of $\phi$ as an orthonormal basis, so $\dim V_0=\aleph_0$. The other three are exactly Mallat's nesting, density-and-separation, and dyadic-dilation axioms.

q: If $\phi$ is the scaling function of an MRA and $\psi$ the associated wavelet, what is $W_0=V_1\ominus V_0$?
text: If $\phi$ is the scaling function of an MRA and $\psi$ the associated wavelet, what is $W_0=V_1\ominus V_0$? By construction $\psi\in V_1$ is orthogonal to $V_0$, and $\{\psi(\cdot-k)\}_{k\in\mathbb{Z}}$ is an orthonormal basis of the orthogonal complement $W_0$. Iterating dilations gives $\{\psi_{j,k}\}$ as an orthonormal basis of $L^2(\mathbb{R})=\bigoplus_j W_j$.

q: Take $V_j=\{f\in L^2(\mathbb{R}): f$ is constant on every dyadic interval $[2^{-j}k,2^{-j}(k+1))\}$ (the Haar MRA). What is the integral $\int_{\mathbb{R}}\phi(x)\,dx$ for the canonical scaling function $\phi=\chi_{[0,1)}$?
text: Take $V_j=\{f\in L^2(\mathbb{R}): f$ is constant on every dyadic interval $[2^{-j}k,2^{-j}(k+1))\}$ (the Haar MRA). What is the integral $\int_{\mathbb{R}}\phi(x)\,dx$ for the canonical scaling function $\phi=\chi_{[0,1)}$? $\phi$ is the indicator of a unit interval, so $\int\phi=1$. The normalisation $\int\phi=1$ is forced (up to sign) by $\sum_k\phi(\cdot-k)\equiv 1$ — a partition-of-unity condition any MRA scaling function satisfies.

q: Compute the Haar wavelet coefficient $\langle f,\psi_{0,0}\rangle$ for $f(x)=x$ on $[0,1)$ and $0$ elsewhere, where $\psi_{0,0}=\psi=\chi_{[0,1/2)}-\chi_{[1/2,1)}$.
text: Compute the Haar wavelet coefficient $\langle f,\psi_{0,0}\rangle$ for $f(x)=x$ on $[0,1)$ and $0$ elsewhere, where $\psi_{0,0}=\psi=\chi_{[0,1/2)}-\chi_{[1/2,1)}$. $\langle f,\psi\rangle=\int_0^{1/2}x\,dx-\int_{1/2}^1 x\,dx=\tfrac{1}{8}-\tfrac{3}{8}=-\tfrac{1}{4}$. The first half of the ramp averages to $1/4$, the second half to $3/4$; the Haar coefficient is half their difference times the interval width.

q: Which property of $\{\psi_{j,k}\}_{j,k\in\mathbb{Z}}$ with $\psi$ the Haar wavelet is FALSE?
text: Which property of $\{\psi_{j,k}\}_{j,k\in\mathbb{Z}}$ with $\psi$ the Haar wavelet is FALSE? The Haar wavelet is $\chi_{[0,1/2)}-\chi_{[1/2,1)}$ — a step function with two jump discontinuities. The other claims hold: it is the standard textbook example of a compactly supported orthonormal wavelet basis with one vanishing moment.

q: How many vanishing moments does the Haar wavelet have?
text: How many vanishing moments does the Haar wavelet have? $\int\psi(x)\,dx=\tfrac12-\tfrac12=0$, so the $0$th moment vanishes. But $\int x\psi(x)\,dx=\int_0^{1/2}x\,dx-\int_{1/2}^1 x\,dx=-1/4\ne 0$, so the $1$st moment does not. Exactly one vanishing moment — that's why Haar barely sees linear trends; Daubechies-$N$ wavelets are designed to kill the first $N$ moments.

q: What does it mean for an orthonormal wavelet $\psi$ to have $N$ vanishing moments?
text: What does it mean for an orthonormal wavelet $\psi$ to have $N$ vanishing moments? Vanishing-moment conditions are about $\int x^k\psi=0$ for $k<N$. Equivalently $\hat\psi(\xi)$ has a zero of order $N$ at $\xi=0$. They control how fast wavelet coefficients decay on smooth signals: if $f\in C^N$ near $2^{-j}k$, then $|\langle f,\psi_{j,k}\rangle|=O(2^{-j(N+1/2)})$.

q: The Daubechies-$N$ wavelet $\psi_N$ is supported on which interval?
text: The Daubechies-$N$ wavelet $\psi_N$ is supported on which interval? Daubechies's filter $h_n$ has $2N$ non-zero taps, $n=0,\ldots,2N-1$, so the scaling and wavelet functions have support $[0,2N-1]$. Daubechies-$1$ is the Haar wavelet (support $[0,1]$, one vanishing moment); higher $N$ trades support length for smoothness and more vanishing moments.

q: Why does Daubechies require the QMF condition $|m_0(\xi)|^2+|m_0(\xi+1/2)|^2=1$ on the filter symbol $m_0(\xi)=\tfrac{1}{\sqrt 2}\sum_n h_n e^{-2\pi in\xi}$?
text: Why does Daubechies require the QMF condition $|m_0(\xi)|^2+|m_0(\xi+1/2)|^2=1$ on the filter symbol $m_0(\xi)=\tfrac{1}{\sqrt 2}\sum_n h_n e^{-2\pi in\xi}$? Orthonormality of integer translates of $\phi$ is equivalent (via Poisson summation) to $\sum_k|\hat\phi(\xi+k)|^2=1$, and propagating that through the refinement equation $\hat\phi(2\xi)=m_0(\xi)\hat\phi(\xi)$ produces the QMF identity. Compact support and vanishing moments are independent design knobs imposed on $h_n$ separately.

q: Apply one step of the Haar DWT to the length-$4$ signal $(8,2,4,6)$. What is the first detail coefficient $d_1$ (the high-pass output at position $0$, computed as $(x_0-x_1)/\sqrt 2$)?
text: Apply one step of the Haar DWT to the length-$4$ signal $(8,2,4,6)$. What is the first detail coefficient $d_1$ (the high-pass output at position $0$, computed as $(x_0-x_1)/\sqrt 2$)? The Haar high-pass filter takes pairwise differences and rescales by $1/\sqrt 2$: $d_1=(8-2)/\sqrt 2=6/\sqrt 2=3\sqrt 2\approx 4.243$. The low-pass branch yields $(8+2)/\sqrt 2=5\sqrt 2$ at the same position; together they reconstruct $(8,2)$ via inverse rotation.

q: What is the asymptotic cost of the Mallat pyramid DWT on a length-$n$ signal with a fixed filter of $L$ taps?
text: What is the asymptotic cost of the Mallat pyramid DWT on a length-$n$ signal with a fixed filter of $L$ taps? Each level convolves with a fixed $L$-tap filter and downsamples by $2$, so level $j$ costs $O(L\cdot n/2^j)$; summing the geometric series gives $O(Ln)=O(n)$. Strictly cheaper than the FFT's $O(n\log n)$ — and because each level halves the data, the whole transform is genuinely linear-time.

q: In the standard pyramid algorithm, what does the inverse DWT do at each level?
text: In the standard pyramid algorithm, what does the inverse DWT do at each level? Inversion mirrors analysis: the low-pass and high-pass coefficient streams are upsampled (zero-stuffed to twice their length), each is convolved with its dual reconstruction filter, and the two streams are added. For an orthonormal wavelet the dual filters are time-reversals of the analysis filters; the algorithm stays $O(n)$ overall.

q: A wavelet atom $\psi_{j,k}$ occupies a Heisenberg cell of approximate dimensions
text: A wavelet atom $\psi_{j,k}$ occupies a Heisenberg cell of approximate dimensions Dilation by $2^j$ shrinks support by $2^{-j}$ in space and stretches frequency content by $2^j$. The Heisenberg product $\Delta x\,\Delta\xi\sim 1$ — saturating the uncertainty principle — but the cell shape adapts: high-frequency atoms are short and tall, low-frequency atoms are long and narrow. Gabor windows, by contrast, force a fixed cell shape at every frequency.

q: For which signal class do wavelet expansions outperform Fourier expansions most dramatically?
text: For which signal class do wavelet expansions outperform Fourier expansions most dramatically? A jump discontinuity smears across all Fourier coefficients (Gibbs phenomenon, $|\hat f(\xi)|\sim 1/|\xi|$), but only the wavelets whose support touches the jump have large coefficients — a logarithmic number of them. Stationary or bandlimited signals are exactly where Fourier shines; wavelets give up phase coherence in exchange for spatial localisation.

q: If $f\in C^N$ on a neighbourhood of $2^{-j}k$ and $\psi$ has $N$ vanishing moments, the wavelet coefficient satisfies
text: If $f\in C^N$ on a neighbourhood of $2^{-j}k$ and $\psi$ has $N$ vanishing moments, the wavelet coefficient satisfies Taylor-expand $f$ to order $N$ at the centre and pair against $\psi_{j,k}$: the first $N$ terms vanish by the moment conditions, leaving a remainder bounded by $\|f^{(N)}\|_\infty\cdot 2^{-j(N+1/2)}$ (the extra $1/2$ is the $L^2$ normalisation $2^{j/2}$). This is the decay engine behind wavelet compression of smooth signals.

q: Why does JPEG2000 use a wavelet transform instead of the blocked $8\times 8$ DCT of legacy JPEG?
text: Why does JPEG2000 use a wavelet transform instead of the blocked $8\times 8$ DCT of legacy JPEG? JPEG's tiling produces visible $8\times 8$ blocks at low bitrates because each tile quantises independently. The CDF 9/7 biorthogonal wavelet in JPEG2000 is global and multi-scale, so high quantisation looks like overall smoothing rather than mosaic. Speed and worst-case ratio are not the differentiator.

q: Donoho–Johnstone soft-thresholding denoises a signal by transforming, applying $\hat c=\operatorname{sgn}(c)(|c|-\lambda)_+$ to each wavelet coefficient $c$, and inverting. Why does this work?
text: Donoho–Johnstone soft-thresholding denoises a signal by transforming, applying $\hat c=\operatorname{sgn}(c)(|c|-\lambda)_+$ to each wavelet coefficient $c$, and inverting. Why does this work? The wavelet transform of white Gaussian noise is again white Gaussian (orthogonal change of basis). Smooth signals concentrate energy in few large coefficients; thresholding at $\lambda\sim\sigma\sqrt{2\log n}$ removes the noise floor while keeping signal spikes. Donoho–Johnstone proved this is asymptotically minimax over Besov balls.

q: Why are wavelets a natural basis for Calderón–Zygmund operators (e.g. the Hilbert transform, $-\Delta^{-1}$)?
text: Why are wavelets a natural basis for Calderón–Zygmund operators (e.g. the Hilbert transform, $-\Delta^{-1}$)? Beylkin–Coifman–Rokhlin showed that for a CZ operator $T$ and a wavelet with $N$ vanishing moments, $|\langle T\psi_{j,k},\psi_{j',k'}\rangle|\le C 2^{-(N+1)|j-j'|}(1+|2^j k-2^{j'}k'|)^{-N-1}$. Most entries are negligibly small, so $T$ is approximated by an $O(n)$-entry sparse matrix — wavelets do not diagonalise $T$ exactly, but they nearly do.

title: Vertex operators and locality
blurb: A vertex operator $Y(a, z) = \sum_{n} a_n z^{-n-1}$ is a formal Laurent series of operators on a state space $V$ assigned to each $a \in V$; the axiom of locality $(z-w)^N [Y(a,z), Y(b,w)] = 0$ for $N \gg 0$ replaces commutativity with mutual asymptotic vanishing.

title: VOA axioms — vacuum, translation, locality, Jacobi
blurb: A vertex operator algebra is $(V, Y, \mathbf{1}, \omega)$ satisfying vacuum ($Y(\mathbf{1}, z) = \mathrm{id}$), translation ($[T, Y(a,z)] = \partial_z Y(a,z)$), locality, and the Borcherds–Jacobi identity that ties operator-product expansion to a single equation; all four are equivalent characterisations under mild hypotheses.

title: Virasoro algebra and central charge
blurb: The conformal vector $\omega \in V$ produces operators $L_n$ with $[L_m, L_n] = (m-n) L_{m+n} + \tfrac{c}{12}(m^3 - m)\,\delta_{m+n,0}$; the central charge $c$ is a numerical invariant of the VOA and $L_0$ supplies the integer grading that turns $V$ into a graded module.

title: Modular invariance — Zhu's theorem
blurb: For a $C_2$-cofinite rational VOA, the graded character $\mathrm{ch}_M(\tau) = \tr_M q^{L_0 - c/24}$ of an irreducible module is a component of a vector-valued modular form for $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$; Zhu's theorem (1996) is the bridge between two-dimensional CFT and modular forms.

title: The moonshine module $V^\natural$
blurb: Frenkel–Lepowsky–Meurman constructed $V^\natural$ — a $\mathbb{Z}$-graded VOA of central charge $24$ whose graded dimension is $J(\tau) = j(\tau) - 744$ and whose automorphism group is the Monster; Borcherds' proof of Conway–Norton uses the Monster Lie algebra built from $V^\natural$ via the Goddard–Thorn no-ghost theorem.

title: Applications — chiral CFT and chiral algebras
blurb: VOAs axiomatise the chiral half of two-dimensional conformal field theory; Beilinson–Drinfeld's chiral algebras globalise the construction over algebraic curves, replacing formal power series with $\mathcal{D}$-modules and giving the framework underlying the geometric Langlands program.

q: A vertex operator $Y(a, z)$ associated to a state $a$ in a VOA is:
text: A vertex operator $Y(a, z)$ associated to a state $a$ in a VOA is: $Y(\cdot, z) : V \to \mathrm{End}(V)[[z, z^{-1}]]$ is a state–field correspondence: each $a \in V$ gives the formal series $Y(a, z) = \sum_{n \in \mathbb{Z}} a_n z^{-n-1}$. The mode $a_n$ is the coefficient of $z^{-n-1}$, so $a_{-1}\mathbf{1} = a$ recovers the state.

q: The locality axiom for two vertex operators $Y(a, z)$ and $Y(b, w)$ says:
text: The locality axiom for two vertex operators $Y(a, z)$ and $Y(b, w)$ says: Two fields are mutually local if some power $(z-w)^N$ kills their commutator. This is weaker than commutativity: it allows OPE singularities at $z = w$ but forces them to be of finite order, recovering an algebraic shadow of the physicists' radial-ordering.

q: If $Y(a, z) = \sum_n a_n z^{-n-1}$, what is the index $n$ of the mode whose action on the vacuum returns $a$ — i.e. the $n$ for which $a_n \mathbf{1} = a$?
text: If $Y(a, z) = \sum_n a_n z^{-n-1}$, what is the index $n$ of the mode whose action on the vacuum returns $a$ — i.e. the $n$ for which $a_n \mathbf{1} = a$? By the creation axiom, $Y(a, z)\mathbf{1}|_{z=0} = a$. Reading off the constant term in $z$ (the coefficient of $z^0$) and matching against $\sum_n a_n z^{-n-1}$ forces $-n - 1 = 0$, so $n = -1$. Thus $a = a_{-1}\mathbf{1}$, the standard 'state-from-mode' identification.

q: The vacuum axiom of a VOA requires that:
text: The vacuum axiom of a VOA requires that: The vacuum vector $\mathbf{1}$ is the unit: $Y(\mathbf{1}, z) = \mathrm{id}_V$ (no $z$-dependence). The companion axiom $Y(a, z)\mathbf{1} \in V[[z]]$ with $Y(a, z)\mathbf{1}|_{z=0} = a$ encodes the state–field correspondence.

q: The translation operator $T$ in a VOA satisfies:
text: The translation operator $T$ in a VOA satisfies: Translation covariance: differentiating with respect to the formal variable $z$ is realised by the inner derivation $[T, -]$. The infinitesimal translation $T$ is identified with $L_{-1}$ once a Virasoro element is fixed.

q: Why is the Borcherds–Jacobi identity often preferred over locality plus the vacuum axiom as the definition of a VOA?
text: Why is the Borcherds–Jacobi identity often preferred over locality plus the vacuum axiom as the definition of a VOA? The Jacobi identity $\sum (\cdots) Y(a, z_1) Y(b, z_2) c = \cdots$ is equivalent to locality plus weak associativity (under standard hypotheses) and reduces to a single mode-level relation. It makes algebraic manipulations — computing OPE coefficients, deriving commutator formulas — purely combinatorial.

q: The Virasoro algebra commutation relation in a VOA reads:
text: The Virasoro algebra commutation relation in a VOA reads: The Virasoro algebra is the unique non-trivial central extension of the Witt algebra of vector fields on $S^1$. The cocycle $\tfrac{c}{12}(m^3 - m)\,\delta_{m+n,0}$ scales with the central charge $c$, vanishing on $L_{-1}, L_0, L_1$ (the $\mathfrak{sl}_2$ subalgebra) since $m^3 - m = m(m-1)(m+1)$.

q: For the central charge of the moonshine module $V^\natural$, what is $c$?
text: For the central charge of the moonshine module $V^\natural$, what is $c$? $V^\natural$ has central charge $c = 24$. This is forced by modular invariance: the graded character $\tr q^{L_0 - c/24}$ must transform like a weight-$0$ modular function on $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$, which requires the shift $-c/24 = -1$ so that $L_0 - c/24$ has integer eigenvalues starting at $-1$, matching $J(\tau) = q^{-1} + 196884\, q + \cdots$.

q: The conformal vector $\omega \in V$ supplies the operators $L_n$ via:
text: The conformal vector $\omega \in V$ supplies the operators $L_n$ via: By convention $Y(\omega, z) = \sum_n L_n z^{-n - 2}$ — the shift by $2$ comes from $\omega$ having conformal weight $2$ (it is the stress–energy tensor in CFT language). Then $L_0$ is the coefficient of $z^{-2}$ and acts as the grading operator.

q: Zhu's theorem (1996) states that for a $C_2$-cofinite rational VOA $V$, the graded characters $\mathrm{ch}_M(\tau) = \tr_M q^{L_0 - c/24}$ of irreducible modules:
text: Zhu's theorem (1996) states that for a $C_2$-cofinite rational VOA $V$, the graded characters $\mathrm{ch}_M(\tau) = \tr_M q^{L_0 - c/24}$ of irreducible modules: Zhu showed that the span of the graded characters of irreducible $V$-modules is preserved by the $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$-action $\tau \mapsto (a\tau + b)/(c\tau + d)$; together they assemble into a vector-valued modular form. Individual characters mix under $S : \tau \mapsto -1/\tau$ — that mixing is the fusion-rules data.

q: The shift by $-c/24$ in $\tr q^{L_0 - c/24}$ is necessary because:
text: The shift by $-c/24$ in $\tr q^{L_0 - c/24}$ is necessary because: The $-c/24$ shift comes from the Casimir of the Virasoro vacuum representation; it is exactly the shift in $\eta(\tau) = q^{1/24} \prod(1 - q^n)$. Without it, the character is a $q$-series with no good modular transformation; with it, the character lives on the modular curve $X(1)$ rather than its universal cover.

q: $C_2$-cofiniteness is the requirement that:
text: $C_2$-cofiniteness is the requirement that: $C_2(V)$ is the span of $\{a_{-2} b : a, b \in V\}$; finite-dimensionality of $V / C_2(V)$ is a strong finiteness condition that, combined with rationality, gives Zhu's theorem and the Verlinde formula. Every VOA appearing in moonshine satisfies it.

q: The moonshine module $V^\natural$ of Frenkel–Lepowsky–Meurman is:
text:
The moonshine module $V^
atural$ of Frenkel–Lepowsky–Meurman is: $V^
atural$ is built as a $\mathbb{Z}/2$-orbifold of the lattice VOA on the Leech lattice. Its graded dimension is $\sum_n (\dim V^
atural_n)\, q^n = J(	au)$, and the Monster $\mathbb{M}$ acts as its full automorphism group — the cleanest realisation of McKay's observation.

q: The Conway–Norton conjecture, proved by Borcherds, asserts that for each $g \in \mathbb{M}$ the McKay–Thompson series $T_g(\tau) = \sum_n \tr(g \mid V^\natural_n)\, q^n$ is:
text: The Conway–Norton conjecture, proved by Borcherds, asserts that for each $g \in \mathbb{M}$ the McKay–Thompson series $T_g(\tau) = \sum_n \tr(g \mid V^\natural_n)\, q^n$ is: For each conjugacy class $[g] \subset \mathbb{M}$, $T_g$ is the Hauptmodul of a specific genus-zero subgroup $\Gamma_g \subset \mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{R})$. The 'genus-zero' constraint is what made the conjecture so striking: it pins out a finite list of subgroups out of an infinite family.

q: Borcherds' proof routes through the Monster Lie algebra, which is built from $V^\natural$ via:
text: Borcherds' proof routes through the Monster Lie algebra, which is built from $V^\natural$ via: Borcherds tensors $V^\natural$ with a rank-$2$ Lorentzian-lattice VOA, then applies the Goddard–Thorn no-ghost theorem to extract a generalised Kac–Moody (Borcherds–Kac–Moody) Lie algebra. Its denominator identity furnishes the replication formulas that pin each Thompson series to its Conway–Norton Hauptmodul.

q: In two-dimensional conformal field theory, a vertex operator algebra encodes:
text: In two-dimensional conformal field theory, a vertex operator algebra encodes: A 2D CFT factorises (subject to modular invariance) into a chiral and an antichiral half. Each half is a VOA — the same axioms that codify locality of fields on $S^1$ at fixed time. The full theory is a tensor product of the two halves modulo gluing data.

q: Beilinson–Drinfeld's chiral algebras generalise VOAs by:
text: Beilinson–Drinfeld's chiral algebras generalise VOAs by: A chiral algebra on a curve $X$ is a $\mathcal{D}$-module on $X$ with a chiral product on $\mathcal{A} \boxtimes \mathcal{A}$ over $X \times X$ off the diagonal. Restriction to the formal disc near a point recovers a VOA; the global data is what makes geometric Langlands work.

q: Which of the following is NOT an example of a vertex operator algebra?
text: Which of the following is NOT an example of a vertex operator algebra? Lattice, affine Kac–Moody (at admissible levels), and Heisenberg VOAs are the standard VOA constructions. The universal enveloping algebra $U(\mathfrak{g})$ of an arbitrary finite-dimensional Lie algebra is an associative algebra, not a VOA — to get a VOA from $\mathfrak{g}$ you affinise to $\widehat{\mathfrak{g}}$ and pass to a vacuum module at a chosen level.

title: Time-dependent Schrödinger equation
blurb: $i\hbar\,\partial_t\psi = H\psi$ with $H = -\frac{\hbar^2}{2m}\Delta + V$ governs unitary evolution on $L^2(\mathbb{R}^n)$: self-adjointness of $H$ makes $U_t = e^{-itH/\hbar}$ a one-parameter unitary group, so $\|\psi(t)\|_{L^2} \equiv \|\psi(0)\|_{L^2}$ — total probability is conserved.

title: Stationary states and the eigenvalue problem
blurb: Separating $\psi(x,t) = e^{-iEt/\hbar}\varphi(x)$ reduces the dynamics to $H\varphi = E\varphi$. Energy eigenstates are the spectral resolution of the Hamiltonian: bound states sit in the point spectrum, scattering states in the continuous spectrum, and any wavefunction is the superposition $\psi = \sum c_n \varphi_n + \int c(E)\varphi_E\,dE$.

title: Harmonic oscillator and ladder operators
blurb: For $V = \tfrac{1}{2}m\omega^2 x^2$, the operators $a = \sqrt{m\omega/2\hbar}\,(x + ip/m\omega)$ and $a^\dagger$ satisfy $[a,a^\dagger]=1$ and $H = \hbar\omega(a^\dagger a + \tfrac{1}{2})$. The spectrum is $E_n = \hbar\omega(n + \tfrac{1}{2})$, $n=0,1,2,\dots$, with eigenstates $\varphi_n \propto (a^\dagger)^n \varphi_0$ — Hermite functions.

title: Hydrogen atom
blurb: With Coulomb potential $V = -e^2/r$, the equation separates in spherical coordinates: $\varphi_{n\ell m}(r,\theta,\phi) = R_{n\ell}(r)\,Y_\ell^m(\theta,\phi)$. The radial problem yields bound-state energies $E_n = -E_0/n^2$ with $E_0 = me^4/2\hbar^2 \approx 13.6\,\mathrm{eV}$, each $n^2$-fold degenerate — the spectrum that Bohr guessed.

title: Scattering states and barrier transmission
blurb: Above the potential's asymptotic value, plane waves $e^{ikx}$ are generalized eigenstates of $-\Delta + V$: continuous spectrum. A barrier of height $V_0$ and width $a$ produces transmission $T(E)$ — non-zero even for $E < V_0$ (tunneling). The Schwinger formula and Lippmann–Schwinger expansion bridge this to the spectral theory of the half-line Schrödinger operator.

title: Path integral and the classical limit
blurb: Feynman's reformulation writes the propagator $\langle x_f | e^{-iHT/\hbar} | x_i \rangle = \int e^{iS[q]/\hbar}\,\mathcal{D}q$, summing $e^{iS/\hbar}$ over all paths $q(t)$ from $x_i$ to $x_f$ with action $S = \int L\,dt$. As $\hbar \to 0$, stationary phase concentrates the integral on $\delta S = 0$ — the Euler–Lagrange path — recovering classical mechanics.

q: Why is $\|\psi(t)\|_{L^2}$ conserved under $i\hbar\,\partial_t\psi = H\psi$?
text: Why is $\|\psi(t)\|_{L^2}$ conserved under $i\hbar\,\partial_t\psi = H\psi$? Self-adjointness of $H$ makes $U_t=e^{-itH/\hbar}$ a strongly continuous unitary group (Stone), and unitaries preserve the $L^2$ norm exactly.

q: For a free particle ($V=0$) in 1D, the Schrödinger equation $i\hbar\,\partial_t\psi = -\frac{\hbar^2}{2m}\partial_{xx}\psi$ relates to which classical PDE type?
text: For a free particle ($V=0$) in 1D, the Schrödinger equation $i\hbar\,\partial_t\psi = -\frac{\hbar^2}{2m}\partial_{xx}\psi$ relates to which classical PDE type? The principal symbol matches the heat equation up to a factor of $i$; substituting $t \to -it$ gives a true parabolic equation, which is why Schrödinger evolution is sometimes called 'imaginary-time heat flow'.

q: If $\|\psi(0)\|_{L^2}^2 = 1$ and the Hamiltonian is self-adjoint, what is $\|\psi(10\,\mathrm{s})\|_{L^2}^2$?
text: If $\|\psi(0)\|_{L^2}^2 = 1$ and the Hamiltonian is self-adjoint, what is $\|\psi(10\,\mathrm{s})\|_{L^2}^2$? Unitary evolution preserves the $L^2$ norm exactly — total probability stays at 1 forever.

q: If $H\varphi = E\varphi$, how does the time-evolved state $\psi(x,t)$ depend on $t$?
text: If $H\varphi = E\varphi$, how does the time-evolved state $\psi(x,t)$ depend on $t$? Plug $\psi=e^{-iEt/\hbar}\varphi$ into $i\hbar\,\partial_t\psi=H\psi$: the LHS gives $E\psi$, the RHS gives $E\psi$. Stationary states evolve only by a global phase.

q: What does 'stationary state' mean physically?
text: What does 'stationary state' mean physically? The phase $e^{-iEt/\hbar}$ has modulus 1, so $|\psi(x,t)|^2 = |\varphi(x)|^2$ for all $t$. Observables are stationary; the wavefunction itself precesses in phase.

q: Bound states of $H = -\Delta + V$ correspond to which part of $\sigma(H)$?
text: Bound states of $H = -\Delta + V$ correspond to which part of $\sigma(H)$? Bound states are normalizable eigenfunctions — they lie in $L^2$, so the corresponding $E$ is in the point spectrum. Scattering states (plane-wave-like) lie in the continuous spectrum.

q: What is the ground-state energy $E_0$ of the harmonic oscillator in units of $\hbar\omega$?
text: What is the ground-state energy $E_0$ of the harmonic oscillator in units of $\hbar\omega$? $E_n = \hbar\omega(n+\tfrac{1}{2})$ gives $E_0 = \tfrac{1}{2}\hbar\omega$ — the zero-point energy, a direct consequence of $[x,p]=i\hbar$.

q: What is the commutator $[a, a^\dagger]$ for the ladder operators?
text: What is the commutator $[a, a^\dagger]$ for the ladder operators? Direct computation from $a = \sqrt{m\omega/2\hbar}(x+ip/m\omega)$ and $[x,p]=i\hbar$ gives $[a,a^\dagger]=1$. This is the entire algebraic content of the oscillator.

q: What is the energy spacing $E_{n+1} - E_n$ in units of $\hbar\omega$?
text: What is the energy spacing $E_{n+1} - E_n$ in units of $\hbar\omega$? $E_{n+1}-E_n = \hbar\omega$ — uniform spacing is what makes the oscillator the workhorse of quantization (phonons, photons, Fock space).

q: If $E_1 = -13.6\,\mathrm{eV}$, what is $E_2$ in eV (to 2 decimals)?
text: If $E_1 = -13.6\,\mathrm{eV}$, what is $E_2$ in eV (to 2 decimals)? $E_n = -E_0/n^2$, so $E_2 = -13.6/4 = -3.40\,\mathrm{eV}$. The Lyman-α transition $2\to 1$ releases $13.6 - 3.4 = 10.2\,\mathrm{eV}$.

q: Why does the radial Schrödinger equation in spherical coordinates separate?
text: Why does the radial Schrödinger equation in spherical coordinates separate? Rotational symmetry of $V(r)$ means $[H, L^2]=[H, L_z]=0$, so simultaneous eigenfunctions exist: $\varphi_{n\ell m} = R_{n\ell}(r)\,Y_\ell^m(\theta,\phi)$.

q: How many linearly independent bound states share energy $E_n$ for $n=2$ in hydrogen (ignoring spin)?
text: How many linearly independent bound states share energy $E_n$ for $n=2$ in hydrogen (ignoring spin)? Hydrogen's degeneracy is $n^2$: for $n=2$ that's $4$ states ($2s$ plus three $2p$). The 'accidental' SO(4) symmetry — Runge–Lenz — makes states with different $\ell$ degenerate.

q: For a 1D barrier of height $V_0$ and a particle with energy $E < V_0$, what does quantum mechanics predict?
text: For a 1D barrier of height $V_0$ and a particle with energy $E < V_0$, what does quantum mechanics predict? Inside the barrier the wavefunction decays as $e^{-\kappa x}$ with $\kappa = \sqrt{2m(V_0-E)}/\hbar$, but it doesn't vanish. Matching at the boundaries gives non-zero transmission — the foundation of $\alpha$-decay and STM.

q: Plane-wave solutions $e^{ikx}$ of $-\Delta\,\psi = k^2\psi$ are not in $L^2(\mathbb{R})$. What does this mean spectrally?
text: Plane-wave solutions $e^{ikx}$ of $-\Delta\,\psi = k^2\psi$ are not in $L^2(\mathbb{R})$. What does this mean spectrally? Continuous spectrum means generalized (non-$L^2$) eigenfunctions; the rigorous treatment uses spectral measures or rigged Hilbert spaces. Wavepackets — superpositions $\int c(k)e^{ikx}\,dk$ — are the genuine $L^2$ states.

q: What is the relation between transmission $T$ and reflection $R$ for a 1D barrier?
text: What is the relation between transmission $T$ and reflection $R$ for a 1D barrier? Conservation of probability current gives $T + R = 1$: every particle either transmits or reflects. This is the unitarity of the S-matrix in disguise.

q: In Feynman's path integral $\int e^{iS[q]/\hbar}\,\mathcal{D}q$, what is $S[q]$?
text: In Feynman's path integral $\int e^{iS[q]/\hbar}\,\mathcal{D}q$, what is $S[q]$? $S[q] = \int_{t_i}^{t_f} L(q,\dot q,t)\,dt$ is the classical action — the same one that's stationary on Euler–Lagrange trajectories.

q: How does the classical limit $\hbar \to 0$ emerge from the path integral?
text: How does the classical limit $\hbar \to 0$ emerge from the path integral? As $\hbar \to 0$, the phase $e^{iS/\hbar}$ oscillates wildly except at critical points of $S$ — paths satisfying $\delta S = 0$, i.e. the Euler–Lagrange equation. This is the WKB / stationary-phase picture.

q: Why is the path-integral measure $\mathcal{D}q$ mathematically subtle?
text: Why is the path-integral measure $\mathcal{D}q$ mathematically subtle? Lebesgue-style measures don't exist on infinite-dimensional spaces. Imaginary-time ($t\to -i\tau$) converts the oscillatory integral into a Gaussian one — the Wiener measure on Brownian paths — which is rigorously defined.

title: Modular arithmetic and Euler's theorem
blurb: Public-key cryptography lives in the multiplicative group $(\mathbb{Z}/n)^\times$. Its order is Euler's totient $\phi(n)$, and Fermat–Euler gives $a^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod n$ whenever $\gcd(a,n)=1$. When $n=p$ is prime the group is cyclic of order $p-1$ and admits a primitive root $g$; powers of $g$ enumerate every residue. This algebraic substrate — the finite cyclic group with a hard inverse — is what every classical cryptosystem rents.

title: RSA
blurb: Choose primes $p,q$, set $n=pq$ and $\phi(n)=(p-1)(q-1)$. Pick a public exponent $e$ coprime to $\phi(n)$ and compute the private $d \equiv e^{-1} \pmod{\phi(n)}$. Encryption is $c=m^e \bmod n$, decryption $m=c^d \bmod n$ — Fermat–Euler guarantees $(m^e)^d=m^{ed}=m^{1+k\phi(n)}\equiv m$. Security rests on the conjectured hardness of factoring $n$: a factoriser exposes $\phi(n)$, hence $d$. The RSA assumption is slightly weaker — extracting $e$-th roots mod $n$ — but no separation is known.

title: Discrete logarithm and Diffie–Hellman
blurb: Fix a prime $p$ and primitive root $g\in(\mathbb{Z}/p)^\times$. The discrete log $\log_g h$ is the exponent $x$ with $g^x\equiv h \pmod p$ — easy to verify, conjecturally hard to invert. Diffie–Hellman key exchange (1976) leverages the asymmetry: Alice sends $g^a$, Bob sends $g^b$, both compute $g^{ab}$. The best classical attack is the index calculus, sub-exponential $L_p[1/3,c]$. The DLP underpins ElGamal encryption, DSA signatures, and the entire pre-elliptic public-key stack.

title: Elliptic-curve cryptography
blurb: Replace $(\mathbb{Z}/p)^\times$ with $E(\mathbb{F}_p)$ — points of an elliptic curve over a prime field. The chord-tangent group law gives a finite abelian group of order $\approx p$ (Hasse bound), and on suitable curves the only known DLP attack is Pollard's $\rho$ in $O(\sqrt{p})$ — no index calculus. So 256-bit ECC matches 3072-bit RSA. ECDH and ECDSA are the elliptic analogues of Diffie–Hellman and DSA. Pitfalls: weak curves where the MOV attack pulls the DLP into $\mathbb{F}_{p^k}^\times$, or anomalous curves where the lift to $\mathbb{Z}_p$ is linear. NIST P-256, Curve25519, and secp256k1 (Bitcoin) are the standard hardened choices.

title: Lattice-based and post-quantum cryptography
blurb: Shor's algorithm (1994) breaks factoring and discrete log on a quantum computer in polynomial time, dragging RSA, DH, and ECC down with it. The leading replacement is lattice cryptography. The Shortest Vector Problem (SVP) and Closest Vector Problem (CVP) on a lattice $\Lambda\subset\mathbb{R}^n$ are NP-hard in worst case; Regev's Learning With Errors (LWE, 2005) reduces average-case LWE to worst-case lattice problems via a quantum reduction. CRYSTALS-Kyber, the NIST PQC KEM standard (FIPS 203, 2024), is built on Module-LWE. Signatures (Dilithium / FIPS 204) follow the same blueprint: encode a secret as a short lattice vector, hide it under a noisy linear map.

title: Zero-knowledge proofs
blurb: An interactive proof for a language $L$ is a protocol between prover $P$ and verifier $V$ such that (a) completeness : $x\in L$ ⇒ honest $P$ convinces $V$ with probability $\ge 2/3$; (b) soundness : $x\notin L$ ⇒ no $P^*$ convinces $V$ with probability $> 1/3$; (c) zero-knowledge : the verifier's transcript is simulatable from $x$ alone. Schnorr's protocol (1989) is the canonical example for the discrete-log relation. Modern zk-SNARKs (Groth16, PLONK) compile arbitrary NP statements into a few-kilobyte non-interactive proof — the engine behind Zcash, zk-Rollups, and verifiable computation.

q: Compute $\phi(35)$.
By Fermat–Euler, $7^{40} \bmod 100$ equals which of the following?
text: By Fermat–Euler, $7^{40} \bmod 100$ equals which of the following? $\gcd(7,100)=1$ and $\phi(100)=40$, so $7^{40}\equiv 1 \pmod{100}$.

q: Which integer is a primitive root modulo $7$?
text: Which integer is a primitive root modulo $7$? Powers of $3$ mod $7$ cycle through $3,2,6,4,5,1$ — all six nonzero residues. The order of $3$ is $\phi(7)=6$, so $3$ is primitive. ($2$ has order $3$; $6\equiv -1$ has order $2$.)

q: RSA toy: $p=11,q=13$, so $n=143$ and $\phi(n)=120$. Take $e=7$. Compute the private exponent $d=e^{-1} \bmod 120$.
text: RSA toy: $p=11,q=13$, so $n=143$ and $\phi(n)=120$. Take $e=7$. Compute the private exponent $d=e^{-1} \bmod 120$. Extended Euclid: $7\cdot 103=721=6\cdot 120+1$, so $7\cdot 103\equiv 1 \pmod{120}$, giving $d=103$.

q: Why is RSA decryption $c^d\equiv m \pmod n$ correct?
text: Why is RSA decryption $c^d\equiv m \pmod n$ correct? The key identity is $ed\equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ (not mod $n$); Fermat–Euler then collapses $m^{k\phi(n)}$ to $1$ when $\gcd(m,n)=1$, and a CRT argument handles the remaining cases.

q: Knowing $\phi(n)$ for an RSA modulus $n=pq$ is equivalent to knowing what?
text: Knowing $\phi(n)$ for an RSA modulus $n=pq$ is equivalent to knowing what? From $n=pq$ and $\phi(n)=(p-1)(q-1)=n-(p+q)+1$ one recovers $p+q$, and combined with $pq=n$ this gives a quadratic whose roots are $p$ and $q$. So $\phi(n)$ and the factorization are polynomial-time equivalent.

q: In $(\mathbb{Z}/23)^\times$ with primitive root $g=5$, find $\log_5 10$ (i.e. the smallest $x\ge 0$ with $5^x \equiv 10 \pmod{23}$).
text: In $(\mathbb{Z}/23)^\times$ with primitive root $g=5$, find $\log_5 10$ (i.e. the smallest $x\ge 0$ with $5^x \equiv 10 \pmod{23}$). $5^1=5,\ 5^2=25\equiv 2,\ 5^3=10 \pmod{23}$. So $\log_5 10 = 3$.

q: In Diffie–Hellman, Alice sends $A=g^a$, Bob sends $B=g^b$. The shared secret is:
text: In Diffie–Hellman, Alice sends $A=g^a$, Bob sends $B=g^b$. The shared secret is: Both parties compute $g^{ab}$: Alice raises Bob's $B=g^b$ to her secret $a$, Bob raises $A=g^a$ to his secret $b$. The eavesdropper sees $g^a, g^b$ and faces the computational Diffie–Hellman problem to recover $g^{ab}$.

q: What is the asymptotic complexity of the index calculus for the discrete log in $\mathbb{F}_p^\times$?
text: What is the asymptotic complexity of the index calculus for the discrete log in $\mathbb{F}_p^\times$? Index calculus runs in sub-exponential time $L_p[1/3,c]=\exp((c+o(1))(\log p)^{1/3}(\log\log p)^{2/3})$. This is faster than Pollard's $\rho$ ($O(\sqrt p)$) on $\mathbb{F}_p^\times$ but exploits the smooth-factorization structure unique to the multiplicative group of a field.

q: Why does ECC achieve smaller key sizes than finite-field DH for the same security level?
text: Why does ECC achieve smaller key sizes than finite-field DH for the same security level? On generic elliptic curves no sub-exponential DLP algorithm is known, so a key length matching $O(\sqrt p)$ rather than $L_p[1/3]$ suffices. 256-bit ECC ≈ 3072-bit RSA/DH. (ECC is not post-quantum — Shor breaks it just as it breaks RSA.)

q: By Hasse's theorem, the order $\#E(\mathbb{F}_p)$ satisfies which bound?
text: By Hasse's theorem, the order $\#E(\mathbb{F}_p)$ satisfies which bound? Hasse: $\#E(\mathbb{F}_p)=p+1-a_p$ with $|a_p|\le 2\sqrt p$. So the curve order is approximately $p$, with a $\sqrt p$-sized fluctuation.

q: The MOV attack reduces the elliptic-curve discrete log on a curve $E/\mathbb{F}_p$ to the discrete log in which group?
text: The MOV attack reduces the elliptic-curve discrete log on a curve $E/\mathbb{F}_p$ to the discrete log in which group? The Weil/Tate pairing embeds $E[\ell]$ into $\mathbb{F}_{p^k}^\times$ where $k$ is the smallest integer with $\ell\mid p^k-1$. When $k$ is small, sub-exponential index calculus on $\mathbb{F}_{p^k}^\times$ beats $O(\sqrt p)$ on $E$. Standard curves (P-256, Curve25519) have $k$ astronomically large.

q: Why is RSA / ECC considered "quantum-broken" in principle?
text: Why is RSA / ECC considered "quantum-broken" in principle? Shor (1994) gives polynomial-time quantum algorithms for integer factorization and the discrete log in any abelian group — so RSA, DH, and ECC all fall once a sufficiently large fault-tolerant quantum machine exists. Grover gives only a quadratic speedup on generic search, far weaker.

q: The Learning With Errors (LWE) problem asks the solver to:
text: The Learning With Errors (LWE) problem asks the solver to: LWE is exactly noisy linear algebra mod $q$: without the error term you would solve for $\mathbf{s}$ by Gaussian elimination, but small Gaussian $e_i$ destroys that. Regev showed (quantum reduction) that average-case LWE is at least as hard as worst-case approximate-SVP / GapSVP.

q: Which cryptosystem was selected by NIST in 2022/2024 as the primary post-quantum key-encapsulation standard (FIPS 203)?
text: Which cryptosystem was selected by NIST in 2022/2024 as the primary post-quantum key-encapsulation standard (FIPS 203)? CRYSTALS-Kyber, a Module-LWE KEM, became FIPS 203 in 2024. SIKE was a candidate but was broken in 2022 by the Castryck–Decru attack. Classic McEliece (code-based) and HQC are alternates; NTRU is closely related to Kyber but not the chosen primary standard.

q: An interactive proof system has three properties. Which set is correct?
text: An interactive proof system has three properties. Which set is correct? Completeness (true statements are accepted by an honest prover), soundness (false statements are rejected with high probability against any prover), and zero-knowledge (the verifier learns nothing beyond the truth of the statement — formalised by transcript simulatability).

q: Schnorr's protocol proves knowledge of a discrete log $x$ with $h=g^x$. The prover sends $t=g^r$, the verifier sends a random challenge $c$, the prover replies with $s=r+cx$. The verifier accepts iff:
text: Schnorr's protocol proves knowledge of a discrete log $x$ with $h=g^x$. The prover sends $t=g^r$, the verifier sends a random challenge $c$, the prover replies with $s=r+cx$. The verifier accepts iff: $g^s = g^{r+cx} = g^r \cdot (g^x)^c = t\cdot h^c$. Soundness: a prover who can answer two distinct challenges $c\ne c'$ for the same $t$ reveals $x=(s-s')/(c-c')$. Zero-knowledge: the simulator picks $s, c$ first and sets $t = g^s/h^c$.

q: The acronym SNARK in zk-SNARK stands for which property set?
text: The acronym SNARK in zk-SNARK stands for which property set? Succinct (proof size and verification time poly-logarithmic in the witness), Non-interactive (one message from prover to verifier, often via Fiat–Shamir), Argument of Knowledge (computational soundness, with an extractor pulling out a witness from any convincing prover). Groth16 and PLONK are the workhorses.

title: Knots and links
blurb: An embedding $S^1 \hookrightarrow S^3$ up to ambient isotopy; a link is the same with finitely many disjoint circles. Orientations distinguish a knot from its mirror and reverse, and the unknot is the equivalence class containing the round circle.

title: Reidemeister moves
blurb: Two planar diagrams represent ambient-isotopic knots iff they are connected by a finite sequence of three local moves R1 (twist/untwist), R2 (poke), R3 (slide). Reidemeister's theorem turns 3D isotopy into a combinatorial rewrite system on diagrams.

title: Alexander polynomial
blurb: The first knot polynomial: $\Delta_K(t) \in \mathbb{Z}[t, t^{-1}]$ extracted from a Seifert matrix $V$ via $\det(V - tV^T)$, well-defined up to $\pm t^k$. Symmetric: $\Delta_K(t^{-1}) = \Delta_K(t)$ up to sign and a power of $t$. Detects unknotting only sometimes — fails on the Conway and Kinoshita–Terasaka knots.

title: Jones polynomial
blurb: $V_K(q) \in \mathbb{Z}[q^{1/2}, q^{-1/2}]$ defined by Kauffman's state-sum bracket plus a writhe normalization. Distinguishes the trefoil from its mirror — something Alexander cannot do — and is categorified by Khovanov homology, whose graded Euler characteristic recovers $V_K$.

title: HOMFLY polynomial
blurb: A two-variable invariant $P_K(\alpha, z) \in \mathbb{Z}[\alpha^{\pm 1}, z^{\pm 1}]$ defined by a single skein relation $\alpha P(L_+) - \alpha^{-1} P(L_-) = z\, P(L_0)$ with $P(\text{unknot}) = 1$. Specialises to Alexander at $\alpha = 1$ and to Jones at $\alpha = q^{-1}$, $z = q^{1/2} - q^{-1/2}$.

title: Quantum invariants
blurb: Each ribbon (modular tensor) category supplies a knot invariant by reading a diagram as a morphism: each crossing becomes the $R$-matrix, each cap/cup becomes evaluation/coevaluation. The Jones polynomial is the invariant for $U_q(\mathfrak{sl}_2)$ on the standard 2-dim rep; HOMFLY comes from $U_q(\mathfrak{sl}_n)$.

q: Two knots $K_0$ and $K_1$ in $S^3$ are equivalent as knots when:
text: Two knots $K_0$ and $K_1$ in $S^3$ are equivalent as knots when: Knot equivalence is ambient isotopy of $S^3$ — a continuous family of self-homeomorphisms of the ambient space starting at the identity and ending with one carrying $K_0$ onto $K_1$. Sharing an image is too strong; the other criteria are not isotopy invariants.

q: How many components does a $3$-component link have, by definition?
text: How many components does a $3$-component link have, by definition? An $n$-component link is an embedding of a disjoint union of $n$ copies of $S^1$, so a $3$-component link has $3$ components. A knot is a $1$-component link.

q: The unknot $U$ is the equivalence class of the round circle in $S^3$. Which is true about its complement $S^3 \setminus U$?
text: The unknot $U$ is the equivalence class of the round circle in $S^3$. Which is true about its complement $S^3 \setminus U$? $S^3$ is the union of two solid tori glued along their boundary; the complement of the core circle of one is the open interior of the other, so $S^3 \setminus U \cong S^1 \times \mathring{D}^2$. Its fundamental group is $\mathbb{Z}$ — generated by a meridian linking the unknot once.

q: Reidemeister's theorem states that two planar knot diagrams represent ambient-isotopic knots in $S^3$ if and only if:
text: Reidemeister's theorem states that two planar knot diagrams represent ambient-isotopic knots in $S^3$ if and only if: The theorem gives a combinatorial criterion for 3D ambient isotopy: an isotopy of the knot in $S^3$ corresponds, on a generic projection, to a finite sequence of three local moves on the diagram (plus planar isotopy of the underlying graph).

q: Which Reidemeister move changes the writhe (signed crossing count) of an oriented diagram?
text: Which Reidemeister move changes the writhe (signed crossing count) of an oriented diagram? R1 introduces or removes one crossing of fixed sign, changing the writhe by $\pm 1$. R2 introduces a $+$ and a $-$ crossing simultaneously (writhe unchanged), and R3 is a slide that preserves both the number and signs of crossings.

q: The standard 3-crossing diagram of the right-handed trefoil has all three crossings of sign $+1$. What is its writhe?
text: The standard 3-crossing diagram of the right-handed trefoil has all three crossings of sign $+1$. What is its writhe? Writhe is the sum of crossing signs over the diagram. With three positive crossings the writhe is $+3$; the left-handed mirror trefoil has writhe $-3$, and the difference is what eventually distinguishes the two via the Jones polynomial.

q: The Alexander polynomial $\Delta_K(t)$ is defined up to which ambiguity?
text: The Alexander polynomial $\Delta_K(t)$ is defined up to which ambiguity? Different Seifert surfaces give Seifert matrices that differ by elementary moves and stabilizations; the resulting determinants are equal up to multiplication by units of $\mathbb{Z}[t, t^{-1}]$, which are exactly $\pm t^k$.

q: Which symmetry does the Alexander polynomial satisfy?
text: Which symmetry does the Alexander polynomial satisfy? From $\Delta_K(t) = \det(V - tV^T)$ one computes $\Delta_K(t^{-1}) = \det(V - t^{-1} V^T) = (-t)^{-g} \det(tV - V^T) = (-t)^{-g} \Delta_K(t)$ where $g = \dim V$, so the two agree up to a unit in $\mathbb{Z}[t, t^{-1}]$.

q: The Alexander polynomial of the trefoil knot is $\Delta(t) = t - 1 + t^{-1}$ (up to units). Compute $\Delta(1)$.
text: The Alexander polynomial of the trefoil knot is $\Delta(t) = t - 1 + t^{-1}$ (up to units). Compute $\Delta(1)$. $\Delta(1) = 1 - 1 + 1 = 1$. In fact $|\Delta_K(1)| = 1$ for every knot (not link), because the Alexander matrix at $t=1$ presents $H_1$ of the knot complement, which is always $\mathbb{Z}$.

q: The Kauffman bracket $\langle \cdot \rangle$ on unoriented diagrams is built from the local skein rule $\langle L_\times \rangle = A \langle L_0 \rangle + A^{-1} \langle L_\infty \rangle$ together with the loop value $\langle \bigcirc \sqcup L \rangle = (-A^2 - A^{-2}) \langle L \rangle$. The bracket is invariant under R2 and R3 but not under R1. To fix this, the Jones polynomial uses:
text: The Kauffman bracket $\langle \cdot \rangle$ on unoriented diagrams is built from the local skein rule $\langle L_\times \rangle = A \langle L_0 \rangle + A^{-1} \langle L_\infty \rangle$ together with the loop value $\langle \bigcirc \sqcup L \rangle = (-A^2 - A^{-2}) \langle L \rangle$. The bracket is invariant under R2 and R3 but not under R1. To fix this, the Jones polynomial uses: An R1 move multiplies the bracket by $-A^{\pm 3}$ and changes the writhe by $\pm 1$. Multiplying the bracket by $(-A)^{-3w(D)}$ exactly cancels this, producing an honest knot invariant. Setting $A = q^{-1/4}$ gives the Jones polynomial in $q$.

q: Which is the Jones polynomial able to distinguish that the Alexander polynomial cannot?
text: Which is the Jones polynomial able to distinguish that the Alexander polynomial cannot? $V_{3_1}(q) = -q^{-4} + q^{-3} + q^{-1}$ for the right trefoil, while the mirror has $-q^4 + q^3 + q$. They are not equal, so Jones detects chirality. The Alexander polynomial is unchanged by mirroring (up to units) and so cannot.

q: The Jones polynomial of the unknot is $V_U(q) = 1$. Compute $V_U(2)$.
text: The Jones polynomial of the unknot is $V_U(q) = 1$. Compute $V_U(2)$. $V_U(q) \equiv 1$ as a constant polynomial, so $V_U(q) = 1$ for every value of $q$ — including $q = 2$.

q: The HOMFLY polynomial $P_K(\alpha, z)$ is determined by which two pieces of data?
text: The HOMFLY polynomial $P_K(\alpha, z)$ is determined by which two pieces of data? HOMFLY is uniquely characterised by the value $1$ on the unknot together with the three-term skein relating the two crossings $L_\pm$ and the smoothing $L_0$ at any chosen crossing. From these alone the polynomial is computable on any diagram by skein induction.

q: Under which substitution does HOMFLY specialise to the Jones polynomial $V_K(q)$?
text: Under which substitution does HOMFLY specialise to the Jones polynomial $V_K(q)$? Substituting $\alpha = q^{-1}$ and $z = q^{1/2} - q^{-1/2}$ into the HOMFLY skein recovers exactly the Jones skein $q^{-1} V(L_+) - q V(L_-) = (q^{1/2} - q^{-1/2}) V(L_0)$. Setting $\alpha = 1$ instead recovers Alexander.

q: HOMFLY of the unknot is $P_U(\alpha, z) = 1$. What is its value at $\alpha = 2$, $z = 3$?
text: HOMFLY of the unknot is $P_U(\alpha, z) = 1$. What is its value at $\alpha = 2$, $z = 3$? $P_U \equiv 1$ is constant, so its value is $1$ regardless of $\alpha$ and $z$. The skein relation propagates the unknot value to every other link.

q: In the Reshetikhin–Turaev construction, an oriented tangle diagram is read top-to-bottom and each piece contributes a morphism in a ribbon category. A positive crossing contributes:
text: In the Reshetikhin–Turaev construction, an oriented tangle diagram is read top-to-bottom and each piece contributes a morphism in a ribbon category. A positive crossing contributes: Crossings are precisely the braidings of the ribbon category — the $R$-matrix $R_{V,V}$ for a positive crossing of two strands labelled $V$, and $R^{-1}$ for the negative crossing. Caps and cups give evaluation and coevaluation; vertical strands give identities.

q: Which ribbon category, applied to the standard $2$-dimensional representation, produces the Jones polynomial?
text: Which ribbon category, applied to the standard $2$-dimensional representation, produces the Jones polynomial? The Jones polynomial is the Reshetikhin–Turaev invariant for the quantum group $U_q(\mathfrak{sl}_2)$ acting on its $2$-dimensional standard rep. Replacing $\mathfrak{sl}_2$ by $\mathfrak{sl}_n$ on the standard $n$-dim rep gives the $\mathfrak{sl}_n$ HOMFLY specialisation.

q: The Yang–Baxter equation $R_{12} R_{13} R_{23} = R_{23} R_{13} R_{12}$ on $V^{\otimes 3}$ corresponds to which Reidemeister move?
text: The Yang–Baxter equation $R_{12} R_{13} R_{23} = R_{23} R_{13} R_{12}$ on $V^{\otimes 3}$ corresponds to which Reidemeister move? R3 is the slide of a strand across a crossing — three braidings on three strands rearrange in two ways, and equality of the resulting compositions is exactly the Yang–Baxter equation. R2 corresponds to $R \circ R^{-1} = \mathrm{id}$; R1 is fixed up by the ribbon twist.

title: The Ricci flow equation
blurb: Hamilton's equation $\partial_t g_{ij}=-2R_{ij}$ evolves a Riemannian metric in the direction opposite its Ricci curvature. In harmonic coordinates the principal symbol is the rough Laplacian, so Ricci flow is a (weakly parabolic, gauge-degenerate) heat equation for the metric.

title: Short-time existence and the DeTurck trick
blurb: On a closed manifold $(M,g_0)$ Ricci flow has a unique smooth solution on a maximal interval $[0,T)$. Diffeomorphism invariance breaks parabolicity, but DeTurck's trick adds a Lie-derivative gauge term $\mathcal{L}_W g$ to obtain a strictly parabolic system whose solutions push back to genuine Ricci flows.

title: Singularity formation and ancient solutions
blurb: If $T<\infty$ the curvature blows up: $\sup_M|\mathrm{Rm}|(t)\to\infty$. Hamilton classifies singularities as Type I, IIa/b, or III by the rate. Parabolic rescaling near a singular point produces a $\kappa$-noncollapsed limit on $(-\infty,0]$ — an ancient solution — whose tangent flow encodes the local model.

title: Ricci solitons
blurb: A self-similar solution $g(t)=\sigma(t)\,\phi_t^*g_0$ satisfies $\mathrm{Ric}+\tfrac12\mathcal{L}_X g=\lambda g$ for a vector field $X$ and constant $\lambda$ (shrinking, steady, expanding for $\lambda>0,=0,<0$). Bryant's rotationally symmetric steady soliton, the cigar, and the Kähler–Ricci soliton on $\mathbb{CP}^2\#\overline{\mathbb{CP}^2}$ are the canonical examples.

title: Surgery on $\epsilon$-necks
blurb: Hamilton–Perelman surgery: when a high-curvature region develops as an $\epsilon$-thin neck modelled on a cylinder $S^{n-1}\times\mathbb{R}$, cut along the neck, glue in two standard caps, and continue the flow on the modified manifold. Finitely many surgeries suffice on any time interval, and the surgery times have no accumulation point.

title: Poincaré conjecture and geometrisation
blurb: Perelman's $\mathcal{F}$- and $\mathcal{W}$-entropy and the reduced volume control noncollapsing and singularity formation, so Ricci flow with surgery on a closed simply connected $3$-manifold becomes extinct in finite time, proving the Poincaré conjecture. The same machinery decomposes any closed $3$-manifold into the eight Thurston geometric pieces.

q: Hamilton's Ricci flow equation reads:
text: Hamilton's Ricci flow equation reads: The flow is $\partial_t g_{ij}=-2R_{ij}$. The minus sign tries to round out positive curvature and stretch out negative curvature; the factor of 2 matches the Bianchi identity normalisation that makes the equation parabolic. The metric evolves opposite to the Ricci curvature.

q: On an Einstein metric $\mathrm{Ric}=\lambda g$, the Ricci flow starting from $g_0$ is given by:
text: On an Einstein metric $\mathrm{Ric}=\lambda g$, the Ricci flow starting from $g_0$ is given by: If $g(t)=\rho(t)g_0$ then $R_{ij}(g(t))=R_{ij}(g_0)=\lambda g_0{}_{ij}$ (Ricci is invariant under constant rescaling). So $\rho'(t)=-2\lambda$, giving $\rho(t)=1-2\lambda t$. Spheres ($\lambda>0$) shrink to a point at $t=1/(2\lambda)$; hyperbolic metrics ($\lambda<0$) expand. Try the ansatz $g(t)=\rho(t)g_0$ and substitute.

q: Why is Ricci flow described as a 'heat equation for the metric'?
text: Why is Ricci flow described as a 'heat equation for the metric'? In harmonic coordinates $-2R_{ij}=\Delta g_{ij}+Q(g,\partial g)$ where $Q$ collects nonlinear lower-order terms. So Ricci flow is a quasilinear parabolic system whose principal symbol is the rough Laplacian — that is the precise sense in which it is a heat equation for $g$. Look at the linearisation in a good coordinate gauge.

q: Hamilton's short-time existence theorem says that for any smooth initial metric $g_0$ on a closed manifold:
text: Hamilton's short-time existence theorem says that for any smooth initial metric $g_0$ on a closed manifold: On a closed manifold there is a unique smooth solution on some $[0,T)$ with $T>0$, and $T$ is maximal: either $T=\infty$ or curvature blows up as $t\to T$. The proof requires repairing parabolicity via DeTurck. Compactness and parabolic-system theory give existence + uniqueness up to a maximal time.

q: Why is the bare Ricci flow equation NOT strictly parabolic?
text: Why is the bare Ricci flow equation NOT strictly parabolic? Because Ricci flow is invariant under pull-back by diffeomorphisms, infinitesimal gauge changes $\mathcal{L}_X g$ lie in the kernel of the linearised principal symbol. So the system is only weakly parabolic; strict parabolicity (and standard PDE theory) fails until the gauge is fixed. If $\phi_t$ is a 1-parameter family of diffeomorphisms, $\phi_t^* g(t)$ also solves Ricci flow.

q: DeTurck's trick repairs parabolicity by:
text: DeTurck's trick repairs parabolicity by: DeTurck adds $\mathcal{L}_W g$ where $W^k = g^{ij}(\Gamma^k_{ij}-\tilde\Gamma^k_{ij})$ relative to a fixed background metric. The modified flow is strictly parabolic and has a unique solution by standard theory; pulling back along the diffeomorphisms generated by $-W$ recovers an honest Ricci flow. The added term is a Lie derivative chosen to cancel the gauge degeneracy.

q: If a Ricci flow on a closed manifold reaches its maximal time $T<\infty$, what must happen?
text: If a Ricci flow on a closed manifold reaches its maximal time $T<\infty$, what must happen? Hamilton's continuation criterion: a smooth Ricci flow on a closed manifold extends past $T$ unless $\sup |\mathrm{Rm}|\to\infty$ as $t\to T$. Curvature blow-up is the only obstruction to long-time existence. Smooth solutions extend past $T$ unless a quantity blows up.

q: A Type I singularity is one where, near the singular time $T$:
text: A Type I singularity is one where, near the singular time $T$: $|\mathrm{Rm}|$ scales like $1/\text{(distance to singular time)}$ under parabolic rescaling. Type I means this rate is achieved as an upper bound; Type II is supercritical (e.g. degenerate neckpinches); Type III is for immortal flows. Type I = the natural parabolic blow-up rate.

q: Parabolic rescaling about a singular point produces, in the limit, a solution defined for all $t\in(-\infty,0]$. Such a solution is called:
text: Parabolic rescaling about a singular point produces, in the limit, a solution defined for all $t\in(-\infty,0]$. Such a solution is called: An ancient solution is a Ricci flow defined on $(-\infty,T_0]$. Hamilton and Perelman showed that blow-up limits at Type I/II singularities are $\kappa$-noncollapsed ancient solutions, and tangent flows of these encode the local geometry of the singularity. It exists arbitrarily far back in time.

q: A Ricci soliton is a metric $g_0$ together with a vector field $X$ and constant $\lambda$ such that:
text: A Ricci soliton is a metric $g_0$ together with a vector field $X$ and constant $\lambda$ such that: The soliton equation $\mathrm{Ric}+\tfrac12\mathcal{L}_X g=\lambda g$ encodes self-similarity: $g(t)=\sigma(t)\phi_t^* g_0$ with $\sigma(t)=1-2\lambda t$ and $\phi_t$ the flow of $X/\sigma(t)$ solves Ricci flow. Einstein metrics are the special case $X=0$. The metric should evolve only by scaling and pull-back along $X$'s flow.

q: A soliton is called shrinking, steady, or expanding according to the sign of $\lambda$. Which is which?
text: A soliton is called shrinking, steady, or expanding according to the sign of $\lambda$. Which is which? $\lambda>0$ gives $\sigma(t)$ decreasing — the metric shrinks (and goes singular at $t=1/(2\lambda)$); $\lambda=0$ gives $\sigma\equiv 1$ — translating soliton; $\lambda<0$ gives $\sigma$ increasing — the metric expands. Recall $\sigma(t)=1-2\lambda t$.

q: Which of the following is a steady Ricci soliton?
text: Which of the following is a steady Ricci soliton? Hamilton's cigar $g=\frac{dx^2+dy^2}{1+x^2+y^2}$ on $\mathbb{R}^2$ is the canonical 2-D steady gradient soliton. The round sphere is shrinking ($\lambda>0$, Einstein); hyperbolic space is expanding ($\lambda<0$); flat $\mathbb{R}^n$ is static (also a degenerate soliton). Steady = $\lambda=0$, gradient soliton on a non-compact surface.

q: An $\epsilon$-neck in a 3-D Ricci flow is a region of the manifold which, after rescaling so that the curvature is order 1, is $\epsilon$-close in $C^k$ to:
text: An $\epsilon$-neck in a 3-D Ricci flow is a region of the manifold which, after rescaling so that the curvature is order 1, is $\epsilon$-close in $C^k$ to: An $\epsilon$-neck is a region modelled on $S^{n-1}\times[-\epsilon^{-1},\epsilon^{-1}]$ (in 3-D: $S^2\times\mathbb{R}$) after parabolic rescaling. Singular regions in 3-D Ricci flow develop $\epsilon$-necks generically. Necks look long and thin — one dimension is stretched.

q: Surgery on an $\epsilon$-neck consists of:
text: Surgery on an $\epsilon$-neck consists of: Cut along a cross-sectional $S^2$ inside the neck and glue a standard cap (a $D^3$ with a controlled metric) onto each side. The resulting manifold may have more components, but each surgery is a simple topological operation: removal of a $S^2\times I$ and attachment of two 3-balls. Topologically: cut $S^2\times\mathbb{R}$ at a slice, cap off both pieces with $D^3$.

q: On any finite time interval, Hamilton–Perelman surgery requires:
text: On any finite time interval, Hamilton–Perelman surgery requires: Perelman's noncollapsing and curvature estimates ensure that surgery scales stay bounded below on each finite interval and that volume drops by a fixed amount per surgery, so surgery times are isolated and finite-in-number on any $[0,T]$. Volume bounds and noncollapsing limit how often surgery can fire.

q: Perelman's proof of the Poincaré conjecture shows that on any closed simply connected 3-manifold $M$, Ricci flow with surgery:
text: Perelman's proof of the Poincaré conjecture shows that on any closed simply connected 3-manifold $M$, Ricci flow with surgery: Perelman proved that Ricci flow with surgery on a closed simply connected 3-manifold becomes extinct in finite time. The path of surgeries shows $M$ is a connected sum of finitely many $S^3$'s and $S^2\times S^1$'s; simple connectivity forces $M\cong S^3$, proving Poincaré. Extinction means $M$ becomes empty after finitely many surgeries.

q: Two of Perelman's monotone quantities along Ricci flow are:
text: Two of Perelman's monotone quantities along Ricci flow are: The $\mathcal{F}$-functional is monotone along Ricci flow (steady-soliton energy); the $\mathcal{W}$-entropy is monotone along the flow combined with rescaling and gives the noncollapsing theorem. The reduced volume is the geometric counterpart. Both are functionals on metrics introduced specifically for Ricci flow.

q: Thurston's geometrisation conjecture, proved by Perelman, says every closed orientable 3-manifold decomposes (along spheres and tori) into pieces each carrying one of:
text: Thurston's geometrisation conjecture, proved by Perelman, says every closed orientable 3-manifold decomposes (along spheres and tori) into pieces each carrying one of: Thurston's eight model geometries are the maximal simply connected homogeneous 3-spaces with cocompact isometry group. Geometrisation says every prime 3-manifold is built from pieces locally isometric to one of these; Ricci flow with surgery realises the decomposition geometrically. The list includes $S^3$, $\mathbb{E}^3$, $\mathbb{H}^3$, $S^2\times\mathbb{R}$, $\mathbb{H}^2\times\mathbb{R}$, $\widetilde{SL_2\mathbb{R}}$, Nil, Sol.

title: Topological $K^0(X)$ from vector bundles
blurb: $K^0(X)$ is the Grothendieck group of the abelian monoid $\mathrm{Vect}_\mathbb{C}(X)$ of complex vector bundles on $X$ under direct sum, formal differences $[E]-[F]$. Tensor product makes it a commutative ring; pullback makes it a contravariant functor; rank gives a ring homomorphism $K^0(X)\to\mathbb{Z}$ whose kernel is the reduced theory $\widetilde K^0(X)$.

title: Grothendieck group $K_0$ of an exact category
blurb: For any exact (or abelian) category $\mathcal{C}$, $K_0(\mathcal{C})$ is the free abelian group on isomorphism classes $[X]$ modulo $[B]=[A]+[C]$ for every short exact sequence $0\to A\to B\to C\to 0$ — Quillen's $K_0$. For $\mathcal{C}=\mathrm{Vect}(X)$ the relation reduces to $[E\oplus F]=[E]+[F]$ since bundles are split-exact, recovering $K^0(X)$. For the category of finitely generated projective $R$-modules this is $K_0(R)$.

title: Bott periodicity
blurb: Complex topological K-theory is $2$-periodic: $K^n(X)\cong K^{n+2}(X)$, with the iso induced by external product with the Bott class $\beta=[\mathcal{O}(1)]-[\mathbf 1]\in\widetilde K^0(S^2)$. Real K-theory $KO$ is $8$-periodic. The periodicity makes $K^*$ a $\mathbb{Z}/2$-graded cohomology theory and is the engine behind the Atiyah–Singer index theorem.

title: Chern character
blurb: The Chern character $\mathrm{ch}\colon K^0(X)\to H^{\text{even}}(X;\mathbb{Q})$, $\mathrm{ch}(E)=\sum_i e^{x_i}$ on Chern roots, is a ring homomorphism — additive on $\oplus$ and multiplicative on $\otimes$. After tensoring with $\mathbb{Q}$ it becomes an iso $K^0(X)\otimes\mathbb{Q}\xrightarrow{\sim} H^{\text{even}}(X;\mathbb{Q})$ for finite CW $X$. Rationally K-theory carries no information beyond ordinary cohomology; the integral torsion is the new invariant.

title: Algebraic K-theory $K_n(R)$
blurb: Quillen's higher K-groups $K_n(R) = \pi_n(BGL(R)^+)$ via the plus-construction on $BGL_\infty(R)$. Low-degree concrete pictures: $K_0(R)$ is the projective class group; $K_1(R) = GL_\infty(R)^{\mathrm{ab}}$, agreeing with $\det$ for fields and with units for commutative local rings; $K_2(R)$ is the Steinberg group's centre, encoding universal central extensions and explicit symbols $\{a,b\}$.

title: Applications: index theorem & K-theory of $\mathcal{O}_F$
blurb: Atiyah–Singer: an elliptic operator $D$ on a closed manifold $M$ has a topological symbol class $[\sigma(D)]\in K^0(T^*M)$, and $\ind(D)=\langle\mathrm{ch}([\sigma(D)])\smile\mathrm{Td}(TM\otimes\mathbb{C}),[T^*M]\rangle$. On the algebraic side, Borel computed $K_n(\mathcal{O}_F)\otimes\mathbb{Q}$ for a number field $F$: ranks given by the number of real and complex places, with Quillen's calculation of $K_n(\mathbb{F}_q)$ as input.

q: Why must we pass to the Grothendieck group rather than just take isomorphism classes of vector bundles?
text: Why must we pass to the Grothendieck group rather than just take isomorphism classes of vector bundles? $(\mathrm{Vect}_\mathbb{C}(X),\oplus)$ is an abelian monoid; the universal map to a group adjoins formal inverses, identifying $[E]=[F]$ exactly when $E\oplus G\cong F\oplus G$ for some $G$ (stable equivalence). Even on $S^2$, $E\oplus\mathbf{1}\cong F\oplus\mathbf{1}$ does not force $E\cong F$.

q: What is $K^0(\mathrm{pt})$?
text: What is $K^0(\mathrm{pt})$? $\mathrm{Vect}_\mathbb{C}(\mathrm{pt})\cong\mathbb{N}$ via dimension, whose Grothendieck group is $\mathbb{Z}$. The rank map $K^0(X)\to K^0(\mathrm{pt})=\mathbb{Z}$ is the augmentation. Vector bundles on a point are just vector spaces, classified by dimension.

q: Which structure on $K^0(X)$ comes from the tensor product of bundles?
text: Which structure on $K^0(X)$ comes from the tensor product of bundles? $\oplus$ gives the addition, $\otimes$ gives the multiplication, and the trivial line bundle is the multiplicative unit. Pullback $f^*$ is a ring map, making $K^0$ a contravariant functor to commutative rings. $\otimes$ distributes over $\oplus$ and the trivial line bundle is a unit.

q: In Quillen's $K_0(\mathcal{C})$ of an exact category, what relation does each short exact sequence $0\to A\to B\to C\to 0$ impose?
text: In Quillen's $K_0(\mathcal{C})$ of an exact category, what relation does each short exact sequence $0\to A\to B\to C\to 0$ impose? $K_0(\mathcal{C})$ is the free abelian group on isomorphism classes modulo $[B]-[A]-[C]$ for every short exact sequence; it linearises additivity through extensions. Think of an exact sequence as splitting $B$ into $A$ and $C$ at the level of classes.
