q: What is $K_0(\mathbb{Z})$, the Grothendieck group of finitely generated projective $\mathbb{Z}$-modules?
text: What is $K_0(\mathbb{Z})$, the Grothendieck group of finitely generated projective $\mathbb{Z}$-modules? Projectives over a PID are free, so the rank map is an iso $K_0(\mathbb{Z})\xrightarrow{\sim}\mathbb{Z}$. More generally $K_0(\mathcal{O}_F)=\mathbb{Z}\oplus\mathrm{Cl}(F)$ for a Dedekind domain. Over a PID every finitely generated projective is free, classified by rank.

q: Why does $K_0(\mathrm{Vect}(X))$ recover $K^0(X)$ even though the definition references all short exact sequences?
text: Why does $K_0(\mathrm{Vect}(X))$ recover $K^0(X)$ even though the definition references all short exact sequences? On a paracompact base, any short exact sequence of vector bundles splits (pick a metric and take the orthogonal complement of the sub-bundle). So the only relation surviving in $K_0$ is direct-sum additivity, matching the naive Grothendieck group of the monoid. Choose a Hermitian metric; the orthogonal complement provides a splitting.

q: Bott periodicity says complex topological K-theory satisfies which isomorphism?
text: Bott periodicity says complex topological K-theory satisfies which isomorphism? Complex K-theory is $2$-periodic: $K^n(X)\cong K^{n+2}(X)$ via external product with the Bott class $\beta\in\widetilde K^0(S^2)$. Real K-theory $KO$ is $8$-periodic — the genuine surprise. The Bott class lives on $S^2$, signalling the period.

q: What is the Bott class $\beta\in\widetilde K^0(S^2)$?
text: What is the Bott class $\beta\in\widetilde K^0(S^2)$? $\beta=[\mathcal{O}(1)]-[\mathbf 1]\in\widetilde K^0(S^2)$ generates a copy of $\mathbb{Z}$. Multiplication by $\beta$ is the Bott periodicity isomorphism $\widetilde K^0(X)\xrightarrow{\sim}\widetilde K^0(\Sigma^2 X)$. It is the reduced class of the Hopf line bundle on $\mathbb{CP}^1=S^2$.

q: Bott's original (1959) theorem on the homotopy groups of $U=\varinjlim U(n)$ states which periodicity?
text: Bott's original (1959) theorem on the homotopy groups of $U=\varinjlim U(n)$ states which periodicity? Bott proved $\Omega U\simeq\mathbb{Z}\times BU$, hence $\pi_n(U)\cong\pi_{n-1}(\mathbb{Z}\times BU)$, giving the $2$-periodic pattern $\dots,0,\mathbb{Z},0,\mathbb{Z},\dots$ — the homotopical shadow of the Bott class on $S^2$. $\pi_n(U)=\mathbb{Z}$ for $n$ odd and $0$ for $n$ even (and positive).

q: On formal Chern roots $x_i$ of a bundle $E$, the Chern character is given by which formula?
text: On formal Chern roots $x_i$ of a bundle $E$, the Chern character is given by which formula? $\mathrm{ch}(E)=\sum_i e^{x_i}=\mathrm{rk}(E)+c_1(E)+\tfrac{1}{2}(c_1^2-2c_2)+\cdots$. The first option is the total Chern class; the last is the Todd class. It must be additive on $\oplus$ and multiplicative on $\otimes$ — only the exponential does both.

q: Why does $\mathrm{ch}\colon K^0(X)\otimes\mathbb{Q}\to H^{\text{even}}(X;\mathbb{Q})$ become an isomorphism for finite CW $X$?
text: Why does $\mathrm{ch}\colon K^0(X)\otimes\mathbb{Q}\to H^{\text{even}}(X;\mathbb{Q})$ become an isomorphism for finite CW $X$? The Atiyah–Hirzebruch spectral sequence $H^p(X;K^q(\mathrm{pt}))\Rightarrow K^{p+q}(X)$ collapses after $\otimes\mathbb{Q}$, and $\mathrm{ch}$ realises the resulting iso. So K-theory and cohomology agree rationally; integrally K-theory carries finer torsion. Rationally any cohomology theory is determined by its coefficients.

q: How does the Chern character behave on tensor products?
text: How does the Chern character behave on tensor products? Since $e^{x_i+y_j}=e^{x_i}e^{y_j}$, $\mathrm{ch}(E\otimes F)=(\sum e^{x_i})(\sum e^{y_j})=\mathrm{ch}(E)\smile\mathrm{ch}(F)$. Combined with additivity on $\oplus$, this makes $\mathrm{ch}$ a ring map. Tensor adds Chern roots: $(E\otimes F)$ has roots $x_i+y_j$.

q: For a commutative ring $R$, what is $K_1(R)$ as defined by Quillen's $+$-construction $K_n(R)=\pi_n(BGL(R)^+)$?
text: For a commutative ring $R$, what is $K_1(R)$ as defined by Quillen's $+$-construction $K_n(R)=\pi_n(BGL(R)^+)$? By Whitehead's lemma the commutator subgroup of $GL_\infty(R)$ is $E(R)$, so $K_1(R)=GL_\infty(R)^{\mathrm{ab}}=GL_\infty(R)/E(R)$. Over a field this is $\det\colon GL_\infty\twoheadrightarrow F^\times$; over a commutative local ring the determinant gives $K_1(R)\cong R^\times$. $\pi_1$ of $BG^+$ is the abelianisation of $G$, and the elementary subgroup $E(R)$ is the commutator subgroup of $GL_\infty(R)$ (Whitehead's lemma).

q: What is $K_2(R)$ in Milnor's presentation?
text: What is $K_2(R)$ in Milnor's presentation? $K_2(R)=\ker(\mathrm{St}(R)\twoheadrightarrow E(R))$, the centre of the Steinberg group. Matsumoto's theorem identifies $K_2$ of a field with $F^\times\otimes F^\times/\langle a\otimes(1-a)\rangle$, the Milnor symbols. $\mathrm{St}(R)$ is the universal central extension of $E(R)$.

q: Why must Quillen apply the $+$-construction to $BGL_\infty(R)$ rather than just take its homotopy groups?
text: Why must Quillen apply the $+$-construction to $BGL_\infty(R)$ rather than just take its homotopy groups? $BG$ is a $K(G,1)$, so its homotopy is concentrated in $\pi_1=G$. The plus-construction kills the perfect commutator subgroup $E(R)\subset GL_\infty(R)$ in $\pi_1$, leaving the abelianisation, while preserving homology — and creates higher homotopy. The result $\pi_n(BGL(R)^+)$ is Quillen's $K_n(R)$. We want $K_1=GL_\infty^{\mathrm{ab}}$, but $\pi_1(BG)=G$.

q: In the Atiyah–Singer index theorem, the analytic index $\ind(D)=\dim\ker D-\dim\mathrm{coker}\,D$ is computed as which topological pairing?
text: In the Atiyah–Singer index theorem, the analytic index $\ind(D)=\dim\ker D-\dim\mathrm{coker}\,D$ is computed as which topological pairing? Atiyah–Singer: $\ind(D)=\langle\mathrm{ch}([\sigma(D)])\smile\mathrm{Td}(TM\otimes\mathbb{C}),[T^*M]\rangle$. The symbol class $[\sigma(D)]\in K^0(T^*M)$ encodes $D$ topologically; Chern character converts to rational cohomology and the Todd class corrects for the integrality. Symbol $\to$ K-class on $T^*M$, then push to a number via Chern character and the Todd genus.

q: What is the rank of $K_n(\mathcal{O}_F)$ for a number field $F$ with $r_1$ real and $r_2$ complex places, in Borel's calculation?
text: What is the rank of $K_n(\mathcal{O}_F)$ for a number field $F$ with $r_1$ real and $r_2$ complex places, in Borel's calculation? Borel: $K_{4k+1}(\mathcal{O}_F)\otimes\mathbb{Q}$ has rank $r_1+r_2$ and $K_{4k+3}(\mathcal{O}_F)\otimes\mathbb{Q}$ has rank $r_2$ for $k\ge 1$, while even-degree higher K-groups have torsion only. $K_1(\mathcal{O}_F)=\mathcal{O}_F^\times$ recovers Dirichlet's unit theorem (rank $r_1+r_2-1$, plus roots of unity). The ranks of higher K-groups of $\mathcal{O}_F$ are governed by Dirichlet's unit-theorem pattern, alternating between $r_1+r_2$ and $r_2$.

q: Quillen's calculation of $K_n(\mathbb{F}_q)$ gives which answer for $n\ge 1$?
text: Quillen's calculation of $K_n(\mathbb{F}_q)$ gives which answer for $n\ge 1$? Quillen (1972) computed $K_{2k-1}(\mathbb{F}_q)\cong\mathbb{Z}/(q^k-1)$ and $K_{2k}(\mathbb{F}_q)=0$ for $k\ge 1$, with $K_0(\mathbb{F}_q)=\mathbb{Z}$ and $K_1(\mathbb{F}_q)=\mathbb{F}_q^\times$. Borel's theorem combines this with localisation sequences to get $K_n(\mathcal{O}_F)$. $K$-theory of a finite field is purely torsion in odd degrees and zero in positive even degrees.

title: The index theorem
blurb: For an elliptic differential operator $D\colon\Gamma(E)\to\Gamma(F)$ on a closed manifold $M$, the analytic index $\ind(D)=\dim\ker D-\dim\mathrm{coker}\,D$ is finite and equals a topological invariant determined by the symbol class $[\sigma(D)]\in K^0(T^*M)$ alone. The theorem unifies Hirzebruch–Riemann–Roch, Gauss–Bonnet–Chern, and the signature theorem as instances of one cohomological pairing.

title: Elliptic operators and Fredholm property
blurb: A linear differential operator $D=\sum_{|\alpha|\le m}a_\alpha(x)\partial^\alpha$ has principal symbol $\sigma_m(D)(x,\xi)=\sum_{|\alpha|=m}a_\alpha(x)(i\xi)^\alpha$, a bundle map $\pi^*E\to\pi^*F$ on $T^*M$; ellipticity demands $\sigma_m(D)(x,\xi)$ invertible for $\xi\ne 0$. On a closed manifold, ellipticity plus elliptic regularity give a Fredholm extension between Sobolev spaces $H^s\to H^{s-m}$ with finite-dimensional kernel and cokernel.

title: The topological index via $\mathrm{ch}\cdot\mathrm{Td}$
blurb: The symbol of an elliptic operator defines a compactly supported K-class $[\sigma(D)]\in K^0(T^*M)$. The topological index is $\ind_{\mathrm{top}}(D)=\bigl\langle\mathrm{ch}([\sigma(D)])\smile\mathrm{Td}(TM\otimes\mathbb{C}),\,[T^*M]\bigr\rangle$, where the Todd class $\mathrm{Td}=\prod x_i/(1-e^{-x_i})$ corrects for the non-triviality of $T^*M$ as a K-orientation. Atiyah–Singer asserts $\ind(D)=\ind_{\mathrm{top}}(D)$.

title: Classical specialisations
blurb: Three flagship special cases: Gauss–Bonnet–Chern picks $D=d+d^*$ on even-vs-odd forms, computes $\ind(D)=\chi(M)=\int_M e(TM)$; Hirzebruch signature picks $D$ on self-dual vs anti-self-dual forms, gives $\sigma(M)=\int_M L(TM)$ via the Hirzebruch $L$-genus; Hirzebruch–Riemann–Roch picks $D=\bar\partial$ on a holomorphic bundle $E\to X$ over a complex manifold, yielding $\chi(X,E)=\int_X\mathrm{ch}(E)\cdot\mathrm{Td}(TX)$.

title: Dirac operators and the $\hat A$-genus
blurb: On a spin manifold $M^{2n}$ the spin bundle splits $S=S^+\oplus S^-$ and the Dirac operator $D\colon\Gamma(S^+)\to\Gamma(S^-)$ is elliptic. The index theorem reads $\ind(D)=\int_M\hat A(TM)$, with the $\hat A$-genus $\hat A=\prod (x_i/2)/\sinh(x_i/2)$. Lichnerowicz: positive scalar curvature forces $\ker D=0$ and hence $\hat A(M)=0$; integrality of $\hat A$ on spin manifolds is the first obstruction it produces.

title: Heat kernel, families, equivariant, anomalies
blurb: The McKean–Singer formula $\ind(D)=\tr(e^{-tD^*D})-\tr(e^{-tDD^*})$ is independent of $t>0$; the small-$t$ asymptotics of the heat kernel produce the index density locally, giving a proof that requires no K-theory. The families version replaces an integer by a class in $K^0(B)$ when $D$ varies over a base; the equivariant version refines $\ind(D)$ to a virtual character of a compact Lie group action; in physics, the chiral anomaly and instanton number are direct readings of Dirac indices on curved or gauged backgrounds.

q: What does the analytic index $\ind(D)$ of an elliptic operator $D$ count?
text: What does the analytic index $\ind(D)$ of an elliptic operator $D$ count? $\ind(D)=\dim\ker D-\dim\mathrm{coker}\,D$. Both summands are finite when $D$ is elliptic on a closed manifold; the difference is a deformation-stable integer. It is a signed count of obstructions: solutions minus obstructions to surjectivity.

q: Atiyah–Singer asserts that $\ind(D)$ depends only on which datum?
text: Atiyah–Singer asserts that $\ind(D)$ depends only on which datum? The index is a homotopy invariant of the symbol; it only sees the K-theory class $[\sigma(D)]\in K^0(T^*M)$, not the operator itself. Lower-order perturbations leave the index unchanged; only the leading symbol matters.

q: Why is invariance under deformation a non-trivial fact?
text: Why is invariance under deformation a non-trivial fact? Continuous families of Fredholm operators have constant index even when individual kernel and cokernel dimensions jump — the jumps cancel. This stability is what lets the analytic index match a topological invariant. Eigenvalues can cross zero; one of $\ker, \mathrm{coker}$ may grow while the other shrinks by the same amount.

q: An order-$m$ operator $D=\sum_{|\alpha|\le m}a_\alpha(x)\partial^\alpha$ is elliptic when which condition holds?
text: An order-$m$ operator $D=\sum_{|\alpha|\le m}a_\alpha(x)\partial^\alpha$ is elliptic when which condition holds? Ellipticity is the invertibility of the principal symbol away from the zero section of $T^*M$. The Laplacian $-\Delta$ has $\sigma_2=|\xi|^2$, vanishing only at $\xi=0$ — hence elliptic; the heat operator $\partial_t-\Delta$ has $\sigma_2=|\xi|^2$ which vanishes when $\xi=0$ but the time covector is nonzero, making it parabolic, not elliptic. Look at the leading-order homogeneous part viewed as a function on $T^*M\setminus 0$.

q: Why does ellipticity force $\dim\ker D<\infty$ and $\dim\mathrm{coker}\,D<\infty$ on a closed manifold?
text: Why does ellipticity force $\dim\ker D<\infty$ and $\dim\mathrm{coker}\,D<\infty$ on a closed manifold? On a closed manifold, an elliptic operator admits a pseudodifferential parametrix with $QD=I-S$, $DQ=I-T$ where $S,T$ are smoothing (compact between any Sobolev spaces). This identifies $D$ as a Fredholm map between Sobolev spaces. A parametrix $Q$ inverts $D$ modulo compact errors; Fredholmness then follows.

q: Elliptic regularity says: if $Du=f$ with $f\in H^k$, then …
text: Elliptic regularity says: if $Du=f$ with $f\in H^k$, then … Elliptic regularity: $f\in H^k\Rightarrow u\in H^{k+m}$. Iterating, $f$ smooth implies $u$ smooth. Combined with Fredholmness, this means $\ker D$ consists of smooth sections, so the analytic index is finite. Solving an order-$m$ equation should let you integrate the equation $m$ times.

q: What is the K-theory home of the symbol class of an elliptic operator on $M$?
text: What is the K-theory home of the symbol class of an elliptic operator on $M$? $\sigma(D)$ defines an iso between pulled-back bundles outside the zero section in $T^*M$, hence a compactly supported K-class $[\sigma(D)]\in K^0(T^*M)$. This is the entry point for the topological index. The symbol is a bundle map on $T^*M$, an isomorphism off the zero section.

q: The topological index is given by which cohomology pairing?
text: The topological index is given by which cohomology pairing? Atiyah–Singer's index formula: $\ind_{\mathrm{top}}(D)=\int_{T^*M}\mathrm{ch}([\sigma(D)])\cdot\mathrm{Td}(TM_\mathbb{C})$. The Todd class is the K-theoretic Thom-class correction translating into ordinary cohomology. The Todd class corrects for K-orientation when integrating over $T^*M$.

q: Why does the Todd class $\mathrm{Td}$ appear in the formula?
text: Why does the Todd class $\mathrm{Td}$ appear in the formula? The Riemann–Roch defect: the K-theory pushforward and the ordinary-cohomology pushforward disagree by $\mathrm{Td}(TM_\mathbb{C})$. Matching them is exactly the Riemann–Roch theorem; Atiyah–Singer is its index-theoretic incarnation. Both $\mathrm{ch}$ and the Thom isomorphism need to be reconciled with each other.

q: Applied to the operator $D=d+d^*\colon\Omega^{\mathrm{even}}\to\Omega^{\mathrm{odd}}$ on a closed oriented manifold, the index theorem recovers …
text: Applied to the operator $D=d+d^*\colon\Omega^{\mathrm{even}}\to\Omega^{\mathrm{odd}}$ on a closed oriented manifold, the index theorem recovers … $\ind(d+d^*)=\sum_k(-1)^k\dim H^k(M)=\chi(M)$. The right-hand topological invariant integrates the Pfaffian/Euler form, recovering Gauss–Bonnet–Chern. $\ker(d+d^*)$ on even forms is even-dimensional harmonic cohomology by Hodge theory.

q: On a holomorphic vector bundle $E\to X$ over a closed complex manifold, $\ind(\bar\partial_E)$ equals …
text: On a holomorphic vector bundle $E\to X$ over a closed complex manifold, $\ind(\bar\partial_E)$ equals … $\ind(\bar\partial_E)=\sum_k(-1)^k\dim H^k(X,E)$, the holomorphic Euler characteristic. Atiyah–Singer specialises to $\chi(X,E)=\int_X\mathrm{ch}(E)\cdot\mathrm{Td}(TX)$, which is Hirzebruch–Riemann–Roch. Dolbeault cohomology gives the kernels and cokernels of $\bar\partial$ on $(0,k)$-forms with values in $E$.

q: The signature theorem identifies $\sigma(M)$ with which integral?
text: The signature theorem identifies $\sigma(M)$ with which integral? For the signature operator $D_+\colon\Omega^+\to\Omega^-$ on a $4k$-manifold, the index equals the signature: $\sigma(M)=\int_M L(TM)$. In dimension $4$, $\sigma=\tfrac{1}{3}\langle p_1,[M]\rangle$. $L=\prod x_i/\tanh(x_i)$ in formal Pontryagin roots.

q: On a $2n$-dimensional spin manifold, the index of the Dirac operator $D\colon\Gamma(S^+)\to\Gamma(S^-)$ equals …
text: On a $2n$-dimensional spin manifold, the index of the Dirac operator $D\colon\Gamma(S^+)\to\Gamma(S^-)$ equals … Atiyah–Singer for the Dirac operator: $\ind(D)=\int_M\hat A(TM)$. On a closed spin $4$-manifold, $\hat A=-p_1/24$, so $\ind(D)=-\langle p_1,[M]\rangle/24$, which equals $-\sigma(M)/8$ via the signature theorem. The $\hat A$-genus has formal expansion $\prod (x_i/2)/\sinh(x_i/2)$.

q: Lichnerowicz's vanishing theorem says that on a closed spin manifold of positive scalar curvature, …
text: Lichnerowicz's vanishing theorem says that on a closed spin manifold of positive scalar curvature, … Lichnerowicz: $D^2=\nabla^*\nabla+\tfrac{R}{4}$ where $R$ is the scalar curvature. If $R>0$ everywhere, $\ker D=0$, so $\ind(D)=-\dim\mathrm{coker}\,D\le 0$ and dually $\ge 0$, hence $0$. This obstructs many spin manifolds (e.g. $K3$) from carrying positive-scalar-curvature metrics. The Bochner–Weitzenböck formula for $D^2$ has a positive curvature term forcing $\ker D=0$.

q: Why is the integrality of $\hat A(M)$ a non-trivial output on a spin manifold?
text: Why is the integrality of $\hat A(M)$ a non-trivial output on a spin manifold? The expansion of $\hat A$ involves rational coefficients. The fact that its integral against $[M]$ is an integer when $M$ is spin is one of the first divisibility theorems that the index theorem produces — historically how Rokhlin's $\sigma\equiv 0\pmod{16}$ for spin $4$-manifolds was reproved. Without spin, $\hat A$ would only land in $\mathbb{Q}$, but $\ind(D)$ is an integer.

q: The McKean–Singer formula expresses $\ind(D)$ as which trace?
text: The McKean–Singer formula expresses $\ind(D)$ as which trace? The supertrace of $e^{-tD^2}$ is independent of $t$. As $t\to\infty$ it picks out only $\ker D$ and $\ker D^*=\mathrm{coker}\,D$; as $t\to 0$ the heat-kernel asymptotic produces a local index density. Equating the two limits is the heat-kernel proof. Pair off non-zero eigenvalues of $D^*D$ and $DD^*$; only the kernels survive.

q: In the families index theorem, the index of a family $\{D_b\}_{b\in B}$ of elliptic operators lives in which group?
text: In the families index theorem, the index of a family $\{D_b\}_{b\in B}$ of elliptic operators lives in which group? The kernels and cokernels assemble into a virtual bundle $[\ker D]-[\mathrm{coker}\,D]\in K^0(B)$. The families version of Atiyah–Singer computes this via a fibre-integration of $\mathrm{ch}\cdot\mathrm{Td}$. Generalise an integer to a virtual bundle on the parameter space.

q: In gauge theory, the index of the chiral Dirac operator coupled to a connection $A$ on a $4$-manifold corresponds physically to …
text: In gauge theory, the index of the chiral Dirac operator coupled to a connection $A$ on a $4$-manifold corresponds physically to … $\ind(D_A)=\int_M\hat A(TM)\cdot\mathrm{ch}(E_A)$ — for a $\mathrm{SU}(N)$ bundle in dim $4$ the leading piece is $\int_M c_2(E_A)$, the instanton number. The chiral anomaly in physics is exactly this index. $\int\mathrm{tr}(F\wedge F)$ on the gauge bundle.

title: Sets and functions
blurb: Sets, membership, subsets, unions/intersections, functions as single-valued relations, injections and surjections.

title: Cartesian products and power sets
blurb: Products $A\times B$, function spaces $B^A$, and the power set $\mathcal{P}(A)\cong\{0,1\}^A$ — the basic constructions behind tuples, graphs of functions, and indicator functions.

title: Equivalence relations and quotients
blurb: Equivalence relations partition sets; the quotient set X/∼ as the universal recipient of functions constant on classes.

title: Countability
blurb: Countable vs uncountable, Cantor's diagonal argument, and why |R| > |Q|.

title: Axiom of choice
blurb: A choice function exists for every family of non-empty sets. Equivalent to Zorn's lemma and the well-ordering theorem; behind every basis, maximal ideal, and Tychonoff product.

q: If $|A| = 7$, $|B| = 9$, and $|A \cap B| = 4$, what is $|A \cup B|$?
text: If $|A| = 7$, $|B| = 9$, and $|A \cap B| = 4$, what is $|A \cup B|$? Inclusion–exclusion: $|A \cup B| = |A| + |B| - |A \cap B| = 7 + 9 - 4 = 12$.

q: Let $f\colon \{1,2,3,4\} \to \{a,b,c\}$ be given by $1 \mapsto a,\ 2 \mapsto b,\ 3 \mapsto a,\ 4 \mapsto c$. Which is true?
text: Let $f\colon \{1,2,3,4\} \to \{a,b,c\}$ be given by $1 \mapsto a,\ 2 \mapsto b,\ 3 \mapsto a,\ 4 \mapsto c$. Which is true? The image is $\{a,b,c\}$, the whole codomain, so $f$ is surjective. But $f(1) = f(3) = a$ shows $f$ is not injective. With $|A|=4 > 3 = |B|$ no function $A \to B$ can be injective (pigeonhole).

q: How many subsets does a set of size $6$ have? (That is, compute $|\mathcal{P}(A)|$ for $|A| = 6$.)
text: How many subsets does a set of size $6$ have? (That is, compute $|\mathcal{P}(A)|$ for $|A| = 6$.) Each of the $6$ elements is independently in or out of a subset, so $|\mathcal{P}(A)| = 2^6 = 64$.

q: Let $f\colon A \to B$ and $g\colon B \to C$. Which is the correct chain of implications among injectivity and surjectivity of compositions?
text: Let $f\colon A \to B$ and $g\colon B \to C$. Which is the correct chain of implications among injectivity and surjectivity of compositions? If $g\circ f$ is injective and $f(a)=f(a')$, then $g(f(a))=g(f(a'))$, so $a=a'$; thus $f$ is injective. Conversely $g$ need not be (e.g. $g$ can collapse elements outside $\operatorname{im} f$). If $g\circ f$ is surjective then every $c\in C$ is hit, so $g$ is surjective; $f$ need not be.

q: Sets $A$, $B$, $C$ satisfy $|A|=10$, $|B|=8$, $|C|=6$, $|A\cap B|=3$, $|A\cap C|=2$, $|B\cap C|=4$, $|A\cap B\cap C|=1$. Compute $|A\cup B\cup C|$.
text: Sets $A$, $B$, $C$ satisfy $|A|=10$, $|B|=8$, $|C|=6$, $|A\cap B|=3$, $|A\cap C|=2$, $|B\cap C|=4$, $|A\cap B\cap C|=1$. Compute $|A\cup B\cup C|$. Three-set inclusion–exclusion: $|A\cup B\cup C|=10+8+6-3-2-4+1=16$.

q: Which is a correct counterexample showing that $f^{-1}(f(S)) = S$ can fail?
text: Which is a correct counterexample showing that $f^{-1}(f(S)) = S$ can fail? $f^{-1}(f(S))\supseteq S$ always, with equality iff $f$ is injective on the relevant domain. A non-injective $f$ can collapse elements outside $S$ into $f(S)$, enlarging the preimage. The example above is the minimal witness.

q: If $|A| = 5$ and $|B| = 4$, how many functions are there from $A$ to $B$? (That is, compute $|B^A|$.)
text: If $|A| = 5$ and $|B| = 4$, how many functions are there from $A$ to $B$? (That is, compute $|B^A|$.) Each of the $|A|=5$ elements picks a value in $B$ independently, so $|B^A| = |B|^{|A|} = 4^5 = 1024$.

q: Let $A$ be a set. Under which bijection is $\mathcal{P}(A) \cong \{0,1\}^A$?
text: Let $A$ be a set. Under which bijection is $\mathcal{P}(A) \cong \{0,1\}^A$? A subset $S\subseteq A$ is determined by, and determines, its indicator function $\mathbf{1}_S\colon A\to\{0,1\}$. This bijection is also why $|\mathcal{P}(A)|=2^{|A|}$.

q: Let $|A|=3$ and $|B|=2$. How many elements are in $\mathcal{P}(A\times B)$?
text: Let $|A|=3$ and $|B|=2$. How many elements are in $\mathcal{P}(A\times B)$? $|A\times B| = 3\cdot 2 = 6$, so $|\mathcal{P}(A\times B)| = 2^6 = 64$.

q: Compute $|\mathcal{P}(\mathcal{P}(\{a,b\}))|$, i.e. the size of the power set of the power set of a $2$-element set.
text: Compute $|\mathcal{P}(\mathcal{P}(\{a,b\}))|$, i.e. the size of the power set of the power set of a $2$-element set. $|\{a,b\}|=2$, so $|\mathcal{P}(\{a,b\})|=4$, and $|\mathcal{P}(\mathcal{P}(\{a,b\}))|=2^4=16$.

q: Which of the following correctly describes the relationship between $A\times B$ and $B\times A$?
text: Which of the following correctly describes the relationship between $A\times B$ and $B\times A$? The swap map $(a,b)\mapsto(b,a)$ is a bijection $A\times B\to B\times A$, so they have the same cardinality. However, as sets of ordered pairs they differ: $(a,b)\in A\times B$ has first coordinate in $A$, while $(b,a)\in B\times A$ has first coordinate in $B$. If $A\cap B=\emptyset$ and both are non-empty, no element of $A\times B$ belongs to $B\times A$.

q: How many injective functions are there from a $3$-element set to a $5$-element set?
text: How many injective functions are there from a $3$-element set to a $5$-element set? The first element has $5$ choices, the second $4$, the third $3$: $5\cdot 4\cdot 3=60$.

q: How many equivalence classes does the relation '$a \sim b$ iff $5 \mid a - b$' on $\mathbb{Z}$ have?
text: How many equivalence classes does the relation '$a \sim b$ iff $5 \mid a - b$' on $\mathbb{Z}$ have? The classes are $[0], [1], [2], [3], [4]$ — the residue classes modulo $5$. So $|\mathbb{Z}/5\mathbb{Z}| = 5$.

q: Which of the following relations on $\mathbb{Z}$ is an equivalence relation?
text: Which of the following relations on $\mathbb{Z}$ is an equivalence relation? $a \sim b \iff a^2 = b^2$ is reflexive ($a^2 = a^2$), symmetric ($a^2 = b^2 \Rightarrow b^2 = a^2$), and transitive. The others fail: $\le$ is not symmetric, $\ne$ is not reflexive (and not transitive), and $\mid$ is not symmetric.

q: Let $\pi\colon X \to X/{\sim}$ be the quotient map. Which statement correctly expresses the universal property of the quotient?
text: Let $\pi\colon X \to X/{\sim}$ be the quotient map. Which statement correctly expresses the universal property of the quotient? The universal property: $f\colon X \to Y$ descends to a (unique) $\bar f\colon X/{\sim} \to Y$ with $\bar f \circ \pi = f$ exactly when $f$ respects the equivalence — i.e. $x \sim x' \Rightarrow f(x) = f(x')$.

q: A relation $R$ on a set $A$ is reflexive and transitive but fails symmetry. Which of the following is a correct counterexample?
text: A relation $R$ on a set $A$ is reflexive and transitive but fails symmetry. Which of the following is a correct counterexample? Divisibility on $\mathbb{N}_{>0}$ is a partial order (reflexive, antisymmetric, transitive) but not symmetric: $2\mid 4$ yet $4\nmid 2$. It therefore shows that reflexivity + transitivity do not force symmetry.

q: Which is the correct order of steps in proving that the kernel equivalence $x\sim_f y\iff f(x)=f(y)$ yields the canonical factorisation $f = \bar f\circ\pi$?
text: Which is the correct order of steps in proving that the kernel equivalence $x\sim_f y\iff f(x)=f(y)$ yields the canonical factorisation $f = \bar f\circ\pi$? Standard proof: first define $\bar f([x])=f(x)$, then verify well-definedness ($x\sim_f y\Rightarrow f(x)=f(y)$), then observe $\bar f(\pi(x))=\bar f([x])=f(x)$. Injectivity of $\bar f$ follows because $\bar f([x])=\bar f([y])$ implies $f(x)=f(y)$ implies $[x]=[y]$.

q: The relation $\sim$ on $\{1,2,3,4,5,6\}$ is defined by $a\sim b\iff 3\mid a-b$. How many equivalence classes are there, and what is the size of the class $[2]$? Enter the size of $[2]$.
text: The relation $\sim$ on $\{1,2,3,4,5,6\}$ is defined by $a\sim b\iff 3\mid a-b$. How many equivalence classes are there, and what is the size of the class $[2]$? Enter the size of $[2]$. There are $3$ classes: $[0]=\{3,6\}$, $[1]=\{1,4\}$, $[2]=\{2,5\}$. The class $[2]=\{2,5\}$ has size $2$.

q: Which of the following sets is uncountable?
text: Which of the following sets is uncountable? By Cantor's theorem $|\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})| > |\mathbb{N}|$, so $\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})$ is uncountable (in fact $|\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})| = 2^{\aleph_0} = \mathfrak{c} = |\mathbb{R}|$). The other three are countable: $\mathbb{Q}$ injects into $\mathbb{Z}^2$; $\mathbb{Z}^3$ is a finite product of countables; the algebraic numbers are a countable union of finite root-sets of integer polynomials.

q: Let $s_0, s_1, s_2, \dots$ be an enumeration of binary sequences with $s_0 = 0110\dots$, $s_1 = 1001\dots$, $s_2 = 0011\dots$, $s_3 = 1111\dots$. What are the first four bits of the Cantor diagonal sequence $d$ defined by $d_n = 1 - s_n(n)$?
text: Let $s_0, s_1, s_2, \dots$ be an enumeration of binary sequences with $s_0 = 0110\dots$, $s_1 = 1001\dots$, $s_2 = 0011\dots$, $s_3 = 1111\dots$. What are the first four bits of the Cantor diagonal sequence $d$ defined by $d_n = 1 - s_n(n)$? The diagonal entries are $s_0(0) = 0$, $s_1(1) = 0$, $s_2(2) = 1$, $s_3(3) = 1$. Flipping each bit gives $d = 1100\dots$, which differs from each $s_n$ in position $n$.

q: Under the Cantor zigzag pairing $\langle i, j \rangle = \tfrac{(i+j)(i+j+1)}{2} + i$ (with $i, j \ge 0$), what is $\langle 2, 1 \rangle$?
text: Under the Cantor zigzag pairing $\langle i, j \rangle = \tfrac{(i+j)(i+j+1)}{2} + i$ (with $i, j \ge 0$), what is $\langle 2, 1 \rangle$? $\langle 2, 1 \rangle = \tfrac{(2+1)(2+1+1)}{2} + 2 = \tfrac{3 \cdot 4}{2} + 2 = 6 + 2 = 8$.

q: Cantor's theorem states $|A| < |\mathcal{P}(A)|$ for every set $A$. Which step in the diagonal proof is the critical one that produces a contradiction?
text: Cantor's theorem states $|A| < |\mathcal{P}(A)|$ for every set $A$. Which step in the diagonal proof is the critical one that produces a contradiction? Assume for contradiction $f\colon A\to\mathcal{P}(A)$ is surjective. Form the diagonal set $D=\{a\in A\mid a\notin f(a)\}$. Since $f$ is surjective, $D=f(d)$ for some $d$. Then $d\in D\iff d\notin f(d)=D$, a contradiction. Hence no surjection $A\to\mathcal{P}(A)$ exists. Build a subset guaranteed to differ from every $f(a)$ on the element $a$ itself.

q: Which is a correct proof-step ordering for showing $\mathbb{Q}$ is countable via the zigzag enumeration?
text: Which is a correct proof-step ordering for showing $\mathbb{Q}$ is countable via the zigzag enumeration? Writing each rational in lowest terms gives an injection $\mathbb{Q}\hookrightarrow\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{N}_{>0}$. A countable product of countable sets is countable (shown by the zigzag/Cantor pairing), so $\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{N}_{>0}$ is countable, and any subset of a countable set is countable.

q: Using the Cantor zigzag pairing $\langle i,j\rangle=\frac{(i+j)(i+j+1)}{2}+i$, compute $\langle 3,0\rangle$.
text: Using the Cantor zigzag pairing $\langle i,j\rangle=\frac{(i+j)(i+j+1)}{2}+i$, compute $\langle 3,0\rangle$. $\langle 3,0\rangle=\frac{(3+0)(3+0+1)}{2}+3=\frac{3\cdot 4}{2}+3=6+0=6$.

q: Which of the following is equivalent to the axiom of choice over ZF?
text: Which of the following is equivalent to the axiom of choice over ZF? Zorn's lemma is one of the standard equivalents of AC, alongside the well-ordering theorem and Tukey's lemma. The other choices are theorems of ZF or ZFC but are not equivalent to AC — the continuum hypothesis is independent of ZFC, and Cantor's theorem is provable in ZF with no choice at all.

q: Russell's distinction between shoes and socks illustrates which point?
text: Russell's distinction between shoes and socks illustrates which point? With shoes there is a uniform rule ('pick the left one'), so no AC is needed. With socks there is no such rule, yet AC still asserts the product $\prod_i A_i$ is non-empty. The point is that for infinite families we can always produce a choice function under AC, even when we cannot write down an explicit one.

q: Which of these standard results is not known to require (some form of) the axiom of choice?
text: Which of these standard results is not known to require (some form of) the axiom of choice? The first three all use full AC (or Zorn's lemma, which is equivalent). The countable-union-of-countable-sets statement only needs the weaker countable choice , and over ZF plus countable choice it is provable — but in ZF alone it is not. The question asks for the statement that is 'not known to require AC ' (i.e. full choice).

q: Which is the correct logical chain showing AC $\Rightarrow$ Well-Ordering Theorem $\Rightarrow$ Zorn's Lemma $\Rightarrow$ AC (the classical equivalence circle)?
text: Which is the correct logical chain showing AC $\Rightarrow$ Well-Ordering Theorem $\Rightarrow$ Zorn's Lemma $\Rightarrow$ AC (the classical equivalence circle)? All three are equivalent over ZF. AC → Well-Ordering: use the choice function to pick the least element at each transfinite stage. Well-Ordering → Zorn: in a well-ordered poset every chain has an upper bound (its supremum in the well-order), so a maximal element exists. Zorn → AC: apply Zorn to the poset of partial choice functions ordered by extension.

q: In ZF without AC, which of the following can be proved?
text: In ZF without AC, which of the following can be proved? Cantor's diagonal theorem requires no choice whatsoever — the diagonal set $D=\{a\mid a\notin f(a)\}$ is defined explicitly. The existence of a countably infinite subset of every infinite set requires (at least) Dependent Choice; well-ordering $\mathbb{R}$ needs full AC; and right inverses for surjections are equivalent to AC.

q: Vitali's construction of a non-measurable subset of $[0,1]$ uses AC in which essential step?
text: Vitali's construction of a non-measurable subset of $[0,1]$ uses AC in which essential step? The Vitali set $V$ is built by picking exactly one element from each equivalence class of $x\sim y\iff x-y\in\mathbb{Q}$ restricted to $[0,1]$. There are uncountably many classes and no canonical choice; AC is invoked to guarantee a simultaneous selection. Assuming $V$ is measurable then leads to a contradiction with countable additivity.

title: Syntax: languages, terms, formulas
blurb: A first-order language $\mathcal{L}$ is a triple of constant, function, and relation symbols (with arities). Terms are built from variables, constants, and function applications; formulas from atomic predicates by Boolean connectives and quantifiers $\forall, \exists$. Free vs bound variables; sentences are formulas with no free variables.

title: Tarski semantics and structures
blurb: An $\mathcal{L}$-structure $\mathfrak{A}$ interprets each symbol on a non-empty domain. With a variable assignment $s$, terms denote elements and formulas denote truth values; $\mathfrak{A}\models\varphi[s]$ is defined by recursion. A theory $T$ is satisfiable iff some $\mathfrak{A}\models T$, and $T\models\varphi$ iff every model of $T$ satisfies $\varphi$.

title: Deduction: proof systems and soundness
blurb: A Hilbert-style or natural-deduction calculus assigns formal proofs $\Gamma\vdash\varphi$. Soundness: $\Gamma\vdash\varphi \Rightarrow \Gamma\models\varphi$, proved by induction on proofs. The hard direction — completeness — is the converse and is Gödel's theorem.

title: Gödel's completeness theorem
blurb: $\Gamma\vdash\varphi \iff \Gamma\models\varphi$. Equivalently, every consistent theory has a model. Henkin's proof: add witness constants for every $\exists x\,\psi(x)$, extend to a maximal consistent Henkin theory, then take the term model — closed terms modulo provable equality — as the canonical structure.

title: Compactness and non-standard models
blurb: Compactness theorem: a set of sentences is satisfiable iff every finite subset is. An immediate corollary of completeness, since proofs are finite. Adding a constant $c$ with $c>0, c>1, c>2,\ldots$ to Peano arithmetic gives a consistent extension, hence a non-standard model of $\mathbb{N}$ containing infinite elements.

title: Löwenheim–Skolem and Skolem's paradox
blurb: Downward Löwenheim–Skolem: any satisfiable theory in a countable language has a countable model. Applied to ZFC, this yields a countable model of set theory — even though ZFC proves $\mathbb{R}$ is uncountable. The 'paradox' is resolved: 'uncountable' is internal; the bijection witnessing countability lives outside the model.

title: Decidable theories and quantifier elimination
blurb: A theory $T$ is decidable if there's an algorithm to test $T\vdash\varphi$. Complete + recursively axiomatised $\Rightarrow$ decidable. Quantifier elimination (e.g. for dense linear orders without endpoints, real closed fields, algebraically closed fields) reduces every formula to a quantifier-free one, giving decidability and a clean structure theory of definable sets.

q: In the formula $\forall x\,(P(x) \to \exists y\, R(x,y)) \land Q(z)$, which variables occur free?
text: In the formula $\forall x\,(P(x) \to \exists y\, R(x,y)) \land Q(z)$, which variables occur free? $x$ is bound by $\forall x$ inside the first conjunct; $y$ is bound by $\exists y$. $Q(z)$ is outside any quantifier scope, so $z$ is the only free variable.

q: How many distinct terms of depth at most $1$ can be built from one constant $c$, one unary function $f$, and one variable $x$? (Depth $0$: variable or constant. Depth $1$: $f$ applied to a depth-$0$ term.)
text: How many distinct terms of depth at most $1$ can be built from one constant $c$, one unary function $f$, and one variable $x$? (Depth $0$: variable or constant. Depth $1$: $f$ applied to a depth-$0$ term.) Depth-$0$ terms: $c, x$ (2). Depth-$1$ terms: $f(c), f(x)$ (2). Total $2 + 2 = 4$.

q: Which of the following is a well-formed first-order formula in the language of arithmetic $\{0, 1, +, \cdot, =, <\}$?
text: Which of the following is a well-formed first-order formula in the language of arithmetic $\{0, 1, +, \cdot, =, <\}$? The first is a sentence: 'every $x$ has an additive complement to $1$'. The others misuse symbols: relation/function symbols cannot be quantified over (that would be second-order), and $(x)$ is not a formula.

q: Let $\mathfrak{A} = (\mathbb{Z}, +, 0)$ in the language $\{+, 0\}$. Which sentence is $\mathfrak{A}$ a model of?
text: Let $\mathfrak{A} = (\mathbb{Z}, +, 0)$ in the language $\{+, 0\}$. Which sentence is $\mathfrak{A}$ a model of? Every integer has an additive inverse: $y = -x$. The second fails for $x = 1$. The third asks for a non-zero additive identity. The fourth is trivially false.

q: Let $\mathfrak{A}$ be the structure on $\{a,b,c\}$ where $R$ is interpreted as the relation $\{(a,b),(b,c),(a,c)\}$. How many ordered pairs $(x,y)$ satisfy $\mathfrak{A}\models R(x,y) \lor R(y,x)$?
text: Let $\mathfrak{A}$ be the structure on $\{a,b,c\}$ where $R$ is interpreted as the relation $\{(a,b),(b,c),(a,c)\}$. How many ordered pairs $(x,y)$ satisfy $\mathfrak{A}\models R(x,y) \lor R(y,x)$? The relation $R \cup R^{-1}$ has $3$ pairs each from $R$ and $R^{-1}$, all distinct: $(a,b),(b,c),(a,c),(b,a),(c,b),(c,a)$. So $6$ pairs.

q: Which is the correct definition of $\mathfrak{A}\models \forall x\,\varphi(x)\,[s]$ under Tarski's recursion (with $s$ an assignment, $s[x\mapsto a]$ the update)?
text: Which is the correct definition of $\mathfrak{A}\models \forall x\,\varphi(x)\,[s]$ under Tarski's recursion (with $s$ an assignment, $s[x\mapsto a]$ the update)? The semantic clause for $\forall x$ is: $\varphi$ holds for every value of $x$ ranging over the domain — i.e. for the modified assignment $s[x\mapsto a]$ as $a$ varies. Restricting to closed terms is wrong: domains may contain elements named by no closed term.

q: The soundness theorem $\Gamma\vdash\varphi \Rightarrow \Gamma\models\varphi$ is proved by:
text: The soundness theorem $\Gamma\vdash\varphi \Rightarrow \Gamma\models\varphi$ is proved by: Soundness is proved by induction on proofs. Logical axioms are tautologies, hence valid; rules like modus ponens preserve truth in every structure. Therefore every theorem of the calculus is semantically valid.

q: Which of the following is the universal generalisation rule (or a correct restriction of it)?
text: Which of the following is the universal generalisation rule (or a correct restriction of it)? The side condition '$x$ not free in $\Gamma$' is essential: from $\{P(x)\}$ we must NOT conclude $\forall x\,P(x)$, since $x$ is being used as a name. Dropping the side condition gives an unsound rule.

q: In a Hilbert system with modus ponens as the only inference rule, how many premises does an application of MP take?
text: In a Hilbert system with modus ponens as the only inference rule, how many premises does an application of MP take? Modus ponens: from $\varphi$ and $\varphi\to\psi$ infer $\psi$. Two premises.

q: Gödel's completeness theorem states:
text: Gödel's completeness theorem states: Completeness equates syntactic provability with semantic entailment. The first option is the (false) statement that Gödel's incompleteness theorem refutes. The third and fourth are unrelated.

q: Henkin's proof of completeness builds a model of a consistent theory $T$ by:
text: Henkin's proof of completeness builds a model of a consistent theory $T$ by: Henkin's recipe: (1) add witness constants for every existential, in a chain; (2) Lindenbaum-extend to maximal consistency; (3) define $s\sim t$ iff $T\vdash s=t$; the quotient set of closed terms is the domain. Truth in this term model coincides with provability. Henkin's recipe: (1) add witness constants for every existential, in a chain; (2) Lindenbaum-extend to maximal consistency; (3) define $s\sim t$ iff $T\vdash s=t$; the quotient set of closed terms is…

q: A consistent theory $T$ has at least how many models? (By the completeness theorem, the minimum guaranteed.)
text: A consistent theory $T$ has at least how many models? (By the completeness theorem, the minimum guaranteed.) Consistency $\iff$ existence of a model. So at least $1$.

q: The compactness theorem states that a set $\Sigma$ of first-order sentences is satisfiable iff:
text: The compactness theorem states that a set $\Sigma$ of first-order sentences is satisfiable iff: Finite-satisfiability of every finite subset implies global satisfiability. Proof: by completeness, satisfiability $\iff$ consistency, and inconsistency requires a finite proof, hence a finite inconsistent subset.

q: To build a non-standard model of arithmetic from $\mathrm{Th}(\mathbb{N})$ via compactness, one adds a fresh constant $c$ and the sentences:
text: To build a non-standard model of arithmetic from $\mathrm{Th}(\mathbb{N})$ via compactness, one adds a fresh constant $c$ and the sentences: Each finite subset of $\mathrm{Th}(\mathbb{N}) \cup \{c \ne \overline{n} : n \in \mathbb{N}\}$ is satisfiable in $\mathbb{N}$ (interpret $c$ as a number larger than all numerals mentioned), so the whole set is satisfiable. The resulting model contains $c$ different from every standard numeral — a non-standard element. Each finite subset of $\mathrm{Th}(\mathbb{N}) \cup \{c \ne \overline{n} : n \in \mathbb{N}\}$ is satisfiable in $\mathbb{N}$ (interpret $c$ as a number larger than all numerals mentioned), so the…

q: Downward Löwenheim–Skolem: a satisfiable theory in a countable language has a model of cardinality $\aleph_n$ for some $n$. Enter the smallest such $n$.
text: Downward Löwenheim–Skolem: a satisfiable theory in a countable language has a model of cardinality $\aleph_n$ for some $n$. Enter the smallest such $n$. A countable model has cardinality $\aleph_0$, so $n=0$.

q: The downward Löwenheim–Skolem theorem says:
text: The downward Löwenheim–Skolem theorem says: Downward LS: countable language + satisfiable $\Rightarrow$ countable model. Upward LS would say models can be made arbitrarily large. Uniqueness of models is categoricity, a separate notion.

q: Skolem's paradox concerns the fact that:
text: Skolem's paradox concerns the fact that: Skolem's resolution: 'uncountable in $M$' means 'no bijection $\mathbb{N}^M\to\mathbb{R}^M$ exists inside $M$'. The external bijection is invisible to $M$. Uncountability is not absolute between models.

q: If a first-order theory in a countable language has an infinite model, by upward Löwenheim–Skolem it has models of every cardinality $\kappa \ge \aleph_n$. Enter the smallest such $n$.
text: If a first-order theory in a countable language has an infinite model, by upward Löwenheim–Skolem it has models of every cardinality $\kappa \ge \aleph_n$. Enter the smallest such $n$. Upward LS: an infinite model implies models of every cardinality $\kappa \ge \max(\aleph_0, |\mathcal{L}|) = \aleph_0$. So $n=0$.

q: A complete, recursively axiomatised first-order theory is automatically:
text: A complete, recursively axiomatised first-order theory is automatically: Algorithm: enumerate proofs from the recursive axioms. Since $T$ is complete, exactly one of $\varphi, \neg\varphi$ is a theorem; whichever appears first is the answer. Without completeness, some sentences are independent; without recursive axiomatisation we cannot enumerate proofs.

q: Which theory admits quantifier elimination?
text: Which theory admits quantifier elimination? ACF$_p$ admits QE in the language of rings: every formula is equivalent to a Boolean combination of polynomial equations (Tarski–Chevalley). PA and ZFC are incomplete; true arithmetic is undecidable (Tarski), so it does not admit QE.

q: DLO (dense linear orders without endpoints) is $\aleph_0$-categorical. How many countable models does DLO have, up to isomorphism?
text: DLO (dense linear orders without endpoints) is $\aleph_0$-categorical. How many countable models does DLO have, up to isomorphism? Cantor's back-and-forth: any two countable dense linear orders without endpoints are isomorphic to $(\mathbb{Q}, <)$. Exactly $1$ countable model up to isomorphism.

title: The ZFC axioms
blurb: Extensionality, pairing, union, power-set, infinity, separation, replacement, regularity, choice — the nine axioms that pin down which collections are sets and rule out Russell's paradox.

title: Von Neumann ordinals
blurb: Each ordinal $\alpha$ is the set of strictly smaller ordinals: $0=\emptyset$, $1=\{0\}$, $2=\{0,1\}$, $\omega=\{0,1,2,\dots\}$. Transfinite induction and recursion run along the well-order $\in$.

title: Ordinal arithmetic
blurb: Sum, product, and exponentiation of ordinals defined by transfinite recursion. Non-commutative ($1+\omega=\omega\ne\omega+1$), and the tower $\omega,\omega^\omega,\omega^{\omega^\omega},\dots$ converges to $\varepsilon_0$.

title: Cardinals and cofinality
blurb: $|X|$ as the least ordinal in bijection with $X$; the $\aleph$-hierarchy $\aleph_0,\aleph_1,\aleph_2,\dots$; cofinality $\operatorname{cf}(\kappa)$ classifying regular vs singular cardinals; $|\mathbb R|=2^{\aleph_0}$.

title: The axiom of choice
blurb: AC, Zorn's lemma, and the well-ordering theorem are equivalent over ZF. Tychonoff's product theorem, existence of a Hamel basis for $\mathbb R/\mathbb Q$, and a non-measurable set all need it.

title: The continuum hypothesis
blurb: CH: $2^{\aleph_0}=\aleph_1$. Gödel (1940) showed CH is consistent with ZFC; Cohen (1963) showed $\neg$CH is too — so CH is independent. Forcing is the technique behind Cohen's half.

title: Inaccessible cardinals
blurb: An uncountable regular strong-limit cardinal $\kappa$ — closed under power-set and unions of size $<\kappa$. $V_\kappa$ is then a model of ZFC, so existence of one is strictly stronger than ZFC. Grothendieck universes are exactly $V_\kappa$ for $\kappa$ inaccessible.

q: Which axiom of ZFC blocks Russell's paradoxical collection $R = \{x : x \notin x\}$ from being a set?
text: Which axiom of ZFC blocks Russell's paradoxical collection $R = \{x : x \notin x\}$ from being a set? Naive comprehension $\{x : \varphi(x)\}$ is replaced by Separation: $\{x \in A : \varphi(x)\}$ for an already-given set $A$. Without an ambient set, Russell's $R$ never gets formed; Regularity additionally rules out $x \in x$.

q: Which ZFC axiom asserts the existence of an infinite set?
text: Which ZFC axiom asserts the existence of an infinite set? Infinity posits a set $I$ with $\emptyset \in I$ and $x \in I \Rightarrow x \cup \{x\} \in I$. The intersection of all such $I$ is $\omega$, the set of finite von Neumann ordinals.

q: Replacement strictly extends Separation. Which of the following requires Replacement (not just Separation)?
text: Replacement strictly extends Separation. Which of the following requires Replacement (not just Separation)? Separation only filters elements out of an already-given set. Iterating $\mathcal P$ along $\omega$ produces the function $n \mapsto V_n$; collecting its image into a set is exactly Replacement. Power Set then closes us off; Union assembles $V_\omega$.

q: Under the von Neumann encoding, which set is the ordinal $3$?
text: Under the von Neumann encoding, which set is the ordinal $3$? $0=\emptyset$, $1=\{0\}=\{\emptyset\}$, $2=\{0,1\}=\{\emptyset,\{\emptyset\}\}$, $3=\{0,1,2\}=\{\emptyset,\{\emptyset\},\{\emptyset,\{\emptyset\}\}\}$. Each ordinal is the set of all smaller ordinals.

q: Which statement is true of every ordinal $\alpha$?
text: Which statement is true of every ordinal $\alpha$? An ordinal is by definition a transitive set well-ordered by $\in$. Regularity rules out $\alpha\in\alpha$. Successor ordinals have a maximum; limit ordinals (like $\omega$) do not.

q: Among the ordinals $\omega,\;\omega+1,\;\omega\cdot 2,\;\omega^2$, how many are limit ordinals? (Recall: a limit ordinal is nonzero and not a successor.)
text: Among the ordinals $\omega,\;\omega+1,\;\omega\cdot 2,\;\omega^2$, how many are limit ordinals? (Recall: a limit ordinal is nonzero and not a successor.) $\omega$, $\omega\cdot 2 = \omega+\omega$, and $\omega^2$ are all limits — none has an immediate predecessor. Only $\omega+1$ is a successor (its predecessor is $\omega$). So the count is $3$.

q: Which equality holds in ordinal arithmetic?
text: Which equality holds in ordinal arithmetic? $1+\omega$ is order type $\bullet,0,1,2,\dots$, which has the same order type as $\omega$. But $\omega+1$ has a maximum, so $\omega+1\ne\omega$. Likewise $2\cdot\omega = \omega \ne \omega\cdot 2 = \omega+\omega$, and $2^\omega = \omega$ as ordinals (not $2^{\aleph_0}$ as cardinals).

q: What is $\varepsilon_0$?
text: What is $\varepsilon_0$? $\varepsilon_0$ is the supremum (and limit) of the tower $\omega,\;\omega^\omega,\;\omega^{\omega^\omega},\dots$ — equivalently the least fixed point of $\alpha\mapsto\omega^\alpha$. It is countable but already too large for Peano arithmetic to prove well-founded (Gentzen).

q: Compute the ordinal $(\omega + 3) + (\omega + 5)$ in Cantor normal form, and enter the coefficient of $\omega^1$. (E.g. for $\omega\cdot 4 + 7$, enter $4$.)
text: Compute the ordinal $(\omega + 3) + (\omega + 5)$ in Cantor normal form, and enter the coefficient of $\omega^1$. (E.g. for $\omega\cdot 4 + 7$, enter $4$.) $(\omega+3)+(\omega+5) = \omega + (3+\omega) + 5 = \omega + \omega + 5 = \omega\cdot 2 + 5$. The lower term $3$ is absorbed by the next $\omega$ on its right because addition is left-distributive but not commutative. The coefficient of $\omega^1$ is $2$.

q: Why is $|\mathbb R| = 2^{\aleph_0}$?
text: Why is $|\mathbb R| = 2^{\aleph_0}$? $\mathbb R\hookrightarrow\mathcal P(\mathbb Q)\cong 2^{\aleph_0}$ by sending $r\mapsto\{q\in\mathbb Q: q<r\}$. Conversely $2^{\aleph_0}\hookrightarrow\mathbb R$ via binary expansions (after fixing the $0.0\overline 1=0.1\overline 0$ ambiguity). Cantor–Schröder–Bernstein gives the bijection.

q: What is $\operatorname{cf}(\aleph_\omega)$?
text: What is $\operatorname{cf}(\aleph_\omega)$? $\aleph_\omega = \sup_n \aleph_n$ is the limit of a countable sequence, so its cofinality is $\omega = \aleph_0$. This makes $\aleph_\omega$ a singular cardinal — by König's theorem, $2^{\aleph_0} \ne \aleph_\omega$.

q: Which cardinal is regular?
text: Which cardinal is regular? Successor cardinals $\aleph_{\alpha+1}$ are regular (in ZFC). Limit cardinals $\aleph_\lambda$ have $\operatorname{cf}(\aleph_\lambda) = \operatorname{cf}(\lambda)$, so $\aleph_\omega$ and $\aleph_{\omega+\omega}$ have cofinality $\omega$ — singular. $\aleph_{\omega_1}$ is singular too (cofinality $\omega_1 < \aleph_{\omega_1}$).

q: Which is NOT equivalent to AC over ZF?
text: Which is NOT equivalent to AC over ZF? AC is equivalent to Zorn's lemma, the well-ordering theorem, Tychonoff's theorem, and 'every vector space has a basis'. But 'every infinite set is countable' is false — Cantor showed $\mathbb R$ is uncountable, no choice needed.

q: Tychonoff's theorem (every product of compact spaces is compact) is equivalent to AC. Which restricted form is provable in ZF without choice?
text: Tychonoff's theorem (every product of compact spaces is compact) is equivalent to AC. Which restricted form is provable in ZF without choice? Finite products of compact spaces are compact in ZF. Countable products of compact Hausdorff spaces need countable choice (DC). The general Tychonoff theorem is equivalent to AC (Kelley 1950).

q: Which construction is known to require AC?
text: Which construction is known to require AC? Vitali's construction picks one representative from each coset of $\mathbb Q$ in $\mathbb R/\mathbb Q$, an uncountable family of non-empty sets — this is exactly a choice function. Solovay (1970) showed it is consistent with ZF+DC that every set of reals is Lebesgue measurable, so the non-measurable set really requires AC.

q: What does the continuum hypothesis (CH) assert?
text: What does the continuum hypothesis (CH) assert? CH is the statement $2^{\aleph_0}=\aleph_1$. Equivalently: every infinite subset of $\mathbb R$ is either countable or in bijection with $\mathbb R$ — there is no cardinality in between.

q: What is the status of CH in ZFC?
text: What is the status of CH in ZFC? Gödel (1940) built the constructible universe $L$, an inner model of ZFC where CH (and GCH) hold; this shows ZFC + CH is consistent. Cohen (1963) introduced forcing to build a model where CH fails. Neither half can prove CH itself.

q: The technique Cohen invented to refute CH is now ubiquitous in set theory. What is its name?
text: The technique Cohen invented to refute CH is now ubiquitous in set theory. What is its name? Forcing adjoins to a ground model $M$ a generic filter $G$ on a partial order $\mathbb P\in M$, producing a larger model $M[G]\models$ ZFC where carefully chosen statements (like $\neg$CH) are forced to hold. It is the standard tool for independence results. Forcing adjoins to a ground model $M$ a generic filter $G$ on a partial order $\mathbb P\in M$, producing a larger model $M[G]\models$ ZFC where carefully chosen statements (like $\neg$CH) are forced…

q: An (uncountable) inaccessible cardinal $\kappa$ is required to be:
text: An (uncountable) inaccessible cardinal $\kappa$ is required to be: Inaccessible = uncountable + regular + strong limit. Both conditions ensure $V_\kappa$ is closed under power-set and unions of size $<\kappa$, so $V_\kappa\models$ ZFC.

q: Why is the existence of an inaccessible cardinal strictly stronger than ZFC?
text: Why is the existence of an inaccessible cardinal strictly stronger than ZFC? If ZFC + Inaccessible $\vdash$ Con(ZFC), then ZFC alone cannot prove the existence of an inaccessible — else ZFC would prove Con(ZFC), violating Gödel II. So 'an inaccessible exists' has strictly higher consistency strength than ZFC.

q: What is the connection between inaccessibles and Grothendieck universes?
text: What is the connection between inaccessibles and Grothendieck universes? A Grothendieck universe $\mathcal U$ is a transitive set closed under pairs, unions, power-sets, and replacement-like indexing — exactly the closure conditions $V_\kappa$ has when $\kappa$ is inaccessible. SGA assumes 'every set lies in a Grothendieck universe' for category-theoretic foundations; this is the universe axiom, equivalent to a proper class of inaccessibles. A Grothendieck universe $\mathcal U$ is a transitive set closed under pairs, unions, power-sets, and replacement-like indexing — exactly the closure conditions $V_\kappa$ has when $\kappa$ is…

title: Structures and signatures
blurb: A signature $\sigma$ is a list of constant, function, and relation symbols with arities. A $\sigma$-structure $\mathfrak{A}=(A;c^{\mathfrak{A}},f^{\mathfrak{A}},R^{\mathfrak{A}},\ldots)$ interprets each symbol on a non-empty domain. Groups, ordered sets, graphs, and fields are all structures over the appropriate signature; model theory is the study of what first-order sentences see in them.

title: Elementary equivalence
blurb: Two $\sigma$-structures $\mathfrak{A},\mathfrak{B}$ are elementarily equivalent , written $\mathfrak{A}\equiv\mathfrak{B}$, when they satisfy exactly the same first-order $\sigma$-sentences. Elementary equivalence is much coarser than isomorphism: $(\mathbb{Q},<)$ and $(\mathbb{R},<)$ are elementarily equivalent but not isomorphic; $\mathbb{N}$ and any non-standard model of arithmetic are elementarily equivalent by construction.

title: Isomorphism vs elementary equivalence
blurb: For finite structures the two notions coincide: $\mathfrak{A}\equiv\mathfrak{B}$ implies $\mathfrak{A}\cong\mathfrak{B}$, since the size and complete diagram pin down the isomorphism class. For infinite structures they part ways — Cantor's back-and-forth shows any two countable dense linear orders without endpoints are isomorphic, but $(\mathbb{Q},<)\equiv(\mathbb{R},<)$ are non-isomorphic. The example $(\mathbb{N},<)$ vs $(\mathbb{Z},<)$ shows even elementary equivalence can fail.

title: Types and saturation
blurb: An $n$- type over a parameter set $A$ is a maximal consistent set of formulas $p(x_1,\ldots,x_n)$ with parameters from $A$ — a complete first-order description of an $n$-tuple. By compactness every type is realised in some elementary extension; a structure is $\omega$- saturated if every type over a finite parameter set is already realised in it. Saturation is the model-theoretic analogue of compactness for spaces.

title: Ehrenfeucht–Fraïssé games
blurb: The $n$-round EF game on structures $\mathfrak{A},\mathfrak{B}$ pits Spoiler — who picks elements trying to expose a difference — against Duplicator, who copies them across to preserve a partial isomorphism. Duplicator has a winning strategy in the $n$-round game iff $\mathfrak{A}$ and $\mathfrak{B}$ agree on every sentence of quantifier rank $\le n$. EF games are the standard tool for proving non-definability results, e.g. that connectivity is not first-order definable on graphs.

title: Applications to algebra
blurb: ACF (algebraically closed fields) is a model-complete theory: every embedding between models is elementary, so first-order truth transfers freely along extensions. Compactness + the Lefschetz principle yield Ax–Grothendieck — every injective polynomial map $\mathbb{C}^n\to\mathbb{C}^n$ is surjective — by reduction to algebraic closures of finite fields. These transfer principles are the standard model-theoretic tools used in algebraic geometry.

q: Which of the following is the standard signature for the theory of (unital) rings?
text: Which of the following is the standard signature for the theory of (unital) rings? Ring axioms quantify over $0$, $1$, addition, multiplication, and additive inverse, so the natural signature is $\{0,1,+,\cdot,-\}$. The third option is the signature of groups; the fourth is the signature of set theory; the first is the signature of orders.

q: How many distinct $\sigma$-structures are there on the two-element domain $\{0,1\}$ when $\sigma = \{R\}$ has a single binary relation symbol?
text: How many distinct $\sigma$-structures are there on the two-element domain $\{0,1\}$ when $\sigma = \{R\}$ has a single binary relation symbol? A binary relation on a $2$-element set is a subset of $\{0,1\}^2$, which has $4$ ordered pairs. The number of subsets is $2^4 = 16$.

q: Let $\mathfrak{A} = (\{1,2,3\}; <)$ where $<$ is interpreted as the strict ordering $1<2<3$. Which sentence does $\mathfrak{A}$ satisfy?
text: Let $\mathfrak{A} = (\{1,2,3\}; <)$ where $<$ is interpreted as the strict ordering $1<2<3$. Which sentence does $\mathfrak{A}$ satisfy? Choice 3 says 'there is a minimum'; the element $1$ witnesses it (no $y<1$). Choice 1 fails at $x=3$ (no upper element). Choice 2 says $<$ is symmetric, false. Choice 4 says $<$ is reflexive, false.

q: Two $\sigma$-structures $\mathfrak{A}$ and $\mathfrak{B}$ are elementarily equivalent ($\mathfrak{A}\equiv\mathfrak{B}$) when:
text: Two $\sigma$-structures $\mathfrak{A}$ and $\mathfrak{B}$ are elementarily equivalent ($\mathfrak{A}\equiv\mathfrak{B}$) when: By definition, $\mathfrak{A}\equiv\mathfrak{B}$ iff $\mathrm{Th}(\mathfrak{A}) = \mathrm{Th}(\mathfrak{B})$ — the same first-order sentences are true in both. Choice 1 is isomorphism (strictly stronger). Cardinality is invisible to first-order logic.

q: Which pair of structures is elementarily equivalent in the language of orders $\{<\}$?
text: Which pair of structures is elementarily equivalent in the language of orders $\{<\}$? $(\mathbb{Q},<)$ and $(\mathbb{R},<)$ are both dense linear orders without endpoints, and DLO is a complete theory — so they are elementarily equivalent. $\mathbb{N}$ has a minimum (false in $\mathbb{Z}$), $\mathbb{Z}$ is discrete (every element has an immediate successor; false in $\mathbb{Q}$), and finite structures of different sizes can always be separated by a sentence counting elements.

q: How many distinct first-order theories (= equivalence classes under $\equiv$) are there among finite linear orders in the language $\{<\}$? (Hint: a finite linear order is determined up to isomorphism by its size, and isomorphic structures are elementarily equivalent.)
text: How many distinct first-order theories (= equivalence classes under $\equiv$) are there among finite linear orders in the language $\{<\}$? (Hint: a finite linear order is determined up to isomorphism by its size, and isomorphic structures are elementarily equivalent.) Trick question — there are infinitely many. For each $n\ge 1$ the sentence 'there are exactly $n$ elements' picks out a unique equivalence class, and these classes are all distinct. Enter $0$ to mean the count is unbounded. (Strictly: countably infinitely many.)

q: For two finite $\sigma$-structures over a finite signature, which implication holds?
text: For two finite $\sigma$-structures over a finite signature, which implication holds? For finite structures the two notions coincide. Isomorphism always implies elementary equivalence (any iso preserves truth of every formula). Conversely, on a finite structure the complete first-order theory pins down the isomorphism type — you can write a sentence describing the entire diagram.

q: Which is a correct example of two infinite structures $\mathfrak{A}\equiv\mathfrak{B}$ that are not isomorphic?
text: Which is a correct example of two infinite structures $\mathfrak{A}\equiv\mathfrak{B}$ that are not isomorphic? $(\mathbb{Q},<)$ and $(\mathbb{R},<)$ are both models of DLO (a complete theory), so they are elementarily equivalent; but they have different cardinalities, so they are not isomorphic. The other pairs differ in first-order properties (e.g. divisibility, sentence about $-1$, $\sqrt{-1}$).

q: By Cantor's back-and-forth theorem, how many isomorphism classes of countable dense linear orders without endpoints exist?
text: By Cantor's back-and-forth theorem, how many isomorphism classes of countable dense linear orders without endpoints exist? Cantor proved that any two countable dense linear orders without endpoints are isomorphic, via a back-and-forth construction. So there is exactly $1$ isomorphism class. This is the model-theoretic statement that DLO is $\aleph_0$-categorical.

q: A complete $1$-type $p(x)$ over a structure $\mathfrak{A}$ is:
text: A complete $1$-type $p(x)$ over a structure $\mathfrak{A}$ is: A complete type is the maximal consistent first-order description of (a possible) element. By compactness it is always realised in some elementary extension of $\mathfrak{A}$ — it may or may not be realised inside $\mathfrak{A}$ itself.

q: In the structure $(\mathbb{Q}, q : q\in\mathbb{Q}\}$. Which is true?
text: In the structure $(\mathbb{Q}, q : q\in\mathbb{Q}\}$. Which is true? Every finite subset of $p$ is consistent (witness: any rational larger than the finite list of $q$'s mentioned), so by compactness $p$ is consistent. But no rational is larger than every rational, so $p$ is omitted in $(\mathbb{Q},<)$. Compactness produces an elementary extension where it is realised — that extension contains a 'point at infinity'.

q: A structure $\mathfrak{A}$ is $\omega$-saturated if every type over a finite parameter set is realised in $\mathfrak{A}$. The countable random graph is $\omega$-saturated; the dense linear order $(\mathbb{Q},<)$ also is. How many distinct complete $1$-types over the empty parameter set does the theory DLO have?
text: A structure $\mathfrak{A}$ is $\omega$-saturated if every type over a finite parameter set is realised in $\mathfrak{A}$. The countable random graph is $\omega$-saturated; the dense linear order $(\mathbb{Q},<)$ also is. How many distinct complete $1$-types over the empty parameter set does the theory DLO have? DLO is a complete theory, and over the empty set there is exactly one complete $1$-type — every element of a model satisfies the same formulas in $\{<\}$ with no parameters. (Once you add even one parameter, the type space splits into 'less than', 'equal to', 'greater than' that parameter.)

q: The Ehrenfeucht–Fraïssé theorem states that Duplicator has a winning strategy in the $n$-round EF game on $\mathfrak{A}$ vs $\mathfrak{B}$ iff:
text: The Ehrenfeucht–Fraïssé theorem states that Duplicator has a winning strategy in the $n$-round EF game on $\mathfrak{A}$ vs $\mathfrak{B}$ iff: EF games measure agreement up to quantifier rank : an $n$-round Duplicator strategy exactly captures indistinguishability by rank-$n$ sentences. As $n\to\infty$ this approaches full elementary equivalence (for finite signatures).

q: Consider the linear orders $\mathfrak{A}=(\{0,1,2\},<)$ and $\mathfrak{B}=(\{0,1,2,3\},<)$. What is the largest $n$ for which Duplicator wins the $n$-round EF game on $\mathfrak{A}$ vs $\mathfrak{B}$? (Hint: a sentence of quantifier rank $n$ can count up to roughly $2^n$ elements; here a rank-$2$ sentence already separates them.)
text: Consider the linear orders $\mathfrak{A}=(\{0,1,2\},<)$ and $\mathfrak{B}=(\{0,1,2,3\},<)$. What is the largest $n$ for which Duplicator wins the $n$-round EF game on $\mathfrak{A}$ vs $\mathfrak{B}$? (Hint: a sentence of quantifier rank $n$ can count up to roughly $2^n$ elements; here a rank-$2$ sentence already separates them.) With $1$ round Duplicator can match either's chosen element to a counterpart in the other; both orders look like 'one element' from a first-order perspective at rank $1$. With $2$ rounds Spoiler picks the largest element in $\mathfrak{B}$, then the second-largest; in $\mathfrak{A}$ Duplicator runs out of distinct space to copy this configuration. So $n=1$ is the largest Duplicator winning round count.

q: Which property of finite graphs is not first-order definable, as proved by an EF-game argument?
text: Which property of finite graphs is not first-order definable, as proved by an EF-game argument? Connectivity is the canonical example of a non-FO-definable property. The proof: for any $n$, a long cycle $C_{2n+1}$ (connected) and a disjoint union $C_n \sqcup C_{n+1}$ (disconnected) agree on every sentence of quantifier rank $\le n$, by an EF-game argument. The other three are FO-definable: count vertices, count edges in a triangle, count neighbours.

q: The theory ACF$_0$ (algebraically closed fields of characteristic $0$) is:
text: The theory ACF$_0$ (algebraically closed fields of characteristic $0$) is: ACF$_0$ is a complete theory: any two algebraically closed fields of characteristic $0$ are elementarily equivalent (this is essentially the Lefschetz principle). It is categorical in uncountable cardinalities (Steinitz: transcendence degree determines isomorphism type) but not in $\aleph_0$ — countable ACF$_0$'s of different transcendence degrees are not isomorphic.

q: The Ax–Grothendieck theorem states that every injective polynomial map $f: \mathbb{C}^n \to \mathbb{C}^n$ is surjective. The model-theoretic proof reduces it to:
text: The Ax–Grothendieck theorem states that every injective polynomial map $f: \mathbb{C}^n \to \mathbb{C}^n$ is surjective. The model-theoretic proof reduces it to: The proof: 'injective $\Rightarrow$ surjective' is a first-order schema (one sentence per pair of degree bounds). It holds trivially in every algebraic closure $\overline{\mathbb{F}_p}$ — those fields are unions of finite fields, where injections of finite sets are bijections. By the Lefschetz principle (ACF$_0$ and ACF$_p$ for large $p$ agree on first-order sentences), it holds in $\mathbb{C}$. This is the canonical 'compactness + transfer' application.

q: A theory $T$ is model-complete when every embedding between models is an elementary embedding. ACF is model-complete because:
text: A theory $T$ is model-complete when every embedding between models is an elementary embedding. ACF is model-complete because: Model-completeness is equivalent to: every formula is equivalent (modulo $T$) to an $\exists$-formula whose existential witness, if it exists in any model, exists in any extension. ACF has full quantifier elimination, which implies model-completeness. The other options are true facts about ACF but don't directly give model-completeness.

title: Turing machines
blurb: A Turing machine is a finite-state controller scanning a two-way infinite tape. A configuration is a triple (state, tape contents, head position); the transition function $\delta\colon Q\times\Sigma\to Q\times\Sigma\times\{L,R\}$ rewrites a cell, moves the head, and updates state. Acceptance is by halting in a designated accept state. Variants — multitape, nondeterministic, two-counter — all simulate one another with at most polynomial overhead, which is the empirical content of the Church–Turing thesis.

title: Recursive and partial-recursive functions
blurb: Build $\mathbb{N}^k\to\mathbb{N}$ functions from three primitives — zero, successor, projection — closed under composition, primitive recursion (define $f(n+1)$ from $f(n)$), and the unbounded $\mu$-operator (least $y$ with $g(\vec x,y)=0$). Without $\mu$ you get the primitive-recursive functions (every total, including Ackermann's exception); with $\mu$ you get the partial-recursive functions, which Turing showed coincide exactly with Turing-computable. The Church–Turing thesis asserts this is the right notion of "effectively computable".

title: The halting problem
blurb: Let $H = \{ \langle M,w\rangle : M \text{ halts on } w\}$. Suppose $h$ decides $H$. Build $D(\langle M\rangle)$ which runs $h$ on $\langle M, \langle M\rangle\rangle$ and loops if $h$ says "halts", halts otherwise. Then $D$ on its own code halts iff it doesn't — contradiction. The argument is Cantor's diagonal applied to the countable list of Turing machines. Halting is the prototype undecidable problem; everything else reduces to it.

title: Recursive vs recursively enumerable
blurb: A set $A\subseteq\mathbb{N}$ is recursive (decidable) if its characteristic function is computable; recursively enumerable (semi-decidable, Σ⁰₁) if there is a TM that halts exactly on inputs in $A$. Theorem: $A$ is recursive iff both $A$ and $\mathbb{N}\setminus A$ are r.e. The halting set $H$ is r.e. but not recursive; its complement is not even r.e. The r.e. sets sit one level above the recursive sets in the arithmetical hierarchy.

title: Undecidability via reduction
blurb: $A\le_m B$ (many-one reducible) means there is a computable $f$ with $x\in A \iff f(x)\in B$. If $A$ is undecidable and $A\le_m B$ then $B$ is undecidable. Rice's theorem : every non-trivial property of the languages recognized by Turing machines is undecidable. Reductions from halting kill emptiness, equivalence, totality, regularity for TMs — and via further reductions, Hilbert's tenth problem, the word problem for groups, and the Post correspondence problem.

title: Gödel incompleteness — computability lens
blurb: Any consistent, recursively axiomatised theory $T$ extending Robinson arithmetic can express "$M$ halts on $w$" as a $\Sigma_1$ formula. If $T$ proved every true halting fact and refuted every false one, halting would be decidable — contradicting the halting theorem. So $T$ is incomplete: some true $\Sigma_1$ sentence is unprovable. Strengthening from ω-consistency to plain consistency (Rosser's trick) gives Gödel's first theorem; the second theorem ($T\not\vdash\mathrm{Con}(T)$) follows by formalising the proof inside $T$.

q: A configuration of a Turing machine consists of which triple?
text: A configuration of a Turing machine consists of which triple? A snapshot of a running TM is exactly (current state, full tape contents, head index). The transition function and the alphabets are part of the machine's static description, not its runtime configuration.

q: Why are multitape Turing machines no more powerful than single-tape machines?
text: Why are multitape Turing machines no more powerful than single-tape machines? Encode $k$ tapes as $k$ tracks of one tape, plus marker symbols for each head. One step of the multitape machine becomes a sweep across the simulated tape — quadratic overhead, but still computable.

q: A binary-alphabet TM has $|Q|=4$ states (including accept and reject) and tape alphabet $\{0,1,\sqcup\}$. How many distinct $(q,a)$ input pairs does its transition function $\delta$ need to specify?
text: A binary-alphabet TM has $|Q|=4$ states (including accept and reject) and tape alphabet $\{0,1,\sqcup\}$. How many distinct $(q,a)$ input pairs does its transition function $\delta$ need to specify? $|Q|\times|\Gamma| = 4\times 3 = 12$. Each pair $(q,a)$ gets one row in the transition table.

q: Which closure operator distinguishes the partial-recursive functions from the primitive-recursive functions?
text: Which closure operator distinguishes the partial-recursive functions from the primitive-recursive functions? Composition, primitive recursion, and the basic functions yield the primitive-recursive class — every member is total. Adding the $\mu$-operator (least $y$ with $g(\vec x,y)=0$, possibly undefined) produces partiality and matches Turing-computability.

q: Ackermann's function is total but not primitive-recursive. What does this show?
text: Ackermann's function is total but not primitive-recursive. What does this show? Ackermann diagonalises against any fixed level of nested recursion, so its growth rate dominates every primitive-recursive function. It is still computable — by a Turing machine, or equivalently a $\mu$-recursive scheme — confirming the proper inclusion PR ⊊ partial-recursive.

q: Define $f$ by primitive recursion: $f(0)=2$, $f(n+1)=f(n)+3$. Compute $f(4)$.
text: Define $f$ by primitive recursion: $f(0)=2$, $f(n+1)=f(n)+3$. Compute $f(4)$. $f$ is the arithmetic progression $2,5,8,11,14,\ldots$, so $f(4)=2+4\cdot 3=14$. Primitive recursion is exactly this kind of step-by-step build-up from a base case.

q: What does the diagonal machine $D$ in the halting-problem proof do on input $\langle M\rangle$?
text: What does the diagonal machine $D$ in the halting-problem proof do on input $\langle M\rangle$? If $h(\langle M,\langle M\rangle\rangle)=$ "halts", $D$ enters an infinite loop; otherwise $D$ halts. Running $D$ on its own code yields the contradiction "$D$ halts iff $D$ doesn't halt".

q: Why is countability of Turing machines essential to the diagonal argument?
text: Why is countability of Turing machines essential to the diagonal argument? Each TM has a finite description, so the set of TMs is countable. Cantor's diagonal construction of $D$ depends on enumerating the candidates and disagreeing with each at one diagonal coordinate.

q: Which problem is computationally equivalent to the halting problem?
text: Which problem is computationally equivalent to the halting problem? TM-equivalence is undecidable — Rice's theorem and a direct halting reduction both show it. The other three options are decidable: state count is computable, DFA emptiness is graph reachability, parity of input length is trivial.

q: A set $A$ is recursive iff:
text: A set $A$ is recursive iff: If $A$ and $\mathbb{N}\setminus A$ both have semi-deciders, run them in parallel — exactly one halts on each input, giving a decider for $A$. Conversely a decider trivially yields semi-deciders for both.

q: The halting set $H$ is:
text: The halting set $H$ is: Simulating $M$ on $w$ is a semi-decider for $H$ (halt iff $M$ halts), so $H$ is r.e. The diagonal proof rules out a decider, so $H$ is not recursive. Its complement is not r.e. — that's a separate diagonal.

q: Which is an example of a semi-decidable but not decidable property?
text: Which is an example of a semi-decidable but not decidable property? Dovetail $M$ on $0,1,2,\ldots$; if any branch accepts, halt. That's a semi-decider. By Rice's theorem the property is undecidable, so it is r.e. but not recursive. The other three are recursive (decided by inspecting the description or input directly).

q: $A\le_m B$ means:
text: $A\le_m B$ means: Many-one reduction wraps each instance of $A$ as an instance of $B$ via a total computable map $f$. A decider for $B$ then composes with $f$ to decide $A$ — so undecidability of $A$ propagates upward to $B$.

q: Rice's theorem says, in one line:
text: Rice's theorem says, in one line: Rice rules out semantic decidability — anything that depends only on $L(M)$, that is non-trivial (some TM has it, some doesn't), is undecidable. Syntactic properties of the description ("has 7 states") are unaffected.

q: Suppose $H\le_m B$ (halting reduces to $B$). What can you conclude?
text: Suppose $H\le_m B$ (halting reduces to $B$). What can you conclude? Reductions transfer hardness upward. If $B$ were decidable, composition with the reduction would decide halting — a contradiction. We can't conclude anything sharper without more information about $B$.

q: How does the halting problem give Gödel's first incompleteness theorem?
text: How does the halting problem give Gödel's first incompleteness theorem? "$M$ halts on $w$" is $\Sigma_1$, so a complete sound recursive $T$ would let us search for proofs of either it or its negation, deciding halting. Halting is undecidable, hence $T$ is incomplete: some true $\Sigma_1$ sentence is unprovable.

q: What does Rosser's refinement contribute?
text: What does Rosser's refinement contribute? Gödel's original proof needed $\omega$-consistency; Rosser's modified self-referential sentence ("for every proof of me there is a shorter refutation") only needs $T$ to be consistent.

q: Gödel's second incompleteness theorem says:
text: Gödel's second incompleteness theorem says: Formalise the first-theorem proof inside $T$: from $\mathrm{Con}(T)$, $T$ derives the unprovability of its Gödel sentence $G$, hence $G$ itself — contradicting $T\not\vdash G$. So $T\not\vdash\mathrm{Con}(T)$.

title: Time complexity and big-O
blurb: Fix a (multitape) Turing-machine model and let $t_M(x)$ be the number of steps $M$ runs on input $x$. The class $\mathrm{DTIME}(f(n))$ is the languages decided by some $M$ with $t_M(x)\le f(|x|)$ for all $x$. Asymptotic notation hides constants and lower-order terms: $f=O(g)$ if $\exists c, n_0$ with $f(n)\le c\,g(n)$ for $n\ge n_0$. The polynomial-time class $\mathsf{P}=\bigcup_k \mathrm{DTIME}(n^k)$ is the working notion of "feasible". The exponential gap $n^k$ vs $2^n$ is the central drama.

title: P and NP
blurb: $\mathsf{P}$ is the class of decision problems solvable by a deterministic TM in polynomial time. $\mathsf{NP}$ is the class of problems with a polynomial-time verifier : $L\in\mathsf{NP}$ iff there is a polynomial-time $V(x,w)$ and a polynomial $p$ with $x\in L \iff \exists w,|w|\le p(|x|),\,V(x,w)=1$. Witnesses: a satisfying assignment for SAT, a Hamiltonian cycle for HAM-CYC, a clique for CLIQUE. Equivalently $\mathsf{NP}=\bigcup_k \mathrm{NTIME}(n^k)$ — nondeterministic polynomial time. Whether $\mathsf{P}=\mathsf{NP}$ is the headline open problem.

title: NP-completeness and Cook–Levin
blurb: A language $L$ is NP-hard if every $A\in\mathsf{NP}$ admits a polynomial-time many-one (Karp) reduction $A\le_p L$. It is NP-complete if additionally $L\in\mathsf{NP}$. Cook–Levin (1971/73): SAT is NP-complete.
