q: How many points does the distinguished open set $D(6) = \{\mathfrak{p}:6\notin\mathfrak{p}\} \subset \Spec(\mathbb{Z}/30)$ contain?
text: How many points does the distinguished open set $D(6) = \{\mathfrak{p}:6\notin\mathfrak{p}\} \subset \Spec(\mathbb{Z}/30)$ contain? Primes of $\mathbb{Z}/30$ are $(2),(3),(5)$. $D(6)$ excludes primes containing $6$, i.e. containing $2$ or $3$, so it excludes $(2)$ and $(3)$, leaving $\{(5)\}$: $1$ point.

q: Which subset of $\Spec(\mathbb{Z})$ is NOT closed in the Zariski topology?
text: Which subset of $\Spec(\mathbb{Z})$ is NOT closed in the Zariski topology? Closed sets are $V(I)$ for ideals $I$. $\{(2),(3)\}=V((6))$ is closed; $\Spec=V((0))$; $\emptyset=V((1))$. But $\{(0)\}$ would be $V(I)$ only if $I=(0)$, and $V((0))=\Spec$, not $\{(0)\}$. The generic point $(0)$ is not closed.

q: For a ring $A$, the map $\Spec(A/I)\to\Spec(A)$ induced by $A\twoheadrightarrow A/I$ is:
text: For a ring $A$, the map $\Spec(A/I)\to\Spec(A)$ induced by $A\twoheadrightarrow A/I$ is: Primes of $A/I$ biject with primes of $A$ containing $I$, i.e. with $V(I)$; the bijection is a Zariski homeomorphism. Similarly $\Spec(S^{-1}A)$ embeds as the open set of primes disjoint from $S$. Primes of $A/I$ correspond to primes of $A$ containing $I$.

q: Select all properties that $\Spec(A)$ always satisfies as a topological space.
text: Select all properties that $\Spec(A)$ always satisfies as a topological space. $\Spec(A)$ is always quasi-compact and $T_0$, never Hausdorff unless zero-dimensional (non-closed generic points). Connectedness fails for $A\times B$: $\Spec(A\times B)=\Spec(A)\sqcup\Spec(B)$.

q: How many irreducible components does $\Spec(k[x,y]/(xy(x-1)))$ have?
text: How many irreducible components does $\Spec(k[x,y]/(xy(x-1)))$ have? $(xy(x-1)) = (x)\cap(y)\cap(x-1)$ as a radical ideal. The three minimal primes correspond to three irreducible components: the $y$-axis $(x)$, the $x$-axis $(y)$, and the line $x=1$. Decompose $(xy(x-1))$ into its prime components.

q: The nilradical $\mathrm{nil}(A)$ of a commutative ring $A$ equals:
text: The nilradical $\mathrm{nil}(A)$ of a commutative ring $A$ equals: $\mathrm{nil}(A) = \{x : x^n = 0\text{ some }n\}$. A classical theorem: this equals $\bigcap\mathfrak{p}$ over all primes. The intersection over minimal primes also works (it's the same set).

q: What is the nilradical of $\mathbb{Z}/72\mathbb{Z}$? (Give its smallest nonnegative generator.)
text: What is the nilradical of $\mathbb{Z}/72\mathbb{Z}$? (Give its smallest nonnegative generator.) $72 = 2^3\cdot 3^2$. Nilradical = $(\mathrm{rad}(72)) = (2\cdot 3) = (6)$. An element is nilpotent iff it's divisible by every prime dividing $72$, i.e. by $6$.

q: An element $x\in A$ lies in the Jacobson radical $\mathrm{Jac}(A)$ iff:
text: An element $x\in A$ lies in the Jacobson radical $\mathrm{Jac}(A)$ iff: Characterization: $x\in\mathrm{Jac}(A) \Leftrightarrow 1-xy \in A^\times$ for all $y$. In a local ring this says $x$ is in the maximal ideal; more generally $\mathrm{Jac}(A) = \bigcap\mathfrak{m}$.

q: Which inclusion between nilradical and Jacobson radical is always true for commutative rings $A$?
text: Which inclusion between nilradical and Jacobson radical is always true for commutative rings $A$? Every maximal ideal is prime, so $\bigcap\mathfrak{m} \supseteq \bigcap\mathfrak{p}$, i.e. $\mathrm{Jac}(A)\supseteq\mathrm{nil}(A)$. Equality fails e.g. for $\mathbb{Z}_{(p)}$: $\mathrm{nil}=(0)$ but $\mathrm{Jac}=(p)\ne 0$. Every maximal ideal is prime.

q: Select all rings $A$ for which $\mathrm{nil}(A) = \mathrm{Jac}(A)$.
text: Select all rings $A$ for which $\mathrm{nil}(A) = \mathrm{Jac}(A)$. In Artinian rings, every prime is maximal, so the two intersections agree. Jacobson rings are defined by $\mathrm{nil}(A/I)=\mathrm{Jac}(A/I)$ for every $I$, in particular for $I=0$. For $\mathbb{Z}$: $\mathrm{nil}=(0)$, $\mathrm{Jac}=\bigcap(p)=(0)$ — equal! But $k[x]$: $\mathrm{nil}=(0)$, $\mathrm{Jac}=\bigcap(f)=(0)$... actually these are both $(0)$ for one-dimensional Jacobson domains. Option B is valid too; but stricter rings like $\mathbb{Z}_{(p)}$ fail.

q: Let $A=k[x,y]/(x^2,xy)$. Compute $\dim_k(\mathrm{nil}(A))$ as a $k$-vector space.
text: Let $A=k[x,y]/(x^2,xy)$. Compute $\dim_k(\mathrm{nil}(A))$ as a $k$-vector space. $A$ has basis $\{1,\bar y,\bar y^2,\dots,\bar x\}$ over $k$. Since $A/(\bar x)\cong k[y]$ is reduced, $\mathrm{nil}(A) = (\bar x)$. And $(\bar x) = k\cdot\bar x$ (since $\bar x\bar y = 0$), giving $\dim_k\mathrm{nil}(A)=1$. $A/(\bar x)\cong k[y]$ is reduced, so $\mathrm{nil}(A) \subseteq (\bar x)$.

q: Over $\mathbb{Z}$, which of the following is $\mathbb{Z}/4 \otimes_\mathbb{Z} \mathbb{Z}/6$?
text: Over $\mathbb{Z}$, which of the following is $\mathbb{Z}/4 \otimes_\mathbb{Z} \mathbb{Z}/6$? $\mathbb{Z}/m\otimes_\mathbb{Z}\mathbb{Z}/n \cong \mathbb{Z}/\gcd(m,n)$. Here $\gcd(4,6)=2$.

q: How many $\mathbb{Z}$-submodules does $\mathbb{Z}/6\mathbb{Z}$ have?
text: How many $\mathbb{Z}$-submodules does $\mathbb{Z}/6\mathbb{Z}$ have? Submodules of $\mathbb{Z}/6$ correspond to divisors of $6$: $1,2,3,6$, giving $4$ submodules: $0,\;2\mathbb{Z}/6,\;3\mathbb{Z}/6,\;\mathbb{Z}/6$ itself.

q: For which sequence is the claim 'tensoring with $\mathbb{Z}/2$ over $\mathbb{Z}$ preserves exactness' FALSE?
text: For which sequence is the claim 'tensoring with $\mathbb{Z}/2$ over $\mathbb{Z}$ preserves exactness' FALSE? Tensoring the first sequence with $\mathbb{Z}/2$ gives $\mathbb{Z}/2 \xrightarrow{2=0}\mathbb{Z}/2$, so injectivity fails. The other sequences either split or are tensored with a coprime factor.

q: Let $M, N$ be finitely generated modules over a commutative ring $A$. The canonical map $M^\vee \otimes N \to \Hom(M, N)$ (where $M^\vee = \Hom(M, A)$) is an isomorphism when:
text: Let $M, N$ be finitely generated modules over a commutative ring $A$. The canonical map $M^\vee \otimes N \to \Hom(M, N)$ (where $M^\vee = \Hom(M, A)$) is an isomorphism when: $M^\vee\otimes N\to\Hom(M,N)$ is an iso when $M$ is f.g. projective (dualizable in $A\mathbf{-Mod}$). It fails for general f.g. $M$ (e.g. torsion modules over a domain). The isomorphism requires dualizability of $M$.

q: Select all statements that hold for modules $M$ over a commutative ring $A$.
text: Select all statements that hold for modules $M$ over a commutative ring $A$. $\Hom(A,-)$ and $-\otimes A$ are both the identity functor on $A\mathbf{-Mod}$. Coproducts (direct sums) are left adjoint to diagonal. The last is false: $\mathbb{Z}/n$ is f.g. over $\mathbb{Z}$ but not free.

q: Compute $\dim_{\mathbb{F}_p}(\mathbb{F}_p[x]/(x^3) \otimes_{\mathbb{F}_p[x]} \mathbb{F}_p[x]/(x^7))$.
text: Compute $\dim_{\mathbb{F}_p}(\mathbb{F}_p[x]/(x^3) \otimes_{\mathbb{F}_p[x]} \mathbb{F}_p[x]/(x^7))$. $\mathbb{F}_p[x]/(x^3)\otimes_{\mathbb{F}_p[x]}\mathbb{F}_p[x]/(x^7) \cong \mathbb{F}_p[x]/((x^3)+(x^7))=\mathbb{F}_p[x]/(x^3)$ since $(x^3)\supseteq(x^7)$. Dimension $= 3$. Use $A/I \otimes_A A/J \cong A/(I+J)$.

q: A commutative ring $A$ is Noetherian iff:
text: A commutative ring $A$ is Noetherian iff: The ascending chain condition is the defining property; equivalently, every ideal is finitely generated. Principal ideal domains are stronger; finite prime count fails for $\mathbb{Z}$ (which is Noetherian).

q: Which of the following are Noetherian?
text: Which of the following are Noetherian? $\mathbb{Z}$ is a PID (Noetherian). $k[x,y]/(xy)$ is a quotient of the Noetherian $k[x,y]$. $k[x_1,x_2,\dots]$ has the non-stabilizing chain $(x_1)\subsetneq(x_1,x_2)\subsetneq\cdots$. $C(\mathbb{R})$ has chains of vanishing ideals that don't stabilize.

q: The Hilbert basis theorem says: if $A$ is Noetherian then so is $A[x]$. Applying this repeatedly, $k[x_1,\dots,x_n]$ is Noetherian over any field $k$. How many generators does the ideal $(x^5, x^3 y, y^7)\subset k[x,y]$ need, minimally?
text: The Hilbert basis theorem says: if $A$ is Noetherian then so is $A[x]$. Applying this repeatedly, $k[x_1,\dots,x_n]$ is Noetherian over any field $k$. How many generators does the ideal $(x^5, x^3 y, y^7)\subset k[x,y]$ need, minimally? In a monomial ideal, minimal generators are the monomials not divisible by other monomials in the generating set. $x^5, x^3y, y^7$: none divides another, so all three are needed. $3$ generators.

q: Arrange the steps of the proof of the Hilbert basis theorem (that $A$ Noetherian $\Rightarrow A[x]$ Noetherian).
text: Arrange the steps of the proof of the Hilbert basis theorem (that $A$ Noetherian $\Rightarrow A[x]$ Noetherian). The classical proof: first define $J$ = leading coefficients of elements of $I$ (this is an ideal of $A$), pick generators via Noetherianness of $A$, use them to reduce any $g\in I$ modulo bounded-degree pieces, and finish with f.g. coefficient modules. Start by extracting data from $I$; end by invoking ACC on $A$.

q: Which ring is NOT Noetherian but has an 'ascending chain condition on finitely generated ideals'?
text: Which ring is NOT Noetherian but has an 'ascending chain condition on finitely generated ideals'? Non-discrete valuation rings have totally ordered ideal structure, so principal/f.g. ideals form a chain, but the order-type can be $\mathbb{R}$-like, violating ACC on all ideals. Polynomial rings in $\infty$ variables fail ACC on f.g. ideals too. A valuation ring has totally ordered ideals — but the ordering might not be well-founded.

q: Let $A = k[x,y,z]/(x^2, xy, yz, z^2)$ (monomial quotient). How many associated primes does $A$ have as an $A$-module of itself?
text: Let $A = k[x,y,z]/(x^2, xy, yz, z^2)$ (monomial quotient). How many associated primes does $A$ have as an $A$-module of itself? The only minimal prime containing $(x^2,xy,yz,z^2)$ is the nilradical's associated prime $(x,y,z)$: every nonzero element's annihilator is contained in $(x,y,z)$. One associated prime. Consider annihilators of nonzero elements.

q: Akizuki–Hopkins says a commutative ring is Artinian iff it is:
text: Akizuki–Hopkins says a commutative ring is Artinian iff it is: Artinian = DCC on ideals = Noetherian + Krull dimension $0$. Reduced Artinian rings are products of fields, but the unreduced cases like $k[\epsilon]/(\epsilon^2)$ are also Artinian and are the deformation-theoretic point.

q: What is the length $\ell_k(A)$ (equivalently $\dim_k A$) of $A = k[x,y]/(x,y)^3$ as a $k$-vector space?
text: What is the length $\ell_k(A)$ (equivalently $\dim_k A$) of $A = k[x,y]/(x,y)^3$ as a $k$-vector space? Basis $\{1, x, y, x^2, xy, y^2\}$. Length grows as $1 + 2 + 3 = 6 = \binom{3+1}{2}$. In general $\dim_k k[x_1,\dots,x_d]/(x_1,\dots,x_d)^n = \binom{n+d-1}{d}$.

q: Select all rings that are Artinian local with residue field $k$ (so live in $\mathrm{Art}_k$).
text: Select all rings that are Artinian local with residue field $k$ (so live in $\mathrm{Art}_k$). $k[\epsilon]/(\epsilon^2)$, $k[x,y]/(x^2,xy,y^2)$, and $k[x]/(x^5)$ are Artinian local with residue field $k$ — finite-dimensional over $k$ with a single maximal ideal that is nilpotent. $k[x]$ is not Artinian (infinite-dimensional, dim $1$). $k\times k$ is Artinian but not local — it has two maximal ideals.

q: Which Artinian local $k$-algebra is the universal first-order thickening — the test ring for first-order deformations of $\Spec\,k$?
text: Which Artinian local $k$-algebra is the universal first-order thickening — the test ring for first-order deformations of $\Spec\,k$? $k[\epsilon]/(\epsilon^2)$ — the dual numbers — has length $2$ with $\mathfrak{m}^2 = 0$ and embedding dimension $1$. A morphism $\Spec\,k[\epsilon]/(\epsilon^2) \to X_0$ is exactly a $k$-point together with a tangent vector; flat lifts of $X_0$ to $\Spec\,k[\epsilon]/(\epsilon^2)$ are first-order deformations.

q: Localizing $\mathbb{Z}$ at the prime $(p)$ gives $\mathbb{Z}_{(p)}$. Its unique maximal ideal is:
text: Localizing $\mathbb{Z}$ at the prime $(p)$ gives $\mathbb{Z}_{(p)}$. Its unique maximal ideal is: A localization at a prime is a local ring; its maximal ideal is the extension of that prime. Elements of $\mathbb{Z}_{(p)}$ are fractions $a/b$ with $p\nmid b$, and the non-units are exactly the numerators divisible by $p$.

q: How many primes less than $100$ survive in $\mathbb{Z}[1/2]$ (i.e. remain non-unit primes)?
text: How many primes less than $100$ survive in $\mathbb{Z}[1/2]$ (i.e. remain non-unit primes)? Primes of $S^{-1}A$ correspond to primes of $A$ disjoint from $S$. Inverting $2$ kills $(2)$, leaving odd primes $<100$: $3,5,7,11,\dots,97$, which is $24$ primes.

q: The residue field $A_{\mathfrak{p}}/\mathfrak{p}A_{\mathfrak{p}}$ at a prime $\mathfrak{p}$ of $A$ equals:
text: The residue field $A_{\mathfrak{p}}/\mathfrak{p}A_{\mathfrak{p}}$ at a prime $\mathfrak{p}$ of $A$ equals: Modding $A_{\mathfrak{p}}$ by its maximal ideal yields the fraction field of $A/\mathfrak{p}$. For $A=\mathbb{Z}$, $\mathfrak{p}=(p)$: residue field $\mathbb{F}_p=\mathrm{Frac}(\mathbb{Z}/p)$.

q: Let $A=k[x,y]_{(x,y)}$ (local ring at the origin). Which ring is $A/(xy)$ as a local ring?
text: Let $A=k[x,y]_{(x,y)}$ (local ring at the origin). Which ring is $A/(xy)$ as a local ring? Localization and quotient commute: $A/(xy)=(k[x,y]/(xy))_{(x,y)}$ — the local ring of the node at the origin, non-domain with minimal primes $(x)$ and $(y)$. Localization commutes with quotient.

q: Let $A=\mathbb{Z}_{(5)}$. How many ideals does $A/(125)$ have?
text: Let $A=\mathbb{Z}_{(5)}$. How many ideals does $A/(125)$ have? $A/(125)\cong\mathbb{Z}/125$ as rings: local with chain $(0)\subsetneq(25)\subsetneq(5)\subsetneq(1)$. $4$ ideals.

q: Arrange: why is localization exact? Pick the correct one-line proof.
text: Arrange: why is localization exact? Pick the correct one-line proof. $S^{-1}M\cong M\otimes_A S^{-1}A$; $S^{-1}A$ is flat over $A$ (a direct-limit of free modules), so tensoring preserves exactness.

q: A student claims $\mathbb{Z}/3$ is a flat $\mathbb{Z}$-module. Identify the flawed step in their argument.
text: A student claims $\mathbb{Z}/3$ is a flat $\mathbb{Z}$-module. Identify the flawed step in their argument. Step 2 is the flaw. Tensor product is only right exact; preserving injections IS the definition of flatness, so claiming it 'in general' begs the question. In fact step 1 reveals the issue concretely: the induced map is multiplication by $3$ on $\mathbb{Z}/3$, which is the zero map — not injective. So $\mathrm{Tor}_1^{\mathbb{Z}}(\mathbb{Z}/3,\mathbb{Z}/3) = \mathbb{Z}/3 \ne 0$, witnessing non-flatness. Over a PID, flat $=$ torsion-free, and $\mathbb{Z}/3$ has torsion. Tensor product is right exact, not exact in general — preserving injectivity is exactly the flatness hypothesis we are trying to verify.

q: Compute $\dim_{\mathbb{F}_2}\mathrm{Tor}_1^{\mathbb{Z}}(\mathbb{Z}/2,\mathbb{Z}/2)$.
text: Compute $\dim_{\mathbb{F}_2}\mathrm{Tor}_1^{\mathbb{Z}}(\mathbb{Z}/2,\mathbb{Z}/2)$. Resolve $\mathbb{Z}/2$ via $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{2}\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}/2\to 0$, tensor with $\mathbb{Z}/2$: $\mathbb{Z}/2\xrightarrow{0}\mathbb{Z}/2$, kernel $\mathbb{Z}/2$.

q: For a Noetherian local ring $(A,\mathfrak{m},k)$, a finitely generated $A$-module $M$ is flat iff:
text: For a Noetherian local ring $(A,\mathfrak{m},k)$, a finitely generated $A$-module $M$ is flat iff: Over a Noetherian local ring, f.g. flat = free (local criterion plus Nakayama).

q: Let $0\to M'\to M\to M''\to 0$ be exact with $M''$ flat. Which is true?
text: Let $0\to M'\to M\to M''\to 0$ be exact with $M''$ flat. Which is true? $\mathrm{Tor}_1(M'',N)=0$ gives exactness of $0\to M'\otimes N\to M\otimes N$; extend by $\mathrm{Tor}_2$ for higher statements. So $M$ flat $\Leftrightarrow$ $M'$ flat. Use the long exact Tor sequence.

q: Compute the order of $\mathrm{Tor}_2^{\mathbb{Z}}(\mathbb{Z}/6,\mathbb{Z}/4)$.
text: Compute the order of $\mathrm{Tor}_2^{\mathbb{Z}}(\mathbb{Z}/6,\mathbb{Z}/4)$. $\mathbb{Z}$ has global dimension $1$, so $\mathrm{Tor}_i^{\mathbb{Z}}=0$ for $i\ge 2$. Trivial group has order $1$.

q: Which example shows torsion-free $\ne$ flat over a non-PID?
text: Which example shows torsion-free $\ne$ flat over a non-PID? $(x,y)$ is torsion-free (injects in fraction field), but not flat: $\mathrm{Tor}_1^{k[x,y]}(k[x,y]/(x,y),(x,y))\ne 0$.

q: Let $(A,\mathfrak{m},k)$ be local Noetherian and $M$ a f.g. $A$-module. We want to show: if $\bar m_1,\dots,\bar m_r$ span $M/\mathfrak{m}M$ over $k$, then $m_1,\dots,m_r$ generate $M$ over $A$. Pick the correct next step.
text: Let $(A,\mathfrak{m},k)$ be local Noetherian and $M$ a f.g. $A$-module. We want to show: if $\bar m_1,\dots,\bar m_r$ span $M/\mathfrak{m}M$ over $k$, then $m_1,\dots,m_r$ generate $M$ over $A$. Pick the correct next step. Nakayama corollary: if $M/N$ is f.g. and equals $\mathfrak{m}\cdot(M/N)$, then $M/N = 0$, so $N = M$ and $m_1,\dots,m_r$ generate $M$. Equivalently, minimal generators of $M$ biject with a basis of $M/\mathfrak{m}M$. Nakayama: a f.g. module $N'$ with $\mathfrak{m}\cdot N' = N'$ must vanish.

q: For the nodal cubic $y^2=x^3+x^2$ at the origin $p$, compute $\dim_k(\mathfrak{m}/\mathfrak{m}^2)$ of the local ring at $p$.
text: For the nodal cubic $y^2=x^3+x^2$ at the origin $p$, compute $\dim_k(\mathfrak{m}/\mathfrak{m}^2)$ of the local ring at $p$. Both partials of $y^2-x^3-x^2$ vanish at origin; Jacobian zero. Zariski tangent space has dimension $2$ while the curve is $1$-dim: singular.

q: Select every Noetherian local ring $(A,\mathfrak{m},k)$ that is regular, i.e. has $\dim_k\mathfrak{m}/\mathfrak{m}^2 = \dim A$.
text: Select every Noetherian local ring $(A,\mathfrak{m},k)$ that is regular, i.e. has $\dim_k\mathfrak{m}/\mathfrak{m}^2 = \dim A$. Regularity means $\dim_k\mathfrak{m}/\mathfrak{m}^2 = \dim A$. $\mathbb{Z}_{(p)}$ is a DVR (dim 1, $\mathfrak{m}/\mathfrak{m}^2$ 1-dim). $k[x,y]_{(x,y)}$ is regular of dim 2. $k[[x_1,\dots,x_n]]$ is regular of dim $n$. The node $k[x,y]/(xy)$ at the origin has Krull dim $1$ but $\mathfrak{m}/\mathfrak{m}^2$ is spanned by $x,y$ (dim 2), so it is singular. Regularity: embedding dimension equals Krull dimension. The node has $\dim_k\mathfrak{m}/\mathfrak{m}^2 = 2$ but Krull dimension $1$.

q: Pick the key step in proving $\mathfrak{m}N=N\Rightarrow N=0$ for $N\subseteq M$ a submodule of an f.g. module over a local ring.
text: Pick the key step in proving $\mathfrak{m}N=N\Rightarrow N=0$ for $N\subseteq M$ a submodule of an f.g. module over a local ring. $\mathfrak{m}N=N$ gives matrix equation $Mn=n$; Cayley–Hamilton yields $p(1)\in 1+\mathfrak{m}$ annihilating $N$. $p(1)$ is a unit, so $N=0$. Look for a monic polynomial whose constant term is a unit mod $\mathfrak{m}$.

q: Let $A=k[[t]]$, $\mathfrak{m}=(t)$. Compute $\dim_k\mathfrak{m}^3/\mathfrak{m}^4$.
text: Let $A=k[[t]]$, $\mathfrak{m}=(t)$. Compute $\dim_k\mathfrak{m}^3/\mathfrak{m}^4$. $\mathfrak{m}^n=(t^n)$, so $\mathfrak{m}^3/\mathfrak{m}^4\cong k$ spanned by $t^3$.

q: For a regular local ring $(A,\mathfrak{m},k)$ of dim $d$, which is true of $\mathrm{gr}_\mathfrak{m}A$?
text: For a regular local ring $(A,\mathfrak{m},k)$ of dim $d$, which is true of $\mathrm{gr}_\mathfrak{m}A$? Regular $\Leftrightarrow$ $\mathrm{gr}_\mathfrak{m}A\cong k[x_1,\dots,x_d]$ (polynomial). Completion gives power series.

q: Which is an integral extension?
text: Which is an integral extension? $\sqrt{-5}$ satisfies $t^2+5=0$, monic over $\mathbb{Z}$.

q: By Noether normalization, $B=k[x,y]/(xy-1)$ is finite over $k[z]$ for some $z$. Minimum $[B:k[z]]$?
text: By Noether normalization, $B=k[x,y]/(xy-1)$ is finite over $k[z]$ for some $z$. Minimum $[B:k[z]]$? Choose $z=x+y$: $x,y$ satisfy $t^2-zt+1=0$, so $B$ is free of rank $2$ over $k[z]$.

q: If $A\subseteq B$ is integral of domains, which fails in general?
text: If $A\subseteq B$ is integral of domains, which fails in general? $\overline{\mathbb{Q}}$ is integral over $\mathbb{Z}$ but not f.g.

q: $A\subseteq B$ integral of Noetherian domains, $A$ integrally closed. Primes $\mathfrak{q}_1,\mathfrak{q}_2$ over $\mathfrak{p}$:
text: $A\subseteq B$ integral of Noetherian domains, $A$ integrally closed. Primes $\mathfrak{q}_1,\mathfrak{q}_2$ over $\mathfrak{p}$: Galois group acts transitively on the fiber; in general fibers are incomparable but need not collapse. Incomparability + Galois transitivity.

q: How many primes of $\mathbb{Z}[i]$ lie over $(5)\subset\mathbb{Z}$?
text: How many primes of $\mathbb{Z}[i]$ lie over $(5)\subset\mathbb{Z}$? $5=(2+i)(2-i)$ splits since $5\equiv 1\pmod 4$. Two primes.

q: In Noether normalization, the role of the linear change $x_i\mapsto x_i - \lambda_i x_n$ is to:
text: In Noether normalization, the role of the linear change $x_i\mapsto x_i - \lambda_i x_n$ is to: Generic $\lambda$ ensures the top $x_n$-power coefficient in $f$ is nonzero; after scaling, monic in $x_n$, so $x_n$ is integral over the others.

q: Krull dimension of $k[x_1,x_2,x_3,x_4]$?
text: Krull dimension of $k[x_1,x_2,x_3,x_4]$? $\dim k[x_1,\dots,x_n]=n$; chain $(0)\subsetneq(x_1)\subsetneq\cdots\subsetneq(x_1,\dots,x_4)$.

q: $\dim k[x,y]/(xy)$?
text: $\dim k[x,y]/(xy)$? Two coordinate axes; longest chain is (generic of one axis) $\subsetneq$ (origin): dim $1$.

q: $\dim\mathbb{Z}[x]$?
text: $\dim\mathbb{Z}[x]$? $\dim A[x]=\dim A+1$ for Noetherian $A$. $\dim\mathbb{Z}=1$, so $\dim\mathbb{Z}[x]=2$. Chain: $(0)\subsetneq(p)\subsetneq(p,x)$.

q: Compute $\dim k[x,y,z]/(xz-y^2, yz-x^3, z^2-x^2 y)$ (twisted cubic).
text: Compute $\dim k[x,y,z]/(xz-y^2, yz-x^3, z^2-x^2 y)$ (twisted cubic). Twisted cubic is a $1$-dim curve $t\mapsto(t,t^2,t^3)$.

q: Which is a Noetherian but non-catenary ring?
text: Which is a Noetherian but non-catenary ring? Nagata built a Noetherian domain with chains of two different maximal lengths. Polynomial rings, fields, and $\mathbb{Z}$ are universally catenary.

q: Compute $\dim \mathbb{R}[x,y,z]/(x^2+y^2+z^2-1)$.
text: Compute $\dim \mathbb{R}[x,y,z]/(x^2+y^2+z^2-1)$. $2$-sphere is $2$-dim irreducible variety; $\dim$ = tr.deg = $2$.

q: The transcendence degree $\mathrm{tr.deg}_k(k(x,y))$ of the field of rational functions in two variables is:
text: The transcendence degree $\mathrm{tr.deg}_k(k(x,y))$ of the field of rational functions in two variables is: $\{x,y\}$ is algebraically independent over $k$, so $\mathrm{tr.deg}=2$. This is the same as the Krull dimension of $k[x,y]$.

q: Let $B = k[x,y,z]/(z - xy)$. Compute $\mathrm{tr.deg}_k\,\mathrm{Frac}(B)$.
text: Let $B = k[x,y,z]/(z - xy)$. Compute $\mathrm{tr.deg}_k\,\mathrm{Frac}(B)$. The relation $z=xy$ makes $z$ algebraically dependent on $x,y$. $\mathrm{Frac}(B)=k(x,y)$, with tr.deg $2$. (Matches $\dim B = 2$.)

q: For a finitely generated integral domain $B$ over an algebraically closed field $k$, which theorem asserts $\dim B = \mathrm{tr.deg}_k\,\mathrm{Frac}(B)$?
text: For a finitely generated integral domain $B$ over an algebraically closed field $k$, which theorem asserts $\dim B = \mathrm{tr.deg}_k\,\mathrm{Frac}(B)$? Noether normalization gives a finite map $B\twoheadleftarrow k[y_1,\dots,y_d]$ with $d=\mathrm{tr.deg}$; going-up preserves dimension; $\dim k[y_1,\dots,y_d]=d$.

q: Let $f\colon X\to Y$ be a dominant morphism of irreducible varieties over $\overline k$ with $\dim X > \dim Y$. Pick the correct generic-fiber statement.
text: Let $f\colon X\to Y$ be a dominant morphism of irreducible varieties over $\overline k$ with $\dim X > \dim Y$. Pick the correct generic-fiber statement. The dimension-of-fiber theorem: generic fiber has dim $=\dim X - \dim Y$; special fibers can only jump up. E.g. blow-up has generic fiber a point but exceptional fiber a curve. Upper-semicontinuity of fiber dimension.

q: Compute $\mathrm{tr.deg}_{\mathbb{Q}}(\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2}, \pi, e))$ assuming $\pi, e$ are algebraically independent over $\mathbb{Q}$ (this is open, but assume it).
text: Compute $\mathrm{tr.deg}_{\mathbb{Q}}(\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2}, \pi, e))$ assuming $\pi, e$ are algebraically independent over $\mathbb{Q}$ (this is open, but assume it). $\sqrt{2}$ is algebraic over $\mathbb{Q}$ (contributes $0$ to tr.deg). $\pi, e$ assumed algebraically independent contribute $2$. Total: $2$.

q: For a purely transcendental extension $k\subset k(t_1,\dots,t_n)$, which is true?
text: For a purely transcendental extension $k\subset k(t_1,\dots,t_n)$, which is true? Purely transcendental means a transcendence basis generates the extension. tr.deg = $n$; the field is $k(t_1,\dots,t_n) = \mathrm{Frac}(k[t_1,\dots,t_n])$.

title: Chain complexes and homology
blurb: Complexes encode algebraic data with differentials, and homology measures failure of exactness.

title: Exact sequences
blurb: Exactness at B means im f = ker g; short exact sequences 0→A→B→C→0 are the atomic building blocks of homological algebra.

title: The snake lemma
blurb: A commutative diagram with exact rows yields a connecting map δ:ker γ → coker α and a six-term exact sequence linking kernels to cokernels.

title: The five lemma
blurb: In a commutative ladder of exact rows, if the four outer vertical maps are isomorphisms then so is the middle one — the workhorse diagram-chase for recognizing isomorphisms.

title: Long exact sequence in homology
blurb: A short exact sequence of chain complexes induces a long exact sequence in homology, built by iterating the snake-lemma connecting map in each degree.

title: Projective modules
blurb: A module P is projective iff every surjection onto P splits; equivalently P is a direct summand of a free module and makes Hom(P,−) exact.

title: Injective modules
blurb: Dual to projective: I is injective iff every injection of I extends; Baer's criterion and divisibility characterize injectives over ℤ.

title: Flat modules
blurb: M is flat iff −⊗M preserves exactness; localizations are flat, projectives are flat, and over ℤ flatness coincides with torsion-freeness.

title: Derived functors and Ext/Tor
blurb: Projective or injective resolutions define Ext and Tor as computable invariants; the Ext/Tor tables pin down extensions and torsion.

title: Resolutions: projective, injective, flat
blurb: Every module has projective and injective resolutions; their lengths measure the global dimension of the ring.

title: Abelian categories and triangulated preview
blurb: Abelian categories are the native habitat of homological algebra; triangulated categories encode long exact sequences as distinguished triangles.

title: Spectral sequences
blurb: Spectral sequences stage difficult computations into successive approximation pages.

q: A chain complex $(C_\bullet, \partial)$ is a sequence of abelian groups with maps $\partial_n : C_n \to C_{n-1}$ satisfying:
text: A chain complex $(C_\bullet, \partial)$ is a sequence of abelian groups with maps $\partial_n : C_n \to C_{n-1}$ satisfying: The defining condition of a chain complex is $\partial^2=0$, i.e. $\partial_{n-1}\circ\partial_n=0$. This guarantees $\operatorname{im}\partial_{n+1}\subseteq \ker\partial_n$, so $H_n=\ker\partial_n/\operatorname{im}\partial_{n+1}$ is well-defined.

q: Let $C_\bullet$ be the simplicial chain complex of the standard triangle (boundary of a $2$-simplex) with $C_0=\mathbb{Q}^3,\ C_1=\mathbb{Q}^3$. What is $\dim_\mathbb{Q} H_1$?
text: Let $C_\bullet$ be the simplicial chain complex of the standard triangle (boundary of a $2$-simplex) with $C_0=\mathbb{Q}^3,\ C_1=\mathbb{Q}^3$. What is $\dim_\mathbb{Q} H_1$? The triangle is homotopy equivalent to $S^1$, so $H_0=\mathbb{Q}$ and $H_1=\mathbb{Q}$. Its one independent $1$-cycle is the loop around the triangle.

q: For the torus $T^2$ with rational Betti numbers $(b_0,b_1,b_2)=(1,2,1)$, compute the Euler characteristic $\chi(T^2)=b_0-b_1+b_2$.
text: For the torus $T^2$ with rational Betti numbers $(b_0,b_1,b_2)=(1,2,1)$, compute the Euler characteristic $\chi(T^2)=b_0-b_1+b_2$. $\chi(T^2)=1-2+1=0$. Every closed orientable surface of genus $g$ has $\chi=2-2g$; here $g=1$.

q: Which of the following is a valid chain complex over $\mathbb{Z}$?
text:
Which of the following is a valid chain complex over $\mathbb{Z}$? We need $\partial_{n-1}\circ\partial_n=0$. For option B: $0\circ 2=0$. For A: $3\circ 2=6
e 0$. For C: $\mathrm{id}\circ\mathrm{id}=\mathrm{id}
e 0$. For D: $2\circ 3=6
e 0$.

q: Let $C_\bullet$ be the chain complex $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{\cdot 3}\mathbb{Z}\to 0$ concentrated in degrees $1$ and $0$. Compute $|H_0(C_\bullet)|$ (the order of $H_0$).
text: Let $C_\bullet$ be the chain complex $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{\cdot 3}\mathbb{Z}\to 0$ concentrated in degrees $1$ and $0$. Compute $|H_0(C_\bullet)|$ (the order of $H_0$). $H_0=\ker(C_{-1}\to C_{-2})/\operatorname{im}(\partial_1)=\mathbb{Z}/3\mathbb{Z}$, which has order $3$.

q: A chain map $f_\bullet:C_\bullet\to D_\bullet$ consists of maps $f_n:C_n\to D_n$ satisfying which condition?
text: A chain map $f_\bullet:C_\bullet\to D_\bullet$ consists of maps $f_n:C_n\to D_n$ satisfying which condition? A chain map must commute with the boundary maps: $\partial^D\circ f=f\circ\partial^C$. This ensures $f$ descends to a well-defined map on homology groups.

q: Select every condition that is equivalent to $0\to A\xrightarrow{f}B\xrightarrow{g}C\to 0$ being a short exact sequence.
text: Select every condition that is equivalent to $0\to A\xrightarrow{f}B\xrightarrow{g}C\to 0$ being a short exact sequence. Short exactness asserts $f$ injective, $g$ surjective, and $\operatorname{im}f=\ker g$ — equivalently, $f$ identifies $A$ with $\ker g$ and $g$ induces $B/f(A)\cong C$. (1) is false: $g\circ f=0$ gives only $\operatorname{im}f\subseteq\ker g$, missing the reverse inclusion. (3) is too weak. (4) describes a (very) special split case. So (0) and (2) are correct. Short exactness needs $f$ injective, $g$ surjective, AND $\operatorname{im}f=\ker g$. Just $g\circ f=0$ gives only the inclusion $\operatorname{im}f\subseteq\ker g$.

q: For $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{\cdot n}\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}/n\to 0$, which summary is correct?
text: For $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{\cdot n}\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}/n\to 0$, which summary is correct? $\operatorname{im}(\cdot n)=n\mathbb{Z}=\ker(\text{mod }n)$, so it is short exact. A splitting would require a homomorphism $\mathbb{Z}/n\to\mathbb{Z}$, and there is none for $n\ge 2$ (any image is torsion-free). So it splits iff $n=1$, where the sequence is trivial.

q: For a short exact sequence $0\to A\to B\to C\to 0$ of finite abelian groups with $|A|=6$ and $|C|=10$, compute $|B|$.
text: For a short exact sequence $0\to A\to B\to C\to 0$ of finite abelian groups with $|A|=6$ and $|C|=10$, compute $|B|$. Orders multiply across a short exact sequence: $|B|=|A|\cdot|C|=6\cdot 10=60$. This is the group-theoretic analogue of $\dim B = \dim A + \dim C$ for vector spaces.

q: Which of the following is a counterexample showing that a short exact sequence $0\to A\to B\to C\to 0$ need not split?
text: Which of the following is a counterexample showing that a short exact sequence $0\to A\to B\to C\to 0$ need not split? $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{\cdot 2}\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}/2\to 0$ is short exact but does not split: a splitting would require a homomorphism $\mathbb{Z}/2\to\mathbb{Z}$, which is impossible since $\mathbb{Z}$ is torsion-free.

q: In the long exact sequence arising from $0\to A_\bullet\to B_\bullet\to C_\bullet\to 0$, suppose $H_1(B)=0$, $H_1(C)=\mathbb{Z}^2$, $H_0(A)=\mathbb{Z}^3$, and the connecting map $\partial:H_1(C)\to H_0(A)$ is injective. What is $\dim_{\mathbb{Q}}\operatorname{coker}(\partial)$?
text: In the long exact sequence arising from $0\to A_\bullet\to B_\bullet\to C_\bullet\to 0$, suppose $H_1(B)=0$, $H_1(C)=\mathbb{Z}^2$, $H_0(A)=\mathbb{Z}^3$, and the connecting map $\partial:H_1(C)\to H_0(A)$ is injective. What is $\dim_{\mathbb{Q}}\operatorname{coker}(\partial)$? $\partial:\mathbb{Z}^2\hookrightarrow\mathbb{Z}^3$ is injective (since $H_1(B)=0$ forces exactness). Its cokernel has rank $3-2=1$ over $\mathbb{Q}$.

q: The five-lemma: in a commutative diagram with exact rows, if the four outer vertical maps are isomorphisms, the middle map is:
text: The five-lemma: in a commutative diagram with exact rows, if the four outer vertical maps are isomorphisms, the middle map is: The five-lemma: with exact rows and the four outer verticals being isomorphisms, a standard diagram chase (injectivity first, then surjectivity) proves the middle map is also an isomorphism. Two diagram chases — injectivity then surjectivity — both ride on the outer isomorphisms.

q: Given the snake-lemma diagram with exact rows and vertical maps $\alpha,\beta,\gamma$, the connecting map $\delta$ has which source and target?
text: Given the snake-lemma diagram with exact rows and vertical maps $\alpha,\beta,\gamma$, the connecting map $\delta$ has which source and target? The snake connects $\ker\gamma$ (on the right of the top row) to $\operatorname{coker}\alpha$ (on the left of the bottom row) via the recipe lift $\to$ $\beta$ $\to$ pull back along $i'$.

q: Arrange the six middle terms of the snake-lemma exact sequence in their canonical order. The sequence has the shape $0\to\bullet\to\bullet\to\bullet\xrightarrow{\delta}\bullet\to\bullet\to\bullet\to 0$.
text: Arrange the six middle terms of the snake-lemma exact sequence in their canonical order. The sequence has the shape $0\to\bullet\to\bullet\to\bullet\xrightarrow{\delta}\bullet\to\bullet\to\bullet\to 0$. The snake-lemma sequence is $0\to\ker\alpha\to\ker\beta\to\ker\gamma\xrightarrow{\delta}\operatorname{coker}\alpha\to\operatorname{coker}\beta\to\operatorname{coker}\gamma\to 0$. Kernels appear first (in the same left-to-right order as the original columns), the connecting $\delta$ jumps from $\ker\gamma$ to $\operatorname{coker}\alpha$, then the cokernels finish in the same left-to-right order. Kernels first (left-to-right: $\ker\alpha\to\ker\beta\to\ker\gamma$), then the connecting $\delta$, then cokernels (left-to-right: $\operatorname{coker}\alpha\to\operatorname{coker}\beta\to\operatorname{coker}\gamma$).

q: In the widget's example ($\alpha=0, \beta=\cdot 2, \gamma=0$ on $\mathbb{Z}/2,\mathbb{Z}/4,\mathbb{Z}/2$), what is $\delta(1)\in\operatorname{coker}\alpha=\mathbb{Z}/2$?
text: In the widget's example ($\alpha=0, \beta=\cdot 2, \gamma=0$ on $\mathbb{Z}/2,\mathbb{Z}/4,\mathbb{Z}/2$), what is $\delta(1)\in\operatorname{coker}\alpha=\mathbb{Z}/2$? Lift $c=1$ to $b=1\in\mathbb{Z}/4$; $\beta(b)=2$; pull back along $i':a'\mapsto 2a'$ to get $a'=1\in\mathbb{Z}/2$. Since $\operatorname{im}\alpha=0$, $\delta(1)=1$.

q: In the snake-lemma diagram, which is the correct order of steps to construct $\delta(c)\in\operatorname{coker}\alpha$ from $c\in\ker\gamma$?
text: In the snake-lemma diagram, which is the correct order of steps to construct $\delta(c)\in\operatorname{coker}\alpha$ from $c\in\ker\gamma$? The snake recipe: (1) lift $c$ to some $b\in B$ using surjectivity from $j$, (2) compute $\beta(b)\in B'$, (3) since $j'(\beta(b))=\gamma(j(b))=\gamma(c)=0$, exactness gives $\beta(b)=i'(a')$ for a unique $a'\in A'$, (4) $\delta(c)=[a']\in\operatorname{coker}\alpha$. Independence of lift follows from $\partial^2=0$.

q: Which condition on $\alpha$ and $\gamma$ forces $\delta=0$ identically?
text: Which condition on $\alpha$ and $\gamma$ forces $\delta=0$ identically? If $\alpha$ is surjective then $\operatorname{coker}\alpha=0$, so $\delta$ must be the zero map. Alternatively, if $\gamma$ is injective then $\ker\gamma=0$ and $\delta$ has trivial domain.

q: In a snake-lemma setup with $\alpha=\mathrm{id}_{\mathbb{Z}/4}$, $\beta=\mathrm{id}_{\mathbb{Z}/4}$, $\gamma=\mathrm{id}_{\mathbb{Z}/4}$ and exact rows being the identity sequence, what is $|\ker\delta|$?
text: In a snake-lemma setup with $\alpha=\mathrm{id}_{\mathbb{Z}/4}$, $\beta=\mathrm{id}_{\mathbb{Z}/4}$, $\gamma=\mathrm{id}_{\mathbb{Z}/4}$ and exact rows being the identity sequence, what is $|\ker\delta|$? With all vertical maps being identities, $\ker\gamma=0$ (since $\gamma=\mathrm{id}$, every element is in the image, kernel is trivial — but actually $\ker(\mathrm{id})=0$). Wait: $\ker\delta$ is the kernel of $\delta:\ker\gamma\to\operatorname{coker}\alpha$. Since $\ker\gamma=0$ (identity is injective), the domain is $0$ and $\ker\delta=0$. But if rows are $0\to\mathbb{Z}/4\xrightarrow{\mathrm{id}}\mathbb{Z}/4\to 0$, then $\ker\gamma=\mathbb{Z}/4$, $\operatorname{coker}\alpha=0$, and $\delta=0$ with $\ker\delta=\mathbb{Z}/4$, so $|\ker\delta|=4$.

q: A student is building the connecting map $\delta:\ker\gamma\to\operatorname{coker}\alpha$ in the snake lemma. Exactly one step contains a logical flaw — find it.
text: A student is building the connecting map $\delta:\ker\gamma\to\operatorname{coker}\alpha$ in the snake lemma. Exactly one step contains a logical flaw — find it. Step 4 has the bug. Defining $\delta(c)=a'$ in $A'$ itself is not well-defined because the lift $b$ was a choice, and $a'$ depends on that choice by an element of $\operatorname{im}\alpha$ (as step 5 correctly notes). The fix is exactly what step 5 sets up: define $\delta(c)=[a']\in A'/\operatorname{im}\alpha=\operatorname{coker}\alpha$, which then is well-defined. Compare target spaces: $\delta$ must land in a quotient, not in $A'$ itself.

q: The five lemma concerns a commutative diagram with exact rows $A_1\to A_2\to A_3\to A_4\to A_5$ and $B_1\to B_2\to B_3\to B_4\to B_5$, with vertical maps $f_1,\dots,f_5$. What is its conclusion?
text: The five lemma concerns a commutative diagram with exact rows $A_1\to A_2\to A_3\to A_4\to A_5$ and $B_1\to B_2\to B_3\to B_4\to B_5$, with vertical maps $f_1,\dots,f_5$. What is its conclusion? The five lemma: outer four $f_1,f_2,f_4,f_5$ isomorphisms + exact rows $\Rightarrow$ middle $f_3$ is an isomorphism. Proof is the standard two-step diagram chase (injectivity, then surjectivity).

q: In the four lemma (one half of five), which hypotheses imply $f_3$ is surjective?
text: In the four lemma (one half of five), which hypotheses imply $f_3$ is surjective? The surjectivity half of the five lemma: $f_2$ surjective and $f_4$ injective force $f_3$ surjective. The injectivity half requires $f_2$ injective and $f_4$ surjective — dual conditions, split across the two halves.

q: In a ladder with exact rows, $f_1,f_2,f_4,f_5$ are isomorphisms and $\dim A_3=\dim B_3=7$ (vector spaces). What is the rank of $f_3$?
text: In a ladder with exact rows, $f_1,f_2,f_4,f_5$ are isomorphisms and $\dim A_3=\dim B_3=7$ (vector spaces). What is the rank of $f_3$? By the five lemma $f_3$ is an isomorphism, so its rank equals $\dim A_3=7$.

q: Injectivity half of the five lemma: assume rows exact, $f_1$ surjective, $f_2,f_4$ injective. Show $f_3$ is injective. Suppose $f_3(a_3)=0$. Pick the correct next step.
text: Injectivity half of the five lemma: assume rows exact, $f_1$ surjective, $f_2,f_4$ injective. Show $f_3$ is injective. Suppose $f_3(a_3)=0$. Pick the correct next step. Injectivity of $f_4$ gives $a_4=0$, i.e. $d^A_3(a_3)=0$, so $a_3\in\ker d^A_3=\operatorname{im}d^A_2$. Write $a_3=d^A_2(a_2)$. Continue the chase with $f_2(a_2)$ and use $f_1$ surjective to finish. You have $f_4(a_4)=0$; the injectivity of $f_4$ kills $a_4$, which unlocks exactness at $A_3$.

q: Which configuration is a counterexample showing the five lemma fails if $f_4$ is merely surjective (not injective), while $f_1,f_2,f_5$ remain isomorphisms?
text: Which configuration is a counterexample showing the five lemma fails if $f_4$ is merely surjective (not injective), while $f_1,f_2,f_5$ remain isomorphisms? The five lemma is sharp: weakening any of the four outer hypotheses can break the conclusion. One can construct a concrete diagram where $f_4$ is surjective (not injective), the other three are isomorphisms, yet $f_3$ fails injectivity because a kernel element of $f_4$ hides in $A_4$ but is not the image of anything in $A_3$. You need a map out of $A_4$ that kills a summand of $B_3$, letting $f_3$ miss torsion.

q: The five lemma in an abelian category is proved by:
text: The five lemma in an abelian category is proved by: In an abelian category one either uses element-free chases with generalized elements (morphisms out of a 'test object') or, via Freyd–Mitchell embedding, reduces to $\mathsf{Ab}$ where ordinary element chases work. Mitchell's theorem lets you run the same chase you'd do in $\mathsf{Ab}$.

q: Arrange the consecutive terms of the long exact sequence in homology induced by a short exact sequence of chain complexes $0\to A_\bullet\to B_\bullet\to C_\bullet\to 0$, starting from $H_n(A)$.
text: Arrange the consecutive terms of the long exact sequence in homology induced by a short exact sequence of chain complexes $0\to A_\bullet\to B_\bullet\to C_\bullet\to 0$, starting from $H_n(A)$. The LES of homology runs $\cdots\to H_n(A)\to H_n(B)\to H_n(C)\xrightarrow{\partial}H_{n-1}(A)\to\cdots$. The connecting map $\partial$ comes from applying the snake lemma degree-by-degree, and lowers homological degree by $1$ (for cohomological complexes the analogous $\delta$ raises degree by $1$). $H_n(A)\to H_n(B)\to H_n(C)\xrightarrow{\partial}H_{n-1}(A)$. The connecting map lowers homological degree by 1.

q: Use the Mayer–Vietoris long exact sequence and induction to compute $\dim_\mathbb{Q} H_n(S^n;\mathbb{Q})$ for $n\ge 1$.
text: Use the Mayer–Vietoris long exact sequence and induction to compute $\dim_\mathbb{Q} H_n(S^n;\mathbb{Q})$ for $n\ge 1$. Covering $S^n$ by two contractible hemispheres $U,V$ with $U\cap V\simeq S^{n-1}$, the MV sequence gives $H_n(S^n)\cong H_{n-1}(S^{n-1})$ for $n\ge 2$, and $H_1(S^1)\cong\mathbb{Z}$ directly. Inductively $\dim H_n(S^n;\mathbb{Q})=1$.

q: If $B_\bullet$ is acyclic ($H_n(B)=0$ for all $n$) in $0\to A_\bullet\to B_\bullet\to C_\bullet\to 0$, the long exact sequence collapses to:
text: If $B_\bullet$ is acyclic ($H_n(B)=0$ for all $n$) in $0\to A_\bullet\to B_\bullet\to C_\bullet\to 0$, the long exact sequence collapses to: With the middle terms $H_n(B)=H_{n-1}(B)=0$ the sequence $0\to H_n(C)\xrightarrow{\partial}H_{n-1}(A)\to 0$ forces $\partial$ to be an isomorphism. Acyclic complexes are 'invisible' except for the degree shift they impose.

q: The Mayer–Vietoris sequence for $X=A\cup B$ has connecting map $\partial:H_n(A\cup B)\to H_{n-1}(A\cap B)$. Which description of the connecting map is correct?
text: The Mayer–Vietoris sequence for $X=A\cup B$ has connecting map $\partial:H_n(A\cup B)\to H_{n-1}(A\cap B)$. Which description of the connecting map is correct? A cycle $z$ on $X$ can be written $z=a+b$ (with chains supported in $A$ and $B$ respectively). Then $\partial a = -\partial b$ is a cycle in $A\cap B$; its homology class is $\partial[z]$. This is the snake-lemma connecting map applied to the Mayer–Vietoris short exact sequence of chain complexes.

q: Using the long exact sequence of the pair $(D^2, S^1)$ and the fact that $D^2$ is contractible, compute $\dim_{\mathbb{Q}} H_1(D^2,S^1;\mathbb{Q})$.
text: Using the long exact sequence of the pair $(D^2, S^1)$ and the fact that $D^2$ is contractible, compute $\dim_{\mathbb{Q}} H_1(D^2,S^1;\mathbb{Q})$. The LES gives $H_1(D^2)\to H_1(D^2,S^1)\to H_0(S^1)\to H_0(D^2)$. Since $D^2$ is contractible, $H_1(D^2)=0$ and the map $H_0(S^1)\cong\mathbb{Q}\to H_0(D^2)\cong\mathbb{Q}$ is an isomorphism. So $H_1(D^2,S^1)\cong\mathbb{Q}$, dimension $1$. (By excision this equals $\tilde{H}_1(S^1)\cong\mathbb{Q}$.)

q: Which of the following is the correct proof-step ordering to show naturality of the connecting map $\partial$ in the long exact sequence?
text: Which of the following is the correct proof-step ordering to show naturality of the connecting map $\partial$ in the long exact sequence? Naturality is proved by chasing the snake-lemma diagram for the naturality square: a map of short exact sequences of chain complexes induces a map of long exact sequences, and the connecting homomorphisms commute with the induced maps because each step of the snake construction is natural.

q: Which of the following is equivalent to $P$ being projective as a module over a ring $R$?
text: Which of the following is equivalent to $P$ being projective as a module over a ring $R$? The characterizations: $P$ projective $\iff$ every surjection $M\twoheadrightarrow P$ splits $\iff$ $\Hom(P,-)$ is exact (both left and right) $\iff$ $P$ is a direct summand of a free module. Free is strictly stronger in general (though not over a PID). The characterizations: $P$ projective $\iff$ every surjection $M\twoheadrightarrow P$ splits $\iff$ $\Hom(P,-)$ is exact (both left and right) $\iff$ $P$ is a direct summand of a free…

q: Given $\varphi\colon M\twoheadrightarrow N$ and a map $f\colon P\to N$ with $P$ projective, the lifting property says:
text: Given $\varphi\colon M\twoheadrightarrow N$ and a map $f\colon P\to N$ with $P$ projective, the lifting property says: Projectivity: for any surjection $\varphi\colon M\twoheadrightarrow N$ and any $f\colon P\to N$, there exists a lift $\tilde f\colon P\to M$ with $\varphi\tilde f=f$. This is exactly the dual of the 'extension' property defining injectives.

q: Over $\mathbb{Z}$, which of the following is NOT projective?
text: Over $\mathbb{Z}$, which of the following is NOT projective? Over a PID, projective $\iff$ free. $\mathbb{Z}/2$ is not free (it has torsion), and the surjection $\mathbb{Z}\twoheadrightarrow\mathbb{Z}/2$ does not split (no homomorphism $\mathbb{Z}/2\to\mathbb{Z}$). Hence $\mathbb{Z}/2$ is not projective.

q: Over $R=\mathbb{Z}[x,y]/(xy)$, the module $R/(x)\cong\mathbb{Z}[y]$ is projective but not free. Which invariant distinguishes projective-not-free?
text: Over $R=\mathbb{Z}[x,y]/(xy)$, the module $R/(x)\cong\mathbb{Z}[y]$ is projective but not free. Which invariant distinguishes projective-not-free? Over rings with nontrivial Picard group (like $\mathbb{Z}[x,y]/(xy)$, whose Spec has two components), projectives can be direct summands of free modules without themselves being free. The canonical example: rank-$1$ projectives $\leftrightarrow$ invertible modules $\leftrightarrow$ elements of $\mathrm{Pic}(R)$.

q: Let $P$ be a module. Which statement is FALSE?
text: Let $P$ be a module. Which statement is FALSE? Direct sums and summands of projectives are projective, and projectivity is equivalent to every SES ending in $P$ splitting. But infinite products of projectives need not be projective: e.g. $\prod_{\mathbb{N}}\mathbb{Z}$ is not a free (or projective) $\mathbb{Z}$-module (Baer–Specker group). Sums of projectives are fine; products are not.

q: How many isomorphism classes of projective modules of rank $1$ are there over a Dedekind domain with class number $h=3$?
text: How many isomorphism classes of projective modules of rank $1$ are there over a Dedekind domain with class number $h=3$? Over a Dedekind domain, rank-$1$ projectives are classified by the ideal class group $\mathrm{Cl}(R)$. If $h=|\mathrm{Cl}(R)|=3$, there are exactly $3$ isomorphism classes (the trivial class corresponds to the free module $R$; the other two are non-free rank-$1$ projectives).

q: $I$ is injective iff:
text: $I$ is injective iff: Injectivity: for every injection $A\hookrightarrow B$ and map $A\to I$, there is an extension $B\to I$. Equivalently $\Hom(-,I)$ is exact (both left and right). The 'every injection from $I$ splits' form is also an equivalent formulation.

q: Baer's criterion says: to verify $I$ is injective over a ring $R$, it suffices to check the extension property for:
text: Baer's criterion says: to verify $I$ is injective over a ring $R$, it suffices to check the extension property for: Baer's criterion: $I$ is injective $\iff$ every map from an ideal $\mathfrak{a}\subseteq R$ to $I$ extends to $R\to I$. This reduces an a priori enormous check (all injections of all modules) to a single, tractable one.

q: Which abelian group is NOT injective as a $\mathbb{Z}$-module?
text: Which abelian group is NOT injective as a $\mathbb{Z}$-module? Over $\mathbb{Z}$, injective $\iff$ divisible. $\mathbb{Q}$ and $\mathbb{Q}/\mathbb{Z}$ and Prüfer groups $\mathbb{Z}/p^\infty$ are divisible; $\mathbb{Z}/2$ is not ($2\cdot\mathbb{Z}/2 = 0\ne \mathbb{Z}/2$).

q: Over $\mathbb{Z}$, divisibility characterizes injectivity. Why does Baer's criterion immediately yield this?
text: Over $\mathbb{Z}$, divisibility characterizes injectivity. Why does Baer's criterion immediately yield this? Baer: extension to $\mathbb{Z}$ of a map $(n)\to I$ sending $n\mapsto a$ requires $x\in I$ with $nx = a$. That is exactly divisibility of $a\in I$ by $n$. Since every ideal of $\mathbb{Z}$ is some $(n)$, injectivity $\iff$ divisibility. Lift the generator of $(n)$ and see what's needed.

q: Which statement about injective hulls is correct?
text: Which statement about injective hulls is correct? Every module has an injective hull (or envelope) $M\hookrightarrow E(M)$: the minimal injective extension, unique up to (non-canonical) isomorphism. Over $\mathbb{Z}$, $E(\mathbb{Z}) = \mathbb{Q}$ and $E(\mathbb{Z}/p) = \mathbb{Z}/p^\infty$.

q: Let $I=\mathbb{Q}/\mathbb{Z}$ over $\mathbb{Z}$. How many extensions $\mathbb{Z}\to I$ exist of a given map $f\colon(2)\to I$ with $f(2)=1/4\in\mathbb{Q}/\mathbb{Z}$?
text: Let $I=\mathbb{Q}/\mathbb{Z}$ over $\mathbb{Z}$. How many extensions $\mathbb{Z}\to I$ exist of a given map $f\colon(2)\to I$ with $f(2)=1/4\in\mathbb{Q}/\mathbb{Z}$? We need $\tilde f(1)\in\mathbb{Q}/\mathbb{Z}$ with $2\tilde f(1)=1/4$, i.e. $\tilde f(1)\in\{1/8, 5/8\}\pmod{1}$. Both are valid extensions; there are exactly $2$.

q: $M$ is a flat $R$-module iff:
text: $M$ is a flat $R$-module iff: Flat $=$ tensoring with $M$ preserves exactness. Equivalently preserves injectivity (the right-exactness of $-\otimes M$ holds automatically; flatness is the extra condition that injective maps stay injective after tensoring).

q: Which implication chain is correct for modules over a ring?
text: Which implication chain is correct for modules over a ring? Free $\Rightarrow$ projective (trivial: free is projective); projective $\Rightarrow$ flat (direct summand of free, and free is obviously flat). None of the reverse implications hold over general rings.

q: Over $\mathbb{Z}$, which of the following is NOT flat?
text: Over $\mathbb{Z}$, which of the following is NOT flat? Over a PID, flat $\iff$ torsion-free. $\mathbb{Z}/2$ has torsion; concretely, tensoring $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{\cdot 2}\mathbb{Z}$ with $\mathbb{Z}/2$ gives $\mathbb{Z}/2\xrightarrow{0}\mathbb{Z}/2$, which is not injective. So $\mathbb{Z}/2$ is not flat.

q: A key source of flat modules: localization. Which is the correct statement?
text: A key source of flat modules: localization. Which is the correct statement? Localization is the archetypal non-projective flat module: $S^{-1}R$ is always flat over $R$ (tensor product with localization preserves exact sequences), but usually not projective (e.g. $\mathbb{Q}$ over $\mathbb{Z}$ is flat, not projective). This is why flatness is the 'right' local notion.

q: Lazard's theorem characterizes flat modules as:
text: Lazard's theorem characterizes flat modules as: Lazard's theorem: $M$ is flat $\iff$ $M$ is a filtered colimit of finitely generated free modules. This is why flatness is 'locally free' in a categorical sense, and why tensor products preserve filtered colimits commute cleanly.

q: Let $M = \mathbb{Z}/6$ as a $\mathbb{Z}$-module. Tensor the SES $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{\cdot 4}\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}/4\to 0$ with $M$. What is the order of the kernel of the induced map $\mathbb{Z}/6\to\mathbb{Z}/6$?
text: Let $M = \mathbb{Z}/6$ as a $\mathbb{Z}$-module. Tensor the SES $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{\cdot 4}\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}/4\to 0$ with $M$. What is the order of the kernel of the induced map $\mathbb{Z}/6\to\mathbb{Z}/6$? After tensoring with $\mathbb{Z}/6$, the map $\cdot 4\colon\mathbb{Z}/6\to\mathbb{Z}/6$ has kernel $\{x\in\mathbb{Z}/6 : 4x\equiv 0\pmod 6\}=\{0,3\}$, of order $2$. This measures the failure of flatness of $\mathbb{Z}/6$: $\operatorname{Tor}_1^\mathbb{Z}(\mathbb{Z}/6,\mathbb{Z}/4)=\mathbb{Z}/\gcd(6,4)=\mathbb{Z}/2$.

q: To compute the right derived functors $R^iF(M)$ of a left exact functor $F$, the standard recipe is:
text: To compute the right derived functors $R^iF(M)$ of a left exact functor $F$, the standard recipe is: Right derived functors of a left exact $F$ are computed from an injective resolution $0\to M\to I^0\to I^1\to\cdots$, applying $F$ and taking cohomology of $F(I^\bullet)$. The answer is independent of the resolution.

q: Compute $\dim_{\mathbb{F}_2} \operatorname{Ext}^1_{\mathbb{Z}}(\mathbb{Z}/2,\mathbb{Z}/2)$.
text: Compute $\dim_{\mathbb{F}_2} \operatorname{Ext}^1_{\mathbb{Z}}(\mathbb{Z}/2,\mathbb{Z}/2)$. From $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{2}\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}/2\to 0$, applying $\Hom(-,\mathbb{Z}/2)$ gives $\mathbb{Z}/2\xrightarrow{\times 2=0}\mathbb{Z}/2$, whose cokernel is $\mathbb{Z}/2$. So $\operatorname{Ext}^1_\mathbb{Z}(\mathbb{Z}/2,\mathbb{Z}/2)\cong\mathbb{Z}/2$, dimension $1$ over $\mathbb{F}_2$. From $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{2}\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}/2\to 0$, applying $\Hom(-,\mathbb{Z}/2)$ gives $\mathbb{Z}/2\xrightarrow{\times 2=0}\mathbb{Z}/2$, whose cokernel is…

q: What is $\operatorname{Tor}^\mathbb{Z}_1(\mathbb{Z}/6,\mathbb{Z}/4)$ as $\mathbb{Z}/d$? Give $d$.
text: What is $\operatorname{Tor}^\mathbb{Z}_1(\mathbb{Z}/6,\mathbb{Z}/4)$ as $\mathbb{Z}/d$? Give $d$. $\operatorname{Tor}^\mathbb{Z}_1(\mathbb{Z}/m,\mathbb{Z}/n)\cong\mathbb{Z}/\gcd(m,n)$. Here $\gcd(6,4)=2$.

q: To compute $\operatorname{Ext}^n_{\mathbb{Z}}(\mathbb{Z}/p,M)$ via a projective resolution of $\mathbb{Z}/p$, the correct resolution is:
text: To compute $\operatorname{Ext}^n_{\mathbb{Z}}(\mathbb{Z}/p,M)$ via a projective resolution of $\mathbb{Z}/p$, the correct resolution is: $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{\cdot p}\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}/p\to 0$ is a free resolution of $\mathbb{Z}/p$ of length $1$. Applying $\Hom(-,M)$ and taking cohomology gives $\operatorname{Ext}^0=M^p$ (fixed by $p$) and $\operatorname{Ext}^1=M/pM$; higher Ext groups vanish.

q: Compute $|\operatorname{Tor}^{\mathbb{Z}}_1(\mathbb{Z}/12,\mathbb{Z}/8)|$ (the order of the group).
text: Compute $|\operatorname{Tor}^{\mathbb{Z}}_1(\mathbb{Z}/12,\mathbb{Z}/8)|$ (the order of the group). $\operatorname{Tor}^\mathbb{Z}_1(\mathbb{Z}/m,\mathbb{Z}/n)\cong\mathbb{Z}/\gcd(m,n)$. Here $\gcd(12,8)=4$, so the group is $\mathbb{Z}/4$ with order $4$.

q: Which of the following functors is NOT right exact (and hence genuinely requires derived functors to fix)?
text: Which of the following functors is NOT right exact (and hence genuinely requires derived functors to fix)? $\Hom_R(M,-)$ is left exact but not right exact in general: $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{2}\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}/2\to 0$ applied with $M=\mathbb{Z}$ gives $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{2}\mathbb{Z}$, which fails surjectivity. Its right derived functors are the $\operatorname{Ext}^n$ groups.

q: A projective resolution of a module $M$ is an exact sequence:
text: A projective resolution of a module $M$ is an exact sequence: Projective resolutions go from the left: $\cdots\to P_1\to P_0\twoheadrightarrow M\to 0$, exact at every spot. They are used to compute left-derived functors like $\operatorname{Tor}$ and $L_iF$ for right-exact $F$.

q: $\mathbb{Z}/6$ has what length-$1$ free resolution? Enter the single invariant factor appearing in the presentation matrix (a $1\times 1$ matrix).
text: $\mathbb{Z}/6$ has what length-$1$ free resolution? Enter the single invariant factor appearing in the presentation matrix (a $1\times 1$ matrix). The resolution is $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{\cdot 6}\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}/6\to 0$. The presentation matrix is the $1\times 1$ matrix $[6]$.

q: What is the global dimension of the polynomial ring $k[x_1,\dots,x_n]$ over a field $k$?
text: What is the global dimension of the polynomial ring $k[x_1,\dots,x_n]$ over a field $k$? Hilbert's syzygy theorem: the global dimension of $k[x_1,\dots,x_n]$ is exactly $n$. Equivalently, every finitely generated module has a free resolution of length $\le n$, and there exist modules requiring length exactly $n$.

q: Over the ring $R=k[\varepsilon]/(\varepsilon^2)$ (dual numbers), the module $k=R/(\varepsilon)$ has which minimal free resolution?
text: Over the ring $R=k[\varepsilon]/(\varepsilon^2)$ (dual numbers), the module $k=R/(\varepsilon)$ has which minimal free resolution? $R=k[\varepsilon]/(\varepsilon^2)$ has global dimension $\infty$: $k$ has an infinite $2$-periodic free resolution $\cdots\xrightarrow{\varepsilon}R\xrightarrow{\varepsilon}R\to k$. Consequently $\operatorname{Ext}^i_R(k,k)=k$ for all $i\ge 0$ (not eventually zero). This is a typical non-regular local ring.

q: Over a PID $R$, every finitely generated module has a free resolution of length at most what?
text: Over a PID $R$, every finitely generated module has a free resolution of length at most what? Over a PID, submodules of free modules are free (Smith normal form), so a finitely generated module $M$ has a resolution $0\to R^r\to R^s\to M\to 0$ of length $1$. Hence global dimension of a PID is $\le 1$.

q: Which statement about flat resolutions is correct?
text: Which statement about flat resolutions is correct? $\operatorname{Tor}_i(M,N)$ can be computed from a flat resolution of $M$ (since $-\otimes N$ is right-exact and flat modules give the acyclicity needed). $\operatorname{Ext}$ requires projective (or injective) resolutions — flat is not enough.

q: Which of the following is NOT required of an abelian category?
text: Which of the following is NOT required of an abelian category? Abelian categories need a zero object, kernels/cokernels, biproducts, and every mono/epi to be normal (a kernel/cokernel respectively). They do NOT need every object to be projective — in fact $\mathsf{Ab}$ is abelian and most objects there aren't projective.

q: The Freyd–Mitchell embedding theorem says:
text: The Freyd–Mitchell embedding theorem says: Freyd–Mitchell: any small abelian category $\mathcal{A}$ admits a full exact embedding $\mathcal{A}\hookrightarrow R\text{-Mod}$ for some ring $R$. The point: element-chases in $\mathsf{Ab}$ transfer to any abelian category.

q: In the derived category $D(\mathcal{A})$, a distinguished triangle $X\to Y\to Z\to X[1]$ replaces what in ordinary abelian categories?
text: In the derived category $D(\mathcal{A})$, a distinguished triangle $X\to Y\to Z\to X[1]$ replaces what in ordinary abelian categories? Short exact sequences in $\mathcal{A}$ lift to distinguished triangles in $D(\mathcal{A})$; the rotation/shift structure of triangles encodes long exact sequences of cohomology objects. This is the fundamental move toward derived categories.

q: Which example is an abelian category?
text: Which example is an abelian category? Sheaves of abelian groups form an abelian category (kernels are taken section-wise; cokernels require sheafification). The other examples fail: in non-abelian groups every subgroup isn't normal; in Top monos-that-aren't-kernels exist; fields lack zero objects / biproducts. Abelianness is inherited objectwise in a sheaf setting.

q: Which axiom of a triangulated category fails in a general abelian category $\mathcal{A}$, forcing us to pass to $D(\mathcal{A})$?
text: Which axiom of a triangulated category fails in a general abelian category $\mathcal{A}$, forcing us to pass to $D(\mathcal{A})$? An abelian category is not triangulated (except the trivial one): it lacks a natural shift and distinguished triangle structure. The chain complex category $\mathrm{Ch}(\mathcal{A})$, localized at quasi-isomorphisms, becomes the derived category $D(\mathcal{A})$ — now genuinely triangulated.

q: Which statement about $\operatorname{Ext}$ in the derived category is correct?
text: Which statement about $\operatorname{Ext}$ in the derived category is correct? The fundamental identification: $\Hom_{D(\mathcal{A})}(X,Y[i])\cong\operatorname{Ext}^i_\mathcal{A}(X,Y)$ for $X,Y\in\mathcal{A}$. This is why the derived category is 'the right place' to do homological algebra: all Ext/Tor appear as hom-sets after shifts. Derived category maps read off all Ext groups via shifts.

q: On the $E_r$ page of a (cohomological) spectral sequence, the differential $d_r$ has bidegree:
text: On the $E_r$ page of a (cohomological) spectral sequence, the differential $d_r$ has bidegree: In the standard cohomological convention $d_r:E_r^{p,q}\to E_r^{p+r,q-r+1}$, i.e. bidegree $(r,1-r)$. The next page is defined as $E_{r+1}=H(E_r,d_r)$.

q: A spectral sequence $E_2^{p,q}\Rightarrow H^{p+q}$ is said to degenerate at $E_2$ if:
text: A spectral sequence $E_2^{p,q}\Rightarrow H^{p+q}$ is said to degenerate at $E_2$ if: Degeneration at $E_2$ means $d_r=0$ for all $r\ge 2$, so $E_\infty=E_2$. The abutment $H^n$ is then the associated graded of some filtration with graded pieces $E_2^{p,n-p}$ — you still have an extension problem, but the entries are pinned down.

q: Consider the double complex $C^{0,0}=C^{1,0}=C^{0,1}=C^{1,1}=\mathbb{Z}$, with horizontal maps multiplication by $2$ and vertical maps multiplication by $3$. Taking horizontal homology first, what is the rank of $E_1^{0,0}$ as an abelian group?
text: Consider the double complex $C^{0,0}=C^{1,0}=C^{0,1}=C^{1,1}=\mathbb{Z}$, with horizontal maps multiplication by $2$ and vertical maps multiplication by $3$. Taking horizontal homology first, what is the rank of $E_1^{0,0}$ as an abelian group? The horizontal map $\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{2}\mathbb{Z}$ is injective, so its kernel is $0$. Thus $E_1^{0,0}=\ker(2)=0$, which has rank $0$ (and is in fact the zero group).

q: The Serre spectral sequence for a fibration $F\to E\to B$ has $E_2$-page $E_2^{p,q}=H^p(B;H^q(F))$. If $F=S^1$ and $B=S^2$, which groups are the only potentially nonzero entries on $E_2$?
text: The Serre spectral sequence for a fibration $F\to E\to B$ has $E_2$-page $E_2^{p,q}=H^p(B;H^q(F))$. If $F=S^1$ and $B=S^2$, which groups are the only potentially nonzero entries on $E_2$? $H^p(S^2)$ is $\mathbb{Z}$ for $p=0,2$ and $0$ otherwise. $H^q(S^1)$ is $\mathbb{Z}$ for $q=0,1$ and $0$ otherwise. So the only nonzero corners of the $E_2$ page are $(p,q)\in\{(0,0),(2,0),(0,1),(2,1)\}$.

q: In the Serre spectral sequence for $S^1\to E\to S^2$ converging to $H^*(E)$, the only potentially nontrivial differential on the $E_2$ page is $d_2:E_2^{0,1}\to E_2^{2,0}$. If $E=S^3$ (Hopf fibration), this map is:
text: In the Serre spectral sequence for $S^1\to E\to S^2$ converging to $H^*(E)$, the only potentially nontrivial differential on the $E_2$ page is $d_2:E_2^{0,1}\to E_2^{2,0}$. If $E=S^3$ (Hopf fibration), this map is: For the Hopf fibration $S^1\to S^3\to S^2$, the spectral sequence must give $H^*(S^3)$: $\mathbb{Z}$ in degrees $0$ and $3$. To kill $E_\infty^{0,1}$ and $E_\infty^{2,0}$ we need $d_2:E_2^{0,1}\cong\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{\sim}E_2^{2,0}\cong\mathbb{Z}$ to be an isomorphism.

q: For the Leray–Hirsch spectral sequence of the trivial fibration $E=F\times B$ with $F=S^2,B=S^2$, the spectral sequence degenerates at $E_2$. Compute $\dim_{\mathbb{Q}} H^2(E;\mathbb{Q})$.
text: For the Leray–Hirsch spectral sequence of the trivial fibration $E=F\times B$ with $F=S^2,B=S^2$, the spectral sequence degenerates at $E_2$. Compute $\dim_{\mathbb{Q}} H^2(E;\mathbb{Q})$. Degeneration gives $H^n(E)\cong\bigoplus_{p+q=n}H^p(B)\otimes H^q(F)$. For $n=2$: $(p,q)=(2,0)$ contributes $H^2(S^2)\otimes H^0(S^2)=\mathbb{Q}$, and $(0,2)$ contributes $H^0(S^2)\otimes H^2(S^2)=\mathbb{Q}$. Total $\dim=2$.

title: The homotopy category $K(\mathcal{A})$
blurb: The homotopy category $K(\mathcal{A})$ has chain complexes for objects and homotopy classes of chain maps for morphisms. It identifies maps that differ by a chain homotopy and is the first step toward inverting quasi-isomorphisms.

title: Quasi-isomorphisms and localization
blurb: A quasi-isomorphism is a chain map inducing iso on cohomology. The derived category $D(\mathcal{A})$ is the localization of $K(\mathcal{A})$ at the class of quasi-isomorphisms — "objects up to cohomology agreement."

title: The derived category $D(\mathcal{A})$
blurb: The derived category $D(\mathcal{A})$ is the universal target of a functor out of $\mathcal{A}$ that inverts quasi-isomorphisms. Its bounded variants $D^+, D^-, D^b$ restrict to complexes that are zero in low / high / both extremes.

title: Triangulated structure and exact triangles
blurb: $D(\mathcal{A})$ is a triangulated category: a shift functor $[1]$ and a class of distinguished "exact" triangles $X \to Y \to Z \to X[1]$ replacing short exact sequences. The cohomology long exact sequence is a triangle's projection to $\mathcal{A}$.

title: Derived functors $RF, LF$
blurb: Right (resp. left) derived functors compute $F$ on injective (resp. projective) resolutions. They package the classical $R^iF, L_iF$ into a single triangulated functor $D(\mathcal{A}) \to D(\mathcal{B})$ — Ext and Tor become $\Hom_{D}$ and $\otimes^L$.

title: $t$-structures and hearts
blurb: A $t$-structure $(D^{\le 0}, D^{\ge 0})$ on a triangulated category gives a notion of "objects concentrated in non-positive (resp. non-negative) degree" and a heart $D^{\le 0} \cap D^{\ge 0}$ that is abelian. Recovers $\mathcal{A}$ from $D(\mathcal{A})$ as the heart of the standard $t$-structure.

title: $D^b(\mathrm{Coh}\,X)$ and Fourier–Mukai
blurb: For a smooth projective variety $X$, the bounded derived category of coherent sheaves $D^b(\mathrm{Coh}\,X)$ is the modern home of intersection theory and a finer invariant than Picard or cohomology. Fourier–Mukai transforms are equivalences between $D^b(\mathrm{Coh}\,X)$ and $D^b(\mathrm{Coh}\,Y)$ given by integral kernels.

q: Two chain maps $f, g \colon X^\bullet \to Y^\bullet$ are chain homotopic via $s = (s^n \colon X^n \to Y^{n-1})$. Which equation expresses this relation?
text: Two chain maps $f, g \colon X^\bullet \to Y^\bullet$ are chain homotopic via $s = (s^n \colon X^n \to Y^{n-1})$. Which equation expresses this relation? By definition, $f \simeq g$ iff there exists a degree $-1$ map $s$ with $f - g = ds + sd$. The order of the two terms is conventional (some texts use $f - g = sd + ds$, the same thing); $s$ itself is NOT a chain map. Look at the formula displayed in the worked example.

q: Which of the following are TRUE for any chain homotopy $f \simeq g$ between chain maps $X^\bullet \to Y^\bullet$?
text: Which of the following are TRUE for any chain homotopy $f \simeq g$ between chain maps $X^\bullet \to Y^\bullet$? Chain-homotopic maps induce equal maps on cohomology (so (1) holds) and become equal in $K(\mathcal{A})$ by definition (so (3) holds). They generally differ as set maps (a chain homotopy is a witness, not a triviality), and there is no reason for them to differ by an iso of $X$.

q: Find the flawed step in the following "proof" that nullhomotopic chain maps induce zero on cohomology.
text: Find the flawed step in the following "proof" that nullhomotopic chain maps induce zero on cohomology. Step 4 is wrong: $s(x) \in Y^{n-1}$ has no reason to be a cocycle. The correct conclusion is that $f(x) = d(s(x))$ is a coboundary, hence zero in $H^n(Y)$ — which is what we wanted. Check whether $s(x)$ is required to be a cocycle.

q: Which of the following chain maps in $\mathrm{Ch}(\mathrm{Ab})$ is a quasi-isomorphism?
text: Which of the following chain maps in $\mathrm{Ch}(\mathrm{Ab})$ is a quasi-isomorphism? Option (b) is the prototypical quasi-iso: $H^0(X) = 0 = H^0(Y)$, $H^1(X) = \mathbb{Z}/2 \xrightarrow{\mathrm{id}} \mathbb{Z}/2 = H^1(Y)$. (a) induces multiplication by $2$ on $\mathbb{Z}$, NOT iso. (c) induces the inclusion $\mathbb{Z} \hookrightarrow \mathbb{Q}$, not iso. (d) is the identity, which is always a quasi-iso, no localization needed. Check $H^n(f)$ in each degree.

q: Arrange the steps of "localizing $K(\mathcal{A})$ at quasi-isomorphisms" in correct logical order.
text: Arrange the steps of "localizing $K(\mathcal{A})$ at quasi-isomorphisms" in correct logical order. First descend to $K$ (item index 2), then check Ore conditions there (index 0), then build $D$ via the roof calculus (index 1), then state the universal property (index 3).

q: True or false: every quasi-isomorphism in $K(\mathcal{A})$ is an isomorphism in $K(\mathcal{A})$.
text: True or false: every quasi-isomorphism in $K(\mathcal{A})$ is an isomorphism in $K(\mathcal{A})$. Quasi-isos are NOT iso in $K(\mathcal{A})$ in general. The reduction $(\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{2}\mathbb{Z}) \to \mathbb{Z}/2[1]$ is a quasi-iso but admits no chain map back, even up to homotopy. This is precisely what motivates localizing at the quasi-isos to form $D(\mathcal{A})$. Recall the worked example with $\mathbb{Z}$ and $\mathbb{Z}/2$.

q: Which of the following is NOT one of the Ore conditions verified by quasi-isomorphisms in $K(\mathcal{A})$?
text: Which of the following is NOT one of the Ore conditions verified by quasi-isomorphisms in $K(\mathcal{A})$? (d) is automatic — quasi-isos induce isomorphisms on cohomology, in particular injections — but it's a consequence, not part of the Ore axioms. The genuine Ore conditions are (a), (b), and a roof-completion clause like (c) (sometimes stated dually). "Two-out-of-three" is a standard restatement. Three of these are required by the localization theory; one is automatic.

q: A student claims: "If $f, g\colon X \to Y$ in $K(\mathcal{A})$ are both quasi-isos, then $f = g$ in $K$." Find the bad step.
text: A student claims: "If $f, g\colon X \to Y$ in $K(\mathcal{A})$ are both quasi-isos, then $f = g$ in $K$." Find the bad step. Step (2) is wrong: two isomorphisms can be DIFFERENT isomorphisms. Take $X = Y = \mathbb{Z}[0]$, $f = \mathrm{id}$, $g = -\mathrm{id}$ — both quasi-isos, but $H^0(f) = \mathrm{id} \neq -\mathrm{id} = H^0(g)$. Step (3) compounds the error: $H^*$ is NOT faithful on $K$. Consider $f = \mathrm{id}$ and $g = -\mathrm{id}$ on $\mathbb{Z}[0]$.

q: For $R$ a ring and $M, N \in \mathrm{Mod}\,R$, which classical group is recovered as $\Hom_{D(\mathrm{Mod}\,R)}(M[0], N[i])$?
text: For $R$ a ring and $M, N \in \mathrm{Mod}\,R$, which classical group is recovered as $\Hom_{D(\mathrm{Mod}\,R)}(M[0], N[i])$? This is one of the foundational identifications: $\mathrm{Ext}_R^i(M, N) = \Hom_{D(\mathrm{Mod}\,R)}(M[0], N[i])$. Tor would appear via $\otimes^L$, not Hom. For $i = 0$ this gives ordinary Hom; for $i > 0$ we get the higher Ext groups.

q: Which is the correct definition of $D^b(\mathcal{A})$?
text: Which is the correct definition of $D^b(\mathcal{A})$? $D^b = D^+ \cap D^-$, complexes bounded in both directions. Note: "bounded" can be interpreted strictly (the complex itself has $X^n = 0$ outside a range) or weakly via cohomology — for nice $\mathcal{A}$ the two agree up to quasi-iso.

q: Match each subcategory of $D(\mathcal{A})$ with what it parameterizes.
text: Match each subcategory of $D(\mathcal{A})$ with what it parameterizes. $RF$ requires injective resolutions, which exist for bounded-below complexes ($D^+$). $LF$ dually wants projective resolutions, hence $D^-$. Bounded cohomology is the data of $D^b$. The heart of the standard $t$-structure is $\mathcal{A}$ shifted to degree $0$.

q: Let $R = k[x]/(x^2)$ for a field $k$. Compute $\Hom_{D(\mathrm{Mod}\,R)}(k[0], k[i])$ as a $k$-vector space, where $k = R/(x)$.
text: Let $R = k[x]/(x^2)$ for a field $k$. Compute $\Hom_{D(\mathrm{Mod}\,R)}(k[0], k[i])$ as a $k$-vector space, where $k = R/(x)$. $\Hom_{D}(k, k[i]) = \mathrm{Ext}^i_R(k, k)$. A free resolution of $k$ is $\dots \to R \xrightarrow{x} R \xrightarrow{x} R \to k \to 0$ — periodic. Applying $\Hom_R(-, k)$ gives $k \xrightarrow{0} k \xrightarrow{0} k \to \dots$ (since $x$ acts as $0$ on $k$). So $\mathrm{Ext}^i_R(k, k) = k$ for every $i \ge 0$. This is the prototypical "Koszul-dual" computation: a 0-dimensional non-reduced ring has nontrivial Ext in every degree. Resolve $k$ over $R = k[x]/(x^2)$ — the resolution is periodic.

q: Given a chain map $f\colon X \to Y$ in $\mathrm{Ch}(\mathcal{A})$, the mapping cone $\mathrm{Cone}(f)$ has:
text: Given a chain map $f\colon X \to Y$ in $\mathrm{Ch}(\mathcal{A})$, the mapping cone $\mathrm{Cone}(f)$ has: The cone shifts $X$ up by one and adjoins $Y$ in the same degree, with a twisted differential mixing $d_X, d_Y$, and $f$. The minus sign on $d_X$ is forced by $d^2 = 0$ together with the chain-map condition.

q: Which of the following are axioms or immediate consequences of the triangulated structure on $D(\mathcal{A})$?
text: Which of the following are axioms or immediate consequences of the triangulated structure on $D(\mathcal{A})$? (a), (c), (d) are part of the standard package. (b) is FALSE — uniqueness of the cone is only up to non-canonical iso, and the failure of functoriality of the cone is what prevents triangulated categories from being abelian. Watch out for "canonical" — that's a strong word.

q: A short exact sequence $0 \to A \xrightarrow{\iota} B \xrightarrow{\pi} C \to 0$ in $\mathcal{A}$ becomes a distinguished triangle in $D(\mathcal{A})$:
text: A short exact sequence $0 \to A \xrightarrow{\iota} B \xrightarrow{\pi} C \to 0$ in $\mathcal{A}$ becomes a distinguished triangle in $D(\mathcal{A})$: The triangle has a nonzero connecting map $\delta\colon C \to A[1]$ representing the class $[B]$ in $\mathrm{Ext}^1_{\mathcal{A}}(C, A) = \Hom_D(C, A[1])$. The class is zero iff the SES splits — generically it does not. What if the SES does NOT split?

q: Take the chain map $f\colon \mathbb{Z}[0] \xrightarrow{n} \mathbb{Z}[0]$ in $\mathrm{Ch}(\mathrm{Ab})$ (multiplication by a nonzero integer $n$, both complexes concentrated in degree $0$). What is its mapping cone, viewed in $D^b(\mathrm{Ab})$?
text: Take the chain map $f\colon \mathbb{Z}[0] \xrightarrow{n} \mathbb{Z}[0]$ in $\mathrm{Ch}(\mathrm{Ab})$ (multiplication by a nonzero integer $n$, both complexes concentrated in degree $0$). What is its mapping cone, viewed in $D^b(\mathrm{Ab})$? $\mathrm{Cone}(f)$ is the 2-term complex $\mathbb{Z} \xrightarrow{n} \mathbb{Z}$ supported in two consecutive degrees. Cohomology is concentrated in one degree: kernel of $\times n$ is $0$, cokernel is $\mathbb{Z}/n$. So in $D^b(\mathrm{Ab})$, $\mathrm{Cone}(f)$ is quasi-iso to $\mathbb{Z}/n$ shifted into a single degree. The corresponding distinguished triangle $\mathbb{Z} \xrightarrow{n} \mathbb{Z} \to \mathbb{Z}/n[\text{shift}] \to \mathbb{Z}[1]$ recovers the SES $0 \to \mathbb{Z} \xrightarrow{n} \mathbb{Z} \to \mathbb{Z}/n \to 0$. The cone has cohomology only in one degree.

q: What does it mean for $F\colon \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ to admit a right derived functor $RF\colon D^+(\mathcal{A}) \to D^+(\mathcal{B})$?
text: What does it mean for $F\colon \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ to admit a right derived functor $RF\colon D^+(\mathcal{A}) \to D^+(\mathcal{B})$? The classical construction needs enough injectives in $\mathcal{A}$ (so every $X$ has an injective resolution $X \to I^\bullet$) and additivity of $F$. Left exactness is what forces $RF$ to extend $F$ in degree $0$: $R^0 F(X) = F(X)$ for $X \in \mathcal{A}$. What is needed to choose injective resolutions?

q: Compute $\mathrm{Ext}^1_{\mathbb{Z}}(\mathbb{Z}/6, \mathbb{Z}/4)$ as an abelian group, then enter its order (number of elements).
text: Compute $\mathrm{Ext}^1_{\mathbb{Z}}(\mathbb{Z}/6, \mathbb{Z}/4)$ as an abelian group, then enter its order (number of elements). Use the resolution $0 \to \mathbb{Z} \xrightarrow{6} \mathbb{Z} \to \mathbb{Z}/6 \to 0$. Apply $\Hom(-, \mathbb{Z}/4)$: get $\mathbb{Z}/4 \xrightarrow{6} \mathbb{Z}/4$. Multiplication by $6 \equiv 2 \pmod 4$ has kernel $\{0, 2\}$ and cokernel $\{0, 1\}/\langle 2\rangle = \mathbb{Z}/2$. So $\mathrm{Ext}^1 = \mathbb{Z}/2$, order $2$. (General formula: $\mathrm{Ext}^1_{\mathbb{Z}}(\mathbb{Z}/m, \mathbb{Z}/n) = \mathbb{Z}/\gcd(m,n)$, and $\gcd(6,4) = 2$.) $\mathrm{Ext}^1_{\mathbb{Z}}(\mathbb{Z}/m, \mathbb{Z}/n) = \mathbb{Z}/\gcd(m, n)$.

q: A student computes $R\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})$ for $X = \mathbb{P}^1$, $\mathcal{F} = \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{P}^1}(-2)$. Find the flawed step.
text: A student computes $R\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})$ for $X = \mathbb{P}^1$, $\mathcal{F} = \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{P}^1}(-2)$. Find the flawed step. Step (2) is wrong: $\Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{O}(-2))$ over an affine chart $U_i \cong \mathbb{A}^1$ is the FREE module on a generator (Laurent polynomials in one variable, with a degree shift). It is large, not zero. Computing the Čech cohomology correctly gives $H^0(\mathbb{P}^1, \mathcal{O}(-2)) = 0$ and $H^1(\mathbb{P}^1, \mathcal{O}(-2)) = k$ (Serre duality with $\mathcal{O}(0)$). $R\Gamma$ is not zero — and that's why $\mathcal{O}(-2)$ is a nontrivial derived-category object. What is $\Gamma$ of a line bundle on an affine chart?

q: When does $R(G \circ F) \cong RG \circ RF$ as triangulated functors $D^+(\mathcal{A}) \to D^+(\mathcal{C})$?
text: When does $R(G \circ F) \cong RG \circ RF$ as triangulated functors $D^+(\mathcal{A}) \to D^+(\mathcal{C})$? (c) is the standard composition theorem (Grothendieck spectral sequence's degeneracy criterion). (b) is sufficient (an injective is a-fortiori acyclic) but not the sharpest condition. The general statement uses acyclicity. The Grothendieck SS $E_2^{p,q} = R^p G(R^q F(X)) \Rightarrow R^{p+q}(GF)(X)$ collapses precisely when this hypothesis holds. It involves acyclicity of $F$(injectives) for $G$.

q: Compute $\mathrm{Tor}_2^{\mathbb{Z}}(\mathbb{Z}/4, \mathbb{Z}/6)$.
text: Compute $\mathrm{Tor}_2^{\mathbb{Z}}(\mathbb{Z}/4, \mathbb{Z}/6)$. Over $\mathbb{Z}$, every module has a free resolution of length $\le 1$ (PID). So $\mathrm{Tor}_i^{\mathbb{Z}} = 0$ for $i \ge 2$. (Compare: $\mathrm{Tor}_1^{\mathbb{Z}}(\mathbb{Z}/4, \mathbb{Z}/6) = \mathbb{Z}/2$.) Higher Tor over a Dedekind domain always vanishes. What is the projective dimension of any $\mathbb{Z}$-module?

q: For the standard $t$-structure on $D(\mathrm{Ab})$, the heart $\heartsuit = D^{\le 0} \cap D^{\ge 0}$ is equivalent to:
text: For the standard $t$-structure on $D(\mathrm{Ab})$, the heart $\heartsuit = D^{\le 0} \cap D^{\ge 0}$ is equivalent to: The heart of the standard $t$-structure recovers the abelian category $\mathcal{A}$ exactly: a complex with cohomology only in degree $0$ is quasi-iso to $H^0(X)[0]$, an object of $\mathcal{A}$ shifted to degree $0$. So $\heartsuit \simeq \mathcal{A}$. The heart of the standard $t$-structure recovers the abelian category $\mathcal{A}$ exactly: a complex with cohomology only in degree $0$ is quasi-iso to $H^0(X)[0]$, an object of $\mathcal{A}$…

q: The truncation triangle $\tau_{\le 0} X \to X \to \tau_{\ge 1} X \to (\tau_{\le 0} X)[1]$ has which property?
text: The truncation triangle $\tau_{\le 0} X \to X \to \tau_{\ge 1} X \to (\tau_{\le 0} X)[1]$ has which property? This is the existence and uniqueness clause of the $t$-structure axiom: every $X$ has a unique (up to unique iso) such triangle. Splitting is the special case when $\Hom(\tau_{\ge 1} X, \tau_{\le 0} X[1]) = 0$, which is RARE — the connecting map carries the gluing data.

q: Which of these are TRUE about $t$-structures on a triangulated category $\mathcal{T}$?
text: Which of these are TRUE about $t$-structures on a triangulated category $\mathcal{T}$? (a), (b) are theorems. (d) is the whole point of perverse $t$-structures: $D^b_c(X)$ has both standard and perverse $t$-structures, with hearts $\mathrm{Sh}_c(X)$ vs perverse sheaves — different abelian categories! (c) is FALSE — $t$-structures are extra data, not unique.

q: Bondal–Orlov: when does the derived category $D^b(\mathrm{Coh}\,X)$ recover the smooth projective variety $X$?
text: Bondal–Orlov: when does the derived category $D^b(\mathrm{Coh}\,X)$ recover the smooth projective variety $X$? Bondal–Orlov: if $\omega_X$ is ample (general type) or $\omega_X^{-1}$ is ample (Fano), then $D^b(\mathrm{Coh}\,X)$ determines $X$.
