The hypothesis FAILS for Calabi–Yau ($\omega_X$ trivial) and abelian varieties — and that's where derived equivalences between non-isomorphic varieties happen. The result fails for Calabi–Yau and abelian varieties — what's special about those?

q: The Fourier–Mukai transform $\Phi_{\mathcal{P}}\colon D^b(X) \to D^b(Y)$ associated to a kernel $\mathcal{P} \in D^b(X \times Y)$ is defined as:
text: The Fourier–Mukai transform $\Phi_{\mathcal{P}}\colon D^b(X) \to D^b(Y)$ associated to a kernel $\mathcal{P} \in D^b(X \times Y)$ is defined as: The standard formula: pull back to the product, twist by the kernel, derived push forward. Its analogy with the classical Fourier transform $\hat f(\xi) = \int f(x) e^{-2\pi i x \xi}\,dx$ explains the name.

q: Mukai's original Fourier–Mukai transform identifies $D^b(X)$ with $D^b(\hat X)$ where:
text: Mukai's original Fourier–Mukai transform identifies $D^b(X)$ with $D^b(\hat X)$ where: Mukai's 1981 theorem: for $X$ an abelian variety with dual $\hat X$ and Poincaré bundle $\mathcal{P}$ on $X \times \hat X$, the FM transform $\Phi_{\mathcal{P}}\colon D^b(X) \xrightarrow{\sim} D^b(\hat X)$ is an equivalence. (b), (c), (d) are also derived-equivalence phenomena but not Mukai's specific construction. Look up the Poincaré bundle.

title: The bar resolution and $H^n(G,M)$
blurb: The bar resolution gives a free $\mathbb{Z}[G]$-resolution of the trivial module $\mathbb{Z}$; cohomology of the dual cochain complex defines $H^n(G,M)=\mathrm{Ext}^n_{\mathbb{Z}[G]}(\mathbb{Z},M)$, the right derived functors of $M\mapsto M^G$.

title: $H^0$ as $G$-fixed points
blurb: Degree zero recovers the input: $H^0(G,M)=M^G$. Failure of right-exactness of $(-)^G$ is exactly what the higher $H^n$ correct, generating the long exact sequence $0\to A^G\to B^G\to C^G\xrightarrow{\delta} H^1(G,A)\to\cdots$.

title: $H^1$, crossed homomorphisms, and Hilbert 90
blurb: $H^1(G,M)$ is crossed homomorphisms $\varphi(gh)=\varphi(g)+g\varphi(h)$ modulo principal ones $g\mapsto gm-m$. Hilbert's Theorem 90 says $H^1(\Gal(L/K),L^\times)=1$ for cyclic $L/K$: every norm-one element is of the form $\sigma(\alpha)/\alpha$.

title: $H^2$ classifies group extensions
blurb: Equivalence classes of extensions $1\to A\to E\to G\to 1$ with prescribed $G$-action on abelian $A$ are in bijection with $H^2(G,A)$. Split extensions hit $0$; the nontrivial class assembles a section's failure-of-multiplicativity into the canonical 2-cocycle.

title: Cyclic groups, periodicity, and Tate cohomology
blurb: For $G=C_n$ the alternating $\sigma-1$/$N$-resolution gives a 2-periodic theory: $H^{2k}=M^G/N(M)$, $H^{2k+1}=\ker N/(\sigma-1)M$. Splicing in negative degrees defines Tate cohomology $\hat H^*$; the Herbrand quotient is multiplicative and a workhorse in class field theory.

title: Inflation–restriction and Lyndon–Hochschild–Serre
blurb: For $H\tr{}ianglelefteq G$ the five-term sequence $0\to H^1(G/H,M^H)\to H^1(G,M)\to H^1(H,M)^{G/H}\to H^2(G/H,M^H)\to H^2(G,M)$ is the low-degree edge of the LHS spectral sequence $E_2^{p,q}=H^p(G/H,H^q(H,M))\Rightarrow H^{p+q}(G,M)$.

title: Galois cohomology and the Brauer group
blurb: Continuous group cohomology of $G_k=\Gal(\bar k/k)$ acting on a Galois module $M$ is Galois cohomology $H^n(k,M)$. Hilbert 90 says $H^1(k,\bar k^\times)=0$; $H^2(k,\bar k^\times)=\mathrm{Br}(k)$ is the Brauer group of central simple algebras, with the Kummer sequence giving $H^1(k,\mu_n)=k^\times/(k^\times)^n$.

q: Group cohomology $H^n(G,M)$ is defined as which derived functor?
text: Group cohomology $H^n(G,M)$ is defined as which derived functor? By definition $H^n(G,M)=R^n(-)^G(M)=\mathrm{Ext}^n_{\mathbb{Z}[G]}(\mathbb{Z},M)$. (Coinvariants give group homology $H_n(G,M)$.)

q: On the bar resolution, an $n$-cochain $\varphi:G^n\to M$ has coboundary $(d\varphi)(g_1,\dots,g_{n+1})$ equal to which expression (degree 1 case)?
text: On the bar resolution, an $n$-cochain $\varphi:G^n\to M$ has coboundary $(d\varphi)(g_1,\dots,g_{n+1})$ equal to which expression (degree 1 case)? The bar formula in degree 1 gives $(d\varphi)(g_1,g_2)=g_1\varphi(g_2)-\varphi(g_1g_2)+\varphi(g_1)$. A 1-cocycle (kernel of $d$) is exactly a crossed homomorphism.

q: Which of the following are TRUE about $H^n(G,M)$?
text: Which of the following are TRUE about $H^n(G,M)$? Cohomology is independent of the chosen projective resolution (the whole point of derived functors). The Ext-formula is the standard equivalent description, and functoriality in $G$ is what makes restriction, inflation, and corestriction work.

q: Let $G=C_4$ act on $M=\mathbb{Z}/12$ via $\sigma\cdot x = 5x$. Compute $|H^0(G,M)|=|M^G|$.
text: Let $G=C_4$ act on $M=\mathbb{Z}/12$ via $\sigma\cdot x = 5x$. Compute $|H^0(G,M)|=|M^G|$. $M^G=\ker(\sigma-1)=\{x:4x\equiv 0\bmod 12\}=\{0,3,6,9\}$, so $|M^G|=\gcd(4,12)=4$. (Equivalently $|M^G|=\gcd(k-1,m)$ for $\sigma\cdot x=kx$ on $\mathbb{Z}/m$.)

q: Why does the long exact sequence of cohomology even start? In other words, why does $0\to A^G\to B^G\to C^G$ NOT continue to a $\to 0$ for a short exact sequence $0\to A\to B\to C\to 0$?
text: Why does the long exact sequence of cohomology even start? In other words, why does $0\to A^G\to B^G\to C^G$ NOT continue to a $\to 0$ for a short exact sequence $0\to A\to B\to C\to 0$? $(-)^G=\Hom_{\mathbb{Z}[G]}(\mathbb{Z},-)$ is left exact but not right exact. The obstruction to lifting a fixed point in $C$ to a fixed point in $B$ lives in $H^1(G,A)$ — that is exactly $\delta(c)$.

q: If $G$ acts trivially on $M$, what is $H^0(G,M)$?
text: If $G$ acts trivially on $M$, what is $H^0(G,M)$? Trivial action means every element is fixed: $M^G=M$. (Note: $\Hom(G,M)$ is what $H^1$ becomes under trivial action.)

q: A crossed homomorphism $\varphi:G\to M$ satisfies which identity?
text: A crossed homomorphism $\varphi:G\to M$ satisfies which identity? The cocycle condition $\varphi(gh)=\varphi(g)+g\cdot\varphi(h)$ — this collapses to the homomorphism law when the action is trivial.

q: Hilbert's Theorem 90 states which of the following for a cyclic Galois extension $L/K$ with $G=\langle\sigma\rangle$?
text: Hilbert's Theorem 90 states which of the following for a cyclic Galois extension $L/K$ with $G=\langle\sigma\rangle$? Hilbert 90 in cyclic form: $H^1(G,L^\times)=1$, equivalently norm-one elements are coboundaries $\sigma(\alpha)/\alpha$. The fact about $L$ (additive) is the additive Hilbert 90, and the $H^2$ statement is the definition of relative Brauer.

q: For $L/K=\mathbb{Q}(i)/\mathbb{Q}$ and $\beta=\frac{3+4i}{5}$ (norm 1), Hilbert 90 produces $\alpha\in L^\times$ with $\sigma(\alpha)/\alpha=\beta$. Which witness works?
text: For $L/K=\mathbb{Q}(i)/\mathbb{Q}$ and $\beta=\frac{3+4i}{5}$ (norm 1), Hilbert 90 produces $\alpha\in L^\times$ with $\sigma(\alpha)/\alpha=\beta$. Which witness works? Pythagorean trick: $\alpha=1+\beta$ works whenever it is nonzero, since $\sigma(1+\beta)=1+\bar\beta=1+\beta^{-1}=(1+\beta)/\beta\cdot\beta^{-1}\cdot\beta=\beta^{-1}(1+\beta)$, hence $\sigma(\alpha)/\alpha=\beta^{-1}\cdot 1=\beta$? Carefully: $\sigma(1+\beta)/(1+\beta)=(1+\beta^{-1})/(1+\beta)=\beta^{-1}$, so adjust to $\alpha=\beta(1+\beta)$ if you want $\beta$ rather than $\beta^{-1}$ — the widget shows the explicit calculation. Pythagorean trick: $\alpha=1+\beta$ works whenever it is nonzero, since $\sigma(1+\beta)=1+\bar\beta=1+\beta^{-1}=(1+\beta)/\beta\cdot\beta^{-1}\cdot\beta=\beta^{-1}(1+\beta)$, hence…

q: Equivalence classes of group extensions $1\to A\to E\to G\to 1$ (with prescribed $G$-action on abelian $A$) are classified by:
text: Equivalence classes of group extensions $1\to A\to E\to G\to 1$ (with prescribed $G$-action on abelian $A$) are classified by: The cocycle $c(g,h)$ measuring failure of a section $s:G\to E$ to be a homomorphism is a 2-cocycle, and changing the section by $a:G\to A$ shifts $c$ by $da$. The class $[c]\in H^2(G,A)$ classifies the extension.

q: Which extensions correspond to the zero class $0\in H^2(G,A)$?
text: Which extensions correspond to the zero class $0\in H^2(G,A)$? $[c]=0$ iff $c$ is a coboundary, iff some section $s$ exists with $c=0$, iff $s$ is a homomorphism, iff the extension splits.

q: Compute $H^2(C_2,\mathbb{Z}/2)$ (trivial action). What does each class correspond to?
text: Compute $H^2(C_2,\mathbb{Z}/2)$ (trivial action). What does each class correspond to? By the periodic resolution, $H^2(C_2,\mathbb{Z}/2)=\mathbb{Z}/2$. The trivial class is $V_4$ (split); the nontrivial class lifts $\sigma\in C_2$ to an order-4 element, giving $C_4$.

q: For $G=C_n$ cyclic with generator $\sigma$ and $G$-module $M$, the cohomology in even and odd positive degrees is:
text: For $G=C_n$ cyclic with generator $\sigma$ and $G$-module $M$, the cohomology in even and odd positive degrees is: The two-step periodic resolution alternates $\sigma-1$ and the norm $N=1+\sigma+\dots+\sigma^{n-1}$. Reading off the cochain complex: even degrees give $\ker(\sigma-1)/\mathrm{im}(N)=M^G/N(M)$, odd degrees give $\ker(N)/\mathrm{im}(\sigma-1)$.

q: For $G=C_3$ acting trivially on $M=\mathbb{Z}/6$, compute $|H^2(C_3,\mathbb{Z}/6)|$.
text: For $G=C_3$ acting trivially on $M=\mathbb{Z}/6$, compute $|H^2(C_3,\mathbb{Z}/6)|$. Trivial action: $\sigma-1=0$ so $M^G=M=\mathbb{Z}/6$, and $N=1+1+1=3$ so $N(M)=3\cdot\mathbb{Z}/6=\{0,3\}$. Then $H^2=M^G/N(M)=(\mathbb{Z}/6)/(2$-element subgroup$)=\mathbb{Z}/3$, of order 3.

q: The Herbrand quotient $h(M)=|\hat H^0(G,M)|/|\hat H^{-1}(G,M)|$ has which key property?
text: The Herbrand quotient $h(M)=|\hat H^0(G,M)|/|\hat H^{-1}(G,M)|$ has which key property? Herbrand quotient is multiplicative: $h(B)=h(A)h(C)$ for SES $0\to A\to B\to C\to 0$, and $h(M)=1$ when $M$ is finite. These two properties are what make it a usable invariant in class field theory.

q: For $H\tr{}ianglelefteq G$ and a $G$-module $M$, the inflation–restriction five-term exact sequence reads:
text: For $H\tr{}ianglelefteq G$ and a $G$-module $M$, the inflation–restriction five-term exact sequence reads: This is the low-degree edge of the LHS spectral sequence. The crucial fixed-points $M^H$ in the inflation entry, and the $G/H$-invariants on the restriction entry, are what make the sequence work — both come from how the spectral sequence's $E_2$-page is built.

q: The Lyndon–Hochschild–Serre spectral sequence has $E_2$-page given by:
text: The Lyndon–Hochschild–Serre spectral sequence has $E_2$-page given by: Take cohomology of the normal subgroup first, then of the quotient. Note the order: $H$ goes inside (gives row $q$), and $G/H$ goes outside (gives column $p$).

q: When does the LHS spectral sequence collapse on the row $q=0$ (so $H^p(G,M)=H^p(G/H,M^H)$ in low degrees)?
text: When does the LHS spectral sequence collapse on the row $q=0$ (so $H^p(G,M)=H^p(G/H,M^H)$ in low degrees)? If $|H|$ is invertible in $M$, then $H^q(H,M)=0$ for $q\ge 1$ (cohomology of a finite group with coefficients in a divisible module vanishes). The page is concentrated on row $q=0$, no differentials are possible, so $H^p(G,M)\cong H^p(G/H,M^H)$.

q: For a field $k$ with absolute Galois group $G_k=\Gal(\bar k/k)$, the Brauer group is defined as:
text: For a field $k$ with absolute Galois group $G_k=\Gal(\bar k/k)$, the Brauer group is defined as: $\mathrm{Br}(k):=H^2(G_k,\bar k^\times)$. (Hilbert 90 says $H^1=0$, so the first nontrivial cohomology of $\bar k^\times$ is degree 2.) This $H^2$ classifies central simple $k$-algebras up to Morita equivalence.

q: The Kummer exact sequence (with $n$ invertible in $k$) is $1\to\mu_n\to\bar k^\times\xrightarrow{x\mapsto x^n}\bar k^\times\to 1$. What does it imply about $H^1(k,\mu_n)$?
text: The Kummer exact sequence (with $n$ invertible in $k$) is $1\to\mu_n\to\bar k^\times\xrightarrow{x\mapsto x^n}\bar k^\times\to 1$. What does it imply about $H^1(k,\mu_n)$? Long exact sequence: $k^\times\xrightarrow{n}k^\times\to H^1(k,\mu_n)\to H^1(k,\bar k^\times)=0$ by Hilbert 90. So $H^1(k,\mu_n)=k^\times/(k^\times)^n$ — the Kummer isomorphism, foundation of Kummer theory.

q: Which Brauer group is correct?
text: Which Brauer group is correct? $\mathrm{Br}(\mathbb{R})=\mathbb{Z}/2$ with generator $\mathbb{H}$. (For comparison: $\mathrm{Br}(\bar k)=0$, $\mathrm{Br}(\mathbb{F}_q)=0$ by Wedderburn, $\mathrm{Br}(\mathbb{Q}_p)=\mathbb{Q}/\mathbb{Z}$ via the local invariant.)

title: Axioms and matrix examples
blurb: A Lie algebra is a vector space with a skew-symmetric bracket $[\cdot,\cdot]$ satisfying the Jacobi identity $[x,[y,z]]+[y,[z,x]]+[z,[x,y]]=0$. Standard families: $\mathfrak{gl}_n$ (all matrices), $\mathfrak{sl}_n$ (traceless), $\mathfrak{so}_n$ (skew-symmetric), $\mathfrak{sp}_{2n}$ (symplectic).

title: Derivations and the adjoint representation
blurb: The adjoint $\ad(x)=[x,-]$ makes every $x\in\mathfrak g$ act as a derivation of the bracket; the Killing form $B(x,y)=\tr(\ad(x)\ad(y))$ is the canonical $\mathfrak g$-invariant symmetric bilinear form.

title: Solvable and nilpotent Lie algebras
blurb: The derived series $\mathfrak g\supseteq[\mathfrak g,\mathfrak g]\supseteq\cdots$ measures solvability; the lower-central series measures nilpotence. Engel: $\ad\,x$ nilpotent for every $x$ iff $\mathfrak g$ is nilpotent. Lie: every solvable rep over $\mathbb C$ is upper-triangularizable.

title: Cartan, Killing, and semisimplicity
blurb: Cartan's criterion: $\mathfrak g$ is semisimple iff its Killing form is nondegenerate. A Cartan subalgebra $\mathfrak h$ diagonalizes $\ad$ and yields a root system $\Phi\subset\mathfrak h^*$, encoded combinatorially by a Dynkin diagram.

title: $\mathfrak{sl}_2$-triples, root spaces, weights
blurb: Each root $\alpha\in\Phi$ gives an $\mathfrak{sl}_2$-triple $(e_\alpha,h_\alpha,f_\alpha)$; finite-dimensional irreps of $\mathfrak{sl}_2$ are weight diagrams $V_n$ with weights $n,n-2,\dots,-n$, and every semisimple representation decomposes into such weight strings.

title: Classification of simple Lie algebras
blurb: Over $\mathbb C$ the simple Lie algebras are exactly the four classical series $A_n=\mathfrak{sl}_{n+1}$, $B_n=\mathfrak{so}_{2n+1}$, $C_n=\mathfrak{sp}_{2n}$, $D_n=\mathfrak{so}_{2n}$ together with five exceptions $E_6,E_7,E_8,F_4,G_2$ — read off from connected Dynkin diagrams.

q: Which identity, beyond bilinearity and skew-symmetry $[x,y]=-[y,x]$, is the defining axiom of a Lie algebra?
text: Which identity, beyond bilinearity and skew-symmetry $[x,y]=-[y,x]$, is the defining axiom of a Lie algebra? The Jacobi identity is the third bracket axiom. ($[x,x]=0$ is equivalent to skew-symmetry in characteristic $\neq 2$ and is therefore not the extra defining condition.) Lie algebras are typically not associative — $\ad$ is a derivation, not a homomorphism. It is the cyclic-sum identity that makes $\ad(x)$ a derivation of the bracket.

q: What is $\dim_{\mathbb C}\mathfrak{sl}_3(\mathbb C)$?
text: What is $\dim_{\mathbb C}\mathfrak{sl}_3(\mathbb C)$? $\mathfrak{sl}_n$ is the traceless matrices: $\dim\mathfrak{sl}_n=n^2-1$. For $n=3$ that is $9-1=8$. $n^2$ matrix entries minus one trace constraint.

q: Which set of matrices forms a Lie subalgebra of $\mathfrak{gl}_n$ under the commutator $[X,Y]=XY-YX$?
text: Which set of matrices forms a Lie subalgebra of $\mathfrak{gl}_n$ under the commutator $[X,Y]=XY-YX$? If $X^\top=-X$ and $Y^\top=-Y$, then $[X,Y]^\top=(XY-YX)^\top=Y^\top X^\top - X^\top Y^\top = YX-XY=-[X,Y]$, so the skew-symmetric matrices $\mathfrak{so}_n$ are bracket-closed. Invertibility is not preserved (and $0$ is not invertible); symmetric matrices are not bracket-closed; trace-$1$ is not even a subspace. Compute $[X,Y]^\top$ when $X,Y$ are skew.

q: Why is $\ad\colon\mathfrak g\to\mathrm{End}(\mathfrak g)$ a Lie algebra homomorphism?
text: Why is $\ad\colon\mathfrak g\to\mathrm{End}(\mathfrak g)$ a Lie algebra homomorphism? Apply both sides to $z$: $[\ad(x),\ad(y)]\,z=[x,[y,z]]-[y,[x,z]]$, while $\ad([x,y])\,z=[[x,y],z]$. These agree iff $[x,[y,z]]+[y,[z,x]]+[z,[x,y]]=0$, the Jacobi identity. So $\ad$ is a Lie homomorphism precisely because of Jacobi. Expand $[\ad(x),\ad(y)]$ acting on a third element $z$.

q: The Killing form $B(x,y)=\tr(\ad(x)\ad(y))$ is $\mathfrak g$-invariant in the sense:
text: The Killing form $B(x,y)=\tr(\ad(x)\ad(y))$ is $\mathfrak g$-invariant in the sense: Invariance means $B$ is annihilated by the adjoint action: differentiating $B(\mathrm{Ad}(g)x,\mathrm{Ad}(g)y)=B(x,y)$ gives $B([z,x],y)+B(x,[z,y])=0$. Equivalently, $\ad(z)$ is skew with respect to $B$. The Killing form is rarely positive-definite (it is on compact real forms only). Differentiate the group-level identity $B(\mathrm{Ad}(g)x,\mathrm{Ad}(g)y)=B(x,y)$ at $g=e$.

q: For abelian $\mathfrak g$ (so $[x,y]=0$ identically), the Killing form is:
text: For abelian $\mathfrak g$ (so $[x,y]=0$ identically), the Killing form is: If $\mathfrak g$ is abelian then $\ad(x)=0$ for every $x$, so $B(x,y)=\tr(0\cdot 0)=0$ identically. This is the cleanest example of a degenerate Killing form — abelian Lie algebras are the opposite of semisimple. $\ad(x)=[x,-]$; what is it when the bracket vanishes?

q: The derived series of $\mathfrak g$ is $\mathfrak g^{(0)}=\mathfrak g$, $\mathfrak g^{(k+1)}=[\mathfrak g^{(k)},\mathfrak g^{(k)}]$. $\mathfrak g$ is solvable iff:
text: The derived series of $\mathfrak g$ is $\mathfrak g^{(0)}=\mathfrak g$, $\mathfrak g^{(k+1)}=[\mathfrak g^{(k)},\mathfrak g^{(k)}]$. $\mathfrak g$ is solvable iff: Solvability of a Lie algebra is the direct analogue of solvability of a group: the derived series eventually terminates at $0$. Nilpotence is the stronger condition that the lower-central series $\mathfrak g^0=\mathfrak g$, $\mathfrak g^{k+1}=[\mathfrak g,\mathfrak g^k]$ terminates; nilpotent implies solvable but not conversely. Each step of the derived series replaces $\mathfrak h$ by $[\mathfrak h,\mathfrak h]$ — solvability says you eventually hit $0$.

q: Engel's theorem says: $\mathfrak g$ is nilpotent iff
text: Engel's theorem says: $\mathfrak g$ is nilpotent iff Engel: $\mathfrak g$ is nilpotent precisely when each $\ad(x)$ is a nilpotent endomorphism — pointwise nilpotence of the adjoint forces uniform nilpotence of the lower-central series. (Even though abstract elements need not be matrices, $\ad(x)$ always is.) Lie's theorem is the parallel statement for solvable: every solvable rep over $\mathbb C$ is upper-triangularizable. It is a statement purely about the operators $\ad(x)$, not about the abstract elements.

q: Which of the following Lie algebras is solvable but not nilpotent?
text: Which of the following Lie algebras is solvable but not nilpotent? Upper-triangular $2\times 2$ matrices form the Borel $\mathfrak b_2$. Its derived algebra is the strictly upper-triangular line, which is abelian, so the derived series terminates — solvable. But $\ad(\mathrm{diag}(a,b))$ acting on $E_{12}$ has eigenvalue $a-b\neq 0$, so $\ad$ is not nilpotent. The Heisenberg algebra is nilpotent (so also solvable); diagonals are abelian; $\mathfrak{sl}_2$ is semisimple, not solvable. Look at a Borel — derived once, then abelian, but $\ad$ has nonzero eigenvalues.

q: Cartan's criterion characterizes semisimple Lie algebras as those with:
text: Cartan's criterion characterizes semisimple Lie algebras as those with: A finite-dimensional Lie algebra over a field of characteristic zero is semisimple iff its Killing form $B$ is nondegenerate. Equivalently, the radical (largest solvable ideal) is zero. Trivial center is necessary but not sufficient; semisimple Lie algebras do have only inner derivations, but that is a consequence, not the criterion. It is the bilinear-form condition on $B(x,y)=\tr(\ad(x)\ad(y))$.

q: A Cartan subalgebra $\mathfrak h\subset\mathfrak g$ of a complex semisimple Lie algebra is:
text: A Cartan subalgebra $\mathfrak h\subset\mathfrak g$ of a complex semisimple Lie algebra is: The general definition is: $\mathfrak h$ is nilpotent and equals its own normalizer. For complex semisimple $\mathfrak g$ this is equivalent to being a maximal toral (ad-diagonalizable) subalgebra — the diagonal matrices in $\mathfrak{sl}_n$. Borels are maximal solvable, strictly larger than $\mathfrak h$. Maximal abelian is too weak in general (though it coincides with Cartan in the semisimple case). Two equivalent characterizations: nilpotent and self-normalizing, or maximal toral (in the semisimple case).

q: The root system $A_2$ (associated with $\mathfrak{sl}_3$) has how many roots?
text: The root system $A_2$ (associated with $\mathfrak{sl}_3$) has how many roots? $\mathfrak{sl}_3$ has Cartan subalgebra of rank $2$ and dimension $8=2+6$. The six nonzero roots are $\pm(\alpha_1)$, $\pm(\alpha_2)$, $\pm(\alpha_1+\alpha_2)$ — the vertices of a regular hexagon in $\mathfrak h^*$. $\dim\mathfrak{sl}_3=8$; subtract the rank.

q: In the standard $\mathfrak{sl}_2$-triple $\{e,h,f\}$, the bracket relations are:
text: In the standard $\mathfrak{sl}_2$-triple $\{e,h,f\}$, the bracket relations are: With the standard generators $e=\begin{pmatrix}0&1\\0&0\end{pmatrix}$, $h=\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&-1\end{pmatrix}$, $f=\begin{pmatrix}0&0\\1&0\end{pmatrix}$ one computes directly $[h,e]=2e$, $[h,f]=-2f$, $[e,f]=h$. The factor $2$ on the diagonal action is exactly the root pairing $\alpha(h)=2$. Compute with the standard upper/lower triangular and diagonal $2\times 2$ matrices.

q: Which sequence of weights occurs in the irreducible $\mathfrak{sl}_2$-representation $V_4$ of dimension $5$?
text: Which sequence of weights occurs in the irreducible $\mathfrak{sl}_2$-representation $V_4$ of dimension $5$? $V_n$ has highest weight $n$ and weights $n,n-2,\dots,-n$, each with multiplicity $1$. For $n=4$: $\{4,2,0,-2,-4\}$. The action of $f$ steps from $V_n$ down by $2$ at a time, so weights are integers of the same parity as $n$. Highest weight is $n=4$; the $f$-orbit subtracts $2$ at each step.

q: In the Clebsch–Gordan rule $V_m\otimes V_n=\bigoplus_{k=0}^{\min(m,n)}V_{m+n-2k}$, the decomposition of $V_2\otimes V_2$ is:
text: In the Clebsch–Gordan rule $V_m\otimes V_n=\bigoplus_{k=0}^{\min(m,n)}V_{m+n-2k}$, the decomposition of $V_2\otimes V_2$ is: With $m=n=2$ and $k=0,1,2$: $V_{4}\oplus V_{2}\oplus V_{0}$. This matches the spin-$1\otimes$ spin-$1$ decomposition into spin-$2$, spin-$1$, spin-$0$ familiar from physics. Dimensions: $5+3+1=9=3\cdot 3$. $k$ runs from $0$ to $\min(m,n)=2$; dimensions must add to $9$.

q: Which classical series corresponds to the symplectic Lie algebra $\mathfrak{sp}_{2n}$?
text: Which classical series corresponds to the symplectic Lie algebra $\mathfrak{sp}_{2n}$? The Cartan labels for the four classical infinite series are $A_n=\mathfrak{sl}_{n+1}$, $B_n=\mathfrak{so}_{2n+1}$, $C_n=\mathfrak{sp}_{2n}$, $D_n=\mathfrak{so}_{2n}$. So symplectic is $C_n$. Mnemonic: A special-linear, B/D orthogonal (odd/even), C symplectic.

q: How many exceptional simple complex Lie algebras are there?
text: How many exceptional simple complex Lie algebras are there? The exceptions are $G_2$, $F_4$, $E_6$, $E_7$, $E_8$ — five total. Together with the four classical series $A,B,C,D$ they exhaust the simple complex Lie algebras (Killing–Cartan classification, late 19th century). $G_2$, $F_4$, and the $E$-series.

q: A Dynkin diagram for a simple complex Lie algebra is connected. Which feature distinguishes the diagram of $G_2$ from those of the classical $A,B,C,D$ series?
text: A Dynkin diagram for a simple complex Lie algebra is connected. Which feature distinguishes the diagram of $G_2$ from those of the classical $A,B,C,D$ series? $G_2$ is the unique simple Lie algebra of rank $2$ with a triple bond — the two simple roots have ratio $\sqrt 3$ in length and their angle is $150^\circ$. $A_2$ has a single bond, $B_2=C_2$ has a double bond. Trivalent branch nodes appear in $D_n,E_6,E_7,E_8$. A simple Lie algebra has connected Dynkin diagram by definition. Among rank-$2$ root systems, the bond multiplicities are $1,2,3$ for $A_2,B_2,G_2$.

title: Galois cocycles and $H^i(G,M)$
blurb: For a finite Galois extension $L/K$ with $G=\Gal(L/K)$ acting on a $G$-module $M$, $H^i(G,M)$ is computed by inhomogeneous cochains $c:G^i\to M$ modulo coboundaries. Examples: $H^0=M^G$, $H^1(G,L^\times)=1$ (Hilbert 90), $H^1(G,\mu_n)=K^\times/(K^\times)^n$ (Kummer).

title: $H^1$ classifies torsors and twisted forms
blurb: $H^1(G,A(L))$ parameterises isomorphism classes of $G$-torsors under an algebraic group $A$ — equivalently, $L/K$-twisted forms of an object with automorphism group $A$. For $A=\mathrm{PGL}_n$ the torsors are central simple algebras of degree $n$; for $A=\mathrm{O}_q$ they are quadratic forms in the same genus.

title: $H^2$ and group extensions
blurb: $H^2(G,A)$ parameterises equivalence classes of central extensions $1\to A\to E\to G\to 1$. The Schur multiplier $M(G)=H^2(G,\mathbb{C}^\times)$ controls projective representations: every projective rep lifts to a linear rep of a central extension by $M(G)$.

title: Brauer group $\mathrm{Br}(K) = H^2(K,\bar K^\times)$
blurb: The Brauer group is $\mathrm{Br}(K)=H^2(\Gal(\bar K/K),\bar K^\times)$, equivalently equivalence classes of central simple $K$-algebras under $A\sim B$ iff $M_m(A)\cong M_n(B)$. Each class has a unique division-algebra representative; cyclic algebras $(a,b)_n$ generate via the cup product $K^\times/(K^\times)^n \otimes K^\times/(K^\times)^n \to \mathrm{Br}(K)[n]$.

title: Cup product and Tate local duality
blurb: The cup product $H^p(G,M)\otimes H^q(G,N)\to H^{p+q}(G,M\otimes N)$ pairs cocycles. Over a non-archimedean local field $K$, Tate duality gives a perfect pairing $H^i(K,M)\times H^{2-i}(K,M^*) \to H^2(K,\bar K^\times) = \mathbb{Q}/\mathbb{Z}$ for finite $M$ — the cohomological avatar of local class field theory.

title: Applications: Hasse principle, Selmer, descent
blurb: Albert–Brauer–Hasse–Noether: a central simple algebra splits over $\mathbb{Q}$ iff it splits over every $\mathbb{Q}_v$. The Brauer–Manin obstruction explains why some smooth varieties have local points everywhere yet no global point. Selmer groups in BSD-style descent are kernels of restriction maps between Galois cohomology groups.

q: Let $G=\Gal(\mathbb{Q}(i)/\mathbb{Q})=\langle\sigma\rangle$. Which statement is Hilbert's Theorem 90 in this case?
text: Let $G=\Gal(\mathbb{Q}(i)/\mathbb{Q})=\langle\sigma\rangle$. Which statement is Hilbert's Theorem 90 in this case? Hilbert 90 says $H^1$ of a cyclic Galois group with coefficients in $L^\times$ vanishes. The cocycle attached to $\beta\in\ker N$ is then a coboundary $g\mapsto\sigma(\alpha)/\alpha$. Hilbert 90 is about cyclic Galois extensions and norm-one elements.

q: A 1-cochain $\varphi:G\to M$ is a cocycle iff
text: A 1-cochain $\varphi:G\to M$ is a cocycle iff The 1-cocycle (crossed homomorphism) condition is $\varphi(gh)=\varphi(g)+g\cdot\varphi(h)$. When the action is trivial it collapses to an ordinary homomorphism. The action of $g$ on the second factor twists the homomorphism law.

q: What is $H^0(\Gal(\bar K/K),\bar K^\times)$?
text: What is $H^0(\Gal(\bar K/K),\bar K^\times)$? By definition $H^0(G,M)=M^G$. The fixed field of $\Gal(\bar K/K)$ is $K$, so the fixed elements of $\bar K^\times$ are $K^\times$. $H^0$ is fixed points; a nonzero element of $\bar K$ fixed by every automorphism lies in $K$.

q: A $G$-torsor under $A$ is a $G$-set $X$ on which $A$ acts simply transitively, with the $G$-action and $A$-action compatible. What is the role of $H^1(G,A)$?
text: A $G$-torsor under $A$ is a $G$-set $X$ on which $A$ acts simply transitively, with the $G$-action and $A$-action compatible. What is the role of $H^1(G,A)$? $H^1(G,A)$ parameterises isomorphism classes of $G$-torsors under $A$; the trivial class is the trivial torsor $A$ acting on itself. Trivial torsor $\leftrightarrow$ trivial cohomology class.

q: By Galois descent, $H^1(\Gal(\bar K/K),\mathrm{PGL}_n(\bar K))$ classifies
text: By Galois descent, $H^1(\Gal(\bar K/K),\mathrm{PGL}_n(\bar K))$ classifies $\mathrm{PGL}_n(\bar K)=\mathrm{Aut}(M_n(\bar K))$ as a $\bar K$-algebra, so its $H^1$ classifies $\bar K/K$-twisted forms of $M_n(K)$ — exactly the central simple $K$-algebras of degree $n$. $\mathrm{PGL}_n$ is the automorphism group of $M_n$.

q: Why is $H^1(\Gal(\bar K/K),\mathrm{GL}_n(\bar K))=1$?
text: Why is $H^1(\Gal(\bar K/K),\mathrm{GL}_n(\bar K))=1$? There is only one $K$-vector space of dimension $n$ up to isomorphism, so there is only one $\bar K/K$-twisted form of $K^n$ — i.e. $H^1$ vanishes. This is the matrix-valued Hilbert 90. All $K$-vector spaces of the same dimension are isomorphic.

q: An extension $1\to A\to E\to G\to 1$ with $A$ abelian and central is split iff
text: An extension $1\to A\to E\to G\to 1$ with $A$ abelian and central is split iff Split central extensions are exactly the trivial cocycle classes in $H^2(G,A)$. A non-zero class records an obstruction to choosing a homomorphic section. A splitting is a section of $E\to G$ that is a homomorphism; the obstruction is the cocycle.

q: The Schur multiplier of a finite group $G$ is
text: The Schur multiplier of a finite group $G$ is $M(G)=H^2(G,\mathbb{C}^\times)$ classifies central extensions by $\mathbb{C}^\times$. Every projective representation of $G$ lifts to a linear representation of a central extension by an element of $M(G)$. It controls the projective representations of $G$.

q: Up to equivalence, how many extensions $1\to\mathbb{Z}/2\to E\to\mathbb{Z}/2\to 1$ exist (trivial action)?
text: Up to equivalence, how many extensions $1\to\mathbb{Z}/2\to E\to\mathbb{Z}/2\to 1$ exist (trivial action)? $H^2(\mathbb{Z}/2,\mathbb{Z}/2)=\mathbb{Z}/2$. The zero class gives $V_4=\mathbb{Z}/2\times\mathbb{Z}/2$; the nontrivial class gives $\mathbb{Z}/4$.

q: Which is the Brauer group $\mathrm{Br}(K)$ as a Galois cohomology group?
text: Which is the Brauer group $\mathrm{Br}(K)$ as a Galois cohomology group? By definition $\mathrm{Br}(K)=H^2(G_K,\bar K^\times)$. $H^1$ vanishes by Hilbert 90; $H^2$ first encounters interesting twisting and matches central simple algebras. Hilbert 90 kills the $H^1$.

q: $\mathrm{Br}(\mathbb{R})$ is
text: $\mathrm{Br}(\mathbb{R})$ is Over $\mathbb{R}$ the only finite-dimensional central division algebras are $\mathbb{R}$ and $\mathbb{H}$ (quaternions), giving $\mathrm{Br}(\mathbb{R})=\mathbb{Z}/2$. Frobenius classified finite-dimensional division algebras over $\mathbb{R}$.

q: The cyclic algebra $(a,b)_n$ is the $K$-algebra generated by $x,y$ with $x^n=a$, $y^n=b$, $yx=\zeta_n xy$. Which is true?
text: The cyclic algebra $(a,b)_n$ is the $K$-algebra generated by $x,y$ with $x^n=a$, $y^n=b$, $yx=\zeta_n xy$. Which is true? By Kummer theory $K^\times/(K^\times)^n=H^1(K,\mu_n)$ when $\mu_n\subset K$. The cup product lands in $H^2(K,\mu_n\otimes\mu_n)\cong H^2(K,\mu_n)\subset\mathrm{Br}(K)[n]$ and is represented by the cyclic algebra $(a,b)_n$. Kummer + cup product into $\mathrm{Br}(K)[n]$.

q: Local Tate duality for a non-archimedean local field $K$ with finite Galois module $M$ gives a perfect pairing
text: Local Tate duality for a non-archimedean local field $K$ with finite Galois module $M$ gives a perfect pairing For a local field the Brauer group is canonically $\mathbb{Q}/\mathbb{Z}$, and local Tate duality pairs $H^i(K,M)$ with $H^{2-i}(K,\Hom(M,\bar K^\times))$ into it. The dualizing module is $\bar K^\times$, whose $H^2$ is $\mathrm{Br}(K)=\mathbb{Q}/\mathbb{Z}$ for local $K$.

q: The cup product on Galois cohomology
text: The cup product on Galois cohomology The Alexander–Whitney cup product satisfies graded-commutativity on cohomology classes but only up to coboundaries on cochains. Cocycle-level commutativity holds only up to a coboundary; sign appears on cohomology.

q: Local class field theory says that for a non-archimedean local $K$, the local invariant
text: Local class field theory says that for a non-archimedean local $K$, the local invariant For non-archimedean local $K$, the unramified Brauer group is $\mathrm{Br}(K^{\mathrm{ur}}/K)=\mathbb{Q}/\mathbb{Z}$ via Frobenius, and this exhausts $\mathrm{Br}(K)$. Each finite cyclic unramified extension contributes $\mathbb{Z}/n$.

q: Albert–Brauer–Hasse–Noether says: a central simple algebra $A$ over a number field $K$ is split iff
text: Albert–Brauer–Hasse–Noether says: a central simple algebra $A$ over a number field $K$ is split iff ABHN gives the exact sequence $0\to\mathrm{Br}(K)\to\bigoplus_v\mathrm{Br}(K_v)\xrightarrow{\sum\mathrm{inv}_v}\mathbb{Q}/\mathbb{Z}\to 0$, so vanishing locally everywhere implies vanishing globally. Local-global for the Brauer group.

q: The Brauer–Manin obstruction is
text: The Brauer–Manin obstruction is For a smooth proper variety $X$, points of $X(\mathbb{A}_K)$ orthogonal to every Brauer class on $X$ form $X(\mathbb{A}_K)^{\mathrm{Br}}\supseteq X(K)$; failure of equality is the Brauer–Manin obstruction. It refines the Hasse principle when the principle fails.

q: In BSD-style descent on an elliptic curve $E/K$, the Selmer group $\mathrm{Sel}_n(E/K)$ sits in an exact sequence
text: In BSD-style descent on an elliptic curve $E/K$, the Selmer group $\mathrm{Sel}_n(E/K)$ sits in an exact sequence $\mathrm{Sel}_n$ is the kernel of $H^1(K,E[n])\to\prod_v H^1(K_v,E)$. Its image of the Kummer map is $E(K)/nE(K)$; the cokernel is $\mathrm{III}(E/K)[n]$. Selmer is the local-global Galois cohomology kernel; Tate–Shafarevich is what's left after subtracting the global Mordell–Weil image.

title: What is a topos?
blurb: An elementary topos is a finitely complete cartesian closed category with a subobject classifier $\Omega$. The three pieces — limits, exponentials, $\Omega$ — together let a category function as a universe of generalized sets in which set-theoretic constructions and intuitionist logic make sense.

title: The subobject classifier $\Omega$
blurb: The subobject classifier $\Omega$ is an object equipped with a map $\mathrm{true}\colon 1 \to \Omega$ such that every monomorphism $S \hookrightarrow X$ is the pullback of $\mathrm{true}$ along a unique characteristic map $\chi_S\colon X \to \Omega$. In $\mathbf{Set}$, $\Omega = \{\bot, \top\}$ and $\chi_S$ is the indicator function.

title: Characteristic maps as truth values
blurb: Every subobject of $X$ is named by exactly one map $X \to \Omega$, so $\Omega$ doubles as the object of "truth values" of the topos. Logical operations on subobjects (intersection, union, implication) become operations on $\Omega$ that the topos sees natively.

title: Power objects $P(A)$
blurb: The power object $P(A) = \Omega^A$ represents subobjects of $A$ in the same sense $\mathcal{P}(A)$ does in $\mathbf{Set}$: maps $X \to P(A)$ correspond to subobjects of $X \times A$. Power objects make every topos a model of the basic comprehension principle.

title: Presheaf toposes $\hat{C} = [C^{\mathrm{op}}, \mathbf{Set}]$
blurb: For any small category $C$, the functor category $\hat{C}$ is a topos. Limits and exponentials are computed pointwise; $\Omega(c)$ classifies sieves on $c$. Presheaf toposes are the source of most topos-theoretic intuition outside $\mathbf{Set}$.

title: The topos of $G$-sets
blurb: For a group $G$, the category of left $G$-sets is a topos: it's the presheaf topos on $\mathbf{B}G$ (the one-object groupoid). Its subobject classifier is the lattice of subgroups, generalizing $\{\bot,\top\}$ in $\mathbf{Set}$.

title: Geometric morphisms
blurb: A geometric morphism $f\colon \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{E}$ is an adjoint pair $f^* \dashv f_*$ with $f^*$ left exact. They are the natural notion of "map between toposes," generalizing continuous maps between spaces and ring homomorphisms in the algebraic-geometric direction.

q: Select all categories that are elementary toposes.
text: Select all categories that are elementary toposes. $\mathbf{Set}$ is the prototypical topos. $G\text{-}\mathbf{Set} = [\mathbf{B}G^{\mathrm{op}}, \mathbf{Set}]$ is a presheaf topos. Any presheaf category is a topos. $\mathbf{Top}$ fails cartesian closure (function spaces aren't always topologisable). $\mathbf{Ab}$ has no subobject classifier (a non-trivial $\Omega$ would have to be additive, forcing collapse). A topos requires finite limits, cartesian closure, and a subobject classifier. Which fail in $\mathbf{Top}$ and $\mathbf{Ab}$?

q: Which of the following is NOT one of the three defining axioms of an elementary topos?
text: Which of the following is NOT one of the three defining axioms of an elementary topos? Elementary toposes need only finite limits, not arbitrary colimits. Grothendieck toposes are additionally cocomplete and have a small generator, but elementary toposes are weaker. Compare 'elementary topos' (Lawvere–Tierney) vs 'Grothendieck topos'.

q: By the Mikkelsen / Paré–Tierney theorem, an elementary topos can equivalently be defined as a finitely complete category with which extra structure?
text: By the Mikkelsen / Paré–Tierney theorem, an elementary topos can equivalently be defined as a finitely complete category with which extra structure? A finitely complete category with power objects automatically has exponentials and a subobject classifier $\Omega = P(1)$, hence is a topos. This is the second equivalent definition. The defining feature 'beyond limits' that recovers exponentials and $\Omega$.

q: Match each topos to its subobject classifier $\Omega$.
text: Match each topos to its subobject classifier $\Omega$. $\mathbf{Set}$ has $\Omega = \{\bot, \top\}$ (classical truth values). Presheaf toposes have $\Omega(c) = \mathrm{Sieves}(c)$. Sheaves on a topological space $X$ have $\Omega(U) = $ open subsets of $U$. The topos $G\text{-}\mathbf{Set}$ is a presheaf topos on $\mathbf{B}G$ and its $\Omega$ unwinds to subgroups of $G$ with conjugation action. Each generalises 'truth values'. In a presheaf topos a 'truth value at $c$' is a sieve.

q: Which property uniquely characterises $\Omega$ up to canonical isomorphism in $\mathcal{E}$?
text: Which property uniquely characterises $\Omega$ up to canonical isomorphism in $\mathcal{E}$? $\Omega$ represents $\mathrm{Sub}(-)$: there is a natural bijection $\mathrm{Sub}(X) \cong \Hom(X, \Omega)$. By Yoneda this determines $\Omega$ up to canonical iso. Counter-examples: in non-classical toposes $\Omega$ may have more than two global sections (e.g. presheaf toposes), so option C fails. Yoneda: a representable functor determines its representing object up to unique iso.

q: In the presheaf topos $\hat{C}$ where $C = \{a \le b\}$ is the walking arrow (the two-element chain), how many global sections does $\Omega$ have, i.e. how many maps $1 \to \Omega$?
text: In the presheaf topos $\hat{C}$ where $C = \{a \le b\}$ is the walking arrow (the two-element chain), how many global sections does $\Omega$ have, i.e. how many maps $1 \to \Omega$? Global sections of $\Omega$ in $\hat{C}$ correspond to subobjects of the terminal presheaf $1$, which are sieves on the maximal element compatible with restrictions. For the walking arrow these are: $\emptyset$, $\{a\}$ (down-closed since $a \le b$ is the only non-trivial inclusion and $\{a\}$ is closed under going down), and the whole $\{a, b\}$. So 3 — the topos is 3-valued. The global sections of $\Omega$ are the 'truth values' of the topos. For a poset, count the down-sets.

q: Why does $\mathbf{Ab}$ (the category of abelian groups) fail to have a subobject classifier?
text: Why does $\mathbf{Ab}$ (the category of abelian groups) fail to have a subobject classifier? Subobjects in $\mathbf{Ab}$ are kernels, named by quotient maps $X \to X/S$ — and the codomain depends on $S$, so they cannot all be classified by a single $X \to \Omega$. Concretely, any candidate $\Omega$ in $\mathbf{Ab}$ would be an abelian group, with maps to it given by abelian group homomorphisms, which is far too restrictive. Option C is partially right but misstates the symptom; D is the cleanest articulation. Think about how subobjects in $\mathbf{Ab}$ are usually 'named.'

q: Let $C = \{a, b\}$ be the discrete category with two objects (no non-identity morphisms). What is $\Omega$ in $\hat{C} = \mathbf{Set}^C$?
text: Let $C = \{a, b\}$ be the discrete category with two objects (no non-identity morphisms). What is $\Omega$ in $\hat{C} = \mathbf{Set}^C$? Sieves on $a$ in a discrete category are subsets of the empty set of non-identity arrows into $a$, plus the choice of including or excluding the identity — so $\Omega(a) = \{\emptyset, \{\mathrm{id}_a\}\} = \{\bot,\top\}$. Same for $b$. The topos $\mathbf{Set}^{\{a,b\}} = \mathbf{Set} \times \mathbf{Set}$ has $\Omega = \{\bot,\top\}\times\{\bot,\top\}$, with 4 global sections. A discrete category with two objects gives a product topos $\mathbf{Set} \times \mathbf{Set}$.

q: In $\mathbf{Set}$, let $X = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ and $S = \{2, 4\}$. The characteristic map $\chi_S\colon X \to \Omega = \{\bot, \top\}$ sends:
text: In $\mathbf{Set}$, let $X = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ and $S = \{2, 4\}$. The characteristic map $\chi_S\colon X \to \Omega = \{\bot, \top\}$ sends: The characteristic map of $S \subseteq X$ sends $x \in S$ to $\top$ and $x \notin S$ to $\bot$ — the indicator function. Here $S = \{2, 4\}$ so $\chi_S(2) = \chi_S(4) = \top$ and $\chi_S(1) = \chi_S(3) = \bot$. In $\mathbf{Set}$, $\chi_S$ is the indicator function of $S$.

q: In a topos, the internal conjunction $\wedge\colon \Omega \times \Omega \to \Omega$ is defined as the characteristic map of which subobject of $\Omega \times \Omega$?
text: In a topos, the internal conjunction $\wedge\colon \Omega \times \Omega \to \Omega$ is defined as the characteristic map of which subobject of $\Omega \times \Omega$? $\wedge$ should classify pairs $(p, q)$ with $p = q = \top$. That subobject of $\Omega \times \Omega$ is exactly $\langle \mathrm{true},\mathrm{true}\rangle\colon 1 \hookrightarrow \Omega \times \Omega$. Its characteristic map is $\wedge$. $p \wedge q = \top$ iff both $p$ and $q$ are $\top$. Which subobject of $\Omega \times \Omega$ does that pick out?

q: Order the following steps in the construction of internal conjunction $\wedge\colon \Omega \times \Omega \to \Omega$.
text: Order the following steps in the construction of internal conjunction $\wedge\colon \Omega \times \Omega \to \Omega$. First identify the subobject $\{(\top,\top)\}$ of $\Omega \times \Omega$, then invoke the universal property of $\Omega$ to get its unique classifying map, verify it does what it should via pullback, and name it $\wedge$. Identify the subobject first, then apply the universal property.

q: In $\mathbf{Set}$, let $A = \{a, b, c, d\}$. Compute $|P(A)|$.
text: In $\mathbf{Set}$, let $A = \{a, b, c, d\}$. Compute $|P(A)|$. $P(A) = \Omega^A$ in $\mathbf{Set}$ is the set of functions $A \to \{\bot, \top\}$, i.e. the power set $\mathcal{P}(A)$. $|P(A)| = 2^{|A|} = 2^4 = 16$. $|P(A)| = 2^{|A|}$ in $\mathbf{Set}$.

q: The bijection $\Hom(X \times A, \Omega) \cong \Hom(X, P(A))$ comes from which categorical principle?
text: The bijection $\Hom(X \times A, \Omega) \cong \Hom(X, P(A))$ comes from which categorical principle? Cartesian closure provides $\Hom(X \times A, B) \cong \Hom(X, B^A)$ for every $B$. Plugging $B = \Omega$ gives the displayed bijection with $P(A) = \Omega^A$. It's currying — the right-adjoint of $(-) \times A$.

q: What is the universal property of the membership relation $\in_A \hookrightarrow A \times P(A)$?
text: What is the universal property of the membership relation $\in_A \hookrightarrow A \times P(A)$? $\in_A$ is the universal subobject of $A \times P(A)$: it is classified by the evaluation map $\mathrm{ev}\colon A \times P(A) \to \Omega$ obtained by uncurrying the identity $\mathrm{id}_{P(A)}\colon P(A) \to P(A) = \Omega^A$. Every other subobject $R \hookrightarrow A \times P(A)$ pulls back from $\in_A$ along $\mathrm{id} \times \hat\chi$. The membership relation should be the 'universal' subobject — the one all others pull back from.

q: Let $C = \{a \le b \le c\}$ be the chain of length 3. How many sieves are there on $c$?
text: Let $C = \{a \le b \le c\}$ be the chain of length 3. How many sieves are there on $c$? Sieves on $c$ in a poset are down-closed subsets of $\{a, b, c\}$. The down-sets are: $\emptyset, \{a\}, \{a, b\}, \{a, b, c\}$. So $|\Omega(c)| = 4$. In a poset, sieves on $c$ are down-closed subsets of $\{x : x \le c\}$.

q: In the presheaf topos $\hat{C}$ for $C$ a small category, exponentials $G^F$ are computed by which formula?
text: In the presheaf topos $\hat{C}$ for $C$ a small category, exponentials $G^F$ are computed by which formula? Pointwise definitions don't give the right adjoint. The correct formula uses the representable $よ(c) = \Hom(-, c)$ and natural transformations: $G^F(c) = \Hom_{\hat C}(F \times \Hom(-, c), G)$. Limits in $\hat C$ are pointwise; exponentials are not. Limits in $\hat C$ are pointwise. Exponentials need Yoneda.

q: Which of the following is NOT a presheaf topos?
text: Which of the following is NOT a presheaf topos? Sheaves on a non-discrete space $X$ are a *reflective subcategory* of presheaves on $\mathrm{Open}(X)$, but they are not themselves a presheaf topos in general. Sheafification is needed. The other three are all presheaf categories. Sheaves are presheaves satisfying a gluing condition — and that's a strict subcategory.

q: Let $C$ be the walking-isomorphism category — two objects $a, b$ with arrows $f\colon a \to b$, $g\colon b \to a$, and $g\circ f = \mathrm{id}_a$, $f \circ g = \mathrm{id}_b$. What is $\hat{C}$ equivalent to?
text: Let $C$ be the walking-isomorphism category — two objects $a, b$ with arrows $f\colon a \to b$, $g\colon b \to a$, and $g\circ f = \mathrm{id}_a$, $f \circ g = \mathrm{id}_b$. What is $\hat{C}$ equivalent to? When $C$ is equivalent to a one-object category, so is $\hat C$. The walking iso is equivalent to the terminal category (one object, one identity), so $\hat C \simeq \mathbf{Set}$. Equivalences of categories induce equivalences of presheaf categories. What category is the walking iso equivalent to?

q: In a presheaf topos $\hat{C}$, what is $\Omega$ at the level of 'global elements,' i.e. how many maps $1 \to \Omega$ are there in $\hat{C}$?
text: In a presheaf topos $\hat{C}$, what is $\Omega$ at the level of 'global elements,' i.e. how many maps $1 \to \Omega$ are there in $\hat{C}$? $\Hom_{\hat C}(1, \Omega) = \mathrm{Sub}_{\hat C}(1)$: subobjects of the terminal presheaf. These correspond to *closed sieves on the whole category* — equivalently, idempotent natural transformations $1 \to 1$. For $C = \mathbf{B}G$ this gives 2 (only $\emptyset$ and the maximal sieve survive); for a poset it gives the number of down-closed subsets of $\mathrm{Ob}(C)$. $\Hom(1, \Omega) = \mathrm{Sub}(1)$ — subobjects of the terminal.

q: Let $G = \mathbb{Z}/6$. Which of the following is the lattice of subgroups of $G$ (and hence the lattice of subobjects of $G/1$ in $G\text{-}\mathbf{Set}$)?
text: Let $G = \mathbb{Z}/6$. Which of the following is the lattice of subgroups of $G$ (and hence the lattice of subobjects of $G/1$ in $G\text{-}\mathbf{Set}$)? $\mathbb{Z}/6$ has subgroups $\{0\}, \langle 3 \rangle = \{0, 3\} \cong \mathbb{Z}/2, \langle 2 \rangle = \{0, 2, 4\} \cong \mathbb{Z}/3$, and $\mathbb{Z}/6$ itself. The lattice is a diamond: trivial at the bottom, $\mathbb{Z}/2$ and $\mathbb{Z}/3$ as incomparable middle elements, $\mathbb{Z}/6$ on top. By Lagrange, divisors of 6 are $1, 2, 3, 6$; cyclic groups have one subgroup per divisor.

q: In $G\text{-}\mathbf{Set}$, the subobject classifier $\Omega$ as a $G$-set has underlying set:
text: In $G\text{-}\mathbf{Set}$, the subobject classifier $\Omega$ as a $G$-set has underlying set: $\Omega$ in $G\text{-}\mathbf{Set} = [\mathbf{B}G^{\mathrm{op}}, \mathbf{Set}]$ at the unique object $* \in \mathbf{B}G$ is the set of sieves on $*$, which unwinds to subgroups of $G$. The presheaf-restriction action becomes conjugation: $g \cdot H = gHg^{-1}$. All subgroups appear, not just normal ones. Sieves on the unique object of $\mathbf{B}G$ are exactly subgroups of $G$ (downward closure becomes the subgroup property).

q: When does the topos $G\text{-}\mathbf{Set}$ collapse to (be equivalent to) ordinary $\mathbf{Set}$?
text: When does the topos $G\text{-}\mathbf{Set}$ collapse to (be equivalent to) ordinary $\mathbf{Set}$? When $G$ is trivial, $\mathbf{B}G$ is the terminal category, so $G\text{-}\mathbf{Set} = [\mathbf{B}\{e\}^{\mathrm{op}}, \mathbf{Set}] \simeq \mathbf{Set}$. For any non-trivial $G$, $\Omega$ has more than 2 elements (the lattice of subgroups), so the topos cannot be $\mathbf{Set}$. $\Omega$ is the lattice of subgroups. When does that have only two elements?

q: A geometric morphism $f\colon \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{E}$ consists of an adjoint pair $f^* \dashv f_*$. Which is the inverse-image, which is the direct-image, and which is the constraint?
text: A geometric morphism $f\colon \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{E}$ consists of an adjoint pair $f^* \dashv f_*$. Which is the inverse-image, which is the direct-image, and which is the constraint? By convention the geometric morphism's named direction is the same as $f_*$ (direct image): $f_*\colon \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{E}$ goes the same way as $f$. The inverse-image $f^*\colon \mathcal{E} \to \mathcal{F}$ goes the other way and is required to be left exact (preserve finite limits). Right adjoints are automatically left exact, but the constraint here is on the *left* adjoint $f^*$, which is non-trivial. The 'direct image' is in the same direction as $f$ itself. The other one carries the exactness requirement.

q: For a continuous map $f\colon X \to Y$ of topological spaces, the induced geometric morphism on sheaves goes which direction, and what plays the role of $f^*$?
text: For a continuous map $f\colon X \to Y$ of topological spaces, the induced geometric morphism on sheaves goes which direction, and what plays the role of $f^*$? A continuous $f\colon X \to Y$ induces a geometric morphism $\mathrm{Sh}(X) \to \mathrm{Sh}(Y)$ in the same direction as $f$. The inverse-image functor $f^*\colon \mathrm{Sh}(Y) \to \mathrm{Sh}(X)$ is sheaf-pullback (left exact because $f^{-1}$ preserves intersections of opens); $f_*$ is pushforward (often called direct image of sheaves). Topology: the map of *opens* goes the opposite way of $f$, but the geometric morphism still 'points like $f$.'

q: A *point* of a topos $\mathcal{E}$ is by definition a geometric morphism with which source and target?
text: A *point* of a topos $\mathcal{E}$ is by definition a geometric morphism with which source and target? Points of $\mathcal{E}$ are geometric morphisms $\mathbf{Set} \to \mathcal{E}$, mirroring continuous maps $\{*\} \to X$ for spaces. The data is a left-exact left adjoint $p^*\colon \mathcal{E} \to \mathbf{Set}$ (the 'stalk' functor) plus its right adjoint $p_*\colon \mathbf{Set} \to \mathcal{E}$ (skyscraper). A point is the 'morphism from a single point' — but the source/target convention follows the geometric-morphism convention.

q: The inclusion $\mathrm{Sh}(C, J) \hookrightarrow \hat{C}$ of sheaves into presheaves is a geometric morphism. In which direction does the morphism go, and what is $f^*$?
text: The inclusion $\mathrm{Sh}(C, J) \hookrightarrow \hat{C}$ of sheaves into presheaves is a geometric morphism. In which direction does the morphism go, and what is $f^*$? Sheaves form a *left-exact reflective* subcategory of presheaves. The geometric morphism is $\mathrm{Sh}(C, J) \to \hat{C}$ with $f^* = $ sheafification $a$ (left exact — this is what 'left-exact reflective' means and is the content of the sheafification theorem), and $f_* = $ the inclusion (right adjoint to sheafification). Embedding-as-direct-image, sheafification-as-inverse-image. Sheafification is a *left adjoint* (of the inclusion) and is left exact. That makes it $f^*$.

q: An *essential* geometric morphism $f\colon \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{E}$ is one for which $f^*$ has an additional left adjoint $f_!$. Which of the following is NOT generally true of essential geometric morphisms?
text: An *essential* geometric morphism $f\colon \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{E}$ is one for which $f^*$ has an additional left adjoint $f_!$. Which of the following is NOT generally true of essential geometric morphisms? Being a left adjoint, $f_!$ preserves all colimits — but it is *not* generally left exact. Essential morphisms in fact tend to be the ones where $f^*$ has both adjoints precisely because it has an even nicer 'Kan extension' structure on both sides. Forgetting left exactness of $f_!$ is exactly what allows $f^*$ to remain left exact while $f_!$ does the colimit work. $f_!$ is a *left* adjoint — preserving colimits is automatic. What's special about $f^*$ being left exact, that $f_!$ doesn't share?

title: Heyting algebras
blurb: A Heyting algebra is a bounded lattice with a binary operation $\Rightarrow$ satisfying $a \wedge b \le c \iff a \le b \Rightarrow c$. Boolean algebras are the special case where double-negation is the identity; topological open-set lattices are generic Heyting algebras.

title: $\Omega$ as an internal Heyting algebra
blurb: In any topos, the subobject classifier $\Omega$ carries an internal Heyting-algebra structure: maps $\Omega \times \Omega \to \Omega$ implementing $\wedge, \vee, \Rightarrow$. Subobject lattices $\mathrm{Sub}(X)$ become external Heyting algebras with these operations applied pointwise.

title: The internal Mitchell–Bénabou language
blurb: Every topos has an internal first-order language: types are objects, terms are morphisms, formulas are characteristic maps into $\Omega$. Provability in this language tracks subobject inclusion, and the resulting logic is intuitionist (LEM may fail).

title: Kripke–Joyal forcing semantics
blurb: Kripke–Joyal semantics interprets the internal language stage-by-stage: an object $X$ "forces" a formula $\varphi$ when the corresponding subobject contains all of $X$. In presheaf toposes this collapses to Kripke's classical possible-world semantics for intuitionist logic.

title: Why LEM fails: double negation in toposes
blurb:
Most toposes are not Boolean: $
eg
eg p = p$ generally fails internally. The double-negation topology on a topos extracts the largest Boolean sub-topos and is the algebraic shadow of "forcing $p \vee 
eg p$".

title: Geometric morphisms preserve geometric logic
blurb: Inverse image functors $f^*$ preserve finite limits and arbitrary colimits — exactly the connectives of geometric logic ($\exists, \wedge, \bigvee$). This is why geometric formulas transport across geometric morphisms while implication and $\forall$ generally do not.

q: On the open-set lattice $\tau = \{\varnothing, \{x\}, \{y\}, \{x,y\}, X\}$ of $X = \{x,y,z\}$, what is $\{x\} \Rightarrow \{y\}$?
text: On the open-set lattice $\tau = \{\varnothing, \{x\}, \{y\}, \{x,y\}, X\}$ of $X = \{x,y,z\}$, what is $\{x\} \Rightarrow \{y\}$? $a \Rightarrow b$ is the largest $c$ with $a \wedge c \le b$. Test each candidate: $\{x\} \cap \{y\} = \varnothing \subseteq \{y\}$ ✓, $\{x\} \cap \{x,y\} = \{x\} \not\subseteq \{y\}$, $\{x\}\cap X = \{x\}\not\subseteq\{y\}$. So the largest $c$ that works is $\{y\}$ itself. Compute $\{x\} \cap c$ for each $c$ in the lattice and check which lie inside $\{y\}$ — pick the largest.

q: Which of the following finite lattices are Heyting algebras?
text: Which of the following finite lattices are Heyting algebras? A finite lattice is Heyting iff it is distributive (then $b\Rightarrow c$ exists as the join of all $x$ with $x\wedge b\le c$). $N_5$ and $M_3$ are the two forbidden non-distributive sublattices of Birkhoff's theorem, so they are not Heyting. Boolean algebras are distributive (in fact complemented). Finite chains are distributive. Heyting ⇔ distributive (for finite lattices). The two non-distributive 5-element lattices are exactly $N_5$ and $M_3$.

q: In a Heyting algebra, which identity holds for every element $a$?
text:
In a Heyting algebra, which identity holds for every element $a$? $

eg a$ is provable in intuitionist logic: from $a \le 
eg
eg a$ apply $
eg$ (which reverses) to get $
eg
eg
eg a \le 
eg a$; combine with the dual instance. The first two are LEM/DNE — equivalent to being Boolean. The fourth fails when $a 
e  ot$. Triple negation collapses to single negation in any Heyting algebra — but double negation does not collapse to identity.

q: What is the subobject classifier $\Omega$ in the topos $\mathbf{Set}$?
text: What is the subobject classifier $\Omega$ in the topos $\mathbf{Set}$? In $\mathbf{Set}$, subobjects of $X$ are subsets, and a subset is determined by its indicator function $X \to \{\bot,\top\}$. So $\Omega = \{\bot,\top\}$ with $\mathrm{true}(*)=\top$. Two truth values — exactly the data needed for classical Boolean logic. Subobjects of a set are subsets; an indicator function takes how many values?

q: In the topos $G$-$\mathbf{Set}$ of left $G$-sets, $\Omega$ as a $G$-set has underlying set:
text: In the topos $G$-$\mathbf{Set}$ of left $G$-sets, $\Omega$ as a $G$-set has underlying set: A subobject of the terminal $G$-set $\{*\}$ is a $G$-stable subset of $\{*\}$ — but the elements of $\Omega$ in $G$-$\mathbf{Set}$ are sieves on the unique object of the one-object groupoid $G$, which correspond to subgroups of $G$. The action of $g$ on $H \le G$ is conjugation $H \mapsto gHg^{-1}$. Think of $G$ as a one-object groupoid; $\Omega(c)$ in a presheaf topos is the sieves on $c$.

q: Match each Heyting operation on $\Omega$ with the subobject of $\Omega \times \Omega$ it classifies.
text: Match each Heyting operation on $\Omega$ with the subobject of $\Omega \times \Omega$ it classifies. $\wedge$ classifies the singleton $(\top,\top)$; $\vee$ classifies the union of the two true-lines; $\Rightarrow$ classifies the order $\le$. See Mac Lane–Moerdijk IV.8. Conjunction is true only when both coordinates are true; implication is true when the first coordinate is below the second.

q: In $\mathrm{Sh}(\mathbb{R})$, the section $\Omega(U)$ over an open $U \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ is:
text: In $\mathrm{Sh}(\mathbb{R})$, the section $\Omega(U)$ over an open $U \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ is: In $\mathrm{Sh}(X)$, $\Omega$ is the sheaf $U \mapsto \{\text{open subsets of } U\}$ with $\mathrm{true}_U = U$. A subobject of a sheaf $F$ over $U$ is a sub-sheaf, picked out by 'on which open does each section live.' This makes $\Omega(U)$ a Heyting algebra under $\cap, \cup,$ and the formula $V \Rightarrow W = \mathrm{int}(U \setminus V \cup W)$. Sub-sheaves of the terminal sheaf over $U$ are determined by the maximal open on which the section is defined.

q: Which morphism implements the operation $\neg\colon \Omega \to \Omega$?
text: Which morphism implements the operation $\neg\colon \Omega \to \Omega$? $\neg p = p \Rightarrow \bot$. The map pairs $p$ with the constant $\bot$ (via the unique $\Omega \to 1$ then $\bot\colon 1 \to \Omega$) and applies $\Rightarrow$. There is no canonical 'swap automorphism' on $\Omega$ in non-Boolean toposes. Negation is implication against falsehood.

q: Match each piece of the internal language to its categorical interpretation.
text: Match each piece of the internal language to its categorical interpretation. Closed terms are global sections; formulas are characteristic maps (or equivalently subobjects); $\exists$ is the image / left adjoint; $\forall$ is the right adjoint to pullback. This is the standard Lawvere adjoint correspondence for quantifiers. $\exists$ is image-along-projection; $\forall$ is dual.

q: The formula $\forall x.\,(P(x) \to Q(x))$ is interpreted in $\mathrm{Sub}(1)$ as:
text: The formula $\forall x.\,(P(x) \to Q(x))$ is interpreted in $\mathrm{Sub}(1)$ as: First compute $\{P \to Q\} = \{P\} \Rightarrow \{Q\}$ in $\mathrm{Sub}(A)$. Then quantify universally: $\forall x.\,$ becomes $\Pi_\pi$, the right adjoint to $\pi^*\colon \mathrm{Sub}(1) \to \mathrm{Sub}(A)$. So the answer is $\Pi_\pi(\{P\} \Rightarrow \{Q\})$. Implication first, then $\forall$ as right adjoint to pullback.

q: Provability $\Gamma \vdash \varphi$ in the Mitchell–Bénabou logic translates to which categorical condition?
text: Provability $\Gamma \vdash \varphi$ in the Mitchell–Bénabou logic translates to which categorical condition? $\Gamma \vdash \varphi$ means: in the context $\Gamma$, $\varphi$ is always true. In subobject terms, the subobject $\{\Gamma \mid \varphi\}$ is everything — equal to the top of $\mathrm{Sub}(\llbracket\Gamma\rrbracket)$. The classifying map $\llbracket\varphi\rrbracket$ is then $\mathrm{true}\circ !\colon \llbracket\Gamma\rrbracket \to 1 \to \Omega$. A formula provable from $\Gamma$ holds on the entire context object.

q: On the 2-world Kripke model with $C = (a \to b)$ and valuation $\llbracket p\rrbracket(a)=\varnothing$, $\llbracket p\rrbracket(b)=\{*\}$, which of the following are forced at world $b$?
text:
On the 2-world Kripke model with $C = (a 	o b)$ and valuation $\llbracket prbracket(a)=\varnothing$, $\llbracket prbracket(b)=\{*\}$, which of the following are forced at world $b$? At $b$: (0) $b \Vdash p$ since $\llbracket prbracket(b) 
e \varnothing$. (1) $b 
ot\Vdash 
eg p$ since $\mathrm{id}_b$ forces $p$. (2) $b \Vdash p \vee 
eg p$ via the left disjunct. (3) $b \Vdash 
eg
eg p 	o p$: every accessible $Y 	o b$ that forces $
eg
eg p$ also forces $p$ — direct. (4) $b \Vdash p 	o 
eg
eg p$: an intuitionist tautology. So everything except $
eg p$ is forced at $b$. Note that LEM holds at the maximal world $b$ but fails at the lower world $a$. At the highest world, you can verify $p$ directly; only $
eg p$ requires no future to ever satisfy $p$, which fails.

q: The Kripke–Joyal clause for implication $\varphi \to \psi$ at $X$ is:
text: The Kripke–Joyal clause for implication $\varphi \to \psi$ at $X$ is: Kripke–Joyal $\to$ quantifies over every generalised element $Y \to X$ — this is the persistence/stability condition. Without this universal closure, $\to$ would not be invariant under restriction along arrows, and the clauses wouldn't be sound for the topos's internal logic. Implication in intuitionist Kripke semantics persists along the accessibility relation — the clause must quantify over all accessible worlds.

q: In a sheaf topos $\mathrm{Sh}(X)$, the Kripke–Joyal clause for $\exists y.\,\varphi$ at an open $U$ becomes:
text: In a sheaf topos $\mathrm{Sh}(X)$, the Kripke–Joyal clause for $\exists y.\,\varphi$ at an open $U$ becomes: Existential quantification in sheaf forcing is 'locally a witness exists': there is a covering family on which the witness can be chosen. This is why constructive analysis builds objects locally and glues them by descent rather than picking global sections. Sheaves are about local data; existential is local existence of a witness.

q: Consider the 3-world model $C$ with objects $a, b, c$ and arrows $a \to b, a \to c$ (no arrow $b \leftrightarrow c$). Let $\llbracket p\rrbracket(b) = \{*\}$, $\llbracket p\rrbracket(a) = \llbracket p\rrbracket(c) = \varnothing$. Where does $\neg\neg p$ hold?
text:
Consider the 3-world model $C$ with objects $a, b, c$ and arrows $a 	o b, a 	o c$ (no arrow $b \leftrightarrow c$). Let $\llbracket prbracket(b) = \{*\}$, $\llbracket prbracket(a) = \llbracket prbracket(c) = \varnothing$. Where does $

eg

ot\Vdash

q: The Kripke–Joyal clause for disjunction in a topos requires a cover $\{f_i\colon X_i \to X\}$. In a presheaf topos $\widehat{C}$, what is a cover of a representable $X = y(c)$?
text: The Kripke–Joyal clause for disjunction in a topos requires a cover $\{f_i\colon X_i \to X\}$. In a presheaf topos $\widehat{C}$, what is a cover of a representable $X = y(c)$? In a presheaf topos $\widehat{C}$ (no Grothendieck topology), the only cover of a representable $y(c)$ is the trivial cover $\{\mathrm{id}\}$. This is why Kripke–Joyal forcing on $\widehat{C}$ collapses to the classical Kripke clauses (no genuine 'covering' refinement). Switching to sheaves $\mathrm{Sh}(C, J)$ on a non-trivial site adds genuine covers and is what enables descent. Presheaf toposes have no Grothendieck topology beyond the trivial one — covers are trivial.

q: In which presheaf topos does LEM fail ?
text: In which presheaf topos does LEM fail ? $\widehat{*}$ is just $\mathbf{Set}$ (Boolean). $\widehat{G}$ is $G$-$\mathbf{Set}$, also Boolean (since $G$ is a groupoid). $\widehat{(a\to b)}$ is non-Boolean: $\Omega(a)$ has 3 elements (the sieves $\varnothing, \{a\}, \{a, a\to b\}$ — really the latter two), and the 3-element chain is the smallest non-Boolean Heyting algebra. LEM fails. Theorem: $\widehat{C}$ is Boolean iff $C$ is a groupoid. $\widehat{C}$ is Boolean iff $C$ is a groupoid — the arrow $a \to b$ is not invertible.

q: Select all toposes in which the law of excluded middle holds (i.e. all Boolean toposes).

The double-negation topology $
eg
eg\colon \Omega 	o \Omega$ on a topos $\mathcal{E}$ produces a subtopos $\mathrm{Sh}_{
eg
eg}(\mathcal{E}) \hookrightarrow \mathcal{E}$. What is its defining property?
text:
The double-negation topology $
eg
eg\colon \Omega 	o \Omega$ on a topos $\mathcal{E}$ produces a subtopos $\mathrm{Sh}_{
eg
eg}(\mathcal{E}) \hookrightarrow \mathcal{E}$. What is its defining property? $\mathrm{Sh}_{
eg
eg}(\mathcal{E})$ is the largest Boolean subtopos of $\mathcal{E}$ (Mac Lane–Moerdijk V.2 / VI.9). When $\mathcal{E}$ is the topos of $ eta$-names for a forcing condition, $\mathrm{Sh}_{
eg
eg}$ is the model of ZFC obtained by Cohen forcing — the topos-theoretic shadow of forcing. Double-negation 'rounds down' all subobjects to their $
eg
eg$-stable parts, exactly the ones that obey LEM.

q: Which subobjects of $\mathbb{R}$ in $\mathrm{Sh}(\mathbb{R})$ are $\neg\neg$-stable (i.e. equal to their double-negation)?
text:
Which subobjects of $\mathbb{R}$ in $\mathrm{Sh}(\mathbb{R})$ are $
eg
eg$-stable (i.e. equal to their double-negation)? $
eg
eg U = \mathrm{int}(\overline{U})$ in the open-set lattice. So $U = 
eg
eg U$ iff $U = \mathrm{int}(\overline{U})$, the definition of a regular open set. The regular opens form a complete Boolean algebra, the Booleanisation of the Heyting algebra of opens. $
eg U = \mathrm{int}(U^c)$, so $
eg
eg U = \mathrm{int}(\mathrm{int}(U^c)^c) = \mathrm{int}(\overline{U})$.

q: The classical theorem 'every set has a cardinality' (every set is in bijection with an initial ordinal) requires the axiom of choice. In a topos $\mathcal{E}$, what is the relationship between AC and LEM?
text: The classical theorem 'every set has a cardinality' (every set is in bijection with an initial ordinal) requires the axiom of choice. In a topos $\mathcal{E}$, what is the relationship between AC and LEM? Diaconescu's theorem (1975): if every epi splits internally in a topos $\mathcal{E}$, then $\mathcal{E}$ is Boolean. Constructive proof: from a 2-element quotient $\{0,1\}/{\sim}$ where $\sim$ depends on a proposition $\varphi$, internal AC gives a section that decides $\varphi$. The converse (LEM ⇒ AC) is false: $G$-$\mathbf{Set}$ is Boolean but AC fails. There's a famous theorem of Diaconescu connecting choice and excluded middle in any topos.

q: Which of the following logical connectives are always preserved by the inverse image $f^*$ of a geometric morphism $f\colon \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{E}$?
text: Which of the following logical connectives are always preserved by the inverse image $f^*$ of a geometric morphism $f\colon \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{E}$? Geometric logic is the fragment $\{\top, \bot, \wedge, \bigvee, \exists, =\}$. $f^*$ preserves finite limits ($\wedge$, $=$) and arbitrary colimits ($\bigvee$, $\exists$ as image). $\Rightarrow$, $\forall$, $\neg$ are right-adjoint constructions; left adjoints don't commute with right adjoints in general. Geometric formulas use $\wedge, \bigvee, \exists, =$ — and only those — because $f^*$ preserves finite limits and arbitrary colimits.

q: Why does $f^*$ preserve $\bigvee$ but not $\bigwedge$ (infinitary conjunction)?
text: Why does $f^*$ preserve $\bigvee$ but not $\bigwedge$ (infinitary conjunction)? $f^* \dashv f_*$, so $f^*$ is a left adjoint and preserves all colimits — including infinitary unions $\bigvee$. It only preserves finite limits, not arbitrary ones; $\bigwedge$ over an infinite index is an infinite limit, not preserved. Left adjoints commute with colimits; $f^*$ is the left adjoint of the geometric morphism.

q: If $\mathcal{T}$ is a geometric theory and $\varphi \vdash_x \psi$ is a geometric sequent provable in the classifying topos $\mathrm{Set}[\mathcal{T}]$, then for any topos $\mathcal{F}$ with a $\mathcal{T}$-model $M$:
text: If $\mathcal{T}$ is a geometric theory and $\varphi \vdash_x \psi$ is a geometric sequent provable in the classifying topos $\mathrm{Set}[\mathcal{T}]$, then for any topos $\mathcal{F}$ with a $\mathcal{T}$-model $M$: This is geometric soundness: a $\mathcal{T}$-model in $\mathcal{F}$ corresponds to a geometric morphism $\mathcal{F} \to \mathrm{Set}[\mathcal{T}]$; $f^*$ preserves geometric formulas, so anything provable geometrically in the classifying topos transports along $f^*$ to $\mathcal{F}$. This is the central technical reason for restricting to geometric logic. The whole point of the geometric fragment: it transports along arbitrary geometric morphisms, including those classifying models.

title: Sieves
blurb: A sieve on $c$ is a downward-closed family of arrows into $c$ — equivalently a subfunctor of the representable presheaf $h_c$. Sieves are the building blocks of Grothendieck topologies and play the role that open covers do for ordinary topology.

title: Grothendieck topology axioms
blurb: A Grothendieck topology on $C$ assigns to each object $c$ a collection of "covering" sieves, satisfying maximality, stability under pullback, and a transitivity axiom. The data $(C, J)$ is called a site; sheaves on a site generalize sheaves on a topological space.

title: Examples: small/large Zariski, étale, fpqc
blurb: The small Zariski site of a scheme has open immersions for covers; the étale site loosens to surjective étale maps; the fpqc site is even coarser. Each gives a different sheaf theory — Zariski-locally trivial $\ne$ étale-locally trivial, the difference detecting Galois descent.

title: Sheaves and sheafification
blurb: A sheaf on $(C, J)$ is a presheaf $F$ such that for every covering sieve $S$ of $c$, $F(c)$ is the limit of $F$ over $S$. The sheafification functor is the left adjoint of the inclusion of sheaves into presheaves; it averages a presheaf over all covering sieves.

title: $\mathrm{Sh}(C, J)$ as a topos
blurb: The category $\mathrm{Sh}(C, J)$ of sheaves on a site is a Grothendieck topos — a particular kind of elementary topos that has all small colimits and a small generating set. Every Grothendieck topos arises this way (Giraud's theorem).

title: Geometric morphisms from morphisms of sites
blurb: A continuous map of sites $f\colon (D, K) \to (C, J)$ induces a geometric morphism $\mathrm{Sh}(D, K) \to \mathrm{Sh}(C, J)$. This is how site theory provides examples of geometric morphisms: pullback along $f$ on the level of sites pulls sheaves back.

q: Let $C$ be the 3-object poset $\{x, y, t\}$ with $x \leq t$ and $y \leq t$ (no relation between $x$ and $y$). Which of the following families of arrows form a sieve on $t$?
text: Let $C$ be the 3-object poset $\{x, y, t\}$ with $x \leq t$ and $y \leq t$ (no relation between $x$ and $y$). Which of the following families of arrows form a sieve on $t$? Every arrow into $t$ has source $x$, $y$, or $t$. Since the only arrows out of $x$, $y$, $t$ that land in $\{x, y, t\}$ via composition are themselves identities, every set listed is precomposition-closed. In a poset, sieves on $c$ are exactly down-sets of objects $\leq c$, and all four sets correspond to legitimate down-sets. A sieve is closed under precomposition; in a poset there are no nontrivial composable predecessors to worry about.

q: How many sieves are there on the top object $t$ of the same 3-object poset $\{x, y, t\}$ with $x \leq t$ and $y \leq t$?
text: How many sieves are there on the top object $t$ of the same 3-object poset $\{x, y, t\}$ with $x \leq t$ and $y \leq t$? Sieves on $t$ correspond bijectively to down-sets of $\{d : d \leq t\} = \{x, y, t\}$. With no relations between $x$ and $y$, every subset is automatically a down-set: $\varnothing$, $\{x\}$, $\{y\}$, $\{x, y\}$ — but a sieve on $t$ must contain $\mathrm{id}_t$ to make sense as a subfunctor of $h_t$ at $d = t$? Careful: actually every subset is a sieve, and the empty set is allowed too. The four sieves are $\varnothing,\ \{x \to t\},\ \{y \to t\},\ \{x \to t, y \to t, \mathrm{id}_t\}$ if we close under precomposition — but in a poset closure is trivial. Counting all subsets of the down-set $\{x, y\}$ of strict predecessors of $t$ together with the choice of including $\mathrm{id}_t$, gives $4$ when we identify a sieve with its set of strict $\leq t$ predecessors. In a poset, a sieve on $c$ is a down-set of $\{d \leq c\}$; count down-sets.

q: Which equivalent description of a sieve on $c$ best generalises to arbitrary categories?
text: Which equivalent description of a sieve on $c$ best generalises to arbitrary categories? A sieve is precisely a precomposition-closed family of arrows into $c$, equivalently a subfunctor of the representable presheaf $h_c$. The other options either presuppose extra structure (a topology, a ring) or are nonsensical (equivalence classes don't compose). What does the original definition say about composing on the right of an arrow already in the sieve?

q: Suppose $J$ assigns to every object $c$ a family $J(c)$ of sieves, satisfying maximality and stability under pullback, but transitivity FAILS. Which of the following can go wrong?
text: Suppose $J$ assigns to every object $c$ a family $J(c)$ of sieves, satisfying maximality and stability under pullback, but transitivity FAILS. Which of the following can go wrong? Transitivity is precisely the local-to-global axiom that makes sheafification idempotent (so the plus-construction stabilises after two iterations). Without it, the would-be sheafification functor is not even a left adjoint to the inclusion of sheaves. Maximality and pullback-stability are independent axioms unaffected by failure of transitivity. Transitivity = 'local cover of a local cover is a cover.' Which formal property does that underwrite?

q: Is the trivial topology $J_{\mathrm{triv}}(c) = \{\mathrm{max}_c\}$ — only the maximal sieve covers — a Grothendieck topology?
text: Is the trivial topology $J_{\mathrm{triv}}(c) = \{\mathrm{max}_c\}$ — only the maximal sieve covers — a Grothendieck topology? M is given. For S: $f^*\mathrm{max}_c = \mathrm{max}_d$ since every $g\colon e \to d$ composes with $f$ to land in $\mathrm{max}_c$ — every arrow into $c$ is in the maximal sieve. For T: if $f^*T \in J_{\mathrm{triv}}(d)$ for every $f \in \mathrm{max}_c$, then in particular $\mathrm{id}_c^* T = T \in J_{\mathrm{triv}}(c)$, so $T = \mathrm{max}_c$. The presheaf topos $\widehat{C}$ is the sheaf topos for the trivial topology. Sheaves on the trivial topology are presheaves — which is itself a topos. So this had better be a valid Grothendieck topology.

q: Which of the following are equivalent ways of specifying a Grothendieck topology on $C$?
text: Which of the following are equivalent ways of specifying a Grothendieck topology on $C$? Grothendieck topologies on $C$, Lawvere–Tierney topologies on $\widehat{C}$, and left-exact-reflective (geometric) subcategories of $\widehat{C}$ are all in bijection — this is the unification at the heart of the elementary/Grothendieck topos correspondence. A functor to $\mathsf{Top}$ is unrelated. Three of the four phrase the same data — sheafification adjunction, internal classifier, axiomatic — and the fourth is a red herring.

q: On the category $C$ of open subsets of a topological space $X$, define $J(U)$ to be the collection of sieves $S$ such that $\bigcup_{V \to U \in S} V = U$ (set-theoretic equality of point-sets). Is this a Grothendieck topology?
text: On the category $C$ of open subsets of a topological space $X$, define $J(U)$ to be the collection of sieves $S$ such that $\bigcup_{V \to U \in S} V = U$ (set-theoretic equality of point-sets). Is this a Grothendieck topology? This is the standard Zariski-style topology on $\mathrm{Open}(X)$. M: the maximal sieve $\mathrm{max}_U = \{V \hookrightarrow U\}$ certainly covers $U$ as a union. S: pulling back along $V \to U$ takes a cover of $U$ to its restriction to $V$, still a cover. T: if $S$ covers $U$ and each piece $V$ of $S$ is covered by sieves $T_V$, the union of all $T_V$'s pieces still covers $U$. So all three axioms hold, and the resulting sheaves are exactly the classical sheaves on the topological space. This is just the textbook definition of an open cover.

q: On the category $C = \{*\}$ with one object and one identity arrow, how many Grothendieck topologies are there?
text: On the category $C = \{*\}$ with one object and one identity arrow, how many Grothendieck topologies are there? Sieves on $*$ are subsets of $\{\mathrm{id}\}$: just $\varnothing$ and $\{\mathrm{id}\} = \mathrm{max}_*$. M forces $\mathrm{max}_*$ to be covering. The only choice is whether $\varnothing$ is also covering. If yes (the discrete topology), the only sheaf is the terminal one — sheaves are equivalent to $\{*\}$. If no (the trivial topology), sheaves on $*$ are presheaves on $*$, equivalent to $\mathsf{Set}$. Both satisfy S and T trivially. Count subsets of $\mathrm{max}_*$ that include $\mathrm{max}_*$ — and check axioms hold for each.

q: On $\Spec \mathbb{F}_2$, which of the following structures are Zariski-locally trivial? Which are étale-locally trivial?
text: On $\Spec \mathbb{F}_2$, which of the following structures are Zariski-locally trivial? Which are étale-locally trivial? Over $\mathbb{F}_2$, the polynomial $T^2 - 1 = (T-1)^2$ has only the root $1$, so $\mu_2 \cong \Spec \mathbb{F}_2[T]/(T-1)^2 \cong \mathbb{F}_2[\epsilon]$ — but as a group scheme it's also isomorphic to the constant scheme $\underline{\mathbb{Z}/2}$ Zariski-locally because over $\mathbb{F}_2$ the equation $T^2 = 1$ has a Zariski-trivial set of roots (just the constant $1$). So actually $\mu_2 \cong \underline{\mathbb{Z}/2}$ even Zariski-locally over $\mathbb{F}_2$! Likewise line bundles are always Zariski-locally trivial by definition. Over a field of characteristic $\neq 2$ where $\mu_2 \neq \underline{\mathbb{Z}/2}$, only étale-locally do they coincide. A line bundle is Zariski-locally trivial by the very definition of locally free sheaf of rank 1; whether $\mu_n$ is Zariski-locally trivial depends on whether $n$th roots of $1$ already live in the base ring.

q: Which of the following is a covering family on $\Spec \mathbb{Q}$ in the étale site that is NOT a Zariski cover?
text: Which of the following is a covering family on $\Spec \mathbb{Q}$ in the étale site that is NOT a Zariski cover? A finite separable field extension is a finite étale cover, and $\{\Spec L \to \Spec K\}$ for $L/K$ finite étale is jointly surjective. But it is NOT a Zariski cover — Zariski opens of $\Spec K$ for a field are just $\Spec K$ and $\varnothing$. The other options are either Zariski covers (the identity, $D(2) = \Spec \mathbb{Q}$ since $2$ is a unit in $\mathbb{Q}$) or not even surjective. Zariski opens of a field are very restricted — what's the spectrum of a field as a topological space?

q: Which is the most refined (loosest) of the standard sites on a scheme $X$?
text: Which is the most refined (loosest) of the standard sites on a scheme $X$? The chain Zariski $\subset$ Nisnevich $\subset$ étale $\subset$ smooth $\subset$ fppf $\subset$ fpqc orders sites by how loose the cover condition is. fpqc covers include arbitrary faithfully flat quasi-compact maps with no smoothness or finite-type assumption. Which condition imposes the least on the cover morphisms?
