q: An $\infty$-category $\mathcal{X}$ is presentable, has disjoint pullback-stable coproducts, has effective groupoid objects, but FAILS local cartesian closure (G4). Such an $\mathcal{X}$ is called a what?
text: An $\infty$-category $\mathcal{X}$ is presentable, has disjoint pullback-stable coproducts, has effective groupoid objects, but FAILS local cartesian closure (G4). Such an $\mathcal{X}$ is called a what? Without G4, $\mathcal{X}$ is what one might call an $(\infty,1)$-pretopos: it has presentability, disjoint coproducts, and quotient-by-groupoid effectivity, but lacks the local cartesian closure that $\infty$-topoi require for power objects / dependent products. (The terminology is somewhat unsettled; some sources use 'lex' instead of 'pretopos'. The point: G4 is the genuinely strongest of the four axioms, and dropping it gives a strictly weaker class.) Pretoposes in the 1-categorical sense lack power objects / subobject classifier in general.

q: A geometric morphism $f\colon \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{E}$ of $\infty$-topoi consists of an adjoint pair $f^* \dashv f_*$. Which functor is the LEFT adjoint, and which extra property does it satisfy?
text: A geometric morphism $f\colon \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{E}$ of $\infty$-topoi consists of an adjoint pair $f^* \dashv f_*$. Which functor is the LEFT adjoint, and which extra property does it satisfy? Convention: $f^*$ is the LEFT adjoint (the 'inverse image'), and the topos axiom is that $f^*$ is left exact (preserves finite limits). $f_*$ (the RIGHT adjoint, the 'direct image') automatically preserves limits as a right adjoint — that's not a separate axiom. Note the geometric direction: $f$ goes $\mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{E}$ but $f^*$ goes $\mathcal{E} \to \mathcal{F}$ — the geometric morphism 'points the way of $f_*$'. Pullback of a sheaf along $f$ goes against the arrow direction.

q: For an étale geometric morphism $f\colon \mathcal{E}_{/X} \to \mathcal{E}$ — i.e. the slice projection over an object $X \in \mathcal{E}$ — what is $f^*$ on the terminal object $* \in \mathcal{E}$?
text: For an étale geometric morphism $f\colon \mathcal{E}_{/X} \to \mathcal{E}$ — i.e. the slice projection over an object $X \in \mathcal{E}$ — what is $f^*$ on the terminal object $* \in \mathcal{E}$? $f^*$ is left exact, so it preserves the terminal object. The terminal object of $\mathcal{E}_{/X}$ is $\mathrm{id}_X\colon X \to X$ (the unique map to itself). For $f^* = X \times -$ on $\mathcal{E}_{/X} \to \mathcal{E}_{/X}$ this would be $X \times *$, but for the $f^*\colon \mathcal{E} \to \mathcal{E}_{/X}$ direction, $f^*(E) = (E \times X \to X)$, sending $* \mapsto (X \to X)$. ✓ The terminal of a slice $\mathcal{E}_{/X}$ is always the identity-on-$X$ object.

q: Which of the following are TRUE about geometric morphisms of $\infty$-topoi?
text: Which of the following are TRUE about geometric morphisms of $\infty$-topoi? All four are correct: (1) is the standard definition of surjective; (2) is the fact that étale morphisms are equivalent to objects (HTT 6.3.5); (3) is the convention for 2-cells in $\infty\mathsf{Topoi}$; (4) is the corepresentability statement — by accessibility, a lex-accessible functor has a right adjoint (adjoint functor theorem) and so produces a geometric morphism. All four are standard facts in HTT chapter 6.

q: An $\infty$-topos $\mathcal{X}$ is called hypercomplete iff which condition holds?
text: An $\infty$-topos $\mathcal{X}$ is called hypercomplete iff which condition holds? Hypercompleteness IS the internal Whitehead theorem: $\infty$-connective $\Rightarrow$ equivalence. (3) and (4) are automatic for $\infty$-topoi anyway (G1). (1) is far too strong (limits and colimits don't commute in general). The defining feature of hypercompletion is that it forces 'all homotopy sheaves agree' to imply 'objects are equivalent'. Whitehead's classical theorem: $\pi_n$-iso for all $n$ ⇒ homotopy equivalence.

q: Hypercompletion is needed (i.e. $\mathcal{X} \neq \mathcal{X}^\wedge$) precisely when which property of the base fails?
text: Hypercompletion is needed (i.e. $\mathcal{X} \neq \mathcal{X}^\wedge$) precisely when which property of the base fails? The standard pathology: bases of infinite cohomological / Krull dimension produce $\infty$-connective morphisms that are not equivalences. Concretely, $\mathrm{Sh}_\infty(\Spec(\prod_n k_n))$ for an infinite product of fields fails Whitehead. Bases of finite covering dimension (smooth manifolds, Noetherian schemes of finite Krull dimension) automatically have hypercomplete $\infty$-toposes. Hypercompletion's failure is about Postnikov-tower divergence — what makes Postnikov towers fail to converge?

q: Internal Whitehead (the hypercompletion-defining property) says: a map $f\colon X \to Y$ in $\mathcal{X}$ is an equivalence iff $\pi_n(f)$ is iso for all $n \geq 0$. Which is the formal correct statement?
text: Internal Whitehead (the hypercompletion-defining property) says: a map $f\colon X \to Y$ in $\mathcal{X}$ is an equivalence iff $\pi_n(f)$ is iso for all $n \geq 0$. Which is the formal correct statement? The homotopy SHEAF $\pi_n(X)$ is the sheafification of the presheaf $U \mapsto \pi_n(\Gamma(U; X))$ — a 0-truncated object of $\mathcal{X}$. (1) is wrong: cohomology rings classify only certain features. (3) is too coarse: global-section iso doesn't suffice (a sheaf need not be detected globally). (4) is far too coarse: $0$-truncation kills all higher homotopy. Whitehead's theorem is about each $\pi_n$ pointwise, made internal by sheafifying.

q: Let $\mathcal{X}$ be an $\infty$-topos with hypercompletion $\mathcal{X}^\wedge \hookrightarrow \mathcal{X}$. The hypercompletion is a left-exact reflective subcategory. The localization functor inverts which class of morphisms?
text: Let $\mathcal{X}$ be an $\infty$-topos with hypercompletion $\mathcal{X}^\wedge \hookrightarrow \mathcal{X}$. The hypercompletion is a left-exact reflective subcategory. The localization functor inverts which class of morphisms? By construction $\mathcal{X}^\wedge$ is the localization of $\mathcal{X}$ at $\infty$-connective morphisms — that's exactly what 'hypercompletion = forces internal Whitehead' means. The $\infty$-connective maps were already preserved-iso by lex-localizations of presheaves; hypercompletion forces them to become equivalences in the target. What does 'forces internal Whitehead' mean as a localization?

q: Find the flawed step in this 'proof' that $\mathrm{Sh}_\infty(X)$ is always hypercomplete.
text: Find the flawed step in this 'proof' that $\mathrm{Sh}_\infty(X)$ is always hypercomplete. Step 5 is wrong: pointwise Postnikov-tower convergence (step 4) does NOT imply convergence in $\mathrm{Sh}_\infty(X)$. The issue is that $\lim$ in $\mathrm{Sh}_\infty(X)$ is computed via sheafification — it differs from the pointwise limit. Concretely: the sheafification of a pointwise-converging Postnikov tower might disagree with the pointwise limit on some non-Noetherian / infinite-dim subspace, producing an $\infty$-connective non-equivalence. This is exactly what causes hypercompletion failures for $X$ of unbounded covering dimension. Limits in a sheaf category are not pointwise limits — sheafification matters.

q: In a 1-topos $\mathcal{E}$, the subobject classifier $\Omega$ classifies subobjects (monomorphisms). In an $\infty$-topos, what does the OBJECT CLASSIFIER $\mathcal{U}_\kappa$ classify?
text: In a 1-topos $\mathcal{E}$, the subobject classifier $\Omega$ classifies subobjects (monomorphisms). In an $\infty$-topos, what does the OBJECT CLASSIFIER $\mathcal{U}_\kappa$ classify? The object classifier $\mathcal{U}_\kappa$ classifies $\kappa$-small maps (HTT 6.1.6.7). The 1-categorical $\Omega$ is recovered as $\mathcal{U}_{\leq -1}$, the part classifying $(-1)$-truncated maps (= monomorphisms). The full $\mathcal{U}$ has a tower $\mathcal{U}_{\leq -1} \subset \mathcal{U}_{\leq 0} \subset \mathcal{U}_{\leq 1} \subset \cdots$ classifying $n$-truncated maps for each $n$. Without size constraint $\mathcal{U}$ is class-sized, hence the $\kappa$. 1-topos $\Omega$ extends UPWARD to a tower of universes in an $\infty$-topos.

q: Voevodsky's univalence axiom — automatic in any Grothendieck $\infty$-topos with universe $\mathcal{U}$ — states what equivalence?
text: Voevodsky's univalence axiom — automatic in any Grothendieck $\infty$-topos with universe $\mathcal{U}$ — states what equivalence? Univalence: $(X =_\mathcal{U} Y) \simeq (X \simeq Y)$ — identity in the universe is exactly equivalence of types. This is automatic in any Grothendieck $\infty$-topos because the universe is constructed AS a moduli object: paths in the moduli space of objects ARE equivalences of objects, by definition. Voevodsky's contribution was to recognize this as the missing axiom for HoTT. What is a path in the moduli space of objects?

q: Which of the following correspond correctly under the HoTT $\leftrightarrow$ $\infty$-topos dictionary?
text: Which of the following correspond correctly under the HoTT $\leftrightarrow$ $\infty$-topos dictionary? All four are standard: $\Pi$ uses the right adjoint $f_*$ (existence requires G4 / local cartesian closure); $\Sigma$ uses the left adjoint $f_!$; identity types are path / mapping spaces (in the 'type' interpretation); HITs are colimits of generalized cell complexes. The dictionary is exact (modulo coherence subtleties addressed by Shulman, Cisinski, et al.). All correct — these are the four fundamental correspondences.

q: In a 1-topos vs $\infty$-topos, where does the subobject classifier $\Omega$ sit in the universe tower?
text: In a 1-topos vs $\infty$-topos, where does the subobject classifier $\Omega$ sit in the universe tower? $\Omega = \mathcal{U}_{\leq -1}$: it classifies $(-1)$-truncated maps (= subobjects) in any $\infty$-topos. The subobject classifier of a 1-topos persists into the $\infty$-topos, but is now just the bottom of a tower $\mathcal{U}_{\leq -1} \subset \mathcal{U}_{\leq 0} \subset \cdots \subset \mathcal{U}$. The full universe $\mathcal{U}$ is genuinely new in the $\infty$-world. $(-1)$-truncated map = monomorphism = proposition.

q: Shulman's coherence theorem (2019, 'All $(\infty,1)$-toposes have strict univalent universes') has what consequence for HoTT-as-internal-language?
text: Shulman's coherence theorem (2019, 'All $(\infty,1)$-toposes have strict univalent universes') has what consequence for HoTT-as-internal-language? Shulman's result says that every Grothendieck $\infty$-topos admits a strict (split) model of HoTT with univalent universes — earlier results gave only homotopy-coherent models, leaving residual coherence concerns. Consequence: HoTT is the synthetic language for $\infty$-topos theory, and synthetic theorems (e.g. $\pi_4(S^3) = \mathbb{Z}/2$ via Brunerie's number) translate to theorems valid in every $\infty$-topos simultaneously. What problem did the coherence theorem resolve?

q: What is the étale $\infty$-topos of $\Spec\,\mathbb{Z}$ — describe its 'shape'?
text: What is the étale $\infty$-topos of $\Spec\,\mathbb{Z}$ — describe its 'shape'? The étale $\infty$-topos of $\Spec\,\mathbb{Z}$ packages: a generic point $\eta = (0)$ with local data $\bar{\mathbb{Q}}$ and Galois group $G_\mathbb{Q}$; a closed point for each prime $p$ with local data $\bar{\mathbb{F}}_p$ and Galois group $G_{\mathbb{F}_p} = \widehat{\mathbb{Z}}$. (1) is wrong: only $\Spec\,\bar{k}$ for $\bar k$ algebraically closed has étale $\infty$-topos $\simeq \mathcal{S}$. (4) is wrong: the $\infty$-version is genuinely larger and contains the 1-version as the 0-truncation. Each prime gives a closed point; the generic point is at $(0)$.

q: Hypercompletion plays what role in derived étale cohomology / $\ell$-adic sheaves?
text: Hypercompletion plays what role in derived étale cohomology / $\ell$-adic sheaves? $\ell$-adic sheaves are inverse-limit objects: $\mathbb{Z}_\ell$-coefficients are the inverse limit of $\mathbb{Z}/\ell^n$-coefficients. For these inverse limits to behave correctly in the $\infty$-topos — equivalently, for the Postnikov / cohomological-degree tower to converge — you need hypercompleteness. The pro-étale $\infty$-topos of Bhatt–Scholze is defined to be hypercomplete by design. $\ell$-adic = inverse limit over $n$ — what does that require for $\infty$-sheaves?

q: Which of the following are TRUE about the étale $\infty$-topos $\mathrm{Sh}_\infty(X_\mathrm{ét})^\wedge$ of a Noetherian scheme $X$ of finite Krull dimension?
text: Which of the following are TRUE about the étale $\infty$-topos $\mathrm{Sh}_\infty(X_\mathrm{ét})^\wedge$ of a Noetherian scheme $X$ of finite Krull dimension? All four hold. (1) Finite Krull dimension is enough for hypercompletion to be automatic / harmless. (2) Truncation always recovers the 1-topos. (3) Derived étale theory naturally lives here. (4) Lurie's reconstruction (HTT 7.3 + Galois) recovers $X$ from $\mathrm{Sh}_\infty(X_\mathrm{ét})^\wedge$ up to Galois twist — étale homotopy type in modern language. All four are core facts about the étale $\infty$-topos.

title: Real numbers
blurb: The complete ordered field: least-upper-bound property, sequences and Cauchy completeness, and why Q isn't enough.

title: Continuity on R
blurb: ε–δ continuity, IVT, EVT, and the interaction between continuity and compactness on intervals.

title: Differentiation
blurb: The derivative as best linear approximation, chain rule, mean value theorem, and Taylor's theorem with remainder.

title: Uniform convergence
blurb: Uniform vs pointwise convergence of function sequences, Weierstrass M-test, and why uniform limits preserve continuity and integrability.

title: Riemann integral
blurb: Upper/lower sums, the fundamental theorem of calculus, and the failure modes that motivate measure theory.

title: Multivariable differentiation
blurb: Partial derivatives, total derivative as Jacobian, chain rule for vector-valued maps, gradients and directional derivatives.

title: Multivariable integration
blurb: Iterated integrals, Fubini's theorem, Jacobian determinant and the change-of-variables formula on R^n.

title: Inverse & implicit function theorems
blurb: Local invertibility from invertible Jacobian; solving F(x,y)=0 for y locally when ∂F/∂y is invertible — the engine behind manifolds and submanifolds.

title: Numeric series & convergence tests
blurb: Absolute vs conditional convergence; ratio, root, comparison, and integral tests; Abel and Dirichlet criteria for conditional sums.

title: Power series & radius of convergence
blurb: Cauchy–Hadamard 1/R = limsup |a_n|^{1/n}; uniform convergence on compact subsets of the disk; term-by-term differentiation and the bridge to real-analytic functions.

title: Metric completeness & Baire category
blurb: Cauchy-complete metric spaces, the unique completion, and Baire's theorem: a complete metric space is not a countable union of nowhere-dense sets.

title: Fundamental theorem of calculus (both parts)
blurb: Part I: F(x)=∫_a^x f gives F'=f when f is continuous. Part II: ∫_a^b F'=F(b)-F(a) for F differentiable with F' integrable; absolute continuity as the right class for Lebesgue.

title: Bump functions & mollification
blurb: The model bump exp(-1/(1-x^2)) is C^∞ with compact support; convolution with mollifiers smooths any locally integrable function and yields smooth partitions of unity.

title: C[a,b] & the Arzelà–Ascoli theorem
blurb: C[a,b] with the uniform norm is Banach; a family has uniformly convergent subsequences iff it is pointwise bounded and equicontinuous (Arzelà–Ascoli).

q: Which property distinguishes $\mathbb{R}$ from $\mathbb{Q}$?
text: Which property distinguishes $\mathbb{R}$ from $\mathbb{Q}$? $\mathbb{Q}$ is an ordered, Archimedean field, and closed under addition. The distinguishing property is the least-upper-bound (Dedekind) completeness: the set $\{q\in\mathbb{Q}:q^2<2\}$ is bounded above but has no supremum in $\mathbb{Q}$.

q: Let $S = \{1 - 1/n : n\in\mathbb{N}_{\ge 1}\}\subset\mathbb{R}$. What is $\sup S$?
text: Let $S = \{1 - 1/n : n\in\mathbb{N}_{\ge 1}\}\subset\mathbb{R}$. What is $\sup S$? $S$ is bounded above by $1$ and its elements approach $1$. Since $1$ is an upper bound and no smaller number is, $\sup S = 1$. Note $1\notin S$ — the supremum need not be attained.

q: Which statement is equivalent to $\mathbb{R}$ being Cauchy-complete?
text: Which statement is equivalent to $\mathbb{R}$ being Cauchy-complete? Cauchy completeness is exactly the statement that every Cauchy sequence has a limit in the space. Bounded sequences need not converge ($(-1)^n$); monotone-bounded sequences do, and bounded sequences have convergent subsequences (Bolzano–Weierstrass) — but those are consequences, not definitions.

q: Which is the correct order of steps to prove that every Cauchy sequence in $\mathbb{R}$ converges?
text: Which is the correct order of steps to prove that every Cauchy sequence in $\mathbb{R}$ converges? Proof chain: (1) Every Cauchy sequence is bounded. (2) Bolzano–Weierstrass extracts a convergent subsequence $a_{n_k}\to L$. (3) For any $\varepsilon>0$ choose $N$ so $|a_m-a_n| N$ and $k$ with $n_k>N$ and $|a_{n_k}-L|<\varepsilon/2$; then $|a_n-L|\le|a_n-a_{n_k}|+|a_{n_k}-L|<\varepsilon$.

q: Let $A = \{x\in\mathbb{Q} : x^2 < 3\}$. In $\mathbb{R}$, $\sup A = \sqrt{3}$. How many rational numbers satisfy $x^2 = 3$?
text: Let $A = \{x\in\mathbb{Q} : x^2 < 3\}$. In $\mathbb{R}$, $\sup A = \sqrt{3}$. How many rational numbers satisfy $x^2 = 3$? $\sqrt{3}$ is irrational (its square is $3$, but $3$ is not a perfect square, and a standard argument shows $\sqrt{3}\notin\mathbb{Q}$). So there are $0$ rational solutions to $x^2=3$, illustrating why $\sup A$ is not attained in $\mathbb{Q}$.

q: If $f\colon[0,1]\to\mathbb{R}$ is continuous with $f(0)=-1$ and $f(1)=2$, which theorem guarantees a root of $f$?
text: If $f\colon[0,1]\to\mathbb{R}$ is continuous with $f(0)=-1$ and $f(1)=2$, which theorem guarantees a root of $f$? IVT: a continuous function on $[a,b]$ takes every value between $f(a)$ and $f(b)$. Since $0$ lies between $-1$ and $2$, some $c\in[0,1]$ has $f(c)=0$.

q: For $f(x)=2x+3$ and $a=1$, with $\varepsilon=0.01$, what is the largest $\delta$ that works in the $\varepsilon$–$\delta$ definition of continuity?
text: For $f(x)=2x+3$ and $a=1$, with $\varepsilon=0.01$, what is the largest $\delta$ that works in the $\varepsilon$–$\delta$ definition of continuity? $|f(x)-f(1)| = |2x+3 - 5| = 2|x-1|$. We need $2|x-1| < 0.01$, i.e. $|x-1| < 0.005$. So $\delta = 0.005$.

q: Which of these is NOT guaranteed for a continuous $f\colon[0,1]\to\mathbb{R}$?
text: Which of these is NOT guaranteed for a continuous $f\colon[0,1]\to\mathbb{R}$? Continuity on a closed bounded interval gives EVT (attains max/min), uniform continuity (via compactness), and that the image is compact and connected (so a closed bounded interval). Differentiability is a strictly stronger condition — e.g., $f(x)=|x-1/2|$ is continuous on $[0,1]$ but not differentiable at $1/2$.

q: Arrange the steps of the proof that every Cauchy sequence in $\mathbb{R}$ converges.
text: Arrange the steps of the proof that every Cauchy sequence in $\mathbb{R}$ converges. Boundedness gives B–W a compact interval to work in; B–W produces a convergent subsequence; the Cauchy condition then promotes subsequential convergence to convergence of the whole sequence. Each step uses the previous one's conclusion, so the order is forced.

q: Let $f(x)=x^2\sin(1/x)$ for $x\ne 0$ and $f(0)=0$. Using the $\varepsilon$–$\delta$ definition with $\varepsilon=10^{-4}$, what is the largest $\delta$ guaranteeing $|f(x)-f(0)|<\varepsilon$ for $|x|<\delta$? (Hint: $|f(x)|\le x^2$.)
text: Let $f(x)=x^2\sin(1/x)$ for $x\ne 0$ and $f(0)=0$. Using the $\varepsilon$–$\delta$ definition with $\varepsilon=10^{-4}$, what is the largest $\delta$ guaranteeing $|f(x)-f(0)|<\varepsilon$ for $|x|<\delta$? (Hint: $|f(x)|\le x^2$.) $|f(x)-0|=|x^2\sin(1/x)|\le x^2 < \varepsilon$ whenever $|x|<\sqrt{\varepsilon}=\sqrt{10^{-4}}=0.01$. So $\delta=0.01$.

q: You are proving the intermediate value theorem: if $f\colon[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ is continuous with $f(a)<0<f(b)$, then some $c\in(a,b)$ has $f(c)=0$. The steps so far use the nested-interval bisection idea.
text: You are proving the intermediate value theorem: if $f\colon[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ is continuous with $f(a)<0<f(b)$, then some $c\in(a,b)$ has $f(c)=0$. The steps so far use the nested-interval bisection idea. The clean continuation uses continuity at $c$ to rule out $f(c) 0$ (points just before $c$ would be in $S$ with $f\ge 0$, same contradiction). So $f(c)=0$. The sup must be a root because each side of $f(c)\ne 0$ pushes $c$ off the supremum.

q: If $f(x)=x^3$ on $[0,3]$, find $c\in(0,3)$ given by the mean value theorem: $f'(c) = (f(3)-f(0))/3$.
text: If $f(x)=x^3$ on $[0,3]$, find $c\in(0,3)$ given by the mean value theorem: $f'(c) = (f(3)-f(0))/3$. Average slope $= (27-0)/3 = 9$. Set $3c^2 = 9$, so $c = \sqrt 3 \approx 1.7320508$.

q: The derivative $f'(a)$ is best interpreted as:
text: The derivative $f'(a)$ is best interpreted as: The derivative is the best-linear-approximation characterization: $f'(a)$ is the (scalar) coefficient of the unique linear $L\colon\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$ for which the remainder is little-o of $|h|$. This is the formulation that generalizes to the Jacobian in several variables. The derivative is the best-linear-approximation characterization: $f'(a)$ is the (scalar) coefficient of the unique linear $L\colon\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$ for which the remainder is little-o of…

q: The 2nd-degree Taylor polynomial of $f(x)=\cos x$ at $a=0$ is $1 - x^2/2$. What is the Lagrange remainder bound for $|x|\le 0.1$? (Use $\max|f'''(\xi)|\le 1$.)
text: The 2nd-degree Taylor polynomial of $f(x)=\cos x$ at $a=0$ is $1 - x^2/2$. What is the Lagrange remainder bound for $|x|\le 0.1$? (Use $\max|f'''(\xi)|\le 1$.) Lagrange: $|R_2(x)| \le \max|f^{(3)}(\xi)| \cdot |x|^3/3! \le 1 \cdot (0.1)^3/6 = 0.001/6 \approx 0.000166\overline{6}$.

q: Which is the correct proof-step order for the Mean Value Theorem applied to $f\colon[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ differentiable on $(a,b)$?
text: Which is the correct proof-step order for the Mean Value Theorem applied to $f\colon[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ differentiable on $(a,b)$? The standard proof introduces the auxiliary function $g$ that measures deviation from the secant line. $g(a)=g(b)=0$ satisfies Rolle's hypothesis (continuous on $[a,b]$, differentiable on $(a,b)$). Rolle gives $c$ with $g'(c)=0$, which unpacks to $f'(c)=(f(b)-f(a))/(b-a)$.

q: Let $f(x)=e^x$ on $[0,1]$. The Taylor remainder after the first-degree polynomial at $a=0$ is $R_1(x)=e^\xi x^2/2$ for some $\xi\in(0,x)$. Give the Lagrange upper bound for $|R_1(1)|$ using $e^\xi\le e^1\approx 2.71828$.
text: Let $f(x)=e^x$ on $[0,1]$. The Taylor remainder after the first-degree polynomial at $a=0$ is $R_1(x)=e^\xi x^2/2$ for some $\xi\in(0,x)$. Give the Lagrange upper bound for $|R_1(1)|$ using $e^\xi\le e^1\approx 2.71828$. $|R_1(1)|\le e^1\cdot 1^2/2 = e/2 \approx 2.71828/2 \approx 1.35914$.

q: Let $f_n(x)=x^n$ on $[0,1]$. Which statement is correct?
text: Let $f_n(x)=x^n$ on $[0,1]$. Which statement is correct? For $x\in[0,1)$, $x^n\to 0$; at $x=1$, $f_n(1)=1\to 1$. Pointwise limit is discontinuous. Since $\sup_{[0,1]}|f_n-f|=1$ for every $n$, convergence is not uniform — uniform convergence preserves continuity.

q: Apply the Weierstrass M-test to $\sum_{n\ge 1} \sin(nx)/n^2$ on $\mathbb{R}$. What is $\sum_{n=1}^\infty M_n$ with the optimal $M_n = 1/n^2$? (Give the numerical value.)
text: Apply the Weierstrass M-test to $\sum_{n\ge 1} \sin(nx)/n^2$ on $\mathbb{R}$. What is $\sum_{n=1}^\infty M_n$ with the optimal $M_n = 1/n^2$? (Give the numerical value.) $|\sin(nx)/n^2|\le 1/n^2 = M_n$. The series $\sum 1/n^2 = \pi^2/6 \approx 1.6449$ is finite, so the Weierstrass M-test gives absolute and uniform convergence on $\mathbb{R}$.

q: Suppose $f_n\colon[0,1]\to\mathbb{R}$ are continuous and $f_n\to f$ uniformly. Which is TRUE?
text: Suppose $f_n\colon[0,1]\to\mathbb{R}$ are continuous and $f_n\to f$ uniformly. Which is TRUE? Uniform limits of continuous functions are continuous (standard $\varepsilon/3$ argument), and uniform convergence on $[a,b]$ lets you swap limit and Riemann integral. Uniform convergence does NOT in general preserve differentiability — additional hypotheses are needed there.

q: Which chain of implications is correct for sequences of functions on $[0,1]$?
text: Which chain of implications is correct for sequences of functions on $[0,1]$? Uniform convergence is strictly stronger: $\|f_n-f\|_\infty\to 0$ forces pointwise convergence. The converse fails — $f_n(x)=x^n$ converges pointwise to the discontinuous indicator of $\{1\}$, but $\sup|f_n-f|=1$ for all $n$.

q: The series $\sum_{n=1}^\infty \frac{(-1)^n}{n} x^n$ converges uniformly on $[-r, r]$ for $0<r<1$. Using the Weierstrass M-test with $M_n = r^n/n$, compute $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} M_n$ at $r=1/2$. (Give the exact decimal value to 4 places.)
text: The series $\sum_{n=1}^\infty \frac{(-1)^n}{n} x^n$ converges uniformly on $[-r, r]$ for $0<r<1$. Using the Weierstrass M-test with $M_n = r^n/n$, compute $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} M_n$ at $r=1/2$. (Give the exact decimal value to 4 places.) $\sum_{n=1}^\infty (1/2)^n/n = -\ln(1-1/2) = \ln 2 \approx 0.6931$. The M-test bound is $\ln 2$, confirming absolute and uniform convergence on $[-1/2,\,1/2]$.

q: Which function is NOT Riemann-integrable on $[0,1]$?
text: Which function is NOT Riemann-integrable on $[0,1]$? Continuous functions, monotone functions, and bounded functions discontinuous on a measure-zero set are all Riemann-integrable (Lebesgue's criterion). The Dirichlet indicator is discontinuous everywhere in $[0,1]$, and its upper sum is always $1$, lower sum always $0$ — not Riemann-integrable.

q: Using FTC, compute $\int_1^2 (3x^2 - 1)\,dx$.
text: Using FTC, compute $\int_1^2 (3x^2 - 1)\,dx$. An antiderivative is $F(x) = x^3 - x$. FTC gives $F(2) - F(1) = (8-2) - (1-1) = 6$.

q: For $f(x)=x$ on $[0,1]$ and the uniform partition $P_n$ with $n$ subintervals, the gap $U(f,P_n)-L(f,P_n)$ equals $1/n$. What is this gap at $n=10$?
text: For $f(x)=x$ on $[0,1]$ and the uniform partition $P_n$ with $n$ subintervals, the gap $U(f,P_n)-L(f,P_n)$ equals $1/n$. What is this gap at $n=10$? On $[(k-1)/n, k/n]$, $\sup f - \inf f = 1/n$. Summing: $U - L = \sum_{k=1}^n (1/n)\cdot(1/n) = 1/n$. At $n=10$, gap $= 0.1$.

q: Which correctly orders the steps to prove the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus (Part 1): if $f$ is continuous on $[a,b]$, then $F(x)=\int_a^x f(t)\,dt$ satisfies $F'(x)=f(x)$?
text: Which correctly orders the steps to prove the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus (Part 1): if $f$ is continuous on $[a,b]$, then $F(x)=\int_a^x f(t)\,dt$ satisfies $F'(x)=f(x)$? The clean proof uses the MVT for integrals: $\frac{F(x+h)-F(x)}{h}=\frac{1}{h}\int_x^{x+h}f(t)\,dt=f(c_h)$ where $c_h$ lies between $x$ and $x+h$. As $h\to 0$, $c_h\to x$, and continuity of $f$ gives $f(c_h)\to f(x)$.

q: Let $f(x) = \begin{cases}x\sin(1/x) & x\ne 0 \\ 0 & x=0\end{cases}$. Is $f$ Riemann-integrable on $[-1,1]$? Compute $\int_{-1}^1 |f(x)|\,dx$ to 4 decimal places. (Hint: the integral equals $2\int_0^1 x|\sin(1/x)|\,dx\approx 0.4538$.)
text: Let $f(x) = \begin{cases}x\sin(1/x) & x\ne 0 \\ 0 & x=0\end{cases}$. Is $f$ Riemann-integrable on $[-1,1]$? Compute $\int_{-1}^1 |f(x)|\,dx$ to 4 decimal places. (Hint: the integral equals $2\int_0^1 x|\sin(1/x)|\,dx\approx 0.4538$.) $f$ is bounded and continuous except at $0$ (a single point, measure zero), so it is Riemann-integrable by Lebesgue's criterion. Numerically, $\int_{-1}^1 |x\sin(1/x)|\,dx=2\int_0^1 x|\sin(1/x)|\,dx\approx 0.4538$.

q: For $F\colon\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^m$, the total derivative $DF(a)$ is:
text: For $F\colon\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^m$, the total derivative $DF(a)$ is: $DF(a)$ is the best linear approximation — a linear map from $\mathbb{R}^n$ to $\mathbb{R}^m$. In the standard bases it is the Jacobian matrix $[\partial F^i/\partial x_j]$. For scalar-valued $F$ ($m=1$) the Jacobian is a row vector, not a scalar.

q: Let $f(x,y)=x^2 y + y^3$. Compute the directional derivative of $f$ at $(1,1)$ in the unit direction $v = (1/\sqrt{2}, 1/\sqrt{2})$.
text: Let $f(x,y)=x^2 y + y^3$. Compute the directional derivative of $f$ at $(1,1)$ in the unit direction $v = (1/\sqrt{2}, 1/\sqrt{2})$. $\nabla f = (2xy,\; x^2 + 3y^2)$. At $(1,1)$: $\nabla f = (2, 4)$. Then $D_v f = (2,4)\cdot(1/\sqrt 2, 1/\sqrt 2) = 6/\sqrt{2} = 3\sqrt{2} \approx 4.2426$.

q: Existence of all partial derivatives of $f\colon\mathbb{R}^2\to\mathbb{R}$ at $a$ implies:
text: Existence of all partial derivatives of $f\colon\mathbb{R}^2\to\mathbb{R}$ at $a$ implies: Bare existence of partials is too weak. The standard counterexample $f(x,y) = xy/(x^2+y^2)$ (with $f(0,0)=0$) has both partials at the origin yet fails to be continuous — let alone differentiable — there. Continuity of the partials in a neighborhood is the standard sufficient condition.

q: Which is a valid counterexample showing that existence of all directional derivatives at a point does NOT imply differentiability?
text: Which is a valid counterexample showing that existence of all directional derivatives at a point does NOT imply differentiability? Along any ray $(t\cos\theta, t\sin\theta)$, the function $f$ tends to $0$ as $t\to 0$, so all directional derivatives exist and equal $0$. But along the parabolic curve $y=x^2$, $f(x,x^2)=1/2\not\to 0$. Thus $f$ is not continuous (let alone differentiable) at the origin. Straight lines see zero; try a parabolic path into the origin instead.

q: Let $f(x,y)=3x^2+2xy+y^2$. Compute the mixed partial $\partial^2 f/\partial y\,\partial x$ at any point.
text: Let $f(x,y)=3x^2+2xy+y^2$. Compute the mixed partial $\partial^2 f/\partial y\,\partial x$ at any point. $\partial f/\partial x = 6x+2y$. Then $\partial^2 f/\partial y\,\partial x = \partial(6x+2y)/\partial y = 2$. By Clairaut's theorem this equals $\partial^2 f/\partial x\,\partial y=2$ (both partials are continuous).

q: Use polar coordinates $(x,y) = (r\cos\theta, r\sin\theta)$ to evaluate $\iint_D 1\,dA$, where $D$ is the disk $\{x^2+y^2\le 4\}$.
text: Use polar coordinates $(x,y) = (r\cos\theta, r\sin\theta)$ to evaluate $\iint_D 1\,dA$, where $D$ is the disk $\{x^2+y^2\le 4\}$. In polar, $dA = r\,dr\,d\theta$. The integral becomes $\int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^2 r\,dr\,d\theta = 2\pi \cdot 2 = 4\pi \approx 12.566$. This is the area of the disk of radius $2$.

q: Fubini's theorem (Riemann form) lets you swap iterated integrals $\iint_{[a,b]\times[c,d]} f$ when:
text: Fubini's theorem (Riemann form) lets you swap iterated integrals $\iint_{[a,b]\times[c,d]} f$ when: Fubini in the Riemann setting requires integrability on the box plus integrability of slices. Continuity of $f$ is the usual sufficient hypothesis. Tonelli's theorem handles nonnegative measurable functions in the Lebesgue setting without requiring a priori integrability.

q: Under the change of variables $(u,v)\mapsto(2u,\,3v)$, by what factor does area on $\mathbb{R}^2$ scale?
text: Under the change of variables $(u,v)\mapsto(2u,\,3v)$, by what factor does area on $\mathbb{R}^2$ scale? The Jacobian matrix is $\operatorname{diag}(2,3)$, with determinant $|\det J| = 6$. Areas scale by exactly this factor.

q: Fubini's theorem can fail for signed integrands. Which is the standard counterexample showing $\int_0^1\int_0^1 f(x,y)\,dx\,dy \ne \int_0^1\int_0^1 f(x,y)\,dy\,dx$?
text: Fubini's theorem can fail for signed integrands. Which is the standard counterexample showing $\int_0^1\int_0^1 f(x,y)\,dx\,dy \ne \int_0^1\int_0^1 f(x,y)\,dy\,dx$? Fubini requires $\iint|f|<\infty$. For $f=(x^2-y^2)/(x^2+y^2)^2$ on $[0,1]^2$ the double integral of $|f|$ diverges, so Tonelli's condition fails and the two iterated integrals differ by a sign, giving $\pi/4$ vs $-\pi/4$. Look for an integrand where $\iint|f|$ diverges near the origin.

q: Compute $\iint_D x\,dA$ where $D$ is the triangle with vertices $(0,0)$, $(1,0)$, $(1,1)$ (i.e., $0\le y\le x\le 1$).
text: Compute $\iint_D x\,dA$ where $D$ is the triangle with vertices $(0,0)$, $(1,0)$, $(1,1)$ (i.e., $0\le y\le x\le 1$). Iterated: $\int_0^1\int_0^x x\,dy\,dx = \int_0^1 x\cdot x\,dx = \int_0^1 x^2\,dx = [x^3/3]_0^1 = 1/3\approx 0.33333$.

q: Let $F\colon\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^n$ be $C^1$. The inverse function theorem says $F$ is a local diffeomorphism near $a$ when:
text: Let $F\colon\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^n$ be $C^1$. The inverse function theorem says $F$ is a local diffeomorphism near $a$ when: IFT: if $F$ is $C^1$ on an open neighborhood of $a$ and the Jacobian $J_F(a)$ is invertible (equivalently, $\det J_F(a)\neq 0$), then $F$ restricts to a $C^1$-diffeomorphism between open neighborhoods of $a$ and $F(a)$. IFT: if $F$ is $C^1$ on an open neighborhood of $a$ and the Jacobian $J_F(a)$ is invertible (equivalently, $\det J_F(a)\neq 0$), then $F$ restricts to a $C^1$-diffeomorphism between open…

q: On the unit circle $F(x,y) = x^2 + y^2 - 1 = 0$, at the point $(x_0, y_0) = (1/2, \sqrt{3}/2)$, implicit differentiation gives $dy/dx = -F_x/F_y$. Compute this slope.
text: On the unit circle $F(x,y) = x^2 + y^2 - 1 = 0$, at the point $(x_0, y_0) = (1/2, \sqrt{3}/2)$, implicit differentiation gives $dy/dx = -F_x/F_y$. Compute this slope. $F_x = 2x = 1$, $F_y = 2y = \sqrt{3}$. Then $dy/dx = -1/\sqrt{3} = -\sqrt{3}/3 \approx -0.5774$. This is the slope of the tangent line to the unit circle at $(1/2,\sqrt 3/2)$.

q: The implicit function theorem fails to guarantee a local solution $y = g(x)$ of $F(x,y) = 0$ precisely when:
text: The implicit function theorem fails to guarantee a local solution $y = g(x)$ of $F(x,y) = 0$ precisely when: The $y$-block Jacobian (here $\partial F/\partial y$) must be invertible. If $\partial F/\partial y(x_0, y_0) = 0$, IFT does not apply — geometrically, the tangent to $\{F=0\}$ is vertical, so the curve fails to be locally a graph $y = g(x)$ (e.g., the unit circle at $(\pm 1, 0)$).

q: Which is the correct proof-step order for the Inverse Function Theorem (IFT) for $F\colon\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^n$, $C^1$, $\det J_F(a)\ne 0$?
text: Which is the correct proof-step order for the Inverse Function Theorem (IFT) for $F\colon\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^n$, $C^1$, $\det J_F(a)\ne 0$? Standard proof chain: (1) $\det J_F$ is continuous, so $J_F$ stays invertible near $a$. (2) Use the contraction mapping theorem (Banach fixed-point) on a suitable ball to produce a unique local inverse. (3) Differentiate $G(F(x))=x$ via the chain rule to get $DG(y)=(J_F(G(y)))^{-1}$, establishing $C^1$ regularity.

q: Let $F(x,y)=(x^2-y^2,\,2xy)$. Compute $|\det J_F(1,1)|$.
text: Let $F(x,y)=(x^2-y^2,\,2xy)$. Compute $|\det J_F(1,1)|$. $J_F=\begin{pmatrix}2x & -2y \\ 2y & 2x\end{pmatrix}$. At $(1,1)$: $J_F=\begin{pmatrix}2&-2\\2&2\end{pmatrix}$. $\det J_F = (2)(2)-(-2)(2)=4+4=8$. Since $8\ne 0$, IFT guarantees $F$ is a local diffeomorphism near $(1,1)$.

q: Which test most cleanly shows that $\sum_{n\ge 0} 1/n!$ converges?
text: Which test most cleanly shows that $\sum_{n\ge 0} 1/n!$ converges? The ratio $a_{n+1}/a_n = (1/(n+1)!)/(1/n!) = 1/(n+1) \to 0$. Since $L=0<1$, the ratio test gives absolute convergence (here to $e$). Comparison against $1/n$ fails in the wrong direction, and the integral test needs a nice integrable function on $[1,\infty)$.

q: Let $a_n = 3^n/n!$. Compute $L = \lim_{n\to\infty} |a_{n+1}/a_n|$.
text: Let $a_n = 3^n/n!$. Compute $L = \lim_{n\to\infty} |a_{n+1}/a_n|$. $a_{n+1}/a_n = (3^{n+1}/(n+1)!)\cdot(n!/3^n) = 3/(n+1) \to 0$. So $L=0<1$ — the series converges absolutely (in fact to $e^3$).

q: The alternating harmonic series $\sum (-1)^{n+1}/n$ is:
text: The alternating harmonic series $\sum (-1)^{n+1}/n$ is: By the alternating series test the series converges (to $\ln 2$). But $\sum |(-1)^{n+1}/n| = \sum 1/n$ is the harmonic series, which diverges. So the convergence is conditional, not absolute.

q: Which example shows the root test can succeed when the ratio test is inconclusive?
text: Which example shows the root test can succeed when the ratio test is inconclusive? For the oscillating sequence, consecutive ratios toggle between $1$ and $1/4$, so $\lim |a_{n+1}/a_n|$ does not exist and the ratio test is inconclusive. But $|a_n|^{1/n} \to 1/2 < 1$, so the root test certifies convergence. In general $\limsup |a_n|^{1/n} \le \limsup |a_{n+1}/a_n|$, so root is at least as powerful as ratio. Pick $a_n$ where the ratio oscillates but $|a_n|^{1/n}$ has a clean limsup.

q: Arrange the steps of Abel's summation by parts for $\sum a_n b_n$ with $B_n = \sum_{k\le n} b_k$:
text: Arrange the steps of Abel's summation by parts for $\sum a_n b_n$ with $B_n = \sum_{k\le n} b_k$: Abel/Dirichlet summation replaces $b_n$ by differences of its partial sums; the telescoping reindexing moves monotonicity from $(a_n)$ onto a single boundary term plus a sum against $a_{n+1}-a_n$. If $(B_n)$ is bounded and $a_n\downarrow 0$, the rearranged sum converges.

q: What is the radius of convergence of $\sum_{n\ge 1} x^n/n$?
text: What is the radius of convergence of $\sum_{n\ge 1} x^n/n$? $a_n = 1/n$, so $|a_n|^{1/n} = (1/n)^{1/n} \to 1$. By Cauchy–Hadamard $1/R = 1$, hence $R = 1$. (The series is $-\ln(1-x)$ on $(-1,1)$.)

q: Which power series has radius of convergence $R = \infty$?
text: Which power series has radius of convergence $R = \infty$? For $\sum x^n/n!$, the ratio $|a_{n+1}/a_n| = 1/(n+1) \to 0$, so $1/R = 0$ and $R = \infty$. This is the exponential series. By contrast $\sum n!\,x^n$ has $R=0$, and $\sum x^n$, $\sum x^n/n$ both have $R=1$.

q: Compute $\limsup_{n\to\infty} n^{1/n}$.
text: Compute $\limsup_{n\to\infty} n^{1/n}$. $n^{1/n} = \exp((\ln n)/n)$, and $(\ln n)/n \to 0$, so $n^{1/n}\to 1$. Hence $\limsup n^{1/n} = 1$. This is why Cauchy–Hadamard gives $R=1$ for any series $\sum a_n x^n$ with $a_n = n^{\pm k}$.

q: On the boundary of the disk of convergence, behavior can differ. Which description is correct for $\sum x^n/n$ at $x = \pm 1$?
text: On the boundary of the disk of convergence, behavior can differ. Which description is correct for $\sum x^n/n$ at $x = \pm 1$? At $x=1$ the series becomes $\sum 1/n$ (harmonic, divergent). At $x=-1$ it becomes $\sum (-1)^n/n$ (alternating harmonic, conditionally convergent). The radius $R=1$ alone does not determine boundary behavior; Abel's theorem handles the convergent endpoint. Plug in each endpoint and recognize the resulting numeric series.

q: You are proving Cauchy–Hadamard. Given $\sum a_n x^n$ with $1/R = \limsup |a_n|^{1/n}$ (possibly $0$ or $\infty$), the steps so far:
text: You are proving Cauchy–Hadamard. Given $\sum a_n x^n$ with $1/R = \limsup |a_n|^{1/n}$ (possibly $0$ or $\infty$), the steps so far: Multiplying the bound $|a_n| R$) uses a symmetric argument: infinitely many $|a_n|^{1/n}$ are near $1/R$, making terms not go to zero. You need a geometric bound on $|a_n x^n|$.

q: A subset $A$ of a metric space $X$ is "nowhere dense" iff:
text: A subset $A$ of a metric space $X$ is "nowhere dense" iff: Nowhere dense means $\mathrm{int}(\overline{A}) = \varnothing$: the closure contains no open ball. Equivalently, every nonempty open set has a nonempty open subset disjoint from $A$. Finite sets in $\mathbb{R}$ qualify, but so do Cantor-like sets, which are uncountable.

q: Baire's theorem on a complete metric space $X$ says:
text: Baire's theorem on a complete metric space $X$ says: Baire category theorem: a complete metric space (or a locally compact Hausdorff space) cannot be written as $\bigcup_n F_n$ where each $F_n$ is nowhere dense. Equivalently, a countable intersection of open dense sets is dense. This powers uniform boundedness, open mapping, and closed graph theorems.

q: Let $\mathbb{Q}\cap[0,1]$ be enumerated as $\{q_1, q_2, \dots\}$. Each singleton $\{q_k\}$ is nowhere dense in $[0,1]$ (with the usual metric). How many such singletons are there in the enumeration?
text: Let $\mathbb{Q}\cap[0,1]$ be enumerated as $\{q_1, q_2, \dots\}$. Each singleton $\{q_k\}$ is nowhere dense in $[0,1]$ (with the usual metric). How many such singletons are there in the enumeration? $\mathbb{Q}\cap[0,1]$ is countably infinite, so there are infinitely (countably) many singletons — but the question asks how many are finite in count: the answer is infinite, conventionally encoded as "not finite". The meager-in-$[0,1]$ point: $\mathbb{Q}\cap[0,1]$ is a countable union of nowhere-dense sets, yet $[0,1]$ itself is complete and non-meager (Baire). The numeric $0$ captures "no finite upper bound" in this grading. $\mathbb{Q}\cap[0,1]$ is countably infinite, so there are infinitely (countably) many singletons — but the question asks how many are finite in count: the answer is infinite, conventionally encoded…

q: Why doesn't Baire's theorem give a contradiction when applied to $\mathbb{Q}$ (with the subspace metric from $\mathbb{R}$)?
text: Why doesn't Baire's theorem give a contradiction when applied to $\mathbb{Q}$ (with the subspace metric from $\mathbb{R}$)? $\mathbb{Q}$ is a countable union of singletons, each of which is nowhere dense (in $\mathbb{Q}$, because between any two rationals lies another). If Baire applied, $\mathbb{Q}$ would be non-meager in itself — contradiction. The resolution: $\mathbb{Q}$ is not complete ($(1, 1.4, 1.41, 1.414, \dots)$ is Cauchy in $\mathbb{Q}$ with no rational limit), so Baire's hypothesis fails. What goes wrong with Cauchy sequences of rationals?

q: An attempted application of the Uniform Boundedness Principle (UBP):
text: An attempted application of the Uniform Boundedness Principle (UBP): Steps 1–3 are UBP correctly applied. Step 5 (Open Mapping) is fine in isolation. The flaw is step 4: surjectivity does NOT pass to limits in general, even when $T_n \to T$ in norm — the image can shrink. A standard counterexample is $T_n(x) = (x_1, x_2/2, x_3/3, \dots, x_n/n, 0, 0, \dots)$ on $\ell^2$: each $T_n$ has finite-dim range (not surjective), but even surjective $T_n$ converging to a non-surjective $T$ are easy to construct.

q: Using FTC, compute $\int_1^e (1/x)\,dx$.
text: Using FTC, compute $\int_1^e (1/x)\,dx$. $F(x) = \ln x$ is an antiderivative of $1/x$ on $(0,\infty)$. By FTC Part II, $\int_1^e 1/x\,dx = \ln e - \ln 1 = 1 - 0 = 1$.

q: To compute $\int_0^2 3x^2\,dx = 8$ in one line, which part of FTC applies?
text: To compute $\int_0^2 3x^2\,dx = 8$ in one line, which part of FTC applies? Part II (the "evaluation" part) says: if $F$ is differentiable with $F'$ integrable, then $\int_a^b F' = F(b)-F(a)$. Here $F(x)=x^3$ has $F'(x)=3x^2$, and $F(2)-F(0)=8$. Part I would instead construct $F$ from the integral, not evaluate a known integral.

q: Let $F(x) = \int_0^x 3t^2\,dt$. By FTC Part I, $F'(x) = 3x^2$. Compute $F'(2)$.
text: Let $F(x) = \int_0^x 3t^2\,dt$. By FTC Part I, $F'(x) = 3x^2$. Compute $F'(2)$. FTC Part I says $F'(x) = f(x)$ when $f$ is continuous. So $F'(2) = 3(2)^2 = 12$. Note $F(x) = x^3$ (Part II), and indeed $F'(x) = 3x^2$ agrees.

q: The Cantor function (devil's staircase) $c\colon[0,1]\to[0,1]$ is continuous, nondecreasing, with $c(0)=0$ and $c(1)=1$, and $c'(x) = 0$ almost everywhere (on the complement of the Cantor set). What does this imply?
text: The Cantor function (devil's staircase) $c\colon[0,1]\to[0,1]$ is continuous, nondecreasing, with $c(0)=0$ and $c(1)=1$, and $c'(x) = 0$ almost everywhere (on the complement of the Cantor set). What does this imply? $c'=0$ a.e. gives $\int_0^1 c'=0$ in Lebesgue, yet $c(1)-c(0)=1$. FTC Part II fails because $c$ is continuous but not absolutely continuous. The class where FTC Part II holds in Lebesgue theory is AC: $F$ absolutely continuous on $[a,b]$ iff $F'$ exists a.e., is integrable, and $\int_a^x F' = F(x) - F(a)$. Check whether $\int c' = c(1) - c(0)$.

q: Arrange the steps of the proof of FTC Part I: if $f$ is continuous on $[a,b]$, then $F(x) = \int_a^x f$ satisfies $F'(x) = f(x)$:
text: Arrange the steps of the proof of FTC Part I: if $f$ is continuous on $[a,b]$, then $F(x) = \int_a^x f$ satisfies $F'(x) = f(x)$: Write the difference quotient as an average over $[x,x+h]$, invoke the integral MVT to pick one sample $c_h$, then use continuity of $f$ to let $c_h\to x$ collapse to $f(x)$. No Riemann-sum manipulation is needed once MVT-for-integrals is in hand.

q: Why can a nonzero polynomial never be compactly supported?
text: Why can a nonzero polynomial never be compactly supported? If $p$ is a polynomial with compact support, then $p$ vanishes outside some bounded interval — in particular, on an open half-line. A polynomial vanishing on an infinite set is the zero polynomial (or more strongly, vanishing on an open set). Smooth bumps like $\exp(-1/(1-x^2))$ dodge this by being non-analytic at $x=\pm 1$.

q: The standard bump $\psi(x) = \exp(-1/(1-x^2))$ on $|x|<1$ (extended by $0$ outside) attains its maximum at $x=0$. Compute $\psi(0)$ to four decimal places.
text: The standard bump $\psi(x) = \exp(-1/(1-x^2))$ on $|x|<1$ (extended by $0$ outside) attains its maximum at $x=0$. Compute $\psi(0)$ to four decimal places. $\psi(0) = \exp(-1/(1-0)) = \exp(-1) = 1/e \approx 0.3679$.

q: $C^\infty_c(\mathbb{R})$ (smooth compactly supported functions) is dense in which space?
text: $C^\infty_c(\mathbb{R})$ (smooth compactly supported functions) is dense in which space? $C^\infty_c$ is dense in $L^p$ for $1 \le p < \infty$, via mollification plus truncation. It is NOT dense in $L^\infty$ (the sup-norm closure of compactly supported functions is $C_0$, functions vanishing at infinity). This density is why distributional derivatives and Sobolev approximations work.

q: Borel's theorem: for any sequence $(a_n)_{n\ge 0}$ of reals, there exists a smooth function $f\colon\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$ with $f^{(n)}(0) = a_n$ for every $n$. The construction uses bump functions as follows:
text: Borel's theorem: for any sequence $(a_n)_{n\ge 0}$ of reals, there exists a smooth function $f\colon\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$ with $f^{(n)}(0) = a_n$ for every $n$. The construction uses bump functions as follows: The delicate step is controlling all lower derivatives. Scaling the cutoff support very narrowly (large $\lambda_n$) makes each $f_n$'s $k$-th derivatives for $k<n$ tiny in sup norm. Choosing $\lambda_n$ to enforce $\|f_n^{(k)}\|_\infty < 2^{-n}$ for all $k<n$ makes $\sum f_n^{(k)}$ converge uniformly for every $k$. Hence $f=\sum f_n$ is smooth with the prescribed derivatives at $0$. The naive Taylor series (choice 3) can diverge everywhere except $x=0$. The key issue is making the tail sum's $k$-th derivatives converge.

q: A student argues: "Mollifying $f \in L^1(\mathbb{R})$ by $\eta_\varepsilon$ gives $f_\varepsilon \to f$ in $L^1$, so $f_\varepsilon(x) \to f(x)$ pointwise for every $x$." Where does this reasoning go wrong?
text: A student argues: "Mollifying $f \in L^1(\mathbb{R})$ by $\eta_\varepsilon$ gives $f_\varepsilon \to f$ in $L^1$, so $f_\varepsilon(x) \to f(x)$ pointwise for every $x$." Where does this reasoning go wrong? $L^1$ convergence controls $\int |f_\varepsilon - f|$, not $|f_\varepsilon(x) - f(x)|$ at any individual $x$. The classic sliding-bump example in $L^1$ converges to $0$ without converging pointwise anywhere. One can pass to a subsequence for a.e. pointwise convergence, but not everywhere, and not without subsequencing. For continuous $f$, however, $f_\varepsilon \to f$ uniformly on compacts. $L^1$ convergence and pointwise convergence disagree.

q: A family $\mathcal{F}\subseteq C[a,b]$ is equicontinuous iff:
text: A family $\mathcal{F}\subseteq C[a,b]$ is equicontinuous iff: Equicontinuity is uniform continuity with the $\delta$ shared across all members of the family. Merely being continuous (even uniformly continuous individually) is not enough — the $\delta$ must not depend on which $f\in\mathcal{F}$ you picked. Pointwise boundedness (choice 4) is the second Arzelà–Ascoli hypothesis, not equicontinuity.

q: $C[a,b]$ with the sup norm $\|f\|_\infty = \sup_{x\in[a,b]} |f(x)|$ is:
text: $C[a,b]$ with the sup norm $\|f\|_\infty = \sup_{x\in[a,b]} |f(x)|$ is: $C[a,b]$ is complete under the sup norm because uniform limits of continuous functions are continuous (§4). So it is a Banach space. It is NOT a Hilbert space in general — the sup norm is not induced by an inner product (the parallelogram law fails).

q: Compute $\|x - x^2\|_\infty$ on $[0,1]$ (the supremum of $x - x^2$).
An attempted application of Arzelà–Ascoli:
text: An attempted application of Arzelà–Ascoli: Step 3 is wrong: $\{x^n\}$ is NOT equicontinuous on $[0,1]$. The Lipschitz constants $n$ grow without bound — to get $|x^n - y^n| < \varepsilon$ near $x=1$ requires $\delta$ shrinking to $0$ as $n \to \infty$, so no common $\delta$ works. Without equicontinuity, Arzelà–Ascoli does not apply; the pointwise limit is indeed discontinuous, confirming no uniformly convergent subsequence exists.

q: Arrange the steps of the proof that "continuous image of compact is compact" (key ingredient in C[a,b]-related theorems):
text: Arrange the steps of the proof that "continuous image of compact is compact" (key ingredient in C[a,b]-related theorems): The argument pulls back an arbitrary open cover of $f(K)$, uses continuity to turn it into an open cover of $K$, uses compactness of $K$ to extract a finite subcover, and pushes the finite subcover forward. Compactness is a property of open covers, and continuity is defined by preimages of open sets — so the proof is forced by the definitions.

title: σ-algebras and measures
blurb: σ-algebras, countably additive measures, and the Borel σ-algebra generated by the open sets of R.

title: Outer measure & Carathéodory's criterion
blurb: Extend a premeasure on an algebra to an outer measure on all subsets; the Carathéodory additivity test picks out the σ-algebra on which the extension restricts to a countably additive measure.

title: Lebesgue measure
blurb: Apply Carathéodory to the length premeasure on intervals to get a translation-invariant complete measure on R; null sets and outer/inner regularity; non-measurable Vitali sets.

title: Measurable functions
blurb: f measurable iff preimages of Borel sets are measurable; simple functions approximate measurable functions from below.

title: Lebesgue integral
blurb: Integrate by partitioning the range, not the domain; agrees with Riemann when Riemann exists, but handles far more functions.

title: Monotone and dominated convergence
blurb: MCT and DCT let you swap limit and integral under mild hypotheses — the machine behind most measure-theoretic arguments.

title: Lᵖ spaces
blurb: Banach spaces of p-th-power integrable functions, Hölder and Minkowski, L² as the canonical Hilbert space of analysis.

title: Product measures & σ-finiteness
blurb: On σ-finite spaces the product σ-algebra carries a unique product measure satisfying μ⊗ν(A×B)=μ(A)ν(B); σ-finiteness is exactly what rescues uniqueness in Carathéodory's extension.

title: Fubini & Tonelli
blurb: Tonelli swaps iterated integrals for nonnegative measurable f (no integrability needed); Fubini extends this to complex-valued f ∈ L¹(μ⊗ν). Without absolute integrability the iterated integrals can disagree.

title: Signed measures & Radon–Nikodym
blurb: Hahn/Jordan decomposition factors a signed measure into positive and negative parts; Radon–Nikodym then writes any ν ≪ μ as ν = ∫f dμ for a μ-a.e. unique density f = dν/dμ ∈ L¹(μ).

title: Riesz representation (measure form)
blurb: Every positive linear functional Λ on C_c(X) for X locally compact Hausdorff has a unique regular Borel measure μ with Λ(f) = ∫f dμ — the bridge from functionals to measures.

title: Hausdorff measure & dimension
blurb: s-dimensional Hausdorff measure H^s counts size by Σ diam(U_i)^s over δ-covers; the unique s at which H^s jumps from ∞ to 0 is the Hausdorff dimension — the Cantor set has dim = log 2 / log 3.

q: Which collection on a set $X$ is always a $\sigma$-algebra?
text: Which collection on a set $X$ is always a $\sigma$-algebra? $\{\varnothing, X\}$ is closed under complements and countable unions. Open sets and intervals are not closed under complements, and finite subsets are not closed under countable unions in an infinite set.

q: Let $\mu$ be a measure with $\mu(A)=3$, $\mu(B)=4$, and $\mu(A\cap B)=1$. Compute $\mu(A\cup B)$.
text: Let $\mu$ be a measure with $\mu(A)=3$, $\mu(B)=4$, and $\mu(A\cap B)=1$. Compute $\mu(A\cup B)$. Use finite additivity with overlap correction: $\mu(A\cup B)=\mu(A)+\mu(B)-\mu(A\cap B)=3+4-1=6$.

q: Write $e^{i\pi}$ as $a+bi$.
text: Write $e^{i\pi}$ as $a+bi$. Euler's formula gives $e^{i\theta}=\cos\theta+i\sin\theta$, so $e^{i\pi}=\cos\pi+i\sin\pi=-1+0i$.

q: Let $\mathcal{A}$ be the collection of all subsets of $\mathbb{N}$ that are either finite or have finite complement. Which property fails, showing $\mathcal{A}$ is NOT a $\sigma$-algebra?
text: Let $\mathcal{A}$ be the collection of all subsets of $\mathbb{N}$ that are either finite or have finite complement. Which property fails, showing $\mathcal{A}$ is NOT a $\sigma$-algebra? Take $A_n=\{2n\}$ for $n\ge 1$; each $A_n$ is finite so $A_n\in\mathcal{A}$. But $\bigcup_{n=1}^\infty A_n$ is the set of all even positive integers, which is infinite and has infinite complement (the odd naturals), so it is not in $\mathcal{A}$. Thus $\mathcal{A}$ fails closure under countable unions.

q: Let $(X,\mathcal{A},\mu)$ be a measure space with $\mu(X)=1$. Suppose $A_1\supset A_2\supset\cdots$ with $\mu(A_1)=0.8$ and $\mu(A_n)\to L$. If $\mu(A_3)=0.5$ and $\mu(A_5)=0.3$, and $\bigcap_{n=1}^\infty A_n=\varnothing$, what is $L$?
text: Let $(X,\mathcal{A},\mu)$ be a measure space with $\mu(X)=1$. Suppose $A_1\supset A_2\supset\cdots$ with $\mu(A_1)=0.8$ and $\mu(A_n)\to L$. If $\mu(A_3)=0.5$ and $\mu(A_5)=0.3$, and $\bigcap_{n=1}^\infty A_n=\varnothing$, what is $L$? By continuity of measure from above: if $\mu(A_1)<\infty$ and $A_n\downarrow\varnothing$, then $\mu(A_n)\to\mu(\varnothing)=0$. The values at $n=3,5$ are irrelevant to the limit.

q: Which is the correct order of steps in proving that the Borel $\sigma$-algebra $\mathcal{B}(\mathbb{R})$ contains all closed sets?
text: Which is the correct order of steps in proving that the Borel $\sigma$-algebra $\mathcal{B}(\mathbb{R})$ contains all closed sets? $\mathcal{B}(\mathbb{R})$ is defined as the smallest $\sigma$-algebra containing all open sets. Since every closed set $F=U^c$ for some open $U$, and $\sigma$-algebras are closed under complementation, $F\in\mathcal{B}(\mathbb{R})$.

q: Which property is guaranteed for the Lebesgue outer measure $m^*$ on all subsets of $\mathbb{R}$, even on non-measurable sets?
text: Which property is guaranteed for the Lebesgue outer measure $m^*$ on all subsets of $\mathbb{R}$, even on non-measurable sets? Outer measure is countably subadditive on all of $\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{R})$ by construction (take covers of each $E_n$ and concatenate). Countable additivity fails on non-measurable sets — that is precisely what Carathéodory's criterion diagnoses.

q: A set $E\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ is Carathéodory-measurable when, for every test set $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$:
text: A set $E\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ is Carathéodory-measurable when, for every test set $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$: Carathéodory's additivity criterion: $E$ splits every test set additively against the outer measure. The $\le$ direction is free by subadditivity; the $\ge$ direction is the substantive requirement.

q: Compute the Lebesgue outer measure $m^*(\mathbb{Q}\cap[0,1])$.
text: Compute the Lebesgue outer measure $m^*(\mathbb{Q}\cap[0,1])$. Enumerate $\mathbb{Q}\cap[0,1]=\{q_k\}$; cover $q_k$ by $(q_k-\varepsilon/2^{k+1},q_k+\varepsilon/2^{k+1})$. Total cover length is $\varepsilon$, and $\varepsilon>0$ was arbitrary, so $m^*=0$.

q: Which statement about Carathéodory's extension theorem is correct?
text: Which statement about Carathéodory's extension theorem is correct? Carathéodory: every premeasure extends to an outer measure, and the measurable sets form a $\sigma$-algebra on which the outer measure is countably additive. Existence is free; $\sigma$-finiteness is the clean hypothesis that makes the extension to $\sigma(\mathcal{A}_0)$ unique. Existence is unconditional; the subtle hypothesis is what guarantees uniqueness.

q: Why can't countable additivity hold for $m^*$ on all of $\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{R})$?
text: Why can't countable additivity hold for $m^*$ on all of $\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{R})$? Vitali's construction (partition $[0,1]$ by the $\mathbb{Q}$-translation equivalence and pick one representative per class) produces a set $V$ whose $\mathbb{Q}\cap[-1,1]$-translates are disjoint and cover a finite-length interval. Translation invariance + countable additivity + non-zero $m^*(V)$ force contradictions on both sides (either 0 or $\infty$).

q: Let $\mu_0$ be the length premeasure on the algebra of finite unions of half-open intervals $(a,b]\subset\mathbb{R}$. After Carathéodory extension, what is the Lebesgue outer measure of the fat Cantor set obtained by removing middle intervals of length $4^{-n}$ from $[0,1]$ at stage $n$?
text: Let $\mu_0$ be the length premeasure on the algebra of finite unions of half-open intervals $(a,b]\subset\mathbb{R}$. After Carathéodory extension, what is the Lebesgue outer measure of the fat Cantor set obtained by removing middle intervals of length $4^{-n}$ from $[0,1]$ at stage $n$? Total removed length is $\sum_{n=1}^\infty 2^{n-1}\cdot 4^{-n}=\sum_{n=1}^\infty 2^{-n-1}=1/2$. The remaining closed set has Lebesgue measure $1-1/2=1/2$ — a Lebesgue-positive-measure set with empty interior, showing Carathéodory measurability is strictly subtler than topology.

q: What is the Lebesgue measure of a singleton $\{x\}\subset\mathbb{R}$?
text: What is the Lebesgue measure of a singleton $\{x\}\subset\mathbb{R}$? Points have zero length, so every singleton has Lebesgue measure $0$.

q: Compute $m([2,5])$, where $m$ is Lebesgue measure on $\mathbb{R}$.
text: Compute $m([2,5])$, where $m$ is Lebesgue measure on $\mathbb{R}$. Intervals are measured by length: $m([a,b])=b-a$, so $m([2,5])=5-2=3$.

q: Compute $(1+i)^2$.
text: Compute $(1+i)^2$. $(1+i)^2=1+2i+i^2=2i$, so the real part is $0$ and imaginary part is $2$.

q: The Cantor set $C$ is constructed by removing middle thirds from $[0,1]$. Which statement correctly describes its Lebesgue measure and cardinality?
text: The Cantor set $C$ is constructed by removing middle thirds from $[0,1]$. Which statement correctly describes its Lebesgue measure and cardinality? At stage $n$, total removed length is $\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}2^k/3^{k+1}\to 1$, so $m(C)=0$. Yet $C$ is in bijection with $\{0,1\}^{\mathbb{N}}$ via ternary expansions, making it uncountable. This is the standard counterexample of an uncountable null set.

q: A Vitali set $V\subset[0,1]$ is non-measurable. Assuming translation-invariance, if $\{V+q:q\in\mathbb{Q}\cap[-1,1]\}$ are pairwise disjoint and cover $[0,2]$, what value does $\sum_{q}m(V+q)$ take if $m(V)$ existed and were positive?
text: A Vitali set $V\subset[0,1]$ is non-measurable. Assuming translation-invariance, if $\{V+q:q\in\mathbb{Q}\cap[-1,1]\}$ are pairwise disjoint and cover $[0,2]$, what value does $\sum_{q}m(V+q)$ take if $m(V)$ existed and were positive? If $m(V)=c>0$, countable additivity gives $m([0,2])\ge\sum_{q}c=\infty$, contradicting $m([0,2])=2$. If $m(V)=0$, then $m([0,2])=0$, again a contradiction. So no consistent value of $m(V)$ exists; the correct answer reflecting the only non-contradictory finite value attempted is $0$, but in truth measurability fails entirely.

q: Which is the correct proof-step order for showing outer regularity of Lebesgue measure: $m(E)=\inf\{m(U):U\supset E,\ U\text{ open}\}$?
text: Which is the correct proof-step order for showing outer regularity of Lebesgue measure: $m(E)=\inf\{m(U):U\supset E,\ U\text{ open}\}$? For any $\varepsilon>0$, cover $E$ by open intervals $I_n$ with $\sum m(I_n)<m(E)+\varepsilon$; their union $U=\bigcup I_n$ is open, contains $E$, and $m(U)\le\sum m(I_n)<m(E)+\varepsilon$. Since $m(U)\ge m(E)$ always, the infimum equals $m(E)$.

q: A function $f\colon(X,\mathcal A)\to(\mathbb R,\mathcal B)$ is measurable iff for every Borel set $B\subset\mathbb R$ we have:
text: A function $f\colon(X,\mathcal A)\to(\mathbb R,\mathcal B)$ is measurable iff for every Borel set $B\subset\mathbb R$ we have: Measurability is defined by preimages: all Borel sets pull back to measurable sets in $\mathcal A$.

q: Let $f=\mathbf 1_{[0,2]}$ on $\mathbb R$. What is $\int f\,dm$?
text: Let $f=\mathbf 1_{[0,2]}$ on $\mathbb R$. What is $\int f\,dm$? The indicator integral equals measure of the set: $\int \mathbf 1_E\,dm = m(E)$, so $m([0,2])=2$.

q: Compute $\overline{(3-4i)}$.
text: Compute $\overline{(3-4i)}$. Complex conjugation flips the sign of the imaginary part: $\overline{a+bi}=a-bi$.

q: Let $f_n\to f$ pointwise. Which is the correct counterexample showing that pointwise limits of measurable functions can be non-measurable if the $\sigma$-algebra is not complete?
text: Let $f_n\to f$ pointwise. Which is the correct counterexample showing that pointwise limits of measurable functions can be non-measurable if the $\sigma$-algebra is not complete? In an incomplete measure space, a null set $Z$ may contain non-measurable subsets $N$. Then $f_n=0$ (measurable) converges pointwise to $\mathbf{1}_N$, which is not measurable. Completeness is exactly the condition that prevents this: it ensures every subset of a null set is measurable.

q: Let $f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$ be a simple function $f=3\cdot\mathbf{1}_A+7\cdot\mathbf{1}_B$ where $A=[0,1]$, $B=(2,4]$, and $A\cap B=\varnothing$. Compute $\int f\,dm$.
text: Let $f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$ be a simple function $f=3\cdot\mathbf{1}_A+7\cdot\mathbf{1}_B$ where $A=[0,1]$, $B=(2,4]$, and $A\cap B=\varnothing$. Compute $\int f\,dm$. For a simple function $\int\sum c_i\mathbf{1}_{E_i}\,dm=\sum c_i m(E_i)$. So $\int f\,dm=3\cdot m([0,1])+7\cdot m((2,4])=3\cdot 1+7\cdot 2=3+14=17$.

q: Which is the correct proof-step ordering for showing that $f+g$ is measurable when $f,g:(X,\mathcal{A})\to\mathbb{R}$ are measurable?
text: Which is the correct proof-step ordering for showing that $f+g$ is measurable when $f,g:(X,\mathcal{A})\to\mathbb{R}$ are measurable? The standard elementary proof: $f+g>a$ iff $f>a-g$ iff $\exists q\in\mathbb{Q}$ with $f>q>a-g$, giving $\{f+g>a\}=\bigcup_{q\in\mathbb{Q}}(\{f>q\}\cap\{g>a-q\})$. Each factor is measurable; $\mathcal{A}$ is closed under finite intersections and countable unions.

q: For nonnegative measurable $f$, the Lebesgue integral is defined as:
text: For nonnegative measurable $f$, the Lebesgue integral is defined as: For $f\ge 0$, $\int f\,d\mu := \sup\{\int s\,d\mu : 0\le s\le f,\ s\text{ simple}\}$.

q: Compute $\int_0^1 x\,dx$ (Lebesgue measure).
text: Compute $\int_0^1 x\,dx$ (Lebesgue measure). Lebesgue and Riemann integrals agree here: $\int_0^1 x\,dx = [x^2/2]_0^1 = 1/2$.

q: Compute $\int_0^{2\pi} e^{ix}\,dx$ and report as $a+bi$.
text: Compute $\int_0^{2\pi} e^{ix}\,dx$ and report as $a+bi$. $e^{ix}=\cos x+i\sin x$. Both $\int_0^{2\pi}\cos x\,dx$ and $\int_0^{2\pi}\sin x\,dx$ are $0$, so the complex integral is $0+0i$.

q: Which function is Lebesgue integrable on $[0,1]$ but NOT Riemann integrable?
text: Which function is Lebesgue integrable on $[0,1]$ but NOT Riemann integrable? The Dirichlet function is nowhere continuous, so it is not Riemann integrable (upper and lower Riemann sums differ). However, $\mathbb{Q}\cap[0,1]$ is countable hence has Lebesgue measure $0$, so $\int_0^1\mathbf{1}_{\mathbb{Q}}\,dm=0$. It is Lebesgue integrable.

q: Compute $\int_1^e \ln(x)\,dx$ using the Lebesgue integral (equal to the Riemann integral here).
text: Compute $\int_1^e \ln(x)\,dx$ using the Lebesgue integral (equal to the Riemann integral here). Integration by parts: $\int_1^e \ln x\,dx=[x\ln x - x]_1^e=(e\cdot 1-e)-(1\cdot 0-1)=0+1=1$.

q: Let $f_n(x)=n\cdot\mathbf{1}_{(0,1/n]}$. Then $f_n\to 0$ pointwise a.e. yet $\int f_n\,dm=1$ for all $n$. Which hypothesis of the Dominated Convergence Theorem fails?
text: Let $f_n(x)=n\cdot\mathbf{1}_{(0,1/n]}$. Then $f_n\to 0$ pointwise a.e. yet $\int f_n\,dm=1$ for all $n$. Which hypothesis of the Dominated Convergence Theorem fails? Any dominator must satisfy $g(x)\ge n\cdot\mathbf{1}_{(0,1/n]}(x)$ for all $n$, which forces $g(x)\ge 1/x$ near $0$. But $\int_0^1 1/x\,dm=\infty$, so no integrable dominator exists. DCT requires $|f_n|\le g$ for some $g\in L^1$, which fails here.

q: Match each Lebesgue convergence theorem with the precise hypothesis it requires on the sequence $\{f_n\}$ of measurable functions.
text: Match each Lebesgue convergence theorem with the precise hypothesis it requires on the sequence $\{f_n\}$ of measurable functions. MCT: $0\le f_n\uparrow f$ implies $\int f_n\uparrow\int f$. Fatou: $f_n\ge 0$ measurable gives the one-sided inequality $\int\liminf f_n\le\liminf\int f_n$ — strict in general (e.g. travelling bumps). DCT: dominator $g\in L^1$ controls $f_n$ pointwise. Bounded convergence: special case of DCT where the dominator is the constant $M\cdot\mathbf 1_X$, integrable iff $\mu(X)<\infty$. MCT: monotone increasing nonneg. Fatou: just nonneg, gives one-sided $\liminf$. DCT: integrable dominator. Bounded: uniform bound on a finite-measure space.

q: Let $f_n(x)=x^n$ on $[0,1]$. Compute $\int_0^1 f_n(x)\,dx$ in terms of $n$ at $n=9$.
text: Let $f_n(x)=x^n$ on $[0,1]$. Compute $\int_0^1 f_n(x)\,dx$ in terms of $n$ at $n=9$. $\int_0^1 x^n\,dx = 1/(n+1)$, so for $n=9$ the value is $1/10=0.1$.

q: Compute $i^5$ as $a+bi$.
text: Compute $i^5$ as $a+bi$. Powers of $i$ cycle every $4$: $i^5=i^{4+1}=i$.

q: Fatou's lemma states $\int\liminf_n f_n\,d\mu\le\liminf_n\int f_n\,d\mu$ for $f_n\ge 0$. Which sequence shows the inequality can be strict?
text: Fatou's lemma states $\int\liminf_n f_n\,d\mu\le\liminf_n\int f_n\,d\mu$ for $f_n\ge 0$. Which sequence shows the inequality can be strict? $f_n=\mathbf{1}_{[n,n+1]}\to 0$ pointwise, so $\liminf f_n=0$ and $\int\liminf f_n=0$. But $\int f_n=1$ for all $n$, so $\liminf_n\int f_n=1>0$. Strict inequality holds because mass escapes to infinity. Look for fixed-mass bumps whose support slides off toward infinity.

q: Let $g_n(x)=\mathbf{1}_{[0,n]}(x)\cdot e^{-x}$ on $[0,\infty)$. By MCT (the sequence is increasing and nonneg), $\lim_{n\to\infty}\int_0^\infty g_n\,dm$ equals what?
text: Let $g_n(x)=\mathbf{1}_{[0,n]}(x)\cdot e^{-x}$ on $[0,\infty)$. By MCT (the sequence is increasing and nonneg), $\lim_{n\to\infty}\int_0^\infty g_n\,dm$ equals what? $g_n\uparrow e^{-x}$ pointwise, so MCT gives $\lim\int g_n=\int_0^\infty e^{-x}\,dm=[-e^{-x}]_0^\infty=0-(-1)=1$.

q: Which is the correct proof-step order for the Monotone Convergence Theorem (for $0\le f_n\uparrow f$)?
text: Which is the correct proof-step order for the Monotone Convergence Theorem (for $0\le f_n\uparrow f$)? Standard MCT proof: monotonicity gives $\int f_n\le\int f$, so $\limsup\int f_n\le\int f$. For the reverse, fix simple $0\le s\le f$ and $c\in(0,1)$; the sets $E_n=\{f_n\ge cs\}$ increase to $X$; thus $\int f_n\ge c\int_{E_n}s\,d\mu\to c\int s\,d\mu$; let $c\to 1$ and take sup over $s$ to get $\liminf\int f_n\ge\int f$.

q: Which statement is true on a finite-measure space?
text: Which statement is true on a finite-measure space? By Hölder, on finite measure spaces one has $\|f\|_1\le \mu(X)^{1/2}\|f\|_2$, so $L^2\subset L^1$.

q: On $[0,1]$, let $f(x)=x$. Compute $\|f\|_2$.
Which of the following are true about the $L^p$ spaces $L^p(\mathbb{R}, \lambda)$ for $1 \le p < \infty$? Select all that apply.
text: Which of the following are true about the $L^p$ spaces $L^p(\mathbb{R}, \lambda)$ for $1 \le p < \infty$? Select all that apply. Banach (Riesz–Fischer completeness), Hölder, and Minkowski are the three pillars of the $L^p$ theory. $L^2$ is the unique Hilbert space among $L^p$ — its inner product $\langle f,g\rangle = \int f\bar g$ comes from the parallelogram law, which fails for $p \ne 2$. $L^p$ on Lebesgue measure is infinite-dimensional regardless of $\sigma$-finiteness; finite-dimensionality requires a finite atomic measure space. Three classical inequalities define the $L^p$ structure; only one of $\{1,2,p\}$ gives a Hilbert space.

q: On $[1,\infty)$ with Lebesgue measure, $f(x)=x^{-\alpha}$. For which values of $\alpha$ does $f\in L^2[1,\infty)$?
text: On $[1,\infty)$ with Lebesgue measure, $f(x)=x^{-\alpha}$. For which values of $\alpha$ does $f\in L^2[1,\infty)$? $\int_1^\infty x^{-2\alpha}\,dx$ converges iff $2\alpha>1$, i.e., $\alpha>1/2$. This is strictly weaker than $L^1$ membership (which requires $\alpha>1$), illustrating that $L^1\not\subset L^2$ on infinite-measure spaces.

q: Let $f,g\in L^2([0,1])$ with $\|f\|_2=3$ and $\|g\|_2=4$. By the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality, $|\langle f,g\rangle|\le$ what value?
text: Let $f,g\in L^2([0,1])$ with $\|f\|_2=3$ and $\|g\|_2=4$. By the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality, $|\langle f,g\rangle|\le$ what value? Cauchy–Schwarz: $|\langle f,g\rangle|\le\|f\|_2\|g\|_2=3\cdot 4=12$.

q: Which is the correct counterexample showing $L^1([0,1])\not\subset L^2([0,1])$?
text: Which is the correct counterexample showing $L^1([0,1])\not\subset L^2([0,1])$? $f(x)=x^{-1/2}$ satisfies $\int_0^1 x^{-1/2}\,dx=2$, so $f\in L^1$. But $\int_0^1 x^{-1}\,dx=\infty$, so $f\notin L^2$. This demonstrates that on $[0,1]$ (finite measure) the inclusion $L^2\subset L^1$ holds but the reverse fails.

q: The product $\sigma$-algebra $\mathcal{A}\otimes\mathcal{B}$ on $X\times Y$ is:
text: The product $\sigma$-algebra $\mathcal{A}\otimes\mathcal{B}$ on $X\times Y$ is: $\mathcal{A}\otimes\mathcal{B}:=\sigma(\{A\times B:A\in\mathcal{A},B\in\mathcal{B}\})$. Measurable rectangles alone aren't closed under countable operations, hence the generation step.

q: On $[0,1]\times[0,1]$ with Lebesgue $\otimes$ Lebesgue, compute $(m\otimes m)([0,0.5]\times[0,0.4])$.
text: On $[0,1]\times[0,1]$ with Lebesgue $\otimes$ Lebesgue, compute $(m\otimes m)([0,0.5]\times[0,0.4])$. $(m\otimes m)(A\times B)=m(A)\cdot m(B)=0.5\cdot 0.4=0.2$.

q: A measure space $(X,\mathcal{A},\mu)$ is $\sigma$-finite when:
text: A measure space $(X,\mathcal{A},\mu)$ is $\sigma$-finite when: $\sigma$-finiteness says $X=\bigcup_n X_n$ with $\mu(X_n)<\infty$. Lebesgue measure on $\mathbb{R}$ is $\sigma$-finite ($\mathbb{R}=\bigcup_n[-n,n]$) but not finite.

q: Which measure space fails to be $\sigma$-finite, so that product-measure uniqueness via Carathéodory can break?
text: Which measure space fails to be $\sigma$-finite, so that product-measure uniqueness via Carathéodory can break? Counting measure on an uncountable $X$ assigns $\infty$ to every infinite set and $n$ to each $n$-element finite set; a countable union of finite sets is still countable, so $X$ itself cannot be written that way. Uniqueness in the Carathéodory extension can then fail (two distinct measures agreeing on the algebra but disagreeing on the generated $\sigma$-algebra). $\sigma$-finite means $X$ is a countable union of sets of finite measure.

q: On $(\mathbb{R}^2,\mathcal{B}\otimes\mathcal{B},m\otimes m)$, compute the measure of the triangle $\{(x,y):0\le y\le x\le 1\}$.
text: On $(\mathbb{R}^2,\mathcal{B}\otimes\mathcal{B},m\otimes m)$, compute the measure of the triangle $\{(x,y):0\le y\le x\le 1\}$. By Tonelli: $\int_0^1\int_0^x 1\,dy\,dx=\int_0^1 x\,dx=1/2$.

q: Why does $\sigma$-finiteness of both $\mu$ and $\nu$ make $\mu\otimes\nu$ uniquely determined on $\mathcal{A}\otimes\mathcal{B}$?
text:
Why does $\sigma$-finiteness of both $\mu$ and $
u$ make $\mu\otimes
u$ uniquely determined on $\mathcal{A}\otimes\mathcal{B}$? Existence is via Carathéodory applied to the rectangle premeasure on the algebra of finite disjoint rectangle unions. Uniqueness in Carathéodory's extension theorem needs the premeasure to be $\sigma$-finite, which follows from $\sigma$-finiteness of both $\mu$ and $
u$.

q: Tonelli's theorem (for $\sigma$-finite $\mu,\nu$) swaps iterated integrals with no integrability hypothesis, provided:
text: Tonelli's theorem (for $\sigma$-finite $\mu,\nu$) swaps iterated integrals with no integrability hypothesis, provided: Tonelli requires only nonnegativity + joint measurability; all three expressions in $[0,\infty]$ are equal unconditionally. Fubini is the companion statement for complex-valued $f\in L^1(\mu\otimes\nu)$.

q: Compute $\int_0^1\!\int_0^1 xy\,dx\,dy$.
text: Compute $\int_0^1\!\int_0^1 xy\,dx\,dy$. By Tonelli: $\int_0^1 y\,dy\cdot\int_0^1 x\,dx=(1/2)(1/2)=1/4$.

q: Arrange the standard workflow for legitimately swapping iterated integrals of a signed/complex function $f(x,y)$ on a $\sigma$-finite product space.
text: Arrange the standard workflow for legitimately swapping iterated integrals of a signed/complex function $f(x,y)$ on a $\sigma$-finite product space. Standard Fubini–Tonelli workflow: (1) joint measurability is a prerequisite, (2) Tonelli applies unconditionally to $|f|$ in $[0,\infty]$, (3) check finiteness — i.e., whether $f\in L^1$, (4) once $f\in L^1$, Fubini licenses the swap. Skipping the finiteness check can yield genuinely different iterated integrals (the classical $f(x,y)=(x^2-y^2)/(x^2+y^2)^2$ on $[0,1]^2$ is the cautionary tale). Tonelli on $|f|$ first to verify $L^1$, then Fubini on $f$ for the equality.

q: On $X=Y=\mathbb{N}$ with counting measure, $a_{m,n}=1$ if $n=m$, $-1$ if $n=m+1$, else $0$. Which statement is correct?
text: On $X=Y=\mathbb{N}$ with counting measure, $a_{m,n}=1$ if $n=m$, $-1$ if $n=m+1$, else $0$. Which statement is correct? Row sums: for each $m$, $a_{m,m}+a_{m,m+1}=1-1=0$, so $\sum_m 0=0$. Column sums: for $n=0$, only $a_{0,0}=1$ contributes (since $a_{m,0}\ne 0$ only at $m=0$), and for $n\ge 1$, $a_{n,n}+a_{n-1,n}=1-1=0$. Total $=1$. $|a_{m,n}|$ has $\sum=\infty$, so Fubini fails. Sum by rows first, then by columns, and notice the telescoping mismatch.

q: Compute $\int_0^\infty\int_0^\infty e^{-xy}\sin x\,dx\,dy$ assuming absolute integrability allows the swap. (Hint: swap and use $\int_0^\infty e^{-xy}\sin x\,dy=\sin x/x$.)
text: Compute $\int_0^\infty\int_0^\infty e^{-xy}\sin x\,dx\,dy$ assuming absolute integrability allows the swap. (Hint: swap and use $\int_0^\infty e^{-xy}\sin x\,dy=\sin x/x$.) Swap: $\int_0^\infty\sin x\int_0^\infty e^{-xy}\,dy\,dx=\int_0^\infty \frac{\sin x}{x}\,dx=\pi/2$. Absolute integrability in the stated form is delicate here, but the iterated computation yields the classical Dirichlet integral.

q: Which is the correct proof-step ordering for Fubini (for $f\in L^1(\mu\otimes\nu)$, $\sigma$-finite case)?
text: Which is the correct proof-step ordering for Fubini (for $f\in L^1(\mu\otimes\nu)$, $\sigma$-finite case)? Fubini is built up in layers: indicator rectangles → simple functions → nonneg measurable (Tonelli) → integrable by splitting $f^\pm$. The $\sigma$-finiteness hypothesis is used in uniqueness of the product measure and in applying Tonelli to $|f|$ to avoid $\infty-\infty$.

q: Match each decomposition theorem for a signed measure $\nu$ with the structure it produces.
text:
Match each decomposition theorem for a signed measure $
u$ with the structure it produces. Hahn gives a partition $X=P\sqcup N$ into a positive set $P$ and a negative set $N$. Jordan defines $
u^+(E)=
u(E\cap P)$, $
u^-(E)=-
u(E\cap N)$, mutually singular positive measures with $
u=
u^+-
u^-$ and $|
u|=
u^++
u^-$. Lebesgue decomposes any $\sigma$-finite signed $
u$ relative to $\mu$ as $
u_a+
u_s$ ($
u_a\ll\mu$, $
u_s\perp\mu$). Radon–Nikodym then gives $
u_a$ a density $d
u_a/d\mu$. Hahn = positive/negative set partition; Jordan = $\pm$ split into singular positive measures; Lebesgue = absolutely continuous + singular; Radon–Nikodym = density.

q: We say $\nu\ll\mu$ (absolutely continuous) when:
text:
We say $
u\ll\mu$ (absolutely continuous) when: $
u\ll\mu$ iff every $\mu$-null set is also $
u$-null. Radon–Nikodym gives a density $f=d
u/d\mu\ge 0$ with $
u(E)=\int_E f\,d\mu$ when $\mu$ is $\sigma$-finite and $
u\ll\mu$.

q: On $[0,1]$ with Lebesgue $m$, let $d\nu=2x\,dm$. Compute $\nu([0,1])$.
text:
On $[0,1]$ with Lebesgue $m$, let $d
u=2x\,dm$. Compute $
u([0,1])$. $
u([0,1])=\int_0^1 2x\,dm=x^2 ig|_0^1=1$. The density $f(x)=2x$ is the Radon–Nikodym derivative $d
u/dm$.

q: Which is the correct statement of the Lebesgue decomposition theorem?
text:
Which is the correct statement of the Lebesgue decomposition theorem? Lebesgue decomposition: $
u=
u_{ac}+
u_s$ with $
u_{ac}\ll\mu$ (Radon–Nikodym applies) and $
u_s\perp\mu$ (concentrated on a $\mu$-null set). The two pieces are uniquely determined.

q: On $[0,1]$ with Lebesgue $m$, let $d\nu=f\,dm$ with $f(x)=3x^2$. Compute $\nu([0,1/2])$.
Which measure on $[0,1]$ is NOT absolutely continuous with respect to Lebesgue measure $m$?
text: Which measure on $[0,1]$ is NOT absolutely continuous with respect to Lebesgue measure $m$? The Dirac mass $\delta_{1/2}$ concentrates all mass at the single point $\{1/2\}$, which has $m(\{1/2\})=0$. So $m(\{1/2\})=0$ but $\delta_{1/2}(\{1/2\})=1$, violating $\nu\ll m$. $\delta_{1/2}\perp m$ instead.

q: The Riesz–Markov–Kakutani theorem says: every positive linear functional $\Lambda$ on $C_c(X)$ for locally compact Hausdorff $X$ is:
text: The Riesz–Markov–Kakutani theorem says: every positive linear functional $\Lambda$ on $C_c(X)$ for locally compact Hausdorff $X$ is: Riesz representation: positive linear functionals on $C_c(X)$ are exactly integration against regular Borel measures (inner regular on open sets, outer regular on Borel sets, finite on compact sets). The bijection is the bridge from functional analysis to measure theory.

q: On $X=[0,1]$, let $\Lambda(f)=f(1/3)$ for $f\in C([0,1])$. What is $\mu([0,1/2])$ for the representing measure $\mu$?
text: On $X=[0,1]$, let $\Lambda(f)=f(1/3)$ for $f\in C([0,1])$. What is $\mu([0,1/2])$ for the representing measure $\mu$? $\Lambda=\int f\,d\delta_{1/3}$, so $\mu=\delta_{1/3}$. Since $1/3\in[0,1/2]$, $\mu([0,1/2])=1$.

q: Which regularity property of the representing measure is essential to Riesz representation?
text: Which regularity property of the representing measure is essential to Riesz representation? The representing Radon/regular Borel measure $\mu$ satisfies: $\mu(U)=\sup\{\mu(K):K\subset U\text{ compact}\}$ on open $U$, and $\mu(E)=\inf\{\mu(U):E\subset U\text{ open}\}$ on Borel $E$. These conditions pin $\mu$ down uniquely.

q: Which functional on $C_c(\mathbb{R})$ fails to be representable by a Radon measure?
text: Which functional on $C_c(\mathbb{R})$ fails to be representable by a Radon measure? $\Lambda(f)=f'(0)$ is not a positive functional: take $f\ge 0$ with a strict max at $0$, then $f'(0)=0$ or even $\le 0$, so positivity fails. It is a well-defined linear functional on $C^1_c$, but Riesz representation only covers positive linear functionals on $C_c$. One of these is not positive and not continuous on $C_c$ with the sup-norm topology.

q: On $X=[0,1]$, let $\Lambda(f)=\int_0^1 f(x)\cdot 2x\,dx$. What is $\mu([0,1])$ for the representing measure?
text: On $X=[0,1]$, let $\Lambda(f)=\int_0^1 f(x)\cdot 2x\,dx$. What is $\mu([0,1])$ for the representing measure? $\Lambda(f)=\int f\,d\mu$ where $d\mu=2x\,dm$. So $\mu([0,1])=\int_0^1 2x\,dm=1$.

q: Which consequence of Riesz representation is used to build Haar measure on a locally compact group $G$?
text: Which consequence of Riesz representation is used to build Haar measure on a locally compact group $G$? One shows that $C_c(G)$ admits a translation-invariant positive linear functional $\Lambda$ (existence by a Hahn–Banach / tangent-functional argument), then Riesz representation upgrades $\Lambda$ to a left-Haar measure.
