q: Match each locally compact abelian group with its Pontryagin dual $\widehat G$.
text: Match each locally compact abelian group with its Pontryagin dual $\widehat G$. $\mathbb{R}$ and finite cyclic groups are self-dual; $\mathbb{T}$ and $\mathbb{Z}$ are dual to each other (Fourier series ↔ Fourier coefficients). The general Plancherel theorem on $G$ specializes to all the classical Fourier theorems.

q: What does the Plancherel theorem on a locally compact abelian $G$ say?
text: What does the Plancherel theorem on a locally compact abelian $G$ say? With Haar measures normalized compatibly (via the Plancherel measure on $\widehat G$), the Fourier transform is a unitary isomorphism — the same statement as classical Plancherel on $\mathbb{R}$ or Parseval on $\mathbb{T}$, in one breath. With Haar measures normalized compatibly (via the Plancherel measure on $\widehat G$), the Fourier transform is a unitary isomorphism — the same statement as classical Plancherel on $\mathbb{R}$ or…

title: Classification: elliptic / parabolic / hyperbolic
blurb: Second-order linear PDEs $a u_{xx}+2b u_{xy}+c u_{yy}+\cdots=0$ split by the discriminant $b^2-ac$: elliptic ($ 0$, wave). The sign controls characteristic curves and the well-posed boundary data.

title: Heat equation and the heat kernel
blurb: $u_t=\Delta u$ smooths data instantly: the fundamental solution $K_t(x)=(4\pi t)^{-n/2}e^{-|x|^2/4t}$ converts $\delta_0$ initial data into a Gaussian whose width scales as $\sqrt{t}$, and the maximum principle keeps solutions trapped between their initial and boundary extremes.

title: Wave equation and d'Alembert
blurb: $u_{tt}=c^2\Delta u$ has finite speed $c$: in 1D, d'Alembert writes $u(x,t)=\tfrac12[f(x-ct)+f(x+ct)]+\tfrac{1}{2c}\!\int_{x-ct}^{x+ct}\!g$, two travelling waves carrying the data along characteristics; in odd $n\ge3$ Huygens' principle gives sharp wavefronts.

title: Laplace equation and the Dirichlet problem
blurb: $\Delta u=0$ is the equilibrium of $u_t=\Delta u$: solutions enjoy the mean-value property, hit their extremes only on $\partial\Omega$, and the Dirichlet/Neumann problems prescribe boundary value or normal flux. On the disk, the Poisson kernel writes the unique solution as a boundary integral.

title: Weak solutions and Galerkin approximation
blurb: Multiply $-\Delta u=f$ by a test function and integrate by parts: a weak solution lives in $H^1_0(\Omega)$ and satisfies $\int\nabla u\cdot\nabla v=\int fv$ for every test $v$. Galerkin's method picks finite-dimensional subspaces, solves the projected system, and passes to the limit.

title: Lax–Milgram, elliptic regularity, embedding
blurb: Coercivity + continuity of the bilinear form gives a unique weak solution by Lax–Milgram; elliptic regularity then trades smoothness of $f$ for smoothness of $u$ ($f\in H^k\Rightarrow u\in H^{k+2}$), and Sobolev embedding turns enough weak derivatives into classical continuity.

q: Classify $u_{xx} + 4u_{xy} + 3u_{yy} = 0$ by its discriminant $b^2 - ac$.
text: Classify $u_{xx} + 4u_{xy} + 3u_{yy} = 0$ by its discriminant $b^2 - ac$. $b^2-ac = 4 - 3 = 1 > 0$, so the equation is hyperbolic — like the wave equation, with two real characteristic directions. Read off $a=1$, $b=2$, $c=3$ from the symmetric form $a u_{xx}+2b u_{xy}+c u_{yy}$, then compute $b^2-ac$.

q: Which model PDE is the canonical parabolic example?
text: Which model PDE is the canonical parabolic example? The heat equation $u_t=\Delta u$ is parabolic. Laplace is elliptic and the wave equation is hyperbolic. Parabolic means discriminant zero; that is the case when one of the second derivatives is replaced by a first.

q: True or false: an elliptic equation has real characteristic curves.
text: True or false: an elliptic equation has real characteristic curves. Elliptic means $b^2-ac<0$, so the characteristic polynomial has no real roots — that is precisely why elliptic problems take Dirichlet/Neumann data on a closed boundary rather than initial data along a curve. Characteristic directions are roots of $a\lambda^2 - 2b\lambda + c = 0$.

q: What is the fundamental solution (heat kernel) of $u_t = \Delta u$ on $\mathbb{R}^n$?
text: What is the fundamental solution (heat kernel) of $u_t = \Delta u$ on $\mathbb{R}^n$? The heat kernel is the Gaussian $K_t(x)=(4\pi t)^{-n/2}\exp(-|x|^2/4t)$, the unique solution with $K_0=\delta_0$. The width $\sqrt{2t}$ shows the diffusive scaling. The Gaussian variance grows linearly in $t$: $\sigma^2 = 2t$.

q: Suppose $u$ solves $u_t=\Delta u$ on a bounded domain with continuous data. The maximum principle says the maximum of $u$ is attained:
text: Suppose $u$ solves $u_t=\Delta u$ on a bounded domain with continuous data. The maximum principle says the maximum of $u$ is attained: The parabolic maximum principle: $\max u$ is taken on $\{t=0\}\cup(\partial\Omega\times[0,T])$. Diffusion smooths and dampens, never amplifies. Heat does not spontaneously create hot spots — it can only inherit them.

q: If the initial data is the delta $\delta_0$, the solution at time $t>0$ is:
text: If the initial data is the delta $\delta_0$, the solution at time $t>0$ is: $u(x,t)=K_t*\delta_0=K_t(x)$, a Gaussian with variance $2t$. Information from a point spreads instantly to all of $\mathbb{R}^n$ — heat has infinite propagation speed. Convolution with $\delta_0$ is the identity.

q: On $\mathbb{R}$, the wave equation $u_{tt}=c^2 u_{xx}$ with data $u(x,0)=f$, $u_t(x,0)=0$ has solution:
text: On $\mathbb{R}$, the wave equation $u_{tt}=c^2 u_{xx}$ with data $u(x,0)=f$, $u_t(x,0)=0$ has solution: With zero initial velocity, $u(x,t)=\tfrac12[f(x-ct)+f(x+ct)]$ — the initial profile splits in half, each half traveling at speed $c$ in opposite directions. d'Alembert: each datum splits into a left- and right-moving copy.

q: True or false: the wave equation has finite speed of propagation.
text: True or false: the wave equation has finite speed of propagation. Yes — the value $u(x_0,t_0)$ depends only on data inside the backward light cone $|x-x_0|\le c t_0$. Disturbances propagate at speed at most $c$. Compare with the heat kernel, which is everywhere positive for $t>0$.

q: Huygens' principle (sharp wavefronts) holds in:
text: Huygens' principle (sharp wavefronts) holds in: In odd $n\ge 3$ the solution at $(x_0,t_0)$ depends only on data on the sphere $|x-x_0|=ct_0$, giving sharp wavefronts. In even dimensions there is a residual tail inside the cone. In $n=2$ the disturbance leaves a tail; in $n=3$ it does not.

q: A function harmonic on $\Omega$ and continuous on $\overline{\Omega}$ attains its maximum:
text: A function harmonic on $\Omega$ and continuous on $\overline{\Omega}$ attains its maximum: The strong maximum principle: a non-constant harmonic function takes its max only on the boundary. This is what makes the Dirichlet problem (prescribe $u|_{\partial\Omega}$) well-posed. Mean-value property forces $u$ at any interior point to equal the average over a surrounding sphere.

q: On the unit disk, the unique harmonic extension of a continuous boundary value $g$ is given by:
text: On the unit disk, the unique harmonic extension of a continuous boundary value $g$ is given by: The Poisson integral formula is the explicit solution of the Dirichlet problem on the disk. As $r\to 1$ the kernel concentrates on the boundary point — recovering $g$. The Poisson kernel $P_r(\theta)=(1-r^2)/(1-2r\cos\theta+r^2)$ is the answer.

q: The Neumann problem $\Delta u=0$, $\partial_\nu u=g$ on a bounded domain is solvable only when:
text: The Neumann problem $\Delta u=0$, $\partial_\nu u=g$ on a bounded domain is solvable only when: By divergence, $\int_{\partial\Omega}\partial_\nu u\,dS = \int_\Omega \Delta u\,dx = 0$, so the prescribed flux must have zero mean. The solution is then unique up to an additive constant. Integrate $\Delta u=0$ over $\Omega$ and use the divergence theorem.

q: A weak solution of $-\Delta u = f$ with zero Dirichlet data lives in:
text: A weak solution of $-\Delta u = f$ with zero Dirichlet data lives in: $H^1_0(\Omega)$ is exactly the closure of $C_c^\infty(\Omega)$ in $H^1$: one weak derivative in $L^2$ and zero boundary trace. It is the natural energy space. Integration by parts moves one derivative onto the test function — so we need one weak derivative in $L^2$, plus zero trace.

q: The weak formulation of $-\Delta u=f$ on $H^1_0$ reads:
text: The weak formulation of $-\Delta u=f$ on $H^1_0$ reads: Integration by parts gives $-\int(\Delta u)v = \int\nabla u\cdot\nabla v$ since $v$ vanishes on $\partial\Omega$. This is the energy form $a(u,v)=\langle f,v\rangle$. Integrate $-\Delta u\cdot v$ by parts; the boundary term vanishes because $v\in H^1_0$.

q: Galerkin's method approximates the weak solution by:
text: Galerkin's method approximates the weak solution by: Galerkin: choose $V_n=\mathrm{span}(\phi_1,\ldots,\phi_n)$, find $u_n\in V_n$ with $a(u_n,\phi_j)=\langle f,\phi_j\rangle$ for all $j$, and pass $n\to\infty$. This is the abstract template for finite elements. Pick basis functions; reduce the PDE to a linear system; let the basis grow.

q: Lax–Milgram delivers a unique weak solution when the bilinear form $a$ is:
text: Lax–Milgram delivers a unique weak solution when the bilinear form $a$ is: Lax–Milgram: continuous + coercive bilinear form on a Hilbert space is invertible. Symmetry is not needed — Lax–Milgram is the non-symmetric Riesz representation theorem. Coercive means $a(u,u)\ge\alpha\|u\|^2$ for some $\alpha>0$.

q: Elliptic regularity says: if $-\Delta u = f$ weakly with $f\in H^k$ on a smooth domain, then:
text: Elliptic regularity says: if $-\Delta u = f$ weakly with $f\in H^k$ on a smooth domain, then: Interior elliptic regularity: $u$ has two more weak derivatives than $f$. Bootstrapping: if $f\in C^\infty$ then $u\in C^\infty$. Each Laplacian eats two derivatives — running the equation backwards gains two.

q: For $\Omega\subset\mathbb{R}^n$ bounded and smooth, when does $H^k(\Omega)\hookrightarrow C^0(\overline{\Omega})$ hold?
text: For $\Omega\subset\mathbb{R}^n$ bounded and smooth, when does $H^k(\Omega)\hookrightarrow C^0(\overline{\Omega})$ hold? Sobolev: $H^k\hookrightarrow C^0$ when $k>n/2$ (with $p=2$). In 1D, $k=1$ suffices; in 3D one needs $k\ge 2$. This is how weak solutions become classical. Sobolev embedding trades $kp$ derivatives in $L^p$ for $\lfloor k - n/p\rfloor$ classical derivatives.

title: Laplace's equation
blurb: A $C^2$ function $u\colon\Omega\subseteq\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}$ is harmonic when $\Delta u = \sum \partial_{x_i}^2 u = 0$. In two dimensions every real or imaginary part of a holomorphic function is harmonic, and every harmonic $u$ on a simply connected domain admits a harmonic conjugate.

title: Mean value property
blurb: A continuous $u$ is harmonic iff for every closed ball $\overline{B(x,r)}\subset\Omega$ the value $u(x)$ equals the average over the sphere (and equivalently over the ball). The MVP characterises harmonicity and forces $u\in C^\infty$.

title: Maximum principle
blurb: Weak form: a harmonic $u$ on a bounded $\Omega$ attains $\sup u$ on $\partial\Omega$. Strong form: an interior maximum forces $u$ to be constant on the connected component. Together they yield uniqueness for the Dirichlet problem.

title: Poisson kernel
blurb: The disk's Dirichlet problem has the explicit solution $u(re^{i\theta}) = \tfrac{1}{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi} P_r(\theta-\varphi)\,g(\varphi)\,d\varphi$ with $P_r(\theta) = \tfrac{1-r^2}{1-2r\cos\theta+r^2}$. Boundary values are recovered $L^p$-a.e. by Fatou's theorem.

title: Harnack's inequality
blurb: For positive harmonic $u$ on $B(0,R)$ and $|x|=r<R$: $\tfrac{R-r}{R+r}u(0) \le u(x) \le \tfrac{R+r}{R-r}u(0)$. Consequence: a positive harmonic function on $\mathbb{R}^n$ is constant (Liouville for harmonic functions).

title: Subharmonic functions and Perron's method
blurb: An upper semicontinuous $v$ with $\Delta v \ge 0$ (or equivalently $v(x)\le$ ball average) is subharmonic; max-principle still holds. Perron's method solves the Dirichlet problem on any regular domain by taking the supremum of the family of subharmonic functions bounded above by the boundary data.

q: Which of the following functions on $\mathbb{R}^2$ is harmonic?
text: Which of the following functions on $\mathbb{R}^2$ is harmonic? $u = x^2 - y^2$ has $u_{xx}=2$, $u_{yy}=-2$, so $\Delta u = 0$. It is the real part of $z^2$. The first option has $\Delta u = 4$, the third $\Delta u = 2y$, the fourth $\Delta u = 2e^{x+y}$. Compute $u_{xx} + u_{yy}$ and look for the one that vanishes.

q: If $u(x,y) = \log(x^2 + y^2)$ on $\mathbb{R}^2 \setminus \{0\}$, compute $\Delta u$ at the point $(1, 0)$.
text: If $u(x,y) = \log(x^2 + y^2)$ on $\mathbb{R}^2 \setminus \{0\}$, compute $\Delta u$ at the point $(1, 0)$. In polar form $u = 2\log r$, and the radial Laplacian gives $\Delta(2\log r) = 2(r^{-1})' + 2(r^{-1})\cdot r^{-1} = -2r^{-2} + 2r^{-2} = 0$. So $u$ is harmonic on the punctured plane (its singularity at the origin is the fundamental solution). $\log(x^2+y^2) = 2\log r$ in polar coordinates, and $\Delta(\log r) = 0$ off the origin.

q: If $f = u + iv$ is holomorphic on a connected open $\Omega \subseteq \mathbb{C}$, which statement is correct?
text: If $f = u + iv$ is holomorphic on a connected open $\Omega \subseteq \mathbb{C}$, which statement is correct? From $u_x = v_y$ and $u_y = -v_x$, differentiating gives $u_{xx} = v_{yx}$ and $u_{yy} = -v_{xy}$, so $\Delta u = 0$ (and similarly $\Delta v = 0$). The pair $(u,v)$ are called harmonic conjugates. Differentiate the Cauchy–Riemann equations $u_x = v_y$, $u_y = -v_x$ once more.

q: The mean value property says that if $u$ is harmonic on $\Omega$ and $\overline{B(x_0,r)} \subset \Omega$, then $u(x_0)$ equals…
text: The mean value property says that if $u$ is harmonic on $\Omega$ and $\overline{B(x_0,r)} \subset \Omega$, then $u(x_0)$ equals… The MVP states $u(x_0) = \frac{1}{|\partial B(x_0,r)|}\int_{\partial B(x_0,r)} u\,dS$, and equivalently the volume average over the ball. This holds for every admissible ball and characterises harmonicity for continuous $u$. Harmonic = balanced — at the center $u$ takes the same value as the average of its neighbours.

q: Let $u(x,y) = x^2 - y^2$ (harmonic on $\mathbb{R}^2$). What is the average value of $u$ on the circle of radius $2$ centered at the origin?
text: Let $u(x,y) = x^2 - y^2$ (harmonic on $\mathbb{R}^2$). What is the average value of $u$ on the circle of radius $2$ centered at the origin? By the MVP the average of a harmonic function on any sphere centered at a point is the value at that point. Here $u(0,0)=0$, so the average is $0$. (Direct computation with $x = 2\cos\theta$, $y = 2\sin\theta$ gives $\int_0^{2\pi} 4\cos 2\theta \, d\theta / (2\pi) = 0$, confirming.) Use the MVP: the circular average equals $u(0,0)$.

q: If a continuous $u$ on an open set satisfies the mean value property on every admissible ball, what can we conclude?
text: If a continuous $u$ on an open set satisfies the mean value property on every admissible ball, what can we conclude? The mean value property is equivalent to harmonicity for continuous functions, and any harmonic $u$ is automatically $C^\infty$ (in fact real-analytic) by the Poisson integral formula or elliptic regularity. MVP characterises harmonicity, and harmonic functions enjoy elliptic regularity.

q: Suppose $u$ is harmonic on a bounded open $\Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ and continuous on $\overline{\Omega}$. Where can $u$ attain its maximum?
text: Suppose $u$ is harmonic on a bounded open $\Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ and continuous on $\overline{\Omega}$. Where can $u$ attain its maximum? The weak max principle gives $\sup_{\overline\Omega} u = \sup_{\partial\Omega} u$. The strong form sharpens this: if $u$ attains its supremum at some interior point, then $u$ is constant on that component. Hence non-constant harmonic functions take their max only on the boundary. Strong maximum principle: an interior max forces constancy on the connected component.

q: Two harmonic functions $u_1, u_2$ on a bounded $\Omega$ are continuous on $\overline{\Omega}$ and agree on $\partial\Omega$. What follows?
text: Two harmonic functions $u_1, u_2$ on a bounded $\Omega$ are continuous on $\overline{\Omega}$ and agree on $\partial\Omega$. What follows? The difference $w = u_1 - u_2$ is harmonic on $\Omega$ and vanishes on $\partial\Omega$. By the weak max principle (applied to $\pm w$), $\sup |w| \le \sup_{\partial\Omega}|w| = 0$, so $w \equiv 0$. This is uniqueness for the Dirichlet problem. Apply the maximum principle to $u_1 - u_2$, which is harmonic and zero on $\partial\Omega$.

q: $u$ is harmonic on the open unit disk and continuous on the closed disk, with boundary values $u(e^{i\theta}) = 3 + 5\cos\theta$. Determine $u(0)$.
text: $u$ is harmonic on the open unit disk and continuous on the closed disk, with boundary values $u(e^{i\theta}) = 3 + 5\cos\theta$. Determine $u(0)$. By the MVP, $u(0) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi}(3 + 5\cos\theta)\,d\theta = 3$. Equivalently, $u(z) = 3 + 5\,\mathrm{Re}(z) = 3 + \tfrac{5}{2}(z+\bar z)$ is the harmonic extension and $u(0)=3$. Use the MVP at the center of the disk; integrate the boundary data over $[0, 2\pi]$ and divide by $2\pi$.

q: The Poisson kernel on the unit disk is $P_r(\theta) = \dfrac{1-r^2}{1 - 2r\cos\theta + r^2}$ for $0\le r<1$. Which property does it satisfy?
text: The Poisson kernel on the unit disk is $P_r(\theta) = \dfrac{1-r^2}{1 - 2r\cos\theta + r^2}$ for $0\le r<1$. Which property does it satisfy? Direct integration gives $\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi} P_r\,d\theta = 1$, and $1 - 2r\cos\theta + r^2 = |1 - re^{i\theta}|^2 > 0$ shows positivity. As $r\to 1$, $P_r$ concentrates at $\theta=0$, recovering the boundary data of a continuous $g$ pointwise. It is an approximate identity: a positive kernel of total mass 1 concentrating at $\theta = 0$ as $r \to 1$.

q: The Dirichlet problem on the unit disk has boundary data $g(\theta) = \cos(2\theta)$. The Poisson integral solution evaluated at $z = re^{i\theta}$ is $u(re^{i\theta}) = r^2\cos(2\theta)$. Compute $u(\tfrac{1}{2}, 0)$.
text: The Dirichlet problem on the unit disk has boundary data $g(\theta) = \cos(2\theta)$. The Poisson integral solution evaluated at $z = re^{i\theta}$ is $u(re^{i\theta}) = r^2\cos(2\theta)$. Compute $u(\tfrac{1}{2}, 0)$. Plug in $r=\tfrac{1}{2}$, $\theta=0$: $u = (\tfrac{1}{2})^2 \cdot \cos 0 = \tfrac{1}{4}$. More generally, the Poisson integral acts on $e^{in\theta}$ as multiplication by $r^{|n|}$, so $\cos(n\theta) = \tfrac{1}{2}(e^{in\theta}+e^{-in\theta})$ extends to $r^n\cos(n\theta)$. $z = \tfrac{1}{2}$ means $r = \tfrac{1}{2}$, $\theta = 0$.

q: What does Fatou's theorem say about the Poisson integral $u$ of an $L^1$ boundary function $g$ on the unit circle?
text: What does Fatou's theorem say about the Poisson integral $u$ of an $L^1$ boundary function $g$ on the unit circle? Fatou's theorem: for $g\in L^1$ on the circle, the Poisson integral $u = P*g$ converges nontangentially (in particular radially) to $g$ at almost every boundary point. Continuity is not needed — only $L^1$. Boundary recovery is almost everywhere, not everywhere — measure-theoretic limits.

q: Let $u$ be positive and harmonic on the disk $B(0, R)$ in $\mathbb{R}^2$, with $u(0) = 1$. Use Harnack's inequality at $r = R/2$ to give the largest possible value of $u(x)$ for $|x| = R/2$.
text: Let $u$ be positive and harmonic on the disk $B(0, R)$ in $\mathbb{R}^2$, with $u(0) = 1$. Use Harnack's inequality at $r = R/2$ to give the largest possible value of $u(x)$ for $|x| = R/2$. Harnack: $u(x) \le \tfrac{R+r}{R-r}u(0) = \tfrac{R + R/2}{R - R/2}\cdot 1 = \tfrac{3R/2}{R/2} = 3$. Sharpness is achieved (in the limit) by Poisson kernels with mass concentrated near a single boundary point. Plug $r = R/2$ into the Harnack bound $\tfrac{R+r}{R-r} u(0)$.

q: A positive harmonic function on all of $\mathbb{R}^n$ must be…
text: A positive harmonic function on all of $\mathbb{R}^n$ must be… For positive harmonic $u$ on $\mathbb{R}^n$, Harnack on $B(0,R)$ gives $\tfrac{R-r}{R+r}u(0) \le u(x) \le \tfrac{R+r}{R-r}u(0)$ for $|x|<R$. Letting $R\to\infty$ with $r$ fixed pinches both bounds to $u(0)$, so $u\equiv u(0)$. This is the harmonic Liouville theorem. Apply Harnack on balls of radius $R \to \infty$.

q: Why does Harnack's inequality fail without the positivity assumption?
text: Why does Harnack's inequality fail without the positivity assumption? Harnack bounds are multiplicative in $u(0)$. If $u$ changes sign, $u(0)$ may equal $0$ while $u$ is large elsewhere; the bound $u(x) \le C\cdot u(0) = 0$ is then false. The proof via the Poisson kernel uses positivity of the kernel to compare oscillation. Consider $u(x,y) = x$ on the disk: $u(0)=0$ but $u$ ranges over $[-1,1]$.

q: A function $v$ is subharmonic on $\Omega$ when…
text: A function $v$ is subharmonic on $\Omega$ when… Subharmonicity is $\Delta v \ge 0$ (sign convention: think of $-\Delta$ as positive operator). Equivalently, an upper semicontinuous $v$ is subharmonic iff $v(x) \le \frac{1}{|B|}\int_B v$ for every admissible ball. Every $|f|^p$ for holomorphic $f$ is subharmonic — a key source of examples. Subharmonic = below harmonic majorants. Replace 'equals' in the MVP with 'less than or equal'.

q: Perron's method constructs a candidate Dirichlet solution as…
text: Perron's method constructs a candidate Dirichlet solution as… Perron defines $u(x) = \sup\{v(x) : v \text{ subharmonic on } \Omega,\ \limsup_{y\to\zeta} v(y) \le g(\zeta)\ \forall \zeta\in\partial\Omega\}$. Local 'harmonic lifting' on small balls (replace $v$ by its Poisson integral) shows $u$ is harmonic. Boundary regularity $u(\zeta)=g(\zeta)$ requires a barrier at $\zeta$ — domains where every boundary point has a barrier are called regular. Build the largest subharmonic function below the boundary data; harmonicity follows from sub-mean-value pushforward.

q: Which statement about subharmonic functions and the maximum principle is correct?
text: Which statement about subharmonic functions and the maximum principle is correct? Sub-mean-value implies the same max principle as for harmonic functions: $\sup v$ is attained on the boundary, and an interior max forces local constancy. (Sub' for max — 'sub'harmonic functions sit below harmonic ones, and so can only push their values up to the boundary.) This is exactly the property Perron's method exploits. The MVP-style inequality $v(x) \le$ ball average drives the max principle the same way.

title: Spectrum of a bounded operator
blurb: For $T\in B(H)$, $\sigma(T)=\{\lambda\in\mathbb{C} : T-\lambda I \text{ is not invertible}\}$ is a non-empty compact subset of the closed disk of radius $\|T\|$, decomposing into point, continuous, and residual parts.

title: Spectrum of self-adjoint operators
blurb: Self-adjoint operators have $\sigma(T)\subset\mathbb{R}$, and the spectral radius equals the operator norm: $r(T)=\|T\|$. Residual spectrum is empty.

title: Spectral theorem (compact self-adjoint)
blurb: A compact self-adjoint operator $K$ on a separable Hilbert space admits an orthonormal eigenbasis $(\varphi_n)$ with real eigenvalues $\lambda_n\to 0$, and $K=\sum_n\lambda_n\langle\varphi_n,\cdot\rangle\varphi_n$.

title: Spectral theorem (bounded self-adjoint)
blurb: Every bounded self-adjoint operator $T$ admits a unique projection-valued measure $E$ on $\sigma(T)$ such that $T=\int_{\sigma(T)}\lambda\,dE_\lambda$, giving a continuous functional calculus $f\mapsto f(T)$.

title: Unbounded self-adjoint operators
blurb: Densely defined closed operators (Laplacian, momentum, position) admit a spectral resolution $T=\int\lambda\,dE_\lambda$ on their domain; Stone's theorem then identifies self-adjoint $T$ with strongly continuous unitary groups $e^{itT}$.

title: Applications: quantum mechanics, PDE, harmonic analysis
blurb: Observables in QM are self-adjoint operators whose spectra are measurable energies; eigenvalue problems for elliptic PDE invoke the compact spectral theorem; Fourier transform diagonalizes translation-invariant operators.

q: Which set is $\lambda$ in the spectrum $\sigma(T)$ of a bounded operator $T$?
text: Which set is $\lambda$ in the spectrum $\sigma(T)$ of a bounded operator $T$? The spectrum is the set of $\lambda$ for which $T-\lambda I$ fails to be invertible as an element of $B(H)$. Choices (a) and (d) describe the point spectrum (eigenvalues), which is generally a proper subset of $\sigma(T)$. On infinite-dimensional spaces, $T-\lambda I$ can be injective without being invertible.

q: On $\ell^2(\mathbb{N})$, the right shift $S(x_1,x_2,\dots)=(0,x_1,x_2,\dots)$ has what spectrum?
text: On $\ell^2(\mathbb{N})$, the right shift $S(x_1,x_2,\dots)=(0,x_1,x_2,\dots)$ has what spectrum? The right shift has no eigenvalues (point spectrum is empty), but $\sigma(S)$ equals the closed unit disk: every $|\lambda|<1$ lies in the residual spectrum (range not dense), and $|\lambda|=1$ in the continuous spectrum. By contrast the left shift has the open disk in point spectrum. $S$ is an isometry so $\|S\|=1$, and $\sigma(S)$ is closed inside the disk of radius $\|S\|$.

q: Why is $\sigma(T)$ always non-empty for $T\in B(H)$?
text: Why is $\sigma(T)$ always non-empty for $T\in B(H)$? If $\sigma(T)=\emptyset$ then the resolvent $R(\lambda)=(T-\lambda I)^{-1}$ is entire as a $B(H)$-valued function, and $R(\lambda)\to 0$ as $|\lambda|\to\infty$. Liouville (in the Banach-space-valued form) forces $R\equiv 0$, contradicting $R(\lambda)(T-\lambda I)=I$. So $\sigma(T)$ is non-empty. Apply Liouville's theorem to the operator-valued resolvent.

q: If $T=T^*$ on a Hilbert space, what does $\sigma(T)$ look like?
text: If $T=T^*$ on a Hilbert space, what does $\sigma(T)$ look like? If $T=T^*$ and $\lambda=a+ib$ with $b\ne 0$, then $\|(T-\lambda)\varphi\|^2=\|(T-a)\varphi\|^2+b^2\|\varphi\|^2\ge b^2\|\varphi\|^2$, so $T-\lambda$ is bounded below and has closed range; the same holds for $T-\bar\lambda=(T-\lambda)^*$, so the range is dense, hence $T-\lambda$ is invertible. Therefore $\sigma(T)\subset\mathbb{R}$. Use $\langle T\varphi,\varphi\rangle=\langle\varphi,T\varphi\rangle$ to constrain Im $\lambda$.

q: For self-adjoint $T$, the spectral radius $r(T)=\sup_{\lambda\in\sigma(T)}|\lambda|$ equals:
text: For self-adjoint $T$, the spectral radius $r(T)=\sup_{\lambda\in\sigma(T)}|\lambda|$ equals: For self-adjoint $T$, $\|T^2\|=\|T\|^2$ (because $\|T^2\|=\|T^*T\|=\|T\|^2$), and inductively $\|T^{2^k}\|=\|T\|^{2^k}$. Thus $r(T)=\lim\|T^n\|^{1/n}=\|T\|$. (For general operators only $r(T)\le\|T\|$.) Combine the spectral radius formula $r(T)=\lim\|T^n\|^{1/n}$ with $\|T^2\|=\|T\|^2$ for self-adjoint $T$.

q: Which parts of the spectrum can a bounded self-adjoint operator have?
text: Which parts of the spectrum can a bounded self-adjoint operator have? Self-adjoint operators have empty residual spectrum: if $\lambda\in\mathbb{R}$ and $T-\lambda$ has range not dense, then $\ker(T-\lambda)^*=\ker(T-\lambda)\ne 0$, so $\lambda$ is actually in the point spectrum, contradiction. Point and continuous spectrum are both possible. Use that $\overline{\mathrm{Ran}(T-\lambda)}^\perp = \ker(T-\bar\lambda)$ when $T$ is self-adjoint and $\lambda\in\mathbb{R}$.

q: A compact self-adjoint operator $K\ne 0$ on a separable Hilbert space admits:
text: A compact self-adjoint operator $K\ne 0$ on a separable Hilbert space admits: By the spectral theorem for compact self-adjoint operators, $K=\sum_n\lambda_n\langle\varphi_n,\cdot\rangle\varphi_n$ with $(\varphi_n)$ orthonormal eigenvectors, $\lambda_n\in\mathbb{R}$, and $\lambda_n\to 0$. The eigenspace at 0 may be infinite-dimensional (the kernel). Riesz–Schauder forces a discrete non-zero spectrum.

q: Why must each non-zero eigenvalue $\lambda_n$ of a compact operator have finite-dimensional eigenspace?
text: Why must each non-zero eigenvalue $\lambda_n$ of a compact operator have finite-dimensional eigenspace? On the eigenspace $E_{\lambda_n}$, $K$ acts as multiplication by $\lambda_n\ne 0$. If $E_{\lambda_n}$ were infinite-dimensional, the unit ball (closed and bounded but not compact) would map under $K$ to a bounded set whose closure contains $\lambda_n\cdot$(unit ball), which fails to be compact, contradicting compactness of $K$. On the eigenspace, $K$ acts as a non-zero scalar.

q: The integral operator $(Kf)(x)=\int_0^1 k(x,y)f(y)\,dy$ on $L^2[0,1]$ is compact and self-adjoint when $k$ is:
text: The integral operator $(Kf)(x)=\int_0^1 k(x,y)f(y)\,dy$ on $L^2[0,1]$ is compact and self-adjoint when $k$ is: If $k\in L^2([0,1]^2)$, the integral operator is Hilbert–Schmidt with $\|K\|_{HS}^2=\iint|k(x,y)|^2\,dx\,dy$, hence compact. Self-adjointness $\langle Kf,g\rangle=\langle f,Kg\rangle$ is equivalent to $k(x,y)=\overline{k(y,x)}$. Hilbert–Schmidt $\Rightarrow$ compact; symmetric kernel $\Rightarrow$ self-adjoint.

q: The bounded spectral theorem expresses a self-adjoint operator $T$ as:
text: The bounded spectral theorem expresses a self-adjoint operator $T$ as: Every bounded self-adjoint operator admits a unique projection-valued measure $E$ supported on $\sigma(T)\subset\mathbb{R}$ with $T=\int\lambda\,dE_\lambda$. The discrete sum form is the special case where the spectrum is a finite or countable set of eigenvalues (e.g. compact). Functional calculus produces a PVM, not a discrete sum, when the spectrum is continuous.

q: If $T$ is the multiplication operator $(Tf)(x)=x\cdot f(x)$ on $L^2[0,1]$, what is $\sigma(T)$ and the PVM?
text: If $T$ is the multiplication operator $(Tf)(x)=x\cdot f(x)$ on $L^2[0,1]$, what is $\sigma(T)$ and the PVM? $T$ is self-adjoint with purely continuous spectrum $\sigma(T)=[0,1]$. The associated PVM is $E_\Omega f = \mathbf{1}_\Omega(x) f(x)$ for Borel $\Omega\subset[0,1]$, and $T=\int_0^1\lambda\,dE_\lambda$. Every bounded self-adjoint operator is unitarily equivalent to such a multiplication operator on some $L^2(\mu)$. For multiplication operators, the PVM is multiplication by indicator functions.

q: The continuous functional calculus $f\mapsto f(T)$ for $f\in C(\sigma(T))$ satisfies:
text: The continuous functional calculus $f\mapsto f(T)$ for $f\in C(\sigma(T))$ satisfies: For $T$ self-adjoint (or normal), the map $f\mapsto f(T)$ is an isometric *-homomorphism from $C(\sigma(T))$ to $C^*(T,1)\subset B(H)$: $\|f(T)\|=\sup_{\lambda\in\sigma(T)}|f(\lambda)|$. This is the Gelfand–Naimark theorem applied to the unital commutative $C^*$-algebra generated by $T$. The Gelfand transform is an isometric *-isomorphism.

q: A symmetric operator $A$ ($\langle A\varphi,\psi\rangle=\langle\varphi,A\psi\rangle$ for $\varphi,\psi\in\mathrm{Dom}(A)$) is self-adjoint when:
text: A symmetric operator $A$ ($\langle A\varphi,\psi\rangle=\langle\varphi,A\psi\rangle$ for $\varphi,\psi\in\mathrm{Dom}(A)$) is self-adjoint when: Symmetric ($A\subset A^*$) is weaker than self-adjoint ($A=A^*$). The latter requires the additional domain equality $\mathrm{Dom}(A)=\mathrm{Dom}(A^*)$. Symmetric extensions of $A$ correspond to self-adjoint extensions only when defect indices match — a delicate issue famously solved by von Neumann. For unbounded operators, domain matters as much as the formula.

q: The Laplacian $-\Delta$ on $L^2[0,1]$ with Dirichlet boundary conditions ($u(0)=u(1)=0$) is self-adjoint with eigenvalues:
text: The Laplacian $-\Delta$ on $L^2[0,1]$ with Dirichlet boundary conditions ($u(0)=u(1)=0$) is self-adjoint with eigenvalues: The Dirichlet Laplacian on $[0,1]$ has compact resolvent and discrete spectrum $\lambda_n=(n\pi)^2$ ($n\ge 1$) with $L^2$-orthonormal eigenfunctions $\sqrt{2}\sin(n\pi x)$. Note: on $\mathbb{R}$ (no boundary) the spectrum is $[0,\infty)$ continuous — boundary conditions matter. Solve $-u''=\lambda u$ with $u(0)=u(1)=0$.

q: Stone's theorem says: strongly continuous one-parameter unitary groups $U(t)=e^{itA}$ on $H$ are in bijection with:
text: Stone's theorem says: strongly continuous one-parameter unitary groups $U(t)=e^{itA}$ on $H$ are in bijection with: Stone's theorem: for any strongly continuous unitary group $U(t)$ on $H$ there is a unique self-adjoint $A$ (possibly unbounded) with $U(t)=e^{itA}$, and $A\varphi=-i\,\frac{d}{dt}U(t)\varphi|_{t=0}$ on the natural domain. This is the analytic underpinning of quantum dynamics. The generator of a unitary group must be self-adjoint and is generally unbounded.

q: In quantum mechanics, the energy levels of the harmonic oscillator $H=-\tfrac{\hbar^2}{2m}\partial_x^2+\tfrac{1}{2}m\omega^2 x^2$ are:
text: In quantum mechanics, the energy levels of the harmonic oscillator $H=-\tfrac{\hbar^2}{2m}\partial_x^2+\tfrac{1}{2}m\omega^2 x^2$ are: The harmonic oscillator Hamiltonian has discrete spectrum $E_n=(n+\tfrac12)\hbar\omega$ with $n\ge 0$, evenly spaced by $\hbar\omega$. The ground-state energy $\tfrac12\hbar\omega$ is non-zero — a quantum effect with no classical analogue. The ladder operators give equally spaced eigenvalues with a non-zero ground-state energy.

q: Why does diagonalizing the Laplacian solve the heat equation $\partial_t u = \Delta u$ on a compact domain?
text: Why does diagonalizing the Laplacian solve the heat equation $\partial_t u = \Delta u$ on a compact domain? If $\Delta\varphi_n=-\lambda_n\varphi_n$ and $u_0=\sum c_n\varphi_n$, then $u(t)=e^{t\Delta}u_0=\sum c_n e^{-\lambda_n t}\varphi_n$ solves the heat equation. The compact spectral theorem gives the eigenbasis; the unbounded functional calculus defines $e^{t\Delta}$ as a contraction semigroup. The exponential of a diagonalized operator is diagonal in the same basis.

q: On $L^2(\mathbb{R})$, the translation operators $(T_y f)(x)=f(x-y)$ are simultaneously diagonalized by:
text: On $L^2(\mathbb{R})$, the translation operators $(T_y f)(x)=f(x-y)$ are simultaneously diagonalized by: Translations on $\mathbb{R}$ form a unitary group whose Stone generator is $-i\partial_x$. The Fourier transform $\mathcal{F}\colon L^2(\mathbb{R})\to L^2(\hat{\mathbb{R}})$ diagonalizes $-i\partial_x$ as multiplication by $2\pi\xi$, so $T_y$ becomes multiplication by $e^{-2\pi i y\xi}$. This is the spectral theorem for the abelian group $\mathbb{R}$. Translation has multiplicative character $e^{-2\pi i y\xi}$ in the Fourier domain.

title: Sample spaces, events, and probability measures
blurb: A probability space $(\Omega,\mathcal{F},\mathbb{P})$ is a measure space with $\mathbb{P}(\Omega)=1$; events are measurable subsets of $\Omega$.

title: Conditional probability and Bayes' rule
blurb: $\mathbb{P}(A\mid B)=\mathbb{P}(A\cap B)/\mathbb{P}(B)$, independence as product-factorization, and Bayes' rule as the engine of statistical inference.

title: Random variables
blurb: A random variable is a measurable function $X:\Omega\to\mathbb{R}$; its law $\mathbb{P}_X$ is the push-forward measure on $(\mathbb{R},\mathcal{B})$.

title: Supports, distributions, and densities
blurb: Discrete PMFs, absolutely continuous PDFs, and CDFs package the law of a random variable; the catalog of named distributions (Bernoulli, binomial, Poisson, exponential, Gaussian) organizes the zoo.

title: Expectation, variance, and moments
blurb: $\mathbb{E}[X]=\int_\Omega X\,d\mathbb{P}$ is the centre of mass of the law; variance, covariance, and higher moments live in $L^p$.

title: Moment and characteristic functions
blurb: The MGF $M_X(t)=\mathbb{E}[e^{tX}]$ and characteristic function $\varphi_X(t)=\mathbb{E}[e^{itX}]$ encode the law as a transform; they linearize sums of independents.

title: Modes of convergence
blurb: Almost-sure, in-probability, in $L^p$, and in distribution — four notions with strict implications a.s. $\Rightarrow$ in prob. $\Rightarrow$ in dist., and $L^p\Rightarrow$ in prob.

title: Law of large numbers
blurb: For i.i.d.\ integrable $X_n$ with mean $\mu$, $\bar X_N\to\mu$ — weakly by Chebyshev (WLLN), strongly by Kolmogorov (SLLN).

title: Central limit theorem
blurb: For i.i.d.\ $X_n$ with variance $\sigma^2<\infty$, $\sqrt N(\bar X_N-\mu)/\sigma$ converges in distribution to $\mathcal{N}(0,1)$.

title: Markov chains
blurb: A memoryless stochastic process on a state space; transition matrices compose as a semigroup, and under irreducibility-plus-aperiodicity converge to a unique stationary distribution.

title: Martingales
blurb: A process $(M_n)$ with $\mathbb{E}[M_{n+1}\mid\mathcal{F}_n]=M_n$; Doob's optional-stopping and convergence theorems make martingales the backbone of modern probability.

title: Brownian motion
blurb: The continuous-time Gaussian process $(W_t)_{t\ge 0}$ with independent stationary increments $W_t-W_s\sim\mathcal{N}(0,t-s)$ — the scaling limit of random walks and the fundamental stochastic process.

q: Which of the following is NOT a requirement of a probability measure $\mathbb{P}$ on $(\Omega,\mathcal{F})$?
text: Which of the following is NOT a requirement of a probability measure $\mathbb{P}$ on $(\Omega,\mathcal{F})$? A probability measure may assign $0$ to non-empty events; e.g. $\mathbb{P}(\{x\})=0$ for every singleton under Lebesgue measure on $[0,1]$. The first three are the defining axioms.

q: Let $\mathbb{P}$ be the uniform law on $\Omega=\{1,\ldots,10\}$. Compute $\mathbb{P}(\{\text{odd}\}\cup\{\text{prime}\})$ where prime = $\{2,3,5,7\}$.
text: Let $\mathbb{P}$ be the uniform law on $\Omega=\{1,\ldots,10\}$. Compute $\mathbb{P}(\{\text{odd}\}\cup\{\text{prime}\})$ where prime = $\{2,3,5,7\}$. Odd $=\{1,3,5,7,9\}$; prime $=\{2,3,5,7\}$; union $=\{1,2,3,5,7,9\}$, size $6$, probability $6/10=0.6$.

q: Select all collections that form a $\sigma$-algebra on $\Omega=\{a,b,c\}$.
text: Select all collections that form a $\sigma$-algebra on $\Omega=\{a,b,c\}$. Option 2 fails: $\{a\}\cup\{b\}=\{a,b\}$ is not included, so the collection is not closed under unions. The other three are all valid $\sigma$-algebras.

q: Bonferroni's inequality says $\mathbb{P}(\bigcup_{i=1}^n A_i)\le\sum_i\mathbb{P}(A_i)$. Under what condition does equality hold?
text: Bonferroni's inequality says $\mathbb{P}(\bigcup_{i=1}^n A_i)\le\sum_i\mathbb{P}(A_i)$. Under what condition does equality hold? Countable additivity gives $\mathbb{P}(\bigsqcup A_i)=\sum\mathbb{P}(A_i)$ exactly when the $A_i$ are pairwise disjoint. Otherwise inclusion-exclusion gives a strict inequality. Inclusion-exclusion has only overlap corrections — what makes them vanish?

q: Let $A_n\downarrow A$ be a decreasing sequence of events with $\mathbb{P}(A_1)<\infty$. If $\mathbb{P}(A_n)=1/n$, what is $\mathbb{P}(A)$?
text: Let $A_n\downarrow A$ be a decreasing sequence of events with $\mathbb{P}(A_1)<\infty$. If $\mathbb{P}(A_n)=1/n$, what is $\mathbb{P}(A)$? Continuity from above: $\mathbb{P}(A)=\lim\mathbb{P}(A_n)=\lim 1/n=0$. Use continuity of measure from above.

q: Events $A$ and $B$ are independent iff:
text: Events $A$ and $B$ are independent iff: Independence is exactly the product-factorization $\mathbb{P}(A\cap B)=\mathbb{P}(A)\mathbb{P}(B)$, which is equivalent to $\mathbb{P}(A\mid B)=\mathbb{P}(A)$ when $\mathbb{P}(B)>0$.

q: A test detects a disease with $99\%$ sensitivity and $95\%$ specificity. Prior prevalence is $1\%$. Given a positive test, what is the posterior probability of disease? Report to 3 decimals.
text: A test detects a disease with $99\%$ sensitivity and $95\%$ specificity. Prior prevalence is $1\%$. Given a positive test, what is the posterior probability of disease? Report to 3 decimals. By Bayes: $\mathbb{P}(D\mid+)=\frac{0.99\cdot 0.01}{0.99\cdot 0.01+0.05\cdot 0.99}=\frac{0.0099}{0.0099+0.0495}\approx 0.1667$.

q: Match each identity to its correct name.
text: Match each identity to its correct name. Law of total probability is the partition sum; Bayes flips conditioning; independence is the product rule.

q: Three events $A$, $B$, $C$ are pairwise independent but NOT mutually independent. Which scenario realises this?
text: Three events $A$, $B$, $C$ are pairwise independent but NOT mutually independent. Which scenario realises this? Each of $A$, $B$, $C$ has probability $1/2$; each pairwise intersection is $\{H_1H_2\}$ (probability $1/4=1/2\cdot 1/2$). But $A\cap B\cap C=\{H_1H_2\}$ has probability $1/4\ne 1/8=\mathbb{P}(A)\mathbb{P}(B)\mathbb{P}(C)$. Check both $\mathbb{P}(X\cap Y)=\mathbb{P}(X)\mathbb{P}(Y)$ for each pair and $\mathbb{P}(A\cap B\cap C)=\mathbb{P}(A)\mathbb{P}(B)\mathbb{P}(C)$.

q: In the Monty Hall problem (3 doors, you pick door 1, host opens door 3 showing a goat), what is $\mathbb{P}(\text{car behind door 2}\mid\text{host opens 3})$?
text: In the Monty Hall problem (3 doors, you pick door 1, host opens door 3 showing a goat), what is $\mathbb{P}(\text{car behind door 2}\mid\text{host opens 3})$? By Bayes: the host's choice is uninformative about your initial pick but very informative about door 2; the posterior on door 2 is $2/3\approx 0.6667$. Always switch. Condition on where the car is, then apply the host's rule: always open a goat door that isn't yours.

q: A random variable $X:\Omega\to\mathbb{R}$ is required to be:
text: A random variable $X:\Omega\to\mathbb{R}$ is required to be: By definition, a random variable is a measurable function into $(\mathbb{R},\mathcal{B}(\mathbb{R}))$; continuity, boundedness, and invertibility are extra hypotheses not required.

q: Let $X$ be Bernoulli with $\mathbb{P}(X=1)=0.3$. What is $\mathbb{P}_X(\{1\})$, the push-forward measure of the singleton?
text: Let $X$ be Bernoulli with $\mathbb{P}(X=1)=0.3$. What is $\mathbb{P}_X(\{1\})$, the push-forward measure of the singleton? The law is $\mathbb{P}_X(B)=\mathbb{P}(X\in B)$, so $\mathbb{P}_X(\{1\})=\mathbb{P}(X=1)=0.3$.

q: Which pair of operations preserves measurability of random variables $X,Y$?
text: Which pair of operations preserves measurability of random variables $X,Y$? Both sums and products of measurable functions are measurable, since $(\mathbb{R}^2,\mathcal{B}\otimes\mathcal{B})\to\mathbb{R}$ via $(x,y)\mapsto x+y$ and $(x,y)\mapsto xy$ are Borel-measurable (in fact continuous). Both sums and products of measurable functions are measurable, since $(\mathbb{R}^2,\mathcal{B}\otimes\mathcal{B})\to\mathbb{R}$ via $(x,y)\mapsto x+y$ and $(x,y)\mapsto xy$ are Borel-measurable (in…

q: Why is the indicator $\mathbf{1}_V$ of a Vitali set $V\subset[0,1]$ NOT a random variable on $([0,1],\mathcal{B},m)$?
text: Why is the indicator $\mathbf{1}_V$ of a Vitali set $V\subset[0,1]$ NOT a random variable on $([0,1],\mathcal{B},m)$? $\mathbf{1}_V^{-1}(\{1\})=V$, which is not in $\mathcal{B}([0,1])$ (Vitali is the canonical non-measurable set). So the preimage condition fails. Recall that random variables must have Borel-measurable preimages.

q: Let $X\sim\text{Uniform}[0,1]$ and $Y=-\ln X$. What is $\mathbb{P}(Y>t)$ for $t\ge 0$?
text: Let $X\sim\text{Uniform}[0,1]$ and $Y=-\ln X$. What is $\mathbb{P}(Y>t)$ for $t\ge 0$? At $t=1$: $\mathbb{P}(Y>1)=\mathbb{P}(-\ln X>1)=\mathbb{P}(X<e^{-1})=e^{-1}\approx 0.3679$. In general $Y\sim\text{Exp}(1)$. Set $t=1$; this is the exponential tail formula.

q: For $X\sim\text{Poisson}(\lambda)$, the PMF is:
text: For $X\sim\text{Poisson}(\lambda)$, the PMF is: Poisson PMF is $e^{-\lambda}\lambda^k/k!$ on the non-negative integers; choice 0 is binomial, choice 3 is exponential (PDF, not PMF).

q: For $X\sim\text{Exp}(\lambda=2)$, compute $\mathbb{P}(X>1)$.
text: For $X\sim\text{Exp}(\lambda=2)$, compute $\mathbb{P}(X>1)$. Exponential tail: $\mathbb{P}(X>t)=e^{-\lambda t}=e^{-2}\approx 0.1353$.

q: Match each named distribution to its support.
text: Match each named distribution to its support. Binomial is supported on $\{0,\ldots,n\}$; Poisson on non-negative integers; Exponential on $[0,\infty)$; Gaussian on $\mathbb{R}$.

q: Which of the following pairs has the same first two moments (mean and variance) but differs in higher moments?
text: Which of the following pairs has the same first two moments (mean and variance) but differs in higher moments? Uniform on $[-\sqrt{3},\sqrt{3}]$ has mean $0$ and variance $3/3=1$, matching $\mathcal{N}(0,1)$. But the Gaussian has kurtosis $3$ while the uniform has kurtosis $9/5$. The other pairs differ in mean or variance directly. Match mean $=0$ and variance $=1$ in each pair.

q: Click where the mode of a $\text{Gamma}(k=3,\theta=1)$ density $f(x)=x^2 e^{-x}/2$ lies. (Mode is $(k-1)\theta$ for $k\ge 1$; x-axis is labelled 0..10.)
text: Click where the mode of a $\text{Gamma}(k=3,\theta=1)$ density $f(x)=x^2 e^{-x}/2$ lies. (Mode is $(k-1)\theta$ for $k\ge 1$; x-axis is labelled 0..10.) Setting $f'(x)=0$ gives $x=k-1=2$; at $x/5=20$ in the viewBox. The Gamma$(k,\theta)$ mode is $(k-1)\theta$ for $k\ge 1$.

q: The definition $\mathbb{E}[X]=\int_\Omega X\,d\mathbb{P}$ uses:
text: The definition $\mathbb{E}[X]=\int_\Omega X\,d\mathbb{P}$ uses: Expectation is defined as the Lebesgue integral of $X$ against the probability measure $\mathbb{P}$; finite by definition when $X\in L^1(\Omega,\mathcal{F},\mathbb{P})$.

q: Let $X$ be uniform on $\{1,2,3,4,5,6\}$. Compute $\mathbb{E}[X^2]$.
For $X$ with $\mathbb{E}[X]=2$ and $\mathbb{E}[X^2]=5$, compute $\text{Var}(X)$.
text: For $X$ with $\mathbb{E}[X]=2$ and $\mathbb{E}[X^2]=5$, compute $\text{Var}(X)$. Variance shortcut: $\text{Var}(X)=\mathbb{E}[X^2]-(\mathbb{E}[X])^2=5-4=1$.

q: If $X\sim\text{Cauchy}(0,1)$, which statement is correct?
text: If $X\sim\text{Cauchy}(0,1)$, which statement is correct? Cauchy density decays as $1/x^2$, so $\int|x|/(1+x^2)\,dx=\infty$. The principal-value limit is $0$, but that is not the Lebesgue integral. Expectation is undefined. For the Lebesgue integral to exist, we need $\mathbb{E}[|X|]<\infty$; symmetry is not enough.

q: Let $X$ have density $f(x)=2x$ on $[0,1]$. Compute $\text{Var}(X)$ to 4 decimals.
text: Let $X$ have density $f(x)=2x$ on $[0,1]$. Compute $\text{Var}(X)$ to 4 decimals. $\mathbb{E}[X]=2/3$, $\mathbb{E}[X^2]=1/2$, $\text{Var}(X)=1/2-4/9=1/18\approx 0.0556$. Compute $\mathbb{E}[X]=\int_0^1 2x^2\,dx$ and $\mathbb{E}[X^2]=\int_0^1 2x^3\,dx$.

q: Jensen's inequality states $\varphi(\mathbb{E}[X])\le\mathbb{E}[\varphi(X)]$ for convex $\varphi$. Which is the sharpest correctly-applied consequence for $X>0$ with $\mathbb{E}[X]=\mu$?
text: Jensen's inequality states $\varphi(\mathbb{E}[X])\le\mathbb{E}[\varphi(X)]$ for convex $\varphi$. Which is the sharpest correctly-applied consequence for $X>0$ with $\mathbb{E}[X]=\mu$? $1/x$ is convex on $(0,\infty)$, so by Jensen $\mathbb{E}[1/X]\ge 1/\mathbb{E}[X]=1/\mu$. The others have Jensen's inequality the wrong way: $\ln$ is concave, $x^2$ and $e^x$ are convex (so the inequality in those cases is $\ge$, not $\le$). $\varphi(x)=1/x$ is convex on $(0,\infty)$.

q: Prove $\text{Var}(X+Y)=\text{Var}(X)+\text{Var}(Y)$ when $X,Y$ are independent. Pick the next step.
text: Prove $\text{Var}(X+Y)=\text{Var}(X)+\text{Var}(Y)$ when $X,Y$ are independent. Pick the next step. Independence factorizes the cross term; then $\mathbb{E}[X-\mu_X]=0$. Drop the middle term to finish.

q: The characteristic function of $X$ is defined as:
text: The characteristic function of $X$ is defined as: $\varphi_X(t)=\mathbb{E}[e^{itX}]$ is the characteristic function. The others are the MGF, the PGF, and the CDF, respectively.

q: For $X\sim\mathcal{N}(0,1)$, compute $M_X(1)=\mathbb{E}[e^{X}]$ to 4 decimals.
text: For $X\sim\mathcal{N}(0,1)$, compute $M_X(1)=\mathbb{E}[e^{X}]$ to 4 decimals. Gaussian MGF: $M_X(t)=e^{t^2/2}$, so $M_X(1)=e^{1/2}\approx 1.6487$.

q: For $X\sim\mathcal{N}(0,1)$, compute $\varphi_X(1)$ as $a+bi$.
text: For $X\sim\mathcal{N}(0,1)$, compute $\varphi_X(1)$ as $a+bi$. Gaussian characteristic function: $\varphi_X(t)=e^{-t^2/2}$, so $\varphi_X(1)=e^{-1/2}\approx 0.6065$ (real).

q: If $X_1,\ldots,X_n$ are i.i.d.\ with MGF $M$, the MGF of $S_n=\sum X_i$ is:
text: If $X_1,\ldots,X_n$ are i.i.d.\ with MGF $M$, the MGF of $S_n=\sum X_i$ is: $M_{S_n}(t)=\mathbb{E}[e^{tS_n}]=\mathbb{E}[\prod e^{tX_i}]=\prod\mathbb{E}[e^{tX_i}]=M(t)^n$ by independence. This is why the CLT proof multiplies characteristic functions. Independence turns products of expectations into expectations of products.

q: Why does the Cauchy distribution have a characteristic function but NO moment-generating function?
text: Why does the Cauchy distribution have a characteristic function but NO moment-generating function? The heavy $1/x^2$ tails make $\int e^{tx}/(1+x^2)\,dx=\infty$ for any $t\ne 0$, so the MGF is undefined. The characteristic function $\mathbb{E}[e^{itX}]=e^{-|t|}$ is fine because $|e^{itX}|=1$ is bounded. Check whether $\int e^{tx}/(\pi(1+x^2))\,dx$ converges.

q: Lévy's continuity theorem states that $X_n\xrightarrow{d}X$ iff $\varphi_{X_n}(t)\to\varphi_X(t)$ pointwise AND:
text: Lévy's continuity theorem states that $X_n\xrightarrow{d}X$ iff $\varphi_{X_n}(t)\to\varphi_X(t)$ pointwise AND: The technical hypothesis that rescues convergence-in-distribution from pathologies: $\varphi_{X_n}(t)\to\varphi(t)$ pointwise with $\varphi$ continuous at $0$. Continuity at $0$ is equivalent to $\varphi$ being the CF of a probability measure (not a sub-probability one). Continuity at $0$ is equivalent to tightness of the limit and rules out mass escaping to $\pm\infty$.

q: Which implication ALWAYS holds between modes of convergence?
text: Which implication ALWAYS holds between modes of convergence? a.s. implies in-probability by a standard subsequence argument. The reverse implications fail: in-probability doesn't give a.s. (typewriter sequence), and convergence in distribution is weakest.

q: Let $X_n=n\cdot\mathbf{1}_{A_n}$ where $\mathbb{P}(A_n)=1/n^2$. Does $X_n\to 0$ almost surely? Report $1$ for yes, $0$ for no.
text: Let $X_n=n\cdot\mathbf{1}_{A_n}$ where $\mathbb{P}(A_n)=1/n^2$. Does $X_n\to 0$ almost surely? Report $1$ for yes, $0$ for no. By Borel-Cantelli, $\sum\mathbb{P}(A_n)=\sum 1/n^2<\infty$, so $\mathbb{P}(A_n\text{ i.o.})=0$. Thus $X_n=0$ for all sufficiently large $n$ a.s., so $X_n\to 0$ a.s.

q: Arrange these modes of convergence from STRONGEST to WEAKEST (when the implication holds).
text: Arrange these modes of convergence from STRONGEST to WEAKEST (when the implication holds). a.s. $\Rightarrow$ in prob. $\Rightarrow$ in dist. The chain is strict: each reverse implication fails in general.

q: Select all modes of convergence strictly WEAKER than almost-sure convergence.
text: Select all modes of convergence strictly WEAKER than almost-sure convergence. a.s. $\Rightarrow$ in prob. (strictly); a.s. $\not\Leftrightarrow L^1$ (neither implies the other without extra hypotheses, but both are strictly weaker in that a.s. does not imply $L^1$ without UI, and $L^1$ does not imply a.s.); in dist. is weakest. Subsequential a.s. convergence is a consequence of convergence in probability, not strictly weaker. a.s. implies in-probability; what does NOT imply a.s. back?

q: The 'typewriter' sequence $X_n=\mathbf{1}_{I_n}$ where $I_n\subset[0,1]$ cycles through dyadic intervals of decreasing length exhibits:
text: The 'typewriter' sequence $X_n=\mathbf{1}_{I_n}$ where $I_n\subset[0,1]$ cycles through dyadic intervals of decreasing length exhibits: $|I_n|\to 0$ so $\mathbb{P}(X_n\ne 0)\to 0$ (in probability). But every $\omega$ is hit infinitely often by the cycling, so $X_n(\omega)\not\to 0$ for every $\omega$ — convergence fails a.s. everywhere. At each $\omega\in[0,1]$, how often does $\omega\in I_n$?

q: Scheffé's lemma states that if $f_n\to f$ a.e.\ with $f_n,f$ probability densities, then $\int|f_n-f|\,dm\to 0$. Which mode of convergence does this give for the associated random variables?
text: Scheffé's lemma states that if $f_n\to f$ a.e.\ with $f_n,f$ probability densities, then $\int|f_n-f|\,dm\to 0$. Which mode of convergence does this give for the associated random variables? Total variation distance between laws equals $\tfrac{1}{2}\int|f_n-f|\,dm$. Scheffé says this goes to $0$, which is stronger than convergence in distribution and gives in-probability convergence as well.

q: The Weak Law of Large Numbers says: if $X_1,X_2,\ldots$ are i.i.d.\ with $\mathbb{E}[X_1]=\mu$ and finite variance, then $\bar X_N$ converges to $\mu$:
text: The Weak Law of Large Numbers says: if $X_1,X_2,\ldots$ are i.i.d.\ with $\mathbb{E}[X_1]=\mu$ and finite variance, then $\bar X_N$ converges to $\mu$: WLLN concludes convergence in probability; SLLN (Kolmogorov) strengthens this to a.s.\ convergence, and requires only $\mathbb{E}|X_1|<\infty$.

q: Flip a fair coin $10{,}000$ times. By LLN, the sample mean of indicators is approximately $0.5$. What does Chebyshev's inequality bound $\mathbb{P}(|\bar X-0.5|\ge 0.05)$ by?
text: Flip a fair coin $10{,}000$ times. By LLN, the sample mean of indicators is approximately $0.5$. What does Chebyshev's inequality bound $\mathbb{P}(|\bar X-0.5|\ge 0.05)$ by? $\text{Var}(\bar X)=0.25/10000=2.5\times 10^{-5}$. Chebyshev: $\mathbb{P}(|\bar X-\mu|\ge 0.05)\le\sigma^2/(\epsilon^2)=2.5\times 10^{-5}/0.0025=0.01$.

q: Kolmogorov's SLLN requires:
text: Kolmogorov's SLLN requires: Kolmogorov's SLLN requires only that $X_1,X_2,\ldots$ are i.i.d.\ and $\mathbb{E}|X_1|<\infty$; integrability suffices. Finite variance is enough but not necessary.

q: The SLLN FAILS for i.i.d.\ $X_i\sim\text{Cauchy}(0,1)$. The correct reason is:
text: The SLLN FAILS for i.i.d.\ $X_i\sim\text{Cauchy}(0,1)$. The correct reason is: $\mathbb{E}|X_1|=\int|x|/(\pi(1+x^2))\,dx=\infty$, so the integrability hypothesis of the SLLN fails. In fact, the sample mean of i.i.d.\ Cauchys is again Cauchy, so $\bar X_N$ does not concentrate at all. Check the key hypothesis of Kolmogorov's SLLN.

q: Prove the WLLN via Chebyshev, assuming finite variance $\sigma^2$. Pick the next step.
text: Prove the WLLN via Chebyshev, assuming finite variance $\sigma^2$. Pick the next step. Chebyshev gives $\mathbb{P}(|\bar X_N-\mu|\ge\varepsilon)\le\sigma^2/(N\varepsilon^2)\to 0$ for any $\varepsilon>0$. That is convergence in probability — the definition of WLLN. The Chebyshev bound goes to $0$ as $N\to\infty$.

q: Etemadi's proof of the SLLN (1981) removes which hypothesis from Kolmogorov's original proof?
text: Etemadi's proof of the SLLN (1981) removes which hypothesis from Kolmogorov's original proof? Etemadi's clever SLLN proof uses truncation, Chebyshev, and Borel-Cantelli, needing only pairwise independence plus identical distribution plus $\mathbb{E}|X_1|<\infty$. Full mutual independence is not needed. Kolmogorov used full independence; Etemadi needs only pairs.

q: Glivenko–Cantelli strengthens the SLLN pointwise to uniform convergence of the empirical CDF: $\sup_x|F_N(x)-F(x)|\to 0$ a.s.\ For $N=1000$ samples from uniform $[0,1]$, what is the expected order of magnitude of $\sup_x|F_N(x)-x|$? (Report the exponent $e$ in $N^{-e}$.)
text: Glivenko–Cantelli strengthens the SLLN pointwise to uniform convergence of the empirical CDF: $\sup_x|F_N(x)-F(x)|\to 0$ a.s.\ For $N=1000$ samples from uniform $[0,1]$, what is the expected order of magnitude of $\sup_x|F_N(x)-x|$? (Report the exponent $e$ in $N^{-e}$.) The DKW inequality gives $\mathbb{P}(\sup_x|F_N(x)-F(x)|>\varepsilon)\le 2e^{-2N\varepsilon^2}$, so typical deviations are $O(N^{-1/2})$. Dvoretzky–Kiefer–Wolfowitz inequality.

q: The classical CLT states that if $X_1,X_2,\ldots$ are i.i.d.\ with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2\in(0,\infty)$, then $\sqrt{N}(\bar X_N-\mu)/\sigma$ converges:
text: The classical CLT states that if $X_1,X_2,\ldots$ are i.i.d.\ with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2\in(0,\infty)$, then $\sqrt{N}(\bar X_N-\mu)/\sigma$ converges: The CLT gives convergence in distribution, not almost-sure or in probability. Almost-sure convergence to a non-degenerate Gaussian would be paradoxical — the sample mean does not converge a.s.\ to a random limit.

q: For $S_{100}=\sum_{i=1}^{100}X_i$ with $X_i$ i.i.d.\ Bernoulli$(0.5)$, approximate $\mathbb{P}(S_{100}\ge 60)$ using the Gaussian approximation. Report to 3 decimals.
text: For $S_{100}=\sum_{i=1}^{100}X_i$ with $X_i$ i.i.d.\ Bernoulli$(0.5)$, approximate $\mathbb{P}(S_{100}\ge 60)$ using the Gaussian approximation. Report to 3 decimals. $\mathbb{E}[S_{100}]=50$, $\text{Var}(S_{100})=25$. $Z=(60-50)/5=2$. $\mathbb{P}(Z\ge 2)\approx 0.0228$.

q: Which version of the CLT does NOT require finite variance?
text: Which version of the CLT does NOT require finite variance? When $\text{Var}(X_i)=\infty$ but tails are regularly varying, suitably normalized sums converge to a non-Gaussian stable distribution. Lindeberg, Lyapunov, and Berry-Esseen all require finite variance.

q: The following 'proof' of the CLT has exactly one flaw. Click the invalid step.
text: The following 'proof' of the CLT has exactly one flaw. Click the invalid step. Lévy's continuity theorem concludes convergence in DISTRIBUTION, not almost surely. The sample mean does not converge a.s.\ to a random normal limit (and in fact $S_N/\sqrt{N}$ does not converge a.s.\ to any random variable; its limsup is $+\infty$ by the law of iterated logarithm). What mode of convergence does Lévy's continuity theorem give?

q: The CLT fails without finite variance. For i.i.d.\ $X_i\sim\text{Cauchy}(0,1)$, what is the distribution of $\bar X_N=(1/N)\sum X_i$?
text: The CLT fails without finite variance. For i.i.d.\ $X_i\sim\text{Cauchy}(0,1)$, what is the distribution of $\bar X_N=(1/N)\sum X_i$? The Cauchy distribution is stable: sums of i.i.d.\ Cauchy$(0,1)$ are again Cauchy$(0,N)$, and dividing by $N$ gives Cauchy$(0,1)$. The sample mean does not concentrate — a dramatic failure of both LLN and CLT. The Cauchy is stable with index $\alpha=1$.

q: The Berry-Esseen theorem gives the quantitative rate $\sup_x|F_{S_N/\sqrt{N}}(x)-\Phi(x)|\le C\rho/(\sigma^3\sqrt{N})$ where $\rho=\mathbb{E}|X_1|^3$. The rate $N^{-1/2}$ is:
text: The Berry-Esseen theorem gives the quantitative rate $\sup_x|F_{S_N/\sqrt{N}}(x)-\Phi(x)|\le C\rho/(\sigma^3\sqrt{N})$ where $\rho=\mathbb{E}|X_1|^3$. The rate $N^{-1/2}$ is: For lattice distributions (e.g. Bernoulli), the CDF has jumps of order $1/\sqrt{N}$, so the Kolmogorov distance cannot be smaller than $O(1/\sqrt{N})$. This matches the Berry-Esseen rate up to the constant $C$. For non-lattice distributions one can obtain faster rates (Edgeworth expansions). The Bernoulli case has a lattice distribution.

q: Lindeberg's condition for the triangular-array CLT says: for $S_n=\sum_k X_{n,k}$ with $s_n^2=\sum_k\text{Var}(X_{n,k})$,
text: Lindeberg's condition for the triangular-array CLT says: for $S_n=\sum_k X_{n,k}$ with $s_n^2=\sum_k\text{Var}(X_{n,k})$, Lindeberg's condition ($L_n(\varepsilon)\to 0$ for every $\varepsilon>0$) is necessary and sufficient (under UAN) for the CLT to hold for non-i.i.d.\ triangular arrays. It ensures no single summand dominates. Lindeberg's condition controls the contribution of 'large' summands.

q: A discrete-time Markov chain satisfies the memoryless property:
text: A discrete-time Markov chain satisfies the memoryless property: Markov property: the future depends on the past only through the present. Independence of present from past (choice 1) is too strong; choice 2 is independence of future from present.

q: A two-state chain with transition matrix $P=\begin{pmatrix}0.7&0.3\\0.4&0.6\end{pmatrix}$ has stationary distribution $\pi$ with $\pi P=\pi$. What is $\pi_1$ (mass on state 1)?
text: A two-state chain with transition matrix $P=\begin{pmatrix}0.7&0.3\\0.4&0.6\end{pmatrix}$ has stationary distribution $\pi$ with $\pi P=\pi$. What is $\pi_1$ (mass on state 1)? Solve $0.7\pi_1+0.4(1-\pi_1)=\pi_1$, giving $0.4=0.7\pi_1$, so $\pi_1=4/7\approx 0.5714$. Check: $\pi_2=3/7$.

q: The fundamental theorem for irreducible, aperiodic, finite-state Markov chains says:
text: The fundamental theorem for irreducible, aperiodic, finite-state Markov chains says: For finite-state irreducible aperiodic chains, $P^n\to\Pi$ with $\Pi_{ij}=\pi_j$ (every starting state converges to $\pi$). This is the ergodic theorem for Markov chains.

q: Consider a reducible 2-class chain on $\{1,2,3,4\}$: states $\{1,2\}$ form one closed class, $\{3,4\}$ form another. How many stationary distributions does this chain have?
text: Consider a reducible 2-class chain on $\{1,2,3,4\}$: states $\{1,2\}$ form one closed class, $\{3,4\}$ form another. How many stationary distributions does this chain have? Each closed class has its own stationary distribution $\pi^{(1)}$ (supported on $\{1,2\}$) and $\pi^{(2)}$ (on $\{3,4\}$). Any $\alpha\pi^{(1)}+(1-\alpha)\pi^{(2)}$ for $\alpha\in[0,1]$ is stationary — the space of stationary distributions is a one-dimensional simplex. Uniqueness requires irreducibility. Each closed class has its own stationary distribution; any convex combination is stationary.

q: A random walk on $\mathbb{Z}$ with equal steps $\pm 1$ is recurrent. For a simple random walk on $\mathbb{Z}^3$ (Pólya's theorem), what is $\mathbb{P}(\text{return to origin})\approx$? Report to 3 decimals.
text: A random walk on $\mathbb{Z}$ with equal steps $\pm 1$ is recurrent. For a simple random walk on $\mathbb{Z}^3$ (Pólya's theorem), what is $\mathbb{P}(\text{return to origin})\approx$? Report to 3 decimals. Pólya's theorem: random walks on $\mathbb{Z}$ and $\mathbb{Z}^2$ are recurrent (return probability $1$); on $\mathbb{Z}^d$ for $d\ge 3$ they are transient. The return probability for $\mathbb{Z}^3$ is approximately $0.3405$ (Watson's integrals). $\mathbb{Z}^3$ random walks are transient; return probability is $\approx 0.34$.

q: A Markov chain is reversible w.r.t.\ $\pi$ iff $\pi_i P_{ij}=\pi_j P_{ji}$ for all $i,j$ (detailed balance). Reversibility:
text: A Markov chain is reversible w.r.t.\ $\pi$ iff $\pi_i P_{ij}=\pi_j P_{ji}$ for all $i,j$ (detailed balance). Reversibility: Summing detailed balance over $i$ gives $\sum_i\pi_i P_{ij}=\pi_j$, so reversibility implies stationarity. But not all stationary chains are reversible: asymmetric random walks on $\mathbb{Z}$ (with drift) have a stationary measure but fail detailed balance. Summing $\pi_i P_{ij}=\pi_j P_{ji}$ over $i$ gives stationarity; the reverse needs more.

q: A process $(M_n)_{n\ge 0}$ adapted to a filtration $(\mathcal{F}_n)$ is a martingale if:
text: A process $(M_n)_{n\ge 0}$ adapted to a filtration $(\mathcal{F}_n)$ is a martingale if: Martingale definition: adapted, $L^1$, and the conditional expectation of the future given the past equals the present. This captures 'fair game' gambling intuition.

q: $S_n=\sum_{i=1}^n X_i$ where $X_i$ are i.i.d.\ with $\mathbb{E}[X_i]=0$. What is $\mathbb{E}[S_{10}\mid\mathcal{F}_5]-S_5$?
text: $S_n=\sum_{i=1}^n X_i$ where $X_i$ are i.i.d.\ with $\mathbb{E}[X_i]=0$. What is $\mathbb{E}[S_{10}\mid\mathcal{F}_5]-S_5$? By independence: $\mathbb{E}[S_{10}\mid\mathcal{F}_5]=S_5+\mathbb{E}[X_6+\cdots+X_{10}\mid\mathcal{F}_5]=S_5+0=S_5$. So the difference is $0$: random walk is a martingale.

q: The optional-stopping theorem (Doob) says $\mathbb{E}[M_\tau]=\mathbb{E}[M_0]$ for a martingale $M$ and stopping time $\tau$, provided:
text: The optional-stopping theorem (Doob) says $\mathbb{E}[M_\tau]=\mathbb{E}[M_0]$ for a martingale $M$ and stopping time $\tau$, provided: At least one of these conditions is required; otherwise optional-stopping fails (e.g.\ simple random walk stopped at $+1$ has $\mathbb{E}[M_\tau]=1\ne 0=\mathbb{E}[M_0]$).

q: Consider a simple symmetric random walk $S_n$ and the stopping time $\tau=\inf\{n:S_n=1\}$. Why does the naive optional-stopping fail here?
text: Consider a simple symmetric random walk $S_n$ and the stopping time $\tau=\inf\{n:S_n=1\}$. Why does the naive optional-stopping fail here? $\tau<\infty$ a.s.\ (recurrence of 1D walk) but $\mathbb{E}[\tau]=\infty$. The stopped process is not UI because $\sup_n\mathbb{E}|S_{n\wedge\tau}|=\infty$. The conclusion $\mathbb{E}[S_\tau]=1\ne 0$ shows the hypothesis matters. Check UI: is $\sup_n\mathbb{E}|S_{n\wedge\tau}|<\infty$?

q: Doob's martingale convergence theorem says: if $(M_n)$ is a martingale with $\sup_n\mathbb{E}|M_n|<\infty$, then $M_n$ converges:
text: Doob's martingale convergence theorem says: if $(M_n)$ is a martingale with $\sup_n\mathbb{E}|M_n|<\infty$, then $M_n$ converges: $L^1$-boundedness gives a.s.\ convergence to an integrable limit. $L^1$ convergence additionally requires uniform integrability. Classic counterexample: $(M_n)$ with $\mathbb{E}|M_n|=1$ but $M_n\to 0$ a.s. and $\mathbb{E}[M_n]=1\ne 0=\mathbb{E}[M_\infty]$. $L^1$-bounded is weaker than uniform integrability.

q: Doob's $L^p$ maximal inequality says: for a non-negative sub-martingale $M$ and $p>1$,
text: Doob's $L^p$ maximal inequality says: for a non-negative sub-martingale $M$ and $p>1$, Doob's $L^p$ inequality: $\|\max_{k\le n}M_k\|_p\le(p/(p-1))\|M_n\|_p$ for sub-martingales, $p>1$. This is the key tool for a.s.\ convergence proofs and random-time estimates.

q: An $L^1$-bounded martingale need not converge in $L^1$. The classical counterexample is:
text: An $L^1$-bounded martingale need not converge in $L^1$. The classical counterexample is: $M_n$ is a product martingale with $\mathbb{E}[M_n]=1$. But $M_n=0$ as soon as any $X_i=0$, which happens a.s. So $M_n\to 0$ a.s.\ but $\mathbb{E}[M_n]=1$; convergence fails in $L^1$. UI would rule this out. Product martingale: when does it hit $0$?

q: Standard Brownian motion $(W_t)_{t\ge 0}$ has:
text: Standard Brownian motion $(W_t)_{t\ge 0}$ has: Brownian motion is the Gaussian process with these three properties; its paths are continuous but nowhere differentiable and of unbounded variation on every interval.

q: Compute $\mathbb{P}(W_1>1.96)$ where $W_1\sim\mathcal{N}(0,1)$. Report to 3 decimals.
text: Compute $\mathbb{P}(W_1>1.96)$ where $W_1\sim\mathcal{N}(0,1)$. Report to 3 decimals. Standard Gaussian tail: $\mathbb{P}(Z>1.96)\approx 0.025$ (the two-tail $5\%$ critical value).

q: The quadratic variation of Brownian motion on $[0,t]$ is:
text: The quadratic variation of Brownian motion on $[0,t]$ is: $\sum_k(W_{t_{k+1}}-W_{t_k})^2\to t$ in probability (and a.s.\ along dyadic refinements) as the mesh shrinks. Infinite variation but finite quadratic variation is the signature of Brownian motion.

q: Donsker's invariance principle says: for i.i.d.\ $X_i$ with $\mathbb{E}[X_i]=0$ and $\text{Var}(X_i)=1$, the scaled random walk $W^{(N)}_t=S_{\lfloor Nt\rfloor}/\sqrt{N}$ converges to Brownian motion:
text: Donsker's invariance principle says: for i.i.d.\ $X_i$ with $\mathbb{E}[X_i]=0$ and $\text{Var}(X_i)=1$, the scaled random walk $W^{(N)}_t=S_{\lfloor Nt\rfloor}/\sqrt{N}$ converges to Brownian motion: Donsker's theorem: the path-valued random element converges in distribution in $C([0,1])$ to standard Brownian motion. This extends the CLT (a finite-dimensional statement at $t=1$) to a functional statement about whole paths. Donsker lifts CLT from a single time to the whole path.

q: For a scaled random walk approximation: $N=10^4$ steps of $\pm 1$ per unit time. What is the order of magnitude (exponent $e$ in $N^{e}$) of the step size needed so the process converges to Brownian motion on $[0,1]$?
text: For a scaled random walk approximation: $N=10^4$ steps of $\pm 1$ per unit time. What is the order of magnitude (exponent $e$ in $N^{e}$) of the step size needed so the process converges to Brownian motion on $[0,1]$? Diffusive scaling: to match variance $t$ in time $t$ with $Nt$ steps of variance $\sigma^2$ each, we need $\sigma^2\cdot N=1$, so $\sigma\sim N^{-1/2}$. Exponent $e=-1/2$. Diffusive scaling: step size $\sim 1/\sqrt{N}$.

q: Brownian motion is a martingale. Which of the following is ALSO a martingale?
text: Brownian motion is a martingale. Which of the following is ALSO a martingale? $\mathbb{E}[W_t^2\mid\mathcal{F}_s]=W_s^2+(t-s)$, so $W_t^2-t$ is a martingale. Analogously, $e^{W_t-t/2}$ (not $e^{W_t}$) is the exponential martingale. $|W_t|$ is a sub-martingale, not a martingale. Compute $\mathbb{E}[W_t^2\mid\mathcal{F}_s]$ for $s<t$.

q: Itô's formula for $f(W_t)$ with $f\in C^2$ is:
text: Itô's formula for $f(W_t)$ with $f\in C^2$ is: Itô's formula has the extra $\tfrac{1}{2}f''(W_t)\,dt$ term coming from $(dW)^2=dt$. This is the fundamental calculus of Brownian motion and stochastic analysis. Brownian motion has non-vanishing quadratic variation; the chain rule picks up a second-order term.

title: Filtrations and adapted processes
blurb: A filtration $(\mathcal{F}_n)$ is an increasing family of sub-$\sigma$-algebras encoding the information available at time $n$; a process is adapted when each $X_n$ is $\mathcal{F}_n$-measurable, and a stopping time $\tau$ satisfies $\{\tau\le n\}\in\mathcal{F}_n$.

title: Discrete-time martingales
blurb: An adapted $L^1$ process $(M_n)$ is a martingale when $\mathbb{E}[M_{n+1}\mid\mathcal{F}_n]=M_n$; mean-zero random walk and Doob martingales $\mathbb{E}[Z\mid\mathcal{F}_n]$ are the canonical examples.

title: Optional stopping theorem
blurb: If $\tau$ is a stopping time and either (a) $\tau$ is bounded, (b) $M$ is uniformly integrable, or (c) $\tau<\infty$ a.s. and $(M_{n\wedge\tau})$ is bounded, then $\mathbb{E}[M_\tau]=\mathbb{E}[M_0]$; gambler's ruin and Wald's identity drop out as corollaries.

title: Doob decomposition and quadratic variation
blurb: Every $L^1$ adapted process splits uniquely as $X_n=X_0+M_n+A_n$ with $M$ a martingale and $A$ predictable; for $X=S^2$ with $S$ a mean-zero random walk this recovers $\langle S\rangle_n=n$, the discrete quadratic variation.

title: Martingale convergence theorem
blurb: If $\sup_n\mathbb{E}|M_n|<\infty$ then $M_n\to M_\infty$ a.s. for some $M_\infty\in L^1$ (Doob); uniform integrability upgrades the convergence to $L^1$, while $L^1$-bounded-without-UI examples like the doubling product $\prod X_i$ collapse to $0$ even with $\mathbb{E}[M_n]=1$.

title: Continuous-time martingales and Brownian motion
blurb: Brownian motion $W_t$ is the canonical continuous martingale, with $W_t^2-t$ a martingale and quadratic variation $[W]_t=t$ a.s.; Burkholder–Davis–Gundy ties $L^p$ norms of $\sup_{s\le t}M_s$ to $[M]_t$, setting up Itô calculus.

q: A filtration $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n\ge 0}$ on $(\Omega,\mathcal{F},\mathbb{P})$ is required to satisfy:
text: A filtration $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n\ge 0}$ on $(\Omega,\mathcal{F},\mathbb{P})$ is required to satisfy: By definition the filtration is increasing: $\mathcal{F}_n\subseteq\mathcal{F}_{n+1}$, and each $\mathcal{F}_n$ is a sub-$\sigma$-algebra of $\mathcal{F}$. Information accumulates; nothing is forgotten. A filtration is an increasing chain of sub-$\sigma$-algebras: information accumulates over time.

q: Let $(X_n)$ be a sequence of random variables and $\mathcal{F}_n=\sigma(X_0,\ldots,X_n)$ the natural filtration. Which random time is a stopping time?
text: Let $(X_n)$ be a sequence of random variables and $\mathcal{F}_n=\sigma(X_0,\ldots,X_n)$ the natural filtration. Which random time is a stopping time? $\{\tau\le n\}=\{\exists k\le n: X_k\ge 5\}\in\mathcal{F}_n$, so the first hitting time is a stopping time. The other three involve looking into the future (last visit, all-time max), which $\mathcal{F}_n$ cannot decide. A stopping time $\tau$ requires $\{\tau\le n\}\in\mathcal{F}_n$ — you can decide at time $n$ whether stopping has happened, using only the information available so far.

q: Symmetric simple random walk $S_n$ on $\mathbb{Z}$ starts at $S_0=0$. Let $\tau=\min\{n\ge 0: S_n\in\{-2,3\}\}$. What is $\mathbb{P}(S_\tau=3)$?
text: Symmetric simple random walk $S_n$ on $\mathbb{Z}$ starts at $S_0=0$. Let $\tau=\min\{n\ge 0: S_n\in\{-2,3\}\}$. What is $\mathbb{P}(S_\tau=3)$? Gambler's ruin formula for symmetric walk on $\{-2,\ldots,3\}$: $\mathbb{P}(\text{hit }3\text{ first})=(0-(-2))/(3-(-2))=2/5=0.4$. The fact that $\tau$ is a stopping time is what lets the formula apply. Gambler's ruin on $\{-2,\ldots,3\}$: the probability of hitting $b=3$ before $a=-2$ starting at $0$ is $(0-(-2))/(3-(-2))=2/5$.

q: An adapted, integrable process $(M_n)$ is a martingale when:
text: An adapted, integrable process $(M_n)$ is a martingale when: The defining property is $\mathbb{E}[M_{n+1}\mid\mathcal{F}_n]=M_n$. Constant mean and independent increments are consequences (or stronger conditions) but not equivalent. Monotonicity contradicts the fair-game intuition. The martingale identity is the conditional one: given everything up to time $n$, the best predictor of $M_{n+1}$ is $M_n$.

q: Let $(X_i)$ be i.i.d.\ with $\mathbb{P}(X_i=+1)=\mathbb{P}(X_i=-1)=1/2$ and $S_n=X_1+\cdots+X_n$. Which of the following is also a martingale w.r.t. $\mathcal{F}_n=\sigma(X_1,\ldots,X_n)$?
text: Let $(X_i)$ be i.i.d.\ with $\mathbb{P}(X_i=+1)=\mathbb{P}(X_i=-1)=1/2$ and $S_n=X_1+\cdots+X_n$. Which of the following is also a martingale w.r.t. $\mathcal{F}_n=\sigma(X_1,\ldots,X_n)$? $\mathbb{E}[S_{n+1}^2\mid\mathcal{F}_n]=S_n^2+\mathbb{E}[X_{n+1}^2]+2S_n\mathbb{E}[X_{n+1}]=S_n^2+1$, so $S_n^2-n$ subtracts off the predictable drift to give a martingale. $S_n^2$ is a submartingale; $|S_n|$ likewise; $nS_n$ has drift $S_n$. Compute $\mathbb{E}[S_{n+1}^2\mid\mathcal{F}_n]=\mathbb{E}[(S_n+X_{n+1})^2\mid\mathcal{F}_n]=S_n^2+1$.

q: A Doob martingale is the process
text: A Doob martingale is the process By the tower property $\mathbb{E}[\mathbb{E}[Z\mid\mathcal{F}_{n+1}]\mid\mathcal{F}_n]=\mathbb{E}[Z\mid\mathcal{F}_n]$, so $M_n=\mathbb{E}[Z\mid\mathcal{F}_n]$ is a martingale. Sums and products of i.i.d.\ variables can be martingales too, but those are not the Doob construction. The Doob martingale of a random variable $Z$ is its sequence of conditional expectations along an increasing filtration; the tower property gives the martingale identity automatically.

q: Doob's optional-stopping theorem $\mathbb{E}[M_\tau]=\mathbb{E}[M_0]$ holds when $M$ is a martingale and $\tau$ a stopping time provided
text: Doob's optional-stopping theorem $\mathbb{E}[M_\tau]=\mathbb{E}[M_0]$ holds when $M$ is a martingale and $\tau$ a stopping time provided All three are sufficient; the symmetric random walk stopped at $\tau=\inf\{n:S_n=1\}$ has $\tau<\infty$ a.s. but $\mathbb{E}[S_\tau]=1\ne 0$, showing none of the hypotheses can be dropped. Three classical sufficient hypotheses: bounded $\tau$, uniform integrability of $M$, or a.s.-finite $\tau$ with $(M_{n\wedge\tau})$ uniformly bounded.

q: Symmetric random walk $S_0=0$, $\tau=\inf\{n:S_n\in\{-3,5\}\}$. Use optional stopping on $S_n$ to compute $\mathbb{P}(S_\tau=5)$.
text: Symmetric random walk $S_0=0$, $\tau=\inf\{n:S_n\in\{-3,5\}\}$. Use optional stopping on $S_n$ to compute $\mathbb{P}(S_\tau=5)$. Optional stopping (case (c)): $0=\mathbb{E}[S_0]=\mathbb{E}[S_\tau]=5p-3(1-p)=8p-3$, so $p=3/8=0.375$. The same trick with $S_n^2-n$ gives $\mathbb{E}[\tau]=15$. $\tau$ is bounded a.s.\ and $(S_{n\wedge\tau})$ is bounded by $\max(|-3|,5)=5$, so optional stopping gives $0=\mathbb{E}[S_\tau]=5p+(-3)(1-p)$.

q: Wald's identity says: for i.i.d.\ integrable $X_i$ with $\mathbb{E}[X_i]=\mu$ and a stopping time $\tau$ with $\mathbb{E}[\tau]<\infty$,
text: Wald's identity says: for i.i.d.\ integrable $X_i$ with $\mathbb{E}[X_i]=\mu$ and a stopping time $\tau$ with $\mathbb{E}[\tau]<\infty$, The martingale $M_n=S_n-n\mu$ has $\mathbb{E}[M_\tau]=0$ by optional stopping (under $\mathbb{E}\tau<\infty$ and appropriate integrability), giving $\mathbb{E}[S_\tau]=\mu\,\mathbb{E}[\tau]$. This is Wald's identity. Apply optional stopping to the centred martingale $S_n-n\mu$: under $\mathbb{E}\tau<\infty$ one gets $\mathbb{E}[S_\tau-\mu\tau]=0$.

q: The Doob decomposition writes an adapted $L^1$ process $X_n$ uniquely as
text: The Doob decomposition writes an adapted $L^1$ process $X_n$ uniquely as The compensator is $A_n=\sum_{k=1}^n\mathbb{E}[X_k-X_{k-1}\mid\mathcal{F}_{k-1}]$ (predictable with $A_0=0$); subtracting it gives the martingale part. Sub/super-martingales are exactly those for which $A$ is non-decreasing/non-increasing. Doob: every adapted $L^1$ process splits uniquely as a martingale plus a predictable compensator (with $A_0=0$).

q: Symmetric simple random walk $S_n$, $X_n=S_n^2$. The predictable compensator is $A_n=n$. What is $\mathbb{E}[A_{10}]$?
text: Symmetric simple random walk $S_n$, $X_n=S_n^2$. The predictable compensator is $A_n=n$. What is $\mathbb{E}[A_{10}]$? $\mathbb{E}[X_k-X_{k-1}\mid\mathcal{F}_{k-1}]=\mathbb{E}[2S_{k-1}X_k+X_k^2\mid\mathcal{F}_{k-1}]=0+1=1$, so $A_n=n$. In particular $\mathbb{E}[A_{10}]=10$, and $S_n^2-n$ is the martingale part. Each step contributes $\mathbb{E}[(S_k-S_{k-1})^2\mid\mathcal{F}_{k-1}]=1$, so $A_n=n$ deterministically.

q: For a square-integrable martingale $M$ with $M_0=0$, the predictable quadratic variation $\langle M\rangle_n$ is
text: For a square-integrable martingale $M$ with $M_0=0$, the predictable quadratic variation $\langle M\rangle_n$ is $\langle M\rangle_n$ is the Doob compensator of the submartingale $M_n^2$: predictable, increasing, $\langle M\rangle_0=0$, and $M_n^2-\langle M\rangle_n$ is a martingale. The pathwise sum $[M]_n=\sum(\Delta M_k)^2$ is the (different) optional quadratic variation. By definition $\langle M\rangle$ is the unique predictable increasing process making $M_n^2-\langle M\rangle_n$ a martingale.

q: Doob's martingale convergence theorem says: if $(M_n)$ is a martingale with $\sup_n\mathbb{E}|M_n|<\infty$, then
text: Doob's martingale convergence theorem says: if $(M_n)$ is a martingale with $\sup_n\mathbb{E}|M_n|<\infty$, then $L^1$-boundedness implies a.s. convergence (Doob's upcrossing inequality) to an $L^1$ random variable $M_\infty$. Without UI, $L^1$-convergence can fail and the limit need not equal $M_0$ in expectation. $L^1$-boundedness gives a.s. convergence to an $L^1$ limit; $L^1$-convergence requires the stronger uniform-integrability hypothesis.

q: Let $X_i$ be i.i.d.\ with $\mathbb{P}(X_i=2)=\mathbb{P}(X_i=0)=1/2$, and $M_n=\prod_{i=1}^n X_i$ (product martingale). Which is true?
text: Let $X_i$ be i.i.d.\ with $\mathbb{P}(X_i=2)=\mathbb{P}(X_i=0)=1/2$, and $M_n=\prod_{i=1}^n X_i$ (product martingale). Which is true? $M_n$ is a non-negative martingale with $\mathbb{E}[M_n]=1$, but $M_n=0$ once any $X_i$ takes the value $0$, which happens a.s. So $M_n\to 0$ almost surely. The convergence is not in $L^1$ — this is the canonical $L^1$-bounded-but-not-UI example. $\mathbb{E}[X_i]=1$ so $\mathbb{E}[M_n]=1$ by independence. But $M_n=0$ as soon as any $X_i=0$, which happens a.s.\ in finitely many steps.

q: For the $L^1$-convergence $M_n\to M_\infty$ in $L^1$ to hold, the additional hypothesis required is
text: For the $L^1$-convergence $M_n\to M_\infty$ in $L^1$ to hold, the additional hypothesis required is Uniform integrability is the precise condition: a martingale converges in $L^1$ iff it is uniformly integrable, in which case $M_n=\mathbb{E}[M_\infty\mid\mathcal{F}_n]$. $L^2$-boundedness is sufficient but stronger than necessary. UI is exactly what closes the gap between $L^1$-bounded and $L^1$-convergent: $M_n\to M_\infty$ in $L^1$ iff $(M_n)$ is UI.

q: Standard Brownian motion $W_t$ has
text: Standard Brownian motion $W_t$ has Independent stationary Gaussian increments $W_t-W_s\sim\mathcal{N}(0,t-s)$ and continuous (but nowhere differentiable, infinite total variation) paths characterise Brownian motion. $W_0=0$, increments are independent and $\mathcal{N}(0,t-s)$-distributed for $0\le s<t$, and paths are continuous but nowhere differentiable.

q: Which of the following is NOT a martingale w.r.t.\ the natural filtration of Brownian motion?
text: Which of the following is NOT a martingale w.r.t.\ the natural filtration of Brownian motion? $\mathbb{E}[W_t^2\mid\mathcal{F}_s]=W_s^2+(t-s)>W_s^2$, so $W_t^2$ is a submartingale, not a martingale. The compensator $t$ subtracts off, leaving $W_t^2-t$ as the martingale; the exponential martingale is the engine of Girsanov. $W_t^2$ is a submartingale; subtracting the predictable $t$ gives the martingale $W_t^2-t$.
