q: For Brownian motion on $[0,T]$, the quadratic variation $[W]_T$ along a refining partition converges (in probability) to what value when $T=2$?
text: For Brownian motion on $[0,T]$, the quadratic variation $[W]_T$ along a refining partition converges (in probability) to what value when $T=2$? $\sum_k(W_{t_{k+1}}-W_{t_k})^2\to t$ in probability as the mesh shrinks; at $t=T=2$ this equals $2$. This is the signature of Brownian paths: finite quadratic variation, infinite total variation. $[W]_t=t$ a.s.\ — quadratic variation along any sequence of partitions with mesh $\to 0$ converges in probability to $t$.

title: Itô integral
blurb: For predictable $H$ with $\mathbb{E}\int_0^T H_s^2\,ds<\infty$, the Itô integral $\int_0^t H_s\,dB_s$ is built as the $L^2$ limit of left-endpoint Riemann sums on simple processes; the resulting process is a continuous $L^2$-martingale and the choice of evaluation point is what distinguishes Itô from Stratonovich.

title: Itô's formula
blurb: For $C^2$ functions and Itô process $X_t$, $df(X_t)=f'(X_t)\,dX_t+\tfrac{1}{2}f''(X_t)\,(dX_t)^2$ — the chain rule corrected by the quadratic-variation term that classical calculus misses; equivalently $f(B_t)=f(0)+\int_0^t f'(B_s)\,dB_s+\tfrac{1}{2}\int_0^t f''(B_s)\,ds$.

title: Stochastic differential equations
blurb: For Lipschitz coefficients $b,\sigma$, the SDE $dX_t=b(X_t)\,dt+\sigma(X_t)\,dB_t$ has a unique strong solution by Picard iteration; weak solutions match a target law without fixing the driving Brownian motion, and geometric Brownian motion $dX_t=\mu X_t\,dt+\sigma X_t\,dB_t$ is the canonical multiplicative example.

title: Girsanov's theorem
blurb: The exponential martingale $Z_t=\exp(\int_0^t\theta_s\,dB_s-\tfrac{1}{2}\int_0^t\theta_s^2\,ds)$ defines a measure $\mathbb{Q}=Z_T\,d\mathbb{P}$ under which $\tilde B_t=B_t-\int_0^t\theta_s\,ds$ is Brownian — drifts are absorbed into the measure, the workhorse behind risk-neutral pricing.

title: Feynman–Kac formula
blurb: The solution of the heat-type PDE $\partial_t u+\tfrac{1}{2}\sigma^2\partial_{xx}u+b\,\partial_x u-ru=0$ with terminal data $u(T,x)=g(x)$ equals $\mathbb{E}[e^{-r(T-t)}g(X_T)\mid X_t=x]$ — Brownian expectations solve linear parabolic PDEs and conversely.

title: Applications: Black–Scholes, Kalman, optimal stopping
blurb: Risk-neutral pricing turns Black–Scholes into a Feynman–Kac integral; the Kalman filter is the conditional-expectation projection for a linear-Gaussian state-space SDE; American option pricing is an optimal-stopping problem and free-boundary PDE.

q: Which evaluation point for the integrand on each subinterval $[t_k,t_{k+1}]$ defines the Itô integral?
text: Which evaluation point for the integrand on each subinterval $[t_k,t_{k+1}]$ defines the Itô integral? Itô uses the left endpoint so the integrand is predictable; the resulting integral is then a martingale. The midpoint convention gives Stratonovich, which obeys the classical chain rule but is not a martingale. The choice that keeps the integrand $\mathcal{F}_{t_k}$-measurable, i.e. predictable, is the Itô convention.

q: What integrability condition makes $\int_0^t H_s\,dB_s$ a square-integrable martingale?
text: What integrability condition makes $\int_0^t H_s\,dB_s$ a square-integrable martingale? By the Itô isometry $\mathbb{E}[(\int_0^t H\,dB)^2]=\mathbb{E}\int_0^t H^2\,ds$, so finiteness of the right side is precisely the $L^2$ condition that makes the integral a square-integrable martingale. The Itô isometry equates $\mathbb{E}[(\int H\,dB)^2]$ with $\mathbb{E}\int H^2\,ds$.

q: What is $\mathbb{E}\big[\int_0^t B_s\,dB_s\big]$?
text: What is $\mathbb{E}\big[\int_0^t B_s\,dB_s\big]$? $\int_0^t B_s\,dB_s$ is a martingale starting at $0$, so its expectation is $0$. Itô's formula identifies it as $\tfrac{1}{2}B_t^2-\tfrac{1}{2}t$, whose expectation is also $0$. Itô integrals against Brownian motion are martingales started at $0$.

q: By Itô's formula, what is $d(B_t^2)$ for standard Brownian motion?
text: By Itô's formula, what is $d(B_t^2)$ for standard Brownian motion? With $f(x)=x^2$, $df(B_t)=2B_t\,dB_t+\tfrac{1}{2}\cdot 2\cdot dt=2B_t\,dB_t+dt$. The extra $dt$ is the Itô correction that classical calculus misses. $f(x)=x^2$ has $f'(x)=2x$ and $f''(x)=2$; $(dB_t)^2=dt$.

q: In Itô's formula $df(X_t)=f'(X_t)\,dX_t+\tfrac{1}{2}f''(X_t)(dX_t)^2$, the $(dX_t)^2$ term equals:
text: In Itô's formula $df(X_t)=f'(X_t)\,dX_t+\tfrac{1}{2}f''(X_t)(dX_t)^2$, the $(dX_t)^2$ term equals: $(dX_t)^2$ is shorthand for $d\langle X\rangle_t$. For an Itô process $dX_t=b\,dt+\sigma\,dB_t$ this evaluates to $\sigma^2\,dt$ — only the diffusion term contributes since $(dt)^2=0$ and $dt\,dB=0$. For Brownian motion $\langle B\rangle_t=t$, but for $dX=b\,dt+\sigma\,dB$ the variation is $\sigma^2\,dt$.

q: Apply Itô to $f(t,x)=e^{x}$ with $X_t=B_t$. What is $d(e^{B_t})$?
text: Apply Itô to $f(t,x)=e^{x}$ with $X_t=B_t$. What is $d(e^{B_t})$? $df(B_t)=e^{B_t}\,dB_t+\tfrac{1}{2}e^{B_t}\,dt$. To get a martingale you must subtract the drift, giving the exponential martingale $\exp(B_t-t/2)$. $f'=f''=e^x$ for $f(x)=e^x$; the Itô correction $\tfrac{1}{2}f''\,dt$ adds.

q: What condition on the coefficients $b,\sigma$ guarantees a unique strong solution to $dX_t=b(X_t)\,dt+\sigma(X_t)\,dB_t$?
text: What condition on the coefficients $b,\sigma$ guarantees a unique strong solution to $dX_t=b(X_t)\,dt+\sigma(X_t)\,dB_t$? Lipschitz coefficients (with linear growth) make the Picard iteration in $L^2$ a contraction, yielding existence and pathwise uniqueness — strong solutions in the standard Itô sense. Picard iteration needs a contraction; Lipschitz constants control the iteration's growth.

q: For geometric Brownian motion $dX_t=\mu X_t\,dt+\sigma X_t\,dB_t$ with $X_0=x_0>0$, the closed-form solution is:
text: For geometric Brownian motion $dX_t=\mu X_t\,dt+\sigma X_t\,dB_t$ with $X_0=x_0>0$, the closed-form solution is: Itô on $\log X_t$ gives $d\log X_t=(\mu-\sigma^2/2)\,dt+\sigma\,dB_t$, integrating to $X_t=x_0\exp((\mu-\sigma^2/2)t+\sigma B_t)$. The $-\sigma^2/2$ is the Itô correction. Apply Itô to $\log X_t$; the variance correction $-\sigma^2/2$ appears.

q: A weak solution to an SDE is one where:
text: A weak solution to an SDE is one where: A weak solution allows freedom in choosing the underlying probability space and Brownian motion; only the joint law of $(X,B)$ is required to satisfy the SDE. Strong solutions fix $B$ in advance and demand a pathwise solution. Strong = pathwise on a fixed $B$; weak = only the joint law is required.

q: Under Girsanov with density $Z_T=\exp(\int_0^T\theta_s\,dB_s-\tfrac{1}{2}\int_0^T\theta_s^2\,ds)$, the process $\tilde B_t=B_t-\int_0^t\theta_s\,ds$ is:
text: Under Girsanov with density $Z_T=\exp(\int_0^T\theta_s\,dB_s-\tfrac{1}{2}\int_0^T\theta_s^2\,ds)$, the process $\tilde B_t=B_t-\int_0^t\theta_s\,ds$ is: Girsanov's theorem states exactly this: under the new measure $\mathbb{Q}$ defined by Radon–Nikodym density $Z_T$, the drift-corrected process $\tilde B$ is a standard Brownian motion. Girsanov absorbs the drift $\int\theta\,ds$ into the measure change.

q: Why is the exponential martingale $Z_t=\exp(\int_0^t\theta_s\,dB_s-\tfrac{1}{2}\int_0^t\theta_s^2\,ds)$ a martingale (under Novikov)?
text: Why is the exponential martingale $Z_t=\exp(\int_0^t\theta_s\,dB_s-\tfrac{1}{2}\int_0^t\theta_s^2\,ds)$ a martingale (under Novikov)? Itô on $\log Z_t$ gives $dZ_t=Z_t\theta_t\,dB_t$ — drift-free. Under Novikov's condition $\mathbb{E}\exp(\tfrac{1}{2}\int\theta^2\,ds)<\infty$, this local martingale is a true martingale. Apply Itô to $Z_t$; the $-\tfrac{1}{2}\theta^2\,dt$ in the exponent kills the Itô correction.

q: In risk-neutral pricing, the change of measure from $\mathbb{P}$ to $\mathbb{Q}$:
text: In risk-neutral pricing, the change of measure from $\mathbb{P}$ to $\mathbb{Q}$: Girsanov shifts only the drift: under $\mathbb{Q}$ the discounted asset is a martingale, so $\mu$ is replaced by $r$. Volatility $\sigma$ is invariant under absolutely continuous measure change. Under $\mathbb{Q}$ discounted prices are martingales; only the drift, not the volatility, shifts.

q: The Feynman–Kac formula expresses the solution of which type of PDE as an expectation?
text: The Feynman–Kac formula expresses the solution of which type of PDE as an expectation? Feynman–Kac links second-order linear parabolic and elliptic PDEs (whose generator matches the SDE's infinitesimal generator) to Brownian-functional expectations. The wave equation, by contrast, is hyperbolic and admits no such probabilistic representation. Brownian motion's generator is $\tfrac{1}{2}\Delta$ — a second-order operator.

q: If $u(t,x)=\mathbb{E}[g(B_T)\mid B_t=x]$, then $u$ satisfies:
text: If $u(t,x)=\mathbb{E}[g(B_T)\mid B_t=x]$, then $u$ satisfies: Conditional expectations of a terminal payoff solve the backward heat equation $\partial_t u+\tfrac{1}{2}\partial_{xx}u=0$ with terminal condition $u(T,x)=g(x)$, by Itô applied to $u(t,B_t)$. The backward heat equation with terminal data $g$ at time $T$.

q: Adding a discount factor $e^{-r(T-t)}$ to Feynman–Kac corresponds to which PDE term?
text: Adding a discount factor $e^{-r(T-t)}$ to Feynman–Kac corresponds to which PDE term? Discounting in the expectation corresponds to a $-ru$ potential term in the PDE: $\partial_t u+\tfrac{1}{2}\sigma^2\partial_{xx}u+b\partial_x u-ru=0$. This is the Black–Scholes equation. $e^{-rt}$ in the Itô formula for $u(t,X_t)e^{-rt}$ produces a $-ru$ term.

q: In the Black–Scholes model, the price of a European call is:
text: In the Black–Scholes model, the price of a European call is: Under the risk-neutral measure $\mathbb{Q}$, discounted asset prices are martingales, and the no-arbitrage price equals the discounted expected payoff. Feynman–Kac then identifies this with the Black–Scholes PDE. Risk-neutral pricing combines Girsanov (drift change) with Feynman–Kac (PDE ↔ expectation).

q: The Kalman filter is the optimal estimator for which class of stochastic models?
text: The Kalman filter is the optimal estimator for which class of stochastic models? For linear Gaussian state-space models the conditional law given observations remains Gaussian, so the conditional mean and covariance close under recursion — the Kalman filter equations. Nonlinear or non-Gaussian models need particle filters or other approximations. Linearity + Gaussian noise = closed-form recursive conditional expectations.

q: Pricing an American option leads to which mathematical structure?
text: Pricing an American option leads to which mathematical structure? American options give the holder the right to exercise at any stopping time $\tau\le T$. The price is $\sup_\tau\mathbb{E}_\mathbb{Q}[e^{-r\tau}g(X_\tau)]$, an optimal-stopping problem whose value function solves a free-boundary (variational) PDE. The holder chooses a stopping time $\tau$ to maximise $\mathbb{E}[e^{-r\tau}g(X_\tau)]$.

title: Markov chains and transition matrices
blurb: A discrete-time Markov chain on a finite or countable state space is encoded by a row-stochastic transition matrix $P$; the $n$-step law is $P^n$, and stationary distributions satisfy $\pi P=\pi$.

title: Stationary distributions
blurb: Every finite irreducible aperiodic chain has a unique stationary $\pi$ that is the left Perron eigenvector of $P$; detailed balance $\pi_iP_{ij}=\pi_jP_{ji}$ identifies it for reversible chains.

title: Mixing time
blurb: $d(t)=\max_x \|P^t(x,\cdot)-\pi\|_{\mathrm{TV}}$ measures distance to stationarity; the mixing time $t_{\mathrm{mix}}(\varepsilon)=\min\{t:d(t)\le\varepsilon\}$ is the standard quantitative cost of forgetting the start.

title: Spectral gap and rapid mixing
blurb: For reversible chains the second eigenvalue $\lambda_2$ of $P$ controls mixing: $t_{\mathrm{mix}}(\varepsilon)\le \frac{1}{1-\lambda_2}\log\frac{1}{\pi_{\min}\varepsilon}$ (and a matching lower bound up to constants).

title: Coupling for upper bounds on mixing
blurb: The coupling lemma bounds total-variation distance by the meeting probability of two chains driven on a common probability space; lazy walks on the hypercube and card-shuffles fall out cleanly.

title: Markov-chain Monte Carlo
blurb: Metropolis–Hastings designs a chain with a prescribed stationary distribution $\pi$ via an acceptance ratio; mixing time is the cost of one effectively-independent sample, so spectral-gap bounds become statistical bounds.

q: A row-stochastic matrix $P$ on a finite state space $S$ encodes a Markov chain. Which of the following must hold for every $i\in S$?
text: A row-stochastic matrix $P$ on a finite state space $S$ encodes a Markov chain. Which of the following must hold for every $i\in S$? Row-stochastic means each row is a probability distribution over next states: nonneg entries summing to $1$. Column sums need not be $1$ (that would be doubly stochastic), and $P$ need not be symmetric or invertible.

q: For the chain with $P=\begin{pmatrix}0.5&0.5\\0.25&0.75\end{pmatrix}$, compute the $(1,2)$ entry of $P^2$ (probability of going $1\to 2$ in two steps).
text: For the chain with $P=\begin{pmatrix}0.5&0.5\\0.25&0.75\end{pmatrix}$, compute the $(1,2)$ entry of $P^2$ (probability of going $1\to 2$ in two steps). $(P^2)_{12} = P_{11}P_{12}+P_{12}P_{22} = 0.5\cdot 0.5 + 0.5\cdot 0.75 = 0.25+0.375 = 0.625$.

q: What does the Markov property $\mathbb{P}(X_{n+1}=y\mid X_0,\ldots,X_n)=\mathbb{P}(X_{n+1}=y\mid X_n)$ assert?
text: What does the Markov property $\mathbb{P}(X_{n+1}=y\mid X_0,\ldots,X_n)=\mathbb{P}(X_{n+1}=y\mid X_n)$ assert? The Markov / memoryless property: conditional on the current state $X_n$, the future is independent of the past $X_0,\ldots,X_{n-1}$. Stationarity is a separate condition (time-homogeneity).

q: A probability vector $\pi$ is stationary for $P$ when:
text: A probability vector $\pi$ is stationary for $P$ when: Stationarity: applying one step leaves the distribution unchanged. As a row vector, $\pi P=\pi$. (Equivalently $P^\top\pi^\top=\pi^\top$, but the right-multiplication form is the convention.)

q: Two-state chain with $P=\begin{pmatrix}0.6&0.4\\0.2&0.8\end{pmatrix}$. Solve $\pi P=\pi$ for $\pi_1$ (mass on state 1).
text: Two-state chain with $P=\begin{pmatrix}0.6&0.4\\0.2&0.8\end{pmatrix}$. Solve $\pi P=\pi$ for $\pi_1$ (mass on state 1). $0.6\pi_1+0.2(1-\pi_1)=\pi_1$ gives $0.2=0.6\pi_1$, so $\pi_1=1/3$. Check: $\pi_2=2/3$, and $0.4\cdot 1/3 + 0.8\cdot 2/3 = 2/3$.

q: A chain on $\{1,2,3,4\}$ has two closed classes $\{1,2\}$ and $\{3,4\}$, each with its own stationary distribution. How many stationary distributions does the full chain have?
text: A chain on $\{1,2,3,4\}$ has two closed classes $\{1,2\}$ and $\{3,4\}$, each with its own stationary distribution. How many stationary distributions does the full chain have? Each closed class contributes a stationary $\pi^{(k)}$; any convex combination $\alpha\pi^{(1)}+(1-\alpha)\pi^{(2)}$, $\alpha\in[0,1]$, is also stationary. Uniqueness needs irreducibility.

q: Total variation distance between probability measures $\mu,\nu$ on a finite set is
text:
Total variation distance between probability measures $\mu,
u$ on a finite set is $\|\mu-
u\|_{\mathrm{TV}}=	frac{1}{2}\sum_x|\mu(x)-
u(x)|=\sup_A|\mu(A)-
u(A)|$. The factor of $	frac{1}{2}$ converts the L¹ norm into the supremum-of-events characterisation.

q: The mixing time $t_{\mathrm{mix}}(\varepsilon)$ is defined as:
text: The mixing time $t_{\mathrm{mix}}(\varepsilon)$ is defined as: Mixing time is the first time the worst-case TV distance to $\pi$ drops below $\varepsilon$ (the convention $\varepsilon=1/4$ or $1/(2e)$ is common). Return times and the spectral gap are related but distinct.

q: For a reversible irreducible aperiodic Markov chain with transition matrix $P$, the eigenvalues lie in $[-1,1]$ with $\lambda_1=1$. The (absolute) spectral gap is
text: For a reversible irreducible aperiodic Markov chain with transition matrix $P$, the eigenvalues lie in $[-1,1]$ with $\lambda_1=1$. The (absolute) spectral gap is The absolute gap is $1$ minus the second-largest eigenvalue in magnitude. The relevant nontrivial eigenvalues are $\lambda_2$ (slow-mixing direction) and $\lambda_n$ (parity / near-bipartite); both can be the bottleneck.

q: A reversible chain has $\lambda_2 = 0.9$ and $\pi_{\min}=0.01$. The spectral mixing-time bound is $t_{\mathrm{mix}}(\varepsilon)\le\frac{1}{1-\lambda_2}\log\frac{1}{\pi_{\min}\varepsilon}$. With $\varepsilon=1/4$, give the bound (rounded to nearest integer).
text: A reversible chain has $\lambda_2 = 0.9$ and $\pi_{\min}=0.01$. The spectral mixing-time bound is $t_{\mathrm{mix}}(\varepsilon)\le\frac{1}{1-\lambda_2}\log\frac{1}{\pi_{\min}\varepsilon}$. With $\varepsilon=1/4$, give the bound (rounded to nearest integer). $\frac{1}{1-0.9}=10$, and $\log\frac{1}{0.01\cdot 0.25}=\log 400\approx 5.99$, so $t_{\mathrm{mix}}\le 10\cdot 5.99\approx 60$.

q: If a chain has $\lambda_2$ very close to $1$, what can you conclude about mixing?
text: If a chain has $\lambda_2$ very close to $1$, what can you conclude about mixing? $\lambda_2\to 1$ corresponds to a near-disconnection (Cheeger): a slowly-decaying mode persists for time $\sim 1/(1-\lambda_2)$, so mixing is slow. Bipartiteness is the $\lambda_n=-1$ end.

q: The coupling inequality says that for any coupling $(X_t,Y_t)$ of two copies of the chain with arbitrary starting laws,
text: The coupling inequality says that for any coupling $(X_t,Y_t)$ of two copies of the chain with arbitrary starting laws, The fundamental coupling inequality: TV distance is bounded by the probability that the two copies have not yet met under the coupling. To prove rapid mixing, design a coupling under which meeting is fast.

q: Lazy random walk on the hypercube $\{0,1\}^n$ with $n=10$: at each step, pick a coordinate uniformly and flip it with probability $1/2$. Coupon-collector heuristics give mixing time $\Theta(n\log n)$. Estimate $n\log n$ for $n=10$ (use natural log, round to integer).
text: Lazy random walk on the hypercube $\{0,1\}^n$ with $n=10$: at each step, pick a coordinate uniformly and flip it with probability $1/2$. Coupon-collector heuristics give mixing time $\Theta(n\log n)$. Estimate $n\log n$ for $n=10$ (use natural log, round to integer). $n\log n = 10\ln 10\approx 10\cdot 2.30=23.0$. The standard coupling — couple coordinates as soon as both copies have updated each one — gives mixing in time $\sim \tfrac{1}{2}n\log n$ via coupon collector.

q: Why does coupling only provide an upper bound on mixing time (and not also a matching lower bound)?
text: Why does coupling only provide an upper bound on mixing time (and not also a matching lower bound)? There exists an optimal coupling for which the inequality is an equality (Strassen / Aldous). But finding it is usually impossible, so a constructed coupling gives only an upper bound; lower bounds require different tools (conductance, distinguishing statistics).

q: In Metropolis–Hastings with target distribution $\pi$ and proposal density $q(y\mid x)$, the acceptance probability is
text: In Metropolis–Hastings with target distribution $\pi$ and proposal density $q(y\mid x)$, the acceptance probability is Metropolis–Hastings acceptance ratio. The factor $q(x\mid y)/q(y\mid x)$ is the proposal-asymmetry correction; for symmetric proposals (Metropolis) it reduces to $\pi(y)/\pi(x)$. Detailed balance with $\pi$ holds by construction.

q: Why is mixing time the relevant cost of MCMC sampling, rather than total runtime?
text: Why is mixing time the relevant cost of MCMC sampling, rather than total runtime? After $t_{\mathrm{mix}}$ steps the chain has 'forgotten' its start — past samples are essentially unrelated to the current one. So drawing $N$ approximately-independent samples costs $\sim N\cdot t_{\mathrm{mix}}$ chain steps.

q: A symmetric Metropolis sampler targeting $\pi(x)\propto e^{-x^2/2}$ on $\mathbb{R}$ proposes $y=x+\eta$ with $\eta\sim\mathcal{N}(0,1)$. From $x=0$, what is the acceptance probability for $y=2$? (Round to 3 decimals.)
text: A symmetric Metropolis sampler targeting $\pi(x)\propto e^{-x^2/2}$ on $\mathbb{R}$ proposes $y=x+\eta$ with $\eta\sim\mathcal{N}(0,1)$. From $x=0$, what is the acceptance probability for $y=2$? (Round to 3 decimals.) $\pi(2)/\pi(0) = e^{-2}/e^0 = e^{-2}\approx 0.135$. Symmetric proposal so the $q$-ratio is $1$; acceptance is $\min(1,\pi(y)/\pi(x))=e^{-2}$.

title: Shannon entropy
blurb: For a discrete random variable $X$ with pmf $p$, $H(X)=-\sum_i p_i\log p_i$ measures average uncertainty in bits (log base $2$) or nats (log base $e$); it is concave in $p$, vanishes on point masses, and is maximised at $\log n$ by the uniform law on $n$ symbols.

title: Mutual information
blurb: $I(X;Y)=H(X)-H(X\mid Y)=H(X)+H(Y)-H(X,Y)$ measures the bits about $X$ revealed by observing $Y$; equivalently $I(X;Y)=D(p_{XY}\,\|\,p_X p_Y)$, the KL distance from the joint to the product of marginals.

title: Kullback–Leibler divergence
blurb: $D(p\,\|\,q)=\sum_i p_i\log(p_i/q_i)$ measures the inefficiency of coding $p$-distributed data with a $q$-tuned codebook; Gibbs' inequality gives $D(p\,\|\,q)\ge 0$ with equality iff $p=q$, and $D$ is the canonical rate function of Sanov's theorem.

title: Source coding
blurb: Shannon's source-coding theorem: $H(X)$ is the asymptotic minimum bits-per-symbol for lossless compression. Huffman coding achieves prefix-free codes with average length within one bit of $H(X)$; the asymptotic equipartition property (AEP) explains why typical sequences fill a set of size $\approx 2^{nH(X)}$.

title: Channel coding
blurb: The capacity of a discrete memoryless channel is $C=\max_{p_X}I(X;Y)$. Shannon's noisy-channel theorem: any rate $R C$ has error bounded away from $0$ — capacity is the sharp threshold.

title: Rate–distortion
blurb: For lossy compression with distortion measure $d$ and tolerance $D$, the rate–distortion function is $R(D)=\min\{I(X;\hat X):\mathbb{E}[d(X,\hat X)]\le D\}$ — the minimum bits per symbol that still keep expected distortion under $D$. For a Gaussian source with squared-error distortion, $R(D)=\tfrac{1}{2}\log(\sigma^2/D)$ on $[0,\sigma^2]$.

q: Compute $H(X)$ in bits for the fair coin: $X\in\{0,1\}$ with $p_0=p_1=1/2$.
text: Compute $H(X)$ in bits for the fair coin: $X\in\{0,1\}$ with $p_0=p_1=1/2$. $H(X) = -\sum_i p_i \log_2 p_i = -2\cdot\tfrac{1}{2}\log_2(1/2) = 1$ bit. The fair coin is the maximum-entropy law on two symbols. $H = -2\cdot\tfrac{1}{2}\log_2\tfrac{1}{2} = 1$ bit.

q: Which distribution on $\{1,\ldots,n\}$ maximises $H(X)$?
text: Which distribution on $\{1,\ldots,n\}$ maximises $H(X)$? By Jensen / Lagrange multipliers, the unique maximiser is uniform with $H = \log n$. Point masses give $H = 0$ (the minimum). Concavity of $-x\log x$ with the constraint $\sum p_i=1$ pushes mass to spread out.

q: Binary entropy $h(p) = -p\log_2 p - (1-p)\log_2(1-p)$. Compute $h(1/4)$ in bits (round to 3 decimals).
text: Binary entropy $h(p) = -p\log_2 p - (1-p)\log_2(1-p)$. Compute $h(1/4)$ in bits (round to 3 decimals). $h(1/4) = 0.5 + 0.75\cdot 0.4150 \approx 0.8113$ bits. The binary entropy peaks at $p=1/2$ with value $1$ and is symmetric in $p\leftrightarrow 1-p$. $h(1/4) = -\tfrac{1}{4}\log_2\tfrac{1}{4} - \tfrac{3}{4}\log_2\tfrac{3}{4} = \tfrac{1}{2} + \tfrac{3}{4}\log_2(4/3)$.

q: Which identity for mutual information is FALSE?
text: Which identity for mutual information is FALSE? $H(X,Y) - H(X\mid Y) - H(Y\mid X) = I(X;Y)$ would require subtracting only one conditional, not both. The correct identity is $H(X,Y) = H(X\mid Y) + H(Y) = H(Y\mid X) + H(X)$. Subtracting both conditionals from $H(X,Y)$ double-counts the overlap.

q: If $X$ and $Y$ are independent, what is $I(X;Y)$?
text: If $X$ and $Y$ are independent, what is $I(X;Y)$? Independence is exactly $p_{XY} = p_X p_Y$, hence $D(p_{XY}\,\|\,p_X p_Y) = 0$. Conversely $I(X;Y) = 0$ implies independence. Independence means the joint factors as $p_{XY} = p_X p_Y$, so the KL distance to the product is $0$.

q: The data-processing inequality states that for a Markov chain $X\to Y\to Z$:
text: The data-processing inequality states that for a Markov chain $X\to Y\to Z$: For any Markov chain $X\to Y\to Z$, $I(X;Z) \le I(X;Y)$ — passing through $Y$ is a bottleneck that can only lose information about $X$. This is the data-processing inequality. Post-processing $Y$ into $Z$ cannot create information about $X$ that $Y$ did not already carry.

q: Which property of $D(p\,\|\,q)$ is FALSE?
text: Which property of $D(p\,\|\,q)$ is FALSE? $D(p\,\|\,q)$ is generally not symmetric: e.g. with $p=(1/2,1/2)$ and $q=(1,0)$, $D(p\,\|\,q)=\infty$ but $D(q\,\|\,p)=1$ bit. The other three properties hold. $D$ is not symmetric — it's not a metric.

q: Compute $D(p\,\|\,q)$ in bits for $p=(1/2,1/2)$ and $q=(1/4,3/4)$.
In Sanov's theorem, the empirical distribution of $n$ i.i.d.\ samples from $\mu$ deviates to a set $A$ of distributions with probability decaying like $e^{-n\cdot(\,\cdot\,)}$. The exponent is:
text:
In Sanov's theorem, the empirical distribution of $n$ i.i.d.\ samples from $\mu$ deviates to a set $A$ of distributions with probability decaying like $e^{-n\cdot(\,\cdot\,)}$. The exponent is: Sanov's theorem: $\mathbb{P}(L_n \in A) \asymp \exp(-n\inf_{
u\in A} D(
u\,\|\,\mu))$. KL divergence is the canonical large-deviations rate for empirical measures. Sanov picks the closest distribution in $A$ to the truth $\mu$, in KL distance from the candidate $
u$ to $\mu$.

q: Shannon's source-coding theorem says: for an i.i.d.\ source with entropy $H(X)$, the minimum expected bits per symbol of any lossless code is:
text: Shannon's source-coding theorem says: for an i.i.d.\ source with entropy $H(X)$, the minimum expected bits per symbol of any lossless code is: $H(X)$ is the sharp asymptotic rate: any lossless code uses at least $H(X)$ bits per symbol on average, and block-coding achieves $H(X) + \varepsilon$ for any $\varepsilon > 0$ as the block length grows. $H(X)$ is a lower bound on average code length, achievable in the limit.

q: A source emits symbols $\{a,b,c,d\}$ with probabilities $(1/2, 1/4, 1/8, 1/8)$. Compute $H(X)$ in bits.
text: A source emits symbols $\{a,b,c,d\}$ with probabilities $(1/2, 1/4, 1/8, 1/8)$. Compute $H(X)$ in bits. $H(X) = \tfrac{1}{2}\log_2 2 + \tfrac{1}{4}\log_2 4 + \tfrac{1}{8}\log_2 8 + \tfrac{1}{8}\log_2 8 = 0.5 + 0.5 + 0.375 + 0.375 = 1.75$ bits. The Huffman code $\{0, 10, 110, 111\}$ achieves exactly this. $H = \tfrac{1}{2}(1) + \tfrac{1}{4}(2) + \tfrac{1}{8}(3) + \tfrac{1}{8}(3)$.

q: The asymptotic equipartition property (AEP) states that for $n$ i.i.d.\ samples from $X$, the typical set has size approximately:
text: The asymptotic equipartition property (AEP) states that for $n$ i.i.d.\ samples from $X$, the typical set has size approximately: The AEP says most probability mass concentrates on $\approx 2^{nH(X)}$ "typical" sequences, each with probability $\approx 2^{-nH(X)}$. Source coding then needs $\approx nH(X)$ bits to index them. Each typical sequence has probability about $2^{-nH(X)}$, and the total mass is close to $1$.

q: The binary symmetric channel with cross-over probability $p$ has capacity $C = 1 - h(p)$ bits, where $h$ is binary entropy. Compute $C$ for $p = 0$.
text: The binary symmetric channel with cross-over probability $p$ has capacity $C = 1 - h(p)$ bits, where $h$ is binary entropy. Compute $C$ for $p = 0$. $h(0) = 0$, so $C = 1 - 0 = 1$ bit per channel use — the noiseless binary channel is perfectly clear. A noiseless binary channel transmits one bit per use.

q: For the BSC, what is the capacity $C$ in bits when $p = 1/2$?
text: For the BSC, what is the capacity $C$ in bits when $p = 1/2$? At $p = 1/2$ the output is independent of the input — every transmitted bit is randomised — so $C = 0$. The BSC capacity is symmetric around $1/2$ and minimised there. $h(1/2) = 1$, so $C = 1 - 1 = 0$.

q: Shannon's noisy-channel theorem implies which of the following?
text: Shannon's noisy-channel theorem implies which of the following? Shannon's theorem: for any $R C$, error is bounded away from $0$. $C$ is a sharp threshold. Capacity is the sharp threshold between achievable and unachievable rates.

q: The rate–distortion function $R(D)$ for a source $X$ and distortion $d$ is defined as:
text: The rate–distortion function $R(D)$ for a source $X$ and distortion $d$ is defined as: $R(D) = \min_{p_{\hat X\mid X}: \mathbb{E}[d(X,\hat X)]\le D} I(X;\hat X)$. This is the smallest mutual information (≈ bits per symbol) needed to reconstruct $X$ within average distortion $D$. We minimise the bits used while keeping expected distortion below $D$.

q: For a Gaussian source $X\sim\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2)$ with squared-error distortion, $R(D) = \tfrac{1}{2}\log_2(\sigma^2/D)$ for $0 < D \le \sigma^2$. Compute $R(\sigma^2)$ in bits.
text: For a Gaussian source $X\sim\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2)$ with squared-error distortion, $R(D) = \tfrac{1}{2}\log_2(\sigma^2/D)$ for $0 < D \le \sigma^2$. Compute $R(\sigma^2)$ in bits. $R(\sigma^2) = \tfrac{1}{2}\log_2 1 = 0$ bits — at distortion equal to source variance, the trivial reconstruction $\hat X = 0$ suffices and no information needs to be transmitted. When $D = \sigma^2$, the constant $\hat X = 0$ already achieves the distortion bound.

q: Why is $R(D)$ monotonically non-increasing in $D$?
text: Why is $R(D)$ monotonically non-increasing in $D$? Increasing $D$ expands the feasible set $\{p_{\hat X\mid X}: \mathbb{E}[d]\le D\}$, so $\min I(X;\hat X)$ over a larger set is no larger. Hence $R$ is non-increasing in $D$. Larger $D$ admits more reconstruction laws, so the minimum over a larger set is smaller.

title: Cramér's theorem
blurb: For i.i.d.\ $X_i$ with finite log-MGF $\Lambda(\theta)=\log\mathbb{E}[e^{\theta X}]$, the empirical mean $\bar X_n$ satisfies $\mathbb{P}(\bar X_n\ge x)\asymp e^{-nI(x)}$, where $I(x)=\sup_\theta\{\theta x-\Lambda(\theta)\}$ is the Legendre transform of $\Lambda$.

title: Rate functions and convex duality
blurb: A rate function $I:\mathcal{X}\to[0,\infty]$ is lower semicontinuous, equals $0$ at the LLN limit, and is convex when produced by a Legendre transform; its level sets are the compacts that govern the LDP.

title: Sanov's theorem
blurb:
The empirical distribution $L_n=	frac{1}{n}\sum\delta_{X_i}$ of i.i.d.\ samples from $\mu$ satisfies an LDP on the space of probability measures with rate $H(
u\,\|\,\mu)=\int\log(d
u/d\mu)\,d
u$ — Kullback–Leibler divergence is the canonical rate function.

title: Gärtner–Ellis theorem
blurb: When $\Lambda(\theta)=\lim_n\tfrac{1}{n}\log\mathbb{E}[e^{n\theta\bar X_n}]$ exists, is finite, and is essentially smooth, the LDP holds with rate $I=\Lambda^*$ — the Legendre transform extends Cramér beyond the i.i.d.\ case to weakly dependent and Markov sums.

title: Applications: Schilder, Freidlin–Wentzell, entropy
blurb: Schilder's LDP for scaled Brownian paths, Freidlin–Wentzell for small-noise SDEs (rare exits via the action functional), and Boltzmann's $S=k\log W$ recovered as the rate function of an ideal gas — large deviations is the rigorous bridge between probability and statistical mechanics.

q: For i.i.d. $X_i$ with $\Lambda(\theta)=\log\mathbb{E}[e^{\theta X_1}]$, Cramér's theorem says the rate function is:
text: For i.i.d. $X_i$ with $\Lambda(\theta)=\log\mathbb{E}[e^{\theta X_1}]$, Cramér's theorem says the rate function is: Cramér's theorem states $\frac{1}{n}\log\mathbb{P}(\bar X_n\ge x)\to -I(x)$ where $I(x)=\Lambda^*(x)=\sup_\theta\{\theta x-\Lambda(\theta)\}$ is the Legendre transform of the log-MGF. It is the Legendre–Fenchel transform of $\Lambda$.

q: Let $X_i\sim\mathcal{N}(0,1)$. Compute the Cramér rate function at $x=2$, i.e. $I(2)$.
text: Let $X_i\sim\mathcal{N}(0,1)$. Compute the Cramér rate function at $x=2$, i.e. $I(2)$. $\Lambda(\theta)=\log\mathbb{E}[e^{\theta X}]=\theta^2/2$. Maximising $\theta x-\theta^2/2$ in $\theta$ gives $\theta^*=x$ and $I(x)=x^2/2$. At $x=2$: $I(2)=2$. For the standard normal, $\Lambda(\theta)=\theta^2/2$, so $I(x)=x^2/2$.

q: Cramér's theorem requires which condition on the distribution of $X_1$?
text: Cramér's theorem requires which condition on the distribution of $X_1$? The classical statement requires the log-MGF $\Lambda(\theta)$ to be finite in an open neighbourhood of the origin (the so-called Cramér condition). Heavy-tailed laws like Cauchy fail this and have no Cramér-style LDP. We need exponential moments to even define the Legendre transform.

q: A rate function $I$ produced as a Legendre transform of a convex log-MGF must be:
text: A rate function $I$ produced as a Legendre transform of a convex log-MGF must be: Since $\Lambda(\theta)$ is convex, its Legendre transform $I=\Lambda^*$ is the supremum of affine functions $\theta\mapsto\theta x-\Lambda(\theta)$ — hence convex and lower semicontinuous. It need not be bounded or differentiable. Legendre transforms of convex functions are convex; the sup of affine functions is lsc.

q: For $X_i\sim\text{Bernoulli}(1/2)$, the rate function is $I(x)=x\log(2x)+(1-x)\log(2(1-x))$ on $[0,1]$. Compute $I(0.75)$ in nats (round to 3 decimals).
text: For $X_i\sim\text{Bernoulli}(1/2)$, the rate function is $I(x)=x\log(2x)+(1-x)\log(2(1-x))$ on $[0,1]$. Compute $I(0.75)$ in nats (round to 3 decimals). $I(0.75)=0.75\log(1.5)+0.25\log(0.5)=0.75\cdot 0.4055-0.25\cdot 0.6931\approx 0.3041-0.1733\approx 0.1308$ nats. $I(x)$ is the KL divergence $H(\text{Ber}(x)\,\|\,\text{Ber}(1/2))$. Plug $x=0.75$.

q: At which point does a rate function $I$ for the empirical mean of i.i.d.\ $X_i$ with mean $\mu$ vanish?
text: At which point does a rate function $I$ for the empirical mean of i.i.d.\ $X_i$ with mean $\mu$ vanish? By the law of large numbers $\bar X_n\to\mu$ a.s., so $\mathbb{P}(\bar X_n\approx\mu)\to 1$ and the exponential rate vanishes. Equivalently, $\theta=0$ achieves the sup in $\theta\mu-\Lambda(\theta)$ since $\Lambda'(0)=\mu$. The LLN says $\bar X_n\to\mu$ — so deviations from $\mu$ are not large.

q: Sanov's theorem identifies the rate function for the empirical distribution $L_n$ of i.i.d.\ samples from $\mu$ as:
text: Sanov's theorem identifies the rate function for the empirical distribution $L_n$ of i.i.d.\ samples from $\mu$ as: Sanov: $\mathbb{P}(L_n\in A)\asymp e^{-n\inf_{\nu\in A}H(\nu\,\|\,\mu)}$. Relative entropy / KL divergence is the unique rate function consistent with i.i.d.\ exponential families. The exponential family / Gibbs heuristic.

q: Let $\mu=\text{Ber}(1/2)$ and $\nu=\text{Ber}(0.9)$. Compute the KL divergence $H(\nu\,\|\,\mu)$ in nats (round to 3 decimals).
text:
Let $\mu=	ext{Ber}(1/2)$ and $
u=	ext{Ber}(0.9)$. Compute the KL divergence $H(
u\,\|\,\mu)$ in nats (round to 3 decimals). $H=0.9\log(1.8)+0.1\log(0.2)=0.9\cdot 0.5878+0.1\cdot(-1.6094)\approx 0.5290-0.1609\approx 0.368$ nats. $H(
u\,\|\,\mu)=0.9\log(0.9/0.5)+0.1\log(0.1/0.5)$.

q: Cramér's theorem can be recovered from Sanov's by which mechanism?
text:
Cramér's theorem can be recovered from Sanov's by which mechanism? The contraction principle: if $L_n$ satisfies an LDP with rate $J$ and $f$ is continuous, then $f(L_n)$ satisfies an LDP with rate $I(y)=\inf\{J(
u):f(
u)=y\}$. Applying $f(
u)=\int x\,d
u$ to Sanov recovers Cramér. The empirical mean is a continuous functional of the empirical measure.

q: The Gärtner–Ellis theorem extends Cramér by replacing the i.i.d.\ log-MGF with:
text: The Gärtner–Ellis theorem extends Cramér by replacing the i.i.d.\ log-MGF with: Gärtner–Ellis assumes the limiting log-MGF $\Lambda(\theta)=\lim_n\tfrac{1}{n}\log\mathbb{E}[e^{n\theta\bar X_n}]$ exists, is finite, and is essentially smooth — then the LDP holds with rate $I=\Lambda^*$. Cramér is the i.i.d.\ special case. It is a scaled limit of log-MGFs.

q: An additional regularity hypothesis Gärtner–Ellis requires (beyond existence of $\Lambda$) is:
text: An additional regularity hypothesis Gärtner–Ellis requires (beyond existence of $\Lambda$) is: Essential smoothness: $\Lambda$ is differentiable in the interior of its effective domain and $|\Lambda'(\theta)|\to\infty$ as $\theta$ approaches the boundary. This guarantees every point in the relevant range is exposed and the lower LDP bound holds. Without steepness the lower bound can fail — exposed points become inaccessible.

q: For a stationary AR(1) chain $X_n=\rho X_{n-1}+\xi_n$ with $\xi_n\sim\mathcal{N}(0,1)$ i.i.d.\ and $\rho\in(-1,1)$, the limiting $\Lambda(\theta)$ for $\bar X_n$ equals $\theta^2/(2(1-\rho)^2)$. With $\rho=0.5$, compute $I(1)$.
text: For a stationary AR(1) chain $X_n=\rho X_{n-1}+\xi_n$ with $\xi_n\sim\mathcal{N}(0,1)$ i.i.d.\ and $\rho\in(-1,1)$, the limiting $\Lambda(\theta)$ for $\bar X_n$ equals $\theta^2/(2(1-\rho)^2)$. With $\rho=0.5$, compute $I(1)$. $\Lambda(\theta)=\theta^2/(2(1-\rho)^2)$ is quadratic, so $I(x)=\tfrac{1}{2}(1-\rho)^2 x^2$. With $\rho=0.5$, $I(1)=\tfrac{1}{2}\cdot 0.25=0.125$. $I=\Lambda^*$, so $I(x)=x^2/2 \cdot (1-\rho)^2$.

q: Schilder's theorem gives an LDP for $\sqrt{\varepsilon}\,W$ (scaled Brownian motion) on path space with rate function:
text: Schilder's theorem gives an LDP for $\sqrt{\varepsilon}\,W$ (scaled Brownian motion) on path space with rate function: Schilder: $\mathbb{P}(\sqrt\varepsilon W\in\cdot)$ satisfies an LDP at speed $1/\varepsilon$ with rate $I(\phi)=\tfrac{1}{2}\int_0^T|\dot\phi|^2 dt$ on the Cameron–Martin space (and $+\infty$ otherwise). Most-likely paths minimise kinetic energy. It is the Cameron–Martin / Dirichlet energy of the path.

q: Freidlin–Wentzell theory studies the small-noise SDE $dX^\varepsilon_t=b(X^\varepsilon_t)\,dt+\sqrt\varepsilon\,dW_t$. The most-likely exit path from a basin minimises:
text: Freidlin–Wentzell theory studies the small-noise SDE $dX^\varepsilon_t=b(X^\varepsilon_t)\,dt+\sqrt\varepsilon\,dW_t$. The most-likely exit path from a basin minimises: Freidlin–Wentzell: the rate function is $S(\phi)=\tfrac{1}{2}\int|\dot\phi-b(\phi)|^2\,dt$. Mean exit times grow like $\exp(V/\varepsilon)$ where $V$ is the quasi-potential — the action minimum over paths from the attractor to the boundary. Replace $\dot\phi$ in Schilder's rate by the deviation from the deterministic flow.

q: Boltzmann's entropy formula $S=k\log W$ is recovered from large deviations as:
text: Boltzmann's entropy formula $S=k\log W$ is recovered from large deviations as: Sanov's rate $H(\nu\,\|\,\mu)$ is — up to sign and the Boltzmann constant — Boltzmann–Gibbs entropy. The most likely macrostate maximises entropy subject to energy constraints, recovering the canonical ensemble. This is the rigorous probabilistic foundation of statistical mechanics. The Gibbs measure is the minimiser of free energy = energy minus entropy.

title: Metric spaces
blurb: Distance axioms, open balls, ε-continuity, Cauchy sequences and completeness — the ε–δ archetype before topology gets abstracted.

title: Topological spaces
blurb: A topology as a family of open sets closed under arbitrary unions and finite intersections; bases, subspace and product topologies, with closure and interior as duals.

title: Continuity & homeomorphism
blurb: A map is continuous iff preimages of open sets are open — the coordinate-free definition. Homeomorphisms as the isomorphisms of Top.

title: Compactness
blurb: Every open cover has a finite subcover; preserved under continuous images; Heine–Borel, sequential compactness, and why compact Hausdorff is the right target category.

title: Connectedness
blurb: No separation into disjoint nonempty opens; path-connected implies connected; components partition the space.

title: Separation & countability
blurb: T1/Hausdorff/regular/normal, first- and second-countable; Urysohn's lemma and metrization as the payoff.

q: Which of the following is NOT required for $d\colon X\times X\to[0,\infty)$ to be a metric?
text: Which of the following is NOT required for $d\colon X\times X\to[0,\infty)$ to be a metric? Metrics need not be bounded. The three required axioms are identity of indiscernibles, symmetry, and triangle inequality. Boundedness is a separate property (e.g. discrete metrics are bounded by $1$, Euclidean metrics are not).

q: In $(\mathbb{R}^2, d_\infty)$ with $d_\infty(x,y)=\max(|x_1-y_1|,|x_2-y_2|)$, what is the area of the open ball $B(0,1)$ (as a subset of the plane)?
text: In $(\mathbb{R}^2, d_\infty)$ with $d_\infty(x,y)=\max(|x_1-y_1|,|x_2-y_2|)$, what is the area of the open ball $B(0,1)$ (as a subset of the plane)? The sup-ball of radius $1$ around the origin is the open square $(-1,1)\times(-1,1)$, with area $2\cdot 2 = 4$. By contrast the Euclidean disk has area $\pi\approx 3.14$ and the taxicab diamond has area $2$ — same topology, different shapes.

q: Which space is NOT complete with respect to the indicated metric?
text: Which space is NOT complete with respect to the indicated metric? $(0,1)$ is not complete: the sequence $x_n=1/n$ is Cauchy but its limit $0$ lies outside the space. Completeness is a metric property, not a topological one — $(0,1)$ is homeomorphic to $\mathbb{R}$, which is complete.

q: Define $d(x,y)=|x-y|/(1+|x-y|)$ on $\mathbb{R}$. Which statement is correct?
text: Define $d(x,y)=|x-y|/(1+|x-y|)$ on $\mathbb{R}$. Which statement is correct? The function $t\mapsto t/(1+t)$ is subadditive and increasing on $[0,\infty)$, so $d$ satisfies the triangle inequality and is a valid metric. It generates the standard topology because the identity maps are uniformly continuous in both directions locally. However $d(x,y)<1$ for all $x,y$, so $\mathbb{R}$ becomes bounded — but NOT complete under $d$: a sequence diverging to $\infty$ is $d$-Cauchy yet has no limit.

q: In the taxicab metric on $\mathbb{R}^2$ ($d_1(x,y)=|x_1-y_1|+|x_2-y_2|$), what is the area of the open ball $B(0,1)$?
text: In the taxicab metric on $\mathbb{R}^2$ ($d_1(x,y)=|x_1-y_1|+|x_2-y_2|$), what is the area of the open ball $B(0,1)$? The taxicab unit ball is the open square rotated $45°$: vertices $\pm e_1$ and $\pm e_2$. It is a square with diagonal $2$, so side length $\sqrt{2}$ and area $2$.

q: Which is the correct order of steps to prove the Baire Category Theorem for complete metric spaces?
text: Which is the correct order of steps to prove the Baire Category Theorem for complete metric spaces? The standard proof builds a nested sequence of closed balls avoiding each $F_n$, with radii shrinking to $0$. Completeness supplies a point in $\bigcap \overline{B}_n$, which lies in $U_0$ but in none of the $F_n$, contradicting $X=\bigcup F_n$. Nest shrinking closed balls dodging each $F_n$; completeness hands you the contradiction.

q: Let $X=\{a,b,c\}$. Which of the following is a topology on $X$?
text: Let $X=\{a,b,c\}$. Which of the following is a topology on $X$? Option (b) contains $\emptyset$ and $X$, and the union / intersection of any listed opens remains in the family. Option (a) fails because $\{a\}\cup\{b\}=\{a,b\}$ is missing; (c) fails because $\{a,b\}\cap\{b,c\}=\{b\}$ is missing; (d) fails because $\emptyset$ is missing.

q: Why are topologies required to be closed under FINITE intersections rather than arbitrary intersections?
text: Why are topologies required to be closed under FINITE intersections rather than arbitrary intersections? Countable intersections of opens can collapse to non-open sets: $\bigcap_{n\ge 1}(-1/n,1/n)=\{0\}$ is not open in $\mathbb{R}$. Only finite intersections are safe.

q: How many distinct topologies are there on a 2-element set $X=\{a,b\}$?
text: How many distinct topologies are there on a 2-element set $X=\{a,b\}$? The four topologies are: (i) indiscrete $\{\emptyset,X\}$; (ii) discrete $\{\emptyset,\{a\},\{b\},X\}$; (iii) Sierpiński $\{\emptyset,\{a\},X\}$; (iv) the mirror $\{\emptyset,\{b\},X\}$.

q: How many distinct topologies are there on a 3-element set?
text: How many distinct topologies are there on a 3-element set? By exhaustive enumeration (up to labeling) there are 9 non-homeomorphic topologies, but counting labeled topologies on $\{a,b,c\}$ gives 29. This is a classical combinatorial result.

q: The indiscrete topology $\{\emptyset, X\}$ on a set $X$ with $|X|\ge 2$ is:
text: The indiscrete topology $\{\emptyset, X\}$ on a set $X$ with $|X|\ge 2$ is: In the indiscrete topology no singleton is closed (every nonempty open set is all of $X$), so distinct points cannot be topologically distinguished — the space fails $T_0$. It cannot be metrizable since distinct points in any metric space have disjoint open neighborhoods.

q: Which of the following correctly characterizes the closure of a set $A$ in a topological space?
text: Which of the following correctly characterizes the closure of a set $A$ in a topological space? The closure $\overline{A}$ is the intersection of all closed sets containing $A$ — hence the smallest such closed set. Equivalently it is $A\cup A'$ where $A'$ is the derived set of limit points. The boundary $\partial A=\overline{A}\setminus\mathrm{int}(A)$ need not be open.

q: The topological definition of continuity says $f\colon X\to Y$ is continuous iff:
text: The topological definition of continuity says $f\colon X\to Y$ is continuous iff: $f$ is continuous iff $f^{-1}(V)$ is open in $X$ whenever $V$ is open in $Y$. Images of opens generally need not be open — consider $x\mapsto x^2$ on $\mathbb{R}$, which sends the open interval $(-1,1)$ to the non-open $[0,1)$.

q: Which of these pairs of spaces is NOT a pair of homeomorphic spaces?
text: Which of these pairs of spaces is NOT a pair of homeomorphic spaces? $[0,1]$ is compact and $(0,1)$ is not — continuous images of compact sets are compact, so no continuous bijection between them can be a homeomorphism. Meanwhile $(0,1)\cong \mathbb{R}$ via $t\mapsto \tan(\pi(t-1/2))$, and both (c) and (d) are homeomorphic pairs.

q: Why are the open interval $(0,1)$ and the circle $S^1$ not homeomorphic?
text: Why are the open interval $(0,1)$ and the circle $S^1$ not homeomorphic? $(0,1)$ has the cut-point property: removing any interior point disconnects it. $S^1\setminus\{p\}$ is a connected arc. Since homeomorphisms preserve connectedness and bijections, no homeomorphism can match cut points to non-cut points.

q: A bijective continuous map $f:X\to Y$ is NOT necessarily a homeomorphism. Which example witnesses this?
text: A bijective continuous map $f:X\to Y$ is NOT necessarily a homeomorphism. Which example witnesses this? $f:[0,1)\to S^1$ given by $t\mapsto e^{2\pi it}$ is a continuous bijection. The inverse sends $1$ to $0$ but the preimage of $[0,\varepsilon)$ under $f^{-1}$ is not open in $S^1$, so $f^{-1}$ is discontinuous. This shows compactness of the domain is essential for a continuous bijection to be a homeomorphism.

q: Which is the correct order of steps to prove $f:X\to Y$ continuous iff $f^{-1}(C)$ is closed for every closed $C\subseteq Y$?
text: Which is the correct order of steps to prove $f:X\to Y$ continuous iff $f^{-1}(C)$ is closed for every closed $C\subseteq Y$? Complements interchange open and closed sets and commute with preimages: $f^{-1}(Y\setminus C)=X\setminus f^{-1}(C)$. So the open-set criterion and the closed-set criterion are exact logical duals of each other via complementation.

q: The map $f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$, $f(x)=x^2$, sends the open set $(-2,2)$ to the set $[0,4)$. What is the minimum number of points one must remove from $(-2,2)$ so that the image of the remaining open set is open?
text: The map $f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$, $f(x)=x^2$, sends the open set $(-2,2)$ to the set $[0,4)$. What is the minimum number of points one must remove from $(-2,2)$ so that the image of the remaining open set is open? Removing the single point $0$ from $(-2,2)$ gives $(-2,0)\cup(0,2)$, whose image under $x^2$ is $(0,4)$, which is open. The failure at $0$ was the only obstruction.

q: Match each characterization of compactness with the setting in which it is equivalent to the open-cover definition (every open cover has a finite subcover).
text: Match each characterization of compactness with the setting in which it is equivalent to the open-cover definition (every open cover has a finite subcover). Compactness has many faces. (a) Heine–Borel is specific to $\mathbb{R}^n$ — closed unit balls in infinite-dimensional Banach spaces are closed and bounded but not compact. (b) Sequential compactness coincides with compactness in metric spaces but not in general (e.g. $\beta\mathbb{N}$ is compact but not sequentially compact). (c) Limit-point compactness is strictly weaker without metrizability. (d) FIP is the universal reformulation: $X$ compact $\iff$ every collection of closed sets with the finite intersection property has nonempty intersection. Heine–Borel is the metric-Euclidean shortcut; FIP is the universal reformulation; sequential and limit-point compactness coincide with open-cover compactness in metric spaces.

q: Which of the following subsets of $\mathbb{R}$ is compact?
text: Which of the following subsets of $\mathbb{R}$ is compact? $\{0\}\cup\{1/n\}$ is closed (contains its limit point $0$) and bounded, hence compact. $(0,1)$ is not closed, $[0,\infty)$ is not bounded, $\mathbb{Q}\cap[0,1]$ is not closed in $\mathbb{R}$.

q: Let $K$ be compact, $f\colon K\to\mathbb{R}$ continuous. Which statement is FALSE?
text: Let $K$ be compact, $f\colon K\to\mathbb{R}$ continuous. Which statement is FALSE? The constant function is continuous on any $K$ and is far from injective. The other three are standard consequences of compactness: extreme value theorem, continuous image of compact is compact, Heine–Cantor.

q: The closed unit ball $\overline{B}$ in an infinite-dimensional Hilbert space $H$ is closed and bounded but not compact. Which sequence witnesses the failure of sequential compactness?
text: The closed unit ball $\overline{B}$ in an infinite-dimensional Hilbert space $H$ is closed and bounded but not compact. Which sequence witnesses the failure of sequential compactness? An orthonormal set satisfies $\|e_n\|=1$ (in the ball) and $\|e_n-e_m\|=\sqrt{2}$ for $n\ne m$ by the Pythagorean theorem. No subsequence can be Cauchy, so none converges. This is the standard counterexample showing Heine–Borel fails in infinite dimensions.

q: Which is the correct sequence of implications for a metric space $X$?
text: Which is the correct sequence of implications for a metric space $X$? For metric spaces the three notions — compactness (every open cover has a finite subcover), sequential compactness (every sequence has a convergent subsequence), and limit-point compactness (every infinite subset has a limit point) — are all equivalent. This equivalence fails for general topological spaces.

q: The Cantor set $C\subset[0,1]$ is compact. It is obtained by removing open middle thirds. After $n$ stages, $2^n$ closed intervals remain, each of length $3^{-n}$. What is the total length of $C$ (i.e., its Lebesgue measure)?
text: The Cantor set $C\subset[0,1]$ is compact. It is obtained by removing open middle thirds. After $n$ stages, $2^n$ closed intervals remain, each of length $3^{-n}$. What is the total length of $C$ (i.e., its Lebesgue measure)? After $n$ steps the total length remaining is $(2/3)^n\to 0$. The Cantor set has Lebesgue measure $0$ yet is uncountable and compact — a classic example of a nowhere-dense perfect set.

q: Which of the following spaces is NOT connected?
text: Which of the following spaces is NOT connected? $[0,1]\cup[2,3]$ is disconnected: take $U=(-1,1.5)$, $V=(1.5,4)$; both hit the subspace in disjoint nonempty opens. The other three are connected — intervals and convex sets are path-connected, and path-connected implies connected.

q: The topologist's sine curve $T = \{(x,\sin(1/x)):x\in(0,1]\}\cup(\{0\}\times[-1,1])$ is:
text: The topologist's sine curve $T = \{(x,\sin(1/x)):x\in(0,1]\}\cup(\{0\}\times[-1,1])$ is: $T$ is the closure of a connected set (the graph), so it is connected. But any continuous path from the limit segment to the graph would have to traverse infinitely many oscillations in finite time, which fails to exist. Hence connected but not path-connected.

q: If $X$ is connected and $f\colon X\to\mathbb{R}$ is continuous, then $f(X)$ must be:
text: If $X$ is connected and $f\colon X\to\mathbb{R}$ is continuous, then $f(X)$ must be: Continuous images of connected sets are connected; the connected subsets of $\mathbb{R}$ are exactly the intervals (possibly unbounded, possibly degenerate). This is the abstract form of the intermediate value theorem.

q: Path-connectedness implies connectedness, but the converse fails. Which is the correct chain of implications (strongest to weakest)?
text: Path-connectedness implies connectedness, but the converse fails. Which is the correct chain of implications (strongest to weakest)? Path-connected always implies connected. The converse needs local path-connectedness as a hypothesis: a connected, locally path-connected space is path-connected. Manifolds are locally Euclidean (hence locally path-connected), so for manifolds connected $\iff$ path-connected — but this is a consequence of the chain, not a coincidence.

q: $\mathbb{R}^n\setminus\{0\}$ is connected for $n\ge 2$ but disconnected for $n=1$. Which topological property explains the $n=1$ case?
text: $\mathbb{R}^n\setminus\{0\}$ is connected for $n\ge 2$ but disconnected for $n=1$. Which topological property explains the $n=1$ case? Removing any point $p$ from $\mathbb{R}$ yields two disjoint open rays, disconnecting the line — $p$ is a cut point. In $\mathbb{R}^n$ for $n\ge 2$, any two points in $\mathbb{R}^n\setminus\{0\}$ can be joined by a path avoiding the origin (route around it), so removal of a single point leaves the space path-connected, hence connected.

q: The rational numbers $\mathbb{Q}$ with the subspace topology from $\mathbb{R}$ are totally disconnected: the only connected subsets are singletons. How many connected components does the subspace $\{1/n : n\ge 1\}\subset\mathbb{R}$ have?
text: The rational numbers $\mathbb{Q}$ with the subspace topology from $\mathbb{R}$ are totally disconnected: the only connected subsets are singletons. How many connected components does the subspace $\{1/n : n\ge 1\}\subset\mathbb{R}$ have? Each point $\{1/n\}$ is isolated in the subspace (the sets $\{1/n\}=\{x:1/(n+1)<x<1/(n-1)\}\cap S$ are open in $S$), so every singleton is a connected component. The subspace has countably infinitely many connected components — but the question asks for the count of components that are NOT singletons, which is $0$.

q: Match each separation axiom with its defining condition.
text:
Match each separation axiom with its defining condition. $T_1$: singletons are closed (equivalently, distinct points $x
e y$ admit opens $U
i x$ with $y
otin U$ and vice versa). $T_2$: distinct points can be separated by disjoint open neighborhoods. $T_3$: regular + $T_1$ — closed set and external point separated. $T_4$: normal + $T_1$ — disjoint closed sets separated. Note compactness ('every open cover has a finite subcover') is unrelated to the $T_n$ ladder. $T_n$ separates progressively richer pairs: distinct points (singletons closed → points-by-opens → point-from-closed → closed-from-closed).

q: Which of these is $T_1$ but NOT Hausdorff?
text: Which of these is $T_1$ but NOT Hausdorff? The cofinite topology on $\mathbb{R}$: every singleton is closed (hence $T_1$), but any two nonempty opens have finite complements, so they must intersect — failing Hausdorff. Sierpiński is $T_0$ but not even $T_1$.

q: Select every set of hypotheses that suffices for a topological space $X$ to be metrizable.
text: Select every set of hypotheses that suffices for a topological space $X$ to be metrizable. Urysohn: regular + $T_2$ + second-countable $\Rightarrow$ metrizable. (a) and (e) are direct applications. Smirnov metrization: paracompact $T_2$ + locally metrizable $\Rightarrow$ metrizable. (b) fails: $\{0,1\}^{2^{\mathbb{N}}}$ is compact Hausdorff but not metrizable (not second-countable). (d) is far too weak: $T_0$ is the bottom of the separation ladder. Urysohn requires regular + $T_2$ + second-countable; compact $T_2$ alone fails (e.g. uncountable products $\{0,1\}^I$ are compact $T_2$ but not metrizable). Smirnov is the standard non-second-countable upgrade.

q: Urysohn's Lemma states: if $X$ is normal ($T_4$) and $A,B$ are disjoint closed sets, then there exists a continuous $f:X\to[0,1]$ with $f|_A=0$ and $f|_B=1$. Which step is the key inductive construction in its proof?
text: Urysohn's Lemma states: if $X$ is normal ($T_4$) and $A,B$ are disjoint closed sets, then there exists a continuous $f:X\to[0,1]$ with $f|_A=0$ and $f|_B=1$. Which step is the key inductive construction in its proof? The proof constructs a nested family of open sets $\{U_r\}$ indexed by dyadic rationals via repeated application of normality (which guarantees open sets separating disjoint closed sets). The function $f(x)=\inf\{r:x\in U_r\}$ is then continuous by construction.

q: The Sorgenfrey line ($\mathbb{R}$ with the lower-limit topology, basis $\{[a,b):a<b\}$) is $T_{3.5}$ (Tychonoff) but its square $\mathbb{R}_\ell^2$ is NOT normal. Which property does $\mathbb{R}_\ell^2$ fail that witnesses non-normality?
text: The Sorgenfrey line ($\mathbb{R}$ with the lower-limit topology, basis $\{[a,b):a<b\}$) is $T_{3.5}$ (Tychonoff) but its square $\mathbb{R}_\ell^2$ is NOT normal. Which property does $\mathbb{R}_\ell^2$ fail that witnesses non-normality? Jones's lemma: a normal separable space has no uncountable closed discrete subspace. The anti-diagonal $D=\{(x,-x)\}$ is closed and discrete (each $[x,x+1)\times[-x,-x+1)$ meets $D$ only at $(x,-x)$), and $\mathbb{R}_\ell^2$ is separable ($\mathbb{Q}^2$ is dense). This contradicts normality.

q: A second-countable space is separable, but a separable space need not be second-countable. Which example shows this?
text: A second-countable space is separable, but a separable space need not be second-countable. Which example shows this? $\mathbb{R}_\ell$ is separable because $\mathbb{Q}$ is dense. But a second-countable space satisfies the countable chain condition (no uncountable disjoint family of opens). The family $\{[x,x+1):x\in\mathbb{R}\setminus\mathbb{Q}\}$ is an uncountable pairwise-disjoint open family, so $\mathbb{R}_\ell$ is not second-countable.

title: 7. Euler characteristic and a taste of cohomology
Paths and homotopy
blurb: Continuous paths I→X, homotopy rel endpoints, and the concatenation that makes loop classes a group.

title: Simply connected domains
blurb: Path-connected space with trivial fundamental group — the domain hypothesis behind existence theorems in complex analysis.

title: Fundamental group
blurb: π₁(X,x₀): loop classes under concatenation. π₁(S¹)=ℤ, basepoint-change, functoriality.

title: Universal cover of S¹
blurb: The exponential map $p: \mathbb{R} \to S^1$, $t \mapsto e^{2\pi i t}$, is a covering with discrete fibre $\mathbb{Z}$. Lifting loops to paths in $\mathbb{R}$ converts the topology of $S^1$ into integer arithmetic — every loop has a winding number, and that's the iso $\pi_1(S^1) \cong \mathbb{Z}$.

title: Covering spaces
blurb: Locally trivial surjections p:E→B with discrete fibers; the Galois correspondence between covers of B and subgroups of π₁(B).

title: Singular homology
blurb: Chains, boundaries, Hₙ(X) as a homotopy invariant; Mayer–Vietoris and the Euler characteristic.

q: Two paths $\gamma_0,\gamma_1:[0,1]\to X$ with the same endpoints are homotopic rel endpoints if:
text: Two paths $\gamma_0,\gamma_1:[0,1]\to X$ with the same endpoints are homotopic rel endpoints if: Homotopy rel endpoints is exactly a continuous deformation $H$ through paths with endpoints fixed for all deformation times.

q: How many boundary points does the interval $[0,1]$ have?
text: How many boundary points does the interval $[0,1]$ have? As a manifold with boundary, $[0,1]$ has boundary $\{0,1\}$, so there are $2$ boundary points.

q: On the loop $\gamma(t)=e^{2\pi i t}$, compute $\gamma(1/4)$ as $a+bi$.
text: On the loop $\gamma(t)=e^{2\pi i t}$, compute $\gamma(1/4)$ as $a+bi$. $\gamma(1/4)=e^{i\pi/2}=\cos(\pi/2)+i\sin(\pi/2)=i$.

q: Path concatenation $\alpha*\beta$ is associative up to homotopy rel endpoints. Which statement is a correct counterexample showing it fails on the nose (not just up to homotopy)?
text: Path concatenation $\alpha*\beta$ is associative up to homotopy rel endpoints. Which statement is a correct counterexample showing it fails on the nose (not just up to homotopy)? $(\alpha*\beta)*\gamma$ spends the first quarter of $[0,1]$ on $\alpha$, the second on $\beta$, and the last half on $\gamma$, whereas $\alpha*(\beta*\gamma)$ spends the first half on $\alpha$. The functions are genuinely different even though they are homotopic rel endpoints via a linear reparametrisation homotopy.

q: Let $\gamma:[0,1]\to\mathbb{C}^*$ be a loop with $\gamma(t)=e^{4\pi i t}$. The homotopy class of $\gamma$ in $\pi_1(\mathbb{C}^*,1)\cong\mathbb{Z}$ equals what integer?
text: Let $\gamma:[0,1]\to\mathbb{C}^*$ be a loop with $\gamma(t)=e^{4\pi i t}$. The homotopy class of $\gamma$ in $\pi_1(\mathbb{C}^*,1)\cong\mathbb{Z}$ equals what integer? $\gamma$ winds around the origin $4\pi/2\pi=2$ full times, so its winding number is $2$.

q: Which of the following is the correct order of steps in proving that the path-homotopy relation $\simeq$ is an equivalence relation on paths from $x_0$ to $x_1$?
text: Which of the following is the correct order of steps in proving that the path-homotopy relation $\simeq$ is an equivalence relation on paths from $x_0$ to $x_1$? Standard proof order: first the constant homotopy $H(t,s)=\gamma(t)$ gives reflexivity; reversing the deformation parameter gives symmetry; stacking $H_1(t,s)$ on $[0,1/2]$ and $H_2(t,2s-1)$ on $[1/2,1]$ in $s$ gives transitivity.

q: Select every space that is simply connected.
text: Select every space that is simply connected. $\mathbb R^n$ is contractible, hence simply connected. $S^2$ has $\pi_1=0$ (every loop contracts; $S^n$ is simply connected for $n\ge 2$). $S^1$ has $\pi_1\cong\mathbb Z$ (winding number). $\mathbb R^2\setminus\{0\}$ deformation-retracts onto $S^1$, so $\pi_1\cong\mathbb Z$. $T^2$ has $\pi_1\cong\mathbb Z^2$. Simply connected means path-connected with $\pi_1=0$. Convex/contractible Euclidean spaces are simply connected; spheres $S^n$ are simply connected for $n\ge 2$; circles, punctured planes, and tori carry non-contractible loops.

q: What is the rank of $\pi_1(\mathbb{R}^2)$ as an abelian group?
text: What is the rank of $\pi_1(\mathbb{R}^2)$ as an abelian group? $\pi_1(\mathbb{R}^2)=0$, the trivial group, so its rank is $0$.

q: Compute $e^{2\pi i}$.
text: Compute $e^{2\pi i}$. A full turn on the unit circle lands back at $1$: $e^{2\pi i}=1+0i$.

q: $S^2$ is simply connected. Which argument correctly shows every loop $\gamma:S^1\to S^2$ is null-homotopic?
text: $S^2$ is simply connected. Which argument correctly shows every loop $\gamma:S^1\to S^2$ is null-homotopic? A space-filling loop would require $\gamma$ to be surjective; by a small transversality/smooth perturbation argument every smooth loop misses a point. Removing that point gives $\mathbb{R}^2$, and any loop in $\mathbb{R}^2$ contracts to a point via the straight-line homotopy, so $\gamma$ was null-homotopic in $S^2$. Reduce to a plane by using that loops miss at least one point.

q: Which of these spaces is NOT simply connected?
text: Which of these spaces is NOT simply connected? $\mathbb{R}^3$ minus a line is homotopy equivalent to $S^1$ (retract radially onto a circle linking the line), so $\pi_1\cong\mathbb{Z}\neq 0$. The other three spaces are all simply connected.

q: The van Kampen theorem applied to $S^2 = U \cup V$ (each a contractible open hemisphere with $U\cap V\simeq S^1$) gives $\pi_1(S^2)\cong$ the trivial group. How many generators does $\pi_1(U)*_{\pi_1(U\cap V)}\pi_1(V)$ have after the amalgamation kills all generators?
text: The van Kampen theorem applied to $S^2 = U \cup V$ (each a contractible open hemisphere with $U\cap V\simeq S^1$) gives $\pi_1(S^2)\cong$ the trivial group. How many generators does $\pi_1(U)*_{\pi_1(U\cap V)}\pi_1(V)$ have after the amalgamation kills all generators? $\pi_1(U)=\pi_1(V)=0$, so the amalgamated free product $0*_{\mathbb{Z}}0$ forces the single generator of $\pi_1(U\cap V)\cong\mathbb{Z}$ to map to $0$ in both factors, yielding the trivial group with $0$ generators.

q: Which is true about $\pi_1(S^1)$?
text: Which is true about $\pi_1(S^1)$? Loops on $S^1$ are classified by winding number, giving an isomorphism with $\mathbb{Z}$.

q: If a loop winds $-3$ times around $S^1$, what integer does it represent in $\pi_1(S^1)\cong\mathbb{Z}$?
text: If a loop winds $-3$ times around $S^1$, what integer does it represent in $\pi_1(S^1)\cong\mathbb{Z}$? The winding number itself is the class in $\mathbb{Z}$.

q: Compute $(e^{i\pi/3})^3$ as $a+bi$.
By van Kampen's theorem, $\pi_1(T^2)\cong\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{Z}$. Which decomposition is used?
text: By van Kampen's theorem, $\pi_1(T^2)\cong\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{Z}$. Which decomposition is used? $\pi_1$ preserves products: $\pi_1(S^1\times S^1)\cong\pi_1(S^1)\times\pi_1(S^1)\cong\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{Z}$. The two generators correspond to the meridian and longitude loops.

q: The Klein bottle $K$ has $\pi_1(K)=\langle a,b\mid abab^{-1}=1\rangle$. This group is non-abelian. Which element witnesses non-commutativity?
text: The Klein bottle $K$ has $\pi_1(K)=\langle a,b\mid abab^{-1}=1\rangle$. This group is non-abelian. Which element witnesses non-commutativity? Rewrite the relation as $ab=ba^{-1}$. This means $a$ and $b$ do NOT commute (unless $a^2=1$), so the group is non-abelian; it is an HNN extension of $\mathbb{Z}$ by $\mathbb{Z}$.

q: Let $X=\mathbb{R}^2\setminus\{p_1,p_2\}$ (the plane with two punctures). The rank of $\pi_1(X)$ as a free group equals how many?
text: Let $X=\mathbb{R}^2\setminus\{p_1,p_2\}$ (the plane with two punctures). The rank of $\pi_1(X)$ as a free group equals how many? $\mathbb{R}^2$ minus $n$ points deformation-retracts onto a wedge of $n$ circles, so $\pi_1\cong F_n$. For $n=2$ the free group $F_2$ has rank $2$.

q: Under the exponential covering $p\colon\mathbb{R}\to S^1$, $p(t)=e^{2\pi i t}$, what is the fibre $p^{-1}(*)$ over the basepoint $*=1$?
text: Under the exponential covering $p\colon\mathbb{R}\to S^1$, $p(t)=e^{2\pi i t}$, what is the fibre $p^{-1}(*)$ over the basepoint $*=1$? $e^{2\pi i t}=1$ exactly when $t\in\mathbb{Z}$, so the fibre over the basepoint is the integer lattice $\mathbb{Z}\subset\mathbb{R}$ — the discrete set whose elements label the sheets of the cover.

q: Lift the loop $\gamma(t)=e^{4\pi i t}$ to a path in $\mathbb{R}$ starting at $0$. What integer winding number $\tilde\gamma(1)$ do you get?
text: Lift the loop $\gamma(t)=e^{4\pi i t}$ to a path in $\mathbb{R}$ starting at $0$. What integer winding number $\tilde\gamma(1)$ do you get? The unique lift through $0$ is $\tilde\gamma(t)=2t$, since $p(2t)=e^{2\pi i (2t)}=e^{4\pi i t}=\gamma(t)$. Its endpoint is $\tilde\gamma(1)=2$, so the loop winds twice.

q: Which property of $\mathbb{R}$ makes $p\colon\mathbb{R}\to S^1$ the *universal* cover of $S^1$ (rather than just *some* cover)?
text: Which property of $\mathbb{R}$ makes $p\colon\mathbb{R}\to S^1$ the *universal* cover of $S^1$ (rather than just *some* cover)? A connected cover is universal precisely when its total space is simply connected ($\pi_1=0$). $\mathbb{R}$ is contractible, hence simply connected, so $\mathbb{R}\to S^1$ dominates every other connected cover via a unique factoring map. "Universal" means initial among connected covers — what does that require of the total space's $\pi_1$?

q: By the Galois correspondence for covering spaces, connected covers of a (suitable) base $X$ biject with conjugacy classes of subgroups of $\pi_1(X)$. For $X=S^1$ with $\pi_1(X)\cong\mathbb Z$, match each cover with the subgroup $H\le\mathbb Z$ to which it corresponds.
text: By the Galois correspondence for covering spaces, connected covers of a (suitable) base $X$ biject with conjugacy classes of subgroups of $\pi_1(X)$. For $X=S^1$ with $\pi_1(X)\cong\mathbb Z$, match each cover with the subgroup $H\le\mathbb Z$ to which it corresponds. Galois theory of covering spaces: covers of $X$ correspond to subgroups of $\pi_1(X)$, with sheet count equal to index. For $X=S^1$, $\pi_1=\mathbb Z$. The universal cover $\mathbb R\to S^1$ has fiber $\mathbb Z$ over each point — corresponds to trivial subgroup $\{0\}$. The $n$-sheeted cover $z\mapsto z^n$ corresponds to $n\mathbb Z$ (index $n$). The identity is the cover for $\mathbb Z$ itself (index 1). Cover sheets equal $[\pi_1(X):H]$. Universal cover ↔ trivial subgroup, $n$-sheeted ↔ index-$n$ subgroup, trivial cover ↔ full group.

q: For $p_n:S^1\to S^1$, $p_n(z)=z^n$, how many points lie over a generic point of the target circle when $n=5$?
text: For $p_n:S^1\to S^1$, $p_n(z)=z^n$, how many points lie over a generic point of the target circle when $n=5$? The map $z\mapsto z^n$ is an $n$-sheeted cover, so generic fibers have $n$ points.

q: Under $p_2(z)=z^2$, one preimage of $-1$ is $i$. Give the other preimage as $a+bi$.
text: Under $p_2(z)=z^2$, one preimage of $-1$ is $i$. Give the other preimage as $a+bi$. Solutions to $z^2=-1$ are $\pm i$. If one is $i$, the other is $-i$.

q: The covering space correspondence says connected covers of $X$ (up to isomorphism) correspond to conjugacy classes of subgroups of $\pi_1(X,x_0)$. For $X=S^1$ with $\pi_1\cong\mathbb{Z}$, which cover corresponds to the subgroup $3\mathbb{Z}\le\mathbb{Z}$?
text: The covering space correspondence says connected covers of $X$ (up to isomorphism) correspond to conjugacy classes of subgroups of $\pi_1(X,x_0)$. For $X=S^1$ with $\pi_1\cong\mathbb{Z}$, which cover corresponds to the subgroup $3\mathbb{Z}\le\mathbb{Z}$? The subgroup $n\mathbb{Z}\le\mathbb{Z}$ has index $n$ and corresponds to the $n$-sheeted cyclic cover $z\mapsto z^n$. For $n=3$ this is $p_3$, whose deck transformation group is $\mathbb{Z}/3\mathbb{Z}$.

q: The universal cover of $T^2=S^1\times S^1$ is $\mathbb{R}^2$. The deck transformation group acts by integer translations. How many deck transformations map the origin $(0,0)$ to the point $(2,3)$?
text: The universal cover of $T^2=S^1\times S^1$ is $\mathbb{R}^2$. The deck transformation group acts by integer translations. How many deck transformations map the origin $(0,0)$ to the point $(2,3)$? Deck transformations of a regular cover act freely and transitively on each fiber. There is exactly $1$ deck transformation sending any chosen fiber point to any other given fiber point.

q: Path lifting: given a covering $p:\tilde{X}\to X$, a path $\gamma:[0,1]\to X$, and a lift $\tilde{\gamma}(0)=\tilde{x}_0\in p^{-1}(\gamma(0))$, how many lifts $\tilde{\gamma}$ exist?
text: Path lifting: given a covering $p:\tilde{X}\to X$, a path $\gamma:[0,1]\to X$, and a lift $\tilde{\gamma}(0)=\tilde{x}_0\in p^{-1}(\gamma(0))$, how many lifts $\tilde{\gamma}$ exist? The unique path-lifting property of covering spaces guarantees exactly one lift $\tilde{\gamma}:[0,1]\to\tilde{X}$ with $\tilde{\gamma}(0)=\tilde{x}_0$ and $p\circ\tilde{\gamma}=\gamma$.

q: Arrange the steps to set up the Mayer–Vietoris long exact sequence for a space $X = A\cup B$ with $A,B$ open.
text: Arrange the steps to set up the Mayer–Vietoris long exact sequence for a space $X = A\cup B$ with $A,B$ open. Mayer–Vietoris flow: (a) build the SES of chain complexes from the inclusion data, (b) check exactness, (c) replace the third term $C_*(A)+C_*(B)$ by $C_*(X)$ in homology — content of the small-simplices theorem (every chain in $X$ is homologous to one supported in $A$ or $B$), (d) the snake lemma converts the SES of chain complexes into the LES in homology with connecting morphism $\partial_*\colon H_n(X)\to H_{n-1}(A\cap B)$. Build the SES of chain complexes, verify exactness, identify the third term with $C_*(X)$ via subdivision, then take homology.

q: Using reduced homology ranks for a torus $T^2$ ($\beta_0,\beta_1,\beta_2)=(1,2,1)$, compute $\chi(T^2)=\beta_0-\beta_1+\beta_2$.
text: Using reduced homology ranks for a torus $T^2$ ($\beta_0,\beta_1,\beta_2)=(1,2,1)$, compute $\chi(T^2)=\beta_0-\beta_1+\beta_2$. $\chi(T^2)=1-2+1=0$.

q: Compute $\dfrac{1+i}{1-i}$ as $a+bi$.
text: Compute $\dfrac{1+i}{1-i}$ as $a+bi$. Multiply numerator/denominator by $1+i$: $\frac{(1+i)^2}{1+1}=\frac{1+2i-1}{2}=i$.

q: A continuous map $f:X\to Y$ induces $f_*:H_n(X)\to H_n(Y)$. If $f$ is homotopic to a constant map, which is true for all $n\ge 1$?
text: A continuous map $f:X\to Y$ induces $f_*:H_n(X)\to H_n(Y)$. If $f$ is homotopic to a constant map, which is true for all $n\ge 1$? Homotopy invariance: homotopic maps induce the same map on $H_n$. A constant map factors through a point, whose reduced homology vanishes in degree $\ge 1$, so $f_*=0$ there.

q: Use Mayer–Vietoris on $S^2=U\cup V$ with $U,V$ contractible open sets and $U\cap V\simeq S^1$ to compute $\chi(S^2)$ from $\chi(U)+\chi(V)-\chi(U\cap V)$. Recall $\chi(\mathrm{pt})=1$ and $\chi(S^1)=0$.
text: Use Mayer–Vietoris on $S^2=U\cup V$ with $U,V$ contractible open sets and $U\cap V\simeq S^1$ to compute $\chi(S^2)$ from $\chi(U)+\chi(V)-\chi(U\cap V)$. Recall $\chi(\mathrm{pt})=1$ and $\chi(S^1)=0$. Inclusion–exclusion for Euler characteristic: $\chi(S^2)=\chi(U)+\chi(V)-\chi(U\cap V)=1+1-0=2$, matching $\beta_0-\beta_1+\beta_2=1-0+1$.

title: 11. Connections to the rest of the notebook
Smooth manifolds and atlases
blurb: Smooth manifolds glue Euclidean charts by smooth transition maps.

title: Examples: spheres, projective spaces, matrix groups
blurb: S^n, RP^n, tori, Grassmannians, and classical matrix groups are the canonical manifolds to have in hand before doing anything intrinsic.

title: Smooth maps and diffeomorphisms
blurb: A map is smooth when every chart representative is C^∞; diffeomorphisms are the isomorphisms of the smooth category.

title: Tangent spaces
blurb: T_pM has three equivalent definitions — equivalence classes of curves, chart velocities, and derivations of C^∞(M) — all naturally isomorphic.

title: Tangent bundle and vector fields
blurb: TM assembles all tangent spaces into a rank-n vector bundle; smooth sections are vector fields and act as derivations on C^∞(M).

title: Lie bracket of vector fields
blurb: [X,Y] = XY−YX is a vector field; it measures the failure of flows to commute and gives 𝔛(M) a Lie algebra structure.

title: Immersions, submersions, and submanifolds
blurb: Rank conditions on df control local models: immersions inject tangentially, submersions project, embeddings land as submanifolds; Whitney says every n-manifold embeds in R^{2n}.

title: Integral curves and flows
blurb: A vector field X integrates to a flow Phi^X_t: the fundamental theorem gives a unique maximal integral curve through each point, complete when M is compact.

title: Partitions of unity
blurb: Subordinate partitions of unity exist on any smooth manifold and are the standard device for gluing local constructions into global ones.

title: Orientation of manifolds
blurb: An orientation is a coherent choice of sign on the top exterior power of every tangent space; equivalently, an atlas whose transitions have positive Jacobian.

q: A smooth atlas on $M$ is a collection of charts $\{(U_\alpha,\varphi_\alpha)\}$ covering $M$ such that:
text: A smooth atlas on $M$ is a collection of charts $\{(U_\alpha,\varphi_\alpha)\}$ covering $M$ such that: Smoothness of transition maps between Euclidean patches is exactly what lifts calculus from $\mathbb{R}^n$ to $M$. No disjointness, compactness, or linearity is required.

q: On $S^2$ with the two-chart stereographic atlas from $N$ and $S$, the overlap $U_N\cap U_S$ is $S^2$ minus how many points?
text: On $S^2$ with the two-chart stereographic atlas from $N$ and $S$, the overlap $U_N\cap U_S$ is $S^2$ minus how many points? The north chart misses $N$ and the south chart misses $S$, so the overlap is $S^2\setminus\{N,S\}$ — two missing points.

q: The transition map between the two stereographic charts on $S^2$ is:
text: The transition map between the two stereographic charts on $S^2$ is: Composing the two stereographic projections gives $(u,v)\mapsto(u,v)/(u^2+v^2)$, a $C^\infty$ diffeomorphism of $\mathbb{R}^2\setminus\{0\}$.

q: The circle $S^1$ admits a smooth atlas with two charts. Which statement is a correct counterexample showing that a single chart cannot cover $S^1$?
text: The circle $S^1$ admits a smooth atlas with two charts. Which statement is a correct counterexample showing that a single chart cannot cover $S^1$? A single chart would require a homeomorphism from all of $S^1$ onto an open subset of $\mathbb{R}$. But $S^1$ is compact and a continuous bijection from a compact space to a Hausdorff space is a homeomorphism onto its image; no open subset of $\mathbb{R}$ is compact, so no such homeomorphism exists.

q: The torus $T^2 = S^1\times S^1$ carries a smooth atlas derived from the product of two two-chart atlases on $S^1$. What is the minimum number of charts in such a product atlas?
text: The torus $T^2 = S^1\times S^1$ carries a smooth atlas derived from the product of two two-chart atlases on $S^1$. What is the minimum number of charts in such a product atlas? Each factor $S^1$ needs at least $2$ charts, so the product atlas has $2\times 2 = 4$ charts of the form $U_i\times V_j$.

q: Which of the following is the correct order of steps in verifying that two charts $(U,\varphi)$ and $(V,\psi)$ are smoothly compatible?
text: Which of the following is the correct order of steps in verifying that two charts $(U,\varphi)$ and $(V,\psi)$ are smoothly compatible? Two charts are smoothly compatible when their overlap is open (possibly empty) and both transition maps $\psi\circ\varphi^{-1}$ and $\varphi\circ\psi^{-1}$ are $C^\infty$. If the overlap is empty they are trivially compatible, skipping the smoothness check.

q: What is the dimension of the matrix Lie group $\mathrm{SO}(n)$?
text: What is the dimension of the matrix Lie group $\mathrm{SO}(n)$? $\mathrm{SO}(n)$ has dimension $n(n-1)/2$; for $n=3$ this is $3(3-1)/2=3$ — reflecting the three Euler angles.

q: Real projective space $\mathbb{RP}^n$ can be described as:
text: Real projective space $\mathbb{RP}^n$ can be described as: $\mathbb{RP}^n = S^n/\{\pm 1\}$ identifies antipodal points; the resulting space is a compact smooth $n$-manifold, with standard affine charts $\{x_i\ne 0\}$.

q: The Grassmannian $\mathrm{Gr}(k,n)$ of $k$-planes in $\mathbb{R}^n$ has dimension $k(n-k)$. What is $\dim\mathrm{Gr}(2,5)$?
text: The Grassmannian $\mathrm{Gr}(k,n)$ of $k$-planes in $\mathbb{R}^n$ has dimension $k(n-k)$. What is $\dim\mathrm{Gr}(2,5)$? $\dim\mathrm{Gr}(2,5)=2\cdot(5-2)=6$. The charts are graphs of linear maps $W_0\to W_0^\perp$, giving $k(n-k)$ parameters.

q: The unitary group $\mathrm{U}(n)$ is cut out of $\mathrm{GL}_n(\mathbb{C})$ by $A^*A=I$. What is its real dimension for $n=2$?
text: The unitary group $\mathrm{U}(n)$ is cut out of $\mathrm{GL}_n(\mathbb{C})$ by $A^*A=I$. What is its real dimension for $n=2$? $\mathrm{U}(n)$ has real dimension $n^2$. For $n=2$ this gives $4$. (Concretely: $\mathrm{U}(2)\cong \mathrm{U}(1)\times\mathrm{SU}(2)/\mathbb{Z}_2$, with $\dim\mathrm{U}(1)=1$ and $\dim\mathrm{SU}(2)=3$.)

q: Why is the cone $C=\{(x,y,z):z^2=x^2+y^2\}\subset\mathbb{R}^3$ NOT a smooth manifold near the apex $(0,0,0)$?
text: Why is the cone $C=\{(x,y,z):z^2=x^2+y^2\}\subset\mathbb{R}^3$ NOT a smooth manifold near the apex $(0,0,0)$? Take any small neighbourhood $U$ of the apex in $C$. Then $U\setminus\{0\}$ has two connected components (the two half-cones). But for any point in a smooth $2$-manifold, removing it leaves a connected punctured disk.
