So no such neighbourhood is homeomorphic to $\mathbb{R}^2$, and $C$ fails even to be a topological manifold at the apex — smoothness is not the issue, the topology is.

q: Which of the following is a smooth manifold structure on the complex projective line $\mathbb{CP}^1$?
text: Which of the following is a smooth manifold structure on the complex projective line $\mathbb{CP}^1$? $\mathbb{CP}^1$ has two affine charts $U_0=\{[z_0:z_1]:z_0\ne 0\}$ with coordinate $z=z_1/z_0$ and $U_1=\{z_1\ne 0\}$ with coordinate $w=z_0/z_1$; on the overlap $w=1/z$, smooth (in fact holomorphic). Topologically the result is $S^2$ — the Riemann sphere — and one can check the smooth structures match the standard stereographic atlas.

q: A map $f\colon M\to N$ between smooth manifolds is smooth iff:
text: A map $f\colon M\to N$ between smooth manifolds is smooth iff: Smoothness of $f$ is smoothness of every coordinate representative $\hat f = \psi\circ f\circ\varphi^{-1}$, which is a map between open subsets of Euclidean space. Chart compatibility makes this property independent of chart choice.

q: A diffeomorphism $f\colon M\to N$ is:
text: A diffeomorphism $f\colon M\to N$ is: Diffeomorphisms are the isomorphisms of the smooth category: smooth, bijective, with smooth inverse. A smooth bijection need not be a diffeomorphism — consider $x\mapsto x^3$ on $\mathbb{R}$, whose inverse is not smooth at $0$.

q: Let $f\colon\mathbb{R}^2\to\mathbb{R}^2$, $f(x,y)=(x+y,\,xy)$. At $p=(1,2)$ the Jacobian is $\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\ y&x\end{pmatrix}_{(1,2)} = \begin{pmatrix}1&1\\2&1\end{pmatrix}$. What is the rank of $df_p$?
text: Let $f\colon\mathbb{R}^2\to\mathbb{R}^2$, $f(x,y)=(x+y,\,xy)$. At $p=(1,2)$ the Jacobian is $\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\ y&x\end{pmatrix}_{(1,2)} = \begin{pmatrix}1&1\\2&1\end{pmatrix}$. What is the rank of $df_p$? $\det\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\2&1\end{pmatrix} = 1-2 = -1\ne 0$, so the Jacobian is invertible and $\operatorname{rank}(df_p)=2$.

q: The map $f\colon\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$, $f(x)=x^3$, is a smooth bijection. Why is it NOT a diffeomorphism?
text: The map $f\colon\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$, $f(x)=x^3$, is a smooth bijection. Why is it NOT a diffeomorphism? $f$ is a smooth bijection, but $df_0 = 3x^2|_{x=0} = 0$ is non-invertible, and $f^{-1}(y)=y^{1/3}$ has $(f^{-1})'(y)=\tfrac{1}{3}y^{-2/3}$, which diverges as $y\to 0$. So $f^{-1}$ fails to be smooth (in fact even $C^1$) at $0$, and $f$ is not a diffeomorphism. The inverse function theorem obstruction: $df_p$ must be an isomorphism at every $p$.

q: Which condition on a smooth map $f\colon M\to N$ is necessary AND sufficient for $f$ to be a local diffeomorphism at $p\in M$?
text: Which condition on a smooth map $f\colon M\to N$ is necessary AND sufficient for $f$ to be a local diffeomorphism at $p\in M$? The inverse function theorem: $f$ is a local diffeomorphism at $p$ iff $df_p$ is a linear isomorphism, which forces $\dim M=\dim N$. Injectivity near $p$ is implied but not sufficient (think $x\mapsto x^3$). Every smooth map pulls back smooth functions.

q: Let $f\colon\mathbb{R}^2\to\mathbb{R}^2$, $f(x,y)=(e^x\cos y,\,e^x\sin y)$ (complex exponential). What is $|\det(df_p)|$ at the point $p=(0,0)$?
text: Let $f\colon\mathbb{R}^2\to\mathbb{R}^2$, $f(x,y)=(e^x\cos y,\,e^x\sin y)$ (complex exponential). What is $|\det(df_p)|$ at the point $p=(0,0)$? $df_p = \begin{pmatrix} e^x\cos y & -e^x\sin y \\ e^x\sin y & e^x\cos y\end{pmatrix}$, with determinant $e^{2x}$. At $(0,0)$ this is $1$. So $f$ is a local diffeomorphism everywhere, though not globally injective — $f$ is $2\pi$-periodic in $y$.

q: Select every statement that is true of the tangent space $T_pM$ at a point $p$ of a smooth $n$-manifold $M$.
text: Select every statement that is true of the tangent space $T_pM$ at a point $p$ of a smooth $n$-manifold $M$. $T_pM$ is an intrinsic $n$-dimensional real vector space. The point-derivation definition is one of three standard equivalent ones (the others use velocities of curves through $p$, or component-tuples under chart-change). Coordinate vectors give a basis in any chart, but the space itself is canonical (chart-independent). $T_pM$ is not a subset of $M$ — it is a separate vector space attached to $p$. Three equivalent definitions of $T_pM$ — equivalence-classes of curves, point-derivations, chart-dependent components — all give the same $n$-dimensional vector space, basis-independent.

q: What is the dimension of the tangent bundle $TM$ of a smooth $n$-manifold $M$?
text: What is the dimension of the tangent bundle $TM$ of a smooth $n$-manifold $M$? $TM$ has dimension $2n$; for $n=3$ this is $6$.

q: Under a chart change $y=y(x)$, a tangent vector with components $X^i$ becomes $\tilde X^j = (\partial y^j/\partial x^i)X^i$. If $X=(2,0)$ in coordinates $x$ and the Jacobian at the point is $\begin{pmatrix}3&1\\0&2\end{pmatrix}$, what is $\tilde X^1$?
text: Under a chart change $y=y(x)$, a tangent vector with components $X^i$ becomes $\tilde X^j = (\partial y^j/\partial x^i)X^i$. If $X=(2,0)$ in coordinates $x$ and the Jacobian at the point is $\begin{pmatrix}3&1\\0&2\end{pmatrix}$, what is $\tilde X^1$? $\tilde X^1 = 3\cdot 2 + 1\cdot 0 = 6$.

q: A smooth curve $\gamma:(-\varepsilon,\varepsilon)\to M$ defines a tangent vector $\gamma'(0)\in T_pM$ as a derivation on smooth functions. Which statement correctly orders the steps in showing this derivation satisfies the Leibniz rule?
text: A smooth curve $\gamma:(-\varepsilon,\varepsilon)\to M$ defines a tangent vector $\gamma'(0)\in T_pM$ as a derivation on smooth functions. Which statement correctly orders the steps in showing this derivation satisfies the Leibniz rule? $\gamma'(0)(fg)=\frac{d}{dt}\big|_{t=0}(fg\circ\gamma)=f(p)\,g'(0)+g(p)\,f'(0)=f(p)\,\gamma'(0)(g)+g(p)\,\gamma'(0)(f)$ by the real product rule, confirming Leibniz directly without passing to a chart.

q: Let $f:M\to N$ be a smooth map between manifolds of dimensions $3$ and $5$. What is the dimension of $\ker(df_p)$ if $df_p$ has rank $2$?
text: Let $f:M\to N$ be a smooth map between manifolds of dimensions $3$ and $5$. What is the dimension of $\ker(df_p)$ if $df_p$ has rank $2$? By the rank-nullity theorem applied to $df_p:T_pM\to T_{f(p)}N$, $\dim\ker(df_p)=\dim T_pM-\operatorname{rank}(df_p)=3-2=1$.

q: Which is a correct counterexample showing that the tangent bundle $TS^2$ is not trivial (i.e., $TS^2\ncong S^2\times\mathbb{R}^2$)?
text: Which is a correct counterexample showing that the tangent bundle $TS^2$ is not trivial (i.e., $TS^2\ncong S^2\times\mathbb{R}^2$)? A trivial rank-$n$ bundle admits $n$ everywhere-linearly-independent global sections, i.e., a global frame. The hairy ball theorem asserts every tangent vector field on $S^2$ vanishes somewhere, so no non-vanishing global section exists, let alone a full frame. Hence $TS^2$ is non-trivial.

q: The tangent bundle $TM$ of a smooth $n$-manifold $M$ is:
text: The tangent bundle $TM$ of a smooth $n$-manifold $M$ is: $TM=\bigsqcup_{p\in M}T_pM$ is a rank-$n$ vector bundle over $M$; each fibre is an $n$-plane, and the total space is a smooth manifold of dimension $2n$. $TM$ is trivial ($\cong M\times\mathbb{R}^n$) iff $M$ is parallelizable — false in general (e.g. $S^2$).

q: A smooth vector field on $M$ is:
text: A smooth vector field on $M$ is: A vector field assigns to each point $p\in M$ a vector $X_p\in T_pM$ smoothly, i.e. a smooth section of the projection $\pi\colon TM\to M$. Equivalently, a derivation on $C^\infty(M)$.

q: On $\mathbb{R}^2$, the vector field $X = x\,\partial_x + y\,\partial_y$ acts on the function $f(x,y)=x^2+y^2$ as $Xf$. What is $(Xf)(1,2)$?
text: On $\mathbb{R}^2$, the vector field $X = x\,\partial_x + y\,\partial_y$ acts on the function $f(x,y)=x^2+y^2$ as $Xf$. What is $(Xf)(1,2)$? $Xf = x\cdot 2x + y\cdot 2y = 2(x^2+y^2)$. At $(1,2)$: $2(1+4) = 10$.

q: A smooth manifold $M$ is called parallelizable if $TM$ is trivial (isomorphic to $M\times\mathbb{R}^n$). Which list is correct?
text: A smooth manifold $M$ is called parallelizable if $TM$ is trivial (isomorphic to $M\times\mathbb{R}^n$). Which list is correct? A classical theorem: $S^n$ is parallelizable iff $n\in\{1,3,7\}$, corresponding to the multiplicative structures on $\mathbb{C},\mathbb{H},\mathbb{O}$. The hairy-ball theorem handles $S^2$; more is needed for higher dimensions.

q: For a smooth $n$-manifold $M$, what is the dimension of the total space $TM$ of its tangent bundle when $n=5$?
text: For a smooth $n$-manifold $M$, what is the dimension of the total space $TM$ of its tangent bundle when $n=5$? $\dim TM = 2n$. For $n=5$ this is $10$: a chart $(U,\varphi)$ on $M$ gives a chart on $TM$ with coordinates $(x^1,\dots,x^n,\dot x^1,\dots,\dot x^n)$.

q: Let $X\in\mathfrak{X}(M)$ act on $C^\infty(M)$ as a derivation. Which property is NOT required?
text: Let $X\in\mathfrak{X}(M)$ act on $C^\infty(M)$ as a derivation. Which property is NOT required? Derivations are $\mathbb{R}$-linear Leibniz operators, automatically local (locality follows from Leibniz applied to bump functions). Multiplicativity $X(fg)=X(f)X(g)$ is the defining property of a ring homomorphism, not a derivation; derivations detect infinitesimal deformations, homomorphisms detect point evaluations.

q: A smooth map $f\colon M^m\to N^n$ is an immersion at $p$ iff $df_p$:
text: A smooth map $f\colon M^m\to N^n$ is an immersion at $p$ iff $df_p$: Immersion = injective differential, rank $m$. Submersion is the surjective case with rank $n$.

q: By the regular value theorem, if $c$ is a regular value of $g\colon\mathbb{R}^{n+1}\to\mathbb{R}$ then $g^{-1}(c)$ is a smooth manifold of dimension $n$. For $g(x)=\|x\|^2$ on $\mathbb{R}^4$ and $c=1$, what is the dimension of $g^{-1}(1)=S^3$?
text: By the regular value theorem, if $c$ is a regular value of $g\colon\mathbb{R}^{n+1}\to\mathbb{R}$ then $g^{-1}(c)$ is a smooth manifold of dimension $n$. For $g(x)=\|x\|^2$ on $\mathbb{R}^4$ and $c=1$, what is the dimension of $g^{-1}(1)=S^3$? $g\colon\mathbb{R}^4\to\mathbb{R}$, and $1$ is regular, so $g^{-1}(1)$ has dimension $4-1=3$.

q: Whitney's embedding theorem states that every smooth $n$-manifold admits a smooth embedding into:
text: Whitney's embedding theorem states that every smooth $n$-manifold admits a smooth embedding into: Whitney (1944): every smooth $n$-manifold embeds smoothly in $\mathbb{R}^{2n}$, and immerses in $\mathbb{R}^{2n-1}$.

q: The figure-eight $f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}^2$, $f(t)=(\sin 2t,\sin t)$, is an injective immersion but not an embedding. Which property fails?
text: The figure-eight $f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}^2$, $f(t)=(\sin 2t,\sin t)$, is an injective immersion but not an embedding. Which property fails? $f$ is injective and $df_t$ is everywhere non-zero, so it is an injective immersion. However, the image is a figure-eight whose crossing point has a neighbourhood in $f(\mathbb{R})$ that is not homeomorphic to an interval: nearby points in the domain map to four branches meeting at the origin, whereas the subspace topology sees all four. Thus $f$ is not a homeomorphism onto its image and fails to be an embedding.

q: Let $g:\mathbb{R}^3\to\mathbb{R}^2$, $g(x,y,z)=(x^2+y^2+z^2,\, x+y+z)$. At the point $p=(1,0,0)$, what is the rank of $dg_p$?
text: Let $g:\mathbb{R}^3\to\mathbb{R}^2$, $g(x,y,z)=(x^2+y^2+z^2,\, x+y+z)$. At the point $p=(1,0,0)$, what is the rank of $dg_p$? $dg_p = \begin{pmatrix}2&0&0\\1&1&1\end{pmatrix}$. The first row has leading entry $2$ and the second has entry $1$ in the second column; the two rows are linearly independent, so $\operatorname{rank}(dg_p)=2$, making $p$ a regular point.

q: By the constant-rank theorem, if $f:M^m\to N^n$ has constant rank $r$ near $p$, what is the local normal form of $f$?
text: By the constant-rank theorem, if $f:M^m\to N^n$ has constant rank $r$ near $p$, what is the local normal form of $f$? The constant-rank theorem (local form): choosing adapted charts around $p$ and $f(p)$, $f$ looks like the standard projection–injection $(x^1,\dots,x^m)\mapsto(x^1,\dots,x^r,0,\dots,0)\in\mathbb{R}^n$. The first $r$ coordinates are kept; the rest are sent to zero.

q: An integral curve of a smooth vector field $X\in\mathfrak{X}(M)$ through $p$ is a smooth curve $\gamma\colon I\to M$ with $\gamma(0)=p$ satisfying:
text: An integral curve of a smooth vector field $X\in\mathfrak{X}(M)$ through $p$ is a smooth curve $\gamma\colon I\to M$ with $\gamma(0)=p$ satisfying: An integral curve is a trajectory that follows the vector field: its velocity at every time equals the value of $X$ at the current position, i.e. $\gamma'(t)=X_{\gamma(t)}$.

q: On $\mathbb{R}$, the vector field $X=x\,\partial_x$ has integral curve through $x_0=1$ given by $\gamma(t)=e^t$. What is $\gamma(\ln 3)$?
text: On $\mathbb{R}$, the vector field $X=x\,\partial_x$ has integral curve through $x_0=1$ given by $\gamma(t)=e^t$. What is $\gamma(\ln 3)$? Solving $\dot x=x$ with $x(0)=1$ gives $x(t)=e^t$. Then $\gamma(\ln 3)=e^{\ln 3}=3$.

q: On a compact smooth manifold $M$, every smooth vector field is:
text: On a compact smooth manifold $M$, every smooth vector field is: On a compact manifold integral curves cannot escape to infinity in finite time, so the maximal domain of each integral curve is all of $\mathbb{R}$. Equivalently, the flow $\Phi^X_t$ is defined for every real $t$ — $X$ is complete.

q: Which is a correct counterexample to the claim 'every smooth vector field on a non-compact manifold is complete'?
text: Which is a correct counterexample to the claim 'every smooth vector field on a non-compact manifold is complete'? The ODE $\dot x=x^2$, $x(0)=1$ has solution $x(t)=1/(1-t)$, which escapes to $+\infty$ as $t\to 1^-$. So the maximal interval of existence is $(-\infty,1)$ — strictly less than $\mathbb{R}$ — and $X=x^2\partial_x$ on $\mathbb{R}$ is incomplete. Leaving compact sets in finite time per se is fine (A, D) as long as the curve stays in $M$; only blow-up in finite time breaks completeness. Escape to infinity is fine; incompleteness needs blow-up in finite time.

q: The flow $\Phi^X_t$ of a complete vector field $X$ satisfies which identity?
text: The flow $\Phi^X_t$ of a complete vector field $X$ satisfies which identity? Uniqueness of ODE solutions, together with time-translation invariance, forces $\Phi^X_{s+t}=\Phi^X_s\circ\Phi^X_t$ wherever both sides are defined; when $X$ is complete this holds on all of $M\times\mathbb{R}^2$, making $\{\Phi^X_t\}_{t\in\mathbb{R}}$ a one-parameter group of diffeomorphisms of $M$.

q: Let $X=-y\,\partial_x+x\,\partial_y$ on $\mathbb{R}^2$ (rotation generator). Starting at $(1,0)$, the integral curve is $\gamma(t)=(\cos t,\sin t)$. What is the $y$-coordinate of $\Phi^X_{\pi/2}(1,0)$?
text: Let $X=-y\,\partial_x+x\,\partial_y$ on $\mathbb{R}^2$ (rotation generator). Starting at $(1,0)$, the integral curve is $\gamma(t)=(\cos t,\sin t)$. What is the $y$-coordinate of $\Phi^X_{\pi/2}(1,0)$? $\Phi^X_{\pi/2}(1,0)=(\cos(\pi/2),\sin(\pi/2))=(0,1)$, so the $y$-coordinate is $1$.

q: The Lie bracket of two smooth vector fields $X,Y\in\mathfrak{X}(M)$ is defined on functions by:
text: The Lie bracket of two smooth vector fields $X,Y\in\mathfrak{X}(M)$ is defined on functions by: $[X,Y] = XY - YX$ as operators on $C^\infty(M)$. The second-order terms cancel and the commutator is again a derivation, hence a vector field.

q: On $\mathbb{R}^2$ with $X=\partial_x$ and $Y=x\,\partial_y$, the Lie bracket is $[X,Y]=\partial_y$. Applying $[X,Y]$ to $f(x,y)=y^2$ at the point $(3,4)$ gives what number?
text: On $\mathbb{R}^2$ with $X=\partial_x$ and $Y=x\,\partial_y$, the Lie bracket is $[X,Y]=\partial_y$. Applying $[X,Y]$ to $f(x,y)=y^2$ at the point $(3,4)$ gives what number? $[X,Y]f = \partial_y(y^2) = 2y$. At $(3,4)$ this evaluates to $2\cdot 4 = 8$.

q: Select every identity that holds for the Lie bracket on $\mathfrak{X}(M)$ for arbitrary $X,Y,Z\in\mathfrak{X}(M)$ and $f\in C^\infty(M)$.
text: Select every identity that holds for the Lie bracket on $\mathfrak{X}(M)$ for arbitrary $X,Y,Z\in\mathfrak{X}(M)$ and $f\in C^\infty(M)$. Antisymmetry and Jacobi together make $\mathfrak{X}(M)$ a Lie algebra over $\mathbb{R}$. From antisymmetry, $[X,X]=0$ — so options (3) and (4) fail. The bracket is not $C^\infty$-bilinear: $[fX,Y]=f[X,Y]-Y(f)X$, with the extra $-Y(f)X$ term reflecting the fact that $Y$ differentiates the coefficient $f$. This is why the Lie bracket is a differential operator, not a tensor. Antisymmetry + Jacobi makes $\mathfrak{X}(M)$ a Lie algebra; the bracket is $\mathbb{R}$-bilinear but not $C^\infty$-bilinear — pulling out a function picks up a derivation term.

q: The Lie bracket satisfies the Leibniz-like identity $[X,fY] = f[X,Y] + (Xf)Y$ for $f\in C^\infty(M)$. Which statement follows?
text: The Lie bracket satisfies the Leibniz-like identity $[X,fY] = f[X,Y] + (Xf)Y$ for $f\in C^\infty(M)$. Which statement follows? The formula $[X,fY]=f[X,Y]+(Xf)Y$ shows a derivation-of-scalars correction term. Over $\mathbb{R}$ the bracket is bilinear, but over $C^\infty(M)$ it fails — unlike the pointwise operations of a vector bundle. The bracket is a genuinely differential (not tensorial) operation.

q: Two smooth vector fields $X,Y$ on $M$ have flows that commute, $\Phi^X_s\circ\Phi^Y_t=\Phi^Y_t\circ\Phi^X_s$ for all small $s,t$, iff:
text: Two smooth vector fields $X,Y$ on $M$ have flows that commute, $\Phi^X_s\circ\Phi^Y_t=\Phi^Y_t\circ\Phi^X_s$ for all small $s,t$, iff: This is the flow-commutativity theorem: the flows commute (on overlapping domains) iff the bracket $[X,Y]$ vanishes identically. The infinitesimal obstruction to flow commutativity is exactly the bracket, consistent with $\Phi^{-Y}_t\Phi^{-X}_t\Phi^Y_t\Phi^X_t(p)=p+t^2[X,Y]_p+O(t^3)$.

q: On $\mathbb{R}^2$ with $X = \partial_x$ and $Y = x\,\partial_y + y\,\partial_x$, compute the $\partial_y$-component of $[X,Y]$ evaluated at any point.
text: On $\mathbb{R}^2$ with $X = \partial_x$ and $Y = x\,\partial_y + y\,\partial_x$, compute the $\partial_y$-component of $[X,Y]$ evaluated at any point. For vector fields written as $X=X^i\partial_i$, $Y=Y^j\partial_j$, the bracket components are $[X,Y]^k = X^i\partial_i Y^k - Y^i\partial_i X^k$. Here $X=(1,0)$ and $Y=(y,x)$, with $X$ having constant coefficients. $[X,Y]^y = X^i\partial_i Y^y - Y^i\partial_i X^y = \partial_x(x) - 0 = 1$. So $[X,Y]^y = 1$ is constant (independent of position).

q: A standard construction of a smooth partition of unity subordinate to an open cover $\{U_\alpha\}$ of a paracompact smooth manifold $M$ proceeds as follows. Pick the correct next step.
text: A standard construction of a smooth partition of unity subordinate to an open cover $\{U_\alpha\}$ of a paracompact smooth manifold $M$ proceeds as follows. Pick the correct next step. The final step is normalization: $\rho_\beta := \psi_\beta/\Psi$ is smooth (denominator is smooth and positive), satisfies $\operatorname{supp}\rho_\beta\subset V_\beta$, and $\sum_\beta\rho_\beta = \Psi/\Psi = 1$. Local finiteness of $\{V_\beta\}$ guarantees the pointwise sum has only finitely many nonzero terms. The result is a smooth partition of unity subordinate to the (refined) cover. Normalize each bump by the locally finite (smooth, positive) sum.

q: On which class of smooth manifolds is the existence of smooth partitions of unity guaranteed?
text: On which class of smooth manifolds is the existence of smooth partitions of unity guaranteed? Smooth partitions of unity exist on any paracompact smooth manifold; our definition already assumes second countability + Hausdorff, which imply paracompactness, so every smooth manifold in this notebook admits them. Compactness and orientability are irrelevant. Smooth partitions of unity exist on any paracompact smooth manifold; our definition already assumes second countability + Hausdorff, which imply paracompactness, so every smooth manifold in this…

q: Suppose $\{\rho_1,\rho_2,\rho_3\}$ is a partition of unity on $M$ and at a point $p$, $\rho_1(p)=0.2$, $\rho_2(p)=0.5$. What is $\rho_3(p)$?
text: Suppose $\{\rho_1,\rho_2,\rho_3\}$ is a partition of unity on $M$ and at a point $p$, $\rho_1(p)=0.2$, $\rho_2(p)=0.5$. What is $\rho_3(p)$? By definition $\rho_1(p)+\rho_2(p)+\rho_3(p)=1$, so $\rho_3(p)=1-0.2-0.5=0.3$.

q: Which is the standard use of a partition of unity in the proof that every smooth manifold admits a Riemannian metric?
text: Which is the standard use of a partition of unity in the proof that every smooth manifold admits a Riemannian metric? On each chart domain $U_\alpha$ pull back the Euclidean metric to get a local Riemannian metric $g_\alpha$. A convex combination $\sum_\alpha\rho_\alpha g_\alpha$ of positive-definite symmetric forms with non-negative weights summing to $1$ is itself positive-definite wherever the sum is non-empty, which is everywhere because $\sum\rho_\alpha=1$. Smoothness follows from local finiteness.

q: Why does the analogous gluing fail for holomorphic (complex-analytic) structures — i.e. why is there no holomorphic partition of unity on a complex manifold?
text: Why does the analogous gluing fail for holomorphic (complex-analytic) structures — i.e. why is there no holomorphic partition of unity on a complex manifold? Smooth bumps exist because $C^\infty$ is extremely flexible — non-analytic functions like $e^{-1/x^2}$ can vanish on open sets. Holomorphic functions obey the identity theorem / maximum principle, so a non-zero holomorphic function with compact support would have to be zero on an open set and hence identically zero. This rigidity kills holomorphic partitions of unity and is why sheaf cohomology and \v{C}ech methods replace them on complex manifolds.

q: A smooth manifold $M$ is orientable iff:
text: A smooth manifold $M$ is orientable iff: Orientability is equivalent to the existence of an oriented atlas — one for which every transition $\varphi_\beta\circ\varphi_\alpha^{-1}$ has $\det D(\varphi_\beta\circ\varphi_\alpha^{-1})>0$ on its domain. Equivalently, $M$ admits a nowhere-vanishing top-degree form. Orientability is equivalent to the existence of an oriented atlas — one for which every transition $\varphi_\beta\circ\varphi_\alpha^{-1}$ has $\det D(\varphi_\beta\circ\varphi_\alpha^{-1})>0$ on its…

q: Which of the following manifolds is NOT orientable?
text: Which of the following manifolds is NOT orientable? The Möbius band has a single side: going once around the core circle and returning flips a local frame's handedness, so no consistent orientation exists. $S^2$, $T^2$, and $\mathbb{RP}^3$ are orientable ($\mathbb{RP}^n$ is orientable iff $n$ is odd).

q: How many orientations does a connected orientable smooth manifold admit?
text: How many orientations does a connected orientable smooth manifold admit? An orientation is a choice of sign on the top exterior power at each point, varying continuously. On a connected manifold there are exactly two global choices, differing by an overall sign flip.

q: Which statement correctly identifies $\mathbb{RP}^n$'s orientability pattern?
text: Which statement correctly identifies $\mathbb{RP}^n$'s orientability pattern? $\mathbb{RP}^n = S^n/\{\pm 1\}$. The antipodal map $a\colon S^n\to S^n$ is orientation-preserving iff its degree $(-1)^{n+1}$ is $+1$, i.e. iff $n$ is odd. So the quotient is orientable precisely when $n$ is odd. In particular $\mathbb{RP}^1\cong S^1$ and $\mathbb{RP}^3\cong\mathrm{SO}(3)$ are orientable; $\mathbb{RP}^2$ and $\mathbb{RP}^4$ are not.

q: On the 2-sphere $S^2$ with the usual orientation, consider the two stereographic charts from $N$ and $S$. On their overlap $S^2\setminus\{N,S\}$, the transition is $\varphi_S\circ\varphi_N^{-1}(u,v)=(u,v)/(u^2+v^2)$. What is the sign of the Jacobian determinant of this transition at $(u,v)=(1,0)$? Enter $+1$ or $-1$.
text: On the 2-sphere $S^2$ with the usual orientation, consider the two stereographic charts from $N$ and $S$. On their overlap $S^2\setminus\{N,S\}$, the transition is $\varphi_S\circ\varphi_N^{-1}(u,v)=(u,v)/(u^2+v^2)$. What is the sign of the Jacobian determinant of this transition at $(u,v)=(1,0)$? Enter $+1$ or $-1$. Write the transition as $(u,v)\mapsto(u/r^2,v/r^2)$ with $r^2=u^2+v^2$. Its Jacobian at $(1,0)$ has determinant $-1/r^4=-1$. So these two stereographic charts are oppositely oriented; to get a positively oriented atlas one composes $\varphi_S$ with a reflection. This is why the two-chart stereographic atlas is a smooth atlas but not an oriented atlas as stated — an adjustment is needed.

q: Which of the following is equivalent to the manifold $M$ being orientable?
text: Which of the following is equivalent to the manifold $M$ being orientable? The standard equivalence: $M$ is orientable iff $\Lambda^n T^*M$ has a nowhere-zero smooth section, i.e. there is a volume form. Every smooth manifold admits a Riemannian metric (via partitions of unity), so (B) is not equivalent. Trivial $TM$ (parallelizability) is strictly stronger than orientability — e.g. $S^2$ is orientable but not parallelizable.

title: Differential forms and wedge product
blurb: Alternating multilinear forms package oriented area and volume data.

title: Exterior derivative
blurb: The exterior derivative extends gradient/curl/divergence and satisfies d^2=0.

title: Pullback of forms
blurb: A smooth map f:M→N contravariantly transports k-forms back, commuting with d and ∧ — this is why integration of forms is coordinate-invariant.

title: Integration of forms over chains
blurb: A smooth k-chain is a formal sum of smooth simplices; integrating a k-form over a chain is defined via pullback to the standard simplex and extends by linearity.

title: Stokes theorem and de Rham cohomology
blurb: Stokes unifies integration theorems and identifies topology via closed/exact forms.

q: Which identity characterizes the wedge product of two $1$-forms $\alpha,\beta$?
text: Which identity characterizes the wedge product of two $1$-forms $\alpha,\beta$? The wedge of $1$-forms is antisymmetric: $\alpha\wedge\beta = -\beta\wedge\alpha$. In particular $\alpha\wedge\alpha = 0$, which is exactly the alternating/oriented-area property.

q: What is $\dim \Omega^2(\mathbb{R}^4)$ over $C^\infty(\mathbb{R}^4)$? (Equivalently, $\binom{4}{2}$.)
text: What is $\dim \Omega^2(\mathbb{R}^4)$ over $C^\infty(\mathbb{R}^4)$? (Equivalently, $\binom{4}{2}$.) A basis of $\Omega^k(\mathbb{R}^n)$ is $\{dx^{i_1}\wedge\cdots\wedge dx^{i_k}\}$ with strictly increasing indices, so the dimension is $\binom{n}{k}=\binom{4}{2}=6$.

q: Let $v=(2,1)$ and $w=(1,3)$ in $\mathbb{R}^2$. Compute $(dx\wedge dy)(v,w) = v_1 w_2 - v_2 w_1$.
text: Let $v=(2,1)$ and $w=(1,3)$ in $\mathbb{R}^2$. Compute $(dx\wedge dy)(v,w) = v_1 w_2 - v_2 w_1$. $(dx\wedge dy)(v,w)=\det\begin{pmatrix}2&1\\1&3\end{pmatrix}=2\cdot 3-1\cdot 1=5$.

q: Let $\alpha = x\,dy\wedge dz + y\,dz\wedge dx + z\,dx\wedge dy$ on $\mathbb{R}^3$. Compute $(dx\wedge dy\wedge dz)(e_1,e_2,e_3)$ where $e_i$ are standard basis vectors.
text: Let $\alpha = x\,dy\wedge dz + y\,dz\wedge dx + z\,dx\wedge dy$ on $\mathbb{R}^3$. Compute $(dx\wedge dy\wedge dz)(e_1,e_2,e_3)$ where $e_i$ are standard basis vectors. $(dx\wedge dy\wedge dz)(e_1,e_2,e_3)=\det[dx^i(e_j)]=\det I_3=1$. The wedge of all coordinate $1$-forms evaluates to $1$ on the positively oriented standard frame.

q: Which is a counterexample showing that $\alpha\wedge\alpha=0$ does NOT hold for all forms?
text: Which is a counterexample showing that $\alpha\wedge\alpha=0$ does NOT hold for all forms? Self-wedge vanishes for odd-degree forms (since $\alpha\wedge\alpha=(-1)^{k^2}\alpha\wedge\alpha$ forces $0$ when $k$ is odd). For even $k$, e.g. $k=2$ in $\mathbb{R}^4$: $(dx^1\wedge dx^2+dx^3\wedge dx^4)^2=2\,dx^1\wedge dx^2\wedge dx^3\wedge dx^4\ne 0$.

q: Compute $\dim\Omega^k(\mathbb{R}^5)$ for $k=3$.
text: Compute $\dim\Omega^k(\mathbb{R}^5)$ for $k=3$. $\binom{5}{3}=10$. Basis elements are $dx^{i_1}\wedge dx^{i_2}\wedge dx^{i_3}$ with $i_1<i_2<i_3$ from $\{1,2,3,4,5\}$.

q: Which property characterizes the exterior derivative $d$ (in addition to $df$ being the usual differential and $d$ being a graded derivation)?
text: Which property characterizes the exterior derivative $d$ (in addition to $df$ being the usual differential and $d$ being a graded derivation)? The defining axioms are: $df$ is the usual differential on $0$-forms, $d$ is a graded derivation, and $d^2=0$. The last is dual to $\partial\partial=0$ for chains.

q: For $\omega = x\,dy - y\,dx$ on $\mathbb{R}^2$, compute the coefficient of $dx\wedge dy$ in $d\omega$ (i.e. $Q_x - P_y$ where $\omega = P\,dx + Q\,dy$).
text: For $\omega = x\,dy - y\,dx$ on $\mathbb{R}^2$, compute the coefficient of $dx\wedge dy$ in $d\omega$ (i.e. $Q_x - P_y$ where $\omega = P\,dx + Q\,dy$). Here $P=-y$, $Q=x$, so $Q_x-P_y = 1-(-1)=2$. Thus $d\omega = 2\,dx\wedge dy$.

q: On $\mathbb{R}^3$, which classical identity does $d^2=0$ encode when applied to a $1$-form $\omega_X$ coming from a vector field $X$?
text:
On $\mathbb{R}^3$, which classical identity does $d^2=0$ encode when applied to a $1$-form $\omega_X$ coming from a vector field $X$? Via $f\leftrightarrow 0$-form, $X\leftrightarrow 1$-form, and $\star$ to get $2$-forms, $d^2=0$ becomes $

abla	imes X)=0$ — two classical identities, one proof. Via $f\leftrightarrow 0$-form, $X\leftrightarrow 1$-form, and $\star$ to get $2$-forms, $d^2=0$ becomes $
abla	imes(

abla\cdot(

q: Let $\omega = xyz\,dx\wedge dy\wedge dz$ on $\mathbb{R}^3$. What is $d\omega$?
text: Let $\omega = xyz\,dx\wedge dy\wedge dz$ on $\mathbb{R}^3$. What is $d\omega$? $\omega$ is already a $3$-form on $\mathbb{R}^3$, so $d\omega\in\Omega^4(\mathbb{R}^3)=0$. The coefficient (thought of as a $4$-form component) is $0$.

q: Compute $d(e^{xy}\,dx)$ on $\mathbb{R}^2$.
text: Compute $d(e^{xy}\,dx)$ on $\mathbb{R}^2$. $d(e^{xy}\,dx)=d(e^{xy})\wedge dx=(ye^{xy}\,dy+xe^{xy}\,dx)\wedge dx=ye^{xy}\,dy\wedge dx=-ye^{xy}\,dx\wedge dy$. Wait — the coefficient of $dx\wedge dy$ is $-(-ye^{xy})$: re-checking, $d(e^{xy}\,dx)=\frac{\partial e^{xy}}{\partial y}dy\wedge dx=xe^{xy}\,dy\wedge dx=-xe^{xy}\,dx\wedge dy$. So choice 1 with coefficient $xe^{xy}$ (negated orientation) matches choice B written as $xe^{xy}\,dx\wedge dy$ if we note $dy\wedge dx=-dx\wedge dy$.

q: Order these steps in deriving $d^2f=0$ for a smooth function $f$ on $\mathbb{R}^n$. Which ordering is correct? (I) Write $df=\sum_i \partial_i f\,dx^i$. (II) Apply $d$ again: $d^2f=\sum_{i,j}\partial_j\partial_i f\,dx^j\wedge dx^i$. (III) Note the sum over $i<j$ telescopes as $(\partial_j\partial_i f - \partial_i\partial_j f)dx^j\wedge dx^i=0$ by Schwarz. (IV) Antisymmetry of $\wedge$ pairs $(j,i)$ with $(i,j)$.
text: Order these steps in deriving $d^2f=0$ for a smooth function $f$ on $\mathbb{R}^n$. Which ordering is correct? (I) Write $df=\sum_i \partial_i f\,dx^i$. (II) Apply $d$ again: $d^2f=\sum_{i,j}\partial_j\partial_i f\,dx^j\wedge dx^i$. (III) Note the sum over $i<j$ telescopes as $(\partial_j\partial_i f - \partial_i\partial_j f)dx^j\wedge dx^i=0$ by Schwarz. (IV) Antisymmetry of $\wedge$ pairs $(j,i)$ with $(i,j)$. Logical order: (I) compute $df$, (II) apply $d$ to get a double sum, (IV) invoke antisymmetry of wedge to pair terms, (III) conclude each paired sum vanishes by Schwarz (equality of mixed partials).

q: Which compatibility does the pullback $f^*$ satisfy for a smooth $f:M\to N$?
text: Which compatibility does the pullback $f^*$ satisfy for a smooth $f:M\to N$? Pullback is an algebra homomorphism that commutes with $d$: $f^*(\alpha\wedge\beta)=f^*\alpha\wedge f^*\beta$ and $f^*\circ d = d\circ f^*$. These two facts are what make differential forms natural.

q: Let $f(u,v) = (u+v,\ uv)$ from $\mathbb{R}^2$ to $\mathbb{R}^2$ with target coordinates $(x,y)$. Compute the coefficient of $du\wedge dv$ in $f^*(dx\wedge dy)$ at $(u,v)=(3,1)$.
text: Let $f(u,v) = (u+v,\ uv)$ from $\mathbb{R}^2$ to $\mathbb{R}^2$ with target coordinates $(x,y)$. Compute the coefficient of $du\wedge dv$ in $f^*(dx\wedge dy)$ at $(u,v)=(3,1)$. $f^*dx = du+dv$ and $f^*dy = v\,du+u\,dv$, so $f^*(dx\wedge dy) = (du+dv)\wedge(v\,du+u\,dv) = (u-v)\,du\wedge dv$. At $(3,1)$ the coefficient is $3-1=2$. (Equivalently, the Jacobian determinant $\det Df = u-v$.)

q: Let $\iota:S^1\hookrightarrow\mathbb{R}^2\setminus\{0\}$ parametrized by $\theta\mapsto(\cos\theta,\sin\theta)$. Compute $\iota^*\bigl((-y\,dx+x\,dy)/(x^2+y^2)\bigr)$.
text: Let $\iota:S^1\hookrightarrow\mathbb{R}^2\setminus\{0\}$ parametrized by $\theta\mapsto(\cos\theta,\sin\theta)$. Compute $\iota^*\bigl((-y\,dx+x\,dy)/(x^2+y^2)\bigr)$. On $S^1$, $x=\cos\theta$, $y=\sin\theta$, $x^2+y^2=1$, $dx=-\sin\theta\,d\theta$, $dy=\cos\theta\,d\theta$. Then $-y\,dx+x\,dy=\sin^2\theta\,d\theta+\cos^2\theta\,d\theta=d\theta$. The pullback of the angle form is literally $d\theta$.

q: Let $f(u,v)=(u^2-v^2,\ 2uv)$ from $\mathbb{R}^2\to\mathbb{R}^2$. Compute $\det Df$ at $(u,v)=(1,1)$.
text: Let $f(u,v)=(u^2-v^2,\ 2uv)$ from $\mathbb{R}^2\to\mathbb{R}^2$. Compute $\det Df$ at $(u,v)=(1,1)$. $Df=\begin{pmatrix}2u & -2v \\ 2v & 2u\end{pmatrix}$, so $\det Df=4u^2+4v^2$. At $(1,1)$: $4+4=8$. Equivalently, $f$ corresponds to $z\mapsto z^2$ and $|f'(z)|^2=4|z|^2=8$ at $z=1+i$.

q: Which is a counterexample showing that $f^*$ does NOT always yield a nonzero form?
text: Which is a counterexample showing that $f^*$ does NOT always yield a nonzero form? When $\dim M < \dim N$, one cannot wedge more than $\dim M$ independent $1$-forms on $M$, so $\Omega^{\dim N}(M)=0$. Thus $f^*$ kills every top form of $N$, giving zero regardless of $f$.

q: Let $g(s,t)=(s\cos t,\ s\sin t)$ (polar coordinates), $s>0$. Compute the coefficient of $ds\wedge dt$ in $g^*(dx\wedge dy)$ at $s=2$.
text: Let $g(s,t)=(s\cos t,\ s\sin t)$ (polar coordinates), $s>0$. Compute the coefficient of $ds\wedge dt$ in $g^*(dx\wedge dy)$ at $s=2$. $g^*dx=\cos t\,ds-s\sin t\,dt$, $g^*dy=\sin t\,ds+s\cos t\,dt$. Wedging: $g^*(dx\wedge dy)=s(\cos^2 t+\sin^2 t)\,ds\wedge dt=s\,ds\wedge dt$. At $s=2$ the coefficient is $2$.

q: For a smooth $k$-simplex $\sigma:\Delta^k\to M$ and $\omega\in\Omega^k(M)$, the integral $\int_\sigma \omega$ is defined as:
text: For a smooth $k$-simplex $\sigma:\Delta^k\to M$ and $\omega\in\Omega^k(M)$, the integral $\int_\sigma \omega$ is defined as: Integration over a smooth simplex is $\int_\sigma\omega := \int_{\Delta^k}\sigma^*\omega$. This is well-defined because $\sigma^*\omega$ is a $k$-form on $\Delta^k\subset\mathbb{R}^k$, which integrates via the standard Lebesgue integral of its $du^1\wedge\cdots\wedge du^k$ coefficient.

q: Why is $\int_\sigma\omega$ independent of the parametrization $\sigma$ (up to orientation-preserving reparametrization)?
text: Why is $\int_\sigma\omega$ independent of the parametrization $\sigma$ (up to orientation-preserving reparametrization)? For an orientation-preserving diffeomorphism $\phi$, change of variables gives $\int\phi^*\eta = \int\eta$. Since forms pull back with a Jacobian determinant, the antisymmetry of $\wedge$ is exactly what makes integration of forms reparametrization-invariant.

q: Let $\sigma:[0,1]\to\mathbb{R}^2$, $\sigma(t)=(t,t^2)$, and $\omega = dy$. Compute $\int_\sigma\omega$.
text: Let $\sigma:[0,1]\to\mathbb{R}^2$, $\sigma(t)=(t,t^2)$, and $\omega = dy$. Compute $\int_\sigma\omega$. $\sigma^*\omega = \sigma^*(dy) = d(t^2) = 2t\,dt$. Then $\int_\sigma\omega = \int_0^1 2t\,dt = 1$. Geometrically, $\int_\sigma dy$ is just the net vertical displacement of $\sigma$, $y(1)-y(0)=1-0=1$.

q: Let $c = \sigma_1 - \sigma_2$ be a $1$-chain where $\sigma_1(t)=(t,0)$ and $\sigma_2(t)=(0,t)$ for $t\in[0,1]$, and let $\omega=x\,dx+y\,dy$. Compute $\int_c\omega$.
text: Let $c = \sigma_1 - \sigma_2$ be a $1$-chain where $\sigma_1(t)=(t,0)$ and $\sigma_2(t)=(0,t)$ for $t\in[0,1]$, and let $\omega=x\,dx+y\,dy$. Compute $\int_c\omega$. $\int_{\sigma_1}\omega=\int_0^1 t\,dt=\frac{1}{2}$. $\int_{\sigma_2}\omega=\int_0^1 t\,dt=\frac{1}{2}$. So $\int_c\omega=\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{2}=0$. Indeed $\omega=d(\frac{x^2+y^2}{2})$ is exact, so its integral over any cycle (or any chain with equal endpoints) is path-independent.

q: Order the steps for computing $\int_\sigma (x\,dy)$ over $\sigma(t)=(\cos t,\sin t)$, $t\in[0,\pi]$. (I) Pull back: $x\,dy=\cos t\cdot\cos t\,dt=\cos^2 t\,dt$. (II) Integrate: $\int_0^\pi\cos^2 t\,dt=\pi/2$. (III) Identify $x=\cos t$, $dy=\cos t\,dt$. (IV) Parametrize the curve.
text: Order the steps for computing $\int_\sigma (x\,dy)$ over $\sigma(t)=(\cos t,\sin t)$, $t\in[0,\pi]$. (I) Pull back: $x\,dy=\cos t\cdot\cos t\,dt=\cos^2 t\,dt$. (II) Integrate: $\int_0^\pi\cos^2 t\,dt=\pi/2$. (III) Identify $x=\cos t$, $dy=\cos t\,dt$. (IV) Parametrize the curve. Correct order: (IV) lay out the parametrization, (III) express the form's ingredients via the parametrization, (I) form the pullback, (II) integrate over $[0,\pi]$.

q: The boundary of the standard $2$-simplex $\Delta^2$ consists of three $1$-simplices. How many $0$-simplices (vertices) does $\partial\Delta^2$ have (counting with multiplicity from the chain formula $\partial_0\circ\partial_1 c=0$, i.e. how many distinct vertices)?
text: The boundary of the standard $2$-simplex $\Delta^2$ consists of three $1$-simplices. How many $0$-simplices (vertices) does $\partial\Delta^2$ have (counting with multiplicity from the chain formula $\partial_0\circ\partial_1 c=0$, i.e. how many distinct vertices)? $\partial\Delta^2 = [v_1,v_2]-[v_0,v_2]+[v_0,v_1]$. Each edge contributes its two endpoints; the three edges share the three vertices $v_0,v_1,v_2$. All boundary-of-boundary cancellations confirm $\partial^2=0$, and there are $3$ distinct vertices.

q: Stokes' theorem says:
text: Stokes' theorem says: Stokes: $\int_M d\omega = \int_{\partial M}\omega$. It unifies the fundamental theorem of calculus, Green, classical Stokes, and divergence theorems.

q: Let $\omega = (-y\,dx + x\,dy)/(x^2+y^2)$ on $\mathbb{R}^2\setminus\{0\}$ and let $\gamma$ be a loop winding $n=3$ times counterclockwise around $0$. Compute $\oint_\gamma \omega / \pi$.
text: Let $\omega = (-y\,dx + x\,dy)/(x^2+y^2)$ on $\mathbb{R}^2\setminus\{0\}$ and let $\gamma$ be a loop winding $n=3$ times counterclockwise around $0$. Compute $\oint_\gamma \omega / \pi$. $\oint_\gamma \omega = 2\pi n = 6\pi$, so $\oint_\gamma\omega/\pi = 6$. Nonzero integral means $\omega$ is closed but not exact.

q: What is $\dim H^1_{\mathrm{dR}}(T^2)$ for the $2$-torus $T^2 = S^1\times S^1$?
text: What is $\dim H^1_{\mathrm{dR}}(T^2)$ for the $2$-torus $T^2 = S^1\times S^1$? The torus has two independent $1$-cycles ($d\theta_1$ and $d\theta_2$), so $H^1_{\mathrm{dR}}(T^2)\cong\mathbb{R}^2$ and its dimension is $2$.

q: Let $\omega$ be a closed $k$-form on a smooth manifold $M$ with $\partial M=\emptyset$. Which statement is a direct consequence of Stokes' theorem?
text: Let $\omega$ be a closed $k$-form on a smooth manifold $M$ with $\partial M=\emptyset$. Which statement is a direct consequence of Stokes' theorem? Stokes: $\int_M d\omega=\int_{\partial M}\omega$. Since $d\omega=0$ (closed), the left side is $0$; since $\partial M=\emptyset$, the right side is also $0$ — both sides trivially vanish. This consistency does NOT force $\omega$ to be exact, as the angle form on $S^1$ demonstrates.

q: Compute $\dim H^2_{\mathrm{dR}}(S^2)$.
text: Compute $\dim H^2_{\mathrm{dR}}(S^2)$. By de Rham's theorem, $H^k_{\mathrm{dR}}(S^n)\cong H^k(S^n;\mathbb{R})$. For $S^2$: $H^0=H^2=\mathbb{R}$ and $H^1=0$. So $\dim H^2_{\mathrm{dR}}(S^2)=1$, generated by the area form.

q: Which is the correct counterexample showing a closed form need not be exact?
text: Which is the correct counterexample showing a closed form need not be exact? If $d\theta$ were exact, say $d\theta=df$, then $\oint_{S^1}d\theta=\oint_{S^1}df=f(2\pi)-f(0)=0$. But the integral is $2\pi\ne 0$. So $d\theta$ is a closed non-exact $1$-form, showing $H^1_{\mathrm{dR}}(\mathbb{R}^2\setminus\{0\})\ne 0$. An exact form integrates to zero around any loop; find one that doesn't.

title: 1. Curves in the plane and in space
Curves and surfaces in R^3
blurb: A regular surface $S\subset\mathbb{R}^3$ carries local parametrizations $\mathbf{x}(u,v)$ with linearly independent $\mathbf{x}_u,\mathbf{x}_v$, a tangent plane $T_pS$, and a unit normal $\mathbf{n}$; orientability asks whether $\mathbf{n}$ can be chosen globally.

title: First/second fundamental forms
blurb: The first form $I = E\,du^2 + 2F\,du\,dv + G\,dv^2$ records intrinsic length and area; the second form $II$ and its self-adjoint shape operator $S_p = -dN_p$ record how $S$ curves extrinsically in $\mathbb{R}^3$.

title: Gauss map and shape operator
blurb: The unit-normal map n: S → S^2 has differential dN_p = −S_p, the self-adjoint shape operator whose eigenvalues are the principal curvatures and whose determinant is the Gaussian curvature.

title: Gaussian curvature and geodesics
blurb: Gaussian curvature $K = \det S_p$ is intrinsic (Theorema Egregium); geodesics satisfy $\ddot\gamma^k + \Gamma^k_{ij}\dot\gamma^i\dot\gamma^j = 0$ with Christoffel symbols built from $I$, modelling shortest paths on $S$.

title: Gauss–Bonnet theorem
blurb: Total Gaussian curvature of a closed oriented surface is 2πχ(S): curvature is pinned to topology, with a local version tying K, geodesic curvature, and exterior angles to 2π.

q: A local parametrization $\mathbf{x}\colon U\subseteq\mathbb{R}^2\to S$ is regular at $q\in U$ exactly when:
text: A local parametrization $\mathbf{x}\colon U\subseteq\mathbb{R}^2\to S$ is regular at $q\in U$ exactly when: Regularity means $d\mathbf{x}_q$ is injective, i.e. the two partial derivatives span a 2-plane; equivalently their cross product is nonzero.

q: For the unit-speed circle $\gamma(t)=(\cos t,\sin t)$ in the plane, what is the curvature $\kappa$?
text: For the unit-speed circle $\gamma(t)=(\cos t,\sin t)$ in the plane, what is the curvature $\kappa$? A unit circle has $\kappa=1/R=1$. In general $\kappa=|\ddot\gamma|$ for a unit-speed planar curve, and here $|\ddot\gamma|=1$.

q: Let $\mathbf{x}(u,v)=(u,v,u^2-v^2)$ (the saddle). At $(u,v)=(0,0)$, $\mathbf{x}_u\times\mathbf{x}_v=(a,b,c)$. Give $a+bi$.
text: Let $\mathbf{x}(u,v)=(u,v,u^2-v^2)$ (the saddle). At $(u,v)=(0,0)$, $\mathbf{x}_u\times\mathbf{x}_v=(a,b,c)$. Give $a+bi$. $\mathbf{x}_u=(1,0,2u)$, $\mathbf{x}_v=(0,1,-2v)$. At $(0,0)$ the cross product is $(0,0,1)$, so $a=0,b=0$.

q: The Frenet–Serret frame $(T,N,B)$ satisfies $T'=\kappa N$, $N'=-\kappa T+\tau B$, $B'=-\tau N$ for a unit-speed space curve. Which step in proving $B'$ is parallel to $N$ is the correct first step?
text: The Frenet–Serret frame $(T,N,B)$ satisfies $T'=\kappa N$, $N'=-\kappa T+\tau B$, $B'=-\tau N$ for a unit-speed space curve. Which step in proving $B'$ is parallel to $N$ is the correct first step? The clean argument: $|B|=1 \Rightarrow B\cdot B'=0$ (so $B'\perp B$). Differentiating $B\cdot T=0$ gives $B'\cdot T+B\cdot T'=B'\cdot T+\kappa B\cdot N=B'\cdot T=0$. Hence $B'$ is perpendicular to both $B$ and $T$, so $B'\parallel N$, and we define $\tau$ by $B'=-\tau N$. Differentiate unit-length and orthogonality constraints to pin down the direction.

q: The helix $\gamma(t)=(\cos t,\sin t,t)/\sqrt{2}$ is unit-speed. Its curvature $\kappa=|\gamma''|$. Compute $\kappa$ (give an exact decimal or fraction).
text: The helix $\gamma(t)=(\cos t,\sin t,t)/\sqrt{2}$ is unit-speed. Its curvature $\kappa=|\gamma''|$. Compute $\kappa$ (give an exact decimal or fraction). $\gamma'(t)=(-\sin t,\cos t,1)/\sqrt{2}$ has $|\gamma'|=1$ (unit-speed). Differentiating: $\gamma''(t)=(-\cos t,-\sin t,0)/\sqrt{2}$. Then $|\gamma''|=\sqrt{\cos^2t+\sin^2t}/\sqrt{2}=1/\sqrt{2}\approx 0.7071$. For a helix $(a\cos t,a\sin t,bt)$ the curvature formula is $\kappa=a/(a^2+b^2)$; here after arc-length reparametrization $a=b=1/\sqrt{2}$, giving $\kappa=(1/\sqrt{2})/(1/2+1/2)=1/\sqrt{2}$. Differentiate twice and use the helix formula $\kappa = a/(a^2+b^2)$.

q: Which of these surfaces CANNOT be given an atlas of regular parametrizations as a smooth surface in $\mathbb{R}^3$?
text: Which of these surfaces CANNOT be given an atlas of regular parametrizations as a smooth surface in $\mathbb{R}^3$? At the apex of the cone, any attempted parametrization $\mathbf{x}$ has $\mathbf{x}_u\times\mathbf{x}_v=0$, so the regularity condition fails. The cone with its apex removed is a smooth surface, but the full cone (apex included) is not. Look for a singular point where the tangent plane collapses.

q: The first fundamental form $I=E\,du^2+2F\,du\,dv+G\,dv^2$ encodes:
text: The first fundamental form $I=E\,du^2+2F\,du\,dv+G\,dv^2$ encodes: $I$ is the restricted Euclidean inner product on each tangent plane — it determines intrinsic length, angle, and area. Bending information lives in the second fundamental form.

q: For the cylinder $\mathbf{x}(u,v)=(\cos u,\sin u,v)$, compute $E\cdot G-F^2$.
text: For the cylinder $\mathbf{x}(u,v)=(\cos u,\sin u,v)$, compute $E\cdot G-F^2$. $\mathbf{x}_u=(-\sin u,\cos u,0)$, $\mathbf{x}_v=(0,0,1)$, so $E=1$, $G=1$, $F=0$ and $EG-F^2=1$.

q: For the sphere of radius $R$ with $\mathbf{x}(u,v)=(R\sin u\cos v,R\sin u\sin v,R\cos u)$, compute $E$ at any $(u,v)$.
text: For the sphere of radius $R$ with $\mathbf{x}(u,v)=(R\sin u\cos v,R\sin u\sin v,R\cos u)$, compute $E$ at any $(u,v)$. $\mathbf{x}_u=(R\cos u\cos v,R\cos u\sin v,-R\sin u)$ — wait, with $R=1$ we get $E=\mathbf{x}_u\cdot\mathbf{x}_u=\cos^2u+\sin^2u=1$. For general $R$, $E=R^2$; taking $R=1$ gives $1$.

q: Two surfaces have identical first fundamental forms (they are locally isometric). Which conclusion is definitely NOT warranted?
text: Two surfaces have identical first fundamental forms (they are locally isometric). Which conclusion is definitely NOT warranted? Theorema Egregium guarantees $K$ is intrinsic (determined by $I$ alone), and geodesics are also intrinsic. But the second fundamental form and mean curvature $H$ depend on the embedding — the classic example is a cylinder (locally isometric to a plane, $K=0$) yet $H=1/2\neq 0$. Separate intrinsic invariants from those that need the embedding.

q: For the torus $\mathbf{x}(u,v)=((R+r\cos u)\cos v,(R+r\cos u)\sin v,r\sin u)$ with $R=3,r=1$, compute $F=\mathbf{x}_u\cdot\mathbf{x}_v$ at $(u,v)=(0,0)$.
text: For the torus $\mathbf{x}(u,v)=((R+r\cos u)\cos v,(R+r\cos u)\sin v,r\sin u)$ with $R=3,r=1$, compute $F=\mathbf{x}_u\cdot\mathbf{x}_v$ at $(u,v)=(0,0)$. $\mathbf{x}_u=(-r\sin u\cos v,-r\sin u\sin v,r\cos u)$ and $\mathbf{x}_v=(-(R+r\cos u)\sin v,(R+r\cos u)\cos v,0)$. Their dot product is $r\sin u(R+r\cos u)\sin v\cos v - r\sin u(R+r\cos u)\sin v\cos v+0=0$ for all $(u,v)$, confirming $F=0$ everywhere on the torus. The torus parametrization yields orthogonal coordinate curves.

q: For the saddle $\mathbf{x}(u,v)=(u,v,u^2-v^2)$, the second fundamental form coefficients at $(0,0)$ are $e=\mathbf{x}_{uu}\cdot\mathbf{n}$, $f=\mathbf{x}_{uv}\cdot\mathbf{n}$, $g=\mathbf{x}_{vv}\cdot\mathbf{n}$. Compute $eg-f^2$ (which equals $K\cdot(EG-F^2)$).
text: For the saddle $\mathbf{x}(u,v)=(u,v,u^2-v^2)$, the second fundamental form coefficients at $(0,0)$ are $e=\mathbf{x}_{uu}\cdot\mathbf{n}$, $f=\mathbf{x}_{uv}\cdot\mathbf{n}$, $g=\mathbf{x}_{vv}\cdot\mathbf{n}$. Compute $eg-f^2$ (which equals $K\cdot(EG-F^2)$). At $(0,0)$: $\mathbf{n}=(0,0,1)$ (unit normal from $\mathbf{x}_u\times\mathbf{x}_v=(0,0,1)$). $\mathbf{x}_{uu}=(0,0,2)$, $\mathbf{x}_{uv}=(0,0,0)$, $\mathbf{x}_{vv}=(0,0,-2)$. So $e=2$, $f=0$, $g=-2$, giving $eg-f^2=2\cdot(-2)-0=-4$. Since $EG-F^2=1$ here, $K=-4$. Second derivatives of the saddle give opposite-sign principal curvatures.

q: The shape operator $S_p = -dN_p$ on $T_pS$ is:
text: The shape operator $S_p = -dN_p$ on $T_pS$ is: Differentiating $\langle N,\mathbf{x}_u\rangle=\langle N,\mathbf{x}_v\rangle=0$ shows $S_p$ is self-adjoint for $I$, so it diagonalizes over $\mathbb{R}$ with eigenvalues $k_1\ge k_2$ (the principal curvatures) along orthogonal principal directions. Differentiating $\langle N,\mathbf{x}_u\rangle=\langle N,\mathbf{x}_v\rangle=0$ shows $S_p$ is self-adjoint for $I$, so it diagonalizes over $\mathbb{R}$ with eigenvalues $k_1\ge k_2$ (the principal…

q: On the round sphere of radius $R$ the Gauss map is $\mathbf{n}(p)=p/R$, so $S_p=(1/R)\,\mathrm{id}$. Give the Gaussian curvature $K=\det S_p$ when $R=3$.
text: On the round sphere of radius $R$ the Gauss map is $\mathbf{n}(p)=p/R$, so $S_p=(1/R)\,\mathrm{id}$. Give the Gaussian curvature $K=\det S_p$ when $R=3$. Both principal curvatures equal $1/R$, so $K=k_1k_2=1/R^2$. For $R=3$, $K=1/9\approx 0.1111$.

q: For a cylinder of radius $1$, the image of the Gauss map $\mathbf{n}:S\to S^2$ is:
text: For a cylinder of radius $1$, the image of the Gauss map $\mathbf{n}:S\to S^2$ is: On a cylinder the unit normal always lies in the horizontal plane, so $\mathbf{n}$ traces out a great circle. The image has measure zero on $S^2$, consistent with $K\equiv 0$.

q: The Gauss map area-distortion formula says the area of the Gauss map image $\mathbf{n}(R)$ for a region $R\subset S$ equals $\int_R |K|\,dA$ when $K$ does not change sign. For a compact surface where $K>0$ everywhere, which statement follows?
text: The Gauss map area-distortion formula says the area of the Gauss map image $\mathbf{n}(R)$ for a region $R\subset S$ equals $\int_R |K|\,dA$ when $K$ does not change sign. For a compact surface where $K>0$ everywhere, which statement follows? For a convex closed surface ($K>0$), the Gauss map is a local diffeomorphism onto $S^2$. The total area counted with multiplicity is $\int_S K\,dA$. By Gauss–Bonnet this equals $2\pi\chi(S)=4\pi$ for a genus-0 surface, matching the area of $S^2$ itself and confirming the Gauss map is degree 1. Couple Gauss-map degree to Gauss–Bonnet on a convex genus-zero surface.

q: For a surface with principal curvatures $k_1=3$ and $k_2=-1$, compute the mean curvature $H=(k_1+k_2)/2$.
text: For a surface with principal curvatures $k_1=3$ and $k_2=-1$, compute the mean curvature $H=(k_1+k_2)/2$. $H=(k_1+k_2)/2=(3+(-1))/2=2/2=1$. Mean curvature is the average of principal curvatures.

q: Which is the correct logical chain establishing that $K = eg - f^2$ (using second-fundamental-form coefficients) equals the determinant of the shape operator in the $\{\mathbf{x}_u,\mathbf{x}_v\}$ basis?
text: Which is the correct logical chain establishing that $K = eg - f^2$ (using second-fundamental-form coefficients) equals the determinant of the shape operator in the $\{\mathbf{x}_u,\mathbf{x}_v\}$ basis? The shape operator in coordinate basis satisfies $[S_p]=(g_{ij})^{-1}(h_{ij})$ where $h_{ij}$ are the second-fundamental-form coefficients. Its determinant is $\det(g^{-1})\det(h)=(eg-f^2)/(EG-F^2)$. For an orthonormal eigenbasis $K=k_1 k_2=\det(S_p)$, confirming the formula. Write the shape operator as metric-inverse times second fundamental form.

q: Theorema Egregium says Gaussian curvature $K$ is:
text: Theorema Egregium says Gaussian curvature $K$ is: Gauss's remarkable theorem: $K$ is computable from $(E,F,G)$ and their derivatives alone, so it is an isometric invariant — intrinsic.

q: On the sphere of radius $R$, the Gaussian curvature is constant. Compute $K$ when $R=2$.
text: On the sphere of radius $R$, the Gaussian curvature is constant. Compute $K$ when $R=2$. For a round sphere of radius $R$, $K=1/R^2$. With $R=2$ that is $1/4=0.25$.

q: A geodesic triangle on the unit sphere has interior angles $\pi/2,\pi/2,\pi/2$. By Gauss–Bonnet, its area equals the angle excess. What is the area?
text: A geodesic triangle on the unit sphere has interior angles $\pi/2,\pi/2,\pi/2$. By Gauss–Bonnet, its area equals the angle excess. What is the area? Angle sum is $3\pi/2$; excess over $\pi$ is $\pi/2$. On the unit sphere $K=1$, so area $=\int K\,dA=\pi/2\approx 1.5708$.

q: The geodesic equations on a surface (in isothermal coordinates where $E=G=\lambda^2(u,v)$, $F=0$) reduce to a system of ODEs. Which statement correctly identifies the key property that makes great circles geodesics on the sphere?
text: The geodesic equations on a surface (in isothermal coordinates where $E=G=\lambda^2(u,v)$, $F=0$) reduce to a system of ODEs. Which statement correctly identifies the key property that makes great circles geodesics on the sphere? Both are valid: locally, geodesics are characterized by $\kappa_g=0$ (the acceleration $\gamma''$ is normal to $S$), which holds for great circles. Globally, a reflection symmetry whose fixed-point set is a curve forces that curve to be a geodesic, since the geodesic from $p$ to $q$ must be mapped to itself. Both the vanishing geodesic curvature and symmetry arguments work.

q: On the hyperbolic plane with $K\equiv -1$, a geodesic triangle has angle sum $\pi/6$. Compute its area using the Gauss–Bonnet formula $\text{Area}=\pi-(\alpha+\beta+\gamma)$.
text: On the hyperbolic plane with $K\equiv -1$, a geodesic triangle has angle sum $\pi/6$. Compute its area using the Gauss–Bonnet formula $\text{Area}=\pi-(\alpha+\beta+\gamma)$. $\text{Area}=\pi-\pi/6=5\pi/6\approx 2.6180$. Use the angle-defect area formula for $K=-1$.

q: Which counterexample shows that two surfaces with the same Gaussian curvature function need NOT be locally isometric?
text: Which counterexample shows that two surfaces with the same Gaussian curvature function need NOT be locally isometric? Minding's theorem says surfaces of the same CONSTANT $K$ are locally isometric. But a sphere ($K=1$ everywhere) and a (non-round) ellipsoid (variable $K$, all positive) have the same sign of $K$ but are not locally isometric. Choice C is actually true (Minding), and D gives a true isometry example (catenoid–helicoid); choice A correctly gives a counterexample for non-constant curvature. Minding only rescues local isometry when $K$ is constant.

q: The global Gauss–Bonnet theorem states that for a compact oriented surface $S$ without boundary:
text: The global Gauss–Bonnet theorem states that for a compact oriented surface $S$ without boundary: Total Gaussian curvature equals $2\pi$ times the Euler characteristic $\chi(S)=V-E+F=2-2g$. It couples differential geometry ($K$) to topology ($\chi$).

q: For a closed genus-$3$ orientable surface, compute $\int_S K\,dA$.
text: For a closed genus-$3$ orientable surface, compute $\int_S K\,dA$. $\chi=2-2g=2-6=-4$, so $\int_S K\,dA=2\pi\chi=-8\pi\approx -25.1327$.

q: A geodesic triangle on a surface of constant curvature $K=-1$ has interior angles $\alpha+\beta+\gamma=\pi/3$. By local Gauss–Bonnet, what is the area of the triangle?
text: A geodesic triangle on a surface of constant curvature $K=-1$ has interior angles $\alpha+\beta+\gamma=\pi/3$. By local Gauss–Bonnet, what is the area of the triangle? For a geodesic triangle, local Gauss–Bonnet reduces to $(\alpha+\beta+\gamma)-\pi=\int_\tr{}iangle K\,dA$. With $K\equiv -1$ and $\alpha+\beta+\gamma=\pi/3$: $-2\pi/3 = -\mathrm{Area}$, so $\mathrm{Area}=2\pi/3\approx 2.0944$.

q: A compact surface $S$ admits a Riemannian metric with $K>0$ everywhere. What does Gauss–Bonnet immediately imply about the topology of $S$?
text: A compact surface $S$ admits a Riemannian metric with $K>0$ everywhere. What does Gauss–Bonnet immediately imply about the topology of $S$? If $K>0$ everywhere then $\int_S K\,dA>0$, so $2\pi\chi(S)>0$, forcing $\chi(S)>0$. For orientable surfaces $\chi(S)=2-2g$, so $g=0$ (sphere). Non-orientable surfaces with $\chi>0$ are $\mathbb{R}P^2$ ($\chi=1$). Thus $K>0$ rules out genus $\ge 1$. Positive integral of $K$ forces Euler characteristic positive.

q: Use the local Gauss–Bonnet formula for a geodesic polygon. A geodesic quadrilateral on the unit sphere ($K=1$) has interior angles $\pi/2,\pi/2,\pi/2,\pi/2+\varepsilon$ with $\varepsilon=\pi/4$. Compute its area.
text: Use the local Gauss–Bonnet formula for a geodesic polygon. A geodesic quadrilateral on the unit sphere ($K=1$) has interior angles $\pi/2,\pi/2,\pi/2,\pi/2+\varepsilon$ with $\varepsilon=\pi/4$. Compute its area. For a geodesic $n$-gon: Area $= \sum_i\alpha_i - (n-2)\pi$. Here $n=4$, $\sum\alpha_i = 3(\pi/2)+(\pi/2+\pi/4)=2\pi+\pi/4$. Area $=2\pi+\pi/4-2\pi=\pi/4\approx 0.7854$. Area equals angle sum minus $(n-2)\pi$ for a geodesic $n$-gon.

q: Ordering the steps in a proof that the Euler characteristic $\chi(S)$ is a topological invariant via Gauss–Bonnet: which sequence is logically correct?
text: Ordering the steps in a proof that the Euler characteristic $\chi(S)$ is a topological invariant via Gauss–Bonnet: which sequence is logically correct? The standard proof: triangulate, apply local Gauss–Bonnet to each geodesic triangle ($\sum\alpha_i-\pi=\int_{\tr{}iangle_i}K\,dA$), sum over all triangles. The angle contributions at interior vertices telescope to $2\pi V$, and the combinatorial identity $2E=3F$ for a triangulation gives $V-E+F=\chi$, yielding $\int_S K\,dA=2\pi\chi$. Since $\chi$ is a topological invariant (homotopy type), so is this integral. Triangulate, sum local angle defects, collapse to $V-E+F$.

title: 11. Connections to the rest of the notebook
Riemannian metrics
blurb: A smoothly varying inner product on tangent spaces defines length, angle, and volume.

title: Levi-Civita connection and geodesics
blurb: The unique torsion-free metric connection defines covariant differentiation and geodesic equations.

title: Curvature tensor and comparison
blurb: Sectional curvature controls topology and global metric behavior through comparison theorems.

title: Ricci curvature and Einstein manifolds
blurb: The trace of the Riemann tensor measures average sectional curvature, governs geodesic-ball volumes, and drives Einstein's equations.

title: Riemannian volume form and divergence
blurb: The metric produces a canonical volume form \sqrt{\det g}\,dx^1\wedge\cdots\wedge dx^n, giving integration, divergence, and the Laplace-Beltrami operator on (M,g).

q: A Riemannian metric $g$ on a smooth manifold $M$ is:
text: A Riemannian metric $g$ on a smooth manifold $M$ is: A Riemannian metric is a smooth, symmetric, positive-definite inner product on each tangent space. Symmetry alone is not enough; positive-definiteness is what gives lengths and angles.

q: On the round sphere with $ds^2 = d\theta^2 + \sin^2\theta\,d\varphi^2$, compute the squared length of the vector $v = \partial_\varphi$ at the equator $\theta=\pi/2$.
text: On the round sphere with $ds^2 = d\theta^2 + \sin^2\theta\,d\varphi^2$, compute the squared length of the vector $v = \partial_\varphi$ at the equator $\theta=\pi/2$. The metric assigns $g(\partial_\varphi,\partial_\varphi) = \sin^2\theta$. At $\theta=\pi/2$, $\sin^2(\pi/2)=1$.

q: The hyperbolic half-plane metric is $ds^2 = (du^2+dv^2)/v^2$. What is the hyperbolic length of the vertical segment from $(0,1)$ to $(0,e)$?
text: The hyperbolic half-plane metric is $ds^2 = (du^2+dv^2)/v^2$. What is the hyperbolic length of the vertical segment from $(0,1)$ to $(0,e)$? Parametrize $v(t)=t$ for $t\in[1,e]$; the length is $\int_1^e dt/t = \ln e - \ln 1 = 1$.

q: Two Riemannian metrics $g$ and $\tilde g$ on $M$ are conformally equivalent if $\tilde g = e^{2f}g$ for a smooth $f$. Which property is NOT preserved under conformal change?
text: Two Riemannian metrics $g$ and $\tilde g$ on $M$ are conformally equivalent if $\tilde g = e^{2f}g$ for a smooth $f$. Which property is NOT preserved under conformal change? Conformal changes preserve angles (the ratio $g(u,v)/\sqrt{g(u,u)g(v,v)}$ is unchanged) and hence the conformal structure, but they alter lengths and therefore change the Levi-Civita connection and its geodesics. Conformal rescaling keeps angles but not lengths.

q: On the hyperbolic half-plane with $ds^2=(du^2+dv^2)/v^2$, what is $\sqrt{\det g_{ij}}$ at the point $(u,v)=(3,2)$?
text: On the hyperbolic half-plane with $ds^2=(du^2+dv^2)/v^2$, what is $\sqrt{\det g_{ij}}$ at the point $(u,v)=(3,2)$? The metric matrix is $\mathrm{diag}(1/v^2,1/v^2)$, so $\det g_{ij}=v^{-4}$ and $\sqrt{\det g}=v^{-2}=1/4$ at $v=2$. Compute $\sqrt{\det g}$ from the diagonal conformal factor.

q: Which is the correct proof-step order for showing every smooth manifold admits a Riemannian metric?
text: Which is the correct proof-step order for showing every smooth manifold admits a Riemannian metric? The standard existence proof: (1) cover $M$ by coordinate charts, (2) pull back the Euclidean metric on each chart to get local positive-definite tensors $g_\alpha$, (3) choose a partition of unity $\{\rho_\alpha\}$, (4) set $g=\sum_\alpha \rho_\alpha g_\alpha$. Positive-definiteness is preserved by convex combinations with non-negative coefficients summing to 1. Glue local Euclidean metrics with a partition of unity.

q: The Levi-Civita connection is characterized as the unique affine connection that is:
text: The Levi-Civita connection is characterized as the unique affine connection that is: The fundamental theorem of Riemannian geometry: there is a unique torsion-free connection with $\nabla g = 0$. Flatness is not required — curvature can be nonzero.

q: The geodesic equation in local coordinates reads:
text: The geodesic equation in local coordinates reads: A geodesic satisfies $\nabla_{\dot\gamma}\dot\gamma = 0$, which in coordinates is $\ddot x^k + \Gamma^k_{ij}\dot x^i \dot x^j = 0$.

q: For the Euclidean metric $g_{ij}=\delta_{ij}$ on $\mathbb{R}^n$, how many of the Christoffel symbols $\Gamma^k_{ij}$ are nonzero?
text: For the Euclidean metric $g_{ij}=\delta_{ij}$ on $\mathbb{R}^n$, how many of the Christoffel symbols $\Gamma^k_{ij}$ are nonzero? $\Gamma^k_{ij} = \tfrac12 g^{k\ell}(\partial_i g_{j\ell}+\partial_j g_{i\ell}-\partial_\ell g_{ij})$. With $g_{ij}$ constant, every partial vanishes, so all Christoffels are zero.

q: Which is the correct order of steps in the proof that the Levi-Civita connection is unique?
text: Which is the correct order of steps in the proof that the Levi-Civita connection is unique? Uniqueness follows from the Koszul identity $2g(\nabla_X Y,Z)=Xg(Y,Z)+Yg(X,Z)-Zg(X,Y)+g([X,Y],Z)-g([Y,Z],X)+g([Z,X],Y)$, which is determined entirely by $g$ and the bracket. Any two metric-compatible torsion-free connections satisfy this same identity and hence agree. Koszul's formula pins the connection from $g$ and brackets alone.

q: On the round $S^2$ (radius $1$, coordinates $\theta,\varphi$), the only non-zero independent Christoffel symbol components are $\Gamma^\theta_{\varphi\varphi}=-\sin\theta\cos\theta$ and $\Gamma^\varphi_{\theta\varphi}=\cos\theta/\sin\theta$. Evaluate $\Gamma^\theta_{\varphi\varphi}$ at $\theta=\pi/4$. Express your answer to six decimal places.
text: On the round $S^2$ (radius $1$, coordinates $\theta,\varphi$), the only non-zero independent Christoffel symbol components are $\Gamma^\theta_{\varphi\varphi}=-\sin\theta\cos\theta$ and $\Gamma^\varphi_{\theta\varphi}=\cos\theta/\sin\theta$. Evaluate $\Gamma^\theta_{\varphi\varphi}$ at $\theta=\pi/4$. Express your answer to six decimal places. $\Gamma^\theta_{\varphi\varphi}=-\sin(\pi/4)\cos(\pi/4)=-(\frac{\sqrt2}{2})(\frac{\sqrt2}{2})=-\frac12=-0.5$. Substitute $\theta=\pi/4$ into $-\sin\theta\cos\theta$.

q: A geodesic $\gamma$ on a Riemannian manifold is a counterexample to which false claim?
text: A geodesic $\gamma$ on a Riemannian manifold is a counterexample to which false claim? On $S^2$, the longer arc of a great circle between two non-antipodal points is a geodesic (satisfies the ODE) but is NOT the shortest path; it is only a local saddle of the length functional. Geodesics are always locally minimising but can fail to be globally minimising. Think of the long arc of a great circle on the sphere.

q: Bonnet–Myers theorem states that a complete Riemannian $n$-manifold with $\operatorname{Ric}\ge (n-1)\kappa g$ (for $\kappa>0$) is:
text: Bonnet–Myers theorem states that a complete Riemannian $n$-manifold with $\operatorname{Ric}\ge (n-1)\kappa g$ (for $\kappa>0$) is: Uniformly positive Ricci forces geodesics to refocus within distance $\pi/\sqrt{\kappa}$, which makes $M$ compact with bounded diameter and finite $\pi_1$.

q: On the round 2-sphere of radius $1$, the sectional curvature is:
text: On the round 2-sphere of radius $1$, the sectional curvature is: The unit sphere has constant Gaussian (sectional) curvature $K=+1$.

q: For a geodesic triangle on the round unit 2-sphere enclosing area $A$, the Gauss–Bonnet formula gives the angle excess $(\alpha+\beta+\gamma)-\pi = \int K\,dA$. If $K=1$ and $A=\pi/2$, what is $(\alpha+\beta+\gamma)-\pi$?
text: For a geodesic triangle on the round unit 2-sphere enclosing area $A$, the Gauss–Bonnet formula gives the angle excess $(\alpha+\beta+\gamma)-\pi = \int K\,dA$. If $K=1$ and $A=\pi/2$, what is $(\alpha+\beta+\gamma)-\pi$? With $K=1$, the excess equals the area $A=\pi/2 \approx 1.5708$.

q: The Riemann tensor satisfies the first Bianchi identity $R_{ijkl}+R_{jkil}+R_{kijl}=0$. This is a consequence of which property of the Levi-Civita connection?
text:
The Riemann tensor satisfies the first Bianchi identity $R_{ijkl}+R_{jkil}+R_{kijl}=0$. This is a consequence of which property of the Levi-Civita connection? The algebraic (first) Bianchi identity $\mathfrak{S}_{ijk} R_{\ell ijk}=0$ follows directly from the torsion-free condition: expanding $R(X,Y)Z+R(Y,Z)X+R(Z,X)Y$ using $
abla_{[X,Y]}=(
abla_X
abla_Y-
abla_Y
abla_X)$ and the vanishing torsion gives the cyclic sum zero. The cyclic identity comes from vanishing torsion.

q: A geodesic triangle on the hyperbolic plane ($K=-1$) has area $A$. Its angle excess is $(\alpha+\beta+\gamma)-\pi = -A$ (Gauss–Bonnet with $K=-1$). If $\alpha=\pi/6$, $\beta=\pi/6$, $\gamma=\pi/6$, what is the area $A$?
text: A geodesic triangle on the hyperbolic plane ($K=-1$) has area $A$. Its angle excess is $(\alpha+\beta+\gamma)-\pi = -A$ (Gauss–Bonnet with $K=-1$). If $\alpha=\pi/6$, $\beta=\pi/6$, $\gamma=\pi/6$, what is the area $A$? Gauss–Bonnet on the hyperbolic plane ($K=-1$): $\alpha+\beta+\gamma-\pi = \int K\,dA = -A$, so $A = \pi-(\alpha+\beta+\gamma)$. With $\alpha=\beta=\gamma=\pi/6$: $A=\pi-3(\pi/6)=\pi-\pi/2=\pi/2\approx 1.5708$. Area equals $\pi$ minus the angle sum when $K=-1$.

q: Cartan–Hadamard theorem: a complete simply-connected Riemannian manifold with $K\le 0$ everywhere is diffeomorphic to $\mathbb{R}^n$. Which step in the proof is critical?
text: Cartan–Hadamard theorem: a complete simply-connected Riemannian manifold with $K\le 0$ everywhere is diffeomorphic to $\mathbb{R}^n$. Which step in the proof is critical? The key steps: (1) Non-positive curvature implies Jacobi fields along geodesics from $p$ never vanish (no conjugate points), so $d(\exp_p)$ is everywhere non-singular and $\exp_p$ is a local diffeomorphism. (2) Completeness + Hopf–Rinow make $\exp_p$ surjective. (3) Simple-connectedness then shows $\exp_p$ has trivial monodromy, hence is a global diffeomorphism. No conjugate points when $K\le 0$ via Jacobi fields.

q: The Ricci tensor $\operatorname{Ric}(v,v)$ on an $n$-manifold equals:
text: The Ricci tensor $\operatorname{Ric}(v,v)$ on an $n$-manifold equals: Contracting the Riemann tensor yields $\operatorname{Ric}(v,v)=\sum_i \langle R(v,e_i)e_i,v\rangle$ for an orthonormal basis $\{e_i\}$ with $e_1=v/\|v\|$; summing the $(n-1)$ sectional curvatures of planes through $v$ gives exactly $(n-1)$ times their average. Contracting the Riemann tensor yields $\operatorname{Ric}(v,v)=\sum_i \langle R(v,e_i)e_i,v\rangle$ for an orthonormal basis $\{e_i\}$ with $e_1=v/\|v\|$; summing the $(n-1)$ sectional curvatures of…

q: On the round $n$-sphere of radius $1$, every sectional curvature equals $1$. What is the scalar curvature $S$ for $n=3$? (Use $S = n(n-1)\,\overline{K}$ with $\overline{K}$ the average sectional curvature.)
text: On the round $n$-sphere of radius $1$, every sectional curvature equals $1$. What is the scalar curvature $S$ for $n=3$? (Use $S = n(n-1)\,\overline{K}$ with $\overline{K}$ the average sectional curvature.) With $K\equiv 1$ we have $\overline{K}=1$, so $S = n(n-1) = 3\cdot 2 = 6$.

q: An Einstein manifold is a Riemannian manifold satisfying:
text: An Einstein manifold is a Riemannian manifold satisfying: Einstein's condition in pure geometry is $\operatorname{Ric}=\lambda g$: the Ricci tensor is proportional to the metric. Spheres, hyperbolic spaces, and complex projective spaces with their symmetric metrics are all Einstein.

q: The contracted second Bianchi identity $\nabla^j G_{ij}=0$ (where $G_{ij}=\operatorname{Ric}_{ij}-\tfrac12 Sg_{ij}$ is the Einstein tensor) implies which physical conservation law in general relativity?
text:
The contracted second Bianchi identity $
abla^j G_{ij}=0$ (where $G_{ij}=\operatorname{Ric}_{ij}-	frac12 Sg_{ij}$ is the Einstein tensor) implies which physical conservation law in general relativity? The second contracted Bianchi identity gives $
abla^\mu G_{\mu
u}=0$ identically. Einstein's equation $G_{\mu
u}=8\pi T_{\mu
u}$ then forces $
abla^\mu T_{\mu
u}=0$, which encodes local conservation of energy and momentum. Divergence-free Einstein tensor forces divergence-free $T$.

q: On a compact Einstein $n$-manifold with $\operatorname{Ric}=\lambda g$, the scalar curvature is $S=n\lambda$. If $n=4$ and $\lambda=3$, what is $S$?
text: On a compact Einstein $n$-manifold with $\operatorname{Ric}=\lambda g$, the scalar curvature is $S=n\lambda$. If $n=4$ and $\lambda=3$, what is $S$? $S = g^{ij}\operatorname{Ric}_{ij} = g^{ij}(\lambda g_{ij}) = \lambda\,\delta^i{}_i = \lambda n = 3\cdot 4 = 12$. Trace the Einstein condition to get $S=n\lambda$.

q: Which manifold is an Einstein manifold with $\lambda<0$?
text: Which manifold is an Einstein manifold with $\lambda<0$? Hyperbolic space has $\operatorname{Ric}=-(n-1)g$, so $\lambda=-(n-1) 0$, Euclidean space and flat tori have $\lambda=0$. Negative-curvature model space gives negative $\lambda$.

q: On an oriented Riemannian $n$-manifold $(M,g)$, the Riemannian volume form in local coordinates is:
text: On an oriented Riemannian $n$-manifold $(M,g)$, the Riemannian volume form in local coordinates is: The metric-compatible, orientation-compatible top form is $d\mathrm{vol}_g=\sqrt{\det g_{ij}}\,dx^1\wedge\cdots\wedge dx^n$. The factor $\sqrt{\det g}$ is exactly the volume of the parallelepiped spanned by the coordinate vectors in the metric $g$.

q: For the round metric $ds^2 = d\theta^2 + \sin^2\theta\, d\varphi^2$ on $S^2$ with $\theta\in[0,\pi],\,\varphi\in[0,2\pi)$, what is the total surface area $\int_{S^2} d\mathrm{vol}_g$?
text: For the round metric $ds^2 = d\theta^2 + \sin^2\theta\, d\varphi^2$ on $S^2$ with $\theta\in[0,\pi],\,\varphi\in[0,2\pi)$, what is the total surface area $\int_{S^2} d\mathrm{vol}_g$? $\sqrt{\det g}=\sin\theta$, so $\mathrm{Area}=\int_0^{2\pi}\!\!\int_0^\pi \sin\theta\,d\theta\,d\varphi = 2\pi\cdot 2 = 4\pi\approx 12.566$.

q: The Laplace-Beltrami operator on $(M,g)$ acts on a smooth function $f$ as:
text: The Laplace-Beltrami operator on $(M,g)$ acts on a smooth function $f$ as: The metric divergence of $\operatorname{grad}_g f = g^{ij}\partial_j f\,\partial_i$ computed against the volume form $\sqrt{\det g}\,dx^1\wedge\cdots\wedge dx^n$ gives exactly $\Delta_g f = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{\det g}}\partial_i(\sqrt{\det g}\,g^{ij}\partial_j f)$. On Euclidean space ($g_{ij}=\delta_{ij}$) this collapses to the usual Laplacian.

q: The Riemannian volume form $d\mathrm{vol}_g$ is NOT preserved under which transformation?
text: The Riemannian volume form $d\mathrm{vol}_g$ is NOT preserved under which transformation? Under $\tilde g=e^{2f}g$ in $n$ dimensions, $\sqrt{\det\tilde g}=e^{nf}\sqrt{\det g}$, so $d\mathrm{vol}_{\tilde g}=e^{nf}d\mathrm{vol}_g\ne d\mathrm{vol}_g$ whenever $f$ is non-constant. Isometries pull $d\mathrm{vol}_g$ back to itself by definition. Conformal rescaling multiplies the volume by $e^{nf}$.

q: Using the Laplace–Beltrami operator on the round $S^2$ (radius $1$), the first non-zero eigenvalue is $\lambda_1=l(l+1)$ for the lowest $l\ge 1$. What is $\lambda_1$?
text: Using the Laplace–Beltrami operator on the round $S^2$ (radius $1$), the first non-zero eigenvalue is $\lambda_1=l(l+1)$ for the lowest $l\ge 1$. What is $\lambda_1$? Spherical harmonics of degree $l$ satisfy $-\Delta_{S^2}Y_l^m=l(l+1)Y_l^m$. The smallest positive eigenvalue is at $l=1$: $\lambda_1=1\cdot 2=2$. Plug $l=1$ into the spherical-harmonic eigenvalue formula.

q: The divergence theorem on a compact oriented Riemannian manifold $(M,g)$ with boundary states $\int_M \operatorname{div}_g X\,d\mathrm{vol}_g = ?$
text: The divergence theorem on a compact oriented Riemannian manifold $(M,g)$ with boundary states $\int_M \operatorname{div}_g X\,d\mathrm{vol}_g = ?$ Stokes' theorem applied to the $(n-1)$-form $\iota_X d\mathrm{vol}_g$ yields $\int_M \mathcal{L}_X d\mathrm{vol}_g = \int_{\partial M} \iota_X d\mathrm{vol}_g$, which is $\int_M \operatorname{div}_g X\,d\mathrm{vol}_g = \int_{\partial M}\langle X,\nu\rangle\,d\sigma$ where $d\sigma$ is the induced volume form on $\partial M$. Stokes applied to $\iota_X d\mathrm{vol}_g$ gives the boundary flux.

title: Lie groups, Lie algebras, and the exponential map
Lie groups and matrix examples
blurb: Lie groups combine smooth manifolds with group multiplication.

title: Lie algebra as tangent space at the identity
blurb: The Lie algebra $\mathfrak{g} = T_e G$ linearizes a Lie group at the identity; for matrix groups it is cut out by differentiating the defining equation along $A(0)=I$.

title: Lie algebra and exponential map
blurb: The exponential map $\exp:\mathfrak{g}\to G$ integrates each $X\in\mathfrak g$ to the one-parameter subgroup $t\mapsto\exp(tX)$, turning infinitesimal data into group elements.

title: Bracket, commutators, and BCH
blurb: The commutator $[X,Y]=XY-YX$ measures how $\exp(X)$ and $\exp(Y)$ fail to commute; Baker–Campbell–Hausdorff reconstructs the group product as an iterated-bracket series.

title: SO(3), SU(2), and the double cover
blurb: The simply-connected $\mathrm{SU}(2)=S^3$ is the universal cover of $\mathrm{SO}(3)=\mathbb{RP}^3$ with kernel $\{\pm I\}$ — the spinor sign that distinguishes fermions from bosons.

title: Adjoint representation and Killing form
blurb: Conjugation $\mathrm{Ad}_g(X)=gXg^{-1}$ gives the adjoint rep; its differential recovers the bracket, and the Killing form $B(X,Y)=\tr(\ad_X\ad_Y)$ detects semisimplicity.

title: Adjoint action and root data
blurb: Adjoint representations and root systems classify semisimple behavior.

q: A Lie group is best described as:
text: A Lie group is best described as: A Lie group is simultaneously a smooth manifold and a group, where the multiplication $\mu:G\times G\to G$ and inversion $\iota:G\to G$ are smooth maps.

q: What is the real dimension of $\mathrm{SU}(2)$?
text: What is the real dimension of $\mathrm{SU}(2)$? $\mathrm{SU}(n)$ has real dimension $n^2-1$. For $n=2$ this gives $3$, consistent with $\mathrm{SU}(2)\cong S^3$.

q: What is the real dimension of $\mathrm{SO}(n)$ for $n=4$?
text: What is the real dimension of $\mathrm{SO}(n)$ for $n=4$? $\dim\mathrm{SO}(n)=\binom{n}{2}=\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$. For $n=4$: $\binom{4}{2}=6$.

q: Which of the following is NOT a Lie group?
text: Which of the following is NOT a Lie group? The set $M_n(\mathbb{R})$ under addition is a Lie group (a vector group), so that is fine. The intended distractor is none — all four are Lie groups. Revisiting: the real point is to test that $\mathrm{GL}_n$ is open in $M_n$ (hence smooth), $\mathrm{SL}_n$ is a closed submanifold, $M_n$ under addition is $\mathbb{R}^{n^2}$, and $S^1\subset\mathbb{C}^*$ is a Lie group. The only non-example from a standard list would be a discrete group lacking a smooth structure, but all four options here are valid Lie groups. Which option hides a non-Lie structure or dimension issue?

q: What is the real dimension of $\mathrm{U}(n)$ for $n=3$?
text: What is the real dimension of $\mathrm{U}(n)$ for $n=3$? $\mathrm{U}(n)$ has real dimension $n^2$. For $n=3$: $3^2=9$. This follows because a unitary matrix satisfies $U^\dagger U=I$, giving $n^2$ real constraints on $2n^2$ real parameters, leaving $n^2$ free dimensions. Real dimension of $\mathrm{U}(n)$ is $n^2$.

q: To verify $G=\{A\in M_2(\mathbb{R})\mid A^\top A=I,\,\det A=1\}=\mathrm{SO}(2)$ is a Lie group, which is the correct sequence of steps?
text: To verify $G=\{A\in M_2(\mathbb{R})\mid A^\top A=I,\,\det A=1\}=\mathrm{SO}(2)$ is a Lie group, which is the correct sequence of steps? The closed-subgroup theorem (Cartan's theorem) states that any closed subgroup of a Lie group is itself a Lie group. Since $\mathrm{SO}(2)$ is defined by continuous equations inside $\mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{R})$, it is closed, and the theorem applies directly. Closed-subgroup theorem gives smooth structure for free.

q: For a closed matrix Lie group $G\subset\mathrm{GL}_n(\mathbb{R})$, its Lie algebra $\mathfrak{g}=T_eG$ is computed as:
text: For a closed matrix Lie group $G\subset\mathrm{GL}_n(\mathbb{R})$, its Lie algebra $\mathfrak{g}=T_eG$ is computed as: By definition $\mathfrak{g}=T_eG$ is the space of velocity vectors at the identity. Concretely, differentiate a curve through $I$ lying in $G$ and collect all such tangent vectors. This recovers $\mathfrak{sl}_n$ (trace zero), $\mathfrak{so}(n)$ (skew-symmetric), etc.

q: Differentiating $A(t)^\top A(t)=I$ at $t=0$ with $A(0)=I$ gives which equation on $X=A'(0)$? Enter the answer as the value of $(X^\top+X)_{1,1}$ when $X$ satisfies this equation.
text: Differentiating $A(t)^\top A(t)=I$ at $t=0$ with $A(0)=I$ gives which equation on $X=A'(0)$? Enter the answer as the value of $(X^\top+X)_{1,1}$ when $X$ satisfies this equation. Differentiation yields $X^\top+X=0$, i.e. $X$ is skew-symmetric, so every diagonal entry vanishes. In particular $(X^\top+X)_{1,1}=2X_{1,1}=0$.

q: What is the real dimension of $\mathfrak{sl}_3(\mathbb{R})$ (the traceless $3\times 3$ real matrices)?
text: What is the real dimension of $\mathfrak{sl}_3(\mathbb{R})$ (the traceless $3\times 3$ real matrices)? $\mathfrak{sl}_n$ is cut out of $M_n\cong\mathbb{R}^{n^2}$ by the single linear condition $\tr{}X=0$, so $\dim\mathfrak{sl}_n=n^2-1$. For $n=3$: $9-1=8$.

q: A Lie algebra $\mathfrak{g}$ is an abstract vector space with a bracket $[\,,\,]$. Which list of axioms characterises it?
text: A Lie algebra $\mathfrak{g}$ is an abstract vector space with a bracket $[\,,\,]$. Which list of axioms characterises it? A Lie algebra is a vector space with a bilinear bracket satisfying $[X,X]=0$ (antisymmetry in characteristic $\ne 2$) and the Jacobi identity. Lie brackets are never associative; Jacobi is the "weak" associativity replacement. Lie brackets replace associativity with Jacobi.

q: Let $\mathfrak{g}=\mathfrak{so}(3)$ with basis $E_1,E_2,E_3$ satisfying $[E_i,E_j]=\varepsilon_{ijk}E_k$ (the cross product). Compute the second component of $[E_1,E_2]+[E_2,E_3]+[E_3,E_1]$ in the $E_1,E_2,E_3$ basis.
text: Let $\mathfrak{g}=\mathfrak{so}(3)$ with basis $E_1,E_2,E_3$ satisfying $[E_i,E_j]=\varepsilon_{ijk}E_k$ (the cross product). Compute the second component of $[E_1,E_2]+[E_2,E_3]+[E_3,E_1]$ in the $E_1,E_2,E_3$ basis. $[E_1,E_2]=E_3$, $[E_2,E_3]=E_1$, $[E_3,E_1]=E_2$. Sum is $E_1+E_2+E_3$, whose second component (coefficient of $E_2$) is $1$. Notice this is NOT zero — Jacobi $[X,[Y,Z]]+[Y,[Z,X]]+[Z,[X,Y]]=0$ applies to triple brackets, not iterated pairwise brackets. Use $\varepsilon_{ijk}$ directly; don't confuse with Jacobi.

q: Lie's correspondence associates to each real Lie algebra $\mathfrak{g}$ exactly one simply-connected Lie group $\widetilde G$. Which pair illustrates that the non-simply-connected $G$ is NOT uniquely determined by $\mathfrak{g}$?
text: Lie's correspondence associates to each real Lie algebra $\mathfrak{g}$ exactly one simply-connected Lie group $\widetilde G$.
