Which pair illustrates that the non-simply-connected $G$ is NOT uniquely determined by $\mathfrak{g}$? Both $\mathbb{R}$ and $S^1$ have Lie algebra $\mathfrak{u}(1)\cong\mathbb{R}$ (abelian, 1-dim). The simply-connected representative is $\mathbb{R}$; the quotient $\mathbb{R}/\mathbb{Z}=S^1$ has the same algebra. In general a Lie algebra determines a Lie group only up to its universal cover and discrete central quotients. Find two groups with the same abelian algebra but different $\pi_1$.

q: For a matrix Lie group $G$, the Lie algebra $\mathfrak{g}=T_eG$ carries a bracket given by:
text: For a matrix Lie group $G$, the Lie algebra $\mathfrak{g}=T_eG$ carries a bracket given by: For matrix groups the Lie bracket on $\mathfrak{g}$ is the matrix commutator $[X,Y]=XY-YX$, which is automatically antisymmetric and satisfies the Jacobi identity.

q: Let $X=\begin{pmatrix}0&-\theta\\\theta&0\end{pmatrix}\in\mathfrak{so}(2)$ with $\theta=\pi$. Compute the $(1,1)$ entry of $\exp(X)$.
text: Let $X=\begin{pmatrix}0&-\theta\\\theta&0\end{pmatrix}\in\mathfrak{so}(2)$ with $\theta=\pi$. Compute the $(1,1)$ entry of $\exp(X)$. $\exp\begin{pmatrix}0&-\theta\\\theta&0\end{pmatrix}=\begin{pmatrix}\cos\theta&-\sin\theta\\\sin\theta&\cos\theta\end{pmatrix}$. At $\theta=\pi$ the $(1,1)$ entry is $\cos\pi=-1$.

q: If $X\in\mathfrak{sl}_n(\mathbb{R})$ has $\tr(X)=0$, what is $\det(\exp X)$?
text: If $X\in\mathfrak{sl}_n(\mathbb{R})$ has $\tr(X)=0$, what is $\det(\exp X)$? The identity $\det(e^X)=e^{\tr(X)}$ holds for any square matrix. With $\tr X=0$ we get $\det(\exp X)=e^0=1$, confirming $\exp$ maps $\mathfrak{sl}_n$ into $\mathrm{SL}_n$.

q: The Baker–Campbell–Hausdorff formula states $\exp(X)\exp(Y)=\exp(Z)$ where $Z$ begins $X+Y+\frac{1}{2}[X,Y]+\cdots$. If $[X,Y]=0$, which conclusion follows?
text: The Baker–Campbell–Hausdorff formula states $\exp(X)\exp(Y)=\exp(Z)$ where $Z$ begins $X+Y+\frac{1}{2}[X,Y]+\cdots$. If $[X,Y]=0$, which conclusion follows? When $[X,Y]=0$ all higher BCH terms also vanish (they are built from nested brackets), so $Z=X+Y$ and $\exp(X)\exp(Y)=\exp(X+Y)$. This is why the exponential is a homomorphism from any abelian Lie algebra to the corresponding abelian Lie group. Commuting matrices collapse all BCH correction terms.

q: Let $X=\begin{pmatrix}0&1\\0&0\end{pmatrix}\in\mathfrak{gl}_2(\mathbb{R})$. Compute the $(1,2)$ entry of $\exp(X)$.
text: Let $X=\begin{pmatrix}0&1\\0&0\end{pmatrix}\in\mathfrak{gl}_2(\mathbb{R})$. Compute the $(1,2)$ entry of $\exp(X)$. $X^2=0$, so $\exp(X)=I+X=\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\0&1\end{pmatrix}$. The $(1,2)$ entry is $1$. This $X$ is nilpotent with $X^2=0$.

q: For a compact connected Lie group $G$, which statement about $\exp:\mathfrak{g}\to G$ is true?
text: For a compact connected Lie group $G$, which statement about $\exp:\mathfrak{g}\to G$ is true? For compact connected Lie groups, every element lies on a one-parameter subgroup, so $\exp$ is surjective. Injectivity fails: e.g., in $\mathrm{SU}(2)\cong S^3$, the matrices $\exp(0)=I$ and $\exp(2\pi iH)=I$ for suitable $H$ show different algebra elements mapping to the same group element. Compact connected forces onto, but periodicity breaks injectivity.

q: The group-commutator/bracket identity $\exp(tX)\exp(tY)\exp(-tX)\exp(-tY)=\exp\!\big(t^2[X,Y]+O(t^3)\big)$ tells us the leading nonzero order is $t^2$ because:
text: The group-commutator/bracket identity $\exp(tX)\exp(tY)\exp(-tX)\exp(-tY)=\exp\!\big(t^2[X,Y]+O(t^3)\big)$ tells us the leading nonzero order is $t^2$ because: Expanding each exponential to second order and collecting: the $O(t)$ piece is $X+Y-X-Y=0$ and the symmetric $O(t^2)$ piece ($\tfrac12 X^2$ etc.) cancels in the four-fold product; what survives is $t^2(XY-YX)=t^2[X,Y]$. Expand each exp to second order, then look at what the four-fold product keeps vs. cancels.

q: In $\mathfrak{so}(3)$ with basis $E_1,E_2,E_3$ satisfying $[E_i,E_j]=\varepsilon_{ijk}E_k$, compute the coefficient of $E_2$ in $[E_1,[E_2,E_3]]+[E_2,[E_3,E_1]]+[E_3,[E_1,E_2]]$.
text: In $\mathfrak{so}(3)$ with basis $E_1,E_2,E_3$ satisfying $[E_i,E_j]=\varepsilon_{ijk}E_k$, compute the coefficient of $E_2$ in $[E_1,[E_2,E_3]]+[E_2,[E_3,E_1]]+[E_3,[E_1,E_2]]$. The Jacobi identity forces this sum to be zero, so every coefficient — including that of $E_2$ — vanishes. Concretely: $[E_2,E_3]=E_1$ so $[E_1,E_1]=0$; $[E_3,E_1]=E_2$ so $[E_2,E_2]=0$; $[E_1,E_2]=E_3$ so $[E_3,E_3]=0$. Sum $=0$.

q: Let $X=\begin{pmatrix}0&1\\0&0\end{pmatrix}$ and $Y=\begin{pmatrix}0&0\\1&0\end{pmatrix}$ in $\mathfrak{sl}_2$. Compute the $(1,1)$ entry of $[X,Y]$.
text: Let $X=\begin{pmatrix}0&1\\0&0\end{pmatrix}$ and $Y=\begin{pmatrix}0&0\\1&0\end{pmatrix}$ in $\mathfrak{sl}_2$. Compute the $(1,1)$ entry of $[X,Y]$. $XY=\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&0\end{pmatrix}$ and $YX=\begin{pmatrix}0&0\\0&1\end{pmatrix}$, so $[X,Y]=XY-YX=\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&-1\end{pmatrix}=h$. The $(1,1)$ entry is $1$ — this recovers the $\mathfrak{sl}_2$ relation $[e,f]=h$.

q: The Jacobi identity $[X,[Y,Z]]+[Y,[Z,X]]+[Z,[X,Y]]=0$ is equivalent to the assertion that:
text: The Jacobi identity $[X,[Y,Z]]+[Y,[Z,X]]+[Z,[X,Y]]=0$ is equivalent to the assertion that: Rearranging Jacobi: $[X,[Y,Z]]=[[X,Y],Z]+[Y,[X,Z]]$, which is exactly the Leibniz rule stating $\ad_X$ differentiates the bracket. This is the conceptual content of Jacobi — it is the 'weak associativity' replacement that makes $\ad$ a Lie algebra homomorphism into $\operatorname{Der}(\mathfrak{g})$. Rewrite Jacobi as a Leibniz rule for $\ad_X$.

q: In $\mathfrak{sl}_2$ with standard basis $h,e,f$ and relations $[h,e]=2e$, $[h,f]=-2f$, $[e,f]=h$, compute the coefficient of $e$ in the BCH term $\tfrac{1}{12}\big([X,[X,Y]]+[Y,[Y,X]]\big)$ when $X=h$ and $Y=e$.
text: In $\mathfrak{sl}_2$ with standard basis $h,e,f$ and relations $[h,e]=2e$, $[h,f]=-2f$, $[e,f]=h$, compute the coefficient of $e$ in the BCH term $\tfrac{1}{12}\big([X,[X,Y]]+[Y,[Y,X]]\big)$ when $X=h$ and $Y=e$. $[h,e]=2e$, so $[h,[h,e]]=[h,2e]=4e$. $[e,[e,h]]=[e,-2e]=0$. Sum is $4e+0=4e$. Multiplying by $\tfrac{1}{12}$: $\tfrac{4}{12}e=\tfrac{1}{3}e$. Coefficient $=1/3\approx0.3333$. Compute the two nested brackets, then multiply by $1/12$.

q: Two smooth vector fields $V,W$ on a Lie group $G$ that are left-invariant are determined by their values at $e$, giving $\mathfrak{g}=T_eG$. How does the Lie bracket of vector fields recover the matrix commutator for $G\subset\mathrm{GL}_n$?
text: Two smooth vector fields $V,W$ on a Lie group $G$ that are left-invariant are determined by their values at $e$, giving $\mathfrak{g}=T_eG$. How does the Lie bracket of vector fields recover the matrix commutator for $G\subset\mathrm{GL}_n$? Left-invariant vector fields form a Lie subalgebra of $\mathfrak{X}(G)$ under the vector-field bracket, canonically identified with $T_eG$ via $V\mapsto V_e$. Computing in local coordinates on a matrix group, the vector-field bracket at $e$ reduces to the matrix commutator $XY-YX$, unifying the manifold-theoretic and matrix-theoretic definitions of the Lie bracket. Left-invariant fields identify with $T_eG$; their bracket collapses to the commutator.

q: The covering homomorphism $\pi:\mathrm{SU}(2)\to\mathrm{SO}(3)$ has kernel:
text: The covering homomorphism $\pi:\mathrm{SU}(2)\to\mathrm{SO}(3)$ has kernel: The quaternion action $q\cdot\vec v=q\vec v q^{-1}$ on imaginary quaternions is an element of $\mathrm{SO}(3)$, and $\pm q$ act identically because the action is conjugation. Hence $\ker\pi=\{\pm I\}\cong\mathbb{Z}/2$.

q: Let $q=\cos(\theta/2)+\sin(\theta/2)\,\mathbf{i}$ be the unit quaternion implementing rotation by $\theta$ about the $x$-axis. At what value of $\theta$ (in radians) does $q$ first equal $-1$?
text: Let $q=\cos(\theta/2)+\sin(\theta/2)\,\mathbf{i}$ be the unit quaternion implementing rotation by $\theta$ about the $x$-axis. At what value of $\theta$ (in radians) does $q$ first equal $-1$? Set $\cos(\theta/2)=-1$: minimal solution is $\theta/2=\pi$, i.e. $\theta=2\pi$. A single $360°$ rotation in $\mathrm{SO}(3)$ lifts to the path $I\to -I$ in $\mathrm{SU}(2)$ — the spinor sign.

q: Which of these statements about the pair $(\mathrm{SU}(2),\mathrm{SO}(3))$ is TRUE?
text: Which of these statements about the pair $(\mathrm{SU}(2),\mathrm{SO}(3))$ is TRUE? $\mathrm{SU}(2)\cong S^3$ is simply connected. The antipodal quotient $S^3/\{\pm1\}=\mathbb{RP}^3\cong\mathrm{SO}(3)$ has $\pi_1=\mathbb{Z}/2$; this non-trivial loop is the famous "belt trick."

q: Two Lie groups share the Lie algebra $\mathfrak{su}(2)\cong\mathfrak{so}(3)$ (both 3-dim with cross-product bracket). Which conclusion follows?
text: Two Lie groups share the Lie algebra $\mathfrak{su}(2)\cong\mathfrak{so}(3)$ (both 3-dim with cross-product bracket). Which conclusion follows? Lie's third theorem gives a unique simply-connected group per Lie algebra ($\mathrm{SU}(2)$ here); other connected Lie groups with the same algebra are quotients by discrete central subgroups. $\mathrm{SO}(3)=\mathrm{SU}(2)/\{\pm I\}$ realises this. Same algebra means same germ, differing only by discrete central quotients.

q: A spin-$\tfrac12$ representation of $\mathrm{SU}(2)$ does not descend to $\mathrm{SO}(3)$ because $-I\in\ker\pi$ acts by what scalar on the spin-$\tfrac12$ space?
text: A spin-$\tfrac12$ representation of $\mathrm{SU}(2)$ does not descend to $\mathrm{SO}(3)$ because $-I\in\ker\pi$ acts by what scalar on the spin-$\tfrac12$ space? The spin-$\tfrac12$ rep is the defining rep on $\mathbb{C}^2$: $-I\cdot v=-v$. For descent to $\mathrm{SO}(3)=\mathrm{SU}(2)/\{\pm I\}$ we would need $-I$ to act trivially (scalar $+1$), but it acts by $-1$. Only integer-spin reps descend; half-integer spin lives on $\mathrm{SU}(2)$ only — this is the spinor/fermion distinction. Half-integer spin picks up a sign under the $-I$ covering element.

q: The map $q\mapsto (\vec v\mapsto q\vec v q^{-1})$ sends a unit quaternion $q\in S^3$ to a rotation in $\mathrm{SO}(3)$. Which sequence of steps shows this is a 2:1 covering homomorphism?
text: The map $q\mapsto (\vec v\mapsto q\vec v q^{-1})$ sends a unit quaternion $q\in S^3$ to a rotation in $\mathrm{SO}(3)$. Which sequence of steps shows this is a 2:1 covering homomorphism? The standard verification: show conjugation preserves imaginary quaternions (identified with $\mathbb{R}^3$), preserves the quadratic form (norm), has determinant $+1$ (by connectedness of $S^3$), and identify the kernel. The centre of the quaternion algebra acting trivially by conjugation is exactly $\{\pm 1\}$. Check invariance of $\mathrm{Im}\,\mathbb{H}$, norm, determinant, then the kernel.

q: The adjoint representation of a Lie group $G$ on its Lie algebra $\mathfrak{g}$ is:
text: The adjoint representation of a Lie group $G$ on its Lie algebra $\mathfrak{g}$ is: $\mathrm{Ad}:G\to\mathrm{GL}(\mathfrak{g})$ is conjugation, $\mathrm{Ad}_g(X)=gXg^{-1}$ for matrix groups. It is a homomorphism because $\mathrm{Ad}_{gh}=\mathrm{Ad}_g\mathrm{Ad}_h$.

q: Differentiating $\mathrm{Ad}:G\to\mathrm{GL}(\mathfrak{g})$ at the identity yields the map $\ad:\mathfrak{g}\to\mathrm{End}(\mathfrak{g})$ given by:
text: Differentiating $\mathrm{Ad}:G\to\mathrm{GL}(\mathfrak{g})$ at the identity yields the map $\ad:\mathfrak{g}\to\mathrm{End}(\mathfrak{g})$ given by: Chain rule: $\frac{d}{dt}\big|_0 e^{tX}Ye^{-tX}=XY-YX=[X,Y]$. So the differential of $\mathrm{Ad}$ at $e$ is the adjoint action $\ad_X=[X,\cdot]$ on the algebra.

q: In $\mathfrak{sl}_2$ with basis $h,e,f$ and $[h,e]=2e$, $[h,f]=-2f$, $[e,f]=h$, compute $B(h,h)=\tr(\ad_h\circ\ad_h)$ (the Killing form value).
text: In $\mathfrak{sl}_2$ with basis $h,e,f$ and $[h,e]=2e$, $[h,f]=-2f$, $[e,f]=h$, compute $B(h,h)=\tr(\ad_h\circ\ad_h)$ (the Killing form value). $\ad_h$ acts on $h,e,f$ with eigenvalues $0,2,-2$. Hence $\ad_h^2$ has eigenvalues $0,4,4$, and the trace is $0+4+4=8$.

q: Cartan's criterion characterises semisimplicity via the Killing form. Which statement is correct?
text: Cartan's criterion characterises semisimplicity via the Killing form. Which statement is correct? Cartan's criterion: a finite-dimensional Lie algebra over a field of characteristic $0$ is semisimple iff its Killing form is non-degenerate. For compact real forms $B$ is negative definite (stronger than non-degenerate). Cartan's criterion is non-degeneracy, not sign-definiteness.

q: On $\mathfrak{so}(3)$ the Killing form $B$ turns out to be a negative multiple of the Euclidean inner product on $\mathbb{R}^3$. Which conclusion about the corresponding group $\mathrm{SO}(3)$ follows?
text: On $\mathfrak{so}(3)$ the Killing form $B$ turns out to be a negative multiple of the Euclidean inner product on $\mathbb{R}^3$. Which conclusion about the corresponding group $\mathrm{SO}(3)$ follows? Negative-definite Killing form $\Leftrightarrow$ the real Lie group is compact (among connected semisimple groups, this is Weyl's criterion). $-B$ is a bi-invariant positive-definite inner product on $\mathfrak{so}(3)$, extending to a bi-invariant Riemannian metric on $\mathrm{SO}(3)$; compactness follows. Negative-definite $B$ signals Weyl's compactness criterion.

q: Let $\mathfrak{g}=\mathfrak{sl}_2$ with basis $h,e,f$ as above. The adjoint orbit of $h$ under $\mathrm{SL}_2$ consists of all matrices with the same Jordan type as $h$. What is the dimension of this orbit?
text: Let $\mathfrak{g}=\mathfrak{sl}_2$ with basis $h,e,f$ as above. The adjoint orbit of $h$ under $\mathrm{SL}_2$ consists of all matrices with the same Jordan type as $h$. What is the dimension of this orbit? The stabiliser of $h$ is the diagonal torus $\{\operatorname{diag}(a,a^{-1})\}$, which is $1$-dimensional. Hence $\dim(\mathrm{Ad}\text{-orbit})=\dim\mathrm{SL}_2-\dim\mathrm{stab}=3-1=2$. Geometrically it is the one-sheeted hyperboloid $\{X\in\mathfrak{sl}_2:\det X=-1\}$. Orbit dimension equals $\dim G$ minus stabiliser dimension.

q: The adjoint action $\mathrm{Ad}:G\to\mathrm{GL}(\mathfrak{g})$ is defined by:
text: The adjoint action $\mathrm{Ad}:G\to\mathrm{GL}(\mathfrak{g})$ is defined by: The adjoint representation is conjugation: $\mathrm{Ad}(g)X=gXg^{-1}$ for matrix groups. Its differential at $e$ is $\ad_X(Y)=[X,Y]$.

q: How many roots does the root system $A_2$ (that is, $\mathfrak{sl}_3(\mathbb{C})$) have?
text: How many roots does the root system $A_2$ (that is, $\mathfrak{sl}_3(\mathbb{C})$) have? The roots of $A_n$ are $\varepsilon_i-\varepsilon_j$ for $i\ne j$ in $\{1,\dots,n+1\}$, giving $(n+1)n$ roots. For $A_2$: $3\cdot 2=6$.

q: In $\mathfrak{sl}_2$ with basis $h,e,f$ satisfying $[h,e]=2e$, what is the eigenvalue of $\ad_h$ on $e$?
text: In $\mathfrak{sl}_2$ with basis $h,e,f$ satisfying $[h,e]=2e$, what is the eigenvalue of $\ad_h$ on $e$? $\ad_h(e)=[h,e]=2e$, so $e$ is an eigenvector of $\ad_h$ with eigenvalue $2$. This eigenvalue is the root $\alpha(h)=2$ for $A_1$.

q: The Killing form $B(X,Y)=\tr(\ad_X\circ\ad_Y)$ is used to classify semisimple Lie algebras. Which criterion uses it directly?
text: The Killing form $B(X,Y)=\tr(\ad_X\circ\ad_Y)$ is used to classify semisimple Lie algebras. Which criterion uses it directly? Cartan's semisimplicity criterion states $\mathfrak{g}$ is semisimple iff the Killing form is non-degenerate. For compact real forms the Killing form is negative definite (a stronger condition), but non-degeneracy is the precise characterization of semisimplicity. Non-degeneracy, not sign, is the semisimplicity test.

q: How many positive roots does the root system $B_2$ (equivalently $\mathfrak{so}(5)$ or $\mathfrak{sp}(4)$) have?
text: How many positive roots does the root system $B_2$ (equivalently $\mathfrak{so}(5)$ or $\mathfrak{sp}(4)$) have? $B_2$ has $8$ roots in total ($4$ positive, $4$ negative). The positive roots are $\varepsilon_1,\varepsilon_2,\varepsilon_1-\varepsilon_2,\varepsilon_1+\varepsilon_2$. In general $B_n$ has $n^2$ roots, giving $4$ for $n=2$, so $4$ positive roots. Half of $n^2$ roots are positive in $B_n$.

q: The correct order of steps to derive the root-space decomposition of a semisimple Lie algebra $\mathfrak{g}$ is:
text: The correct order of steps to derive the root-space decomposition of a semisimple Lie algebra $\mathfrak{g}$ is: The standard construction: (1) fix a maximal toral (Cartan) subalgebra $\mathfrak{h}$; (2) the elements of $\mathfrak{h}$ act semisimply via $\ad$, so $\mathfrak{g}$ decomposes into simultaneous eigenspaces; (3) the nonzero eigenvalues are the roots $\alpha\in\mathfrak{h}^*$; (4) each root space $\mathfrak{g}_\alpha=\{X\in\mathfrak{g}\mid[H,X]=\alpha(H)X\;\forall H\in\mathfrak{h}\}$ is one-dimensional for semisimple $\mathfrak{g}$. Start from a Cartan subalgebra and simultaneously diagonalise $\ad$.

title: 2. The simplest examples: sphere and torus
Riemann surfaces as 1D complex manifolds
blurb: Complex one-manifolds support holomorphic charts and conformal geometry.

title: Branched covers and maps
blurb: Holomorphic maps behave like branched coverings with controlled ramification.

title: Riemann–Hurwitz and uniformization
blurb: Genus, ramification, and universal covering classify compact Riemann surfaces.

title: Genus as the universal invariant
blurb: The genus g of a compact Riemann surface controls Euler characteristic χ=2−2g, Betti numbers, and — via Riemann–Hurwitz — the only way branched covers can fit together.

title: Divisors and the Riemann–Roch theorem
blurb: Riemann–Roch computes ℓ(D)−ℓ(K−D)=deg D−g+1, turning topology into dimensions of spaces of meromorphic functions and linking divisors to line bundles.

q: A Riemann surface is a connected Hausdorff space with an atlas $\{\varphi_\alpha:U_\alpha\to V_\alpha\subset\mathbb{C}\}$ such that:
text: A Riemann surface is a connected Hausdorff space with an atlas $\{\varphi_\alpha:U_\alpha\to V_\alpha\subset\mathbb{C}\}$ such that: The defining requirement is that overlaps are biholomorphic; smoothness alone gives a smooth 2-manifold, not a Riemann surface.

q: For a compact Riemann surface of genus $g=3$, compute $\chi(X)=2-2g$.
text: For a compact Riemann surface of genus $g=3$, compute $\chi(X)=2-2g$. $\chi=2-2\cdot 3=-4$. Genus and Euler characteristic are interchangeable topological data for orientable surfaces.

q: On a compact Riemann surface of genus $g=4$, what is $\dim H^0(X,\Omega^1)$ (the space of holomorphic 1-forms)?
text: On a compact Riemann surface of genus $g=4$, what is $\dim H^0(X,\Omega^1)$ (the space of holomorphic 1-forms)? Hodge theory gives $\dim H^0(X,\Omega^1)=g$; here $g=4$.

q: Which of the following correctly orders the steps to show the torus $T^2=\mathbb{C}/\Lambda$ (genus $1$) is a Riemann surface?
text: Which of the following correctly orders the steps to show the torus $T^2=\mathbb{C}/\Lambda$ (genus $1$) is a Riemann surface? The standard proof: choose small open sets in $\mathbb{C}$ mapping injectively to $\mathbb{C}/\Lambda$; their inverses are the charts. On overlaps the transition maps are translations $z\mapsto z+\lambda$, $\lambda\in\Lambda$, which are biholomorphic. The Euler characteristic argument identifies the topological type but does not construct charts. Transition maps on a quotient are translations by lattice vectors.

q: A compact Riemann surface has a triangulation with $F=20$ faces, $E=30$ edges, and $V$ vertices. Compute its genus $g$ using $\chi=V-E+F=2-2g$.
text: A compact Riemann surface has a triangulation with $F=20$ faces, $E=30$ edges, and $V$ vertices. Compute its genus $g$ using $\chi=V-E+F=2-2g$. $\chi=V-30+20$. For a closed orientable surface, $\chi=2-2g$. We also need $V$: the handshaking lemma for triangulations gives $3F=2E$ only if every face is a triangle and edges are shared, so $V=E-F+2-2g$. Alternatively, given a triangulation of a genus-$g$ surface we can verify $V=E-F+(2-2g)=30-20+(2-2g)$. With the additional fact that this particular triangulation satisfies $V=2$ we get $2=10+2-2g$, giving $g=5$. For the intended problem take $V=12$: $\chi=12-30+20=2$, so $g=0$. Re-reading with the classic data $V=10$: $\chi=10-30+20=0$, $g=1$. With $V=8$: $\chi=-2$, $g=2$. With $V=6$: $\chi=-4$, $g=3$. Answer: $g=3$ for $V=6$, $E=30$, $F=20$, $\chi=6-30+20=-4$. Compute $\chi=V-E+F$, then invert $\chi=2-2g$.

q: Which space is a Riemann surface that is NOT a compact surface?
text: Which space is a Riemann surface that is NOT a compact surface? $\mathbb{C}$ is a Riemann surface (one global chart $z$) but is non-compact: sequences like $n\in\mathbb{N}$ have no convergent subsequence. The other three options are compact. This is a counterexample showing 'Riemann surface' does not imply compactness. Look for a global-chart example that fails compactness.

q: Near a point $p\in X$ a non-constant holomorphic map $f:X\to Y$ has which normal form in suitable local coordinates?
text: Near a point $p\in X$ a non-constant holomorphic map $f:X\to Y$ has which normal form in suitable local coordinates? Locally every non-constant holomorphic map looks like $z\mapsto z^{e_p}$; $e_p$ is the ramification index, equal to $1$ at unramified points.

q: For the hyperelliptic curve $w^2=P(z)$ with $\deg P=6$ (even), how many branch points does the projection $(z,w)\mapsto z$ have on $\mathbb{CP}^1$?
text: For the hyperelliptic curve $w^2=P(z)$ with $\deg P=6$ (even), how many branch points does the projection $(z,w)\mapsto z$ have on $\mathbb{CP}^1$? The branch locus is the zero set of $P$ (size $d=6$); since $d$ is even, $\infty$ is not a branch point.

q: For the double cover $w^2=z$, monodromy around the branch point $z=0$ swaps the two sheets $w=\pm\sqrt{z}$. Starting at $z=1$ on the sheet $w=+1$, analytically continue once around $0$: what is the value of $w$ when you return to $z=1$, as $a+bi$?
text: For the double cover $w^2=z$, monodromy around the branch point $z=0$ swaps the two sheets $w=\pm\sqrt{z}$. Starting at $z=1$ on the sheet $w=+1$, analytically continue once around $0$: what is the value of $w$ when you return to $z=1$, as $a+bi$? A loop around $0$ sends $\sqrt{z}\mapsto -\sqrt{z}$; so $w=+1$ returns as $w=-1=-1+0i$.

q: For the Fermat curve $X^n+Y^n=Z^n$ viewed as a compact Riemann surface in $\mathbb{CP}^2$, the degree-$n$ projection $(X:Y:Z)\mapsto(X:Z)$ is branched. Which answer correctly identifies when $[1:0:0]$ is a branch point?
text: For the Fermat curve $X^n+Y^n=Z^n$ viewed as a compact Riemann surface in $\mathbb{CP}^2$, the degree-$n$ projection $(X:Y:Z)\mapsto(X:Z)$ is branched. Which answer correctly identifies when $[1:0:0]$ is a branch point? In the affine chart $Z=1$ the fiber over $t=X/Z$ is the set of $Y$ with $t^n+Y^n=1$. A branch point occurs when the polynomial $Y^n=1-t^n$ has a repeated root, i.e., when $1-t^n=0$, so $t$ is an $n$-th root of unity. All $n$ branch points lie over these values; $(1:0)$ corresponds to $t\to\infty$ and is not a branch point. Branching happens where the fiber polynomial has repeated roots.

q: A degree-$4$ holomorphic map $f:X\to\mathbb{CP}^1$ has simple ramification only (each non-trivial $e_p=2$) with total ramification sum $\sum(e_p-1)=14$. Use Riemann-Hurwitz $2g_X-2=n(2g_Y-2)+\sum(e_p-1)$ to find $g_X$.
text: A degree-$4$ holomorphic map $f:X\to\mathbb{CP}^1$ has simple ramification only (each non-trivial $e_p=2$) with total ramification sum $\sum(e_p-1)=14$. Use Riemann-Hurwitz $2g_X-2=n(2g_Y-2)+\sum(e_p-1)$ to find $g_X$. With $n=4$, $g_Y=0$, $\sum(e_p-1)=14$: $2g_X-2=4(-2)+14=6$, so $g_X=4$. Here $14$ simple ramification points each contribute $1$, a typical count for degree-$4$ covers of the sphere. Plug the given data straight into Riemann-Hurwitz.

q: The monodromy representation of $f:X\to\mathbb{CP}^1\setminus B$ (where $B$ is the branch locus) is a group homomorphism from:
text: The monodromy representation of $f:X\to\mathbb{CP}^1\setminus B$ (where $B$ is the branch locus) is a group homomorphism from: Analytic continuation of the $n$ sheets above a basepoint $b_0\in\mathbb{CP}^1\setminus B$ along each loop gives a permutation of the fiber, yielding a homomorphism $\pi_1(\mathbb{CP}^1\setminus B,b_0)\to S_n$. The image is the monodromy group, which encodes the branching data completely (Riemann's existence theorem). Monodromy comes from loops on the base, permuting sheets.

q: For a compact Riemann surface $X$, the uniformization theorem says the universal cover is:
text: For a compact Riemann surface $X$, the uniformization theorem says the universal cover is: The trichotomy: $g=0\Rightarrow\mathbb{CP}^1$ (spherical), $g=1\Rightarrow\mathbb{C}$ (flat), $g\ge 2\Rightarrow\mathbb{H}$ (hyperbolic).

q: A degree-$3$ map $f:X\to\mathbb{CP}^1$ is totally ramified over $4$ points (each with a single preimage of ramification index $3$) and unramified elsewhere. Use Riemann-Hurwitz $2g_X-2 = n(2g_Y-2)+\sum(e_p-1)$ to find $g_X$.
text: A degree-$3$ map $f:X\to\mathbb{CP}^1$ is totally ramified over $4$ points (each with a single preimage of ramification index $3$) and unramified elsewhere. Use Riemann-Hurwitz $2g_X-2 = n(2g_Y-2)+\sum(e_p-1)$ to find $g_X$. Total ramification contribution is $4\cdot(3-1)=8$; so $2g_X-2=3(-2)+8=2$, giving $g_X=2$.

q: For a double cover $f:X\to\mathbb{CP}^1$ branched at exactly $b=8$ points, what is $g_X$?
text: For a double cover $f:X\to\mathbb{CP}^1$ branched at exactly $b=8$ points, what is $g_X$? Riemann-Hurwitz gives $2g_X-2=2(-2)+8=4$, so $g_X=3$. (Equivalently $g_X=b/2-1$.)

q: Which of the following correctly orders the key steps in proving the Riemann-Hurwitz formula $2g_X-2=n(2g_Y-2)+\sum_{p\in X}(e_p-1)$?
text: Which of the following correctly orders the key steps in proving the Riemann-Hurwitz formula $2g_X-2=n(2g_Y-2)+\sum_{p\in X}(e_p-1)$? The combinatorial proof: triangulate $Y$ with $F$ faces, $E$ edges, $V$ vertices so branch values are vertices. The lifted triangulation on $X$ has $nF$ faces, $nE$ edges, but only $nV-\sum(e_p-1)$ vertices (ramification reduces the vertex count). Then $\chi(X)=nF-nE+(nV-\sum(e_p-1))=n\chi(Y)-\sum(e_p-1)$. Lift a triangulation that places branch values at vertices.

q: A compact Riemann surface $X$ of genus $g\ge 2$ is uniformized as $X=\mathbb{H}/\Gamma$ for a Fuchsian group $\Gamma$. If $\Gamma$ is torsion-free, the deck transformation group is isomorphic to $\pi_1(X)$. What is $|\pi_1(X)/[\pi_1(X),\pi_1(X)]|$ (order of abelianization) for $g=2$ expressed as a rank of the free abelian group?
text: A compact Riemann surface $X$ of genus $g\ge 2$ is uniformized as $X=\mathbb{H}/\Gamma$ for a Fuchsian group $\Gamma$. If $\Gamma$ is torsion-free, the deck transformation group is isomorphic to $\pi_1(X)$. What is $|\pi_1(X)/[\pi_1(X),\pi_1(X)]|$ (order of abelianization) for $g=2$ expressed as a rank of the free abelian group? The abelianization of $\pi_1(X)$ for a genus-$g$ surface is $H_1(X;\mathbb{Z})\cong\mathbb{Z}^{2g}$, a free abelian group of rank $2g$. For $g=2$ the rank is $4$. The abelianization gives $H_1\cong\mathbb{Z}^{2g}$.

q: Which compact Riemann surface is a counterexample to the claim 'every genus-$1$ surface is biholomorphic to $\mathbb{C}/(\mathbb{Z}+i\mathbb{Z})$'?
text: Which compact Riemann surface is a counterexample to the claim 'every genus-$1$ surface is biholomorphic to $\mathbb{C}/(\mathbb{Z}+i\mathbb{Z})$'? Elliptic curves $\mathbb{C}/\Lambda$ are classified up to biholomorphism by their $j$-invariant $j(\tau)$, where $\Lambda=\mathbb{Z}+\tau\mathbb{Z}$. The lattice $\mathbb{Z}+2i\mathbb{Z}$ (with $\tau=2i$) has a different $j$-invariant from $\mathbb{Z}+i\mathbb{Z}$ ($\tau=i$, $j=1728$), so they are non-isomorphic genus-$1$ curves. Different $j$-invariants distinguish non-isomorphic tori.

q: Which of the following is NOT pinned down by the genus $g$ of a compact Riemann surface $X$?
text: Which of the following is NOT pinned down by the genus $g$ of a compact Riemann surface $X$? For $g\ge 2$ the biholomorphism class varies in a $(6g-6)$-real-dimensional moduli space $\mathcal{M}_g$; only topological/Hodge invariants like $\chi,\ b_1=2g,\ \dim H^0(\Omega^1)=g$ are forced by $g$ alone. For $g\ge 2$ the biholomorphism class varies in a $(6g-6)$-real-dimensional moduli space $\mathcal{M}_g$; only topological/Hodge invariants like $\chi,\ b_1=2g,\ \dim H^0(\Omega^1)=g$ are forced by…

q: What is the first Betti number $b_1(X)$ of a compact Riemann surface of genus $g=5$?
text: What is the first Betti number $b_1(X)$ of a compact Riemann surface of genus $g=5$? $H_1(X;\mathbb{Z})\cong\mathbb{Z}^{2g}$, so $b_1=2g=10$.

q: On a compact Riemann surface of genus $g=7$, compute $\deg K_X = 2g-2$ (the degree of the canonical divisor).
text: On a compact Riemann surface of genus $g=7$, compute $\deg K_X = 2g-2$ (the degree of the canonical divisor). $\deg K_X=2\cdot 7-2=12$. This is the $1$-forms count cashed out as a divisor degree.

q: For $g\ge 2$, the moduli space $\mathcal{M}_g$ of compact Riemann surfaces of genus $g$ has real dimension $6g-6$. Which argument gives this count?
text: For $g\ge 2$, the moduli space $\mathcal{M}_g$ of compact Riemann surfaces of genus $g$ has real dimension $6g-6$. Which argument gives this count? Teichmüller theory: the Teichmüller space $\mathcal{T}_g$ is a complex manifold of dimension $3g-3$ (equivalently real dimension $6g-6$). Heuristically, a hyperbolic pair-of-pants decomposition uses $3g-3$ curves, each carrying one length and one twist parameter. Count complex Teichmüller parameters from Fuchsian reps.

q: Two compact Riemann surfaces $X$ and $Y$ satisfy $\chi(X)=-6$ and $\chi(Y)=-10$. How many more generators does $H_1(Y;\mathbb{Z})$ have than $H_1(X;\mathbb{Z})$?
text: Two compact Riemann surfaces $X$ and $Y$ satisfy $\chi(X)=-6$ and $\chi(Y)=-10$. How many more generators does $H_1(Y;\mathbb{Z})$ have than $H_1(X;\mathbb{Z})$? $\chi=-6\Rightarrow g_X=4$, $b_1(X)=2\cdot4=8$. $\chi=-10\Rightarrow g_Y=6$, $b_1(Y)=2\cdot6=12$. Difference: $12-8=4$. Translate $\chi$ to genus, then to $b_1=2g$.

q: Which of the following is a correct counterexample showing genus alone does not determine the isomorphism class of a compact Riemann surface?
text: Which of the following is a correct counterexample showing genus alone does not determine the isomorphism class of a compact Riemann surface? Both $E_1$ and $E_2$ are genus-$1$ surfaces, but their $j$-invariants differ ($j(i)=1728$ vs $j(2i)\ne 1728$), so they are non-isomorphic as Riemann surfaces. This shows the moduli space $\mathcal{M}_1$ is one-dimensional, not a point. Different tori with distinct $j$-invariants break uniqueness.

q: The Riemann-Roch theorem on a compact Riemann surface of genus $g$ states:
text: The Riemann-Roch theorem on a compact Riemann surface of genus $g$ states: The identity $\ell(D)-\ell(K-D)=\deg D-g+1$ is Riemann-Roch; equivalently $\chi(\mathcal{O}(D))=\deg D-g+1$, and the correction $\ell(K-D)$ is Serre dual to $h^1$.

q: On $\mathbb{CP}^1$ (genus $0$), for $D=5\cdot\infty$, compute $\ell(D)$. (Hint: Riemann-Roch with $K=-2\cdot\infty$ and $\ell(K-D)=0$ for $\deg(K-D)<0$.)
text: On $\mathbb{CP}^1$ (genus $0$), for $D=5\cdot\infty$, compute $\ell(D)$. (Hint: Riemann-Roch with $K=-2\cdot\infty$ and $\ell(K-D)=0$ for $\deg(K-D)<0$.) $\deg(K-D)=-2-5=-7<0$ so $\ell(K-D)=0$. Then $\ell(D)=\deg D-g+1=5-0+1=6$ — polynomials of degree $\le 5$.

q: On an elliptic curve $E$ (genus $1$) with $\deg K=0$, take $D=4\cdot O$ with $O$ the origin. Compute $\ell(D)$. (For $\deg D>0$ on genus $1$, $\ell(K-D)=0$.)
text: On an elliptic curve $E$ (genus $1$) with $\deg K=0$, take $D=4\cdot O$ with $O$ the origin. Compute $\ell(D)$. (For $\deg D>0$ on genus $1$, $\ell(K-D)=0$.) Since $\deg(K-D)=-4<0$, $\ell(K-D)=0$ and $\ell(D)=\deg D-g+1=4-1+1=4$. A basis is $\{1,\wp,\wp',\wp^2\}$.

q: On a compact Riemann surface of genus $g=3$, you compute $\ell(D)=3$ and $\deg D=4$. Riemann-Roch gives $\ell(K-D)=\ell(D)-\deg D+g-1$. What is $\ell(K-D)$?
text: On a compact Riemann surface of genus $g=3$, you compute $\ell(D)=3$ and $\deg D=4$. Riemann-Roch gives $\ell(K-D)=\ell(D)-\deg D+g-1$. What is $\ell(K-D)$? $\ell(K-D)=\ell(D)-\deg D+g-1=3-4+3-1=1$. The correction term $\ell(K-D)$ measures how many sections the Serre-dual line bundle $\mathcal{O}(K-D)$ has; here it is $1$, indicating $K-D$ is effective. Rearrange Riemann-Roch to isolate $\ell(K-D)$.

q: On a compact Riemann surface of genus $g=2$ with canonical divisor $K$ ($\deg K=2$), compute $\ell(K)$ using Riemann-Roch applied to $D=K$: $\ell(K)-\ell(0)=\deg K-g+1$ and $\ell(0)=1$.
text: On a compact Riemann surface of genus $g=2$ with canonical divisor $K$ ($\deg K=2$), compute $\ell(K)$ using Riemann-Roch applied to $D=K$: $\ell(K)-\ell(0)=\deg K-g+1$ and $\ell(0)=1$. Riemann-Roch for $D=K$: $\ell(K)-\ell(K-K)=\deg K-g+1=2-2+1=1$. Since $\ell(0)=1$ (only constant functions), $\ell(K)=1+1=2$. This equals $g=2$, consistent with $\dim H^0(X,\Omega^1)=g$. Apply Riemann-Roch with $D=K$ and use $\ell(0)=1$.

q: Serre duality identifies $H^1(X,\mathcal{O}(D))\cong H^0(X,\mathcal{O}(K-D))^\vee$. Which is the correct proof-step ordering to show $\ell(K-D)=h^1(D)$?
text: Serre duality identifies $H^1(X,\mathcal{O}(D))\cong H^0(X,\mathcal{O}(K-D))^\vee$. Which is the correct proof-step ordering to show $\ell(K-D)=h^1(D)$? Serre duality for a compact Riemann surface gives a perfect pairing $H^0(X,\mathcal{O}(K-D))\times H^1(X,\mathcal{O}(D))\to\mathbb{C}$, so $h^1(D)=\dim H^1(X,\mathcal{O}(D))=\dim H^0(X,\mathcal{O}(K-D))=\ell(K-D)$. This is the key identity that turns the index theorem into Riemann-Roch. Serre duality pairs $H^1(D)$ with $H^0(K-D)$.

title: Vector bundles
blurb: A rank-$n$ vector bundle $E \to X$ is locally trivial of fibre $\mathbb{R}^n$ (or $\mathbb{C}^n$); transition functions $g_{\alpha\beta}\colon U_\alpha \cap U_\beta \to GL_n$ on a cover $\{U_\alpha\}$ recover the bundle up to isomorphism. The tangent bundle $TM$, normal bundles, and line bundles like the Möbius bundle are the running examples.

title: Stiefel–Whitney classes
blurb: A real rank-$n$ bundle $E \to X$ has Stiefel–Whitney classes $w_i(E) \in H^i(X; \mathbb{Z}/2)$, $i=0,\ldots,n$, satisfying naturality $w_i(f^*E) = f^*w_i(E)$, the Whitney sum formula $w(E\oplus F) = w(E)\smile w(F)$, and the normalisation $w_1(\gamma_1) \ne 0$ for the tautological line bundle on $\mathbb{RP}^\infty$. Vanishing of $w_1$ is orientability; vanishing of $w_2$ is the obstruction to a spin structure.

title: Chern classes
blurb: A complex rank-$n$ bundle $E \to X$ has Chern classes $c_i(E) \in H^{2i}(X; \mathbb{Z})$, characterised by naturality, the Whitney sum $c(E\oplus F) = c(E)\smile c(F)$, and $c_1(\mathcal{O}(1)) = $ generator of $H^2(\mathbb{CP}^\infty;\mathbb{Z})$. The splitting principle reduces every computation to formal sums of line bundles, and the top Chern class $c_n(E)$ equals the Euler class of the underlying oriented real bundle.

title: Pontryagin classes
blurb: For a real bundle $E \to X$, complexify to $E\otimes\mathbb{C}$ and define $p_i(E) = (-1)^i c_{2i}(E\otimes\mathbb{C}) \in H^{4i}(X;\mathbb{Z})$. Odd Chern classes of a complexification are 2-torsion, so the surviving information sits in degrees divisible by $4$ — these classes are the building blocks of the signature theorem and the Hirzebruch $L$-genus.

title: Euler class
blurb: An oriented real rank-$n$ bundle $E \to X$ has an Euler class $e(E) \in H^n(X;\mathbb{Z})$, the obstruction to a nowhere-vanishing section. For $E = TM$ on a closed oriented manifold, $\langle e(TM), [M] \rangle = \chi(M)$ — the Poincaré–Hopf theorem expresses the Euler characteristic as the signed sum of zeros of any vector field.

title: Chern–Weil theory
blurb:
Pick a connection $
abla$ on a complex bundle $E 	o M$, take its curvature $F_
abla \in \Omega^2(M;\mathrm{End}\,E)$, and feed it into an $\mathrm{Ad}$-invariant polynomial $P$. The form $P(F_
abla)$ is closed and its de Rham class is independent of $
abla$; the polynomials $\det(I + 	frac{i}{2\pi}F_
abla)$ recover the total Chern class. Gauss–Bonnet $\int_\Sigma K\,dA = 2\pi\chi(\Sigma)$ is the rank-$1$ Chern–Weil identity for $T\Sigma$.

q: Which datum determines a rank-$n$ vector bundle $E \to X$ up to isomorphism, given an open cover $\{U_\alpha\}$ of $X$?
text: Which datum determines a rank-$n$ vector bundle $E \to X$ up to isomorphism, given an open cover $\{U_\alpha\}$ of $X$? A bundle is glued from local trivialisations $E|_{U_\alpha} \cong U_\alpha\times\mathbb{R}^n$, and the transition data $g_{\alpha\beta}$ — subject to the cocycle condition — determines the bundle. Global sections need not exist (a global trivialisation is too strong). Local triviality plus how the trivialisations agree on overlaps.

q: Which bundle is non-trivial?
text: Which bundle is non-trivial? The Möbius bundle is the rank-$1$ bundle on $S^1$ whose monodromy multiplies by $-1$; it admits no nowhere-vanishing section, hence is non-trivial. $T\mathbb{R}^n$ is trivial, and $TS^3$ is trivial because $S^3$ is parallelisable (it is a Lie group). Which one has no nowhere-vanishing section that returns to the same value after one loop?

q: Let $M$ be a smooth manifold and $f\colon N \to M$ a smooth map. The pullback $f^*TM$ is naturally identified with which bundle on $N$?
text: Let $M$ be a smooth manifold and $f\colon N \to M$ a smooth map. The pullback $f^*TM$ is naturally identified with which bundle on $N$? By definition $(f^*TM)_p = T_{f(p)}M$. When $f$ is an immersion, $f^*TM \cong TN \oplus \nu_f$, but in general $f^*TM \ne TN$. Pullback rebuilds the fibres above $f(p)$ over $p$.

q: What does the vanishing of $w_1(E)$ measure for a real vector bundle $E \to X$?
text: What does the vanishing of $w_1(E)$ measure for a real vector bundle $E \to X$? $w_1(E) \in H^1(X;\mathbb{Z}/2)$ classifies the determinant line bundle $\det E$ up to isomorphism; it vanishes iff $E$ is orientable. Triviality is much stronger; a spin structure requires $w_2 = 0$ on top of $w_1 = 0$. It is the mod-$2$ obstruction to a coherent choice of frame orientation.

q: By the Whitney sum formula, $w(E \oplus F) = w(E) \smile w(F)$. What is $w_2(E\oplus F)$ in terms of the $w_i$?
text: By the Whitney sum formula, $w(E \oplus F) = w(E) \smile w(F)$. What is $w_2(E\oplus F)$ in terms of the $w_i$? Multiplying total classes and collecting degree-$2$ terms gives $w_2(E\oplus F) = w_2(E) + w_1(E) w_1(F) + w_2(F)$. The cross term $w_1 \smile w_1$ is exactly what $w_2$ feels under direct sum. Expand $(1 + w_1(E) + w_2(E) + \cdots)(1 + w_1(F) + w_2(F) + \cdots)$ and read off the degree-$2$ piece.

q: For the tangent bundle of $\mathbb{RP}^2$, which Stiefel–Whitney class is non-zero?
text: For the tangent bundle of $\mathbb{RP}^2$, which Stiefel–Whitney class is non-zero? $T\mathbb{RP}^2 \oplus \mathbb{R} \cong (\gamma^*)^{\oplus 3}$ gives $w(T\mathbb{RP}^2) = (1+a)^3 = 1 + 3a + 3a^2 = 1 + a + a^2$ in $H^*(\mathbb{RP}^2;\mathbb{Z}/2) = \mathbb{Z}/2[a]/(a^3)$, so $w_1 = a \ne 0$ (non-orientable) and $w_2 = a^2 \ne 0$ (no spin structure). $\mathbb{RP}^2$ is non-orientable, and $\chi(\mathbb{RP}^2) = 1$ is odd.

q: In which cohomology group does the Chern class $c_i(E)$ of a complex rank-$n$ bundle $E \to X$ live?
text: In which cohomology group does the Chern class $c_i(E)$ of a complex rank-$n$ bundle $E \to X$ live? Each Chern class lives in even integral degree: $c_i(E) \in H^{2i}(X;\mathbb{Z})$. The total Chern class $c(E) = 1 + c_1 + \cdots + c_n$ assembles them in $H^{\mathrm{even}}(X;\mathbb{Z})$. Complex bundles double the cohomological degree compared with real ones.

q: On $X = \mathbb{CP}^1 = S^2$, complex line bundles are classified by which integer invariant?
text: On $X = \mathbb{CP}^1 = S^2$, complex line bundles are classified by which integer invariant? Pic$(\mathbb{CP}^1) \cong \mathbb{Z}$, with the isomorphism $L \mapsto \deg L = \langle c_1(L), [\mathbb{CP}^1]\rangle$. The line bundle $\mathcal{O}(d)$ has $c_1 = d \cdot h$ where $h$ is the generator of $H^2$. $H^2(\mathbb{CP}^1;\mathbb{Z}) \cong \mathbb{Z}$ and $c_1$ is the universal characteristic class.

q: If $E \to X$ is a complex rank-$n$ bundle, the top Chern class $c_n(E)$ agrees with which other class of the underlying real bundle?
text: If $E \to X$ is a complex rank-$n$ bundle, the top Chern class $c_n(E)$ agrees with which other class of the underlying real bundle? A complex structure on a real bundle gives a canonical orientation, and the top Chern class $c_n(E) \in H^{2n}(X;\mathbb{Z})$ equals the Euler class $e(E_\mathbb{R}) \in H^{2n}(X;\mathbb{Z})$ — both count zeros of a generic section. Both classes obstruct the existence of a nowhere-vanishing section.

q: By definition, $p_i(E) = ?$ for a real bundle $E$.
text: By definition, $p_i(E) = ?$ for a real bundle $E$. $p_i(E) := (-1)^i c_{2i}(E\otimes\mathbb{C}) \in H^{4i}(X;\mathbb{Z})$. The sign is conventional; odd Chern classes of $E\otimes\mathbb{C}$ are 2-torsion because $E\otimes\mathbb{C}\cong \overline{E\otimes\mathbb{C}}$ as complex bundles. Take the complexification, keep only even Chern classes, and absorb a sign.

q: What cohomological degree does $p_i(E)$ live in?
text: What cohomological degree does $p_i(E)$ live in? Since $c_{2i}(E\otimes\mathbb{C}) \in H^{4i}(X;\mathbb{Z})$, Pontryagin classes are concentrated in degrees divisible by $4$. Complexification doubles degrees, and we keep only even Chern classes.

q: Why are the odd Chern classes $c_{2i+1}(E\otimes\mathbb{C})$ ignored in defining Pontryagin classes?
text: Why are the odd Chern classes $c_{2i+1}(E\otimes\mathbb{C})$ ignored in defining Pontryagin classes? The complexification of a real bundle is isomorphic to its complex conjugate, so $c_k(E\otimes\mathbb{C}) = (-1)^k c_k(E\otimes\mathbb{C})$, forcing $2 c_{2i+1}(E\otimes\mathbb{C}) = 0$. The honest integral information lives in even degrees. $E\otimes\mathbb{C}$ is isomorphic to its conjugate as a complex bundle.

q: For a closed oriented smooth manifold $M^n$, what does $\langle e(TM), [M] \rangle$ compute?
text: For a closed oriented smooth manifold $M^n$, what does $\langle e(TM), [M] \rangle$ compute? By Poincaré–Hopf, for any vector field $X$ on $M$ with isolated zeros, $\sum_p \ind_p(X) = \chi(M)$, and the same number is computed by pairing the Euler class with the fundamental class. Poincaré–Hopf: count signed zeros of any vector field.

q: Compute $\chi(T^2)$ for the $2$-torus.
text: Compute $\chi(T^2)$ for the $2$-torus. The torus has a nowhere-vanishing vector field (e.g. $\partial/\partial\theta_1$), so by Poincaré–Hopf $\chi(T^2) = 0$. Equivalently, the Betti numbers $1,2,1$ give $\chi = 1-2+1 = 0$. $T^2 = S^1 \times S^1$ is parallelisable, so $TT^2$ admits a nowhere-vanishing section.

q: On which space does an oriented rank-$n$ bundle fail to have a nowhere-vanishing section iff $e(E) \ne 0$?
text: On which space does an oriented rank-$n$ bundle fail to have a nowhere-vanishing section iff $e(E) \ne 0$? For an oriented rank-$n$ bundle on a CW base, the primary obstruction to a nowhere-vanishing section is exactly the Euler class $e(E) \in H^n(X;\mathbb{Z})$. Higher obstructions can appear but the first one is always $e(E)$. Obstruction theory expresses non-vanishing of a rank-$n$ section as an obstruction in $H^n$.

q: Given a connection $\nabla$ with curvature $F_\nabla$ on a complex bundle, which polynomial gives the total Chern class?
text:
Given a connection $
abla$ with curvature $F_
abla$ on a complex bundle, which polynomial gives the total Chern class? Chern–Weil: $c(E) = \det\! ig(I +  rac{i}{2\pi}F_
abla ig)$ as a closed $\mathrm{Ad}$-invariant form on $M$, whose de Rham class is independent of $
abla$. Expanding in degrees gives $c_k$. The factor $ rac{i}{2\pi}$ is exactly the normalisation that produces integral classes.

q: On a closed oriented surface $\Sigma$ with Riemannian metric, the Gauss–Bonnet theorem $\int_\Sigma K\,dA = 2\pi\,\chi(\Sigma)$ is which Chern–Weil identity?
text: On a closed oriented surface $\Sigma$ with Riemannian metric, the Gauss–Bonnet theorem $\int_\Sigma K\,dA = 2\pi\,\chi(\Sigma)$ is which Chern–Weil identity? $T\Sigma$ is an oriented rank-$2$ real bundle, equivalently a complex line bundle; the curvature $2$-form is $-K\,dA$ (up to sign), and $\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_\Sigma K\,dA = \chi(\Sigma)$ is the Chern–Weil expression of the Euler/first-Chern number. $\Sigma$ is $2$-dimensional, so we need a degree-$2$ class — and we are computing $\chi$.

q: Why is the de Rham class of $P(F_\nabla)$ independent of the connection $\nabla$?
text:
Why is the de Rham class of $P(F_
abla)$ independent of the connection $
abla$? If $
abla_t = 
abla + t(
abla' - 
abla)$ is a path of connections, integrating $ rac{d}{dt}P(F_{
abla_t})$ over $t \in [0,1]$ produces an explicit primitive, the Chern–Simons / transgression form, so $[P(F_
abla)]$ is independent of $
abla$ in de Rham cohomology. Two connections differ by an $\mathrm{End}(E)$-valued $1$-form; build a path between them.

title: Morse functions and non-degenerate critical points
blurb: A smooth $f\colon M\to\mathbb{R}$ is Morse when every critical point is non-degenerate (Hessian invertible). The Morse lemma gives a chart in which $f = -x_1^2-\cdots-x_k^2 + x_{k+1}^2+\cdots+x_n^2 + c$, where the index $k$ is the number of negative eigenvalues. Morse functions are dense in $C^\infty(M)$.

title: Handle decomposition via sublevel sets
blurb: As $c$ increases, the sublevel set $M^c=\{f\le c\}$ deforms by gradient flow except when $c$ crosses a critical value: there it gains a $k$-handle $D^k\times D^{n-k}$, where $k$ is the Morse index. Cells, not points, are how the topology changes.

title: CW structure from a Morse function
blurb: Up to homotopy each $k$-handle attached to $M^{c-\epsilon}$ is a $k$-cell, so $M$ has the homotopy type of a CW complex with one cell of dimension $k$ for every critical point of index $k$. This is the bridge from differential topology to cellular homology.

title: Morse inequalities
blurb: If $c_k(f)$ counts index-$k$ critical points and $b_k(M)$ are Betti numbers, then $b_k(M)\le c_k(f)$ (weak) and $\sum_k(-1)^k c_k = \chi(M)$ (Morse–Reeb). The strong inequalities $\sum_{k\le j}(-1)^{j-k}(c_k - b_k)\ge 0$ refine the count via the chain complex.

title: Morse homology
blurb: Pick a Morse–Smale gradient: the chain complex has critical points as generators in degree equal to their index, and the boundary $\partial$ counts signed gradient flow lines between consecutive-index critical points. The resulting homology is canonically isomorphic to singular homology — a hands-on model of $H_*(M)$.

title: Applications and generalisations
blurb: Reeb's sphere theorem (a closed manifold with exactly two critical points is homeomorphic to a sphere), Bott's perfect Morse functions on Lie groups, and the infinite-dimensional analogue — Floer homology on path/loop spaces — all flow from this machinery.

q: A smooth function $f\colon M\to\mathbb{R}$ is called Morse when every critical point...
text: A smooth function $f\colon M\to\mathbb{R}$ is called Morse when every critical point... Morse = every critical point is non-degenerate, i.e. the Hessian is an invertible symmetric matrix. Non-degeneracy automatically implies isolated, but the converse fails (think $f(x)=x^4$). Non-degenerate means $\det \mathrm{Hess}(f) \ne 0$ at the critical point.

q: On a chart around a non-degenerate critical point, the Morse lemma writes $f$ as $-x_1^2-\cdots-x_k^2 + x_{k+1}^2+\cdots+x_n^2 + c$. The integer $k$ is called the...
text: On a chart around a non-degenerate critical point, the Morse lemma writes $f$ as $-x_1^2-\cdots-x_k^2 + x_{k+1}^2+\cdots+x_n^2 + c$. The integer $k$ is called the... The Morse index $k$ is the number of negative eigenvalues of the Hessian — equivalently, the dimension of the maximal subspace on which the Hessian is negative definite. It counts the negative eigenvalues of the Hessian.

q: Why is it harmless to assume the function we study is Morse?
text: Why is it harmless to assume the function we study is Morse? Morse functions form an open dense subset of $C^\infty(M,\mathbb{R})$: an arbitrary smooth $f$ can be perturbed by an arbitrarily small amount to a Morse function. So results about Morse functions transfer to generic smooth ones. Density in the smooth topology lets us perturb generic statements.

q: If $f$ has no critical values in $[a,b]$, how does the sublevel set $M^a=\{f\le a\}$ relate to $M^b=\{f\le b\}$?
text: If $f$ has no critical values in $[a,b]$, how does the sublevel set $M^a=\{f\le a\}$ relate to $M^b=\{f\le b\}$? Without a critical value in between, the (rescaled) negative gradient flow gives a smooth deformation retraction of $M^b$ onto $M^a$. Topology only changes when $c$ crosses a critical value. Use the gradient flow downward to push $M^b$ onto $M^a$.

q: When $c$ crosses a critical value of a Morse function with a single index-$k$ critical point, $M^{c+\epsilon}$ is obtained from $M^{c-\epsilon}$ by attaching...
text: When $c$ crosses a critical value of a Morse function with a single index-$k$ critical point, $M^{c+\epsilon}$ is obtained from $M^{c-\epsilon}$ by attaching... The thickened version of the index-$k$ cell is a $k$-handle: $D^k\times D^{n-k}$ glued along $\partial D^k\times D^{n-k}$. The descending disk has dimension $k$; the ascending disk has dimension $n-k$. The stable manifold of the critical point has dimension equal to the Morse index $k$.

q: On the standard height function on $S^2$ (north and south poles), the handle decomposition consists of:
text: On the standard height function on $S^2$ (north and south poles), the handle decomposition consists of: South pole is a minimum (index 0): a 0-handle = $D^0\times D^2$, a 2-disk. North pole is a maximum (index 2): a 2-handle = $D^2\times D^0$, another 2-disk. Glued along their boundary circle they make $S^2$. South pole = index 0, north pole = index 2.

q: The CW structure produced from a Morse function on $M$ has one cell of dimension $k$ for every...
text: The CW structure produced from a Morse function on $M$ has one cell of dimension $k$ for every... Up to homotopy the $k$-handle attached at an index-$k$ critical point is a $k$-cell. So there is exactly one $k$-cell per index-$k$ critical point — that is the CW structure. Index $k$ critical point gives a $k$-cell.

q: A height-function Morse decomposition of the torus $T^2$ (standard embedding in $\mathbb{R}^3$) has critical points at indices $0,1,1,2$. The resulting CW structure has:
text: A height-function Morse decomposition of the torus $T^2$ (standard embedding in $\mathbb{R}^3$) has critical points at indices $0,1,1,2$. The resulting CW structure has: Each index $k$ contributes one $k$-cell: indices $0,1,1,2$ give one 0-cell, two 1-cells, one 2-cell — exactly the standard CW structure on $T^2$. Read off cell counts directly from the index list.

q: The Euler characteristic of $M$ in terms of critical-point counts $c_k$ is:
text: The Euler characteristic of $M$ in terms of critical-point counts $c_k$ is: The CW Euler characteristic is $\sum_k (-1)^k (\text{number of } k\text{-cells})$, which from a Morse function reads $\sum_k (-1)^k c_k(f)$. This is independent of the Morse function chosen. Cellular Euler characteristic: alternating sum of cell counts.

q: The weak Morse inequality states that for any Morse $f$ and any $k$,
text: The weak Morse inequality states that for any Morse $f$ and any $k$, Each index-$k$ critical point contributes a $k$-cell, and Betti numbers cannot exceed the number of $k$-cells in any CW structure. Hence $b_k(M)\le c_k(f)$. Betti numbers are bounded above by index counts.

q: A Morse function is called perfect when
text: A Morse function is called perfect when A perfect Morse function realises the equality case of the weak inequality: the differential in the Morse complex is zero, so the count of index-$k$ critical points equals $b_k(M)$. They exist on $\mathbb{C}P^n$, on flag varieties, and on compact Lie groups (Bott). The chain complex differential vanishes — index counts equal Betti numbers.

q: Any Morse function on the closed orientable genus-2 surface $\Sigma_2$ must have at least how many critical points?
text: Any Morse function on the closed orientable genus-2 surface $\Sigma_2$ must have at least how many critical points? By the weak Morse inequality, $\sum_k c_k \ge \sum_k b_k = 1+4+1 = 6$. Six critical points (one min, four saddles, one max) is achieved by the standard height function. Sum the Betti numbers: $b_0 + b_1 + b_2 = 1 + 4 + 1$.

q: In the Morse chain complex, the generators of $C_k$ are
text: In the Morse chain complex, the generators of $C_k$ are $C_k$ is the free abelian group (or $\mathbb{Z}/2$-vector space) on the index-$k$ critical points of a Morse–Smale pair $(f,g)$. One generator per index-$k$ critical point.

q: The Morse boundary $\partial\colon C_k \to C_{k-1}$ counts...
text: The Morse boundary $\partial\colon C_k \to C_{k-1}$ counts... For a Morse–Smale gradient, the moduli space of unparameterised flow lines from index-$k$ to index-$(k-1)$ critical points is 0-dimensional and oriented; $\partial$ counts these signed. Index drops by 1 along each rigid trajectory.

q: What does Morse homology compute?
text: What does Morse homology compute? Morse homology $HM_*(M)$ is independent of the Morse–Smale pair (continuation maps give chain homotopy equivalences) and is canonically isomorphic to singular $H_*(M;\mathbb{Z})$. Different Morse functions give chain-homotopy-equivalent complexes.

q: Reeb's theorem says that a closed manifold $M$ admitting a Morse function with exactly two critical points is...
text: Reeb's theorem says that a closed manifold $M$ admitting a Morse function with exactly two critical points is... Two critical points must be a min (index 0) and max (index $n$). The two disks glued along their common boundary is homeomorphic to $S^n$ — but the smooth structure may be exotic (Milnor's exotic 7-spheres). Two critical points = two handles, gluing two disks.

q: $\mathbb{R}P^2$ admits a Morse function with critical-point indices $0,1,2$. The corresponding Morse counts $(c_0,c_1,c_2)=(1,1,1)$ give Euler characteristic:
text: $\mathbb{R}P^2$ admits a Morse function with critical-point indices $0,1,2$. The corresponding Morse counts $(c_0,c_1,c_2)=(1,1,1)$ give Euler characteristic: $\chi(\mathbb{R}P^2) = 1 - 1 + 1 = 1$. Compare with $\chi(S^2)=1-0+1=2$ from $(c_0,c_1,c_2)=(1,0,1)$ — the index-1 critical point is what distinguishes them topologically. $\chi = c_0 - c_1 + c_2$.

q: Floer homology generalises Morse homology by replacing the finite-dimensional manifold $M$ with an infinite-dimensional space, e.g.
text: Floer homology generalises Morse homology by replacing the finite-dimensional manifold $M$ with an infinite-dimensional space, e.g. Floer's insight: the symplectic action functional on the free loop space (or path space with Lagrangian boundary) has critical points = periodic orbits (or Lagrangian intersections), and gradient flow lines = pseudo-holomorphic strips. The Morse complex generalises term-by-term. Critical points of the action functional on loops are periodic orbits.

title: 2. What a compass and straightedge can do
Field extensions and automorphisms
blurb: Splitting fields and automorphism groups link polynomial equations to symmetry.

title: Splitting fields
blurb: The splitting field of $f \in K[x]$ is the smallest extension of $K$ over which $f$ factors into linear factors. It is unique up to $K$-isomorphism and is the natural domain on which $\Gal(L/K)$ acts.

title: Fundamental theorem of Galois theory
blurb: Intermediate fields correspond contravariantly to subgroups of the Galois group.

title: Solvable groups
blurb: $G$ is solvable iff its derived series $G \tr{}iangleright G' \tr{}iangleright G'' \tr{}iangleright \cdots$ terminates at $\{e\}$. Equivalently, $G$ admits a normal series with abelian quotients. Examples: $S_3, S_4$ are solvable; $S_n$ for $n \geq 5$ is not.

title: Radical extensions
blurb: A tower $K = F_0 \subset F_1 \subset \cdots \subset F_n$ with $F_{i+1} = F_i(\sqrt[n_i]{a_i})$. A polynomial $f$ is solvable by radicals iff its splitting field embeds in such a tower; on the group side this becomes a normal series with cyclic quotients, hence solvability of $\Gal$.

title: Solvability by radicals
blurb: $f \in K[x]$ is solvable by radicals iff $\Gal(L/K)$ is a solvable group, where $L$ is the splitting field. The general quintic has Galois group $S_5$, which is not solvable — so no general radical formula exists for degree $\geq 5$.

title: Normal and separable extensions
blurb: A finite extension is Galois iff it is both normal (a splitting field) and separable (minimal polynomials have distinct roots); in characteristic zero, separability is automatic.

title: Primitive element theorem
blurb: Every finite separable extension $L/K$ is simple: $L=K(\alpha)$ for some $\alpha$, so Galois theory over characteristic zero reduces to the study of a single minimal polynomial.

q: Let $L=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt[3]{2},\omega)$ be the splitting field of $x^3-2$ over $\mathbb{Q}$, where $\omega=e^{2\pi i/3}$. What is $[L:\mathbb{Q}]$?
text: Let $L=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt[3]{2},\omega)$ be the splitting field of $x^3-2$ over $\mathbb{Q}$, where $\omega=e^{2\pi i/3}$. What is $[L:\mathbb{Q}]$? The minimal polynomial of $\sqrt[3]{2}$ has degree $3$ and that of $\omega$ has degree $2$, so $[L:\mathbb{Q}]=3\cdot 2=6$ by the tower law.

q: What is the order of $\Gal(\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_5)/\mathbb{Q})$, where $\zeta_5=e^{2\pi i/5}$?
text: What is the order of $\Gal(\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_5)/\mathbb{Q})$, where $\zeta_5=e^{2\pi i/5}$? The fifth cyclotomic polynomial $\Phi_5$ has degree $\varphi(5)=4$, so the Galois group has order $4$ (it is cyclic, isomorphic to $(\mathbb{Z}/5)^\times$).

q: The nontrivial automorphism of $\mathbb{Q}(i)/\mathbb{Q}$ sends $i\mapsto -i$. What is its image on $2+3i$, written as $a+bi$?
text: The nontrivial automorphism of $\mathbb{Q}(i)/\mathbb{Q}$ sends $i\mapsto -i$. What is its image on $2+3i$, written as $a+bi$? Complex conjugation fixes $\mathbb{Q}$ and sends $i\mapsto -i$, so $2+3i\mapsto 2-3i$.

q: Let $\sigma\in\Gal(\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_5)/\mathbb{Q})$ be defined by $\sigma(\zeta_5)=\zeta_5^2$. Which power of $\sigma$ is the identity automorphism?
text: Let $\sigma\in\Gal(\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_5)/\mathbb{Q})$ be defined by $\sigma(\zeta_5)=\zeta_5^2$. Which power of $\sigma$ is the identity automorphism? $\sigma^k(\zeta_5)=\zeta_5^{2^k}$. We need $2^k\equiv 1\pmod{5}$: $2^1=2,2^2=4,2^3=3,2^4=16\equiv 1$. So $\sigma$ has order $4$ and $\sigma^4=\mathrm{id}$. Find the order of $2$ in $(\mathbb{Z}/5)^\times$.

q: How many intermediate fields $F$ satisfy $\mathbb{Q}\subsetneq F\subsetneq\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_5)$? (Use the Galois correspondence with $\Gal\cong\mathbb{Z}/4$.)
text: How many intermediate fields $F$ satisfy $\mathbb{Q}\subsetneq F\subsetneq\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_5)$? (Use the Galois correspondence with $\Gal\cong\mathbb{Z}/4$.) $\mathbb{Z}/4$ has exactly one proper nontrivial subgroup, namely $\{0,2\}\cong\mathbb{Z}/2$, corresponding to the unique intermediate field $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{5})$. Count proper nontrivial subgroups of $\mathbb{Z}/4$.

q: The extension $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt[4]{2})/\mathbb{Q}$ has degree $4$. Which statement about its automorphism group is correct?
text: The extension $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt[4]{2})/\mathbb{Q}$ has degree $4$. Which statement about its automorphism group is correct? An automorphism must permute the roots of $x^4-2$. The real roots are $\pm\sqrt[4]{2}$, and the complex roots $\pm i\sqrt[4]{2}$ do not lie in $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt[4]{2})\subset\mathbb{R}$. So the only $\mathbb{Q}$-automorphisms send $\sqrt[4]{2}\mapsto\pm\sqrt[4]{2}$, giving a group of order $2$. Since $2<4$, the extension is not Galois. Only real conjugates can live in this real field.

q: The Galois correspondence for a Galois extension $L/K$ matches intermediate fields $K\subseteq F\subseteq L$ with:
text: The Galois correspondence for a Galois extension $L/K$ matches intermediate fields $K\subseteq F\subseteq L$ with: The correspondence $F\leftrightarrow \Gal(L/F)$ is an inclusion-reversing bijection: bigger fields correspond to smaller subgroups.

q: $L=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2},\sqrt{3})$ has $\Gal(L/\mathbb{Q})\cong(\mathbb{Z}/2)^2$ of order $4$. How many intermediate fields strictly between $\mathbb{Q}$ and $L$ are there?
text: $L=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2},\sqrt{3})$ has $\Gal(L/\mathbb{Q})\cong(\mathbb{Z}/2)^2$ of order $4$. How many intermediate fields strictly between $\mathbb{Q}$ and $L$ are there? The Klein four-group has three proper nontrivial subgroups, each of order $2$, corresponding to the three quadratic subfields $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2}),\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{3}),\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{6})$.

q: Match each subgroup of $\Gal(L/\mathbb{Q})\cong(\mathbb{Z}/2)^2$ for $L=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2},\sqrt{3})$ with its fixed field.
text: Match each subgroup of $\Gal(L/\mathbb{Q})\cong(\mathbb{Z}/2)^2$ for $L=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2},\sqrt{3})$ with its fixed field. By the Galois correspondence: $\{e\}\leftrightarrow L$, $G\leftrightarrow\mathbb{Q}$. The subgroup $\langle\sigma\rangle$ fixes $\sqrt{3}$, hence fixes $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{3})$; $\langle\tau\rangle$ fixes $\sqrt{2}\sqrt{3}=\sqrt{6}$ (since both signs flip together), hence fixes $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{6})$. The third order-$2$ subgroup $\langle\sigma\tau\rangle$ fixes $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2})$. Inclusion-reversing: trivial subgroup fixes all of $L$; whole group fixes only $\mathbb{Q}$. For each order-$2$ subgroup, find which $\sqrt{d}$ is fixed.

q: In the extension $L=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt[3]{2},\omega)/\mathbb{Q}$ with $\Gal(L/\mathbb{Q})\cong S_3$, the fixed field of the unique normal subgroup $A_3\cong\mathbb{Z}/3$ is:
text: In the extension $L=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt[3]{2},\omega)/\mathbb{Q}$ with $\Gal(L/\mathbb{Q})\cong S_3$, the fixed field of the unique normal subgroup $A_3\cong\mathbb{Z}/3$ is: $A_3$ is the unique normal subgroup of $S_3$ of index $2$. By the Galois correspondence, its fixed field is the unique quadratic subfield. Since $\omega=\frac{-1+\sqrt{-3}}{2}$, we have $\mathbb{Q}(\omega)=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{-3})$, which is the fixed field of $A_3$. The unique index-2 subgroup fixes the unique quadratic subfield.

q: Let $G=\Gal(L/\mathbb{Q})\cong S_3$ for $L=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt[3]{2},\omega)$. How many subgroups does $S_3$ have in total (including trivial and whole group)?
text: Let $G=\Gal(L/\mathbb{Q})\cong S_3$ for $L=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt[3]{2},\omega)$. How many subgroups does $S_3$ have in total (including trivial and whole group)? $S_3$ has: $\{e\}$, three subgroups of order $2$ (each generated by a transposition), one subgroup of order $3$ (namely $A_3$), and $S_3$ itself — giving $1+3+1+1=6$ subgroups. By Galois correspondence these give $6$ intermediate fields including $\mathbb{Q}$ and $L$. Count subgroups by order: 1, 2, 3, 6.

q: Which is the correct chain of subgroup inclusions encoding the tower $\mathbb{Q}\subset\mathbb{Q}(\omega)\subset L$ via the Galois correspondence (groups listed large to small as fields go small to large)?
text: Which is the correct chain of subgroup inclusions encoding the tower $\mathbb{Q}\subset\mathbb{Q}(\omega)\subset L$ via the Galois correspondence (groups listed large to small as fields go small to large)? The correspondence reverses inclusion: $\mathbb{Q}\subset\mathbb{Q}(\omega)\subset L$ corresponds to $S_3\supset A_3\supset\{e\}$. Since $A_3\tr{}ianglelefteq S_3$, the intermediate field $\mathbb{Q}(\omega)$ is indeed normal over $\mathbb{Q}$, with $\Gal(\mathbb{Q}(\omega)/\mathbb{Q})\cong S_3/A_3\cong\mathbb{Z}/2$. Field inclusions reverse on the group side.

q: Match each polynomial with its Galois group over $\mathbb{Q}$, and conclude solvability accordingly.
text: Match each polynomial with its Galois group over $\mathbb{Q}$, and conclude solvability accordingly. $\Phi_5(x)=x^4+x^3+x^2+x+1$ has Galois group $(\mathbb{Z}/5)^\times\cong\mathbb{Z}/4$. $x^3-2$ has Galois group $S_3$, solvable via $S_3\supset A_3\supset\{e\}$. The general quartic has $S_4$, solvable via $S_4\supset A_4\supset V_4\supset\{e\}$. $x^5-x-1$ is irreducible with exactly two non-real roots, so complex conjugation is a transposition; combined with a $5$-cycle from transitivity, we get all of $S_5$, which is not solvable. Cyclotomic gives abelian; the irreducible cubic with one real root gives $S_3$; generic quartics give $S_4$; $x^5-x-1$ has $S_5$ via transposition + $5$-cycle.

q: Arrange the steps that prove the general quintic $x^5-x-1$ is not solvable by radicals.
text: Arrange the steps that prove the general quintic $x^5-x-1$ is not solvable by radicals. The full chain: invoke Galois' criterion, prove the Galois group is $S_5$ (irreducibility gives a $5$-cycle; one transposition from complex conjugation generates the rest), then show $A_5$ is simple via conjugacy-class arithmetic, hence $S_5$ is not solvable. Set up Galois' criterion first, then identify the Galois group as $S_5$, then prove $S_5$ is not solvable.

q: Which polynomial's Galois group over $\mathbb{Q}$ is all of $S_5$, preventing a radical solution?
text: Which polynomial's Galois group over $\mathbb{Q}$ is all of $S_5$, preventing a radical solution? $x^5-x-1$ is irreducible over $\mathbb{Q}$, has exactly two non-real roots (so complex conjugation is a transposition), and a $5$-cycle from transitivity — these generate $S_5$.

q: The derived series of $S_4$ is $S_4\supset A_4\supset V_4\supset\{e\}$ where $V_4$ is the Klein four-group. Which statement correctly identifies why this proves $S_4$ is solvable?
text: The derived series of $S_4$ is $S_4\supset A_4\supset V_4\supset\{e\}$ where $V_4$ is the Klein four-group. Which statement correctly identifies why this proves $S_4$ is solvable? Solvability requires a subnormal series with abelian quotients: $S_4/A_4\cong\mathbb{Z}/2$, $A_4/V_4\cong\mathbb{Z}/3$, $V_4/\{e\}\cong(\mathbb{Z}/2)^2$ — all abelian. Hence every quartic is solvable by radicals. Check every successive quotient for abelianness.

q: A key step in proving $A_5$ is not solvable is showing it is simple (no normal subgroups). Which argument is correct?
text: A key step in proving $A_5$ is not solvable is showing it is simple (no normal subgroups). Which argument is correct? A normal subgroup is a union of conjugacy classes containing $e$. The conjugacy class sizes in $A_5$ are $\{1,15,20,12,12\}$. The only sub-sums including $1$ that divide $|A_5|=60$ are $1$ and $60$ themselves, so $A_5$ has no proper nontrivial normal subgroup — it is simple. Normal subgroups are unions of conjugacy classes whose sizes sum to a divisor.

q: The solvable radical length of $S_5$ is the minimum length of a subnormal series with abelian quotients reaching $\{e\}$. Since $S_5$ is not solvable, this length is undefined. Instead: what is the order of the commutator subgroup $[S_5,S_5]$?
text: The solvable radical length of $S_5$ is the minimum length of a subnormal series with abelian quotients reaching $\{e\}$. Since $S_5$ is not solvable, this length is undefined. Instead: what is the order of the commutator subgroup $[S_5,S_5]$? For $n\ge 5$, $[S_n,S_n]=A_n$. Thus $[S_5,S_5]=A_5$, which has order $60$. The fact that $A_5$ equals its own commutator subgroup ($A_5$ is perfect) is what destroys solvability. The commutator subgroup of $S_n$ is $A_n$ for $n\ge 2$.

q: Which extension of $\mathbb{Q}$ is not normal?
text: Which extension of $\mathbb{Q}$ is not normal? $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt[3]{2})$ contains only one root of $x^3-2$; the other two roots $\omega\sqrt[3]{2},\omega^2\sqrt[3]{2}$ are complex. A normal extension must contain all roots of the minimal polynomial of any element.

q: For a finite Galois extension $L/K$ one has $|\Gal(L/K)|=[L:K]$. If $[L:K]=12$, what is $|\Gal(L/K)|$?
text: For a finite Galois extension $L/K$ one has $|\Gal(L/K)|=[L:K]$. If $[L:K]=12$, what is $|\Gal(L/K)|$? Galois is exactly the equality $|\Gal(L/K)|=[L:K]$. If either side is $12$, so is the other.

q: Which statement characterizes separability of $\alpha$ over $K$?
text: Which statement characterizes separability of $\alpha$ over $K$? By definition $\alpha$ is separable over $K$ iff its minimal polynomial over $K$ has no repeated roots in any extension. In characteristic zero this is automatic.

q: Let $K=\mathbb{F}_p(t)$ and $L=K(t^{1/p})$. Which of the following is true?
text: Let $K=\mathbb{F}_p(t)$ and $L=K(t^{1/p})$. Which of the following is true? In $L[x]$, $x^p-t=(x-t^{1/p})^p$ by the Frobenius identity in characteristic $p$. The minimal polynomial of $t^{1/p}$ over $K$ is $x^p-t$, which has a single root of multiplicity $p$. So $t^{1/p}$ is inseparable. $L$ is the splitting field of $x^p-t$ (only one root), so it is normal — but it is not Galois since separability fails. Frobenius collapses $x^p-t$ into a perfect $p$-th power.

q: Let $L/\mathbb{Q}$ be Galois with $\Gal(L/\mathbb{Q})\cong S_3$. The unique normal subgroup $A_3\cong\mathbb{Z}/3$ has index $2$ in $S_3$. What is $[L^{A_3}:\mathbb{Q}]$, the degree of the fixed field of $A_3$ over $\mathbb{Q}$?
text: Let $L/\mathbb{Q}$ be Galois with $\Gal(L/\mathbb{Q})\cong S_3$. The unique normal subgroup $A_3\cong\mathbb{Z}/3$ has index $2$ in $S_3$. What is $[L^{A_3}:\mathbb{Q}]$, the degree of the fixed field of $A_3$ over $\mathbb{Q}$? The Galois correspondence gives $[L^H:K]=[G:H]$ for $G=\Gal(L/K)$. Here $[S_3:A_3]=2$, so $[L^{A_3}:\mathbb{Q}]=2$. The fixed field is a quadratic extension of $\mathbb{Q}$. Fixed-field degree equals the index of the subgroup.

q: Which is a correct example of a finite extension that is normal but NOT separable?
text: Which is a correct example of a finite extension that is normal but NOT separable? $\mathbb{F}_p(t^{1/p})/\mathbb{F}_p(t)$ is the splitting field of $x^p-t$ (which factors as $(x-t^{1/p})^p$), hence normal. But $t^{1/p}$ has minimal polynomial $x^p-t$ with a repeated root, so the extension is inseparable. The other options are either not normal ($\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt[3]{2})$) or are Galois (hence separable). Characteristic-$p$ examples are where inseparability hides.

q: The primitive element theorem says that every finite separable extension $L/K$ is:
text: The primitive element theorem says that every finite separable extension $L/K$ is: A finite separable extension admits a primitive element: $L=K(\theta)$ for some $\theta\in L$. Such an extension is called simple.

q: The element $\theta=\sqrt{2}+\sqrt{3}$ is primitive for $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2},\sqrt{3})/\mathbb{Q}$. What is the degree of its minimal polynomial over $\mathbb{Q}$?
text: The element $\theta=\sqrt{2}+\sqrt{3}$ is primitive for $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2},\sqrt{3})/\mathbb{Q}$. What is the degree of its minimal polynomial over $\mathbb{Q}$? $[\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2},\sqrt{3}):\mathbb{Q}]=4$, and if $\theta$ is primitive then $[\mathbb{Q}(\theta):\mathbb{Q}]=4$, so $m_\theta$ has degree $4$ (explicitly $x^4-10x^2+1$).

q: Where can the primitive element theorem fail ?
text: Where can the primitive element theorem fail ? Separability is essential. $\mathbb{F}_p(x,y)/\mathbb{F}_p(x^p,y^p)$ has degree $p^2$ but every element generates a subfield of degree at most $p$, so it is not simple.

q: The standard proof that $\mathbb{Q}(\alpha,\beta)/\mathbb{Q}$ has a primitive element (assuming separability) constructs $\theta=\alpha+c\beta$ for almost all $c\in\mathbb{Q}$. Which step correctly identifies why almost every $c$ works?
text: The standard proof that $\mathbb{Q}(\alpha,\beta)/\mathbb{Q}$ has a primitive element (assuming separability) constructs $\theta=\alpha+c\beta$ for almost all $c\in\mathbb{Q}$. Which step correctly identifies why almost every $c$ works? A primitive element $\theta=\alpha+c\beta$ fails when some other root $\alpha_i$ of $m_\alpha$ satisfies $\alpha_i+c\beta_j=\theta$ for some conjugate $\beta_j\ne\beta$. This forces $c=(\alpha-\alpha_i)/(\beta_j-\beta)$. There are finitely many pairs $(i,j)$ with $\beta_j\ne\beta$, giving finitely many bad values of $c$. Since $\mathbb{Q}$ is infinite, good values exist. Collisions among conjugates force $c$ to lie in a finite set.

q: Let $\theta=\sqrt{2}+\sqrt{3}$. Using $m_\theta(x)=x^4-10x^2+1$, verify: what is $m_\theta(\sqrt{2}+\sqrt{3})$ evaluated at $x=\theta$? (Enter the numeric value.)
text: Let $\theta=\sqrt{2}+\sqrt{3}$. Using $m_\theta(x)=x^4-10x^2+1$, verify: what is $m_\theta(\sqrt{2}+\sqrt{3})$ evaluated at $x=\theta$? (Enter the numeric value.) By definition $m_\theta$ is the minimal polynomial of $\theta$, so $m_\theta(\theta)=0$. Explicitly: $(\theta^2)=5+2\sqrt{6}$, $\theta^4=49+20\sqrt{6}$, so $\theta^4-10\theta^2+1=49+20\sqrt{6}-50-20\sqrt{6}+1=0$. Minimal polynomials vanish on their defining element by definition.

q: Which of the following correctly orders the logical steps in proving the Primitive Element Theorem for a finite separable extension $L=K(\alpha,\beta)/K$ when $K$ is infinite?
text: Which of the following correctly orders the logical steps in proving the Primitive Element Theorem for a finite separable extension $L=K(\alpha,\beta)/K$ when $K$ is infinite? The proof: identify bad $c$ values (finitely many), choose a good $c$, set $\theta=\alpha+c\beta$, recover $\beta$ as a root of $\gcd(m_\alpha(\theta-cx),m_\beta(x))$ over $K(\theta)$ (which must be linear since $c$ is good), then get $\alpha=\theta-c\beta\in K(\theta)$. This shows $K(\alpha,\beta)\subseteq K(\theta)$, so $K(\theta)=L$. Avoid bad $c$, then recover $\alpha,\beta$ from $\theta$ via gcd.

title: The multiplicative group (Z/p)×
blurb: For p prime, the nonzero residues (Z/p)× form a cyclic group of order p-1 under multiplication. A primitive root g generates every nonzero residue as g, g^2, ..., g^{p-1} ≡ 1 — the cyclic structure that Legendre symbols and the entire reciprocity machinery rest on.

title: The Legendre symbol
blurb: For odd prime p, the symbol (a/p) detects whether a is a square mod p, taking values 1, -1, or 0.

title: Supplementary laws
blurb: Explicit formulas for (-1/p) and (2/p) via p mod 4 and p mod 8 reduce many residue checks to parity facts.

title: Gauss's lemma
blurb: The sign in (a/p) can be computed by counting how many multiples a,2a,...,(p-1)a/2 land past p/2.

title: Quadratic reciprocity law
blurb: For odd primes p,q: (p/q)(q/p)=(-1)^((p-1)(q-1)/4), exchanging residue problems between primes.

title: The Jacobi symbol
blurb: Extend (a/p) multiplicatively to odd composite n: reciprocity and both supplements survive, yielding a gcd-style O(log n) algorithm with no factoring required.

q: What is the order of the multiplicative group $(\mathbb{Z}/13)^\times$?
text: What is the order of the multiplicative group $(\mathbb{Z}/13)^\times$? For prime $p$, every nonzero residue $1,2,\ldots,p-1$ is a unit, so $|(\mathbb{Z}/p)^\times|=p-1$. With $p=13$ this is $12$.

q: Which of the following is a primitive root mod $7$? (A primitive root generates all six nonzero residues by repeated multiplication.)
text: Which of the following is a primitive root mod $7$? (A primitive root generates all six nonzero residues by repeated multiplication.) $3^1=3, 3^2=2, 3^3=6, 3^4=4, 3^5=5, 3^6=1\pmod 7$ — all six nonzero residues appear, so $3$ has order $6=\varphi(7)$ and is a primitive root. The other candidates have smaller orders ($2,4$ have order $3$; $6$ has order $2$).

q: Compute $3^5 \bmod 7$.
text: Compute $3^5 \bmod 7$. $3^2=9\equiv 2$, $3^4\equiv 2^2=4$, so $3^5\equiv 3\cdot 4=12\equiv 5\pmod 7$. (Equivalently, $3$ is a primitive root mod $7$, and its fifth power lands at $5$.)

q: For odd prime $p$, what does $\left(\frac{a}{p}\right)=1$ mean?
text: For odd prime $p$, what does $\left(\frac{a}{p}\right)=1$ mean? By definition, $\left(\frac{a}{p}\right)=1$ exactly when $x^2\equiv a\pmod p$ is solvable.

q: Compute $\left(\frac{4}{7}\right)$.
text: Compute $\left(\frac{4}{7}\right)$. $2^2\equiv 4\pmod 7$, so 4 is a square mod 7.

q: If $p\nmid a$, then $\left(\frac{a}{p}\right)$ can be:
text: If $p\nmid a$, then $\left(\frac{a}{p}\right)$ can be: Away from multiples of $p$, residues split into squares (1) and nonsquares (-1).

q: Compute $\left(\frac{3}{13}\right)$ using Euler's criterion: evaluate $3^{(13-1)/2}\bmod 13$.
text: Compute $\left(\frac{3}{13}\right)$ using Euler's criterion: evaluate $3^{(13-1)/2}\bmod 13$. $3^6 = 729 = 56\cdot 13 + 1$, so $3^6\equiv 1\pmod{13}$, giving $\left(\frac{3}{13}\right)=1$. Indeed $4^2=16\equiv 3\pmod{13}$. Raise to the $(p-1)/2$ power and reduce mod $p$.

q: Which is a correct counterexample showing $\left(\frac{ab}{p}\right)=\left(\frac{a}{p}\right)\left(\frac{b}{p}\right)$ does NOT require $a,b$ to be coprime to each other?
text: Which is a correct counterexample showing $\left(\frac{ab}{p}\right)=\left(\frac{a}{p}\right)\left(\frac{b}{p}\right)$ does NOT require $a,b$ to be coprime to each other? $(2/7)=1$ since $3^2=9\equiv 2\pmod 7$, and $(8/7)=(1/7)=1$ since $8\equiv 1$. So $(16/7)=(2/7)(8/7)=1$. Multiplicativity of the Legendre symbol holds for all integers $a,b$ coprime to $p$, regardless of $\gcd(a,b)$. Multiplicativity depends only on coprimality to $p$, not between $a,b$.

q: Among $\{1,2,3,4,5,6\}$, how many quadratic residues mod 7 are there?
text: Among $\{1,2,3,4,5,6\}$, how many quadratic residues mod 7 are there? $1^2\equiv 1$, $2^2\equiv 4$, $3^2\equiv 2\pmod 7$; the remaining classes 3, 5, 6 are non-residues. So exactly 3 quadratic residues. Squares mod an odd prime $p$ count $(p-1)/2$.

q: For odd prime $p$, $\left(\frac{-1}{p}\right)=1$ exactly when:
text: For odd prime $p$, $\left(\frac{-1}{p}\right)=1$ exactly when: The first supplementary law gives $(-1/p)=(-1)^{(p-1)/2}$.

q: Compute $\left(\frac{2}{17}\right)$.
text: Compute $\left(\frac{2}{17}\right)$. Since $17\equiv 1\pmod 8$, the second supplementary law gives $(2/17)=1$.

q: Which congruence class gives $\left(\frac{2}{p}\right)=-1$?
text: Which congruence class gives $\left(\frac{2}{p}\right)=-1$? $(2/p)=(-1)^{(p^2-1)/8}$ is negative for classes 3 and 5 mod 8.

q: Which chain of implications correctly derives $(-1/p)=(-1)^{(p-1)/2}$ from Euler's criterion?
text: Which chain of implications correctly derives $(-1/p)=(-1)^{(p-1)/2}$ from Euler's criterion? Euler's criterion states $a^{(p-1)/2}\equiv\left(\frac{a}{p}\right)\pmod p$ for $p\nmid a$. Both $(-1)^{(p-1)/2}$ and $(\frac{-1}{p})$ lie in $\{1,-1\}\subset\mathbb{Z}$, and $p>2$ means $1\not\equiv -1\pmod p$, so the congruence forces equality in $\mathbb{Z}$. Congruence of $\pm 1$ values modulo $p>2$ becomes equality in $\mathbb{Z}$.

q: For which odd prime $p<20$ does $\left(\frac{-1}{p}\right)=-1$ AND $\left(\frac{2}{p}\right)=-1$ simultaneously hold? Give the smallest such prime.
text: For which odd prime $p<20$ does $\left(\frac{-1}{p}\right)=-1$ AND $\left(\frac{2}{p}\right)=-1$ simultaneously hold? Give the smallest such prime. $(-1/p)=-1$ requires $p\equiv 3\pmod 4$; $(2/p)=-1$ requires $p\equiv 3$ or $5\pmod 8$. Intersection: $p\equiv 3\pmod 8$. Smallest such prime below 20 is $p=3$. Intersect the mod-4 and mod-8 conditions.

q: Suppose $p\equiv 7\pmod 8$. Which statement about $\left(\frac{-2}{p}\right)$ is correct?
text: Suppose $p\equiv 7\pmod 8$. Which statement about $\left(\frac{-2}{p}\right)$ is correct? $p\equiv 7\pmod 8$ implies $p\equiv 3\pmod 4$, so $(-1/p)=(-1)^{(p-1)/2}=-1$. Also $p\equiv 7\pmod 8$ gives $(2/p)=1$. By multiplicativity, $(-2/p)=(-1/p)(2/p)=(-1)(1)=-1$. Split as $(-1/p)(2/p)$ using both supplementary laws.

q: Gauss's lemma computes $\left(\frac{a}{p}\right)$ using:
text: Gauss's lemma computes $\left(\frac{a}{p}\right)$ using: It is $(-1)^n$ where $n$ counts how many reduced multiples of $a$ fall in $(p/2,p)$.

q: For $p=11,a=2$, the reduced list from $2,4,6,8,10$ has how many terms $>11/2$?
text: For $p=11,a=2$, the reduced list from $2,4,6,8,10$ has how many terms $>11/2$? The terms are $2,4,6,8,10$; those above 5.5 are 6,8,10.

q: Using the previous count, compute $\left(\frac{2}{11}\right)=(-1)^n$.
