The standard cleanest threshold is $2g + 1$ ⇒ very ample, so 'at least $2g$' is the correct intuition. Of the four options, (a) is the canonical sufficient condition for very ampleness.

q: On a smooth projective curve of genus $g = 5$, what is $\deg K_C$?
text: On a smooth projective curve of genus $g = 5$, what is $\deg K_C$? $\deg K_C = 2g - 2 = 2 \cdot 5 - 2 = 8$.

q: If $C$ is a non-hyperelliptic smooth projective curve of genus $g = 4$, into what projective space $\mathbb{P}^N$ does the canonical map embed $C$? Give $N$.
text: If $C$ is a non-hyperelliptic smooth projective curve of genus $g = 4$, into what projective space $\mathbb{P}^N$ does the canonical map embed $C$? Give $N$. $\varphi_K\colon C \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}^{g-1} = \mathbb{P}^3$ for $g = 4$. So $N = g - 1 = 3$. Canonical map lands in $\mathbb{P}^{g-1}$.

q: A smooth projective curve $C$ of genus $g \ge 3$ is non-hyperelliptic if and only if:
text: A smooth projective curve $C$ of genus $g \ge 3$ is non-hyperelliptic if and only if: Max Noether/Enriques–Babbage: $\varphi_K$ is an embedding ⇔ $C$ is non-hyperelliptic (assuming $g \ge 2$). For hyperelliptic $C$, $\varphi_K$ factors through the $2:1$ map to $\mathbb{P}^1$ followed by the rational normal curve.

q: Which of the following is the correct genus-$3$ canonical model dichotomy?
text: Which of the following is the correct genus-$3$ canonical model dichotomy? For $g = 3$: non-hyperelliptic curves embed as smooth plane quartics ($\deg = 2g-2 = 4$ in $\mathbb{P}^{g-1} = \mathbb{P}^2$); hyperelliptic curves have $\varphi_K$ factoring through $C \xrightarrow{2:1} \mathbb{P}^1 \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}^2$ as a smooth conic. Choice (c) is the genus-4 dichotomy.

q: A smooth canonical curve of genus $g = 6$ in $\mathbb{P}^5$ has degree:
text: A smooth canonical curve of genus $g = 6$ in $\mathbb{P}^5$ has degree: Canonical degree is $2g - 2 = 2 \cdot 6 - 2 = 10$. $\deg K = 2g - 2$.

q: How many branch points does a hyperelliptic cover $\pi\colon C \to \mathbb{P}^1$ have when $C$ has genus $g = 5$?
text: How many branch points does a hyperelliptic cover $\pi\colon C \to \mathbb{P}^1$ have when $C$ has genus $g = 5$? For a hyperelliptic curve of genus $g$, the degree-$2$ cover has $2g + 2$ branch points (Riemann–Hurwitz with $d = 2$, simple ramification: $2g - 2 = 2(-2) + b \Rightarrow b = 2g + 2$). For $g = 5$: $b = 12$. Number of branch points is $2g + 2$.

q: Select all curves which are hyperelliptic. (Assume each is smooth projective over $\bar k$ and the equations are written affinely.)
text: Select all curves which are hyperelliptic. (Assume each is smooth projective over $\bar k$ and the equations are written affinely.) (a) $y^2 = $ (degree 5) is hyperelliptic of genus 2. (c) $y^2 = $ (degree 7) is hyperelliptic of genus 3. (d) every genus-2 curve is hyperelliptic (canonical map is the unique $g^1_2$). The smooth plane quartic in (b) is the canonical model of a non-hyperelliptic genus-3 curve (generic case). Hyperelliptic ⇔ $y^2 = f(x)$ with $f$ separable of degree $2g+1$ or $2g+2$, OR genus $\le 2$.

q: On a hyperelliptic curve of genus $g = 4$ with hyperelliptic involution $\sigma$, how many fixed points does $\sigma$ have on $C$?
text: On a hyperelliptic curve of genus $g = 4$ with hyperelliptic involution $\sigma$, how many fixed points does $\sigma$ have on $C$? Fixed points of $\sigma$ are exactly the ramification points of $\pi\colon C \to \mathbb{P}^1$, which sit one above each branch point. Number = $2g + 2 = 10$. Ramification points = fixed points of involution = $2g + 2$.

q: What is $\dim \mathcal{M}_g$ for $g = 7$?
text: What is $\dim \mathcal{M}_g$ for $g = 7$? For $g \ge 2$, $\dim \mathcal{M}_g = 3g - 3 = 3 \cdot 7 - 3 = 18$. Use $\dim \mathcal{M}_g = 3g - 3$.

q: What is the codimension of the hyperelliptic locus $\mathcal{H}_g \subset \mathcal{M}_g$ for $g = 5$?
text: What is the codimension of the hyperelliptic locus $\mathcal{H}_g \subset \mathcal{M}_g$ for $g = 5$? $\mathcal{H}_g$ has dimension $2g - 1$ inside $\mathcal{M}_g$ of dimension $3g - 3$, so codimension $(3g-3) - (2g-1) = g - 2$. For $g = 5$: codim $= 3$. Codimension is $g - 2$.

q: The Deligne–Mumford compactification $\overline{\mathcal{M}}_g$ adds boundary divisors $\Delta_0, \Delta_1, \ldots, \Delta_{\lfloor g/2 \rfloor}$. The divisor $\Delta_0$ parametrises:
text: The Deligne–Mumford compactification $\overline{\mathcal{M}}_g$ adds boundary divisors $\Delta_0, \Delta_1, \ldots, \Delta_{\lfloor g/2 \rfloor}$. The divisor $\Delta_0$ parametrises: $\Delta_0$ is the boundary divisor of irreducible nodal curves: pinch a non-separating loop in $C$ to a node, get an irreducible curve whose normalisation has genus $g - 1$. The arithmetic genus stays $g$. Pinch a non-separating loop on $C$ — what stays connected?

q: For $g = 5$, how many distinct boundary divisors $\Delta_i$ does $\overline{\mathcal{M}}_g$ have? (Count all $\Delta_i$ with $0 \le i \le \lfloor g/2 \rfloor$.)
text: For $g = 5$, how many distinct boundary divisors $\Delta_i$ does $\overline{\mathcal{M}}_g$ have? (Count all $\Delta_i$ with $0 \le i \le \lfloor g/2 \rfloor$.) Boundary divisors are $\Delta_0, \Delta_1, \Delta_2$ (since $\lfloor 5/2 \rfloor = 2$ and $\Delta_i$ for $i = 0, 1, 2$ are distinct: $\Delta_0$ irreducible nodal, $\Delta_1$ the (1, 4)-split, $\Delta_2$ the (2, 3)-split). Total $= 3$. Count $\lfloor g/2 \rfloor + 1$ for $g$ odd.

q: The codimension of any single boundary divisor $\Delta_i \subset \overline{\mathcal{M}}_g$ is:
text: The codimension of any single boundary divisor $\Delta_i \subset \overline{\mathcal{M}}_g$ is: By definition / construction $\Delta_i$ is a boundary divisor — codimension $1$. Pinching $k$ disjoint cycles produces a stratum of codimension $k$; pinching exactly one cycle (the generic point of $\Delta_i$) produces codim 1.

title: Čech cocycles and cohomology
blurb: Cover-based cocycles detect obstruction classes for global patching.

title: Leray acyclic covers and affine vanishing
blurb: When every finite intersection is $\mathcal{F}$-acyclic, Čech cohomology on the cover equals the derived-functor cohomology. Affine covers of schemes are Leray for quasi-coherent sheaves by Serre's affine vanishing theorem.

title: Long exact sequence in cohomology
blurb: A short exact sequence of sheaves induces a long exact sequence of cohomology groups, with a connecting homomorphism $\delta$ that measures obstructions to lifting sections.

title: Derived functor sheaf cohomology
blurb: Right derived functors of global sections define H^i(X,F) abstractly.

title: Picard groups and Serre duality signals
blurb: Line bundles, extensions, and duality theorems are naturally cohomological.

q: Given a cover $\mathcal{U}=\{U_\alpha\}$ of $X$ and a sheaf $\mathcal{F}$, a $1$-cocycle is a family $\{t_{\alpha\beta}\in\mathcal{F}(U_{\alpha\beta})\}$ satisfying which identity on $U_{\alpha\beta\gamma}$?
text: Given a cover $\mathcal{U}=\{U_\alpha\}$ of $X$ and a sheaf $\mathcal{F}$, a $1$-cocycle is a family $\{t_{\alpha\beta}\in\mathcal{F}(U_{\alpha\beta})\}$ satisfying which identity on $U_{\alpha\beta\gamma}$? The alternating Čech differential gives $(d t)_{\alpha\beta\gamma}=t_{\beta\gamma}-t_{\alpha\gamma}+t_{\alpha\beta}$; setting it to zero is the cocycle condition on triple overlaps.

q: Why is $\check{H}^0(\mathcal{U},\mathcal{F})=\mathcal{F}(X)$?
text: Why is $\check{H}^0(\mathcal{U},\mathcal{F})=\mathcal{F}(X)$? A $0$-cochain is $\{s_\alpha\in\mathcal{F}(U_\alpha)\}$; $(ds)_{\alpha\beta}=s_\beta-s_\alpha=0$ forces the $s_\alpha$ to glue by the sheaf axiom, yielding exactly a global section.

q: For the circle $S^1$ with the constant sheaf $\underline{\mathbb{Z}}$ and a standard good cover by two overlapping arcs whose intersection has two connected components, compute $\dim_{\mathbb{Q}} (\check{H}^1(S^1,\underline{\mathbb{Z}})\otimes\mathbb{Q})$.
text: For the circle $S^1$ with the constant sheaf $\underline{\mathbb{Z}}$ and a standard good cover by two overlapping arcs whose intersection has two connected components, compute $\dim_{\mathbb{Q}} (\check{H}^1(S^1,\underline{\mathbb{Z}})\otimes\mathbb{Q})$. $\check{H}^1(S^1,\underline{\mathbb{Z}})\cong\mathbb{Z}$ (the winding-number class), so rationally it is $1$-dimensional.

q: For the torus $T^2$ with the constant sheaf $\underline{\mathbb{Z}}$, which statement about a cover by two open sets is correct?
text: For the torus $T^2$ with the constant sheaf $\underline{\mathbb{Z}}$, which statement about a cover by two open sets is correct? Two contractible open sets $U,V$ covering $T^2$ must have $U\cap V\simeq S^1\sqcup S^1$ (not contractible), so the cover fails the Leray acyclicity condition on intersections. This is precisely why a good cover (all intersections contractible) is required for Čech to recover sheaf cohomology. A two-set cover cannot capture $H^2(T^2) = \mathbb{Z}$ but can catch $H^1$.

q: For the cover of $S^2$ by two contractible open hemispheres $U,V$ with $U\cap V\simeq S^1$, compute $\dim_{\mathbb{Q}}\check{H}^1(\mathcal{U},\underline{\mathbb{Q}})$ directly from the Čech complex $\check{C}^0\to\check{C}^1$.
text: For the cover of $S^2$ by two contractible open hemispheres $U,V$ with $U\cap V\simeq S^1$, compute $\dim_{\mathbb{Q}}\check{H}^1(\mathcal{U},\underline{\mathbb{Q}})$ directly from the Čech complex $\check{C}^0\to\check{C}^1$. $\check{C}^0=\underline{\mathbb{Q}}(U)\oplus\underline{\mathbb{Q}}(V)\cong\mathbb{Q}^2$ and $\check{C}^1=\underline{\mathbb{Q}}(U\cap V)$. Since $U\cap V\simeq S^1$, $H^0(U\cap V,\underline{\mathbb{Q}})\cong\mathbb{Q}$. The coboundary $d\colon\mathbb{Q}^2\to\mathbb{Q}$, $(a,b)\mapsto b-a$, is surjective with kernel $\mathbb{Q}$, giving $\ker/\mathrm{im}=0$. So $\check{H}^1=0$, consistent with $H^1(S^2)=0$. Use $H^1(S^1) = \mathbb{Q}$ for the overlap.

q: Which is the correct order of steps to verify that a Čech cocycle $\{t_{\alpha\beta}\}$ is a coboundary?
text: Which is the correct order of steps to verify that a Čech cocycle $\{t_{\alpha\beta}\}$ is a coboundary? A $1$-cochain $\{t_{\alpha\beta}\}$ is a coboundary iff there exist local sections $s_\alpha\in\mathcal{F}(U_\alpha)$ with $t_{\alpha\beta}=s_\beta|_{U_{\alpha\beta}}-s_\alpha|_{U_{\alpha\beta}}$. Exhibiting explicit $s_\alpha$ is the direct verification; the cocycle condition is necessary but not sufficient. Trivialize on a refinement to exhibit the coboundary.

q: A cover $\mathcal{U} = \{U_\alpha\}$ of $X$ is Leray for a sheaf $\mathcal{F}$ when:
text: A cover $\mathcal{U} = \{U_\alpha\}$ of $X$ is Leray for a sheaf $\mathcal{F}$ when: The Leray condition asks that $\mathcal{F}$ be acyclic on every finite intersection of the cover. When satisfied, the canonical map $\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U},\mathcal{F}) \to H^p(X,\mathcal{F})$ is an isomorphism for every $p$.

q: Which of the following guarantees that an affine open cover of a scheme is Leray for any quasi-coherent sheaf?
text: Which of the following guarantees that an affine open cover of a scheme is Leray for any quasi-coherent sheaf? Serre proved that affine schemes have no higher quasi-coherent cohomology, so every finite intersection of affine opens (also affine when the scheme is separated) is $\mathcal{F}$-acyclic. Hence an affine open cover is Leray.

q: The standard affine cover of $\mathbb{P}^2$ has three charts $U_0,U_1,U_2$. How many terms does the resulting Čech complex $\check C^0 \to \check C^1 \to \check C^2$ have in $\check C^1$?
text: The standard affine cover of $\mathbb{P}^2$ has three charts $U_0,U_1,U_2$. How many terms does the resulting Čech complex $\check C^0 \to \check C^1 \to \check C^2$ have in $\check C^1$? $\check C^1$ indexes strictly-increasing pairs $(\alpha_0,\alpha_1)$ from $\{0,1,2\}$, so its rank is $\binom{3}{2} = 3$. (Triple intersections contribute $\binom{3}{3}=1$ term in $\check C^2$.)

q: Let $X=\mathbb{P}^1_k$ with affine cover $U_0=\{x_0\ne 0\}$, $U_1=\{x_1\ne 0\}$, and $\mathcal{F}=\mathcal{O}(-3)$. Which statement correctly explains why this cover is Leray for $\mathcal{O}(-3)$?
text: Let $X=\mathbb{P}^1_k$ with affine cover $U_0=\{x_0\ne 0\}$, $U_1=\{x_1\ne 0\}$, and $\mathcal{F}=\mathcal{O}(-3)$. Which statement correctly explains why this cover is Leray for $\mathcal{O}(-3)$? Each chart and their intersection are affine schemes. Serre's vanishing theorem for quasi-coherent sheaves on affine schemes ensures $H^i>0$ vanishes on each piece, making the cover Leray and the Čech computation exact. On affine opens, quasi-coherent cohomology vanishes in positive degree.

q: Using the Leray cover $\{U_0,U_1\}$ of $\mathbb{P}^2$ (standard affine cover by three charts), the Čech complex for a quasi-coherent sheaf has terms in degrees $0,1,2$. How many terms does $\check{C}^2$ have?
text: Using the Leray cover $\{U_0,U_1\}$ of $\mathbb{P}^2$ (standard affine cover by three charts), the Čech complex for a quasi-coherent sheaf has terms in degrees $0,1,2$. How many terms does $\check{C}^2$ have? $\check{C}^2$ indexes strictly-increasing triples from $\{0,1,2\}$: only $(0,1,2)$, giving $\binom{3}{3}=1$ term. Check that triple intersections are affine.

q: If $\mathcal{U}$ is a Leray cover for $\mathcal{F}$ but NOT for $\mathcal{G}$, and there is a map $\mathcal{F}\to\mathcal{G}$, which conclusion is valid?
text: If $\mathcal{U}$ is a Leray cover for $\mathcal{F}$ but NOT for $\mathcal{G}$, and there is a map $\mathcal{F}\to\mathcal{G}$, which conclusion is valid? The Leray isomorphism $\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U},\mathcal{F})\cong H^p(X,\mathcal{F})$ applies sheaf-by-sheaf; $\mathcal{F}$ satisfies the hypothesis but $\mathcal{G}$ does not, so the Čech-to-derived comparison holds for $\mathcal{F}$ and may fail for $\mathcal{G}$. Spectral sequence mismatch breaks Leray calculation for $\mathcal{G}$.

q: A short exact sequence of sheaves $0\to\mathcal{F}'\to\mathcal{F}\to\mathcal{F}''\to 0$ produces a long exact sequence whose connecting homomorphism $\delta$ goes in which direction?
text: A short exact sequence of sheaves $0\to\mathcal{F}'\to\mathcal{F}\to\mathcal{F}''\to 0$ produces a long exact sequence whose connecting homomorphism $\delta$ goes in which direction? The connecting map raises degree by one and jumps from the quotient sheaf back to the subsheaf: $\delta\colon H^p(\mathcal{F}'') \to H^{p+1}(\mathcal{F}')$. In Čech language it lifts a local section of $\mathcal{F}''$ to one of $\mathcal{F}$; the failure of the lifts to glue is a class in $H^{p+1}(\mathcal{F}')$.

q: In the exponential sequence $0 \to \underline{\mathbb{Z}} \to \mathcal{O} \to \mathcal{O}^\times \to 0$ on $\mathbb{C}^\times$, what does $\delta\colon H^0(\mathcal{O}^\times) \to H^1(\underline{\mathbb{Z}})$ send the global section $z$ to?
text: In the exponential sequence $0 \to \underline{\mathbb{Z}} \to \mathcal{O} \to \mathcal{O}^\times \to 0$ on $\mathbb{C}^\times$, what does $\delta\colon H^0(\mathcal{O}^\times) \to H^1(\underline{\mathbb{Z}})$ send the global section $z$ to? On $\mathbb{C}^\times$ the global section $z$ has no single-valued logarithm, so its image under $\delta$ is non-trivial. Concretely the class is $1\in H^1(\mathbb{C}^\times,\underline{\mathbb{Z}}) = \mathbb{Z}$, the winding-number generator.

q: From $0\to\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{P}^1}(-1)\to\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{P}^1}\to\mathcal{O}_p\to 0$ (skyscraper at a point) and the vanishing $H^i(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{P}^1}(-1))=0$ for all $i$, deduce $\dim H^0(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}_p)$.
text: From $0\to\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{P}^1}(-1)\to\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{P}^1}\to\mathcal{O}_p\to 0$ (skyscraper at a point) and the vanishing $H^i(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{P}^1}(-1))=0$ for all $i$, deduce $\dim H^0(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}_p)$. The sandwiching $H^i(\mathcal{O}(-1)) = 0$ forces $H^0(\mathcal{O}) \xrightarrow{\sim} H^0(\mathcal{O}_p)$. Since $\dim H^0(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}) = 1$, we get $\dim H^0(\mathcal{O}_p) = 1$ — the stalk at $p$.

q: From the exponential sequence $0\to\underline{\mathbb{Z}}\to\mathcal{O}_X\to\mathcal{O}_X^\times\to 0$ on a compact Riemann surface $X$, the connecting map $\delta\colon H^1(X,\mathcal{O}_X^\times)\to H^2(X,\underline{\mathbb{Z}})$ sends a line bundle $L$ to which invariant?
text: From the exponential sequence $0\to\underline{\mathbb{Z}}\to\mathcal{O}_X\to\mathcal{O}_X^\times\to 0$ on a compact Riemann surface $X$, the connecting map $\delta\colon H^1(X,\mathcal{O}_X^\times)\to H^2(X,\underline{\mathbb{Z}})$ sends a line bundle $L$ to which invariant? The connecting homomorphism $\delta$ in the long exact sequence of the exponential sequence sends a holomorphic line bundle (class in $H^1(\mathcal{O}^\times)=\mathrm{Pic}(X)$) to its first Chern class in $H^2(X,\underline{\mathbb{Z}})\cong\mathbb{Z}$, which equals the degree of the line bundle. Extract $H^1(X, \mathbb{Z}) \to H^1(X, \mathcal{O}) \to H^1(X, \mathcal{O}^\times) \to H^2(X, \mathbb{Z})$.

q: On $\mathbb{P}^1$, apply the long exact sequence of $0\to\mathcal{O}(-1)\to\mathcal{O}^{\oplus 2}\to\mathcal{O}(1)\to 0$ (Euler sequence twisted). Given $\dim H^0(\mathcal{O})=1$, $\dim H^0(\mathcal{O}(-1))=0$, $\dim H^1(\mathcal{O}(-1))=0$, compute $\dim H^0(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}(1))$.
text: On $\mathbb{P}^1$, apply the long exact sequence of $0\to\mathcal{O}(-1)\to\mathcal{O}^{\oplus 2}\to\mathcal{O}(1)\to 0$ (Euler sequence twisted). Given $\dim H^0(\mathcal{O})=1$, $\dim H^0(\mathcal{O}(-1))=0$, $\dim H^1(\mathcal{O}(-1))=0$, compute $\dim H^0(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}(1))$. From the long exact sequence: $0\to H^0(\mathcal{O}(-1))\to H^0(\mathcal{O})^2\to H^0(\mathcal{O}(1))\to H^1(\mathcal{O}(-1))\to\cdots$. Since the flanking terms vanish, the middle map is an isomorphism followed by a surjection: $\dim H^0(\mathcal{O}(1))=2\cdot 1=2$. Apply $H^0$ and use the Euler exact sequence.

q: Which is the correct order of steps to construct the connecting homomorphism $\delta\colon H^p(X,\mathcal{F}'')\to H^{p+1}(X,\mathcal{F}')$ from a SES $0\to\mathcal{F}'\to\mathcal{F}\to\mathcal{F}''\to 0$?
text: Which is the correct order of steps to construct the connecting homomorphism $\delta\colon H^p(X,\mathcal{F}'')\to H^{p+1}(X,\mathcal{F}')$ from a SES $0\to\mathcal{F}'\to\mathcal{F}\to\mathcal{F}''\to 0$? The standard construction: embed each sheaf in an injective sheaf, extend to a SES of injective resolutions (which splits in each degree by injectivity of $I'^p$), apply $\Gamma$, and apply the snake lemma to the resulting SES of cochain complexes. The resulting boundary map on cohomology is $\delta$. Zig-zag in the snake lemma gives $\delta$.

q: Which property of the global-sections functor $\Gamma(X,-)$ is measured by the derived functors $R^i\Gamma$?
text: Which property of the global-sections functor $\Gamma(X,-)$ is measured by the derived functors $R^i\Gamma$? $\Gamma(X,-)$ is left exact but not right exact; $R^i\Gamma$ measures exactly the right-exactness defect via a long exact sequence.

q: Which class of sheaves is NOT automatically $\Gamma$-acyclic?
text: Which class of sheaves is NOT automatically $\Gamma$-acyclic? Injective, flabby, and soft (on paracompact Hausdorff) sheaves are all $\Gamma$-acyclic. A locally constant sheaf like $\underline{\mathbb{Z}}$ on $S^1$ has nonzero $H^1$, so it is not acyclic in general.

q: On $\mathbb{P}^1_k$ the line bundle $\mathcal{O}(-5)$ has $h^0=0$. Using $\dim_k H^1(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}(n))=\max(-n-1,0)$, compute $\dim_k H^1(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}(-5))$.
text: On $\mathbb{P}^1_k$ the line bundle $\mathcal{O}(-5)$ has $h^0=0$. Using $\dim_k H^1(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}(n))=\max(-n-1,0)$, compute $\dim_k H^1(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}(-5))$. $\max(-(-5)-1,0)=\max(4,0)=4$.

q: Which of these sheaves on $S^1$ is NOT $\Gamma$-acyclic, and why?
text: Which of these sheaves on $S^1$ is NOT $\Gamma$-acyclic, and why? The constant sheaf $\underline{\mathbb{Z}}$ on $S^1$ has $H^1(S^1,\underline{\mathbb{Z}})\cong\mathbb{Z}$, witnessing the winding-number class. Flabby and injective sheaves are acyclic by construction; skyscraper sheaves at a point have $H^i=0$ for $i>0$ since points are acyclic. Constant sheaves on $S^1$ have nontrivial $H^1$ detecting the loop.

q: For $\mathbb{P}^2_k$, the formula $\dim H^1(\mathbb{P}^2,\mathcal{O}(n))=0$ for all $n$. Using this and the exact sequence $0\to\mathcal{O}(-1)\to\mathcal{O}\to\mathcal{O}_H\to 0$ (restriction to a hyperplane $H\cong\mathbb{P}^1$), compute $\dim H^0(\mathbb{P}^2,\mathcal{O})$ given $\dim H^0(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O})=1$.
text: For $\mathbb{P}^2_k$, the formula $\dim H^1(\mathbb{P}^2,\mathcal{O}(n))=0$ for all $n$. Using this and the exact sequence $0\to\mathcal{O}(-1)\to\mathcal{O}\to\mathcal{O}_H\to 0$ (restriction to a hyperplane $H\cong\mathbb{P}^1$), compute $\dim H^0(\mathbb{P}^2,\mathcal{O})$ given $\dim H^0(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O})=1$. The long exact sequence gives $0\to H^0(\mathcal{O}(-1))\to H^0(\mathcal{O})\to H^0(\mathcal{O}_H)\to H^1(\mathcal{O}(-1))\to\cdots$. Since $H^0(\mathcal{O}(-1))=0$ and $H^1(\mathcal{O}(-1))=0$, we get $H^0(\mathbb{P}^2,\mathcal{O})\cong H^0(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O})=1$. Combine short exact sequence with vanishing of $H^1(\mathcal{O}(n))$.

q: Why does the global-sections functor $\Gamma(X,-)$ fail to be right exact on $\mathbf{Ab}(X)$ (abelian sheaves on $X$)?
text: Why does the global-sections functor $\Gamma(X,-)$ fail to be right exact on $\mathbf{Ab}(X)$ (abelian sheaves on $X$)? A morphism of sheaves is surjective iff it is surjective on stalks. Global sections need not be surjective: the exponential $\mathcal{O}\to\mathcal{O}^\times$ is locally surjective (every holomorphic function has local logarithms), but $z\in H^0(\mathbb{C}^\times,\mathcal{O}^\times)$ has no global logarithm, so $H^0(\mathcal{O})\to H^0(\mathcal{O}^\times)$ is not surjective. Sections functor is only left exact without injective resolutions.

q: The Picard group of a ringed space $(X,\mathcal{O}_X)$ is canonically isomorphic to which Čech cohomology group?
text: The Picard group of a ringed space $(X,\mathcal{O}_X)$ is canonically isomorphic to which Čech cohomology group? Line bundles are classified by their transition cocycles in $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ modulo coboundaries: $\mathrm{Pic}(X)\cong\check{H}^1(X,\mathcal{O}_X^\times)$.

q: On $\mathbb{P}^1$ with $\omega_{\mathbb{P}^1}=\mathcal{O}(-2)$, Serre duality gives $\dim H^1(\mathcal{O}(-7))=\dim H^0(\mathcal{O}(7-2))$. Compute this common dimension.
text: On $\mathbb{P}^1$ with $\omega_{\mathbb{P}^1}=\mathcal{O}(-2)$, Serre duality gives $\dim H^1(\mathcal{O}(-7))=\dim H^0(\mathcal{O}(7-2))$. Compute this common dimension. $\dim H^0(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}(5))=5+1=6$, and $\max(-(-7)-1,0)=6$, so the duality check holds.

q: Use Riemann–Roch on a smooth projective curve of genus $g=4$ together with Serre duality to compute $\dim H^0(C,L)-\dim H^0(C,\omega_C\otimes L^{-1})$ for a line bundle $L$ of degree $d=10$.
text: Use Riemann–Roch on a smooth projective curve of genus $g=4$ together with Serre duality to compute $\dim H^0(C,L)-\dim H^0(C,\omega_C\otimes L^{-1})$ for a line bundle $L$ of degree $d=10$. Riemann–Roch: $h^0(L)-h^1(L)=d-g+1=10-4+1=7$, and Serre duality identifies $h^1(L)=h^0(\omega_C\otimes L^{-1})$.

q: On an elliptic curve $E$ (genus $1$, $\omega_E\cong\mathcal{O}_E$), Serre duality gives $H^1(E,L)\cong H^0(E,L^{-1})^\vee$. For a degree-$3$ line bundle $L$, which statement is correct?
text: On an elliptic curve $E$ (genus $1$, $\omega_E\cong\mathcal{O}_E$), Serre duality gives $H^1(E,L)\cong H^0(E,L^{-1})^\vee$. For a degree-$3$ line bundle $L$, which statement is correct? Serre duality: $H^1(E,L)\cong H^0(E,\omega_E\otimes L^{-1})^\vee = H^0(E,L^{-1})^\vee$. Since $L^{-1}$ has degree $-3<0$, $H^0(E,L^{-1})=0$, so $\dim H^1(E,L)=0$. Then Riemann–Roch gives $\dim H^0(E,L)=\deg L -g+1=3$. Serre duality gives $H^1 \cong H^0$-dual for self-dual canonical.

q: For the projective line $\mathbb{P}^1$ and the line bundle $\mathcal{O}(n)$, Serre duality says $h^1(\mathcal{O}(n))=h^0(\mathcal{O}(-n-2))$. Compute $h^1(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}(-4))$ using this duality.
text: For the projective line $\mathbb{P}^1$ and the line bundle $\mathcal{O}(n)$, Serre duality says $h^1(\mathcal{O}(n))=h^0(\mathcal{O}(-n-2))$. Compute $h^1(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}(-4))$ using this duality. $h^1(\mathcal{O}(-4))=h^0(\mathcal{O}(-(-4)-2))=h^0(\mathcal{O}(2))=2+1=3$. Compute $h^1(\mathcal{O}(n)) = h^0(\mathcal{O}(-n-2))$.

q: The Picard group $\mathrm{Pic}(\mathbb{P}^1_k)\cong\mathbb{Z}$ is generated by $\mathcal{O}(1)$. The exponential sequence on a smooth projective curve $C$ over $\mathbb{C}$ gives an exact sequence involving $\mathrm{Pic}^0(C)$. Which statement correctly identifies $\mathrm{Pic}^0(C)$?
text: The Picard group $\mathrm{Pic}(\mathbb{P}^1_k)\cong\mathbb{Z}$ is generated by $\mathcal{O}(1)$. The exponential sequence on a smooth projective curve $C$ over $\mathbb{C}$ gives an exact sequence involving $\mathrm{Pic}^0(C)$. Which statement correctly identifies $\mathrm{Pic}^0(C)$? From the exponential sequence, the degree-zero line bundles form $\mathrm{Pic}^0(C)=\ker(c_1)$. The long exact sequence gives $H^1(C,\mathcal{O})\to H^1(C,\mathcal{O}^\times)\xrightarrow{c_1} H^2(C,\mathbb{Z})$, and $\mathrm{Pic}^0(C)\cong H^1(C,\mathcal{O})/\mathrm{image}(H^1(C,\underline{\mathbb{Z}}))$, a $g$-dimensional complex torus — the Jacobian of $C$. Pic of $\mathbb{P}^1$ is detected by $H^1$ of structure sheaf cocycles.

title: Kähler differentials $\Omega^1_{X/k}$
blurb: $\Omega^1_{X/k}$ is the universal recipient of $k$-derivations — algebraic 1-forms on $X$. For $X$ smooth of dimension $n$, $\Omega^1_{X/k}$ is locally free of rank $n$ and $\Omega^p_{X/k} := \bigwedge^p \Omega^1_{X/k}$ encodes algebraic $p$-forms.

title: The algebraic de Rham complex $\Omega^\bullet_{X/k}$
blurb: The complex $\Omega^0_{X/k} \xrightarrow{d} \Omega^1_{X/k} \xrightarrow{d} \Omega^2_{X/k} \to \cdots$ has differential given by exterior derivative. Its hypercohomology is the algebraic de Rham cohomology $H^*_{dR}(X/k)$ — a purely algebraic invariant.

title: Comparison with Betti cohomology
blurb: For a smooth complex variety $X$, Grothendieck's comparison theorem identifies $H^*_{dR}(X/\mathbb{C})$ with the singular cohomology $H^*(X(\mathbb{C}), \mathbb{C})$. The algebraic side computes the same invariant — without any analytic input.

title: The Hodge filtration $F^\bullet$
blurb: $F^p H^n_{dR}(X) = \mathrm{im}\,H^n(\sigma^{\ge p} \Omega^\bullet_{X/k})$ is the decreasing filtration coming from the trivial truncation of the de Rham complex. For $X$ smooth projective over $\mathbb{C}$, the associated graded is $\bigoplus_p H^{n-p}(X, \Omega^p_X)$ — the Hodge decomposition.

title: Hodge numbers $h^{p,q}(X)$
blurb: $h^{p,q}(X) = \dim H^q(X, \Omega^p_X)$ is a sheaf-cohomology dimension — but for $X$ smooth projective over $\mathbb{C}$ they assemble into a Hodge diamond reflecting Serre duality and complex conjugation symmetry. Topological data refining the Betti numbers.

title: Worked example: de Rham of a smooth curve
blurb: For a smooth projective curve $C$ of genus $g$ over $\mathbb{C}$: $H^0_{dR}(C) = \mathbb{C}$, $H^2_{dR}(C) = \mathbb{C}$ (canonical class), and $H^1_{dR}(C)$ has dimension $2g$ split as $H^0(C, \Omega^1) \oplus H^1(C, \mathcal{O})$ by the Hodge filtration.

q: Let $A = k[x, y]/(y^2 - x^3 - x)$ with $\mathrm{char}\,k \ne 2$ — the affine model of an elliptic curve. What is $\Omega^1_{A/k}$ as an $A$-module?
text: Let $A = k[x, y]/(y^2 - x^3 - x)$ with $\mathrm{char}\,k \ne 2$ — the affine model of an elliptic curve. What is $\Omega^1_{A/k}$ as an $A$-module? The conormal sequence reads $(f) / (f^2) \to A\,dx \oplus A\,dy \to \Omega^1_{A/k} \to 0$ where $f = y^2 - x^3 - x$ and the first map sends $f \mapsto df = 2y\,dy - (3x^2 + 1)\,dx$. Since the curve is smooth (the gradient $(\partial_x f, \partial_y f) = (-3x^2 - 1, 2y)$ is nowhere zero), the resulting rank-1 module is locally free: choose either $dx$ or $dy$ as the local generator depending on which partial derivative is invertible. Smoothness of the curve says the gradient of $f$ is nowhere zero — exactly when $\Omega^1$ is locally free of rank $\dim X = 1$.

q: Which property characterises the universal $k$-derivation $d\colon A \to \Omega^1_{A/k}$?
text: Which property characterises the universal $k$-derivation $d\colon A \to \Omega^1_{A/k}$? The universal property says $\Hom_A(\Omega^1_{A/k}, M) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathrm{Der}_k(A, M)$ via $\varphi \mapsto \varphi \circ d$. This is what makes $\Omega^1_{A/k}$ the 'right' module of differentials — every other $k$-derivation factors through it uniquely. Note $d$ is not a ring homomorphism (Leibniz, not multiplicativity), and $d$ is rarely surjective. The universal property is a Hom-isomorphism: $\Omega^1$ represents the functor $\mathrm{Der}_k(A, -)$.

q: On $X = \mathbb{A}^2_k = \Spec\,k[x, y]$, what is $\Omega^2_{X/k}$?
text: On $X = \mathbb{A}^2_k = \Spec\,k[x, y]$, what is $\Omega^2_{X/k}$? $\Omega^p_{X/k} = \bigwedge^p \Omega^1_{X/k}$. Here $\Omega^1$ is free of rank $2$ on $dx, dy$, so $\Omega^2 = \bigwedge^2$ is free of rank $\binom{2}{2} = 1$, generated by $dx \wedge dy$. The top form $\Omega^n$ on $\mathbb{A}^n$ is always rank 1 — it's the canonical bundle. $\Omega^p$ is the $p$-th exterior power of $\Omega^1$. Rank: $\binom{n}{p}$ when $\dim X = n$.

q: Why is $d^2 = 0$ on the algebraic de Rham complex $\Omega^\bullet_{X/k}$?
text: Why is $d^2 = 0$ on the algebraic de Rham complex $\Omega^\bullet_{X/k}$? $(\Omega^\bullet, d)$ is a chain complex by construction: $d$ is built so that $d(da) = 0$ on 1-forms, and the Leibniz rule $d(\alpha \wedge \beta) = d\alpha \wedge \beta + (-1)^{|\alpha|} \alpha \wedge d\beta$ propagates this. Compute $d^2(a\,db) = d(da \wedge db) = d(da) \wedge db - da \wedge d(db) = 0$. This is purely algebraic — works over any base, smooth or singular. The proof of $d^2 = 0$ is the same algebraic identity as in smooth de Rham — no smoothness or characteristic assumption.

q: What is $H^*_{dR}(\mathbb{A}^1_k / k)$ for $k$ a field of characteristic 0?
text: What is $H^*_{dR}(\mathbb{A}^1_k / k)$ for $k$ a field of characteristic 0? On $\mathbb{A}^1 = \Spec\,k[x]$, the complex is $k[x] \xrightarrow{f \mapsto f' dx} k[x]\,dx$. In char 0, $d/dx$ has kernel $k$ (the constants) and is surjective (every $g\,dx$ is $d$ of $\int g$). So $H^0 = k$ and $H^1 = 0$, matching $\mathbb{A}^1(\mathbb{C}) \simeq \mathbb{C}$ being contractible. Every polynomial $g(x)$ has an antiderivative in $k[x]$; only constants are in the kernel of $d/dx$.

q: Why does computing $H^n_{dR}$ generally require hyper cohomology rather than cohomology of global sections?
text: Why does computing $H^n_{dR}$ generally require hyper cohomology rather than cohomology of global sections? Hypercohomology is the right derived functor of $\Gamma$ applied to a complex; it incorporates both the de Rham differential $d$ and the sheaf cohomology of each term $\Omega^p$. On affine $X$ each $\Omega^p$ is acyclic so the two coincide; on $\mathbb{P}^1$ the term $\Omega^1$ has $H^1(\mathbb{P}^1, \Omega^1) = \mathbb{C}$ which contributes the $H^2_{dR}(\mathbb{P}^1) = \mathbb{C}$. On $\mathbb{P}^1$, the global sections $\Gamma(\mathbb{P}^1, \Omega^1) = 0$ would falsely suggest $H^*_{dR}$ has nothing in degree 2 — but $H^2_{dR}(\mathbb{P}^1) = \mathbb{C}$.

q: On $X = \mathbb{A}^1_k$ over a field $k$ of characteristic $p > 0$, what is $H^1_{dR}(X/k)$?
text: On $X = \mathbb{A}^1_k$ over a field $k$ of characteristic $p > 0$, what is $H^1_{dR}(X/k)$? In characteristic $p$, $d(x^p) = p x^{p-1}\,dx = 0$, so $x^{p-1}\,dx$ is closed but not exact. More generally $x^{kp - 1}\,dx$ for $k \ge 1$ is in the cokernel of $d$. The kernel of $d$ also expands to include all $x^{kp}$ (so $H^0_{dR}$ is infinite-dimensional too). This is one motivation for the Cartier operator and for crystalline cohomology — algebraic de Rham fails to compute the 'right' answer on affine varieties in positive characteristic. What is $d(x^p)$ in characteristic $p$? Now think about the cokernel of $d/dx$ as a $k$-vector space.

q: For $X$ smooth proper over $\mathbb{C}$, the Hodge-to-de-Rham spectral sequence $E_1^{p,q} = H^q(X, \Omega^p) \Rightarrow H^{p+q}_{dR}(X)$ degenerates at $E_1$. Which of the following is an immediate consequence?
text: For $X$ smooth proper over $\mathbb{C}$, the Hodge-to-de-Rham spectral sequence $E_1^{p,q} = H^q(X, \Omega^p) \Rightarrow H^{p+q}_{dR}(X)$ degenerates at $E_1$. Which of the following is an immediate consequence? $E_1$-degeneration means $E_1 = E_\infty$, so for each $n$, $\sum_{p + q = n} \dim E_1^{p, q} = \dim H^n_{dR}(X/\mathbb{C})$ — i.e. the Betti numbers split into Hodge components. Torsion-freeness of integral cohomology is a separate (and not always true) statement; $\Omega^p$ is generically not acyclic on a proper variety; rationality has no relation to $E_1$-degeneration. $E_1$-degeneration is exactly the statement that the alternating sum becomes a sum: dimensions add along antidiagonals.

q: Grothendieck's algebraic-de-Rham comparison theorem applies to which of the following?
text: Grothendieck's algebraic-de-Rham comparison theorem applies to which of the following? Grothendieck's theorem is for $X$ smooth of finite type over $\mathbb{C}$. Properness is not required (in fact the theorem is most striking for affine varieties like $\mathbb{C}^*$). For singular $X$ one needs $H$-modules or mixed Hodge complexes. Over fields other than $\mathbb{C}$ the right-hand side has to be reinterpreted (e.g. étale, crystalline) — there is no 'singular cohomology with $k$-coefficients' for $k = \overline{\mathbb{F}_p}$. The theorem needs analytic input (Poincaré lemma + GAGA), so the base must be $\mathbb{C}$. Smoothness — but not properness — is essential.

q: What is $H^1_{dR}(\mathbb{C}^* / \mathbb{C})$, and what is a generator?
text: What is $H^1_{dR}(\mathbb{C}^* / \mathbb{C})$, and what is a generator? $\mathbb{C}^* = \Spec\,\mathbb{C}[z, z^{-1}]$. The differential $z^n \mapsto n z^{n-1}\,dz$ has image spanning every $z^m\,dz$ except $m = -1$, so $H^1_{dR} = \mathbb{C}\cdot\frac{dz}{z}$. The form $dz$ is exact ($= d(z)$) so it represents the zero class. Affine varieties absolutely can have higher de Rham cohomology — that's the point of $\mathbb{C}^*$ as a witness. Algebraic 1-forms on $\mathbb{C}^*$ are $\sum a_n z^n\,dz$. Which one fails to be the differential of a Laurent polynomial?

q: The integration pairing $\int_\gamma \frac{dz}{z}$ where $\gamma$ is the unit circle in $\mathbb{C}^*$ gives:
text: The integration pairing $\int_\gamma \frac{dz}{z}$ where $\gamma$ is the unit circle in $\mathbb{C}^*$ gives: $\int_\gamma \frac{dz}{z} = 2\pi i$ by the residue theorem. The pairing $H^1_{dR}(\mathbb{C}^*/\mathbb{C}) \times H_1(\mathbb{C}^*; \mathbb{Q}) \to \mathbb{C}$ is the comparison isomorphism for $\mathbb{C}^*$ over $\mathbb{Q}$: the algebraic class $[\frac{dz}{z}] \in H^1_{dR}(\mathbb{C}^*/\mathbb{Q})$ pairs with the topological class $[\gamma] \in H_1$ to give the period $2\pi i$. Residue theorem: $\int_{|z|=1} \frac{dz}{z} = 2\pi i \cdot \mathrm{Res}_{z=0}(1/z)$.

q: Match each gadget on the left with the gadget on the right that it equals (for $X$ smooth proper over $\mathbb{C}$, by $E_1$-degeneration of the Hodge spectral sequence).
text: Match each gadget on the left with the gadget on the right that it equals (for $X$ smooth proper over $\mathbb{C}$, by $E_1$-degeneration of the Hodge spectral sequence). By definition, $\mathrm{gr}^p_F H^n = F^p / F^{p+1}$ (so the first two entries match the same right entry — but the question is structural: both equal $H^{n-p}(X, \Omega^p)$ once you accept $E_1$-degeneration). $H^q(X, \Omega^p) = H^{p, q}$ by the Dolbeault-style notation, with dimension $h^{p, q}$. Note: a clean Matching uses each right entry once — the intent here is the chain $\mathrm{gr}^p_F = E_\infty^{p, n-p} = E_1^{p, n-p} = H^{n-p}(X, \Omega^p) = H^{p, n-p}$. $E_1$-degeneration says $E_\infty = E_1$, and the associated graded of the Hodge filtration on $H^n_{dR}$ is exactly $\bigoplus_p E_\infty^{p, n-p}$.

q: What is $F^1 H^2_{dR}(\mathbb{P}^1_\mathbb{C} / \mathbb{C})$?
text: What is $F^1 H^2_{dR}(\mathbb{P}^1_\mathbb{C} / \mathbb{C})$? The Hodge diamond of $\mathbb{P}^1$ is $h^{0,0} = h^{1,1} = 1$, all others zero. So $H^2_{dR}(\mathbb{P}^1) = H^{1, 1} = \mathbb{C}$ and $F^1 H^2 = \bigoplus_{p \ge 1} H^{p, 2-p} = H^{1, 1} = \mathbb{C}$. The whole of $H^2$ sits in $F^1$ — it is pure of type $(1, 1)$. (Note: $H^1_{dR}(\mathbb{P}^1) = 0$, so the question about $H^1$ is degenerate.) On $\mathbb{P}^1$ all nonzero Hodge numbers are on the diagonal $p = q$. Only one type contributes to each $H^n$.

q: Why does the Hodge filtration $F^\bullet$ make sense over any field $k$ (not just $\mathbb{C}$), while the Hodge decomposition $H^n = \bigoplus_{p + q = n} H^{p, q}$ only makes sense over $\mathbb{C}$?
text: Why does the Hodge filtration $F^\bullet$ make sense over any field $k$ (not just $\mathbb{C}$), while the Hodge decomposition $H^n = \bigoplus_{p + q = n} H^{p, q}$ only makes sense over $\mathbb{C}$? $F^p H^n_{dR}(X/k) = \mathrm{im}(\mathbb{H}^n(\sigma^{\ge p}\Omega^\bullet) \to H^n_{dR})$ uses only the de Rham complex and its trivial truncation — both defined over any base. The decomposition $H^n = \bigoplus H^{p, q}$ uses $H^{q, p} = \overline{H^{p, q}}$ which requires complex conjugation. This distinction is foundational for $p$-adic Hodge theory — the filtration survives, the decomposition is replaced by other structures (Frobenius, etc.). Complex conjugation: $\overline{H^{p,q}} = H^{q,p}$. Does that make sense over $\overline{\mathbb{F}_p}$?

q: Griffiths transversality says: if $\pi\colon X \to S$ is a smooth proper family of varieties with Gauss-Manin connection $\nabla$ on $\mathcal{H}^n := R^n \pi_* \Omega^\bullet_{X/S}$, then for the Hodge filtration $F^\bullet \subset \mathcal{H}^n$:
text:
Griffiths transversality says: if $\pi\colon X 	o S$ is a smooth proper family of varieties with Gauss-Manin connection $
abla$ on $\mathcal{H}^n := R^n \pi_* \Omega^ ullet_{X/S}$, then for the Hodge filtration $F^ ullet \subset \mathcal{H}^n$: Griffiths transversality is the constraint $
abla(F^p) \subseteq F^{p-1} \otimes \Omega^1_S$. Equivalently: $
abla\colon F^p 	o F^{p-1} \otimes \Omega^1_S$ on the associated graded becomes $\overline{
abla}\colon \mathrm{gr}^p_F 	o \mathrm{gr}^{p-1}_F \otimes \Omega^1_S$, the Kodaira-Spencer-type differential. This is the structural constraint of a 'variation of Hodge structure' and underlies the Griffiths period domain. Differentiating an algebraic class along a parameter shifts its 'holomorphic-degree' by at most one — that's the content.

q: Suppose $X$ is a smooth projective variety over a field of positive characteristic $p > 0$. Does the Hodge-to-de-Rham spectral sequence degenerate at $E_1$?
text: Suppose $X$ is a smooth projective variety over a field of positive characteristic $p > 0$. Does the Hodge-to-de-Rham spectral sequence degenerate at $E_1$? Deligne–Illusie (1987) gave an algebraic proof of Hodge-to-de-Rham degeneration in characteristic $p$ under the hypotheses (i) $X$ lifts to $W_2(k)$ and (ii) $\dim X < p$. In dimension $\ge p$ Mumford and others constructed counterexamples (smooth projective surfaces over $\overline{\mathbb{F}_p}$ where the spectral sequence does not degenerate). Curves of any dimension trivially degenerate (only two columns), so the answer 'only when X is a curve' is too restrictive. The Deligne–Illusie machine in particular gives a new proof of degeneration over $\mathbb{C}$ via reduction mod $p$. Deligne and Illusie revolutionised the subject in 1987 by giving a characteristic-$p$ proof — but only under technical hypotheses on lifting and dimension.

q: Compute $h^{1, 1}(\mathbb{P}^3_\mathbb{C})$.
text: Compute $h^{1, 1}(\mathbb{P}^3_\mathbb{C})$. By Bott vanishing, $h^{p, q}(\mathbb{P}^n) = 1$ if $p = q \le n$ and $0$ otherwise. So $h^{1, 1}(\mathbb{P}^3) = 1$. The full diamond of $\mathbb{P}^3$ is concentrated on the diagonal: $h^{0,0} = h^{1,1} = h^{2,2} = h^{3,3} = 1$, all others zero. Total Betti $= 4 = n + 1$, matching the cell decomposition. $\mathbb{P}^n$'s Hodge diamond is purely diagonal: $h^{p, q} = 1$ iff $p = q$.

q: For a smooth projective variety $X$ of complex dimension $n$, Serre duality gives:
text: For a smooth projective variety $X$ of complex dimension $n$, Serre duality gives: Serre duality on a smooth projective $X$ of dimension $n$ says $H^q(X, \mathcal{F}) \cong H^{n-q}(X, \omega_X \otimes \mathcal{F}^\vee)^\vee$. Applied to $\mathcal{F} = \Omega^p_X$ with $\omega_X = \Omega^n_X$: $H^q(X, \Omega^p) \cong H^{n-q}(X, \Omega^{n-p})^\vee$, hence $h^{p, q} = h^{n-p, n-q}$. The other symmetry $h^{p, q} = h^{q, p}$ is complex conjugation, a different (and complementary) reflection. Serre duality pairs $\Omega^p$ with $\Omega^{n-p}$ (their tensor product is $\omega_X = \Omega^n$) and $H^q$ with $H^{n-q}$.

q: A K3 surface has Hodge numbers $h^{0,0} = h^{2,2} = 1$, $h^{1, 0} = h^{0, 1} = h^{2, 1} = h^{1, 2} = 0$, $h^{2, 0} = h^{0, 2} = 1$, $h^{1, 1} = 20$. What is its topological Euler characteristic?
text: A K3 surface has Hodge numbers $h^{0,0} = h^{2,2} = 1$, $h^{1, 0} = h^{0, 1} = h^{2, 1} = h^{1, 2} = 0$, $h^{2, 0} = h^{0, 2} = 1$, $h^{1, 1} = 20$. What is its topological Euler characteristic? $\chi_{\mathrm{top}} = \sum_n (-1)^n b_n = b_0 - b_1 + b_2 - b_3 + b_4 = 1 - 0 + (1 + 20 + 1) - 0 + 1 = 24$. This is one of the canonical numerical invariants of a K3 surface — and the same for every K3, since they are all deformation-equivalent. Euler characteristic = alternating sum of Betti numbers = alternating sum of Hodge numbers (since Hodge numbers refine Betti).

q: For a complex abelian variety $A$ of dimension $g$ (e.g. the Jacobian of a smooth projective curve of genus $g$), $h^{p, q}(A) = \binom{g}{p} \binom{g}{q}$. Compute $h^{1, 1}$ for $g = 4$.
text: For a complex abelian variety $A$ of dimension $g$ (e.g. the Jacobian of a smooth projective curve of genus $g$), $h^{p, q}(A) = \binom{g}{p} \binom{g}{q}$. Compute $h^{1, 1}$ for $g = 4$. $h^{1, 1}(A^4) = \binom{4}{1} \cdot \binom{4}{1} = 4 \cdot 4 = 16$. The full diamond is $\binom{g}{p}\binom{g}{q}$, so the row sums are $b_n = \binom{2g}{n}$ — i.e. the cohomology ring is the exterior algebra on $H^1$, exactly as for the torus $(S^1)^{2g}$, and the Hodge structure refines this. The cohomology of a complex torus $\mathbb{C}^g / \Lambda$ is the exterior algebra on $H^1 = \mathbb{C}^{2g}$; the Hodge decomposition splits $H^1 = \mathbb{C}^g \oplus \overline{\mathbb{C}^g}$.

q: For a smooth projective complex Calabi–Yau threefold (e.g. the quintic in $\mathbb{P}^4$), the Hodge diamond has $h^{0,0} = h^{3,3} = 1$, $h^{3,0} = h^{0,3} = 1$, $h^{1,0} = h^{0,1} = h^{2,0} = h^{0,2} = h^{3,1} = h^{1,3} = h^{3,2} = h^{2,3} = 0$. Mirror symmetry predicts the existence of a mirror Calabi–Yau $X^\vee$ with what relation between Hodge numbers?
text: For a smooth projective complex Calabi–Yau threefold (e.g. the quintic in $\mathbb{P}^4$), the Hodge diamond has $h^{0,0} = h^{3,3} = 1$, $h^{3,0} = h^{0,3} = 1$, $h^{1,0} = h^{0,1} = h^{2,0} = h^{0,2} = h^{3,1} = h^{1,3} = h^{3,2} = h^{2,3} = 0$. Mirror symmetry predicts the existence of a mirror Calabi–Yau $X^\vee$ with what relation between Hodge numbers? Mirror symmetry (in the Calabi–Yau threefold setting) predicts a mirror manifold $X^\vee$ with Hodge diamond rotated by $90°$: $h^{p, q}(X) = h^{n - p, q}(X^\vee)$ where $n = \dim X = 3$. Equivalently the Hodge diamond reflects through its vertical axis. For $X$ the quintic (so $h^{1, 1} = 1$, $h^{2, 1} = 101$), the mirror has $h^{1, 1} = 101$ and $h^{2, 1} = 1$ — Greene–Plesser's original example. Mirror symmetry exchanges Kähler $(h^{1,1})$ and complex-structure $(h^{2,1})$ moduli — i.e. swaps $p$ and $n - p$.

q: Compute $\dim_\mathbb{C} H^1_{dR}(C / \mathbb{C})$ for a smooth projective curve $C$ of genus $g = 7$.
text: Compute $\dim_\mathbb{C} H^1_{dR}(C / \mathbb{C})$ for a smooth projective curve $C$ of genus $g = 7$. $\dim H^1_{dR}(C / \mathbb{C}) = b_1(C) = h^{1, 0} + h^{0, 1} = g + g = 2g$. For $g = 7$: $\dim = 14$. This matches the topological $H^1$ of an oriented surface of genus $g$, which is $\mathbb{Z}^{2g}$ (so $H^1$ with $\mathbb{C}$-coefficients is $\mathbb{C}^{2g}$). On a curve, $H^1_{dR} \cong H^0(\Omega^1) \oplus H^1(\mathcal{O})$ by the Hodge decomposition; both pieces have dimension $g$.

q: Compute $h^{1, 0}(C)$ for a smooth projective curve of genus $g = 5$.
text: Compute $h^{1, 0}(C)$ for a smooth projective curve of genus $g = 5$. $h^{1, 0}(C) = \dim H^0(C, \Omega^1_C) = g$ — this is the analytic definition of the genus. For $g = 5$ the answer is $5$. Equivalently $h^{0, 1} = h^1(\mathcal{O}_C) = g$ by Serre duality. The full curve diamond is $\{1, g, g, 1\}$ (corners $= 1$, middle row both $= g$). The space of holomorphic 1-forms on a genus-$g$ curve is $g$-dimensional — that's how the genus is detected over $\mathbb{C}$.

q: On an elliptic curve $E\colon y^2 = x^3 + ax + b$ over $\mathbb{C}$, the form $\omega = \frac{dx}{y}$ is:
text: On an elliptic curve $E\colon y^2 = x^3 + ax + b$ over $\mathbb{C}$, the form $\omega = \frac{dx}{y}$ is: The form $\omega = dx/y$ is the famous Néron differential: regular everywhere on a smooth Weierstrass elliptic curve (verify: at infinity, change to local parameter $t = x/y$, then $\omega = -dt + \text{higher order}$, regular). It spans the 1-dimensional $H^0(E, \Omega^1_E)$ and represents a generator of the $F^1$ piece of the Hodge filtration on $H^1_{dR}(E)$. The other generator of $H^1_{dR}$ is $x\,dx/y$ (a 'second-kind' form, with poles but a non-trivial de Rham class). Compute $\omega$ in a local parameter at the point at infinity using $t = x/y$ and verify regularity.

title: Moduli problems and parameters
blurb: A moduli problem asks for a geometric space classifying objects in families.

title: Fine versus coarse moduli
blurb: Universal families may fail; coarse moduli still classify isomorphism classes.

title: Automorphisms obstruct fine moduli
blurb: Nontrivial automorphisms let trivial families be twisted into non-isomorphic families, killing representability of $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$.

title: Level structure and compactification ideas
blurb: Rigidifying data often repairs representability and leads to richer moduli stacks/spaces.

title: Higher-genus moduli and stable curves
blurb: For $g\ge 2$ the coarse space $M_g$ has dimension $3g-3$; Deligne–Mumford compactify by adding stable nodal curves.

q: A moduli problem for elliptic curves over $\mathbb{C}$ is encoded by a functor $\mathcal{M}\colon\mathrm{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}\to\mathrm{Set}$. What does $\mathcal{M}(S)$ contain?
text: A moduli problem for elliptic curves over $\mathbb{C}$ is encoded by a functor $\mathcal{M}\colon\mathrm{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}\to\mathrm{Set}$. What does $\mathcal{M}(S)$ contain? The moduli functor sends a base $S$ to iso classes of families $E\to S$ of the objects of interest — here, flat proper families of elliptic curves.

q: Over $\mathbb{C}$, the $j$-invariant induces a bijection between iso classes of elliptic curves and $\mathbb{A}^1(\mathbb{C})=\mathbb{C}$. What is the (complex) dimension of this parameter space?
text: Over $\mathbb{C}$, the $j$-invariant induces a bijection between iso classes of elliptic curves and $\mathbb{A}^1(\mathbb{C})=\mathbb{C}$. What is the (complex) dimension of this parameter space? $\mathbb{A}^1(\mathbb{C})=\mathbb{C}$ has complex dimension $1$, matching $\dim M_{1,1}=1$.

q: For the curve $y^2=x^3+1$ (so $a=0$, $b=1$), compute the discriminant $\Delta=-16(4a^3+27b^2)$ as $a+bi$.
text: For the curve $y^2=x^3+1$ (so $a=0$, $b=1$), compute the discriminant $\Delta=-16(4a^3+27b^2)$ as $a+bi$. $\Delta=-16(0+27)=-16\cdot 27=-432$, which is nonzero, confirming the curve is smooth (hence an elliptic curve).

q: The moduli functor $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ is NOT representable. Which chain of reasoning correctly identifies the first obstruction?
text: The moduli functor $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ is NOT representable. Which chain of reasoning correctly identifies the first obstruction? A representable functor $h_M$ cannot distinguish two families with the same classifying map. Twisting a constant family by a nontrivial $\mathbb{Z}/6$-torsor (at $j=0$) gives an isotrivial but non-trivial family, exposing the failure: $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}\not\cong h_M$ for any scheme $M$. Automorphisms create non-isomorphic families with the same $j$-invariant.

q: Let $E: y^2 = x^3 + x$ (so $a=1$, $b=0$). Compute the discriminant $\Delta = -16(4a^3+27b^2)$.
text: Let $E: y^2 = x^3 + x$ (so $a=1$, $b=0$). Compute the discriminant $\Delta = -16(4a^3+27b^2)$. $\Delta = -16(4\cdot1+0) = -16\cdot4 = -64$. Since $\Delta\ne 0$, the curve is smooth. Compute $-16(4 \cdot 1) = -64$.

q: A family of elliptic curves $\mathcal{E}\to S$ induces a morphism $f:S\to\mathbb{A}^1_j$. If $\mathbb{A}^1_j$ were a fine moduli space with universal family $\mathcal{U}$, one would need $\mathcal{E}\cong f^*\mathcal{U}$. Why does this fail for the family $y^2=x^3+t^2$ over $\mathbb{A}^1_t$?
text: A family of elliptic curves $\mathcal{E}\to S$ induces a morphism $f:S\to\mathbb{A}^1_j$. If $\mathbb{A}^1_j$ were a fine moduli space with universal family $\mathcal{U}$, one would need $\mathcal{E}\cong f^*\mathcal{U}$. Why does this fail for the family $y^2=x^3+t^2$ over $\mathbb{A}^1_t$? For $y^2=x^3+t^2$, scaling $x=u^2 x'$, $y=u^3 y'$, $t=u^3 t'$ shows constant $j=1728$, so $f:\mathbb{A}^1_t\to\mathbb{A}^1_j$ is constant. Yet the family is non-trivial (the twist by $t^2$ cannot be removed over $\mathbb{Z}$), contradicting $\mathcal{E}\cong f^*\mathcal{U}$. Fine moduli means every family comes from a unique classifying map.

q: Which statement characterises a fine moduli space $M$ for $\mathcal{M}$?
text: Which statement characterises a fine moduli space $M$ for $\mathcal{M}$? Fine moduli is exactly representability: $\mathcal{M}\cong h_M$. The identity $\mathrm{id}_M\in h_M(M)$ corresponds to the universal family $\mathcal{E}\to M$.

q: The $j$-line $\mathbb{A}^1_j$ is a moduli space for elliptic curves. Which type?
text: The $j$-line $\mathbb{A}^1_j$ is a moduli space for elliptic curves. Which type? The $j$-line is the coarse moduli space of $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$: geometric points match iso classes bijectively, but no universal family exists because of automorphisms.

q: The obstruction to fine moduli for $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ is the presence of nontrivial automorphisms. Every elliptic curve has at least the $[-1]$ involution. What is the order of the automorphism group of a generic elliptic curve (with $j\ne 0,1728$)?
text: The obstruction to fine moduli for $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ is the presence of nontrivial automorphisms. Every elliptic curve has at least the $[-1]$ involution. What is the order of the automorphism group of a generic elliptic curve (with $j\ne 0,1728$)? For generic $j$, the only nontrivial automorphism is $[-1]:(x,y)\mapsto(x,-y)$, so $|\mathrm{Aut}(E)|=2$.

q: Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a moduli functor with a coarse moduli space $M$. Which combination of properties does $M$ satisfy, but a fine moduli space additionally requires?
text: Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a moduli functor with a coarse moduli space $M$. Which combination of properties does $M$ satisfy, but a fine moduli space additionally requires? A coarse moduli space satisfies (i) the bijection on $\bar{k}$-points and (ii) initiality. Fine moduli additionally demands representability: a universal family over $M$ inducing $\mathcal{F}\cong h_M$ as functors. Coarse moduli universalizes on points but not on families.

q: The curve $y^2=x^3-x$ has $j=1728$. Its automorphism group over $\bar{\mathbb{Q}}$ is cyclic of order $4$, generated by $(x,y)\mapsto(-x,iy)$. How many elements does $\mathrm{Aut}(E)$ have?
text: The curve $y^2=x^3-x$ has $j=1728$. Its automorphism group over $\bar{\mathbb{Q}}$ is cyclic of order $4$, generated by $(x,y)\mapsto(-x,iy)$. How many elements does $\mathrm{Aut}(E)$ have? At $j=1728$ the automorphism group is $\mathbb{Z}/4$, strictly larger than the generic $\mathbb{Z}/2$ but smaller than $\mathbb{Z}/6$ at $j=0$. Order-$4$ automorphism comes from $(x, y) \mapsto (-x, iy)$.

q: Which statement is a correct counterexample showing the $j$-line $\mathbb{A}^1_j$ is NOT a fine moduli space?
text: Which statement is a correct counterexample showing the $j$-line $\mathbb{A}^1_j$ is NOT a fine moduli space? Both families send every point to $j=0$ ($j(y^2=x^3+c)=0$ for any $c\ne 0$), yet twisting by $t$ gives a non-trivial $\mathbb{Z}/6$-torsor. If $\mathbb{A}^1_j$ were fine, the two families would be pulled back from the universal one via the same map and hence isomorphic — contradiction. Twist families over $\Spec\,k[t, t^{-1}]$ break fine representability.

q: Why does the existence of nontrivial automorphisms prevent $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ from being representable?
text: Why does the existence of nontrivial automorphisms prevent $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ from being representable? A representable functor $h_M$ can only see families that pull back from a single classifying map. Twisting by a nontrivial cocycle in $H^1(S,\underline{\mathrm{Aut}})$ produces genuinely different families with identical classifying maps, which $h_M$ cannot distinguish.

q: The curve $y^2=x^3+1$ has $j=0$. Its automorphism group over $\bar{\mathbb{Q}}$ is cyclic of what order? (Hint: it is generated by $(x,y)\mapsto(\zeta_3 x, -y)$.)
text: The curve $y^2=x^3+1$ has $j=0$. Its automorphism group over $\bar{\mathbb{Q}}$ is cyclic of what order? (Hint: it is generated by $(x,y)\mapsto(\zeta_3 x, -y)$.) At $j=0$ the automorphism group is $\mathbb{Z}/6$, generated by the order-6 map $(x,y)\mapsto(\zeta_3 x,-y)$ where $\zeta_3$ is a primitive cube root of unity. This is the maximum automorphism group for an elliptic curve in characteristic $\ne 2,3$.

q: The family $E_2: y^2=x^3+t^6$ over $\mathbb{A}^1_{\mathbb{Q}}\setminus\{0\}$ is a nontrivial twist of $E_1: y^2=x^3+1$. The obstruction class lives in:
text: The family $E_2: y^2=x^3+t^6$ over $\mathbb{A}^1_{\mathbb{Q}}\setminus\{0\}$ is a nontrivial twist of $E_1: y^2=x^3+1$. The obstruction class lives in: Twists of a fixed family are classified by first Čech cohomology with coefficients in the automorphism sheaf. The nontriviality of this $H^1$ is exactly the obstruction to representability.

q: Order the steps in showing that $[-1]\in\mathrm{Aut}(E)$ obstructs representability of $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$:
text: Order the steps in showing that $[-1]\in\mathrm{Aut}(E)$ obstructs representability of $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$: The standard three-step argument: (A) construct the twist, (B) identify $[-1]$ as the monodromy of the torsor, (C) apply Yoneda to conclude the representing object would need to distinguish the two families via the same map — impossible. Nontrivial torsors for $[-1]$ give distinct families with the same invariant.

q: Let $E_0: y^2=x^3+1$ (so $j=0$, $|\mathrm{Aut}|=6$) and $E_1: y^2=x^3-1$. The twist $E_1$ is a non-trivial torsor under $\mathbb{Z}/6$. Over $\mathbb{C}$, $E_0\cong E_1$ because $j(E_0)=j(E_1)=0$. How many distinct isomorphism classes of elliptic curves over $\mathbb{C}$ have $j=0$?
text: Let $E_0: y^2=x^3+1$ (so $j=0$, $|\mathrm{Aut}|=6$) and $E_1: y^2=x^3-1$. The twist $E_1$ is a non-trivial torsor under $\mathbb{Z}/6$. Over $\mathbb{C}$, $E_0\cong E_1$ because $j(E_0)=j(E_1)=0$. How many distinct isomorphism classes of elliptic curves over $\mathbb{C}$ have $j=0$? Over $\mathbb{C}$ the $j$-invariant is a complete isomorphism invariant, so $j=0$ corresponds to exactly one iso class. Twists become isomorphic over $\mathbb{C}$ because $\mathbb{C}^*$ is algebraically closed. The twist corresponds to a square root of a non-square element.

q: Consider a $\mathbb{Z}/2$-torsor $P\to S$ twisted by a class in $H^1(S,\mathbb{Z}/2)$. If $S=\mathbb{A}^1_t\setminus\{0\}$ and the torsor is $\Spec\mathcal{O}_S[u]/(u^2-t)$, this class is:
text: Consider a $\mathbb{Z}/2$-torsor $P\to S$ twisted by a class in $H^1(S,\mathbb{Z}/2)$. If $S=\mathbb{A}^1_t\setminus\{0\}$ and the torsor is $\Spec\mathcal{O}_S[u]/(u^2-t)$, this class is: $\mathcal{O}(\mathbb{A}^1\setminus\{0\})=k[t,t^{-1}]$ does not contain $\sqrt{t}$, so the $\mathbb{Z}/2$-cover $u^2=t$ is a genuinely non-trivial torsor, representing a nontrivial class in $H^1(S,\mathbb{Z}/2)$. Monodromy around the puncture detects the nontrivial torsor.

q: Why is a level-$N$ structure required to satisfy $N\ge 3$ to rigidify $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ into a fine moduli space?
text: Why is a level-$N$ structure required to satisfy $N\ge 3$ to rigidify $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ into a fine moduli space? On $E[2]$, multiplication by $-1$ is the identity because $2P=0$ implies $-P=P$. So $[-1]$ fixes any basis of $E[2]$, i.e. level-$2$ is not rigid enough. For $N\ge 3$, $[-1]\ne 1$ on $E[N]$.

q: For smooth projective curves of genus $g\ge 2$, $\dim M_g=3g-3$. Compute $\dim M_4$.
text: For smooth projective curves of genus $g\ge 2$, $\dim M_g=3g-3$. Compute $\dim M_4$. $3(4)-3=9$. This is the deformation-theoretic count $\dim H^1(C,T_C)$.

q: The Deligne–Mumford compactification $\overline{M}_{g,n}$ adds stable curves. Using $\dim M_{g,n}=3g-3+n$ for $g\ge 2$, compute $\dim M_{2,3}$.
text: The Deligne–Mumford compactification $\overline{M}_{g,n}$ adds stable curves. Using $\dim M_{g,n}=3g-3+n$ for $g\ge 2$, compute $\dim M_{2,3}$. $3(2)-3+3=3+3=6$.

q: Why does adding a level-$N$ structure for $N\ge 3$ rigidify $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$, turning the Deligne–Mumford stack into a representable functor (a scheme)?
text:
Why does adding a level-$N$ structure for $N\ge 3$ rigidify $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$, turning the Deligne–Mumford stack into a representable functor (a scheme)? For $N\ge 3$, $[-1]
e\mathrm{id}$ on $E[N]\cong(\mathbb{Z}/N)^2$: it sends every basis vector $v$ to $-v
e v$ (since $N\ge 3$ means $-1
e 1$ in $\mathbb{Z}/N$). Hence $[-1]$ does not preserve any level-$N$ basis, so the automorphism group of a level-$N$-structured elliptic curve is trivial and the functor becomes representable. Level-$N$ with $N \ge 3$ kills the generic automorphism group.

q: Compute $\dim\overline{M}_{3,2}$ using $\dim M_{g,n}=3g-3+n$.
text: Compute $\dim\overline{M}_{3,2}$ using $\dim M_{g,n}=3g-3+n$. $3(3)-3+2=6+2=8$. Apply $\dim = 3 \cdot 3 - 3 + 2$.

q: The boundary $\overline{M}_g\setminus M_g$ parametrises stable nodal curves. Which type of degeneration corresponds to an irreducible component of the boundary divisor $\Delta_0\subset\overline{M}_g$?
text: The boundary $\overline{M}_g\setminus M_g$ parametrises stable nodal curves. Which type of degeneration corresponds to an irreducible component of the boundary divisor $\Delta_0\subset\overline{M}_g$? $\Delta_0$ consists of irreducible nodal curves: a smooth curve of genus $g-1$ with two points identified to create one node. Boundary divisors $\Delta_h$ for $h\ge 1$ consist of two components of genera $h$ and $g-h$ joined at a node. Irreducible nodal curves sit on the boundary of $\overline{M}_g$.

q: Compute $\dim M_g$ for $g=6$ using the Riemann–Roch formula $\dim M_g=3g-3$.
text: Compute $\dim M_g$ for $g=6$ using the Riemann–Roch formula $\dim M_g=3g-3$. $3(6)-3=15$. This matches $\dim H^1(C,T_C)$, the first-order deformation space of a smooth genus-$6$ curve.

q: A stable $n$-pointed curve $(C, p_1,\dots,p_n)$ of genus $g$ is required to have at-worst-nodal singularities, be proper and connected, and satisfy which finiteness condition?
text: A stable $n$-pointed curve $(C, p_1,\dots,p_n)$ of genus $g$ is required to have at-worst-nodal singularities, be proper and connected, and satisfy which finiteness condition? Stability is exactly finiteness of the automorphism group. Equivalent combinatorial conditions: every smooth rational component has $\ge 3$ special points, every genus-$1$ component has $\ge 1$. This is what cuts out a Deligne–Mumford stack.

q: Which of the following is NOT a stable curve of genus $2$?
text: Which of the following is NOT a stable curve of genus $2$? A smooth $\mathbb{P}^1$ alone has arithmetic genus $0$, not $2$, and also has infinite automorphism group $\mathrm{PGL}_2$ — it fails stability on every count. The other options are standard boundary strata of $\overline{M}_2$ and $\overline{M}_{2,3}$.

q: Order the steps in proving that $\overline{M}_{g,n}$ is a proper Deligne–Mumford stack for $2g-2+n>0$:
text: Order the steps in proving that $\overline{M}_{g,n}$ is a proper Deligne–Mumford stack for $2g-2+n>0$: Deligne–Mumford's argument: first introduce the stable-curve notion (finiteness of $\mathrm{Aut}$), then verify the valuative criterion (semistable reduction theorem provides the extension), and deduce properness from the algebraic stack structure. Geometric invariant theory first, then properness via stable reduction.

q: A stable rational ($g=0$) curve with $n$ marked points exists only for $n\ge 3$ (stability condition $2\cdot0-2+n>0$ and finite $\mathrm{Aut}$). What is the minimum $n$ for which $\overline{M}_{0,n}$ is a non-empty proper scheme?
text: A stable rational ($g=0$) curve with $n$ marked points exists only for $n\ge 3$ (stability condition $2\cdot0-2+n>0$ and finite $\mathrm{Aut}$). What is the minimum $n$ for which $\overline{M}_{0,n}$ is a non-empty proper scheme? For $g=0$, stability requires $\mathrm{Aut}(\mathbb{P}^1,p_1,\dots,p_n)$ finite, which forces $n\ge 3$. $\overline{M}_{0,3}$ is a single point (three points on $\mathbb{P}^1$ are rigidified by $\mathrm{PGL}_2$), so $n=3$ is the minimum. Automorphism groups become finite with three or more marked points.

q: Which of the following is a stable genus-$1$ curve with $1$ marked point (an element of $\overline{M}_{1,1}$)?
text: Which of the following is a stable genus-$1$ curve with $1$ marked point (an element of $\overline{M}_{1,1}$)? A rational nodal cubic has arithmetic genus $1$ and one node. With a smooth marked point $p$, the automorphism group is finite (it equals $\mathbb{Z}/2$ in many cases), satisfying the stability condition. A $\mathbb{P}^1$ with one point has $\mathrm{Aut}=\mathbb{C}^*$ — infinite, hence not stable. A stable genus-$1$ curve with one marked point is a nodal cubic with a smooth point.

title: Why schemes aren't enough
blurb: Some natural quotients (e.g. $\mathbb{A}^1 / \mathbb{Z}$ over a field, or non-free group actions on schemes) are not schemes. Algebraic spaces are the smallest enlargement of schemes that closes the category under such étale quotients while keeping a representable definition.

title: Étale equivalence relations
blurb: An étale equivalence relation on a scheme $U$ is a monomorphism $R \hookrightarrow U \times U$ which is an equivalence relation in the categorical sense, with both projections étale. Quotients of these are exactly algebraic spaces.

title: Algebraic spaces as étale sheaves
blurb: An algebraic space is a sheaf $X$ on the étale site of schemes whose diagonal $X \to X \times X$ is representable and which admits an étale surjection $U \twoheadrightarrow X$ from a scheme. Equivalently, $X = U/R$ for an étale equivalence relation $R \rightrightarrows U$.

title: Morphisms of algebraic spaces
blurb: Morphisms of algebraic spaces are morphisms of étale sheaves; étale-locally on the source and target they reduce to scheme morphisms. Most scheme-morphism properties (étale, smooth, proper, finite type) extend verbatim.

title: Examples: free quotients and a non-scheme
blurb: Any scheme is an algebraic space. The free $\mathbb{Z}$-action on $\mathbb{A}^1$ over a field has quotient an algebraic space that is not a scheme (no separated quasi-projective representative exists). Hironaka's example gives a smooth proper algebraic space over $\mathbb{C}$ that isn't a scheme.

title: Algebraic spaces sit between schemes and DM stacks
blurb: An algebraic space is a stack with trivial automorphism groups; a DM stack with trivial automorphism groups is an algebraic space. The hierarchy schemes ⊂ algebraic spaces ⊂ DM stacks ⊂ Artin stacks tracks how much non-trivial isotropy the geometric object can carry.

q: Why does the étale-sheaf quotient $X = \mathbb{A}^1 / \mathbb{Z}$ fail to be a scheme over a field $k$?
text: Why does the étale-sheaf quotient $X = \mathbb{A}^1 / \mathbb{Z}$ fail to be a scheme over a field $k$? The pullback of $\Delta_X$ to $\mathbb{A}^1 \times \mathbb{A}^1$ is the equivalence relation $R = \mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{A}^1$. For $X$ to be a scheme, $\Delta_X$ would need to be quasi-compact (in fact, locally closed); but $R$ is an infinite disjoint union of $\mathbb{A}^1$'s and so not quasi-compact. The action is free; finiteness of the group is irrelevant; properness of $\mathbb{A}^1$ is unrelated.

q: Which of the following statements about $\mathbb{A}^1 / \mathbb{Z}$ over $k$ are true? (select all)
text: Which of the following statements about $\mathbb{A}^1 / \mathbb{Z}$ over $k$ are true? (select all) It is a sheaf (étale-sheafification of the presheaf quotient), and the natural projection $\mathbb{A}^1 \twoheadrightarrow \mathbb{A}^1/\mathbb{Z}$ is étale and surjective; combined with the diagonal pullback $R = \mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{A}^1$ being a scheme, $X$ is an algebraic space. It is not quasi-separated, because $\Delta_X$ is not quasi-compact. It is a sheaf (étale-sheafification of the presheaf quotient), and the natural projection $\mathbb{A}^1 \twoheadrightarrow \mathbb{A}^1/\mathbb{Z}$ is étale and surjective; combined with the diagonal…

q: Spot the flawed step in the following "proof" that $\mathbb{A}^1 / \mathbb{Z}$ is a scheme.
text: Spot the flawed step in the following "proof" that $\mathbb{A}^1 / \mathbb{Z}$ is a scheme. Step 3 invokes "GAGA" — a comparison theorem between projective complex-analytic and projective algebraic geometry — to conclude something is quasi-projective. GAGA does not produce schemes from étale quotient sheaves, and a free étale action on a scheme need not produce a scheme quotient (e.g. $\mathbb{A}^1/\mathbb{Z}$ itself). The correct conclusion is only that the quotient is an algebraic space.

q: Which of the following data does not define an étale equivalence relation on a scheme $U$?
text: Which of the following data does not define an étale equivalence relation on a scheme $U$? Choices A, B, and C are all valid étale equivalence relations: the diagonal (trivial), free finite group action, and the codiagonal (i.e. $\pi_0$ collapses to a point). Choice D fails transitivity — $x \sim x+1$ and $x+1 \sim x+2$ should imply $x \sim x+2$, but $(x, x+2) \not\in R$. To make this an equivalence relation you would need $R = \bigcup_{n \in \mathbb{Z}} \{(x, x+n)\} = \mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{A}^1$ — the relation generating $\mathbb{A}^1/\mathbb{Z}$.

q: Match each scheme-theoretic gadget with its role in an étale equivalence relation $j = (s,t)\colon R \hookrightarrow U \times U$.
text: Match each scheme-theoretic gadget with its role in an étale equivalence relation $j = (s,t)\colon R \hookrightarrow U \times U$. Reflexivity = diagonal factors through $R$. Symmetry = swap restricts. Transitivity = the fibre product $R \times_U R$ (composing equivalences) lands in $R$. Étale projections is the topology condition that makes the quotient an algebraic space rather than just any sheaf.

q: Let $G$ be a finite group acting on a scheme $U$. The associated map $G \times U \to U \times U$, $(g,u) \mapsto (u, g\cdot u)$, defines an étale equivalence relation if and only if which condition holds?
text: Let $G$ be a finite group acting on a scheme $U$. The associated map $G \times U \to U \times U$, $(g,u) \mapsto (u, g\cdot u)$, defines an étale equivalence relation if and only if which condition holds? The map $G \times U \to U \times U$ is automatically a monomorphism iff the action is free. Each projection $G \times U \to U$ is a disjoint union of $|G|$ copies of $U$, which is étale exactly when the source is reduced and the action is free in the geometric sense. Abelian-ness, quasi-projectivity, and invertibility of $|G|$ are unrelated to whether the relation is étale; they affect properties of the quotient (e.g. tameness) but not the equivalence-relation structure itself.

q: Which of the following is the cleanest characterisation of an algebraic space?
text: Which of the following is the cleanest characterisation of an algebraic space? By definition (Knutson, after Artin), an algebraic space is exactly a sheaf $X$ on $(\mathrm{Sch})_{\acute e t}$ with (1) representable diagonal and (2) an étale-surjective scheme atlas. Choice A is the definition of (a quasi-affine) scheme. Choice C describes DM stacks. Choice D is the classical definition of a scheme.

q: Arrange the following arrows / objects in the order in which they appear in the definition of an algebraic space (atlas first, equivalence relation second, etc.).
text: Arrange the following arrows / objects in the order in which they appear in the definition of an algebraic space (atlas first, equivalence relation second, etc.). Logical order: first $X$ is required to be an étale sheaf; then $\Delta_X$ is asked to be representable; this turns any test atlas $U$ into a representable picture, with $R = U\times_X U$ a scheme; and finally the existence of an étale surjective $U \twoheadrightarrow X$ closes the definition. (Some textbooks phrase it as $X = U/R$ for an étale equivalence relation; that's the equivalent étale-quotient form.) Logical order: first $X$ is required to be an étale sheaf; then $\Delta_X$ is asked to be representable; this turns any test atlas $U$ into a representable picture, with $R = U\times_X U$ a scheme;…

q: If $X$ is an algebraic space and $U \twoheadrightarrow X$ is an étale atlas, what is $R := U \times_X U$ as a scheme?
text: If $X$ is an algebraic space and $U \twoheadrightarrow X$ is an étale atlas, what is $R := U \times_X U$ as a scheme? Pulling back $\Delta_X$ along the atlas $U \times U \to X \times X$ gives $R = U \times_X U \hookrightarrow U \times U$. This is the étale equivalence relation, and $X = U/R$ as étale sheaves. It is generally not $\Delta_U$ (that would force the atlas to be an isomorphism) and only equals $U \sqcup U$ in trivial cases.

q: Suppose $X$ is an algebraic space with representable diagonal $\Delta_X$. Which extra hypothesis on $\Delta_X$ recovers the notion of quasi-separated ?
text: Suppose $X$ is an algebraic space with representable diagonal $\Delta_X$. Which extra hypothesis on $\Delta_X$ recovers the notion of quasi-separated ? By definition, $X$ is quasi-separated iff $\Delta_X$ is quasi-compact. "$\Delta_X$ a closed immersion" gives the strictly stronger condition separated ; étale or proper diagonals are different conditions altogether. The example $\mathbb{A}^1 / \mathbb{Z}$ from §1 fails quasi-separatedness exactly because $\Delta_X$ is not quasi-compact.

q: Spot the flawed step in the following "proof" that every algebraic space $X$ is a scheme.
text: Spot the flawed step in the following "proof" that every algebraic space $X$ is a scheme. Step 3 is exactly where schemes fail: étale equivalence relations of schemes do not in general have scheme quotients — that's the whole reason algebraic spaces were invented. The classical theorem only guarantees a scheme quotient under extra hypotheses (e.g. all orbits in a single affine, or finite flat $G$-action with quasi-projective $U$). Without such hypotheses, $U/R$ is only an algebraic space.

q: Let $f \colon X \to Y$ be a morphism of algebraic spaces, presented by $\tilde f \colon U \to V$ on étale atlases. Which property of $f$ is not determined by the corresponding property of $\tilde f$?
text: Let $f \colon X \to Y$ be a morphism of algebraic spaces, presented by $\tilde f \colon U \to V$ on étale atlases. Which property of $f$ is not determined by the corresponding property of $\tilde f$? Étale, smooth, and locally of finite presentation are all étale-local on the target, so $f$ has the property iff $\tilde f$ does. Separatedness is more subtle: it is the condition that $\Delta_f \colon X \to X \times_Y X$ is a closed immersion, which involves the relation $R \rightrightarrows U$ and is not determined by $\tilde f$ alone.

q: Select all properties of scheme morphisms that are étale-local on the target (and hence transfer verbatim to morphisms of algebraic spaces).
text: Select all properties of scheme morphisms that are étale-local on the target (and hence transfer verbatim to morphisms of algebraic spaces). Étale, flat, proper, finite, and isomorphism are all étale-local on the target. Separated is the exception — it is a condition on the diagonal $\Delta_f$ rather than on $f$ itself, and depends on the equivalence relation in the algebraic-space presentation.

q: Suppose $X$ and $Y$ are both schemes, viewed as algebraic spaces. A morphism of algebraic spaces $f \colon X \to Y$ is the same data as which of the following?
text: Suppose $X$ and $Y$ are both schemes, viewed as algebraic spaces. A morphism of algebraic spaces $f \colon X \to Y$ is the same data as which of the following? The Yoneda embedding (schemes) $\hookrightarrow$ (étale sheaves) is fully faithful, and the further inclusion into algebraic spaces is too. So a morphism between two schemes-as-algebraic-spaces is exactly a morphism of schemes — no extra data, no quotient by anything.

q: Why is the quotient $\mathbb{A}^1/\mathbb{Z}$ over a field not a separated, quasi-projective scheme?
text: Why is the quotient $\mathbb{A}^1/\mathbb{Z}$ over a field not a separated, quasi-projective scheme? Quasi-projective schemes are quasi-separated, so their diagonals must be quasi-compact. But the diagonal of $\mathbb{A}^1/\mathbb{Z}$ pulls back along $\mathbb{A}^1 \times \mathbb{A}^1 \to X\times X$ to $\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{A}^1$ — an infinite disjoint union, not quasi-compact. The other choices are either false ($\mathbb{A}^1$ is affine and one would expect properness to be unrelated to schemehood of a quotient) or red herrings.

q: In Hironaka's smooth proper non-scheme example, what is the obstruction to being a scheme?
text: In Hironaka's smooth proper non-scheme example, what is the obstruction to being a scheme? Hironaka's space $H$ is smooth and proper, but it contains a proper curve $C$ (the descended image of $\tilde C_0$) on which every line bundle has degree zero. This forbids the existence of an ample line bundle, hence projectivity, hence (for proper smooth 3-folds over $\mathbb{C}$) schemehood. The space is reduced, separated, and 3-dimensional.

q: Select all examples of algebraic spaces that are not schemes.
text: Select all examples of algebraic spaces that are not schemes. $\Spec\mathbb{Z}$ and $\mathbb{P}^n$ are obviously schemes. An étale double cover of $\mathbb{P}^1$ is also a scheme (it's a smooth curve). Only $\mathbb{A}^1/\mathbb{Z}$ (not quasi-separated) and Hironaka's $H$ (smooth proper non-projective) are genuine non-scheme algebraic spaces.

q: Let $G$ be a finite group acting freely on a quasi-projective scheme $U$ over a field. Why is the quotient $U/G$ always a scheme in this case?
text: Let $G$ be a finite group acting freely on a quasi-projective scheme $U$ over a field. Why is the quotient $U/G$ always a scheme in this case? The classical theorem (SGA 1 or [EGA III]) says that if $G$ is finite and every orbit on $U$ is contained in some affine open (which is automatic for quasi-projective $U$, since one can take a $G$-stable affine using ample-line-bundle averaging), then $U/G$ exists as a scheme. The Zariski-local structure is $\Spec(R^G)$. Without quasi-projectivity, the conclusion can fail.

q: Spot the flawed step in this attempted proof that Hironaka's space $H$ is projective.
text: Spot the flawed step in this attempted proof that Hironaka's space $H$ is projective. Step 3 is false. Ampleness is not preserved under proper birational pullback — it can become only nef, and in Hironaka's example precisely this happens: the pullback restricts to degree zero on the descended curve $C$ that obstructs schemehood. Chow's lemma exists for algebraic spaces (giving a projective birational cover), but the pulled-back line bundle is only nef, not ample, so the conclusion fails.

q: Which condition characterises algebraic spaces among Artin stacks?
text: Which condition characterises algebraic spaces among Artin stacks? An Artin stack is an algebraic space iff every geometric stabiliser is trivial — iff $\Delta_{\mathcal X}$ is a monomorphism. Choice A characterises DM stacks; choice C is necessary but not sufficient (DM stacks also have étale atlases by schemes, e.g. $B(\mathbb{Z}/2)$ has the trivial étale cover from a point times $\mathbb{Z}/2$); choice D is unrelated.

q: Match each rung of the hierarchy with the condition on its diagonal.
text: Match each rung of the hierarchy with the condition on its diagonal. The hierarchy is fully captured by progressively weaker conditions on the diagonal: locally closed immersion (schemes) $\Rightarrow$ monomorphism (algebraic spaces) $\Rightarrow$ unramified (DM) $\Rightarrow$ representable (Artin). Each weakening corresponds to allowing more structure in the geometric stabilisers (none, none, finite étale, any algebraic group). The hierarchy is fully captured by progressively weaker conditions on the diagonal: locally closed immersion (schemes) $\Rightarrow$ monomorphism (algebraic spaces) $\Rightarrow$ unramified (DM)…

q: Let $\mathcal X = [\mathbb{A}^1 / \mathbb{Z}/2]$ where $\mathbb{Z}/2$ acts by $x \mapsto -x$. What kind of geometric object is $\mathcal X$?
text: Let $\mathcal X = [\mathbb{A}^1 / \mathbb{Z}/2]$ where $\mathbb{Z}/2$ acts by $x \mapsto -x$. What kind of geometric object is $\mathcal X$? The action is not free: the origin $0 \in \mathbb{A}^1$ is fixed, with stabiliser $\mathbb{Z}/2$. So $\mathcal X = [\mathbb{A}^1/(\mathbb{Z}/2)]$ has nontrivial isotropy at the origin, making it a DM stack, not an algebraic space. (The naïve geometric quotient $\Spec k[x^2] \cong \mathbb{A}^1$ is the coarse moduli space , which loses the stabiliser information.)

q: Suppose $\mathcal X$ is an Artin stack with diagonal $\Delta$. Which characterisation is correct?
text: Suppose $\mathcal X$ is an Artin stack with diagonal $\Delta$. Which characterisation is correct? The clean characterisation is: $\mathcal X$ is DM $\Leftrightarrow$ $\Delta_{\mathcal X}$ is unramified $\Leftrightarrow$ all geometric stabilisers are finite étale group schemes (and an étale atlas exists). Choice B is wrong: algebraic spaces have $\Delta$ a monomorphism , not étale (étale + mono = open immersion, too strong). Choices C and D are unrelated to the standard characterisations.

q: Which of the following are DM stacks but not algebraic spaces?
text: Which of the following are DM stacks but not algebraic spaces? $B(\mathbb{Z}/n)$, $\overline{\mathcal M}_{1,1}$ (with $\mathbb{Z}/2$ stabiliser at the $j=1728$ point), and $\mathcal M_g$ for $g \ge 2$ are all DM stacks with nontrivial finite stabilisers, hence not algebraic spaces. $\mathbb{P}^1$ is a scheme. $BGL_n$ has positive-dimensional stabiliser $GL_n$, so it is an Artin stack that is not DM.

title: Groupoids, fibered categories, descent
blurb: Stacks encode objects with automorphisms while preserving gluing across covers.

title: Classifying stacks BG
blurb: BG packages principal G-bundles functorially and acts as a universal quotient object.

title: Quotient stack [X/G]
blurb: The stack [X/G] records a G-action on X while remembering stabilisers; BG is the case X = point.

title: Deligne–Mumford vs. Artin stacks
blurb: Dropping 'étale atlas' to 'smooth atlas' enlarges DM stacks to Artin stacks, accommodating positive-dimensional automorphisms.

title: Deligne–Mumford stacks and coarse spaces
