blurb: DM stacks model orbifold-like moduli with étale atlases and coarse moduli maps.

q: Why must a moduli functor $\mathcal{M}:\mathrm{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}\to\mathrm{Set}$ for elliptic curves fail to be representable?
text: Why must a moduli functor $\mathcal{M}:\mathrm{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}\to\mathrm{Set}$ for elliptic curves fail to be representable? Every elliptic curve has the automorphism $[-1]$; twisting a family by this automorphism along a cover produces a non-isomorphic family with the same classifying map to any putative fine moduli scheme. Passing to groupoid-valued functors is precisely what records this data. Every elliptic curve has the automorphism $[-1]$; twisting a family by this automorphism along a cover produces a non-isomorphic family with the same classifying map to any putative fine moduli…

q: A fibered category $\mathcal{X}\to\mathrm{Sch}$ is a stack (in the étale topology) iff for every cover $\{U_i\to U\}$:
text: A fibered category $\mathcal{X}\to\mathrm{Sch}$ is a stack (in the étale topology) iff for every cover $\{U_i\to U\}$: The stack axiom has two layers: isomorphism sheaves must glue locally, and objects given by compatible local data (a descent datum satisfying the cocycle on triple overlaps) must glue to a global object unique up to unique isomorphism. The stack axiom has two layers: isomorphism sheaves must glue locally, and objects given by compatible local data (a descent datum satisfying the cocycle on triple overlaps) must glue to a global…

q: Let $G$ be a groupoid with three objects, each having automorphism group $\mathbb{Z}/2$, all connected by isomorphisms. How many isomorphism classes does $\pi_0(G)$ have?
text: Let $G$ be a groupoid with three objects, each having automorphism group $\mathbb{Z}/2$, all connected by isomorphisms. How many isomorphism classes does $\pi_0(G)$ have? All three objects are connected by isomorphisms, so they form a single isomorphism class: $\pi_0(G)$ has one element. The three $\mathbb{Z}/2$ automorphism groups are what is lost when one collapses to $\pi_0$.

q: A fibered category $p:\mathcal{X}\to\mathcal{C}$ satisfies descent for a cover $\{U_i\to U\}$ but the isomorphism-sheaf condition fails for some pair of objects. Which axiom of a stack does this violate?
text: A fibered category $p:\mathcal{X}\to\mathcal{C}$ satisfies descent for a cover $\{U_i\to U\}$ but the isomorphism-sheaf condition fails for some pair of objects. Which axiom of a stack does this violate? A stack requires two conditions: (1) $\mathrm{Isom}(x,y)$ is a sheaf (morphism sheaf axiom) and (2) descent data for objects glue. Failure of the Isom sheaf condition violates the first axiom while leaving the second intact. Descent is effective only if isomorphism sheaves are actually sheaves.

q: Which of these is a counterexample showing that the naive moduli set-functor $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}^{\mathrm{naive}}:\mathrm{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}\to\mathrm{Set}$ (assigning iso-classes of elliptic curves) is NOT a sheaf in the étale topology?
text: Which of these is a counterexample showing that the naive moduli set-functor $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}^{\mathrm{naive}}:\mathrm{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}\to\mathrm{Set}$ (assigning iso-classes of elliptic curves) is NOT a sheaf in the étale topology? Twisting a constant family by a non-trivial $\mu_2$-torsor (via the $[-1]$ automorphism) over an étale cover produces an isotrivial but non-trivial family. The cocycle data exist, but they glue to different isomorphism-class representatives depending on the chosen descent isomorphisms — violating sheafness. Automorphisms collapse objects with $j$-invariant $0$ or $1728$.

q: Let $\mathcal{C}$ be the groupoid with objects $\{A,B,C\}$, morphisms forming a single isomorphism class, and $\mathrm{Aut}(A)=\{e\}$. After passing to $\pi_0$, how many objects remain?
text: Let $\mathcal{C}$ be the groupoid with objects $\{A,B,C\}$, morphisms forming a single isomorphism class, and $\mathrm{Aut}(A)=\{e\}$. After passing to $\pi_0$, how many objects remain? All three objects are isomorphic, so they represent a single isomorphism class. $|\pi_0(\mathcal{C})|=1$. The trivial automorphism groups are irrelevant to $\pi_0$. Automorphism groups give isotropy, not multiplicity.

q: The classifying stack $BG$ is defined by $BG(R)=$ groupoid of $G$-torsors on $\Spec\,R$. Why can $BG$ not be a scheme?
text: The classifying stack $BG$ is defined by $BG(R)=$ groupoid of $G$-torsors on $\Spec\,R$. Why can $BG$ not be a scheme? A scheme's functor of points lands in sets, so its geometric points have trivial automorphism groups. $BG$ has automorphism group $G$ at its trivial torsor, which records pure automorphism data and prevents representability by any scheme.

q: How many isomorphism classes of $\mathbb{Z}/3$-torsors are there on $\Spec\,\mathbb{F}_q$ for a finite field $\mathbb{F}_q$? (Equivalently, $|H^1(\Spec\,\mathbb{F}_q,\mathbb{Z}/3)|$, using that the absolute Galois group of $\mathbb{F}_q$ is $\widehat{\mathbb{Z}}$ and $\Hom(\widehat{\mathbb{Z}},\mathbb{Z}/3)=\mathbb{Z}/3$.)
text: How many isomorphism classes of $\mathbb{Z}/3$-torsors are there on $\Spec\,\mathbb{F}_q$ for a finite field $\mathbb{F}_q$? (Equivalently, $|H^1(\Spec\,\mathbb{F}_q,\mathbb{Z}/3)|$, using that the absolute Galois group of $\mathbb{F}_q$ is $\widehat{\mathbb{Z}}$ and $\Hom(\widehat{\mathbb{Z}},\mathbb{Z}/3)=\mathbb{Z}/3$.) $\Hom_{\text{cont}}(\widehat{\mathbb{Z}},\mathbb{Z}/3)\cong \mathbb{Z}/3$ has 3 elements, one for each element of $\mathbb{Z}/3$. Hence $|\pi_0(B(\mathbb{Z}/3)(\mathbb{F}_q))|=3$.

q: For $G=\mathbb{Z}/2$ over $\mathbb{R}$: $\Gal(\mathbb{C}/\mathbb{R})=\mathbb{Z}/2$, so $|\Hom(\mathbb{Z}/2,\mathbb{Z}/2)|=?$ (this counts iso classes of $\mathbb{Z}/2$-torsors over $\Spec\,\mathbb{R}$).
text: For $G=\mathbb{Z}/2$ over $\mathbb{R}$: $\Gal(\mathbb{C}/\mathbb{R})=\mathbb{Z}/2$, so $|\Hom(\mathbb{Z}/2,\mathbb{Z}/2)|=?$ (this counts iso classes of $\mathbb{Z}/2$-torsors over $\Spec\,\mathbb{R}$). $\Hom(\mathbb{Z}/2,\mathbb{Z}/2)=\{0,\mathrm{id}\}$ has 2 elements: the trivial torsor and the non-trivial one corresponding to $\Spec\,\mathbb{C}\to\Spec\,\mathbb{R}$.

q: For any algebraic group $G$, the inertia stack $I(BG)=BG\times_{BG\times BG}BG$ is equivalent to which stack?
text: For any algebraic group $G$, the inertia stack $I(BG)=BG\times_{BG\times BG}BG$ is equivalent to which stack? The inertia stack of $BG$ parametrises pairs (torsor, automorphism), which is the same data as a $G$-torsor together with a section of the associated group scheme — i.e., an element of $G$ up to conjugation. This is exactly $[G/G]$ for the conjugation action. The inertia stack parameterizes objects with an automorphism.

q: For $G=\mathbb{Z}/5$ over an algebraically closed field $k$ with $\mathrm{char}(k)\ne 5$, how many isomorphism classes of $G$-torsors are there over $\Spec\,k$?
text: For $G=\mathbb{Z}/5$ over an algebraically closed field $k$ with $\mathrm{char}(k)\ne 5$, how many isomorphism classes of $G$-torsors are there over $\Spec\,k$? Over an algebraically closed field, $H^1_{\text{ét}}(\Spec\,k,G)=0$ for any finite group $G$ of order coprime to the characteristic. So there is exactly $1$ isomorphism class: the trivial torsor. Use $|H^1(\Spec\,k, \mathbb{Z}/5)| = 1$.

q: The correct chain of implications among stack conditions is:
text: The correct chain of implications among stack conditions is: Every scheme is an algebraic space (trivial groupoid structure); every algebraic space is a DM stack (étale atlas, trivial automorphisms); every DM stack is an Artin stack (étale implies smooth). The reverse implications all fail: $B\mathbb{G}_m$ is Artin but not DM; $[\mathbb{A}^1/\mu_2]$ is DM but not an algebraic space. Separated implies quasi-separated; Artin is weaker than Deligne-Mumford.

q: A $T$-point of the quotient stack $[X/G]$ is:
text: A $T$-point of the quotient stack $[X/G]$ is: By definition $[X/G](T)$ is the groupoid of pairs $(P\to T,\ \phi:P\to X)$ with $P$ a $G$-torsor and $\phi$ equivariant. Setting $X=\mathrm{pt}$ recovers $BG$; setting $G=\{e\}$ recovers $X$ itself.

q: When does $[X/G]$ coincide (as stacks) with the quotient scheme $X/G$?
text: When does $[X/G]$ coincide (as stacks) with the quotient scheme $X/G$? If the action is free, every stabiliser is trivial and no automorphism data needs to be remembered, so $[X/G]$ is the ordinary quotient $X/G$. Non-free actions create non-trivial stabilisers and force stacky structure.

q: For $G=\mu_3$ acting on $\mathbb{A}^1$ by $\zeta\cdot x = \zeta x$, the stabiliser at $x=0$ is all of $\mu_3$ and is trivial elsewhere. What is the order of the automorphism group of the origin in $[\mathbb{A}^1/\mu_3]$?
text: For $G=\mu_3$ acting on $\mathbb{A}^1$ by $\zeta\cdot x = \zeta x$, the stabiliser at $x=0$ is all of $\mu_3$ and is trivial elsewhere. What is the order of the automorphism group of the origin in $[\mathbb{A}^1/\mu_3]$? $\mathrm{Stab}_{\mu_3}(0)=\mu_3$ has order $3$. Geometrically this is the stacky enhancement at the fixed point; away from $0$ the quotient is an ordinary scheme.

q: Let $G=\mathbb{G}_m$ act on $X=\mathbb{A}^2\setminus\{0\}$ by $\lambda\cdot(x,y)=(\lambda x,\lambda y)$. The quotient stack $[X/G]$ is equivalent to which classical geometric object?
text: Let $G=\mathbb{G}_m$ act on $X=\mathbb{A}^2\setminus\{0\}$ by $\lambda\cdot(x,y)=(\lambda x,\lambda y)$. The quotient stack $[X/G]$ is equivalent to which classical geometric object? $\mathbb{G}_m$ acts freely on $\mathbb{A}^2\setminus\{0\}$ (no non-trivial stabilisers), so $[X/G]$ is an algebraic space and in fact coincides with the ordinary quotient scheme $\mathbb{P}^1$. The scheme-theoretic quotient is $\mathbb{P}^1$; stacky data is trivial here.

q: A $T$-point of $[\mathrm{pt}/G]=BG$ is a $G$-torsor on $T$. What is the correct chain of equivalences proving $[\mathrm{pt}/G]=BG$?
text: A $T$-point of $[\mathrm{pt}/G]=BG$ is a $G$-torsor on $T$. What is the correct chain of equivalences proving $[\mathrm{pt}/G]=BG$? A $T$-point of $[X/G]$ is $(P\to T, \phi:P\to X)$ with $P$ a $G$-torsor and $\phi$ equivariant. For $X=\mathrm{pt}$, $\phi$ is forced to be the unique map $P\to\mathrm{pt}$, so the data reduces to just the $G$-torsor $P\to T$. Hence $[\mathrm{pt}/G](T)\simeq BG(T)$ for all $T$. Unwind definitions: principal bundle over $T$ is a $G$-torsor.

q: For $G=\mu_2$ acting on $\mathbb{A}^1$ by $\zeta\cdot x=-x$, the coarse moduli space of $[\mathbb{A}^1/\mu_2]$ is $\mathbb{A}^1$ (via $x\mapsto x^2$). At the stacky point $x=0$, the automorphism group has order?
text: For $G=\mu_2$ acting on $\mathbb{A}^1$ by $\zeta\cdot x=-x$, the coarse moduli space of $[\mathbb{A}^1/\mu_2]$ is $\mathbb{A}^1$ (via $x\mapsto x^2$). At the stacky point $x=0$, the automorphism group has order? The stabiliser of $0$ under the $\mu_2$ action $x\mapsto -x$ is all of $\mu_2$, which has order $2$. Away from $0$ the stabiliser is trivial. Inertia at the origin is $\mu_2$; elsewhere trivial.

q: Which condition distinguishes a Deligne-Mumford stack from a general Artin stack?
text: Which condition distinguishes a Deligne-Mumford stack from a general Artin stack? Artin requires a smooth atlas and a representable diagonal; DM additionally requires the atlas to be étale, equivalently the diagonal to be unramified. This forces automorphism schemes to be $0$-dimensional (finite and unramified).

q: Which of the following stacks is Artin but NOT Deligne-Mumford?
text: Which of the following stacks is Artin but NOT Deligne-Mumford? $B\mathbb{G}_m$ has automorphism group $\mathbb{G}_m$, which is $1$-dimensional — so it is Artin but not DM. $B\mu_n$ and $[\mathbb{A}^1/\mu_3]$ have finite automorphism groups, and $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ has finite automorphisms away from char $2,3$.

q: What is the dimension of the automorphism group at the generic geometric point of $B\mathbb{G}_m$?
text: What is the dimension of the automorphism group at the generic geometric point of $B\mathbb{G}_m$? $B\mathbb{G}_m$ has automorphism group $\mathbb{G}_m$ (the multiplicative group) at every geometric point, which is $1$-dimensional. That positive dimension is exactly why $B\mathbb{G}_m$ fails to be DM.

q: Which is the correct order of steps in proving that an Artin stack $\mathcal{X}$ with an étale atlas is Deligne-Mumford?
text: Which is the correct order of steps in proving that an Artin stack $\mathcal{X}$ with an étale atlas is Deligne-Mumford? The standard proof: (1) representability of the diagonal is required for any Artin stack; (2) if the diagonal is also unramified, automorphism groups are finite and unramified (0-dimensional); (3) this is equivalent to the existence of an étale atlas, the defining condition of DM. Find an atlas, then check fibers are etale.

q: Which is a counterexample showing that $B\mathbb{G}_m$ is not Deligne-Mumford?
text: Which is a counterexample showing that $B\mathbb{G}_m$ is not Deligne-Mumford? A stack is DM iff its diagonal is unramified (equivalently, automorphism schemes are 0-dimensional). Since $\mathbb{G}_m$ is 1-dimensional, the inertia stack $I(B\mathbb{G}_m)\cong[\mathbb{G}_m/\mathbb{G}_m]$ has positive-dimensional fibers, so $B\mathbb{G}_m$ is Artin but not DM. The inertia group $\mathbb{G}_m$ is positive-dimensional, failing DM.

q: An Artin stack $\mathcal{X}$ has a smooth atlas $U\to\mathcal{X}$ with $U$ a scheme of dimension $3$ and the fibers of the atlas map have dimension $1$. What is $\dim(\mathcal{X})$?
text: An Artin stack $\mathcal{X}$ has a smooth atlas $U\to\mathcal{X}$ with $U$ a scheme of dimension $3$ and the fibers of the atlas map have dimension $1$. What is $\dim(\mathcal{X})$? For a smooth atlas $U\to\mathcal{X}$ of relative dimension $d$, $\dim(\mathcal{X})=\dim(U)-d=3-1=2$. Here the fiber dimension $1$ corresponds to the $1$-dimensional automorphism group typical of an Artin (non-DM) stack. Dimension of the stack is atlas dimension minus fiber dimension.

q: Which condition separates a Deligne-Mumford stack from a general Artin (algebraic) stack?
text: Which condition separates a Deligne-Mumford stack from a general Artin (algebraic) stack? The defining upgrade from Artin to DM is the étale (versus merely smooth) atlas, which forces automorphism schemes to be finite and unramified. $BG$ for $G$ finite is DM; $BG$ for $G$ positive-dimensional (e.g. $B\mathbb{G}_m$) is Artin but not DM.

q: Keel-Mori produces a coarse moduli map $\pi:\mathcal{X}\to X$ for a finite-type DM stack with finite inertia. Which statement about $\pi$ is true?
text: Keel-Mori produces a coarse moduli map $\pi:\mathcal{X}\to X$ for a finite-type DM stack with finite inertia. Which statement about $\pi$ is true? $\pi$ is a bijection on geometric points and any scheme-valued map from $\mathcal{X}$ factors uniquely through $\pi$. But the fiber of $\pi$ over a stacky point is a classifying stack $B\mathrm{Aut}$, so automorphism data is discarded; e.g. $\mathrm{CH}^*(\mathcal{M}_{1,1})_{\mathbb{Z}}=\mathbb{Z}[t]/(12t)\ne \mathrm{CH}^*(\mathbb{A}^1)$.

q: For $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ over $\Spec\,\mathbb{Z}[1/6]$, what is the order of the automorphism group of the elliptic curve $E:y^2=x^3+1$ (the $j=0$ point)?
text: For $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ over $\Spec\,\mathbb{Z}[1/6]$, what is the order of the automorphism group of the elliptic curve $E:y^2=x^3+1$ (the $j=0$ point)? At $j=0$, $E$ has complex multiplication by $\mathbb{Z}[\zeta_3]$ and $\mathrm{Aut}(E)=\mu_6$, of order $6$. After quotienting by the generic $\mu_2$ ($[-1]$), residual automorphism is $\mu_3$, so locally $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ looks like $[\mathbb{A}^1/\mu_3]$ near $j=0$.

q: The correct sequence of steps in constructing the coarse moduli space via Keel-Mori is:
text: The correct sequence of steps in constructing the coarse moduli space via Keel-Mori is: Keel-Mori: after checking finite inertia and finite type, one locally reduces to a $\mu_n$-gerbe over an algebraic space, handles the tame case via étale-local arguments, then glues the local coarse spaces using the étale topology to produce a global algebraic space (or scheme in good cases). Construct the space, then check universal property.

q: Which is a counterexample showing the coarse space map $\pi:\mathcal{M}_{1,1}\to\mathbb{A}^1_j$ does NOT preserve integral Chow rings?
text: Which is a counterexample showing the coarse space map $\pi:\mathcal{M}_{1,1}\to\mathbb{A}^1_j$ does NOT preserve integral Chow rings? The Hodge line bundle $\lambda$ on $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ satisfies $12\lambda=0$ in $\mathrm{Pic}(\mathcal{M}_{1,1})$ (reflecting the $\mu_2$ automorphism at every point and the $\mu_6$ at $j=0$), giving $\mathrm{CH}^1(\mathcal{M}_{1,1})\cong\mathbb{Z}/12$. Meanwhile $\mathrm{CH}^1(\mathbb{A}^1)=0$, so $\pi^*$ is not an isomorphism integrally. Coarse spaces have different integral classes at orbifold points.

q: For the DM stack $[\mathbb{A}^1/\mu_n]$ (with $\mu_n$ acting by scaling), the coarse moduli map $\pi:[\mathbb{A}^1/\mu_n]\to\mathbb{A}^1$ sends the stacky point $0$ to $0$. The local Picard group of the stack at $0$ is $\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}$. For $n=4$, what is the order of this local Picard group?
text: For the DM stack $[\mathbb{A}^1/\mu_n]$ (with $\mu_n$ acting by scaling), the coarse moduli map $\pi:[\mathbb{A}^1/\mu_n]\to\mathbb{A}^1$ sends the stacky point $0$ to $0$. The local Picard group of the stack at $0$ is $\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}$. For $n=4$, what is the order of this local Picard group? The stacky point $0\in[\mathbb{A}^1/\mu_n]$ has automorphism group $\mu_n$, contributing a local $\mathrm{Pic}\cong\widehat{\mu_n}\cong\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}$. For $n=4$ this has order $4$. The coarse map is degree one but ramifies at the origin.

title: Algebraic cycles and rational equivalence
blurb: An algebraic $k$-cycle on $X$ is a finite formal $\mathbb{Z}$-linear combination of closed subvarieties of dimension $k$. Two cycles are rationally equivalent if their difference is the principal divisor of a rational function on a $(k+1)$-cycle — the right replacement for homotopy in the algebraic setting.

title: Chow groups $A_*(X)$
blurb: The Chow group $A_k(X)$ is the free abelian group on $k$-cycles modulo rational equivalence. The total Chow group $A_*(X) = \bigoplus_k A_k(X)$ is the algebraic analogue of singular homology — it sees algebraic but not transcendental cycles.

title: Intersection product on smooth $X$
blurb: On a smooth variety $X$, two transverse subvarieties $Y, Z$ of complementary codimension intersect in finitely many points; the count gives the intersection number $Y \cdot Z \in A_0(X)$. The product extends to all of $A_*(X)$ via moving lemmas, making $A^*(X)$ a graded ring.

title: $A^*(\mathbb{P}^n) = \mathbb{Z}[h]/(h^{n+1})$
blurb: The Chow ring of projective space is generated by the hyperplane class $h$ with relation $h^{n+1} = 0$. Bezout's theorem $C \cdot D = (\deg C)(\deg D)$ for plane curves is the case $n = 2$ — multiplication of degrees in $\mathbb{Z}[h]/(h^3)$.

title: Chern classes
blurb: A vector bundle $E$ of rank $r$ on a smooth $X$ has Chern classes $c_i(E) \in A^i(X)$ for $i = 0, \ldots, r$, encoding obstructions to $E$ trivializing. The total Chern class $c(E) = 1 + c_1 + \cdots + c_r$ is multiplicative in short exact sequences.

title: Grothendieck–Riemann–Roch (preview)
blurb: GRR replaces the classical Riemann–Roch with an equality in $A^*(X) \otimes \mathbb{Q}$ involving the Chern character $\mathrm{ch}(E)$ and the Todd class $\mathrm{td}(T_X)$. It packages every Riemann–Roch in algebraic geometry — for curves, surfaces, and beyond — as instances of one functorial statement.

q: Which of the following is the cleanest definition of rational equivalence on $Z_k(X)$?
text: Which of the following is the cleanest definition of rational equivalence on $Z_k(X)$? Rational equivalence is an algebraic homotopy: $\alpha - \beta = \sum \mathrm{div}_{W_j}(f_j)$ with $\dim W_j = k+1$. Singular homology is a coarser invariant; degree is much coarser still. Think of $\mathbb{P}^1$ as the algebraic interval — divisors of rational functions are the connecting homotopies.

q: Match each cycle on $\mathbb{P}^1$ with the unique class it represents in $A_0(\mathbb{P}^1)$.
text: Match each cycle on $\mathbb{P}^1$ with the unique class it represents in $A_0(\mathbb{P}^1)$. $A_0(\mathbb{P}^1) = \mathbb{Z}$ via degree. A degree-$0$ cycle is $0$; a degree-$1$ cycle is $[\mathrm{pt}]$; a degree-$2$ cycle is $2[\mathrm{pt}]$. Compute the degree of each right-hand cycle.

q: Find the flawed step in this attempted proof that $[P] - [Q] = 0$ in $A_0(C)$ for a smooth projective curve $C$ of genus $g \ge 1$ and any two points $P, Q$.
text: Find the flawed step in this attempted proof that $[P] - [Q] = 0$ in $A_0(C)$ for a smooth projective curve $C$ of genus $g \ge 1$ and any two points $P, Q$. Step 1 is the bug: a rational function with divisor $[P] - [Q]$ exists iff $[P] = [Q]$ in $\mathrm{Pic}^0(C)$, i.e. iff $P, Q$ have the same image under Abel–Jacobi. For $g \ge 1$ the Jacobian is non-trivial, so generic $P \neq Q$ are inequivalent. Step 4 thus fails: $A_0(C) \cong \mathbb{Z} \oplus \mathrm{Pic}^0(C)$. On $\mathbb{P}^1$ every degree-$0$ divisor is principal — but $\mathbb{P}^1$ is the genus-$0$ exception.

q: What is the rank of $A_*(\mathbb{P}^4)$ as a $\mathbb{Z}$-module?
text: What is the rank of $A_*(\mathbb{P}^4)$ as a $\mathbb{Z}$-module? $A_*(\mathbb{P}^n) = \bigoplus_{k=0}^n \mathbb{Z} \cdot [L^k] \cong \mathbb{Z}^{n+1}$, with one generator in each codimension $0, 1, \ldots, n$. For $n = 4$ that's $5$. Linear subspaces of dimension $0, 1, \ldots, n$ generate the group, one per dimension.

q: What is $A_*(\mathbb{A}^n)$?
text: What is $A_*(\mathbb{A}^n)$? Every cycle on $\mathbb{A}^n$ of dimension $< n$ is rationally equivalent to $0$ via a translation family pushing it to infinity. So $A_*(\mathbb{A}^n) = A_n(\mathbb{A}^n) \cong \mathbb{Z}$, generated by the fundamental class $[\mathbb{A}^n]$. Affine space is contractible in the rational-equivalence sense.

q: Which of the following are true about Chow groups $A_*(X)$ for smooth quasi-projective $X$?
text: Which of the following are true about Chow groups $A_*(X)$ for smooth quasi-projective $X$? Push-forward (covariant, proper) and pull-back (contravariant, flat) are the two basic functorialities. The cycle map exists but generally fails to be either injective or surjective — Griffiths' work on Fano threefolds gives counterexamples to injectivity, and transcendental Hodge classes obstruct surjectivity. Simple-connectedness has no bearing on $A_*$.

q: What is the rank of $A_*(\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1)$ as a $\mathbb{Z}$-module?
text: What is the rank of $A_*(\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1)$ as a $\mathbb{Z}$-module? $A_*(\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1) = \mathbb{Z}[h, k]/(h^2, k^2)$ has $\mathbb{Z}$-basis $\{1, h, k, hk\}$, so rank $4$. (Equivalently, the Künneth formula: $A_*(X \times Y) \cong A_*(X) \otimes A_*(Y)$ when one factor has a cell decomposition; rank $2 \cdot 2 = 4$.) Künneth + the answer for $A_*(\mathbb{P}^1)$.

q: Two distinct lines $L_1, L_2$ in $\mathbb{P}^2$ meet in $[L_1] \cdot [L_2] = ?$ point(s) (counted with multiplicity).
text: Two distinct lines $L_1, L_2$ in $\mathbb{P}^2$ meet in $[L_1] \cdot [L_2] = ?$ point(s) (counted with multiplicity). Each line has class $h \in A^1(\mathbb{P}^2) = \mathbb{Z}$, so $[L_1] \cdot [L_2] = h \cdot h = h^2 \in A^2(\mathbb{P}^2) = \mathbb{Z}$, with degree $1$. Two lines in the projective plane meet in exactly one point.

q: On $\mathbb{P}^3$, a smooth quadric surface $Q$ (degree $2$) and a smooth cubic surface $S$ (degree $3$) intersect generically in a curve of degree $\deg(Q \cdot S) = ?$.
text: On $\mathbb{P}^3$, a smooth quadric surface $Q$ (degree $2$) and a smooth cubic surface $S$ (degree $3$) intersect generically in a curve of degree $\deg(Q \cdot S) = ?$. $[Q] = 2h$ and $[S] = 3h$ in $A^*(\mathbb{P}^3) = \mathbb{Z}[h]/(h^4)$. Their product is $[Q] \cdot [S] = 6 h^2$, the class of a curve of degree $6$ (the degree of a $1$-cycle in $\mathbb{P}^3$ is the coefficient of $h^2$ when paired with the line class $h$). Multiply degrees: this is the projective Bézout in $\mathbb{P}^3$.

q: Why does the moving lemma matter for defining $[Y] \cdot [Z]$ when $Y, Z$ do not meet transversally?
text: Why does the moving lemma matter for defining $[Y] \cdot [Z]$ when $Y, Z$ do not meet transversally? On smooth quasi-projective $X$, the moving lemma (Chow, Roberts) lets you replace either factor by a rationally-equivalent cycle in general position with the other; transverse-only intersections then define the product on classes. On singular $X$ the moving lemma fails; one falls back to the bivariant theory of Fulton–MacPherson. Definitions need representatives in 'general position'; moving moves them there.

q: On $\mathbb{P}^2$, a smooth conic $C$ (degree $2$) and a tangent line $L$ at one of its points: what is the intersection multiplicity $i_p(C, L)$ at the tangent point $p$?
text: On $\mathbb{P}^2$, a smooth conic $C$ (degree $2$) and a tangent line $L$ at one of its points: what is the intersection multiplicity $i_p(C, L)$ at the tangent point $p$? $\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{P}^2, p}/(F, G)$ has length $2$ when $L$ is tangent to $C$ at $p$ — equivalently, two transverse intersections collide as you specialise from a secant to a tangent. Bézout still gives $\deg(C \cdot L) = 2$, but the intersection is concentrated at one point with $i_p = 2$. Bézout gives the total — what makes the count survive when two transverse points collide?

q: What is the self-intersection $h \cdot h$ on $\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1$, where $h = [\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathrm{pt}]$ is the class of a horizontal ruling?
text: What is the self-intersection $h \cdot h$ on $\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1$, where $h = [\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathrm{pt}]$ is the class of a horizontal ruling? Two horizontal rulings $\mathbb{P}^1 \times \{a\}$ and $\mathbb{P}^1 \times \{b\}$ never meet for $a \neq b$, so by the moving lemma $h \cdot h = 0$ in $A^*(\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1) = \mathbb{Z}[h, k]/(h^2, k^2)$. Only $h \cdot k = [\mathrm{pt}]$ is non-zero. Two parallel rulings of the same type don't meet — move one to verify.

q: In $A^*(\mathbb{P}^5) = \mathbb{Z}[h]/(h^6)$, what is $h^3 \cdot h^2$? (Enter the integer coefficient if the result is $c \cdot h^k$ for some $k \le 5$, or $0$ if the product vanishes.)
text: In $A^*(\mathbb{P}^5) = \mathbb{Z}[h]/(h^6)$, what is $h^3 \cdot h^2$? (Enter the integer coefficient if the result is $c \cdot h^k$ for some $k \le 5$, or $0$ if the product vanishes.) $h^3 \cdot h^2 = h^5 \in A^5(\mathbb{P}^5)$, the class of a point — coefficient $1$. (We didn't hit the relation $h^6 = 0$ because $3 + 2 = 5 \le 5$.) Add the exponents and check whether you exceed $n = 5$.

q: In $A^*(\mathbb{P}^4)$, what is $h^3 \cdot h^3$?
text: In $A^*(\mathbb{P}^4)$, what is $h^3 \cdot h^3$? $3 + 3 = 6 > 4$, so $h^6 = 0$ by the relation $h^{n+1} = h^5 = 0$ which forces all higher powers to vanish. Check whether the sum of exponents exceeds the dimension of the ambient space.

q: Order these computations from smallest to largest as integers (degree of the resulting $0$-cycle on the indicated $\mathbb{P}^n$).
text: Order these computations from smallest to largest as integers (degree of the resulting $0$-cycle on the indicated $\mathbb{P}^n$). First: $h^2 \cdot h^2 = h^4 = $ class of point on $\mathbb{P}^4$, degree $1$. Second: $(2h)(3h) = 6 h^2$, degree $6$ on $\mathbb{P}^2$. Third: $(4h)(5h)(6h) = 120\, h^3$, degree $120$ on $\mathbb{P}^3$. So the order $1 < 6 < 120$. Compute each as an integer first, then sort.

q: What is the total Chern class $c(\mathcal{O}(2) \oplus \mathcal{O}(3))$ on $\mathbb{P}^2$?
text: What is the total Chern class $c(\mathcal{O}(2) \oplus \mathcal{O}(3))$ on $\mathbb{P}^2$? By Whitney multiplicativity: $c(\mathcal{O}(2)) = 1 + 2h$, $c(\mathcal{O}(3)) = 1 + 3h$. Product: $(1 + 2h)(1 + 3h) = 1 + 5h + 6h^2$ in $A^*(\mathbb{P}^2)$. $c(\mathcal{O}(d)) = 1 + dh$ for line bundles; multiply for direct sums.

q: On $\mathbb{P}^2$, what is $c_2(T_{\mathbb{P}^2})$ as an integer (writing it as $c_2 = N \cdot h^2$, give $N$)?
text: On $\mathbb{P}^2$, what is $c_2(T_{\mathbb{P}^2})$ as an integer (writing it as $c_2 = N \cdot h^2$, give $N$)? Euler sequence $0 \to \mathcal{O} \to \mathcal{O}(1)^{\oplus 3} \to T_{\mathbb{P}^2} \to 0$. By multiplicativity (after tensoring properly): $c(\mathcal{O})\, c(T_{\mathbb{P}^2}) = c(\mathcal{O}(1))^3 = (1+h)^3 = 1 + 3h + 3h^2 + h^3$. Truncate at $h^3 = 0$: $c(T_{\mathbb{P}^2}) = 1 + 3h + 3h^2$, so $c_2 = 3$. Use the Euler sequence and read off the coefficient of $h^2$ in $(1+h)^3$.

q: Which of the following are axioms of Chern classes (i.e., used to characterise them, not derived)?
text: Which of the following are axioms of Chern classes (i.e., used to characterise them, not derived)? Naturality, Whitney sum, and the line-bundle normalisation are Grothendieck's three axioms; vanishing $c_i$ above the rank is a consequence (every rank-$r$ bundle has all non-trivial Chern classes in degrees $\le r$). The axioms must be enough to compute everything; the rank vanishing is forced by the splitting principle.

q: On $\mathbb{P}^3$, compute $c_2(T_{\mathbb{P}^3})$ as the integer coefficient of $h^2$.
text: On $\mathbb{P}^3$, compute $c_2(T_{\mathbb{P}^3})$ as the integer coefficient of $h^2$. Euler: $c(T_{\mathbb{P}^3}) = (1+h)^4 = 1 + 4h + 6h^2 + 4h^3 + h^4$. Truncate at $h^4 = 0$: $c(T_{\mathbb{P}^3}) = 1 + 4h + 6h^2 + 4h^3$. So $c_2 = 6$. Generalize the $\mathbb{P}^2$ Euler-sequence calculation: $(1+h)^{n+1}$.

q: Why does the splitting principle work? (i.e. why can we pretend a rank-$r$ vector bundle $E$ splits as a direct sum of line bundles when computing Chern classes?)
text: Why does the splitting principle work? (i.e. why can we pretend a rank-$r$ vector bundle $E$ splits as a direct sum of line bundles when computing Chern classes?) The flag bundle $\mathbb{F}(E)$ parametrises complete flags in fibres of $E$; on it the pulled-back $E$ acquires a tautological filtration with line-bundle quotients. The pull-back $A^*(X) \to A^*(\mathbb{F}(E))$ is split injective (Chow's projective-bundle formula iterated), so any identity true on $\mathbb{F}(E)$ — where $E$ does split — descends to $X$. Vector bundles emphatically do not split in general (e.g., $T_{\mathbb{P}^2}$). Look at the flag bundle of $E$ — it adds the splitting data without changing the Chern classes.

q: By Riemann–Roch on a smooth projective curve $C$ of genus $g = 3$, compute $\chi(C, \mathcal{O}(D))$ where $\deg D = 7$.
text: By Riemann–Roch on a smooth projective curve $C$ of genus $g = 3$, compute $\chi(C, \mathcal{O}(D))$ where $\deg D = 7$. $\chi(C, \mathcal{O}(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g = 7 + 1 - 3 = 5$. $\chi = \deg D + 1 - g$ for any divisor on a smooth projective curve.

q: Compute $\chi(\mathbb{P}^2, \mathcal{O}(4))$ via Riemann–Roch / GRR.
text: Compute $\chi(\mathbb{P}^2, \mathcal{O}(4))$ via Riemann–Roch / GRR. $\chi(\mathbb{P}^2, \mathcal{O}(d)) = \binom{d+2}{2} = \binom{6}{2} = 15$. (For $d \ge 0$ this also equals $\dim H^0(\mathbb{P}^2, \mathcal{O}(d))$ since higher cohomology vanishes.) GRR on $\mathbb{P}^2$ for $\mathcal{O}(d)$ gives $(d+1)(d+2)/2$.

q: Arrange the steps of GRR on $X = \mathbb{P}^2$ for the sheaf $\mathcal{F} = \mathcal{O}(d)$, in the order one carries them out.
text: Arrange the steps of GRR on $X = \mathbb{P}^2$ for the sheaf $\mathcal{F} = \mathcal{O}(d)$, in the order one carries them out. Compute each ingredient, multiply, then take degree (= push-forward to a point). This is the universal recipe for Hirzebruch–Riemann–Roch on $\mathbb{P}^n$. First gather the ingredients (ch and td), then combine them, then specialise.

q: On a smooth projective surface $S$ with $K_S$ trivial (e.g. a K3 surface, where $\chi(\mathcal{O}_S) = 2$), compute $\chi(S, \mathcal{O}(D))$ for a divisor $D$ with $D \cdot D = 4$. (Use the surface Riemann–Roch $\chi = \tfrac12 D \cdot (D - K_S) + \chi(\mathcal{O}_S)$.)
text: On a smooth projective surface $S$ with $K_S$ trivial (e.g. a K3 surface, where $\chi(\mathcal{O}_S) = 2$), compute $\chi(S, \mathcal{O}(D))$ for a divisor $D$ with $D \cdot D = 4$. (Use the surface Riemann–Roch $\chi = \tfrac12 D \cdot (D - K_S) + \chi(\mathcal{O}_S)$.) With $K_S = 0$: $\chi = \tfrac12 D \cdot D + \chi(\mathcal{O}_S) = \tfrac12 \cdot 4 + 2 = 4$. $K_S = 0$ kills the $-D \cdot K_S$ term; just plug into the formula.

q: Why is $\mathrm{td}(T_X)$ — rather than just $\mathrm{ch}(T_X)$ — the right correction term in GRR?
text: Why is $\mathrm{td}(T_X)$ — rather than just $\mathrm{ch}(T_X)$ — the right correction term in GRR? Hirzebruch chose td precisely because $\mathrm{td}(L)$ for $c_1(L) = \alpha$ equals $\alpha / (1 - e^{-\alpha})$, whose coefficients reproduce $\chi(\mathbb{P}^n, \mathcal{O}) = 1$ in all dimensions. Equivalently, $\int_{\mathbb{P}^n} \mathrm{td}(T_{\mathbb{P}^n}) = 1$, which uniquely characterises td among multiplicative classes. Test the formula on $\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{P}^n}$ — what value of $\chi$ does it predict?

title: What is a group scheme?
blurb: A group scheme over $S$ is an $S$-scheme $G$ equipped with multiplication $m: G \times_S G \to G$, inverse $\iota: G \to G$, and identity $e: S \to G$ satisfying the group axioms diagrammatically. Equivalently, a group object in the category of $S$-schemes.

title: Examples: $\mathbb{G}_a, \mathbb{G}_m, \mu_n, \alpha_p$
blurb: $\mathbb{G}_a = \Spec\,k[t]$ (additive), $\mathbb{G}_m = \Spec\,k[t, t^{-1}]$ (multiplicative), $\mu_n = \Spec\,k[t]/(t^n - 1)$ (roots of unity), and in characteristic $p$ the infinitesimal $\alpha_p = \Spec\,k[t]/(t^p)$. The four basic affine group schemes.

title: Affine group schemes ↔ commutative Hopf algebras
blurb: An affine group scheme $G = \Spec\,A$ is the same data as a commutative Hopf algebra $A$: comultiplication $\Delta: A \to A \otimes A$, counit $\epsilon: A \to k$, and antipode $S: A \to A$ from the multiplication, identity, and inverse maps.

title: Étale, connected, and infinitesimal pieces
blurb: Over a perfect field, every finite commutative group scheme decomposes canonically as $G$ as a semidirect product of a connected (infinitesimal) piece $G^\circ$ and an étale piece $G^{\acute{e}t}$, with $G^\circ$ connected (infinitesimal) and $G^{\acute{e}t}$ étale. In characteristic 0, $G^\circ$ is trivial and group schemes reduce to ordinary discrete groups; in char $p$, $\alpha_p, \mu_p$ live in $G^\circ$.

title: Lie algebra $\mathrm{Lie}(G)$
blurb: $\mathrm{Lie}(G) = \ker(G(k[\epsilon]/\epsilon^2) \to G(k))$ — the tangent space at the identity, with bracket from the adjoint action. Recovers the classical Lie algebra of a Lie group when $k = \mathbb{R}$ or $\mathbb{C}$ and $G$ is smooth.

title: $G$-torsors and $H^1(X, G)$
blurb: A $G$-torsor over $X$ is a scheme $P \to X$ with a free transitive $G$-action that is étale-locally trivial. Isomorphism classes of $G$-torsors are classified by the étale cohomology $H^1_{\acute{e}t}(X, G)$ — generalizes Galois cohomology and the Picard group ($G = \mathbb{G}_m$).

q: Which of the following are group schemes over $\mathbb{Z}$?
text: Which of the following are group schemes over $\mathbb{Z}$? $\mathrm{GL}_n$ and $\mathbb{G}_a$ are group schemes (one comes with matrix multiplication, the other with addition). The bare $\mathbb{A}^1$ without designated $(m, e, \iota)$ is just a scheme — same underlying scheme as $\mathbb{G}_a$ but the group structure is part of the data, not implicit. $\mathbb{P}^1$ admits no group-scheme structure (it's a non-trivial projective variety; the only connected projective group schemes are abelian varieties, and $\mathbb{P}^1$ has genus 0 not 1). A group scheme is a scheme equipped with $(m, e, \iota)$, not just a scheme.

q: For the affine group scheme $\mathbb{G}_a = \Spec\,k[t]$ (additive group), what is the comultiplication $\Delta\colon k[t] \to k[t] \otimes k[t]$ encoding the multiplication $m\colon \mathbb{G}_a \times \mathbb{G}_a \to \mathbb{G}_a$?
text: For the affine group scheme $\mathbb{G}_a = \Spec\,k[t]$ (additive group), what is the comultiplication $\Delta\colon k[t] \to k[t] \otimes k[t]$ encoding the multiplication $m\colon \mathbb{G}_a \times \mathbb{G}_a \to \mathbb{G}_a$? On $T$-points, $\mathbb{G}_a(T) = \Gamma(T,\mathcal{O}_T)$ with addition. So the multiplication map sends $(a, b) \mapsto a + b$, dualising to $\Delta(t) = t \otimes 1 + 1 \otimes t$. The choice $t \otimes t$ would encode multiplication of units, which is $\mathbb{G}_m$, not $\mathbb{G}_a$. Read off what the group law does to functions: $f(a+b)$ in terms of $f(a)$ and $f(b)$.

q: If $G$ is a group scheme over $S$, what does the functor of points $h_G\colon T \mapsto G(T) = \Hom_S(T, G)$ land in?
text: If $G$ is a group scheme over $S$, what does the functor of points $h_G\colon T \mapsto G(T) = \Hom_S(T, G)$ land in? By definition, the structure morphisms $(m, e, \iota)$ on $G$ make each $G(T)$ a group, and morphisms in $T$ induce group homomorphisms in $G(T)$. So $h_G$ factors through $\mathbf{Grp}$. This is the functor-of-points characterisation. What does the multiplication $m\colon G \times G \to G$ induce on $T$-points?

q: Compute $|\mu_{15}(\bar{\mathbb{F}_5})|$, the number of $\bar{\mathbb{F}_5}$-points of $\mu_{15} = \Spec\,\mathbb{F}_5[t]/(t^{15} - 1)$.
text: Compute $|\mu_{15}(\bar{\mathbb{F}_5})|$, the number of $\bar{\mathbb{F}_5}$-points of $\mu_{15} = \Spec\,\mathbb{F}_5[t]/(t^{15} - 1)$. Over $\bar{\mathbb{F}_5}$, $t^{15} - 1$ has $\gcd(15, 5)$-related splitting: write $15 = 3 \cdot 5$. Then $t^{15} - 1 = (t^3 - 1)^5$ in characteristic $5$, so the only roots are the 3rd roots of unity, of which there are 3 (since $\gcd(3,5) = 1$). General formula: $|\mu_n(\bar{\mathbb{F}_p})| = n / p^{v_p(n)}$ — divide $n$ by the largest power of $p$ that divides it. Factor out the $p$-part: $\mu_n = \mu_{p^k} \times \mu_{n/p^k}$ where $n = p^k m$ with $\gcd(m,p) = 1$. The $p^k$ piece is purely infinitesimal.

q: Over $\bar{\mathbb{F}_p}$, which of the following are étale (no infinitesimal piece)?
text: Over $\bar{\mathbb{F}_p}$, which of the following are étale (no infinitesimal piece)? Étale over $\bar{\mathbb{F}_p}$ ⇔ no $\bar{\mathbb{F}_p}$-multiplicity, i.e. the defining polynomial is separable. $\mu_n$ is étale iff $n$ is invertible in $k$, so for $\gcd(n,p) = 1$. The constant group scheme $\mathbb{Z}/n$ is always étale. $\mu_p$ has $t^p - 1 = (t-1)^p$ — connected, infinitesimal. $\alpha_p = \Spec\,k[t]/(t^p)$ is purely connected/infinitesimal. Étale ⇔ separable defining polynomial, which fails when char divides the multiplicity.

q: What is the order of $\mu_n$ as a finite group scheme over $\mathbb{Q}$ (where order is $\dim_{\mathbb{Q}} \Gamma(\mu_n, \mathcal{O}))$?
text: What is the order of $\mu_n$ as a finite group scheme over $\mathbb{Q}$ (where order is $\dim_{\mathbb{Q}} \Gamma(\mu_n, \mathcal{O}))$? $\mu_n = \Spec\,\mathbb{Q}[t]/(t^n - 1)$ has coordinate ring of $\mathbb{Q}$-dimension $n$ (basis $1, t, t^2, \ldots, t^{n-1}$). The Galois action splits this into eigenspaces by primitive $d$-th roots for $d \mid n$, but the rank — the order of the group scheme — is $n$. Distinguish from $|\mu_n(\mathbb{Q})|$, which depends on what's in $\mathbb{Q}$. Order of a finite group scheme = rank of its coordinate algebra over $k$, not the number of $k$-points.

q: Match each affine group scheme to its comultiplication $\Delta(t)$ on the indicated generator.
text: Match each affine group scheme to its comultiplication $\Delta(t)$ on the indicated generator. $\mathbb{G}_m$ and $\mu_n$ both have $\Delta(t) = t \otimes t$ — they encode the same multiplication law on units, just with different relations on $t$. $\mathbb{G}_a$ and $\alpha_p$ both have $\Delta(t) = t \otimes 1 + 1 \otimes t$ — additive comultiplication, with $\alpha_p$ adding the relation $t^p = 0$. Multiplicative groups have $\Delta(t) = t \otimes t$; additive groups have $\Delta(t) = t \otimes 1 + 1 \otimes t$.

q: What is the antipode $S\colon k[t, t^{-1}] \to k[t, t^{-1}]$ for the Hopf algebra of $\mathbb{G}_m$?
text: What is the antipode $S\colon k[t, t^{-1}] \to k[t, t^{-1}]$ for the Hopf algebra of $\mathbb{G}_m$? The antipode pulls back the inverse map $\iota\colon \mathbb{G}_m \to \mathbb{G}_m$, $a \mapsto a^{-1}$. So $S(t) = t^{-1}$. The check: $\mu \circ (\mathrm{id} \otimes S) \circ \Delta(t) = \mu(t \otimes t^{-1}) = t \cdot t^{-1} = 1 = \eta(\epsilon(t))$. The choice $S(t) = -t$ is for $\mathbb{G}_a$. What is the inverse of $a \in T^\times$? Pull that back along $\Delta$ via $a \mapsto a^{-1}$.

q: The category of affine group schemes over $k$ is equivalent to which category?
text: The category of affine group schemes over $k$ is equivalent to which category? $\Spec$ is contravariant: morphisms of affine schemes go opposite to morphisms of rings. So affine group schemes / $k$ are equivalent to the opposite of commutative Hopf algebras / $k$. (Equivalently: covariantly equivalent to the category of commutative Hopf algebras with arrows reversed.) Cocommutative Hopf algebras correspond to commutative group schemes, a subclass. Spec reverses arrows.

q: Let $A$ be the Hopf algebra of an affine group scheme $G$ over $k$. The Lie algebra $\mathrm{Lie}(G)$ can be recovered from $A$ as which of the following?
text: Let $A$ be the Hopf algebra of an affine group scheme $G$ over $k$. The Lie algebra $\mathrm{Lie}(G)$ can be recovered from $A$ as which of the following? $\mathrm{Lie}(G) = T_e G = \Hom_k(\mathfrak{m}_e/\mathfrak{m}_e^2, k)$ where $\mathfrak{m}_e = I$ is the maximal ideal at the identity. The cotangent space at the identity is $I/I^2$; the tangent space is its $k$-linear dual. The bracket on $\mathrm{Lie}(G)$ comes from the adjoint coaction, also encoded purely in the Hopf-algebra data. Tangent space at a closed point is dual to maximal-ideal-mod-its-square.

q: Cartier duality is an anti-equivalence on the category of finite commutative group schemes. For $G$ with Hopf algebra $A$, the Cartier dual $G^\vee$ has Hopf algebra:
text: Cartier duality is an anti-equivalence on the category of finite commutative group schemes. For $G$ with Hopf algebra $A$, the Cartier dual $G^\vee$ has Hopf algebra: Cartier duality on a finite Hopf algebra is the linear dual $A^* = \Hom_k(A, k)$, with the multiplication and comultiplication swapped (and similarly $\eta \leftrightarrow \epsilon$, $S^\vee = S$). For $G$ finite, $A$ is finite-dimensional so $A^*$ is again finite-dimensional and is again a (commutative, since $G$ commutative) Hopf algebra. Examples: $(\mathbb{Z}/n)^\vee = \mu_n$, $\alpha_p^\vee = \alpha_p$ (Cartier self-dual). On a finite-dimensional Hopf algebra, dualising swaps algebra and coalgebra structures.

q: What is the étale part of $\mu_p$ over a perfect field $k$ of characteristic $p$?
text: What is the étale part of $\mu_p$ over a perfect field $k$ of characteristic $p$? Over $k$ of char $p$, $\mu_p = \Spec\,k[t]/(t^p - 1) = \Spec\,k[t]/((t-1)^p)$ — the coordinate ring is local Artinian with one closed point, all nilpotents above. So $\mu_p$ is connected (infinitesimal) and its étale quotient is trivial. Equivalently: $\mu_p$ has only one $\bar k$-point. $t^p - 1 = (t-1)^p$ in characteristic $p$. How many distinct roots?

q: What is the connected part of $\mathbb{Z}/n$ (the constant group scheme of order $n$) over any field?
text: What is the connected part of $\mathbb{Z}/n$ (the constant group scheme of order $n$) over any field? The constant group scheme $\mathbb{Z}/n = \bigsqcup_{i=0}^{n-1} \Spec\,k$ is a disjoint union of $n$ copies of $\Spec\,k$ — étale by definition (it is finite étale of degree $n$). Connected component of identity is just the identity copy of $\Spec\,k$, i.e. trivial. A constant group scheme is the disjoint union of $|G|$ copies of $\Spec\,k$. What is its identity component?

q: Which of the following are true over a perfect field $k$ of characteristic $p > 0$?
text: Which of the following are true over a perfect field $k$ of characteristic $p > 0$? (0) is the connected/étale decomposition over a perfect field. (1) is FALSE — $\alpha_p$ and $\mu_p$ are non-isomorphic: their Lie algebras are both $k$ (1-dim) but their Cartier duals differ ($\alpha_p^\vee = \alpha_p$, $\mu_p^\vee = \mathbb{Z}/p$). (2) is FALSE — finite étale group schemes over $k$ correspond to finite groups with continuous Galois action; lots of them. (3) is TRUE: in char 0, every finite scheme is reduced (no non-trivial nilpotents), hence étale. $\alpha_p$ and $\mu_p$ are both purely infinitesimal — but are they the same?

q: Let $G$ be a finite commutative group scheme of order $p^n$ over $\bar{\mathbb{F}_p}$. Cartier duality interchanges connected and étale pieces. What is the Cartier dual of $\alpha_p$?
text: Let $G$ be a finite commutative group scheme of order $p^n$ over $\bar{\mathbb{F}_p}$. Cartier duality interchanges connected and étale pieces. What is the Cartier dual of $\alpha_p$? Cartier duality is given by $G^\vee = \Hom(G, \mathbb{G}_m)$ in an appropriate sense; on finite group schemes, on Hopf algebras it is linear dualisation $A \mapsto A^*$. For $A = k[t]/(t^p)$ with $\Delta(t) = t \otimes 1 + 1 \otimes t$, the dual Hopf algebra is again $k[t]/(t^p)$ with the same comultiplication — $\alpha_p$ is Cartier self-dual. The pair $(\mu_p, \mathbb{Z}/p)$ are also Cartier dual to each other. $\alpha_p$ is purely infinitesimal — its dual must also be purely infinitesimal (Cartier swaps connected and étale, but here both pieces are connected on each side).

q: Over a non-perfect field $k$ of characteristic $p$, the connected/étale decomposition $1 \to G^\circ \to G \to G^{ét} \to 1$ may fail to:
text: Over a non-perfect field $k$ of characteristic $p$, the connected/étale decomposition $1 \to G^\circ \to G \to G^{ét} \to 1$ may fail to: Over a perfect field, the sequence splits canonically. Over a non-perfect field, the splitting can fail — there are obstructions involving $p$-th-root-extraction (essentially, the imperfection ideal $k^{1/p} \setminus k$). The sequence is still exact, $G^\circ$ is still connected, and $G^{ét}$ is still étale, but $G$ need not be the semidirect product. Examples come from forms of $\alpha_p$ over function fields like $\mathbb{F}_p(s)$. What goes wrong when $k$ is non-perfect — does the splitting use $p$-th roots?

q: Compute $\dim_k \mathrm{Lie}(\mathrm{GL}_n)$ for $n = 4$.
text: Compute $\dim_k \mathrm{Lie}(\mathrm{GL}_n)$ for $n = 4$. $\mathrm{Lie}(\mathrm{GL}_n) = \mathfrak{gl}_n = \mathrm{Mat}_n(k)$, dimension $n^2$. For $n = 4$: $4^2 = 16$. Verify: a $k[\epsilon]$-point of $\mathrm{GL}_n$ lifting $I$ is $I + \epsilon X$ for any $X \in \mathrm{Mat}_n(k)$ — the determinant condition $\det(I + \epsilon X) = 1 + \epsilon\,\tr(X) \in k[\epsilon]^\times$ is automatic. $\mathrm{Lie}(\mathrm{GL}_n)$ is what set of matrices?

q: What is $\mathrm{Lie}(\mathbb{G}_m)$ as a Lie algebra over $k$?
text: What is $\mathrm{Lie}(\mathbb{G}_m)$ as a Lie algebra over $k$? A $k[\epsilon]$-point of $\mathbb{G}_m$ lifting $1$ is a unit of the form $1 + \epsilon a$, $a \in k$. So $\mathrm{Lie}(\mathbb{G}_m) = k$ as a vector space. Since $\mathbb{G}_m$ is commutative, the bracket is identically zero. This is the 1-dimensional abelian Lie algebra. $\mathbb{G}_m$ is commutative — what does that say about the bracket on its Lie algebra?

q: If $G \hookrightarrow H$ is a closed immersion of group schemes (i.e. $G$ is a closed subgroup of $H$), the induced map on Lie algebras $\mathrm{Lie}(G) \to \mathrm{Lie}(H)$ is:
text: If $G \hookrightarrow H$ is a closed immersion of group schemes (i.e. $G$ is a closed subgroup of $H$), the induced map on Lie algebras $\mathrm{Lie}(G) \to \mathrm{Lie}(H)$ is: The Lie functor preserves closed immersions: if $G \hookrightarrow H$ is closed, then $G(k[\epsilon]) \hookrightarrow H(k[\epsilon])$ is injective, and restricting to the kernels of reduction-to-$G(k)$ / $H(k)$ gives $\mathrm{Lie}(G) \hookrightarrow \mathrm{Lie}(H)$. Surjectivity fails in general (e.g. for $\mathrm{SL}_n \subset \mathrm{GL}_n$, the codimension is $1$). Closed immersions on dual-number points stay closed immersions on the kernels.

q: What is $H^1_{ét}(\Spec\,k, \mathbb{G}_m)$ for $k$ a field?
text: What is $H^1_{ét}(\Spec\,k, \mathbb{G}_m)$ for $k$ a field? Hilbert 90: $H^1(\Gal(\bar k/k), \bar k^\times) = 0$ for any field $k$. Equivalently, $H^1_{ét}(\Spec\,k, \mathbb{G}_m) = \mathrm{Pic}(\Spec\,k) = 0$ — over a field there is only the trivial line bundle. Every $\mathbb{G}_m$-torsor over $\Spec\,k$ trivialises. Hilbert 90 in its sheaf-cohomological form.

q: Which of the following gives a non-trivial $\mu_2$-torsor on $\Spec\,\mathbb{Q}$?
text: Which of the following gives a non-trivial $\mu_2$-torsor on $\Spec\,\mathbb{Q}$? By Kummer theory, $H^1_{ét}(\Spec\,\mathbb{Q}, \mu_2) = \mathbb{Q}^\times/(\mathbb{Q}^\times)^2$. The class of $a \in \mathbb{Q}^\times$ corresponds to the torsor $\Spec\,\mathbb{Q}[\sqrt{a}]$. For $a = 2$, this is non-trivial because $2 \not\in (\mathbb{Q}^\times)^2$. Choice (0) is the trivial torsor (it is $\mu_2 \times \Spec\,\mathbb{Q}$). Choice (3) is reducible: $t^2 - 1 = (t-1)(t+1)$, also trivial. Non-trivial $\mu_2$-torsors on $\Spec\,\mathbb{Q}$ correspond to non-square rationals $a$; the torsor is $\Spec\,\mathbb{Q}[\sqrt{a}]$.

q: For $X = \mathbb{P}^1_k$ over a field $k$, what is $H^1_{ét}(X, \mathbb{G}_m)$?
text: For $X = \mathbb{P}^1_k$ over a field $k$, what is $H^1_{ét}(X, \mathbb{G}_m)$? $H^1_{ét}(\mathbb{P}^1, \mathbb{G}_m) = \mathrm{Pic}(\mathbb{P}^1) = \mathbb{Z}$, generated by the class of $\mathcal{O}(1)$. The Picard group of projective space is the group of line bundles up to isomorphism; for $\mathbb{P}^n$ this is always $\mathbb{Z}$ (the degree map is an isomorphism). $H^1_{ét}(X, \mathbb{G}_m) = \mathrm{Pic}(X)$. What is the Picard group of $\mathbb{P}^1$?

q: Over a field $k$ of characteristic $p$, the Artin–Schreier sequence $0 \to \mathbb{Z}/p \to \mathbb{G}_a \xrightarrow{F - \mathrm{id}} \mathbb{G}_a \to 0$ (where $F$ is Frobenius) gives, for $X = \Spec\,k$ with $k$ separably closed:
text: Over a field $k$ of characteristic $p$, the Artin–Schreier sequence $0 \to \mathbb{Z}/p \to \mathbb{G}_a \xrightarrow{F - \mathrm{id}} \mathbb{G}_a \to 0$ (where $F$ is Frobenius) gives, for $X = \Spec\,k$ with $k$ separably closed: If $k$ is separably closed, every $\mathbb{Z}/p$-torsor (i.e. every $\mathbb{Z}/p$-Galois étale cover) of $\Spec\,k$ is trivial because the absolute Galois group of $k$ is trivial. Equivalently, the Artin–Schreier map $F - \mathrm{id}\colon k \to k$ is surjective for $k$ separably closed (every element of $k$ is $a^p - a$ for some $a$). For non-sep-closed $k$, the answer is $k/(F-\mathrm{id})k$. $\Spec$ of a separably closed field has trivial étale fundamental group.

q: For $X$ a smooth projective curve of genus $g \geq 1$ over $\bar k$, the group $H^1_{ét}(X, \mu_n)$ for $\gcd(n, \mathrm{char}\,k) = 1$ is:
text: For $X$ a smooth projective curve of genus $g \geq 1$ over $\bar k$, the group $H^1_{ét}(X, \mu_n)$ for $\gcd(n, \mathrm{char}\,k) = 1$ is: By the Kummer sequence $1 \to \mu_n \to \mathbb{G}_m \xrightarrow{[n]} \mathbb{G}_m \to 1$ (exact in the étale topology for $n$ invertible in $k$), we get $\Gamma^\times/(\Gamma^\times)^n \to H^1(X, \mu_n) \to \mathrm{Pic}(X)[n] \to 0$. For $X$ a complete curve over $\bar k$, $\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X) = \bar k$, so the first piece is trivial. Then $H^1(X, \mu_n) = \mathrm{Pic}(X)[n] = \mathrm{Pic}^0(X)[n] = J(X)[n] = (\mathbb{Z}/n)^{2g}$ (the $n$-torsion of the Jacobian, for $\gcd(n, p) = 1$). $\mathrm{Pic}^0(X)$ is the Jacobian, an abelian variety of dimension $g$; its $n$-torsion has rank $2g$ when $n$ is coprime to char.

title: Finite étale covers
blurb: A finite étale cover $Y \to X$ is a morphism that is finite, flat, and unramified — the algebro-geometric replacement for a topological covering space. The category $\mathrm{F\acute{E}t}/X$ of finite étale covers is the right combinatorial object on which to define a fundamental group.

title: The fiber functor at a geometric point
blurb: Choosing a geometric point $\bar{x}: \Spec\,\bar{k} \to X$ gives a fiber functor $F_{\bar{x}}: \mathrm{F\acute{E}t}/X \to \mathrm{FinSet}$ sending $Y \to X$ to the underlying set of the fiber $Y_{\bar{x}}$. The étale fundamental group is the automorphism group of this functor.

title: $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(X, \bar{x}) = \mathrm{Aut}(F_{\bar{x}})$
blurb: $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(X, \bar{x})$ is the profinite group $\mathrm{Aut}(F_{\bar{x}})$, with topology induced by the pro-finite-set structure of fibers. It classifies finite étale covers via $\mathrm{F\acute{E}t}/X \simeq \pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(X, \bar{x})\text{-FinSet}$ — Grothendieck's Galois theory.

title: $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(\Spec\,k) = \Gal(\bar{k}/k)$
blurb: For a field $k$, the étale fundamental group of $\Spec\,k$ is the absolute Galois group $\Gal(\bar{k}/k)$. Finite étale covers $\Spec\,L \to \Spec\,k$ are exactly finite separable extensions; the equivalence is classical Galois theory.

title: Frobenius generates $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(\Spec\,\mathbb{F}_q)$
blurb: $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(\Spec\,\mathbb{F}_q) = \hat{\mathbb{Z}}$, topologically generated by the Frobenius $\mathrm{Frob}_q\colon x \mapsto x^q$. Every cover of $\Spec\,\mathbb{F}_q$ is determined by the action of $\mathrm{Frob}_q$ on a finite set.

title: Comparison with topological $\pi_1$
blurb: For a smooth complex variety $X$, the étale fundamental group $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(X)$ is the profinite completion of the topological $\pi_1(X(\mathbb{C}))$ (Riemann existence). For varieties over fields of characteristic 0 this captures all finite-cover information.

q: Which conditions together define a finite étale morphism $f\colon Y \to X$ of schemes?
text: Which conditions together define a finite étale morphism $f\colon Y \to X$ of schemes? Finite étale = finite + flat + unramified. Equivalently, finite + smooth of relative dimension 0. Properness is implied by finiteness; surjectivity is not in the definition (a finite étale cover may have multiple connected components mapping to a connected base). There are exactly three independent local conditions.

q: Let $X = \Spec\,\mathbb{Q}[t,t^{-1}] = \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathbb{Q}}$ and let $Y = \Spec\,\mathbb{Q}[s,s^{-1}]$ with $s^5 = t$. What is the degree (i.e. rank of $f_*\mathcal{O}_Y$) of this finite étale cover $f\colon Y \to X$?
text: Let $X = \Spec\,\mathbb{Q}[t,t^{-1}] = \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathbb{Q}}$ and let $Y = \Spec\,\mathbb{Q}[s,s^{-1}]$ with $s^5 = t$. What is the degree (i.e. rank of $f_*\mathcal{O}_Y$) of this finite étale cover $f\colon Y \to X$? $\mathbb{Q}[s,s^{-1}]$ is free of rank 5 over $\mathbb{Q}[t,t^{-1}]$ with basis $1, s, s^2, s^3, s^4$. The fiber over any geometric point has 5 elements (the 5 fifth roots of the chosen value of $t$). Count a free basis of $\mathbb{Q}[s,s^{-1}]$ over $\mathbb{Q}[t,t^{-1}]$.

q: Where does this argument that $f\colon \Spec\,\mathbb{F}_p[s] \to \Spec\,\mathbb{F}_p[t]$, $t = s^p$, is finite étale go wrong?
text: Where does this argument that $f\colon \Spec\,\mathbb{F}_p[s] \to \Spec\,\mathbb{F}_p[t]$, $t = s^p$, is finite étale go wrong? Step 3 is wrong. $\Omega^1_{Y/X}$ is a free rank-1 $\mathbb{F}_p[s]$-module (generated by $ds$), not zero — that is the opposite of unramified. The map $s \mapsto s^p$ is purely inseparable in characteristic $p$, hence not étale; the argument that 'one generator means small' is bogus. Look carefully at the relation $d(s^p) = p\,s^{p-1}\,ds$ in characteristic $p$.

q: For $X = \Spec\,\mathbb{Q}$ and $\bar{x} = \Spec\,\overline{\mathbb{Q}}$, what is the cardinality of $F_{\bar{x}}(\Spec\,\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt[3]{2}))$?
text: For $X = \Spec\,\mathbb{Q}$ and $\bar{x} = \Spec\,\overline{\mathbb{Q}}$, what is the cardinality of $F_{\bar{x}}(\Spec\,\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt[3]{2}))$? $F_{\bar{x}}(\Spec\,L) = \Hom_{\mathbb{Q}}(L, \overline{\mathbb{Q}})$, which has cardinality $[L:\mathbb{Q}] = 3$ for $L = \mathbb{Q}(\sqrt[3]{2})$ (the embeddings send $\sqrt[3]{2}$ to $\sqrt[3]{2}$, $\omega\sqrt[3]{2}$, or $\omega^2\sqrt[3]{2}$). Note that $L/\mathbb{Q}$ is not Galois — $|\mathrm{Aut}(L/\mathbb{Q})| = 1$, but the fiber still has 3 points. Count $\mathbb{Q}$-algebra embeddings of $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt[3]{2})$ into $\overline{\mathbb{Q}}$.

q: Which of the following is NOT a property of the fiber functor $F_{\bar{x}}\colon \mathrm{F\acute{E}t}/X \to \mathrm{FinSet}$?
text: Which of the following is NOT a property of the fiber functor $F_{\bar{x}}\colon \mathrm{F\acute{E}t}/X \to \mathrm{FinSet}$? Faithfulness, exactness, and finite values are exactly the axioms making $\mathrm{F\acute{E}t}/X$ a Galois category. $F_{\bar{x}}$ is only pro-representable in general — by the universal cover viewed as a pro-object of $\mathrm{F\acute{E}t}/X$ — not representable by a single finite étale cover (covers of $X$ by simply-connected schemes don't exist when $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}$ is infinite). What kind of object would represent it, and does it live in the category $\mathrm{F\acute{E}t}/X$ itself?

q: Match each cover of $\Spec\,\mathbb{R}$ with $|F_{\bar{x}}(\,\cdot\,)|$ for $\bar{x} = \Spec\,\mathbb{C}$.
text: Match each cover of $\Spec\,\mathbb{R}$ with $|F_{\bar{x}}(\,\cdot\,)|$ for $\bar{x} = \Spec\,\mathbb{C}$. $|F_{\bar{x}}(\Spec\,A)| = \dim_{\mathbb{R}} A$ for a finite étale $\mathbb{R}$-algebra $A$. Trivial cover: 1. $\mathbb{C}/\mathbb{R}$: 2. $\mathbb{C}\times\mathbb{R}$: $2+1 = 3$. The fiber sees the underlying rank, not the field-theoretic structure. $|F_{\bar{x}}(\Spec\,A)| = \mathrm{rank}_{\mathbb{R}}(A)$ for finite étale $A$.

q: Two geometric points $\bar{x}, \bar{x}'$ over the same scheme-theoretic point of $X$ generally give non-equal fiber functors $F_{\bar{x}}, F_{\bar{x}'}$. What is the precise relationship between them?
text: Two geometric points $\bar{x}, \bar{x}'$ over the same scheme-theoretic point of $X$ generally give non-equal fiber functors $F_{\bar{x}}, F_{\bar{x}'}$. What is the precise relationship between them? Any two fiber functors at geometric points are (non-canonically) isomorphic — this is the analogue of 'change of basepoint' in topological $\pi_1$. The set of isomorphisms is a torsor under either fundamental group, and choosing one identifies the two groups up to inner automorphism. Compare with the classical fact that based vs. free homotopy classes of paths give a torsor under $\pi_1$.

q: Where does this 'proof' that the fiber functor on $\Spec\,k$ is representable by a single finite étale cover go wrong (assume $k$ is a field with infinite absolute Galois group)?
text: Where does this 'proof' that the fiber functor on $\Spec\,k$ is representable by a single finite étale cover go wrong (assume $k$ is a field with infinite absolute Galois group)? Step 3 is wrong. $P$ exists only as a pro-object : $P = \varprojlim_{L} \Spec\,L$ over all finite separable $L/k^{\mathrm{sep}}$. This is $\Spec\,k^{\mathrm{sep}}$, which has infinite-dimensional structure sheaf when $k^{\mathrm{sep}}/k$ is an infinite extension (e.g. $k = \mathbb{Q}$). It is not a finite étale cover. The closure-under-products argument fails because finite étale covers form a category closed under finite products but not arbitrary pro-objects. Could $P = \Spec\,\overline{\mathbb{Q}}$ ever be finite étale over $\Spec\,\mathbb{Q}$?

q: Arrange the steps of Grothendieck's construction of $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}$ in their logical order.
text: Arrange the steps of Grothendieck's construction of $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}$ in their logical order. Geometric point → fiber functor → automorphism group → topology → equivalence of categories. Each step uses the previous; the equivalence is the payoff. What's the very first datum you choose, and what's the last theorem you prove?

q: Under the equivalence $\mathrm{F\acute{E}t}/X \simeq \pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(X,\bar{x})\text{-FinSet}$, what corresponds to a connected finite étale cover $Y \to X$ of degree $n$?
text: Under the equivalence $\mathrm{F\acute{E}t}/X \simeq \pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(X,\bar{x})\text{-FinSet}$, what corresponds to a connected finite étale cover $Y \to X$ of degree $n$? Connected covers ↔ transitive actions on finite sets ↔ open subgroups (the stabilizer of a fiber point), index = degree. Normality is the extra condition that singles out Galois (regular) covers, where the cover is itself a torsor under $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}/H$. Transitive actions on $n$-element sets ↔ stabilizer subgroups of which index?

q: Which of the following are properties of $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(X, \bar{x})$ for $X$ a connected scheme?
text: Which of the following are properties of $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(X, \bar{x})$ for $X$ a connected scheme? Profinite by construction (inverse limit of finite groups), so equal to its own profinite completion (a profinite group is its own profinite completion). Choice of basepoint changes the group up to inner automorphism. Continuous action on each fiber is the equivalence-of-categories statement. NOT finitely generated: $\hat{\mathbb{Z}}$ is not finitely generated as an abstract group (only topologically). $\hat{\mathbb{Z}}$ is uncountable — could it be finitely generated as an abstract group?

q: Let $Y \to X$ be a Galois cover with Galois group $G$. What is the relationship between $G$ and $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(X, \bar{x})$?
text: Let $Y \to X$ be a Galois cover with Galois group $G$. What is the relationship between $G$ and $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(X, \bar{x})$? A Galois cover with group $G$ corresponds to an open normal subgroup $H \tr{}iangleleft \pi_1^{\acute{e}t}$ with $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}/H = G$. So $G$ is a finite continuous quotient — exactly the way $\Gal(L/k)$ is a quotient of $\Gal(k^{\mathrm{sep}}/k)$. Galois covers correspond to open normal subgroups; what does the quotient look like?

q: Prove that $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}$ of a connected scheme is profinite. The next step after the given ones is:
text: Prove that $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}$ of a connected scheme is profinite. The next step after the given ones is: Each naturality condition $F_{\bar{x}}(g)\circ\sigma_Y = \sigma_{Y'}\circ F_{\bar{x}}(g)$ is a closed condition (an equality of permutations in a finite group — its complement is open in the product topology). Intersection of closed sets is closed. A closed subgroup of a profinite group is profinite. Naturality conditions are equalities — what's the topological character of an equality cut?

q: What is $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(\Spec\,\mathbb{R})$?
text: What is $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(\Spec\,\mathbb{R})$? $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(\Spec\,k) = \Gal(k^{\mathrm{sep}}/k)$. For $k = \mathbb{R}$, $k^{\mathrm{sep}} = \mathbb{C}$ and $\Gal(\mathbb{C}/\mathbb{R}) = \mathbb{Z}/2$, generated by complex conjugation. The only nontrivial connected finite étale cover of $\Spec\,\mathbb{R}$ is $\Spec\,\mathbb{C}$. List the finite separable extensions of $\mathbb{R}$.

q: Match each open subgroup of $\Gal(\overline{\mathbb{Q}}/\mathbb{Q})$ with the connected finite étale cover of $\Spec\,\mathbb{Q}$ it corresponds to.
text: Match each open subgroup of $\Gal(\overline{\mathbb{Q}}/\mathbb{Q})$ with the connected finite étale cover of $\Spec\,\mathbb{Q}$ it corresponds to. Open $H \le \Gal(\overline{\mathbb{Q}}/\mathbb{Q})$ ↔ finite separable $L \subset \overline{\mathbb{Q}}$ via $L = \overline{\mathbb{Q}}^H$, with $[L:\mathbb{Q}] = [\Gal:H]$. Note that $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt[3]{2})/\mathbb{Q}$ is not Galois — the corresponding subgroup is not normal — but the equivalence works on all (connected) covers, not just Galois ones. Index of $H$ = degree of the cover.

q: How many connected finite étale covers does $\Spec\,\mathbb{F}_5$ have of degree dividing 4 (counting up to isomorphism, i.e. counting subgroups of $\Gal(\overline{\mathbb{F}_5}/\mathbb{F}_5)$ of index dividing 4)?
text: How many connected finite étale covers does $\Spec\,\mathbb{F}_5$ have of degree dividing 4 (counting up to isomorphism, i.e. counting subgroups of $\Gal(\overline{\mathbb{F}_5}/\mathbb{F}_5)$ of index dividing 4)? Connected covers of degree $n$ ↔ open subgroups of index $n$ in $\hat{\mathbb{Z}}$, of which there is exactly one (corresponding to $\Spec\,\mathbb{F}_{5^n}$). Indices dividing 4: $n=1,2,4$, giving 3 covers (no $n=3$ contribution since $3 \nmid 4$). How many open subgroups of $\hat{\mathbb{Z}}$ have index $n$ exactly?

q: Why do we use $k^{\mathrm{sep}}$ rather than $\bar{k}$ in defining the geometric basepoint $\bar{x} = \Spec\,k^{\mathrm{sep}}$?
text: Why do we use $k^{\mathrm{sep}}$ rather than $\bar{k}$ in defining the geometric basepoint $\bar{x} = \Spec\,k^{\mathrm{sep}}$? Finite étale extensions of fields are exactly finite separable extensions. Purely inseparable extensions $k(t^{1/p})/k(t)$ in characteristic $p$ are not étale — they fail unramifiedness ($\Omega^1 \ne 0$). So the étale fundamental group only sees finite quotients of $\Gal(k^{\mathrm{sep}}/k)$; the difference between $k^{\mathrm{sep}}$ and $\bar{k}$ disappears for perfect $k$ (e.g. characteristic 0 or finite fields).

q: What is $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(\Spec\,\mathbb{F}_q)$ as an abstract topological group?
text: What is $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(\Spec\,\mathbb{F}_q)$ as an abstract topological group? $\mathbb{F}_q$ has a unique extension $\mathbb{F}_{q^n}/\mathbb{F}_q$ in each degree $n$, and they fit into a tower with $\Gal(\mathbb{F}_{q^n}/\mathbb{F}_q) = \mathbb{Z}/n$ (generated by Frobenius). The inverse limit is $\hat{\mathbb{Z}}$. Note: NOT $\mathbb{Z}$ — $\hat{\mathbb{Z}}$ is the profinite completion of $\mathbb{Z}$ and is uncountable. Take an inverse limit over all finite extensions of $\mathbb{F}_q$.

q: Frobenius $\mathrm{Frob}_q$ acts on the geometric fiber of $\Spec\,\mathbb{F}_{q^{12}} \to \Spec\,\mathbb{F}_q$. How many orbits does $\mathrm{Frob}_q^4$ have on this 12-point fiber?
text: Frobenius $\mathrm{Frob}_q$ acts on the geometric fiber of $\Spec\,\mathbb{F}_{q^{12}} \to \Spec\,\mathbb{F}_q$. How many orbits does $\mathrm{Frob}_q^4$ have on this 12-point fiber? Frobenius $\mathrm{Frob}_q$ acts as a 12-cycle on the fiber (the cover is connected, of degree 12). Its 4th power $\mathrm{Frob}_q^4$ acts as a permutation with $\gcd(4, 12) = 4$ disjoint cycles, each of length $12 / 4 = 3$. So 4 orbits. $\mathrm{Frob}_q^a$ on a $\mathbb{Z}/n$-cycle has $\gcd(a,n)$ orbits.

q: Which integers $a \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$ have the property that $\mathrm{Frob}_q^a$ generates the same cyclic group $\mathbb{Z}/6$ as $\mathrm{Frob}_q$ on the 6-point fiber of $\Spec\,\mathbb{F}_{q^6} \to \Spec\,\mathbb{F}_q$?
text: Which integers $a \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$ have the property that $\mathrm{Frob}_q^a$ generates the same cyclic group $\mathbb{Z}/6$ as $\mathrm{Frob}_q$ on the 6-point fiber of $\Spec\,\mathbb{F}_{q^6} \to \Spec\,\mathbb{F}_q$? $\mathrm{Frob}_q^a$ generates $\mathbb{Z}/6$ iff $\gcd(a, 6) = 1$, i.e. $a \in \{1, 5\}$ (these are the units mod 6). For $a = 6$, $\mathrm{Frob}_q^6$ is the identity, not a generator. For $a \in \{2, 3, 4\}$, the action has multiple orbits — the cover decomposes when restricted to $\mathbb{F}_{q^d}$ for $d = 6/\gcd(a,6)$. Generators of $\mathbb{Z}/n$ are exactly the units mod $n$.

q: For a smooth complex variety $X$, what is the relationship between $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(X)$ and $\pi_1^{\mathrm{top}}(X(\mathbb{C}))$?
text: For a smooth complex variety $X$, what is the relationship between $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(X)$ and $\pi_1^{\mathrm{top}}(X(\mathbb{C}))$? Riemann's existence theorem: every finite topological cover of $X(\mathbb{C})$ is the analytification of a unique finite étale cover, so finite quotients agree. Taking inverse limits: $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(X) = \widehat{\pi_1^{\mathrm{top}}(X(\mathbb{C}))}$. They are NOT isomorphic in general — $\pi_1^{\mathrm{top}}(\mathbb{C}^\times) = \mathbb{Z}$ while $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(\mathbb{G}_{m,\mathbb{C}}) = \hat{\mathbb{Z}}$. Profinite completion always loses non-finite-quotient information.

q: Match each smooth complex variety with its étale fundamental group.
text: Match each smooth complex variety with its étale fundamental group. $\mathbb{A}^1_{\mathbb{C}}$ topologically $\simeq \mathbb{C}$ is contractible: $\pi_1^{\mathrm{top}} = 1$, so $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t} = 1$. $\mathbb{G}_m$: $\pi_1^{\mathrm{top}} = \mathbb{Z}$, completion $\hat{\mathbb{Z}}$. Elliptic curve: $\pi_1^{\mathrm{top}} = \mathbb{Z}^2$, completion $\hat{\mathbb{Z}}^2$. $\mathbb{P}^1_{\mathbb{C}} = S^2$: simply connected. Compute the topological $\pi_1$ first, then take the profinite completion.

q: Where does this 'proof' that $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(\mathbb{A}^1_{\mathbb{F}_p}) = 1$ go wrong?
text: Where does this 'proof' that $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(\mathbb{A}^1_{\mathbb{F}_p}) = 1$ go wrong? Step 3 is wrong. $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}$ is highly sensitive to the base field. Over $\mathbb{F}_p$, $\mathbb{A}^1$ admits Artin–Schreier covers $y^p - y = f(x)$ for any $f \in \mathbb{F}_p[x]$ — these are étale, connected, and Galois with group $\mathbb{Z}/p$. So $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(\mathbb{A}^1_{\mathbb{F}_p})$ is enormous (a huge profinite group with infinitely many $\mathbb{Z}/p$ quotients). Riemann existence applies only over $\mathbb{C}$. Have you ever seen $y^p - y = x$? Is it étale?

q: Let $X$ be a smooth proper variety over $\overline{\mathbb{Q}}$. Embed $\overline{\mathbb{Q}}\hookrightarrow\mathbb{C}$ to form $X_{\mathbb{C}}$. What is the relationship between $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(X)$ and $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(X_{\mathbb{C}})$?
text: Let $X$ be a smooth proper variety over $\overline{\mathbb{Q}}$. Embed $\overline{\mathbb{Q}}\hookrightarrow\mathbb{C}$ to form $X_{\mathbb{C}}$. What is the relationship between $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(X)$ and $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(X_{\mathbb{C}})$? For a geometrically connected proper variety over an algebraically closed field, étale base change to a larger algebraically closed field is an equivalence on $\mathrm{F\acute{E}t}/X$ (this uses properness — without it, base change can introduce new covers, e.g. $\mathbb{A}^1$ acquires more covers under $\overline{\mathbb{Q}}\hookrightarrow\mathbb{C}$ via transcendental extensions). So $\pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(X) = \pi_1^{\acute{e}t}(X_{\mathbb{C}})$ for $X$ proper smooth. Properness is essential — what fails for $X = \mathbb{A}^1$?

title: First-order deformations
blurb: A first-order deformation of a scheme $X_0$ over a field $k$ is a flat $k[\epsilon]/\epsilon^2$-scheme $X$ with $X \otimes k = X_0$. Geometrically: a map from the dual numbers — the tangent direction in moduli space at $X_0$.

title: Tangent space of moduli is $H^1(X_0, T_{X_0})$
blurb: Isomorphism classes of first-order deformations of a smooth $X_0$ form a $k$-vector space identified with $H^1(X_0, T_{X_0})$. This is the tangent space at $X_0$ to the moduli space — when smooth — and gives the dimension count via Riemann–Roch.

title: Obstructions live in $H^2(X_0, T_{X_0})$
blurb: The obstruction to lifting a deformation from $\Spec\,k[\epsilon]/\epsilon^2$ to $\Spec\,k[t]/(t^3)$ (and beyond) is a class in $H^2(X_0, T_{X_0})$. Vanishing of $H^2$ implies the deformation functor is unobstructed and the moduli space is smooth at $X_0$.

title: Deformation functors and Schlessinger's criteria
blurb: The deformation functor $\mathrm{Def}_{X_0}: \mathrm{Art}_k \to \mathrm{Set}$ assigns to each Artinian local $k$-algebra $A$ (a finite-length local ring with residue field $k$ — prime and maximal coincide there) the set of flat lifts of $X_0$ to $\Spec\,A$. Schlessinger's criteria characterize when $\mathrm{Def}_{X_0}$ has a hull or is pro-representable.

title: The cotangent complex $L_{X/Y}$
blurb: Illusie's cotangent complex $L_{X/Y}$ generalizes the relative cotangent sheaf $\Omega^1_{X/Y}$ to a complex in $D^{\le 0}(X)$. Tangent and obstruction spaces of any deformation problem live in $\mathrm{Ext}^i$ of $L_{X/Y}$ — the unifying object behind the previous concepts.

title: Worked example: deformations of a smooth curve
blurb: For a smooth projective curve $C$ of genus $g \ge 2$: $H^1(C, T_C)$ has dimension $3g - 3$ (the dimension of $\mathcal{M}_g$ at $C$), and $H^2(C, T_C) = 0$ (curves have no obstructions). The moduli space $\mathcal{M}_g$ is smooth of dimension $3g - 3$.

q: Which of the following are first-order deformations of $X_0 = \Spec\,k[x,y]/(xy)$ (a node) over $D = k[\epsilon]/(\epsilon^2)$? (Pick all flat lifts whose special fibre is $X_0$.)
text: Which of the following are first-order deformations of $X_0 = \Spec\,k[x,y]/(xy)$ (a node) over $D = k[\epsilon]/(\epsilon^2)$? (Pick all flat lifts whose special fibre is $X_0$.) A first-order deformation must (i) be flat over $D$, (ii) restrict to $X_0$ modulo $\epsilon$. Choices 0 and 1 both restrict to $xy = 0$ and are flat (the second is the smoothing direction). Choice 2 collapses to the trivial deformation since $\epsilon^2 = 0$. Choice 3 restricts to $x^2 = 0$, not $xy = 0$, so its special fibre is wrong.

q: What is the trivial first-order deformation of $X_0 = \Spec\,k$ (a single $k$-point)?
text: What is the trivial first-order deformation of $X_0 = \Spec\,k$ (a single $k$-point)? $X_0 \times_k \Spec\,D = \Spec\,(k \otimes_k D) = \Spec\,D$ — the dual numbers themselves form the unique first-order deformation of a single point. (And indeed $\mathrm{Def}_{X_0}(D) = H^1(\mathrm{pt}, T) = 0$ — only the trivial class.)

q: Two first-order deformations $X, X'$ of $X_0$ are equivalent iff:
text: Two first-order deformations $X, X'$ of $X_0$ are equivalent iff: Equivalence is $D$-isomorphism fixing the special fibre — the right notion to detect non-trivial deformation classes. An abstract isomorphism is too lax: two non-equivalent deformations of the node are abstractly isomorphic schemes (both are $\Spec$ of a 4-dimensional $k$-vector space).

q: Compute $\dim H^1(\mathbb{P}^n, T_{\mathbb{P}^n})$ for any $n \ge 1$.
text: Compute $\dim H^1(\mathbb{P}^n, T_{\mathbb{P}^n})$ for any $n \ge 1$. By the Euler sequence $0 \to \mathcal{O} \to \mathcal{O}(1)^{n+1} \to T_{\mathbb{P}^n} \to 0$ and the standard cohomology of $\mathbb{P}^n$, $H^i(\mathbb{P}^n, T_{\mathbb{P}^n}) = 0$ for $i \ge 1$. Hence $\mathbb{P}^n$ is rigid: it has no first-order deformations. Use the Euler sequence and the cohomology of $\mathcal{O}(d)$ on $\mathbb{P}^n$.

q: Compute $\dim T_{[C]} \mathcal{M}_g$ for a smooth projective curve $C$ of genus $g = 5$.
text: Compute $\dim T_{[C]} \mathcal{M}_g$ for a smooth projective curve $C$ of genus $g = 5$. $\dim T_{[C]} \mathcal{M}_g = \dim H^1(C, T_C) = 3g - 3 = 12$ for $g = 5$. Riemann–Roch on $T_C$ (degree $2 - 2g = -8$) plus $h^0(T_C) = 0$ for $g \ge 2$ gives the formula. $\dim \mathcal{M}_g = 3g - 3$ for $g \ge 2$.

q: For an elliptic curve $E$, what is $\dim H^1(E, T_E)$?
text: For an elliptic curve $E$, what is $\dim H^1(E, T_E)$? $T_E$ is trivial on an elliptic curve (the tangent bundle of an abelian variety is trivial), so $H^1(E, T_E) = H^1(E, \mathcal{O}_E) \cong k$. The formula $3g - 3$ specifically applies for $g \ge 2$; the genus-$1$ case is $1$, giving the moduli space of elliptic curves a 1-parameter $j$-line.

q: Compute $\dim H^0(C, \Omega^{\otimes 2}_C)$ — the space of quadratic differentials on a smooth projective curve $C$ of genus $g = 6$. (Equal to $\dim H^1(C, T_C)$ by Serre duality.)
text: Compute $\dim H^0(C, \Omega^{\otimes 2}_C)$ — the space of quadratic differentials on a smooth projective curve $C$ of genus $g = 6$. (Equal to $\dim H^1(C, T_C)$ by Serre duality.) By Serre duality, $H^1(C, T_C) \cong H^0(C, \Omega^{\otimes 2}_C)^\vee$. Apply Riemann–Roch to $\Omega^{\otimes 2}_C$ (degree $4g - 4 = 20$): for $g \ge 2$ the degree exceeds $2g - 2 = 10$, so $h^0 = \deg - g + 1 = 20 - 6 + 1 = 15 = 3g - 3$. Apply Riemann–Roch to $\Omega^{\otimes 2}_C$, which has degree $4g - 4$.

q: Why is $\mathbb{P}^n$ infinitesimally rigid — i.e., has $H^1(T) = 0$ — but $\mathcal{M}_g$ for $g \ge 2$ is positive-dimensional?
text: Why is $\mathbb{P}^n$ infinitesimally rigid — i.e., has $H^1(T) = 0$ — but $\mathcal{M}_g$ for $g \ge 2$ is positive-dimensional? The dichotomy is purely cohomological: positivity of $T_{\mathbb{P}^n}$ kills all higher cohomology, while $T_C$ has negative degree on a curve of $g \ge 2$, forcing $H^0 = 0$ but leaving $H^1 = 3g - 3$ from Riemann–Roch. Compare $\deg T_{\mathbb{P}^n}$ and $\deg T_C$ for curves $g \ge 2$.

q: When is the deformation functor $\mathrm{Def}_{X_0}$ unobstructed at $X_0$?
text: When is the deformation functor $\mathrm{Def}_{X_0}$ unobstructed at $X_0$? Unobstructedness is precisely the statement that every obstruction class — which lives in $H^2(X_0, T_{X_0})$ — is forced to vanish. So $H^2 = 0$ is sufficient (and the cleanest sufficient condition). Vanishing of $H^1$ would mean no deformations at all (rigid), and $H^0$ controls infinitesimal automorphisms, not obstructions.

q: Which of the following are infinitesimally rigid ($H^1(X_0, T_{X_0}) = 0$)?
text: Which of the following are infinitesimally rigid ($H^1(X_0, T_{X_0}) = 0$)? $\mathbb{P}^n$ has $H^1(T) = 0$ via the Euler sequence. The smooth quadric $\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1$ also has $H^1(T) = 0$ (both factors are rigid and the product preserves this). Elliptic curves have $\dim H^1(T_E) = 1$ (the $j$-line). A genus-$4$ curve has $\dim H^1(T_C) = 3(4) - 3 = 9$, very much non-rigid.

q: What does it mean for a first-order lift $X_1$ to be obstructed ?
text: What does it mean for a first-order lift $X_1$ to be obstructed ? Obstruction is exactly the (provably zero or non-zero) class $\mathrm{ob}_1(X_1) \in H^2(X_0, T_{X_0})$ which controls whether a 2nd-order lift extending $X_1$ exists. Non-zero obstruction = no extension; this is what 'obstructed deformation' means.

q: A Calabi–Yau threefold $X$ has $H^2(X, T_X)$ generally non-zero . Why are its deformations nonetheless unobstructed?
text: A Calabi–Yau threefold $X$ has $H^2(X, T_X)$ generally non-zero . Why are its deformations nonetheless unobstructed? BTT (Bogomolov–Tian–Todorov), via Ran's $T^1$-lifting, shows that a non-vanishing holomorphic volume form (the trivial canonical bundle $K_X = 0$ defining Calabi–Yau) makes every obstruction class trivial even though the codomain $H^2(T_X)$ is non-zero. So Calabi–Yau threefolds have unobstructed but very interesting moduli. Bogomolov–Tian–Todorov.

q: Why is $H^2(C, T_C) = 0$ automatic for any smooth projective curve $C$?
text: Why is $H^2(C, T_C) = 0$ automatic for any smooth projective curve $C$? Grothendieck's vanishing theorem: on a Noetherian topological space $X$ of dimension $n$, $H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ for $i > n$ and any abelian sheaf $\mathcal{F}$. For a curve $n = 1$, hence $H^{\ge 2} = 0$ automatically. This is why every smooth curve has unobstructed deformations. Dimension reasoning, not Riemann–Roch.

q: Which of the following are among Schlessinger's conditions (H1)–(H4) for a functor $F\colon \mathrm{Art}_k \to \mathrm{Set}$ with $F(k) = \{*\}$?
text: Which of the following are among Schlessinger's conditions (H1)–(H4) for a functor $F\colon \mathrm{Art}_k \to \mathrm{Set}$ with $F(k) = \{*\}$? (H1) is the surjectivity condition; (H2) the bijectivity in the special-extension case; the finite-dimensional-tangent condition in choice 3 is (H3). Choice 2 is wrong: $F$ does not need to take values in finite sets — only the tangent-space condition is required.

q: When does $\mathrm{Def}_{X_0}$ have a hull ?
text: When does $\mathrm{Def}_{X_0}$ have a hull ? Schlessinger's theorem: $\mathrm{Def}_{X_0}$ has a hull iff (H1)–(H3) hold; it is pro-representable iff (H1)–(H4) all hold. (H4) is the bijectivity strengthening of (H1).

q: What does $\mathrm{Def}_{X_0}(k[\epsilon]/(\epsilon^2))$ compute?
